EARNINGS ALERT: TTD, CRWV, COIN

Schwab Network(284.0K)·23:30·3.7K views·2026-05-07
Oliver RenickHost, Schwab Network
TL;DRPost-market trading saw a sharp sell-off in major tech and ad-tech names as Trade Desk and Cloudflare plummeted on disappointing guidance and margin compression, overshadowing a massive $100 billion backlog report from AI-infrastructure provider CoreWeave.
Market...

TL;DR

Post-market trading saw a sharp sell-off in major tech and ad-tech names as Trade Desk and Cloudflare plummeted on disappointing guidance and margin compression, overshadowing a massive $100 billion backlog report from AI-infrastructure provider CoreWeave.

The Trade Desk (TTD): Decelerating Growth and Executive Turnover

  • Market Reaction: Shares dropped approximately 15% in after-hours trading following a mixed report and weak forward guidance.
  • Financial Performance:
      • Earnings Miss: Adjusted EPS came in at $0.28, missing the $0.32 consensus estimate.
      • Revenue Beat: First-quarter revenue hit $689 million, slightly exceeding the $679.5 million expected.
  • Outlook Concerns:
      • Guidance Shortfall: Q2 revenue guidance of $750 million fell short of the $772.4 million street estimate.
      • Margin Compression: Adjusted EBITDA margins narrowed to 30%, down from 34% in the same period last year.
  • Strategic Headwinds:
      • Leadership Exit: The Chief Strategy Officer is departing to join OpenAI, adding to investor uncertainty.
      • Competitive Pressure: Analysts noted increasing competition from "walled garden" giants like Google, Meta, and Amazon.
      • Potential Tailwinds: Some analysts (Wedbush) suggest upcoming FIFA World Cup and political ad spending could provide a summer boost.

CoreWeave (CRWV): Massive AI Backlog Amidst Heavy Spending

  • Backlog Growth: The company reported a massive revenue backlog (RPO) of $100 billion, up from $67 billion in the previous quarter, driven by deals with Meta and Anthropic.
  • Mixed Financials:
      • Revenue: Beat expectations at $2.08 billion (vs. $1.97 billion estimate).
      • Profitability: Reported a wider-than-expected net loss of $740 million ($1.40 per share).
  • Operational Scaling:
      • Power Capacity: Management aims to surpass 1 gigawatt of active power soon, with a long-term goal of 8 gigawatts by 2030.
      • Business Model: Functioning as a "neo-cloud," CoreWeave services the compute overflow that major hyperscalers like AWS or Google cannot currently handle.

Cloudflare (NET): Valuation "Rug Pull" and Workforce Trimming

  • Stock Plummet: Shares fell 12–15% despite raising full-year EPS guidance, as investors balked at the company's high valuation multiples.
  • Margin Miss: Gross margins came in at 72.8%, missing the 75% target, which analyst George Tsilis identified as a primary concern for the "momentum chasers."
  • Labor Shift: The company announced a workforce reduction of approximately 1,100 employees to optimize the current structure.
  • Comparison: In contrast, competitor Akamai (AKAM) saw shares surge 24% after hours, as investors pivoted toward more reasonably valued names in the content delivery space.

Coinbase (COIN): Persistent Cash Burn and Transaction Lag

  • Revenue Miss: Q1 revenue of $1.4 billion missed the $1.5 billion estimate, leading to a 3% decline in share price.
  • Financial Health:
      • Net Loss: The company reported a net loss of $394.1 million for the quarter.
      • Subscription Weakness: Q2 subscription and services revenue guidance ($565M–$645M) was entirely below street expectations.
  • Growth Strategy: Management is pivoting toward derivatives trading via their "Everything Exchange" to offset slowing spot crypto trading volumes.
KG (Analyst): "We don't have a bottom yet when it comes to the fundamentals for [The Trade Desk]... the revenue growth is still decelerating. I'm not sure the CEO is showing any type of results when it comes to profitability or taking market share."
George Tsilis: "The challenge with [Cloudflare] is the valuation... effectively going into this the stock was already expensive on an earnings basis... right now there's a bit of a rug pull happening because it was just not enough."
Sam (Schwab Network): "The market is very sensitive to the level of spending here... there's a view that they need to be showing that they've got supporting revenue growth."
So we touched all-time highs at the open but quickly retreated here. The S&P 500 just losing about four-tenths of one percent, it's 7,337 there. The NASDAQ-100 was managing to hold its head above water for most of the day, thanks to some of those software names and names like Nvidia, Microsoft, etc., but didn't manage to hold, finishing just fractionally lower as we crossed the finish line there. The Dow Jones was underwater by about 270-odd points there, half of a percent, and the Russell 2000 was our clear underperformer on the day, shedding about 1.6 percent there. And it did come as we mentioned, we did see oil ticking up higher, particularly in the afternoon session, and yields also creeping up across the curve throughout the course of the day as well with the 10-year at 4.39 right now. And that 30-year actually getting closer to that 4.97 mark right now as well. So, technology looking a bit softer on the day, comm services on the line, discretionary on the line as well. Staples was lower, three-tenths of a percent as we can see here. Looking at the rest of the sectors here, I mean most were in the red all but comm services as we saw here. Utilities were lower by 1.3. And we're getting some of these earnings coming through and so I see Trade Desk has just hit the wire. So Marley, I'll send things back over to you with some of these numbers. All right, hit the wire, Sam, and sentiment after-market trading down significantly for this name. We're down already almost nine percent. EPS coming in at 28 cents, that is lower than the expectation that was looking for 32 cents. Revenue for the first quarter coming in at 689 million, that is a beat from the 679.5 that the street was looking for. So a bit of a mixed report there. Their second-quarter outlook though, I want to get to this too. EBITDA, they're looking for 260 million. Let's keep going here because they're saying their first quarter was another strong quarter for the Trade Desk, revenue growing, representing 12 percent year-over-year growth. We're encouraged by the impact of strategic upgrades we've been making across the company. But their EBITDA outlook for this coming quarter of 260 million is actually below the consensus here. So some potential flags there as we're starting to see things move down about nine percent here. Taking a look at more of these first-quarter numbers here, revenue, first-quarter EBITDA, that came in at 206 million. Operating income of 66.6 million, net income 40 million, and adjusted net income of 134 million, their margins at 30 percent. Also just getting their outlook for revenue here for this current quarter, 750 million, that also looking below the consensus here. So they are having a little bit of a miss here. That was a pretty significant miss on that second-quarter revenue too. They were looking for 772.4 million. So guidance weaker than expected, we've got a mixed report in terms of that EPS miss but the revenue did beat. KG, as you take a look at the Trade Desk, and we knew that they had disappointing guidance in their recent quarters. So investors were looking for some reassurance here, it does not appear that we got it. We don't have a bottom yet when it comes to the fundamentals for this company and unfortunately, it is moving to the downside. Let's see what happens at that 20 dollar level that was actually a pretty significant area or strike level if you're looking at puts for tomorrow's expiration as well as the monthly expiration for next week. So there might be some buyers that try to step in at the 20 dollar level. If you're looking at these numbers, adjusted EBITDA margin is actually once again narrowing from what we saw this time last year, coming in at around 30 percent. This time last year it was 34 percent. You're not really getting the revenue growth even though we did see the beat on the top line, the revenue growth is still decelerating. And I'm not sure, I mean the CEO is doing his job, but at the end of the day, not showing any type of results when it comes to profitability or even taking market share. In fact, they're probably still losing some market share. So this is not a good report by any stretch of the imagination and obviously, we are seeing the result of that as the stock is getting hammered and testing this 20 dollar level now. Yeah, and Sam, interestingly enough, you were having that OpenAI conversation and we're also getting news of a management change here that their chief strategy officer is departing to join OpenAI leaving the Trade Desk. So we're seeing this drop off here but I mean we've talked extensively about the competition here in this ad space from giants, you know, Google, Amazon, Meta all here in this space too and how important these numbers were going to be. You know, what's sticking out to you most here? What do you think the focus of the call will be? Yeah, obviously there's a view that the competition is getting tougher, as we know. I mean investors are also worried about the company's story and the growth outlook here. It's interesting because Wedbush has actually come out earlier this week and sort of said that there might be a few things that could offset that and that is more advertising spending related to the FIFA World Cup. So maybe there might be a question about that this summer. Not that we're necessarily getting that in the guidance just yet so maybe need a little bit more detail and then also some political spending this year, obviously being in a very highly political year. With the revenue, I know that Wedbush had also highlighted earlier this week the situation with Publicis Group. Remember reportedly actually failing to audit the company and removing it from a preferred partner list. So there was some expectation at least some view out there that that might have slightly hurt revenue in Q1 and Q2. But at the same time, on the same token, they don't believe that they're seeing advertisers necessarily leaving in large numbers here. But no doubt, of course, the pricing is a big focus so that could be something highlighted on the call and sort of business terms and transparency around ad tech companies as well. That's certainly what I've been reading going into this print. Yeah, and KG, you know we're down about 15 percent now on these numbers. Options market was pricing in a volatile 13 percent move but bigger than expected already after hours. You know, what are some of your final thoughts here on the Trade Desk while we wait for CoreWeave to come out? Yeah, you start to wonder if their business model itself and the way that they actually target ads across different platforms, if it's something that's going to sustain over the longer term or are we starting to see a little bit more of a shift. And I think that's a big concern, probably for the overall industry as well. I'm not sure if you're kind of looking at it, maybe some other platforms don't have the issue but that is probably something that we are seeing when it comes to their financials. It's not only just maybe a cyclical type of situation taking place here, it seems like it's a little bit more structural especially when it comes to the monetization. And we could have enterprises spending money elsewhere that provide, you know, better return on investment when it comes to their advertising dollars. Yeah, and KG, you brought up a really good point because I was reading some of the similar notes that Sam saw and there are some potential tailwinds here particularly around the FIFA ads, but there was a lot of question about whether the growth is merely normalizing or structurally slowing. I think there'll be a lot of questions about that on the call. But as I'm talking, CoreWeave is coming across the wire here so I'm going to pivot us over to CoreWeave and take a look at these numbers here. Their first quarter they had a net loss here of 740 million, the estimate was looking for 628. So a greater loss than expected. The loss per share coming in at a dollar 40. The estimate was supposed to be about a dollar 20, somewhere between 90 cents and a dollar 20 depending on which metric you were using. But either way, greater than expected loss there. Revenue coming in at 2.08 billion, the estimate was for 1.97. So we do have a beat on revenue. Their adjusted EBITDA for the first quarter, their margin coming in at 56 percent. That's a little bit light, the street wanted 57.4 there. For their EBITDA overall for the first quarter, we're at 1.16 billion, that is slightly better than the estimate looking for 1.14. But a bit of a mixed bag here, a bigger miss than was expected. Revenue grew a bit more than was expected here. But we are seeing a reaction here, let's see, we're changing a lot right now. Right now we're up 4 percent. So KG, as we look at how we're processing these numbers, we don't have any guidance out yet here or anything yet, just these first-quarter numbers. Revenue beating, we were expected to see revenue double year-over-year, we got that. But the EPS loss is far greater than what was expected. So you know, we're seeing a nice move to the upside though, what do you think? So their revenue backlog I think is where the market is focusing on. That came in at 100 billion dollars. They do believe that they're going to be able to surpass 1 gigawatt of active power and also believe that they're on their way for 8 gigawatts of power by 2030. So you're talking about in four years, almost 8x-ing their consumption. And so I think that's probably a little bit more of an optimistic take. This is a neo-cloud, so they basically, I mean they do have their own customers, but they're basically getting the overflow from let's say Google or Amazon, AWS or what have you, right? Those that do not have the capacity, CoreWeave is really the ones that are actually servicing them. For now they are definitely needed but I think in the future, I think the big question is going to be their ability to keep up margins and their pricing ability while these other big hyperscalers are still building out their own infrastructure. Now they have a lot of deals in the backlog. Let's see if it actually does come to fruition here. But if you're kind of looking at that growth rate of what the commentary is showing, you're talking about once again 8x by 2030, that's a pretty lofty goal. If they're able to do that then you could make the case that this name could go higher here over the next couple of years. Yeah, and certainly Sam, we talked about CoreWeave a couple times today together and you know the RPO was a big number, they're coming off of 67 billion in the previous quarter. KG remarking on some of those big deals that they announced, Meta, Anthropic included just in the last month. Expectation was for about 95 billion, now we're looking at north of 100 billion. And you even commented sometimes the CEO likes to pop down to the NYSE floor and make some commentary. But some you know nice commentary in advance of the call here, the strongest bookings quarter in CoreWeave history and that 8 gigawatts by 2030 saying we've positioned our capital structure to scale with the opportunity ahead. I mean the street likes it even though the quarter, oh actually we've turned slightly red, we're unchanged right now. A lot of questions likely coming on this call. So I mean, what do you think we'll hear? Yeah, I think obviously they're going to have to flesh out some of the details around that. I mean the commentary does look good, as you say, strongest bookings that they've seen in the history as far as the quarter. I know that the market is very sensitive to the level of spending here so there's a view that basically they need to be showing that they've got supporting revenue growth and so the revenue looks okay here. I know that was an expectation going into this print that it would echo sort of the strong results that we'd heard from say the Amazons and the Googles because of the demand we're just seeing for AI compute broadly speaking and also just because of how much these companies are actually spending. Obviously this is in the business of actually leasing the Nvidia chip computing power and some of the concern has been, and you and I talked about this earlier Marley, is the debt that they're taking on to actually buy the chips to rent out that computing then. And you know in February I remember the stock crashed off the back of a Capex announcement. So I know that they've been very sensitive to that as well. But yeah, the recently announced agreements with Meta and Anthropic, Jane Street are all focal points. I know that if the Capex does exceed expectations it's going to be whether the margins keep expanding as well. I know that obviously that's been a big focus and also want to hear about this relationship with OpenAI because as Gil mentioned, we have had this Journal report out about not meeting its target. I mean OpenAI needs a lot of compute so we want to know from CoreWeave as to how much capacity they have for OpenAI even though it is diversified across other platforms. Yeah, I mean absolutely questions about you know any exposure there in these clients that we're talking about here. But I'm not seeing the Capex number anywhere and they did go out and say we aren't going to give our guidance until we're on the call. So you know KG, is that why we're seeing this sort of holding pattern here? Everybody's just waiting for that next piece of information. Yeah, that might be the case. I mean if you're kind of looking at the metrics, they were able to beat on EBITDA on the bottom line there, so that's a good sign. The margin when you're looking at EBITDA margin, it did not beat the street's expectations. So where did we see that downside beat? Or that, yeah, that downside beat? That's really because of volume and the amount of deals that they are doing, that's where you can kind of extract that. So you have the revenue outpacing, margins maybe not holding up as much as the street expected but you're able to beat bottom line, I think that's a good sign. That's exactly what you want to see. We'll see what their quarter is going to look like for their guidance on the conference call. Maybe a little bit concerned about the Capex spend, but this is the right direction that you'd like to see for this company compared to what we saw two quarters ago, three quarters ago. Looks like they're going in the right direction. All right, and as we look at CoreWeave now, we're down about three and a half percent but obviously a lot of questions remain as everyone's waiting to hear what that guidance is in about 45 minutes on the call there. So I do want to flip us over to Coinbase here, as I'm looking at Coinbase we're down about two and a half percent right now as these numbers just come across here, let's take a look. As we get their first-quarter revenue coming in at 1.4 billion, the estimate was looking for about 1.49, 1.5 depending on which service or metric you use. So a slight miss there as we look at that one. Their transaction revenue coming in at 756 million which was even lighter than the expectation looking for 785.9. As we take a deeper dive into some of these numbers here, for the second quarter seeing their subscription and services revenue at 565 to 645 million. That's entirely below the expectation which was for 655.5 million. Sales and marketing expenses expected to be 2 to 300 million, the estimate right at the midpoint there about 245.3. And technology development and G&A expenses expected to be 820 million to 870 million. They also expect their yearly adjusted expenses to be flat year-over-year. And they do have a restructuring expense they say coming in this current quarter of between 50 and 60 million dollars. Also saying we saw huge growth in derivatives trading volume driven by our Everything Exchange. And I did talk to their chief strategist the other day on air here and he was talking quite a bit about this Everything Exchange. But we are seeing Coinbase move lower, KG. I mean is this sort of in line with what the expectation was here? It's really not a considerable miss. It's not a considerable miss, but they're burning cash like crazy and that's the problem that you actually have with this particular name here. If you're looking at the net loss for the quarter, it came in at 394.1 million dollars. And you would think that we actually had a better quarter when it comes to crypto trading and it just seems like they did not. Now they have a lot of opportunity and they talk about a lot of these opportunities moving forward especially in the derivative space. Maybe that's going to be something to kind of turn around this company. But the structure that they currently have right now on the trading volume front especially on the crypto side, it seems like it's not really holding up and unfortunately, you're going to continue to see a re-rating to the downside from analysts when it comes to those expectations. You also have a compliance business that actually does relatively well, but maybe the adoption or maybe other players within this space is also maybe eating into some of their margins as well. But this is not really a good sign. We had this outsized burn of cash or net loss that we had last quarter. I don't know if we all kind of remember that. They're following up with another net loss. I think the big question on the conference call is what's going actually turn this around in the near term. How can you turn that around? Is it going to be expense management? Unfortunately, their guidance is saying no. And so how are you going to grow that top-line growth? Let's see if the expectations moving forward if their leadership team is going to really address that. But right now this looks like this is a cash burn for a company that should be for the most part well-established within this industry. All right, well Coinbase moving lower on this report. They are trading down close to 3 percent on the heels of this report. Kevin, really appreciate you being with us to take a closer look at those names as they came across the wire. Coinbase down about 3 percent, CoreWeave down a little bit more than 4 percent, the Trade Desk down 15 percent right now. I do want to welcome George Tsilis into the conversation though to take a look at the earnings from Cloudflare, they're just coming across the wire here. Taking a look at Cloudflare's move, we are down more than 12 percent. So let's get to these numbers here, let's start with the current quarter, the first quarter here. EPS at 25 cents, the estimate was looking for about 23 cents, so a couple pennies beat there. The revenue of 639.8 million, that is a beat over the 622.7 expected, it's 34 percent year-over-year growth. Operating income coming in at 73.1 million, that's also better than the expectation, it's about 31 percent year-over-year growth. And their gross margins coming in at 72.8 percent, the estimate was looking for 75. So a little bit narrower than what was expected here. As far as their second-quarter forecast, EPS of 27 cents right in line with the estimate. Revenue 664 to 665 million, that's below the 666 the street wanted. Adjusted operating income expected to be between 90 and 91 million, the street wanted 88.4 so a little bit better there, mixed guidance as well. Also giving a full-year forecast. They're seeing EPS of a dollar 19 to a dollar 20, that is up from a dollar 11 to a dollar 12. The estimate was looking for 1.13, so better than expected there. Revenue of 2.81 billion is the expectation, that's an increase from their previous range and it is better than what the street was looking for. And for the full-year operating income 418 to 421 million, that was better than the previous range they had forecast of 378 to 382 million. So George, as we take a look at some of these numbers here, I mean not a significant beat on the EPS, a little bit better than expected on the revenue, operating income better than expected, a little bit of a miss on those margins there but the guidance otherwise looks pretty decent. Why are we seeing such a hefty move to the downside? So it was not enough, to be honest with you, because we talked about this earlier, the challenge with this company even though there was a lot of momentum in the last five days or so is the valuation. I mean the numbers came in they look really good on the surface when it comes down to the EPS numbers. So 25 cents last year, 16 cents was the print, so effectively that's more than 50 percent increase. And then again the 640 million versus 479 million for the same quarter last year. I think their gross margin number is a concern because you're looking at the numbers they thought it was going to grow to 75 percent, the last four quarters it was around 74 and a half percent. So they continue to move the needle on gross margins but it looks like based on what you mentioned, they missed that number by about 300 basis points. So that's a problem. And then again the issue is I had before, and you can correct me or add to this as well Marley, in terms of what the guidance for the full fiscal year EPS is. But before today's report it was a dollar 12 and I talked about this earlier that that represents about a 221 times, you know, this year's earnings, 172 times next year's earnings. So effectively going into this the stock was already expensive on an earnings basis. It looks like the momentum chasers pushed it higher and it looks like right now there's a bit of a rug pull happening here on Net because it was just not enough. Yeah, and they did raise that EPS full-year forecast to 1.19 to 1.20 but you know if the concern was already that it was priced a little too high, you know seeing that uptick here. But revenue you know a little slightly better than expected, a slight increase there as well but pretty much in line, not a significant move there. But Sam as we're looking at this, I mean we've seen time and time again this earnings season, this it just simply wasn't enough. The bar is just so high for some of these names where we've seen these run-ups. I mean just in the last month up 19 percent, but up more than 100 percent year-over-year for Cloudflare. Yeah, that's right and I would agree with George. I mean at this point the valuation looking a bit rich at this point. But I mean the thing is is that it's expected to continue to benefit from that shift to AI particularly agentic AI and maybe that's why it wasn't enough because the thinking is with as far as I was seeing, the adoption of the AI agents. I mean the internet activities basically expanding and enterprises are also moving away from some of the traditional models into some of the AI agents and sort of more flexible ones and so Cloudflare is becoming sort of an attractive alternative here. And it's also been benefiting from its overseas expansion as well. I know the company makes a big chunk of its revenue from outside of the US so it's fairly diversified as well. And in that sense, yeah, maybe it's just the fact that it wasn't enough given the high expectations and high bar for that one. Yeah, I mean George, as I'm going through the report here, I mean it's a little light on detail. I think the call, a lot of this stuff will come out on the call too but you know large customers, how much is that a potential sway here? Their RPO any deferred revenue growth that they're seeing? So I'll tell you what, I mean I'm just going to use a couple other stocks to help out here. If you look at Akamai, I hope your producer could pull this up. They reported it just now as well. And Akamai is also a content delivery cloud computing company that competes with Net. Akamai's valuation very much more in line with normal averages and the stock is ramping up about 30 bucks. It was 116 at the close now 145. So I just think that what's happening here is is that you know prices across the board in this group, Fastly, Net, Akamai, these are three competitors, were moving in tandem. Fastly moved down significantly today due to valuation concerns, I think Net's moving down as well based on that. The only thing that could have changed I think Net would have been something of a partnership, a deal with maybe some larger hyperscaler, maybe one of the AI players like of course Anthropic. I'm just throwing these things out there. But I think really right now, and this is also contingent upon the market, the market was a little bit soft today overall. There's no appetite for high-value names, at least right now in the tech space for networking or content delivery cloud names like Net. So just looking back, like I said, look at Akamai, I just noticed that it's higher significantly in the after-hours. Yeah, certainly as Akamai up about 24 and a half percent while on the flip of that we're looking at Cloudflare down about 12 percent. Also announcing that they are cutting their current workforce by about 1100 people. So we've been expecting more of these workforce trimming announcements and here comes another one with Cloudflare. George, really appreciate you being with us to take a closer look at Cloudflare with that 12 plus percent move to the downside that we're seeing. Oh, now it's jumped to 15 percent.

Extreme Mortman: When DC Laughed at Itself: Dan Glickman and the Golden Age of Political Comedy

C-SPAN(1.8M)·40:22·271 views·2026-05-07
Dan GlickmanFormer U.S. Secretary of Agriculture Howard MortmanHost, C-SPAN
TL;DRFormer Secretary of Agriculture Dan Glickman argues that self-deprecating humor is an essential, yet vanishing, tool for effective political leadership and bipartisan cooperation.
Leadership Utility: Glickman cites President Eisenhower’s belief that a sense of humor is...

TL;DR

Former Secretary of Agriculture Dan Glickman argues that self-deprecating humor is an essential, yet vanishing, tool for effective political leadership and bipartisan cooperation.

The Strategic Value of Political Humor

  • Leadership Utility: Glickman cites President Eisenhower’s belief that a sense of humor is essential for leadership, as it makes a leader more likable and persuasive.
  • Self-Deprecation: Defusing Criticism: Glickman posits that when politicians highlight their own "blemishes" through humor, it takes the "edge off" the jokes made by others and humanizes them to a skeptical public.
  • The "Carson" Model vs. Modern Comedy:
      • Glickman contrasts the "low intensity," unifying humor of Johnny Carson with the "high intensity," overtly partisan humor of modern late-night hosts like Stephen Colbert and Jimmy Kimmel.
      • He argues that modern political comedy often prevents viewers from "relaxing," whereas classic humor helped bridge divides.

Bipartisanship and the "Golden Age" of Cooperation

  • The Dole-Clinton Dynamic: Despite being political rivals, President Bill Clinton and Senate Minority Leader Bob Dole maintained a genuine fondness for one another.
  • Unlikely Advocacy: Secretary of Agriculture Appointment: Glickman reveals that Bob Dole (a Republican) was the primary advocate who convinced Bill Clinton (a Democrat) to appoint Glickman to the Cabinet after Glickman lost his 1994 re-election bid.
  • Confirmation Record: Glickman was confirmed by the Senate with a 93-0 vote, a feat he believes would be nearly impossible in the current polarized political environment.

Career Transitions: From the Heartland to Hollywood

  • USDA Leadership: As Secretary, Glickman navigated the transition of American agriculture into the 21st century, focusing on innovation and maintaining the "envy of the world" status of the U.S. food system.
  • MPAA Tenure: After the USDA, Glickman served as Chairman and CEO of the Motion Picture Association of America (MPAA).
      • Industry Focus: His work centered on intellectual property, film piracy, and overseeing the movie rating system.
      • Personal Connection: His favorite film is *Animal House*, which he claims mirrored his own chaotic fraternity experience at the University of Michigan (1962–1966).

Advocacy for Transparency and C-SPAN

  • Early Support: Glickman was a seminal supporter of C-SPAN, working alongside founder Brian Lamb (whom he met while interning in the 1960s).
  • Institutional Access: Glickman played a key role in the effort to allow C-SPAN cameras into the House of Representatives, arguing that it was vital for the public—and Congress members themselves—to see the legislative process in action.

Notable Quotes

"The key to late-night political humor is that it targets those qualities that we, the politicians, don't want you to see. But if we were to bring out our blemishes into the open, it takes the edge off the jokes that other people make about us." — Dan Glickman
"Dole said to me, 'I just have one question for you... Will this get you out of Kansas forever?' I said, 'Well, if you're asking if I would ever run against you, no.' And so Dole was a big helper." — Dan Glickman
"I decided that I had an interesting career... but I also felt like the humor that exists in this country is kind of missing in action when it comes to natural self-deprecating humor in the political sphere." — Dan Glickman
"I picked him for this job because the Department of Agriculture and rural America, more than ever, need a leader who is experienced, aggressive, and innovative. I know he will be an advocate and a spokesman for making sure that American agriculture enters the 21st century on a prosperous and solid foundation. Our agricultural system is the envy of the world, and it must remain so. I also chose Dan Glickman for his common sense and his good humor. He says he always wears a sunflower on his lapel to remind him of where he's from, the values of the heartland that make him what he is. I hope and expect he will keep wearing that sunflower and keep us in a sunny disposition. Mr. Glickman." "Thank you, Mr. President. Thank you. I better get a supply of them." "That was President Bill Clinton, alongside Dan Glickman, both appearing at the White House in the fabulous Rose Garden. It was December 28th, 1994, just three days after Christmas and just 23 days after Hanukkah. So President Clinton gave Dan Glickman a lovely seasonal present: a post in his cabinet. You heard Dan Glickman open with a joke. That's something he's done many, many times, and it's something you'll experience firsthand in this episode of C-SPAN's podcast 'Extreme Mortman', because we have a very special guest in our studio. It's Dan Glickman himself. We're talking politics and comedy with the former Congressman, the former Agriculture Secretary, the former top lobbyist for the movie industry, a current fellow at the Bipartisan Policy Center, Chairman of the Board of Hunger Free America, and the author of 'Laughing at Myself: My Education in Congress, on the Farm, and at the Movies'. We're thrilled to have him here in person in the glass-enclosed nerve center of Extreme Mortman in beautiful downtown Capitol Hill. So get ready to throw your head back and roar in laughter with an American original and a true political junkie, Dan Glickman!" "Thank you, Howard. Such an introduction, I've never had one like this before." "Oh, you've never had any introduction before? That's right, the first one, nothing like that. All right, all right, it was so much, I want to talk about the book and about your career and all that. Walk us through that clip with President Clinton introducing you in 1994." "Well, the interesting thing is the story of how I got the job. So I had lost my election about a month and a half beforehand. I was part of the sweep out in the Gingrich revolution; historically we remember 1994 when Gingrich had the 'Contract with America', and so I lost, and by a pretty good margin. And so I was for about three days, I was kind of down, thinking, 'What am I going to do the rest of my life?'. And then somebody said to me—it was actually Pat Roberts, who was a senator from Kansas at the time—he said, (he was actually the congressman from Kansas, he became a senator) and a good friend, he said, 'Well, I think you could be Secretary of Agriculture if you really pushed it.' And it turned out that the then-then Secretary of Agriculture, his name was Mike Espy, and he kind of got in a little bit of trouble and the President had to let him go, and he had to get somebody to replace him. And the Democrats had just lost the Congress, both houses of Congress. But I had a secret person that was helping me besides Roberts, and it was Bob Dole. And Bob Dole was the senator from Kansas at the time and so I went to Dole and I said, 'You know, I think that they may be interested in me being Secretary of Agriculture.' And Dole said to me, he says, 'Well, I just have one question for you.' I said, 'What's that?' He says, 'Will this get you out of Kansas forever?' And so I said, 'Well, if you're asking if I would ever run against you, no.' And so Dole was a big helper; Dole was majority leader. And so I had Bob Dole, and Dole went to Clinton and Clinton, I think was nice, thought that I probably could get confirmed, and I'd been on the Agriculture Committee. But the fact that Dole was helping me was the icing on the cake. And so I was nominated and confirmed 93 to zero. I don't know where the other seven went, trying to find them since then. But can you imagine being confirmed with no negative votes whatsoever? Today, I don't think anybody's been confirmed unanimously, at least not for a cabinet position." "You know, the thing, one thing about that story that also is amazing is that the Republican leader is the one who went to a Democratic president and said, 'Here is your cabinet pick,' which also is unheard of now." "It was unheard of, and you know, they liked each other, even though Dole was—could be quite partisan, but he was also a very effective leader. Like, we haven't had anybody like him since then, I don't think. And Clinton always admired Dole; they were different personalities. You know, they were like, Clinton was the boisterous, could talk to anybody anywhere at any time, and Dole was more taciturn, although he had a great biting sense of humor. And—but there was a general—genuine fondness there, and I think I was the beneficiary of that." "Okay, so I want to get to the jokes and the comedy in the book, but I have to point out, you have been a long-time booster and fan and supporter and friend of C-SPAN. And why—tell me about your history with C-SPAN that goes all the way back." "Well, I knew Brian Lamb, who was basically the founder of this, because he worked for Senator Peter Dominick for years. He was a senator from Colorado, Republican senator, and I was an intern in Dominick's office when I was in law school at GW. So I knew Brian well and he liked me and I liked him, and he had a great personality. And then of course, he started C-SPAN, and then and then I was elected to Congress, and I did everything I could to help C-SPAN. Back in the early days, C-SPAN could not get its cameras into the House, and then later into the Senate. And then even though they got their cameras in, you couldn't show them in House offices. And there was a member of Congress who didn't particularly want—didn't like C-SPAN or didn't want them there, his name was Charlie Rose from North Carolina, who was also a good friend of mine. And so I went to Brian, I said 'I want to help you get C-SPAN into the House,' and it was—we accomplished it. I think it was a seminal moment in C-SPAN's life because you know, the whole essence of C-SPAN was showing what was happening in the Congress, and now that the Congress can see themselves in action, it made a big difference." "Okay, great. I want to talk about the book 'Laughing at Myself'. Why did you write the book 'Laughing at Myself'?" "I wanted to see myself on the New York Times bestseller list. And it didn't happen—it happened on my bestseller list, but anyway. No, I decided that I had an interesting career that—this you know, son and grandson of Jewish immigrants who became a congressman and and and became active in agriculture. And then I went into—became a lobbyist for Hollywood for a while and I had this interesting career, coming from the heartland. I also felt like, and I still feel like, there's—there's the humor that exists in this country is kind of missing in action when it comes to natural self-deprecating humor in the political sphere. And so our great presidents, the really great ones, were always ones that had very good senses of humor. You go backwards and Reagan, Roosevelt, Abraham Lincoln, and and there were others, and and so I—just—it's missing, and and it was missing in terms of in Congress and the political humor. And the humor that was there was almost always negative, mean, biting humor, and I said, 'Well, you know what, it made a big difference in my life.' And Eisenhower, who was a Kansan, and we you know, claim him at least as a Kansan, used to say that a good sense of humor was essential for leadership. Because if you—if you're funny and people liked you, they would do what you wanted them to do; it was easier to persuade them. And he talked about that all his time, including up until D-Day, he used to talk about the necessity of a good sense of humor. So that's why I kind of wrote the book." "You know, it's interesting, so many people in Washington write books about their career and the book's focus is how great that person is and how lousy their opponents are and how important their advice was and how they influenced the course of American history. You wrote a book about jokes, which seems that doesn't put you in the club, like that's like nobody writes books like that." "Well, part of that I had parents who were—they just they told it was humor. The whole life was filled with humor, and it wasn't always love and happiness in my family, I don't want to tell you that, this was a Jewish family, we had our problems. But but it was almost it was always a good sense of humor and and so my dad was from the the kind of Rodney Dangerfield, Henny Youngman, the Borscht Belt schtick humor, and my mother was more natural, she could make funny jokes out of what was happening in the world. So that was very much a part of our lives and and so I took that with me and I think it helped me in my years in public life a lot." "You mentioned your family. And actually, I want to read an excerpt from your book. By the way, I'm going to ask you one more question about the book and you probably got this question a lot as you've been talking about it and so forgive me if if you're tired of hearing this question. What is your book's ISBN number?" "I can't tell you because I was in the intelligence world and I have several things that I can't tell you and that's one that I can't tell you." "That's one you can't tell you can't disclose, I totally respect that, totally respect that. Okay, what the hell is he talking about? Totally respect that. Okay, here's an excerpt from the book: 'My mother used to tell us this joke about their marriage. I never once thought of divorcing your father, she said. Murdering him, yes. My dad told us a similar joke: When your mother turns 40, I'm going to trade her in for two 20s.' Yes, did that kill in the Glickman household?" "That was—that was kind of the joke, you know, he once said, 'You know, I proposed to your mother in a garage and then I couldn't back out.' You know, you know so so this was kind of the—we had a lot of that. And my dad, it wasn't all natural, he used to subscribe to joke services, this would be before the internet. And when he died, we found ten legal pads filled—he had handwritten jokes—and then people would call him from all over the country. Former Speaker Jim Wright, who was born on the same day as my dad, they became friends as a result of my service here. Wright used to call my dad up eight, ten times a year and said, 'Milton, I'm giving this speech. Do you have a joke for me?' It was like, you know, who would have thought? Maybe my dad made Jim Wright's career, who knows." "Okay, all right, we're going to play some clips now and get Secretary Dan Glickman to respond and react. Okay, we'll start with two from Dan Glickman's time as a member of Congress. The first clip is from early on, a House floor speech, December 5th, 1980." "I would like to follow up on some of the comments of my colleague from New Jersey. I think there are three issues in this case. One is, is there responsibility on the part of the Army, even if there is no legal liability? And I think that the answer is yes. We have a man who, over 20 years ago, was force-fed LSD during the course of an investigation without his consent. And I would like to read a portion of a letter that the General Counsel of the Army wrote to our Chairman, George Danielson, on December 1st. And I quote: 'The circumstances surrounding the prolonged detention and interrogation of Mr. Thornwell, the surreptitious administration of LSD to him in June 1961, and the subsequent failure by the Army to advise him that he had been given LSD or to provide him appropriate care for almost 15 years after the fact, are set out in my predecessor's report. Suffice it to say that, due to the unconscionable nature of the wrongs done to him,' here is the United States Army saying, 'due to the unconscionable nature of the wrongs done to him, we feel that the Army has a moral obligation to compensate Mr. Thornwell adequately for his injuries.' Both the Department of Justice and the Department of the Army have acknowledged that this man was wronged by being force-fed LSD, and therefore we've made the judgment, yes, there is some responsibility to compensate him. Incidentally, while we have kind of downplayed his condition, the facts indicate that the man has during this period of time and afterwards become somewhat of using this term in its kindest senses I can, a mental vegetable, and therefore the LSD did have enormous effect." "Okay, I want to get, I want you to explain a little bit more about that. But the first thing that comes to mind, to my mind, when you say 'mental vegetable', that's a foreshadowing of you being Secretary of Agriculture. Is that right?" "That's right, and we did a lot of research on that, we could never find a mental vegetable. This reminds me of the time that Bill Clinton gave a State of the Union message and he talked a lot about bad federal spending, you know fraud, and he talked about, believe it or not, the Agriculture Department is giving money for plant stress. And I was Secretary at the time and I said, 'Mr. President, the plant's not going to a psychiatrist. The stress is due to heat, stress and disease.' So maybe I had all this on my mind, the relationship between agriculture and mental health. But that was an issue, I think there was a relative of the Thornwell family that came to me and came to the Judiciary Committee, on which I served, and we had the authority to provide special legislation to compensate people for wrongs the government did. And so I'm remembering that case more and more as I'm hearing my own voice talk about it. But it's been a long time that I haven't dealt with this—that issue." "By the way, what does it feel like to hear your own voice from 1980?" "It's rehabilitating in my own soul." "Nice, nice, nice, very good. All right, and here is a second clip we want to play from Secretary Glickman's time in Congress, from May 21st, 1992, toward the end of his time in Congress. Here's Dan Glickman." "Mr. Speaker, this country has a lot of problems. Jobs moving overseas, lack of access to healthcare, violent crime, urban problems. But one thing that keeps us in good spirits is our sense of humor. A recognition that none of us is so important that we can't laugh at ourselves. Tomorrow night is Johnny Carson's last night on The Tonight Show. Johnny Carson is a uniquely American comedian from the heartland of America, who has never so wrapped up in his own self-importance that he couldn't relate to the problems and needs of average Americans. This country will suffer a profound loss when Johnny Carson leaves The Tonight Show. While I won't miss his frequent jabs at Congress, which have been very frequent lately, America will miss his good-natured and genuinely funny and uniquely American humor. That humor has helped to keep Americans laughing in bad times and in good times, and it has kept even the most important people in America very humble indeed. We will miss you, Johnny. This country will miss you very much. We hope that your humor stays alive and that we will continue to see you in this great country of ours." "So I did that to try to get on the Johnny Carson Show." "You took away my one question I had about that clip, and it didn't work. But I will have to tell you, I could do that same speech right now. And the fact of the matter is, there was nobody like Johnny Carson, and you know, generationally most people don't even know know who he is. But he could be so incredibly funny without being overtly political. Today the people who are on late-night television, they're funny, you know Colbert's funny and and Jimmy Fallon, Jimmy Kimmel are funny, but it's almost all political. It's like it's not what I call warm human what—I remember the lady who had the funny-looking potato chips and Johnny ate one while she was sitting on the side, you know? Or the 102-year-old farmer that was doing something crazy or or remember when he threw the—the hatchet at Ed Ames, you know?" "Yeah, the tomahawk, the tomahawk, and they said, you know, the jokes—I didn't know you were Jewish. Yeah, didn't know you're Jewish." "But anyway, but Carson was just incredibly kind of like relaxing, and you know, my parents used to stay up every night and watched him, and you know it's like—and it was low intensity rather than high intensity comedy. But I loved it." "Do you now, for late-night comedians, you mentioned a couple current ones, do you watch the shows, you watch them on YouTube?" "I watch all—I mean episodically I watch all the shows, and they're interesting, and they politically sometimes they make me feel good because they're almost—well, Jimmy Fallon's more of a vaudevillian, he's not as political. But the other two, Kimmel and and Colbert are very political. And especially Colbert. They're—but they're funny. But they're political, and you know, you don't relax when you—when you hear their humor. You know, you relax with Johnny Carson." "All right, you took away the one question I had: Did you get on the Carson Show? And you didn't. But did they ever acknowledge or did—did anybody notice that you said nice things about him on the floor of the US Congress?" "No, but indirectly there was a other thing that during Saturday Night Live in the early days, it was a 1980 presidential election and they must have—they had a cameraman whose name was Glickman, no relation to me. But anyway for a while they were doing the presidential race and they had Carter, Reagan, and Glickman. And of course I claimed that the Glickman was me, and in the—it was a third, a third, a third of the total vote. And maybe that's what ultimately got me interested in Johnny Carson. Obviously Reagan won that race." "He won that race and that's why Bob Dole wants you you not run for Senate. All right, now we move into Dan Glickman's time as Secretary of Agriculture. First off, from March 21st, 1995, his confirmation hearing in the Senate, here's someone we've talked about, Kansas Republican Bob Dole." "I think we need an experienced and committed advocate at the Department of Agriculture. This is a very difficult time. And I might agree with Pat Leahy: I know Dan Glickman is very smart, in fact when he was chairman of the Intelligence Committee I used to tell people he's smarter than any of us, but he can't tell us anything because they're all secret. Now he can tell us all these things he knows. But when he made this decision to take this job, some may question his his judgment, but I—I didn't. I think it's—I think it's an honor for Dan Glickman, it's certainly an honor for the state of Kansas, because we have someone who'll do the job and do it right. That's—that's the important thing, somebody that we know, somebody we can trust, somebody we can talk to." "All right, thoughts on Bob Dole?" "Well, it brings back fond memories, because you know, Dole was instrumental in me getting the—getting the job. I remember I was in his office as they were voting, and they voted 94 to zero, which I think had a lot to do with Dole, and Dole's relationships over there and being from Kansas and and whatever, you know? And Dole wasn't necessarily all that funny when he announced me. But we had a—good times together and and it was the best job I ever had, to be honest with you, you know." "By the way, Bob Dole mentioned the Intelligence Committee, you mentioned earlier, is there any secret that you've never told that you can tell the listeners of this podcast?" "Well, there's two-three people in this room and I would probably have to kill you." "Oh, what a way to go! Are we all in? Are we all—yes? No, different all right, okay. Another clip, now January 15th, 1997, Secretary Glickman appears at the legendary National Press Club. It's lunchtime, and he's introduced by club president Sonya Hilgren." "Had things gone a bit differently, our speaker today, Agriculture Secretary Dan Glickman, might instead be a US Senator from Kansas. That's a job he always seemed to have his eyes on while representing the Wichita area during nine terms in the US House. Even as a student, while serving as an intern for Colorado Senator Peter Dominick, Glickman climbed into the Senator's chair because he wanted to see what it would feel like to be a sitting Senator. And I heard that from Brian Lamb of C-SPAN, who was here earlier this month." "And then Secretary Glickman opened with this line." "Sonya, it is a pleasure to be here with you, that you have had a very distinguished one-year period of time. I was there at the beginning, kind of. And and I've enjoyed watching you as you have been treated well by C-SPAN viewers. You have hosted people like Benjamin Netanyahu and Carol Channing. I guess I'm somewhere in the middle of those two people." "I thought that was really funny, so." "Yeah, well, you know, I used to tell people I actually wasn't the first Jewish Secretary of Agriculture. I was the first Jewish Secretary of Agriculture in America. But Joseph in the Bible was the first Secretary of Agriculture because remember he got the Pharaoh to do this—six years on, seven years-one year off in terms of fertilizer, making sure that the soil was fertile and having soil banks or having a grain reserves. And these were—lasted till today, so from Joseph to Glickman, you know." "Okay, I want to—I do want to ask about the convention. What's it like being in front of the convention, a national convention, to speak?" "You know, the—truth of the matter is, the larger the crowd it actually the easier it is to—to speak. But all I was thinking about was that 'Why didn't they nominate me?'" "That's so funny. Finally, we want to play a clip from Dan Glickman's time as Chairman and CEO of the Motion Picture Association of America. Here he is, December 1st, 2004, speaking to students at George Washington University." "I just wanted to say, nobody asked me my favorite movie, and I'm kind of nervous to tell you this, because it may speak to my intellectual acuity, but my favorite movie is 'Animal House'." "'Animal House', Secretary Glickman." "Yeah, you know, it's true, I've seen—I've seen that movie about 50 times maybe, and it reminded me of my fraternity at the University of Michigan where we there were no values, no—no barriers, and and in history. Fortunately none of those things came up during any of my confirmation hearings. But but you know, John Belushi and crew, it was such a great movie." "Wait, what year? You were at Michigan, what years?" "62 to 66." "The same year as Faber College in 'Animal House' would have been roughly the same year." "Roughly the same type Faber, I didn't know the Dean—Dean Wormer, that's right. But there were several characters in that book, and in my fraternity was—was truly an 'Animal House'. I wasn't like that. There were two of us in the fraternity that were kind of half-serious, but we obviously coped with the craziness. And we in fact I remember, they didn't have separate bedrooms in the fraternity, we all slept in one room, there were 70 beds. And there was—the windows were kept open because they didn't have heat up there, and we all had electric blankets. And the house ultimately burned down, and I remember my father came to visit me and he said, 'This isn't going to last, this this house is going to get demolished by fire,' and it did happen." "It strikes me, one way to problem have a problem with electric blanket burning is have a waterbed right next to it, for an emergency, is that..." "It could be, something, but we didn't—we didn't have that. Anyway, anyway, so no, that was certainly an interesting time of my life." "I want to—we're going to conclude shortly, and I want to talk about the book one more time and and all that. But your time at the Motion Picture Association of America, tell us a little bit about that and your son is a legend in Hollywood as well." "Well, I was you know the Motion Picture Association came up when Jack Valenti, who had been the head of it for years, decided to leave. And he knew me because I'd been on the House Judiciary Committee and was very involved with intellectual property issues and piracy issues. So then I had be—then I became obviously Secretary of Agriculture and I think he wanted when he decided to retire, he wanted somebody with some status, which I provided, and also somebody who knew something about film piracy and intellectual property issues. And my son had already become a producer and later is still a producer, my son's now the CEO of Miramax Studios in in Los Angeles, so he's been involved in the making of lots and lots of movies. And that was a very interesting job, particularly the part of the job I loved was I ran the rating system. So if a movie was going to be rated what, everything from G to—we didn't have X anymore, but NC-17—and everything in between. And I was very much involved in that system. It gave me a lot of power to be honest with you. And I liked that." "Okay, I tell us again about the book, how to get your book and where we can find, if people want to buy your wonderful book about jokes." "Well, they can always get it on Amazon or the University of Kansas Press, because I did come home to get it published. But but it's available on Amazon." "Oh, you went through an academic publisher for a joke book? Pardon?" "The University of Kansas Press, an academic publisher." "That hadn't even occurred to me, that's great. Okay." "You know, you you've elevated it." "You know, I was told if I had if to get to the New York Times, probably an academic publisher wasn't the way to go. But I said, 'I'm going to try anyway.' But in any event, so it's the book is out and it's done okay, and you know, it's like—it reads the way I would want it to read and I'm—it's all me in the book, and it's a lot about my family, and and it's a lot about comedy and how it's impacted my life and how it can impact other people's lives as well. So." "How many editions of the book have been printed?" "This is the second—second-two editions have been printed." "Okay, here's a joke that you as a guest and as a thank you to appearing, here's a joke all yours to use, you don't even have to cite me. Say the book is in its second printing, the first one got smeared. Do you want me to say that? Yeah, no, you could use that whenever you want." "The book is in its second printing, the first one got smeared." "Yeah, perfect, you nailed it. You nailed the ending on that. All right, all right, and now a bonus clip. Dan Glickman doesn't just tell funny jokes, he also sings funny songs, like the time he competed in the Funniest Celebrity in Washington contest, January 29th, 2003, and once again appearing at DC's premier comedy venue, the National Press Club." "Okay, since I left USDA I've given a lot of thought about what I want to do with my political future, and I decided instead of telling you jokes tonight, I'm going to sing you my life. And I hope there's nobody here from American Idol judging me tonight, but I would like the band to start in this a little rendition of what I want to be when I grow up. Two years ago this winter I left USDA. I didn't want to go but Bush told me I could not stay. He hired Veneman and then I really got annoyed. Golly goodness, I was unemployed. Dear Daniel R. Glickman, what are you to do? The Bushies are in power and there's nothing for you. Your next chance for comeback will be in '04, looks like you're going to have to go for Gore. Bet on Gore, bet on Gore, bet on Gore, Gore, Gore. You're going to have to bet on Gore." "You know, I was the—the first Jewish Agriculture Secretary and people ask me, 'What are your qualifications for this job?' and I said, 'Well, my mother would say: eat, eat, eat!'" "But Al Gore isn't running and Edwards is too new. Dick Gephardt lost the House and Joseph Lieberman's a Jew. John Kerry lacks charisma and I've never heard of Dean, how the heck are we supposed to win? Dear Daniel R. Glickman, don't be so forlorn. You have to get a job that doesn't legislate corn. You get or get busy, you better get fast. Check out the school in Cambridge, Mass." "Okay, with apologies to Stephen Sondheim. What was going through your mind, Secretary Glickman?" "I don't know, because I—that's I'm—thank God I've never heard that since that time. And I didn't win that year. I won at a later time when I was on again, but not singing, many years—future years. You must have—they must have not got the clip for that particular year." "I don't have a clip for what—what year do you remember what year?" "No, but it would have been in the last eight-ten years. But I did win that year and there was a little clip in the paper that I had won, you know so that made me feel good. But nobody picked up my singing voice. Actually, I sing better than I just heard, I want you to know that. And 'Officer Krupke', West Side Story, they have didn't come after me for copyright infringement, even though I'd been at the MPAA all those years. But a parody, a parody is often exempt from copyright, anyway, so." "Oh, I didn't know that." "Not always, it depends it depends on the nature of the song and where you do it." "I love how you maneuver between the—the legislature, the policymaker, and the comedy maker, just it's—you navigate that quite nicely." "Yeah, I'm very slick that way." "I I have to tell you, since we pulled the clip, I have not been able to get 'Officer Krupke' out of my mind, and it's just every morning I wake up and I sing to myself 'Officer Krupke' and I have to thank Secretary Dan Glickman for that. That's quite a milestone. By the way, you we've played three clips and we're almost over here from the National Press Club, you telling jokes. Are you going to create resonance at the National Press Club, like would that be your permanent venue to see the comedy stylings of Secretary Dan Glickman?" "You know, it's a lot easier than the—Hollywood Roosevelt Hotel at the Academy Awards, you know. So—so yeah, to the extent they want me, I'm available." "All right, well Secretary Glickman, thank you for joining us on the Extreme Mortman podcast. Hope you had a good time. Huge thank you for joining us. The book again, 'Laughing at Myself: My Education in Congress, on the Farm, and at the Movies'. And when you rush to order it right now on Amazon, make sure to tell them Extreme Mortman sent you. Any final words from you before we go to our closing music?" "I'm just thinking about my next few years and what I'm going to do to top what I've done in the past." "Fabulous, and now for our closing music, we return to 2003 and the Funniest Celebrity in Washington contest. Once again, sit back, relax, and enjoy the musical stylings of Daniel R. Glickman." "And then those crazy midterms provided me a plan. If Liddy Dole can do it then you bet Dan Glickman can. From Cabinet to Senate, it's the step that works for me. I'll be like Lamar from Tennessee. Yes, Daniel R. Glickman, I think this is best. Tell Summers that you're quitting and be like Cornel West. The Senate's your place, you belong on the floor. I don't think we're in Kansas anymore. Anymore, anymore, anymore, more, more. Yes, we're not in Kansas anymore."

AI dealmaking surges, from roll-ups to Anthropic's compute push — 5/7/2026

CNBC Television(3.4M)·56:56·6.6K views·2026-05-07
Alex TaubmanCEO, Long Lake Madhu NamburiManaging Director, General Catalyst
TL;DRThe AI sector has shifted from experimental technology to a "transformation economy" characterized by the rise of "AI roll-ups" taking legacy public companies private and a fundamental move toward autonomous "agentic engineering" where AI now writes 100% of its own code.

TL;DR

The AI sector has shifted from experimental technology to a "transformation economy" characterized by the rise of "AI roll-ups" taking legacy public companies private and a fundamental move toward autonomous "agentic engineering" where AI now writes 100% of its own code.

The Rise of the "AI Roll-Up" Strategy

Venture capital firms are moving beyond selling software to enterprises; they are now acquiring legacy service companies outright to implement AI-driven productivity gains internally.

  • The Long Lake-Amex GBT Deal: Long Lake, a three-year-old venture-backed company, agreed to take American Express Global Business Travel private for $6.3 billion at a 65% premium.
      • Strategy Focus: The goal is to apply AI to "non-tech" service industries (healthcare, insurance, property management) that have historically struggled to scale without massive headcount increases.
  • "Service as Software": Madhu Namburi (General Catalyst) describes a shift where high-retention service businesses are transformed by AI to drive operating leverage, allowing companies to grow revenue by 20%+ without adding staff.
  • Nexus Platform: Long Lake uses a proprietary AI platform to customize models for specialized workflows (e.g., HOA management), reportedly performing 5x better than off-the-shelf models.
  • Long-Term Outlook: Long Lake CEO Alex Taubman plans to "own and operate these businesses forever," suggesting that these AI-integrated service companies may eventually return to public markets as a new category of "Applied AI" stocks.

The Founder Landscape and the "AI Gap"

Morgan Stanley’s inaugural Founders Summit revealed a significant disconnect between the perceived importance of AI and the actual support available for its implementation.

  • The 23% Support Gap: While 95% of founders identify AI as critical to their business, only 23% feel supported in its deployment—the widest gap found in the bank’s research.
  • Operational Challenges: The struggle is no longer about the technology itself but "operationalizing" it—managing change, integrating it into workflows, and keeping up with the rapid pace of new model releases.
  • Liquidity Trends: Despite the abundance of private capital, two-thirds of founders still plan to IPO eventually. However, "secondary" paths (tenders and private share lending) have become mainstream; Morgan Stanley handled $20 billion in tender transactions last year alone.
  • Founder Performance: Research confirms that founder-led companies continue to outperform the broader market once public.

Industrial-Scale Compute and PE Partnerships

The demand for compute is currently exceeding even the most "extreme internal forecasts" at frontier labs, leading to massive new infrastructure deals and private equity vehicles.

  • SpaceX & Anthropic Deal: Anthropic announced a massive compute partnership with SpaceX to utilize the Colossus 1 data center in Memphis, bringing 220,000 Nvidia GPUs and 300 megawatts online this month.
  • Private Equity Shift: Traditional PE firms are now striking direct deals with AI labs to fund deployment:
      • OpenAI: Launched a $10 billion deployment vehicle with TPG, Brookfield, Advent, and Bain.
      • Anthropic: Formed a $1.5 billion joint venture with Blackstone, Hellman & Friedman, and Goldman Sachs.
  • Expanding Infrastructure: This is in addition to Anthropic's existing 5GW partnerships with Amazon and Google, and Microsoft’s $30 billion partnership with Nvidia.

From "Vibe Code" to Agentic Engineering

Anthropic's development team reports a "phase change" in how software is created, moving toward a world where humans manage autonomous agents.

  • 100% AI-Written Code: One year ago, 90-95% of Anthropic’s "Claude Code" was written by the model. Today, that figure is 100%. Engineers no longer write source code; they manage agents that manage other agents.
  • A "Printing Press Moment": Boris Cherny (Anthropic) compares the current moment to the invention of the printing press, predicting that the democratization of software creation will trigger a new industrial revolution.
  • Proactive Agents: New features like "Loops" (scheduling tasks) and "Routines" (event-based responses) allow AI to work autonomously behind the scenes without constant human prompting.
  • The Evolving Role of Engineers: The focus for human developers is shifting from syntax and execution to "judgment, taste, and deciding what is worth building."

Notable Quotes

"Software, SaaS, the definition was software as a service. I believe it's going to be service as software... the era has come and I think it's going to happen a lot more." — Madhu Namburi, General Catalyst
"We took growth from zero to 5 percent up to over 20 percent revenue growth. When you grow 20 percent... you need more people. Even with the productivity gain, we're creating a lot of jobs." — Alex Taubman, Long Lake
"To me this is like a printing press moment. Everyone is starting to become able to write software in a way that was not the case before... Without reading and writing the Renaissance could not have happened." — Boris Cherny, Anthropic
"The 95 percent point speaks to the consensus that the Fourth Industrial Revolution is here... But the 23 percent who are struggling with it? That's the teething process of moving from being awed by the technology to operationalizing it." — Mandell Crawley, Morgan Stanley
Hi everyone. No pullback for the AI trade and this week alone tells you why. There were major deals everywhere that you looked. A venture-backed roll-up taking a public company private, the two biggest AI labs both striking just enormous private equity partnerships on the same day, and then you had Anthropic announcing a massive new compute deal with SpaceX. It is Thursday, May 7th. I am live from Napa Valley at Morgan Stanley's inaugural Founders Summit. So let's get into it. Start with the deals. On Monday, a three-year-old venture-backed company called Long Lake agreed to take American Express Global Business Travel private for 6.3 billion dollars, a 60 percent premium. That is the second time in five months that a venture-backed roll-up has crossed into public markets. It's called an AI roll-up. If you haven't heard of it, you certainly will. The CEO of Long Lake, Alex Taubman, and General Catalyst's Madhu Namburi, they are on the stream today breaking down the strategy and talking next targets. Okay, so that is the new buyout opportunity. But traditional private equity is looking at AI transformation in a different way. They're doing deals directly with the AI labs. This week, OpenAI and Anthropic each announced major partnerships with private equity firms. OpenAI launched a 10 billion dollar deployment vehicle with TPG, Brookfield, Advent and Bain. Anthropic announced a one and a half billion dollar JV with Blackstone, Hellman & Friedman and Goldman. And then there's compute. We are always talking about compute. On Wednesday, Anthropic announced a deal with SpaceX to take over all the—to take over capacity at their Colossus 1 data center in Memphis. 220,000 Nvidia GPUs, 300 megawatts coming online this month. And that is on top of 5 gigawatts with Amazon, 5 gigawatts with Google and Broadcom, and a 30 billion dollar partnership with Microsoft and Nvidia. So the co-heads of Claude Code, this is now sort of the massive engine within Anthropic, Boris Cherny and Cat Wu, they also are going to be joining the live stream to talk about compute and how growth is running above their most extreme internal forecast. Which brings me to where I am right now, Morgan Stanley's inaugural Founders Summit in Napa Valley. This is the bank that sits in the middle of all of this. They led the hottest IPOs of the last few years, including Reddit, CoreWeave, and Figma. They're the front-runner on SpaceX, and this week they published research on the founders that are building this trade. Lots of really good stats in there. And joining me first to break all of that down—it's just out, by the way, so we get this exclusive look at this report—we've got Mandell Crawley, Chief Client Officer at Morgan Stanley, who is hosting this summit, which I have to say has been incredible. I mean, I feel like everywhere I walk, I'm running into founders that, you know, are behind some of the biggest companies, biggest deals. The research is really great too. So you're the exact right person to start with. Sure. Sure, Deirdre. First of all, you know, thank you so much for having me. And it is great of you to join us here at this conference. As you mentioned, it's the inaugural Founders Summit for Morgan Stanley. We've- we've long had this ambition at the firm to deliver our integrated platform to clients, and we want to do that as consistently and systematically as we can. And when you think about the clients that we serve, what better community than founders? I mean, they are tailor-made for the integrated firm in action. And what I mean by that is that it's a community—and you touched on this at the opening in terms of just they're builders. They're so critical to the economy. It's where job creation happens. It's where innovation ultimately lives. But it's also a community that has a unique set of needs. And we've got founders represented across each of the stages, from Series A to Series C plus. But they're all focused on growth. They're all thinking about capital, liquidity and the varying paths to liquidity. And of course, talent matters. And so when you put all of that together and I think about the platform that Morgan Stanley has to literally be their partner throughout their life cycle, whether that's in the corporate context, the founder context or the employee context. And so this conference is all about creating those connections. Right. I know we're focused on sort of the private founder here before you reach the public markets, but it's so interesting when you look at the stats, right, in public markets. It's founder-led companies that tend to outperform. So really interesting to, like, get them at this moment and see what's on their mind. I wonder why now? Why do your first one now? Is there something special or unique about this cohort of founders? And I know your research says that a lot of them are actually thinking about that path to being a public company. Well, and the why now question is—it's a- it's a fair one, but in many respects we've been doing this for a long time. As you know, we've been doing our TMT conference for over 30 years. We do a great Next Gen conference in Utah. So we've been in this ecosystem for a very long time. And you know our track record in the- in the capital markets space in terms of being on this journey with founders from the private space to the- to the- to the public market. But, you know, we wanted to be—just given the- the trends that we're seeing, whether it's the blurring of lines between private markets and public markets, companies are staying private for longer. When I think about things that are happening in the secondaries context which obviously has connectivity to this community and Morgan Stanley, we've been quite intentional about the secondary market. That- that was part of the industrial logic of our acquisition of Equidate. We built this massive workplace platform. So when you put all of that together, we felt like this community was worth having a summit devoted to it all on- unto itself. And I have to say, thank you for having me here because I know this is closed to press. All of this stuff is off-record. We're lucky enough to have you here to tell us what's going on. But you coincided it with this Founders report, which is really interesting. And by the way, this is Mandell's first live stream. So get your questions in and comments on real-time. We'll ask it to him, like, right here. We already have a few rolling in. But let me ask you first about the report. Is there anything that surprised you the most about it? There were a number of takeaways from the report and—and I don't know how much time we have, but—but there are a number of things I could touch on. I would say there are three- two or three, maybe three or four themes that jump out to me. Number one, which was somewhat obvious, they're all focused on- on growth. There's a great deal of urgency around, you know, growing their companies and making sure that—you know, they're growing revenue responsibly and frankly at all costs. Second was this notion of liquidity. And as we know, the path to liquidity has diversified. Historically, liquidity was essentially when you went to IPO. Now there are many other tools, right, for founders to access liquidity. The third thing that jumped out to me, which kind of gets back to this idea of partnerships and why networks are so important, was founders who have outperformed or reached, you know, breakout status. They were the ones to really talked about, you know, the competitive edge that comes by having high-quality networks and partnerships and what have you. And then the last was something that won't be a surprise to you, this idea that personal wealth and business success are like two parallel paths? They're one. It's one path, right? So those are the big themes that really jumped out to me. Okay, let me give you the thing that surprised me the most. Okay. Everyone's talking about AI here, right, obviously. That is the least surprising thing in the world. What is AI? Yeah, yeah, yeah. No, no. The thing that was surprising actually came from your report. Let me read this out. 95 percent of founders told you that AI is critical. Okay, like, obviously. Only 23 percent feel supported on it and that was the widest gap in the whole survey. Does that surprise you? It- it did. Especially because you have such a sort of cohort from here that lives in tech and I would have thought that maybe like especially the private founder sorted it out. Like where does that leave the public market ones? Well, here- here's how I think about that, right? The 95 percent point, like I think it just speaks to the consensus that the Fourth Industrial Revolution is here. The massive platform shift that is AI, I think everyone's a believer in that.
