So that I don't think is all that controversial. But the 23 percent who are struggling with it, that's not entirely surprising to me either. And the reason I say that is because now we've moved away from being awed by the technology and now it's operationalizing it. Like the change management that comes with it and now you're dealing with people, you're figuring out how to get it into your workflows, etc. And the word that we have been using is that there's this teething process that I think everybody is going through. But what I get excited about is a couple of things. One, at Morgan Stanley, again, we're doing a tremendous amount of work to operationalize this in the context of our client coverage teams. We're going to be able to do more, spending a lot more time on high-quality, high-value work with clients, move out some of the mundane tasks. So again, really excited about that. This idea that we can do more with our existing staff. And then you think about the abundance of the capital spend. We're in this massive CapEx cycle and as a financing matter, Morgan Stanley is in the middle of that. You know, the debt financing in particular where we're a market leader in that context. And then bringing it back to this conference and founders and what are we doing to help them address a bit of the disconnect that's implied in the question—that is a couple of things. One is convening forums like this where you get this community together to share ideas, best practices and frankly, oftentimes you get folks who are willing to actually do some business together. So that's a positive. But then at Morgan Stanley, we have this track record of really working with founders and getting them ready for enterprise. I guess it surprised me because when you look at like the public markets, right, the AI trade especially in the last few weeks has just been like up and to the right and there's been less talk about that ROI. So it's really interesting to hear from founders themselves and say like, we're not quite there yet. There's still this gap. We're all using it, but do we feel totally supported? Like not yet. How does that compare though? Because this is one slice of what you look after as Chief Client Officer. How does that compare to non-tech or non-founder clients? I think it's consistent. I think this is a ubiquitous feeling that most folks are having. Again, because it's like we know that this is going to be great and now how do we incorporate this technology responsibly into not just again, I talked about our client-facing businesses, but into our infrastructure stack, our control functions, and how do we do all of that stuff in a very responsible way. And so there's an- anxiety is probably not the right word but there's just a- and by the way, the other thing you have to remember is just the pace of change. That's the other thing that I think is driving a little bit of some of that sentiment that came through in the survey is just that just when you think you've got your arms around it, there's a new model, there's a new capability. And so I just think the pace and the expectations and the human aspect of the change management dynamic is the piece that creates a little bit of the complexity. Right. Let me get to a question from the live stream. This is from Carson, loyal viewer to us. Thank you, Carson. What are the ages of the founders of AI-oriented companies? I would think younger people would come up with the breakthrough new AI company business models. The demographic definitely skews a bit—a bit younger. But if you just were to do a quick demographic check of the 70, 65 or 70 founders that we have here, it's a little bit of all over; it's all of the above. So you have first-time founders, you've got folks who are on their third or fourth company. And frankly, that's the power of this new technology. Yeah, I actually was talking to a founder yesterday, was on his fourth company, was an AI company, did things that were totally different before that. Let's talk a little bit about IPOs. You said this at the beginning, Morgan Stanley has really been like a leading IPO banker in the valley for a long time on some of the hottest ones. How are founders feeling about that milestone? Because—this is kind of anecdotal, but I've been, you know, reporting in San Francisco on tech for 10 years and it feels like the IPO fell out of favor a little bit. You would hear often founders say, you know, why would I go public? There's liquidity and there's huge amounts of capital in the private markets. I'm going to be on a hamster wheel with quarterly reporting. Do you think that's changing? Look, I think that there continues to be a bit of this debate around private or public. I'll go back to the survey. About two-thirds of our founders still plan to have an IPO liquidity moment in their future. So I do- I do think a disproportionate percentage of founders still plan to ultimately go public over time and the discipline that ultimately comes with that. There- there are many who view that as a feature. But to your point about just the abundance of capital, there are still some folks who prefer to stay in the private context. I think the thing that we've been excited about is just the diversity of options that founders now have as relates to liquidity. Whether it's tenders, secondaries, which I talked about before, and then a relatively new feature in the lending context, you know, private share lending. As you know with tender offers, this gives your employees an opportunity to get access to liquidity without altering your IPO plans in a material way. Just give you a little bit of nuance here: last year we transacted 20 billion dollars of tender transactions at Morgan Stanley. This year we've already done as many, right, which just gives you a sense of the level of engagement that we're seeing from clients for that particular option. Secondaries, as I said earlier, I mean, it's a space that we're really bullish on. Again, a big part of why we bought Equidate. A third of our founders in the survey talked about having a secondary path, you know, in their not-too-distant future, but they still plan to go public over time. And then as I mentioned, you know, we've been doing some really cool co-creation with clients around—in the corporate and in the asset management space—around private share lending. One thing that I appreciated about this report is you asked founders about the macroeconomic environment and I don't think we talk enough about that here. It's almost like it doesn't exist when you're in this sort of AI bubble. And it was interesting, how much does that go into their decisions to raise- to raise capital, to fundraise? Look, I- I think that, you know, founders almost by definition, you know, they've got these big visions and they have this deep-seated belief that they can do the impossible. And they gotta convince people that they can ultimately achieve that. You know, these are the folks who ultimately are going to give them capital over time. But, yeah, the macroeconomic environment obviously matters. We've got a geopolitical landscape that's created a little bit of instability. But, you know, through the founders that we talk to, our clients, there is an underlying sentiment of being very constructive and they're seeing what's happening in the capital market, whether it's in the IPO context and the M&A context. I mean the vibrancy there I think is giving them the confidence that there's- there's- brighter days ahead. It's interesting. There's some founders that want to raise more because they're nervous about the macro. Or their investors are. Ali Ghodsi is one of them. He told us like a month or two ago that he raised more because he wasn't sure what was going to happen this year. And then some founders don't want to raise more than what they need. There's a whole—a whole spectrum. Let me ask you about this idea. We often talk about wartime and peacetime CEOs. And it's interesting applied to founders. Like, is a founder always wartime because it's do or die for them? Where- where are we right now in this moment, do you think? Well, look, I think depending on one's perspective, you can- you can take an overly optimistic view of the world or you can get pretty pessimistic. Again, depending on one's perspective. I think when you're a founder, and I think most founders will tell you and by the way, you go back generations, I think there's certain things that are just evergreen. The tension around growth, and growth continues to be the main thing. But then capital, and it's not just about, you know, raising capital at any price. It's who am I taking capital from? And what are the benefits, right, in the spirit of partnership—you know, can they deliver my company? I think the real point of tension is liquidity. Right? Because you have the tension around your early investors as well as your- as well as your talent, right? It becomes a—it's a really important part of the overall business strategy. And then—again, back to this idea of being—of partnerships and making sure that you're partnering with the right companies that are giving you the right advice as relates to who you should be raising capital from. A lot of talk about growth, capital. What about profitability? Where are we? Are we growth at all costs? Are founders concerned with profitability right now when you have an OpenAI and Anthropic burning billions? I- I think it varies by segment, but I think the bias continues to be top-line growth. Top-line growth. Yeah. Well, thank you for giving us some of your time and inviting us here. Will you do it again next year? Absolutely. I mean, just hearing from, you know, the founders who are here and—and by the way, they brought their partners with them. And the sentiment has been off the charts. And so like I said at the very beginning, this is a group that is tailor-made for Morgan Stanley, the integrated firm and—and seeing that in action. But I really appreciate you being here. And you'll do the report next year too so we can see how that sentiment is changing? Looking forward to that. Absolutely. Absolutely we will. And maybe we'll have some of these in the public markets if this IPO, if it continues. Mandell, thank you so much. Pleasure to chat with you. Thank you. Thanks a lot. Thank you. So Mandell just laid out the gap. 95 percent of founders say that AI is critical. Only 23 percent feel supported on it. So this is a deployment issue everyone's been trying to solve for years. So venture capital is what I want to get to next. Venture capital is doing something different. It's called the AI roll-up. Instead of selling AI to big companies, telling them how to use it or dropping in for a few months, they're buying the companies outright. So put your own engineers inside, run them with AI immediately. On Monday, this trend crossed into public markets for the second time in just a few months. Long Lake, a three-year-old venture-backed company probably haven't heard of, it agreed to take American Express Global Business Travel private for 6.3 billion dollars, a 60 percent premium. General Catalyst is backing them. Josh Kushner's Thrive running a similar playbook. You've got Lightspeed, Andreessen, Khosla, they're in this trend too. The targets are industries that tech has never really touched, like healthcare, insurance, property management, construction. Wall Street should definitely be paying attention to this because this is an emerging M&A theme. A new buyer, a new playbook and a new exit for public companies that cannot make the AI transition on their own or for a public market that may be a little less patient. I sat down this week with Alex Taubman, the CEO of Long Lake, and Madhu Namburi, the General Catalyst managing director leading this strategy. Here's what they told me. Alex, Madhu, thank you so much for joining me here on the set today. Madhu, let me start with you because the simple version of this story is that General Catalyst has built an AI roll-up strategy. Five months ago you took Jance Henderson public—private, I mean. Yesterday you announced GBT through Long Lake. So walk me through how GC ended up here. When did this become the strategy? General Catalyst has been doing this, actually, now three, four years. And it has only accelerated a lot more specifically with AI. Put in place a lot of tools. So we are at a point where we have- we have helped amazing companies like Long Lake who are becoming category definers in the- in the verticals they play and giving them all the tools needed to really become the big transformation play in the industry. When you think of venture capital though, you think about technology companies, earlier stage startups. Was it always the strategy to go after non-tech or did the AI age enable that? If I step back, services is a 20 trillion dollar market. One of the biggest issues in services was its inability to scale. Growth is slower, margins are lower because every time these services companies had to grow, they had to add a lot of bodies. And at the heart of it, AI is enabling a fundamental transformation. Where these services companies—by the way, they have amazing customer retention, amazing stickiness of the business—but now AI is enabling to grow these companies without adding more head count. Right. So does that mean that the head count stays the same? And I understand no one wants to say we're going to cut, but more CEOs publicly have been saying that like Marc Benioff, Andy Jassy. We saw Coinbase this week. Is- is that something that you, you know, someone like you doesn't want to say or is that just the reality of the work you're doing? No. I mean, look, we're- we're generally creating jobs. And so look, the simple anecdote is, if you have an HOA manager, for example, who's now three times as productive, you don't say—you want to have more of those people. They're basically generating more productivity for customers, for the company. And so we've actually been creating jobs in all these industries. And one of the things is as we acquire more businesses, we're actually scaling our distribution. That allows us to make our products and services even better. We've become the best place to work in all these industries and we're actually able to pay our people more. Madhu, zoom out then to this broader AI roll-up strategy. Do you see this ultimately as actually adding jobs even to these companies like AMX GBT, without going into specifics there? That these workforces actually become larger? So at the core of General Catalyst's strategy, exactly as Alex mentioned, we believe AI transformation has to drive an enhanced customer experience, which in our view will drive growth. And that's how we drive the operating leverage, as opposed to, hey, where do we take the heads out. I think that was the old-school way of thinking, but that's not how we are approaching. I mean, this is where we think these people will transform how business is delivered. And I think, you know, I use this word and it's been said before—software, SaaS, the definition was software as a service. I believe it's going to be service as software. And I think that's the era has come and I think it's going to happen lot more. It was so interesting that increasingly, and this is on the GC side, you're looking at public targets, the billions of dollars of value. What are you looking at, Alex, in terms of what you think is viable to sort of change, bring private—why do they need to be private to do so? Well, we think, first of all, we think every company in America needs an AI transformation strategy. And that's what we formed Long Lake three years ago with the ambition of being the premier partner. And whether that's a private company or a public company, we think every company is going to need a partner that looks somewhat like us or a strategy that looks somewhat like us to be—to sort of manage this next technology transformation. And in the 30 companies that we've bought so far, we've seen significant outsized growth, we've created a lot of jobs, our- satisfaction of customers, team members going way up. And so I think every company wants that, whether you're small, medium or large. If we can drive that kind of an outcome—I think—so I- I do think we're going to- you're going to see more of these larger deals happening with people that look like us and we certainly, you know, expect to be part of that. Are you looking at more public companies? You know, we're always staying flexible and if there's great companies that, you know, fit our strategy, you know, we'd- we'd like to partner with them. What's the profile of a company that fits your strategy? So we typically look for businesses like AMX GBT that have many years, in this case over 100 years of customer trust. So we're looking for established businesses that have a wonderful reputation with their customers and then we look at how can we take our AI platform—and we have a proprietary platform called Nexus—so we can deploy into the companies to deliver more value to the customers. Madhu, you were on the banking side for many years. What happens to an AMX GBT or another sort of legacy company that can't make that transition, you know, quick enough in public? What happens if they don't sort of take this route? We're seeing this right now overall. Right? There are a lot of amazing companies. Valuations have gotten hit hard because there's a lot of fear of the unknown. And- and it's very—investors usually do not take the risk, especially you don't know what the outcome will be in three, five years out and there's a lot of concern. However, if you peel back, there are companies like GBT that have done—that have an exceptional customer trust, exactly as Alex alluded to—has done an amazing job driving their technology transition. Could we now put an amazing platform like Long Lake on top of it and turbocharge with AI? I think that's the opportunity we see. Whether it's public or private, we're looking at across the board. We see there are some public companies which you can't make those hard transitions as a public because it does go through some volatility. And it's better to do that as a private company because then we like—we believe in growth, we want to drive growth, and it allows us much more flexibility to go invest in companies and play- you know- tools which you can't do as a public company. Where are you guys seeing the most value right now? Is it beaten-down public stocks or what does it look like in the private market? You know, for us it's- it's because we're- we're a company. We're going to- we plan to own and operate these businesses forever. So we don't- we're not that focused on value upfront. We think about what is the correct service lines for us to be in, where can we drive the most value for customers, where can we drive the most growth. Own and operate forever. So what does that mean for sort of the longer run? Does that mean that eventually a Long Lake would think about a public listing? I think so, yeah. I think probably Long Lake one day will be public if we execute well. And part of the thesis is here that we want to continue scaling. And we think the- the TAM here is really the entire economy. It's a 20 plus trillion dollar TAM. I think it's the biggest TAM; it's multiple times the size of the software TAM. And so in order to continue to scale and sort of meet the- the opportunity, we're going to need to have to continue having access to capital at attractive prices. And I joked with Madhu that my childhood dream was to one day be an investment grade bond issuer—but— We're going to do the IPO. Do better. Well, and okay, this is a perfect question to ask you, Madhu, because you were on the banking and the IPO side and M&A side for so long. What is the public comp for something like a Long Lake? Is it a private equity company? Is it sort of this new AI field? What do you think of this? So—great question. This is my view here's what's going to happen and there's a lot of focus on: are the IPO markets back, not back. If I step back and this is across the last decade, a third of the IPOs, give or take, were tech companies. Another third was, give or take, healthcare. And everybody else was the last third. Now within the tech, roughly, depending on the cycle, 50 to 80 percent were software. So here's what's going to happen in my view. I think the mix within tech will transform. You'll have some of the hardware, you know, applied AI companies actually will take a bigger portion. Labs? Well, I'm talking of the IPO. Okay. But the most fascinating thing, which in my view you will see, is the emergence of a whole category for IPOs which will come really from the third bucket. I think that's going to become very prominent. It may take some time. I probably think Long Lake is the biggest in applied AI and, you know, hopefully we'll get this IPO the first. But I think that's where you'll see emergence. By the way, they all will be much ahead of the rule of 40, by miles. So—so how do you value something like that? Is it tech or legacy? So I think you will value them like a growth company. That's why I used the word the rule of 40. Right? There will be some metrics, you know, do you have the same customer retention, are you able to—what is the use of AI? I think there's going to be new KPIs that will come together. I do think those companies will be valued by far at a premium because the risk profile on those companies is lot lower. You can actually model in what the outlook looks like five years out and you can have a predictability that these are high-growth, high-margin, defensible businesses with a specific moat. And I think Long Lake in my view, especially when we look at the portfolio of all the companies, will be absolute best-in-class. I know it's early, but what do you think that timeline looks like? You know, we'll- we'll see what the market conditions are, but we continue to see, you know, a lot of growth in our strategy and I think it could be in the next couple of years. Okay. Let's move on to sort of traditional private equity. You mentioned this, Madhu—yesterday Blackstone, Goldman, Hellman & Friedman put one and a half billion dollars into a JV with Anthropic. OpenAI announced its own version with Apollo. What do you think traditional PE is trying to do with these partnerships? I—as I mentioned, I do think all these companies have to embrace AI transformation. It's very easy to say, it's very hard to build that capability. And General Catalyst and Long Lake, we built that two, two and a half years ago and we have continued to perfect that. The difference though is that they already hold these companies. So it's a little bit more of playing defense, right? They're not actually acquiring new companies like Long Lake is. Why is your version, both of you, more effective? I think on the Long Lake side we're purpose-built. So we have a very different—we're a permanent owner so we can make these transformations. Some of these bets will take time to fully play out and I don't think a three to five year hold period of traditional private equity is optimized for that. Number one. And number two, you know, I think that being deeply AI-native in your founding team. So our team was, you know, the founding team which came together mostly through network is—was 75, 80 percent applied AI engineers from places like, you know, Palantir, Google, Robinhood, Ramp, places like that. And we think that sort of having that deep technical DNA when you're looking at businesses, when you're partnering with businesses, when you're building a plan for businesses, is just a real differentiator. But what's also very notable is that traditional PE, you know, the Thoma Bravos, the Vista Equities, have gone after software companies. You're not going after software companies. You're going after non-tech services companies. So, Madhu, like just talk a little bit about why that's the better bet right now and what happens to software. Is that a harder industry to turn around? I think these guys have done an exceptional job in the last decade plus to create a lot of value through software. I just—as I mentioned, we are in such a new era right now where the market is basically telling us that AI is going to—is an existential question for software now. So we at General Catalyst, we are looking at all the areas wherever we have a high conviction. I think Long Lake is our bet and it's an amazing—has worked out, fingers crossed, very, very well on the services which in our view is massive. As I said, it's much bigger than the entire software—multiple times bigger. We're also making a bet similarly in the healthcare. You know, we have- we have other sub-sectors. So our view is we're going to look at everything that has lot more potential to drive this transformation at a faster rate. We don't want to approach from a defensive standpoint. How do you pitch limited partners on this strategy because correct me if I'm wrong the returns in this kind of strategy look more like two or three times versus a venture strategy where you're betting on 10x from an outlier. We believe we have an unfair advantage given all the things I've mentioned. It should—our hope and target is to create that—a disproportionately more attractive returns. I'm not going to get into specific numbers. We think we have all the playbook. I mean even if we look at so General Catalyst was the first investor in when we led the first round Long Lake raised. And from that point versus what it's worth, it has been an absolute grand slam. I was going to play devil's advocate and ask you sort of what makes you good at this because the private equity firms, Vista, Thoma Bravo, they spent 30 years building operating teams before they actually ran take privates. You're three years old, right? First of all, everything moves faster with AI. So three years in AI is—is—is actually like three decades of pre-AI. But I think to be successful at this strategy you need three things. You need number one, a world-class applied AI engineering team which Long Lake was founded with and continues to accelerate. Two, a world-class change management function and three world-class M&A. And so Long Lake was purpose-built with that in mind and you know, if you're a, you know, let's say HOA management company and you- and you partner with Long Lake and all of a sudden you now have access to this extraordinary team of applied AI engineers. That are going to live in your offices for the next, you know, couple of years helping solve all your biggest pain points and helping you grow and we have this change management operation that helps drive a lot of growth. So I'd like to highlight, I'm very proud of what we've done is that for the companies that we've deployed Nexus into, our AI platform, we've taken growth from zero to 5 percent up to over 20 percent revenue growth. And so when you talk about creating jobs and this is—I know this is different from what's being talked about by a lot of people—but we're seeing it in the real world. When you grow 20 percent and you drive that kind of growth and your customers are asking for more services from you, you need more people. And so even with the productivity gain, we're adding a—we're creating a lot of jobs. And so I actually think that this is going to be much more positive for the economy than anybody realizes. Are those the kind of jobs people want? Services, though. Oh yeah, absolutely. Well first of all, they're much better jobs than they ever were before. Because if you were spending 40 hours a month copying data from one software system into another or taking 13 different software systems and taking the data and putting it into a PowerPoint presentation, now our AI assistant can do most of that for you. And you can spend the rest of your time, which is what you want to do, on-site with the customers or going in winning new customers or thinking of different creative ways to solve problems. You see the, you know, hundreds of billions of CapEx that the labs are investing and that is what's enabling all of this. But somebody's got to take that hundreds of billions of investment and turn it into GDP growth. And so what Long Lake was formed to do is to actually convert that investment into the GDP growth. And that's what we do and it's hard work. It's—it's—it's being all around the country, spending time with the team members, solving problems. Isn't that what OpenAI and Anthropic are supposed to be doing on their own? I mean, if we're in this AI era, do we need sort of a middleman like a Long Lake and when I look at the traditional PE model how they're just partnering directly with an Anthropic or OpenAI, what makes your proprietary platform better than just plugging in Claude and getting Anthropic? Yeah, it's—it's a great question and I think the big difference is each of these industries has very specialized workflows and data sources that are highly specialized. So the HOA management for example is a—is a relatively niche—it's actually a big business it's going to be a multi-hundred billion dollar TAM—but it's a relatively niche use case that the model companies are not optimizing for. And so we sort of take the best-in-class models we harness them in our platform, we give them specific specialized workflows and skills and tools that are heavily customized for these end markets and it drives much better performance in the field. So what we're actually seeing in our custom eval set that we create for these industries, our Nexus platform is actually performing five times better than the off-the-shelf models. Interesting. Well looking forward to hopefully getting more of that data as you guys sort of move towards maybe a public listing in the future. Wonderful to talk to both of you, Alex, Madhu, thanks very much for coming. Thank you. Thanks for having us. Thank you. So none of this works without the models underneath and Anthropic has had one of the most remarkable years in AI in tech as a whole, really. Claude Code, their developer product, it has been what is powering a lot of this transformation and the product itself is now doing something wild. A year ago, Anthropic said that 90 to 95 percent of Claude Code was written by Claude. Today that number is 100 percent. So that means the engineers have stopped writing actual code. Instead they're prompting agents that write code and now agents that manage other agents. I sat down with Boris Cherny and Cat Wu, the two co-leads of Claude Code earlier this week at Anthropic's Developer Day. We got into the SpaceX compute deal, what it means for enterprises trying to catch up, and why they think we're living through a printing press moment for software. Here is that conversation. First, Boris and Cat, thank you so much for making the time on this Developer Day. I know it's really busy, great atmosphere outside. Got music, lots of people around, lots of excitement. I have to start with the news though, because this morning, I mean really just an hour ago, you guys Anthropic announced the SpaceX deal. More than 200,000 GPUs coming online this month, and in response to that you guys have doubled Claude Code rate limits, removed peak hour throttling, raising API limits for Opus models. What changed? Our- our growth has just been explosive. Like—it- it's just so much more than even what we predicted. It was even more than our most extreme forecast. What- what happens is every time that we release a model, growth inflects and it inflected with Opus 4 back in May of last year, 4.5 November of last year, 4.6 in February and now 4.7. So it just like keeps inflecting. And the hard thing with financial forecasting is: how do you plan for this? Right. And so now—it's actually very—very difficult to plan for and we want to be responsible in the way that we plan for it and the thing that we're excited for now is—we have the compute to support all this load and there's going to be even more compute coming online to support all the upcoming load. So Cat, were you constrained before and were there things that you wanted to do that this will now give you the ability to do at Claude Code? I think the main thing that this gives us is builders can just do a lot more with our products and it just means that, you know, as much as there's been just so much demand and this just means that we can give back a bit more. Right. We gotta talk about the Elon Musk of it all because in the past he has called Anthropic "woke AI", "misanthropic". Was there any weirdness on the team when the deal closed and I also want to note, and I know you guys this just happened, so I'll read you a tweet from him, he just put on X: "By way of background for those who care, I spent a lot of time last week with senior members of the Anthropic team to understand what they do to ensure Claude is good for humanity and was impressed. Everyone I met was highly competent and cared a great deal about doing the right thing. No one set off my evil detector, so as long as they engage in critical self-examination, Claude will probably be good." We care so deeply about the mission of safety—and it feels like Elon is very aligned with us and it's just—it's—it's exciting I think from that point of view. It's just from this kind of like deepest belief. Now with this deal, as you guys have been saying, you have a lot more capacity to let developers, enterprises do exactly what they need to do. OpenAI has sort of been going the other way and you talked Boris about being responsible. They've been burning, you know, billions of dollars to essentially subsidize Codex, right? You guys have held the line this year. Now you've got more compute. So does that discipline hold or do you start spending more because you see this maybe as like a critical moment? For us the focus has always been the same. It's always been enterprises and developers and coding and that's not changing. We have always been focused on the long term. We want to continue to be able to do this responsibly in the long term to serve our customers so they can use our products and they can build on our platform and this isn't changing. Did you see anything in the data that made this more urgent? The idea to bring on more compute online? Do you think you would make more deals? Is it sort of that like unlimited space you just want as much as possible? It's very clear that we're on this exponential of growth and so every month there's just far more users than they were the previous month. And so it's definitely critical for us to continue growing our compute to—to make sure that we serve everyone well. And I think it's especially important for us to make sure that we keep serving our existing users like the people who've been with us from the very beginning. And so I think what this compute lets us do is bring on all these new users while keeping the service the same or better for everyone. Did you understand what you had on your hands when you shipped it? Did you think it could have the impact that it has had on entire industries? Claude Code originally was actually not a hit. Right, you weren't even sure if you wanted to ship it, right? We weren't sure. Yeah, it's like this is the journey of building—like—we knew we wanted to build something in coding but the journey of building a product is sort of—it feels like wandering in the desert for a little bit. It's actually like a very lonely journey. And the compass is just talking to users and seeing what they want. And so at the beginning Claude Code was not a hit, even though at Anthropic everyone was using it, we didn't know if people outside of Anthropic would use it. And what happened is at some point we released Opus 4 and it just clicked and it started to work in a way that it didn't and it's just been working more and more and more. It just keeps inflecting. But I think this was like the result of many months of deep belief that there was something amazing and that we just had to improve the product enough and improve the model enough that the rest of the world could intuitively grasp it. And Cat actually understood this really early on. Like—at the beginning it was just the first like couple months it was just me building it. And Cat's a product manager. And, you know, she codes like on—on—on the side. But immediately she started using this product and just sending me feedback like every single day. Like, this is broken, this is broken, this is broken. And then I would just like go in and fix it and as the team grew this just continues to be the way that everyone operates. We love hearing feedback, we love fixing stuff. Nothing is like, you know, we have some good ideas but really it's our users that figure everything out and they're the ones that have the best ideas. But when I talk to people outside of the Bay Area or outside of tech, it still feels like they don't really get it. There's a big group that still doesn't get it and when you talk about the mainstream public, right, AI is still a chatbot, less sort of an agent that can do things on your behalf. How are you seeing that adoption? Some of the things that we're doing are we're sharing more of the use cases that people find successful. So we're going to like the biggest adopters within sales and marketing and legal and interviewing them and saying, hey, what is the killer use case that you use CoWork for every day? Documenting that and then sharing it with the world. Because I think for a lot of people they enter CoWork and they're like, oh, it's a blank slate and like you're saying I don't understand what it can do, what it can't do. And so seeing someone in their role having a workflow that works and being able to copy it I think will be a big unlock. I also think that just as models get better, your success rate on your first task goes up. And I think that's a pretty big indicator for whether you stick around or not. It's so interesting, Cat, first so quickly that you mentioned sales, marketing and legal because I think for many people it still feels like this thing for software engineers but it's not, right? You're going into non-coding. Yeah, CoWork is the thing that's not for software engineers. I—I mean I'm an engineer but I use CoWork all the time. I think there's this magical moment when people realize I can actually combine it. So CoWork can use multiple tools together and this is just where it gets so powerful. For example, I have CoWork looking through a spreadsheet, it's going to look at kind of all of the tasks that need to get done and then, you know, we track all of our team's work in a spreadsheet still. And CoWork can look at that spreadsheet and then it can message people in chat to be like, you know, like, hey, like, is this on track, is this on track? So it can combine multiple tools in this way and, you know, we use it like this all the time. Right. So I think the main thing is just like dive in and try it and most important actually don't be afraid to ask CoWork how do you use CoWork because it can totally tell you. Claude and Excel was the huge unlock for me to be able to use Excel in that way that I haven't in the past. You said that it's extremely powerful. Yes, I think that much is clear this year. And I think that's maybe some of the hesitation on the enterprise side and connecting it with your email and with your calendar. Even something that I'm, you know, I allow it access to a lot but it's still sort of in the back of my mind you want to be really careful with that. What do you say to companies that are nervous and don't want—especially like their employees—don't want their employees to run off and do this on their own and just people who are worried about this? I think there is a bit of an onboarding process. So when you first enter CoWork and you ask the first prompt and you connect Google Calendar, Gmail, etc. or your other apps, CoWork will actually always ask permission before doing something. And so—like—we see this as an important way to make sure that the user is always in control and is able to affirmatively say yes. And then this is also a good way for the user to build intuition for what the agent is really good at and where it might struggle. But that can really slow you down, right? Like let's say you're asking CoWork to complete a browser task and you have to say allow every single time, and I like how it's like "dangerously allow every single time". You can click on that. So how do you balance speed with safety? I think you just build confidence over time. You know, for- for us as a company safety is just by far the most important thing. And so when we release something we need it to be safe by default. That's just extremely important. And so I think this also really depends on the model. If you use like an older model- and Cat was talking about this in your keynote, I thought that was like really good. If you used the model like a year ago it would do a lot of stuff wrong because it just- like- it wasn't very good yet. But now if you use it today it just does the right thing. And so there's this kind of interaction, then maybe like human-computer design problem where you want people to build trust and - you know, as the person using it, you have to kind of learn the boundary of like what can this do. And the hardest part is it changes with every model. You build a mental model of here's what, you know, Opus 4.6 can do and then 4.7 comes out it is so much more capable and you have to kind of relearn what I can safely task it with. Totally. How do you explain that to someone who you know- a- a CFO or a CEO who you know doesn't know exactly how quickly these tools are moving and advancing? I think the most powerful thing we've seen is just try it and get your team in a room and get them to try it. So as a CEO, as a CFO, it is your job to learn to use this tool and then it's your job to teach your team so that you can reorganize all your business processes around AI. And there was this- there was this interesting thing that happened in the 90s. Computers started to become a thing in the workplace and for many years there were not productivity improvements. And this is just very surprising. And then- - the Harvard Business Review, sometime in the early 90s they published this article about how in order to benefit from the productivity impact of computers what you have to do is you have to reorganize all your business processes around computers. Computers have to be at the center. They can't be on the periphery somewhere like- you can't have like filing drawers and- you know- like desk drawers and things like that and then a computer is in the corner, that's not how it works. The computer has to be in the center. And I think this is the thing that people are realizing today. It's exactly the same thing with AI. AI has to be at the center. Right and that sort of the numbers speak for themselves. - you guys said I think a year ago that 90 to 95 percent of Claude Code was written by Claude. What is that number today? 100 percent. 100 percent. So what does your jobs look like? We're prompting it, we're- I think there is a lot more value in like deciding the right thing to build because now that building things is so easy, the judgment of what is worth building, what is an amazing user experience, that part is actually much harder. Do you need software engineers anymore or do you need prompters and project managers? Software engineering has always changed. My- so my grandfather used to program and he programmed in punch cards. And back in the day this is what programming looked like. It was the- it was this paper- piece of paper and- - you put- you put holes in it and this is how you write a program. And then at some point in the 50s this started to become software and people realized actually it doesn't have to be on paper, software can be a thing and that was the thing for like, you know, 70 years. People wrote code as- as software and as an engineer I would write the source code. It's like editing a document. That's what it is. When we launched Claude Code this changed again. And so now the work is to manage an agent and now it's actually changing again. Now I manage an agent that manages agents. And this is a shift that happened with Opus 4.7 and I think is going to accelerate. And I gotta say for me as an engineer, I've just- I've never had this much fun. Like I feel like all of the work that I didn't want to do, the agent can do, and so I can think about the problems that are actually interesting. And, you know, before with the first round of- you know- Claude Code and agents, my productivity went up maybe like 10x and now that agents are managing agents and I'm using routines and loops, it's going up like another 10x. Like- this is a second phase change that we're seeing. One of the talking points this year it feels like, okay, agents are great but they don't have judgment or they don't have taste. And I wonder if you think that that is increasingly like less true and also Boris has Claude ever pushed back on something that you wanted to ship? Did it change your trajectory? Claude actually pushes back fairly often and 4.7 is quite good at this. So- this happened actually like yesterday. I wanted to build something a particular way and Claude told me no, I think that's actually not a good idea and here's the thing to do instead and it was right. And a year ago if it had done this, it probably would not have been right. But, you know, now- now it is. And I- I think we have this tendency to think about well, you know, here's this thing the agent can't do yet and this must be like a thing it can ever do. But actually what's happening is the rate of progress is so quick that something like taste, I think the agent will be able to do quite soon and Claude's sense of taste is improving and I would expect that to continue to improve. Judgment too? Yeah and I think you can see this both in the quality of the designs that it produces so Claude is known to make beautiful front-end apps but if you look over time like the apps have become so much nicer between Sonnet 3.5 and Opus 4.7. And then we also see this with code quality. Like in the past people were like, oh, yeah, like the agents just- like- can't write high-quality code and in the past yes you did have to hand-hold them a lot and give them very specific detailed guidance. But- but now you can actually get there with like a really good Claude MD and memory system and a really good prompt. Yeah we've been trying to find like a- like a new- - word for this because like vibe code just doesn't do it justice anymore. Because now it is- it's just writing the code and the- what- what changed like maybe six months ago- It's not a vibe anymore, is that what you mean? It's not it- it's just better code than what I would have written. It's production code. It's production code. It's like the agent is doing the code. Maybe it should be like agentic engineering. Like there's gotta be some better word to talk about what's happening. Right. We gotta think about that one. Needs to be like buzzy and understandable. Agentic engineering may not fly so well in the media or in the mainstream or on Wall Street for that matter. To me this is like a- to me this is like a printing press moment. Like everyone is starting to become able to write software in a way that was not the case before. And- you know, this was the case for literacy, you know, like 500 years ago. And because everyone can read and write it makes it possible to have the modern world. Without reading and writing the Renaissance could not have happened, the industrial revolution could not have happened. It just opens up the possibility for everyone so much and I- I think this is what we're about to start to see. Like- the amount of innovation that's going to come out of this is just tremendous and it's very difficult to imagine right now. And the fear that comes along with it though too, right, as we go through this like basically huge enormous transition. Fear of displacement. Last question for you guys because we're almost out of time. What is the one thing that you've shipped this year- - that you're going to be proudest of in 10 years and then the thing that you might be most haunted by in 10 years? Cat, maybe first. Ooh. I think we might have the same answer for the excited one. Yeah, yeah, yeah. Does it start with an R? Oh, I was going to say loops, but yeah. Oh yeah, I was going to say routines, yes. - no, I- I think the direction that we're moving in with Claude is that it should be able to do more work for you behind the scenes. You should be able to manage a lot in parallel and it should be proactive. And loops and routines is our first foray into that. So loops let you run things on a schedule and routines let you run things in response to events. And it's cool because in the past you always had to remember to open Claude and you had to write out the task. And now with these you don't have to anymore. That's such a surprising answer to me because it seems so basic given everything that you guys have done this year. Like what is so- what's going to be so lasting about that in 10 years? It's- it's just so powerful. It's like agent- the- the coding agent a year and a half ago went up a layer of abstraction and that's what let me be 10 times more productive. Routines go up another layer of abstraction. It's an agent that prompts Claude. So it's- I don't write the prompt anymore, Claude writes the prompt and now I'm talking to that new Claude that's that's kind of coordinating. It's incredibly powerful. And you're both agreed on that. That's funny. Okay. The thing that you maybe most worried about or most haunted by 10 years from now. I don't know if like haunted is the right word, but- you know, we are always trying to figure out the future. - we're always trying to figure out what people want and I think a lot of parts of the product we're going to unship and we're going to delete as we kind of figure this out. This is the way that we've always done it and, you know, when I was at Instagram this was part of the design philosophy. You want to keep the product simple so it's like nice to use. You don't want to have a thousand features. And so, you know, for us we're just going to keep, you know, deleting things hopefully at the same rate at which we build new stuff. So important to delete things as you go along. Really important, just to keep it simple. Yeah. Right. Thank you both so much for the time. Really appreciate it. Thanks for having us. Thank you. And thank you everyone out there who was watching us. We'll be back again next week. It's been a pleasure to be in Napa. Thank you to Jasmine, Denise, Sammy, Jacob, Lupe, Carrie, behind the cameras for making this possible, and have a great Friday weekend. We'll see you next week.

Brookfield's Bruce Flatt discusses the global markets at the Milken Institute Global Conference

Brookfield(12.1K)·38:36·7.8K views·2026-05-07
Bruce FlattCEO, Brookfield Corporation Michael MilkenChairman, Milken Institute Larry FinkCEO, BlackRock
TL;DRThe global economy is entering a $10 trillion "rewiring" phase driven by AI and digital infrastructure needs, shifting the investment landscape from traditional assets to long-term "continuity" assets managed by private capital firms rather than debt-constrained governments.

TL;DR

The global economy is entering a $10 trillion "rewiring" phase driven by AI and digital infrastructure needs, shifting the investment landscape from traditional assets to long-term "continuity" assets managed by private capital firms rather than debt-constrained governments.

The $10 Trillion Infrastructure "Rewiring"

  • Scale of Investment: Bruce Flatt (CEO, Brookfield) estimates a $10 trillion global expenditure over the next decade to build out power grids, AI factories, data centers, and fiber networks.
  • Infrastructure Pivot: The global economy is transitioning from legacy networks (highways and railways) to a digital backbone. 50% of the assets Brookfield owns today did not exist as investable asset classes 15 years ago.
  • Supply Shortages vs. AI Bubble: Larry Fink (CEO, BlackRock) argues that we are in a supply shortage rather than a bubble. The U.S. is currently short on power, compute capacity, chips, and memory.
  • New Security Paradigms: The rise of drone warfare is necessitating a reevaluation of physical security for infrastructure, potentially moving critical $50B–$75B data center investments underground to protect against domestic and international threats.

Stability and the Private Capital Advantage

  • Asset-Liability Matching: Michael Milken highlighted that recent bank failures (Silicon Valley Bank, First Republic) resulted from mismatched liabilities. In contrast, both Brookfield and BlackRock report that less than 1% of their managed assets are redeemable on a quarterly basis.
  • The Role of Private Markets: With governments facing mounting deficits, the private sector is now the primary engine for massive infrastructure builds. Long-term private capital acts as a stable intermediary that the two-year capital structure of the banking system cannot support.
  • Solution-Based Investing: Large firms are moving away from selling "products" and toward "solutions." This includes bespoke vehicles, such as one mentioned by Flatt that locks in capital for 27 years to match long-tail institutional liabilities.

Global Market Democratization and Retirement

  • Shift to Self-Directed Retirement: BlackRock is working with countries like Saudi Arabia and India to move away from government-sponsored pensions toward personal, defined-contribution plans.
  • The Japanese Catalyst: Fink noted that the Japanese stock market's recent surge was directly tied to the doubling of tax exemptions for "NISA" (self-directed retirement accounts), proving that domestic capital participation drives market valuation.
  • Wages vs. Capital: Fink warns that in an AI-driven world, wages will not keep pace with the growth of capital. Broad economic success now requires getting individual citizens to invest in capital markets rather than keeping money in low-interest bank accounts.

The Strategy of Long-Term Compounding

  • The "Miracle" of 12%: Bruce Flatt emphasized that consistent 12% compounding over long periods is more valuable than chasing volatile 35% returns. The goal is to own "great businesses" for decades rather than churning assets.
  • Consolidation of Winners: The panel predicted a "K-shaped" economy where one or two winners dominate each industry due to the massive CAPEX required to integrate AI.
  • Institutional Scale: The era of $100 billion funds is ending; the market is moving toward a landscape of 30+ funds managing over $1 trillion each. These "mega-funds" require "continuity assets" they can hold indefinitely to avoid the friction of constant asset churning.

Notable Quotes

"We are rewiring the global economy to lay the networks that we laid before, which were highways, utilities, railways. And now we're laying cloud, artificial intelligence factories, and data centers." — Bruce Flatt, CEO of Brookfield Corporation
"There is not an AI bubble. There is the opposite. We have supply shortages. Demand is growing much faster than anyone had ever anticipated." — Larry Fink, CEO of BlackRock
"Having your money in a bank account is one of the worst financial decisions of a lifetime... we are not going to be able to broaden economic success only by wages because wages in this AI world are not going to grow as fast as the potential of the AI growth." — Larry Fink, CEO of BlackRock
"The greatest miracle of investing, finance, in fact, almost everything, is compounding... if you can earn north of 12%, nothing else matters. Don't try to earn 35%." — Bruce Flatt, CEO of Brookfield Corporation
I did not have any financial education as a kid coming up. I didn't get my first bank account until I was probably 18 years old and I was getting ready to go off to college. Having an understanding of finance and having an understanding of how money works and having an understanding of how you can build wealth is super important, and the earlier you start, the better. So, my kids had bank accounts at seven. As soon as they could walk into the bank and write their name onto some paper, we opened some joint accounts and they had debit cards. They had to learn how to keep a register, even though kids don't do that today. But I made them learn that you're going to keep track of this money. You're going to understand how you can earn more with interest. Just understanding how finance works is so important to how far you go. Good morning, everyone, and welcome back to day two. If yesterday's content reminded us of anything, it's this: the people in this room do not observe history, they shape it. And there's a great deal of history to be shaped right now. We gather this week under the theme "Leading in a New Era." I want to sit with that word "new" for just a moment, because there's nothing comfortable about the era we've entered. Geopolitical alignments that held for generations are fracturing. Trade is being rewired. Artificial intelligence is not approaching; it has arrived, and it is already reshaping who works, what gets built, and who benefits. Capital is moving with a speed and scale that can either concentrate wealth or distribute it, depending on the choices leaders like the ones in this room decide to make. That's not a crisis brief; it's an opportunity statement, and it's precisely why we're here. The Milken Institute was built on a conviction that is more urgent today than at any point in our history: that the right ideas, connected to the right capital, in the hands of the right people, can solve problems that governments and markets cannot solve alone. We are a non-profit, non-partisan think tank, but we have never been passive. We bring together the best minds in finance, health, technology, policy, and philanthropy because no single sector has the full answer. The most durable progress has always come at that intersection, and this conference, 29 years in, remains that intersection. This week, more than a thousand speakers and thousands of participants from nearly 80 countries are joining us. They're here because they believe, as we do, that dialogue across difference, capital allocation guided by purpose, and bold thinking on the hardest problems is not optional in this era. It's the whole ballgame. There's no better way to open day two and, frankly, no better frame for everything I just described than the conversation you're about to witness. Bruce Flatt is CEO of Brookfield Corporation, one of the world's largest owners and operators of real assets, with operations spanning renewable power, infrastructure, real estate, and private equity across more than 30 countries. Larry Fink leads BlackRock, the world's largest asset manager. He's spent decades at the intersection of capital markets, policy, and global growth, and his annual letters have become required reading for anyone who cares about where the world is heading. Together, Bruce and Larry represent a combined stewardship of well over $15 trillion in capital. What they choose to invest in and how will help determine the physical and financial architecture of the next decade. Moderating this special conversation is someone who needs no introduction in this room. So, I'll leave you with this: the world is watching how leaders respond to this era. The decisions made in rooms like this one about where capital flows, how technology is deployed, and who gets access to opportunity will reverberate for decades. That's why we're here and that's what we do. Thanks, everyone, and enjoy the day. Please welcome a conversation with BlackRock CEO Larry Fink and Brookfield Corporation CEO Bruce Flatt, moderated by Michael Milken, chairman of the Milken Institute. So, we have two organizations here that have been on the forefront of investment and infrastructure that the world is deploying for a number of years and are probably the two largest investors today in infrastructure. And one of the things I wanted to touch base on is that many of the financial problems that we've seen over the years have been related to a couple of areas. One is the mismatch of liabilities and assets in terms of the dramatic changes in interest rates and what occurred. We lost two great franchises, Silicon Valley Bank and Republic Bank, because they chose to make intermediate investments—seven-year debt in US governments or high-grade mortgages—and borrowed overnight. What looked like a positive spread at 3% or 2 and 3/4, when they were paying 5.5% overnight for money, resulted in both of them not being independent companies today. But both Bruce and Larry have seen this occur time and time again over the last 30 years. And so, I just wanted to start by giving you a feeling of the strength of these organizations. When we talk about the risk of redemptions relative to the size of their organizations, Bruce, let's start with you. What percent of your assets that you manage are redeemable on a quarterly basis? Probably less than 1%. Okay. So, everything you've read about this year regarding private credit and redemptions, it is less than 1% of assets. And Larry, what do you think? It's certainly less than 1%. Yeah, a fraction. And so, we've defined an issue: why did this occur? You could ask yourself, both of these organizations were built to identify long-term investors who could make long-term investments, with themselves serving as a mediator. We're well aware that the banking system and their capital structure is probably two years at best. And so, the challenges they have, these leaders have met the needs to help build the world, the US, and our economy by matching and being the intermediary between people who have long-term assets and people who need long-term investors. So, Bruce, you're one of the couple largest institutional investors in the world, and the largest clean energy investor in the world. Both are fundamental elements in the rollout of AI. Is the opportunity to supply AI infrastructure as exciting as Brookfield has told us? So, I think if you step back, Mike, and there's a lot going on in this, it's exciting. But what's most important is that really what's happening is that we have been and we still are, and for the next 10 years we will be, rewiring the global economy to lay the networks that we laid before, which were highways, utilities, and railways. Now we're laying cloud, artificial intelligence factories, and data centers. And that, on the fundamental basis, that's what's happening. So, there will be $10 trillion from power, AI factories, data centers, and fiber build-out, laid to basically rewire the world for the new economy that's coming. It's been slowly coming. Like, it literally... you end up having things happen and you think they're too much. But we're just rewiring the world, and that's probably the most fundamental thing that's going on in the world. It's going to be the most important thing for the next 10 years, and maybe it's 15, and maybe it's 20. But it's an enormous amount of capital going into these things. So, Larry, we're looking at the multi-trillion dollar build-out across energy, AI, transportation, all of these elements. What are the real constraints on getting capital deployed? Is it capital itself, labor, regulation, or something else? Well, Mike, it's good to be here and I want to thank everybody. It took us 29 years to get you here. We went to the same high school. It's never too late, right? We're both young. Right. Let me just start off with the conclusion. The United States is short power, we're short compute, we're short chips, and there are going to be shortages in all three. And memory—four things. I actually believe a new asset class will be buying futures of compute. We just don't have enough compute power right now. And you think about how many tokens you need to analyze, let's say, your cybersecurity. It is enormous. And we're reading all about the whole issues that's going on. That's just the huge opportunity, the amount of money that needs to be going there so we try to eliminate some of these shortages. But I don't believe we're moving fast enough. There is not an AI bubble. There is the opposite. We have supply shortages. Demand is growing much faster than anyone had ever anticipated. This is just a US phenomenon. We have not begun the whole concept of exploring the opportunities of AI around the world. And there's going to be some huge geopolitical questions as to who gets that technology. And so, you know, the future is today. The AI phenomenon is happening faster than any of the programmers and the designers of this. The speed of intelligence is growing; the opportunity is very large. Yes, it's going to be disruptive, but it is also going to be very opportunistic. And we have to talk about it. And I believe as a country, as a world, we're not spending enough time working with government alongside the private sector to try to solve these very big issues. There was a slide of all the different CAPEX a second ago related to the railroads and all the other issues. The capital expenditure for this is, as Bruce suggested, $10 trillion. That's just for the US. And let me also, though, bring back the whole notion of what's going on in the Middle East, where there's going to be huge opportunities there. Because of drone warfare, we have to relook at all forms of security and the need for rebuilding the GCC region. We need to rebuild it so that it's going to be in a position where it's not going to be obstructed by drone warfare. So, a lot... many more things are going to have to be underground. Even here in the United States, if we're going to be building, let's say, these one-gigawatt data centers, how do we make sure we're protecting those $50 billion, $75 billion investments? We have to relook at everything because of the role of drone warfare. Right now we're looking at it internationally but, you know, one of my concerns is it could be a domestic terrorism using a $3,000 drone. So, all of these things are actually opportunities, not problems. It's going to require, as we said, trillions of dollars of capital. Governments cannot build these out alone. They don't have the wherewithal and, two, their deficits are starting to be mounting, and we spend too little time talking about the US deficits and other countries' deficits. This is going to all be the role of the private sector. And Mike, I was going to just add that when you think of what's going on in private markets, 50%... so we have a trillion-two of assets, it's all private things. 50% of the things that we own today did not exist as an investable asset class 15 years ago. It's not that we don't build pipelines and we don't build rail and we don't own all those things, but the incremental money in the world is going into the fiber backbone, cloud backbone, and now AI backbone. Ten years from today, I expect that 50% number will be 75. And it's just... the world is transforming and the infrastructure of the world is dramatically transforming. So you need to look at what's next, what's coming next, and this build-out is very, very significant. I just want to identify one very simple fact. Today it's impossible for your clinician, your doctor, to give you the best advice. They want to give you the best advice, but it's impossible with a hundred new developments all over the world being released. Since our phones can find you, obviously it won't be long before you're going to have your medical teammate who has all your clinical information. All your medical information will have surveyed everything in the world that's gone on overnight while you were sleeping. And therefore, we have to think who's going to power billions of these? The same thing's going to happen in education. But what we've seen here in this artificial intelligence—and here's a slide—is four times the accuracy of a human doctor. So, these should be available to every single person in the planet someday, and you can imagine. Now, one of the exciting things, Larry and Bruce, when we were financing the telecom and everything, we had a hard time finding an investment-grade credit, okay? It was new, it was risky. And so, talk to me a little bit about who are your clients today that you're helping them build? Larry, you want to start with that? Who are your clients... I'm not speaking now of your investors, but who's knocking on your door every day? Over 50% of our assets, of the $14-plus trillion we manage, are retirement assets. And so, our investors are somebody who awarded us $1,000 of their savings through an IRA account. We also have investors who have allocated over $100 billion of their balance sheet to us. And I wrote about this in my last CEO letter. I think our investors are going to be more and more of those people—families, individuals—who historically kept their money in a bank account. What we have seen over the last 30 years is that wages have not kept up with the role of capital. And what we're seeing worldwide is more and more countries are looking to build out their own capital markets. They are building out their own capital markets through offering a more self-directed retirement plan. Actually, we've just been hired by the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia to help them navigate away from a government-sponsored retirement system to a personal, direct defined contribution plan. Our partnership in India with Reliance is really trying to democratize investing and growing with India and investing in India. These are going to be our investors of the future. More and more countries are focusing on how to build out their own domestic capital markets. If you think of the success of Japan and where the Japanese stock market has gone since October 2023, it's not a coincidence that then-Prime Minister Kishida announced the doubling of the tax exemption of their self-directed retirement account called NISA. From that point on, the Japanese stock market doubled in valuation. And so, there's a confluence going on right now that more and more countries are trying to have more and more of their citizens—getting back to the liability and their assets—instead of having money in a bank account, which is one of the worst financial decisions of a lifetime. So, trying to get more and more people to grow with our country and to invest side-by-side, that is the only way we are going to broaden economic success. We are not going to be able to broaden economic success only by wages because wages in this AI world are not going to grow as fast as the potential of the AI growth. The potential of how capital that is going to be invested in these investments will outperform. Thank you, Larry. So, Bruce, let's... we've talked about the advantages of being long-term investors. How does that mindset influence how you underwrite, allocate capital, and how does it shape the way you think about trade-offs between public and private markets? So, the greatest miracle of investing, finance—in fact, almost everything—is compounding of everything: compounding interest, compounding returns, compounding knowledge, compounding wealth. And if one can compound those things over long periods of time, it's a miracle. If you can earn north of 12, nothing else matters. So, don't try to earn 35. It's good if you earn 35, but don't try to earn 35; earn 12 every year for very long periods of time. Just witness Berkshire Hathaway. And that, I'd say, that's probably the most important thing in finance. So, we're always trying to figure out, and what our clients are telling us increasingly... remember, all we do is private assets. Yes, drawdown funds are important and we have to have them, and we do have them, but many of our investors want to have other things with us outside of drawdown funds. They want to have continuity assets that are the great ones we shouldn't sell and that we can carry on with them for long periods of time. These funds are the large groups in the world, which again are all just individuals. They're all, either in some way, it goes back to individuals, but these funds are very, very significant. And 20 years ago, they were 100 billion and that was a large pool of money, and today it's a trillion. And there will be 30 funds north of a trillion soon. These are going to be $5 to $10 trillion pools of capital. And they can't churn assets all the time. Yes, some things they can churn, but what they should do is own great businesses for long periods of time and compound capital. And that's... that's the success of business, and the more we can do that for our clients increasingly... that's what binds us to them for success. Small groups can do that for small individuals or people. We try to do it in large scale for large groups or large amounts of capital. So, Larry mentioned Japan and Bruce talked about 12%. When Japan was yielding close to zero, if you wanted to save a million dollars, you had to put away 25,000 a year for 40 years for your retirement at zero. If you could get 12% that Bruce is throwing out here, a one-time contribution of $10,800 gets you to a million. So, it is the rate of return. Now, I've noticed, Larry, that since you went public your stock has generated a 22% compound rate of return. We all wish we had invested more in Bruce's stock and your stock today. Mike, the best is yet to come. What? The best is yet to come. No kidding. Okay, Bruce. We're taking you up on that. Okay. All right. Truth... I'm an investor both in BlackRock and in Brookfield, so I'm happy to hear the best is yet to come. So, let's talk, Larry, for a moment here. We addressed the fact that we're going to let individuals get their own money, their own accounts throughout the world, and you in your letter—which has pretty much replaced the Buffett letter now as the letter to read—talked about investing in your country, in your state, wherever you live here. As I mentioned before, if I went back 40 years, we couldn't find an investment-grade company to loan sizeable money to in technology. Today, when you're talking about hyperscalers, you have companies with trillions of dollars of equity at the moment that are many of your largest committed money. What is it like when someone walks in the door, Larry, and says they want to borrow $50 billion? Working with the hyperscalers is a fairly recent phenomenon. Before that, they were generating so much internal cash flow, they actually rarely went to the debt market. And because we were one of the top two largest shareholders, we've known these companies for years and years and years, and almost every hyperscaler, I think, we're either one, two, or three, the largest shareholder. So, we have deep relationships with them. But it wasn't until a few years ago where I had breakfast with one of the major CEOs of one of the top few hyperscalers and their stock was trading around a 30 PE. I did a little studying before I had the breakfast and I saw in their balance sheet they were owning a lot of data centers. I told the CEO at breakfast, "Why do you own these?" I said, "You know, this is... the beauty of the capital markets... we would enjoy a 15% return plus or minus. It is dragging down... your ownership of data centers is actually dragging down your equity return." And from those conversations on, now all you're seeing is all these hyperscalers either raising a lot of debt, or they're partnering with Brookfield or BlackRock or the other firms. They are trying to find financing or partnering, whether they have the ownership of the data centers and doing it through debt, which I don't believe is a good long-term solution for them, or they're partnering with these big, large data centers that we all own. And it's much more capital efficient for these companies to be doing that. I think that's what you're witnessing right now. As Bruce was talking about with Brookfield, we're both in the market of attracting long-term capital to work with these hyperscalers who have great, great financial needs. If you think about it on behalf of the investors, you know, we're trying to secure a 15, 20-year lease with a triple-A company. So, it's a great return for a pension fund, for a 401(k), for a sovereign wealth fund, for an insurance company. And so, much of it is going to be financed by debt, and that's going to be a big role of private credit in financing this build-out of America. So, I believe ultimately we're going to see shortages of capital, the amount of capital that we need. If we go back to if more and more countries are going to be self-directing more of their savings domestically, it's going to be putting some challenges here. And so, we need to be much more thoughtful about it. But right now, I look at this as a perfect opportunity for long-term investors to be part of this unbelievable once-in-a-lifetime growth opportunity and to be an investor in it. Bruce, you over the years have built Brookfield with a number of acquisitions. And one of those was Oaktree, which has been a major source of financing in the credit markets, etc. How do you maintain the culture of Brookfield as you absorb these different organizations? So, as we built out the business, it's been 30 years, I guess, on the asset management side as opposed to our investment business. And there were pieces of the pie that we just weren't strong in. We, over the years, would meet groups and we figured out that if we could slowly bring managers in... Larry's done it a little bit differently. But we decided if you could slowly bring them in over time and buy them out and assimilate them into your organization, we got the best of both. So, we have various partnerships where we own... we have a path to control in all of them, but we've done it slowly and methodically, and we've just added where we needed to broaden out the organization. Because what the big organizations in the world need today is they need solutions for them. They don't need our products. We don't sell products to people anymore. Yes, they buy our funds, but what we need to give them are solutions to their problems. And by having all of those things within house, we can slice up the pieces. Because if you come in for an apple and we try to sell you an orange, because we're only selling oranges this week, it doesn't work. And what we can now do is listen to our clients, hear what their issues are, and we're creating bespoke products for people. We did one the other day... it's a very long-tailed liability for an institution where we're taking money from them. We don't have to give capital back to them for 27 years. And we give money back from year 27 to year 50. It's incredible. It's the compounding machine of all time. And I was super excited about it because it solves one of their needs. And I'd say that's what we're doing and what our partner manager strategy allowed us to do was to just reshape how we were able to deliver products to people. Because yes, you could say we're going to go outside and put a bunch of managers together, but when you have them in-house, it's much more valuable, and we just needed extra pieces to the pie. I think it's important to fully understand how these institutions of Brookfield and BlackRock today can accommodate. It was maybe a decade ago that you had two pension funds that gave money for 10 years, no returns for 10 years, not 27. And you had to compound during that period of time.
