It wasn't like an interview he gave to The Atlantic and he didn't have time to back-brief Trump. If Trump is still doing anything, he's watching television. I'm sure he either watched it or knew what Rubio was saying. And if he isn't watching it, then he is like on his hands and knees actually like making the gold pieces or the steps of his new ballroom, because it's not clear he does anything else. But the notion that Marco Rubio could be taken seriously after doing a press conference like that and then it literally isn't two hours before Trump contradicts him. Like what is... who meets with him? Like with whom do you have any clout? I mean, I think this is why a lot of our allies are looking at different ways that they can work with China now. You saw a Canada-China deal just recently over something that they wouldn't have done before with regards to automobiles, electric automobiles going to Canada because they wouldn't have wanted to anger us and now they're just like, "We can't trust these guys." And moreover, not only is it because we can't trust these guys, but as General Hertling's saying, we don't know who to deal with. Even if we did get to a deal with them, how could we trust that it would stick? You know, and he mentioned there's a lot of times... as a former Republican, I'll say I'm guilty of this. A lot of times there's like mockery of bureaucrats and experts and sometimes the Democrats, you know, have too many meetings. I do think that that's a fair critique. But on a serious document like a deal, a nuclear deal with Iran, I had friends that were working on that deal, like they were legitimate experts. They spent a lot of time like going over the details and the particulars. The idea... and I think that they really believed this madman theory of politics: that Trump could just act crazy because he is crazy and they could bully people and maybe bribe people and send in an outer borough real estate deal to sign a deal with them and that it would be okay. And I think they thought that that would work and I think that we are seeing tragically in disaster for our economy and the whole world economy that that doesn't work. It is indeed what we are living through. Lieutenant General Mark Hertling, thank you so much for joining us today. Tim sticks around a little bit longer. In the spirit of that which is not normal, Donald Trump is locked in a verbal war of aggression with the Pope. And Pope Leo has responded to the latest absurdity from Donald Trump. Donald Trump claiming repeatedly now that the Pope wants Iran to have a nuclear weapon. The Pope said this, quote, "If anyone wants to criticize me for proclaiming the gospel, let them do so with the truth. The church has spoken out against all nuclear weapons for years, there is no doubt about that. I simply hope to be listened to because of the value of God's word." Those comments come as Secretary of State Marco Rubio, who is himself a Catholic, is expected to meet with Pope Leo tomorrow. There are new polls out because Trump is, as I said, in a verbal war with the Pope, that show American Catholics are at odds with Trump on this one. According to Washington Post/Ipsos poll, 61% of U.S. Catholics approve of Pope Leo. Tim is still here. There's some more in these polling. Reaction to Donald Trump posting a picture depicting himself as Jesus: 87% of respondents had a negative view of that. 9%, which is probably like the Mar-a-Lago Winners Club or something, liked it. Hegseth praying publicly to God for, quote, "overwhelming violence of action against those who deserve no mercy," end quote. 27% have a positive view of that, 69% negative. Your thoughts about this war with the Vatican? I think it's pretty telling that they're sending Rubio there, especially after the back-and-forth. Vance was there not so long ago. And to me it says that they're looking at these numbers and they know that they have problems with Catholics and with people of faith and that they think that maybe Marco would be better suited than J.D. given his demeanor to try to smooth things over. And I think that it's a pretty rare sign from this White House, at least in Trump 2.0, of them like kind of demonstrating that they realize they've created a political problem for themselves. Christopher Hale writes a good Substack about the Vatican and I was reading this the other day. There's another theory that's out there, potential reporting, is that Marco could be going there about Trump's potential engagement in Cuba next because we know that Trump has an eye on Cuba and I don't know, maybe there's some thinking that he could diffuse Pope Leo over that. I find... given what we've seen from Pope Leo, I find that very hard to believe, but I think both Cuba and their political problems is what's driving this. The other interesting thing is that Pope Leo doesn't back down. He's afraid of not one thing from them. And you've got, you know, a study in the Republicans cowering for the 10 years we've been covering them. This is not, you know, they're not dealing with Lindsey Graham here in Pope Leo. He's made of something totally different. Yeah, absolutely. And he doesn't have to be responsive to opinion polls. You know, Pope Leo is also not saying anything different than he would have said before. He's been very consistent in his kind of posture within the Catholic Church. And you know, I think I said this last time this came up with you, Nicolle, it's like this is another mess of their own making. And it's not as if the Catholic Church didn't have disagreements with the Biden administration over abortion or other issues, but you just didn't see Joe Biden going onto his social media feed to call them names, right? And I think that they've created this problem for themselves and Pope Leo is certainly... does not seem like he's demonstrating, you know, any malleability on this, and for good reason. And I think it's a good lesson to others about the way that you can stand up to these guys' bullying. Yeah, can you imagine the news cycle if Joe Biden like tweeted, "The Pope is soft on... I don't know, women's issues"? Crazy. Tim Miller, thank you for joining us today.

Is climate denialism on the rise? Prof Katharine Hayhoe on science, faith and algorithms

World Economic Forum(1.1M)·34:40·508 views·2026-05-07
Katharine HayhoeClimate Scientist
TL;DRWhile public concern regarding climate change is at an all-time high due to the increasing frequency of extreme weather, social media algorithms are disproportionately amplifying denialist rhetoric, creating a perceived rise in skepticism that contradicts global polling data.

TL;DR

While public concern regarding climate change is at an all-time high due to the increasing frequency of extreme weather, social media algorithms are disproportionately amplifying denialist rhetoric, creating a perceived rise in skepticism that contradicts global polling data.

The Paradox of Climate Denialism vs. Public Concern

  • Polling Realities: Despite the visible intensity of online debate, data from the US, Canada, UK, and Asia shows that the majority of people are now worried about climate change.
  • The Experience Factor: Personal experience with "supersized" typhoons, heatwaves, and floods has shifted public perception more effectively than scientific data alone.
  • The "Dismissive" Minority: About 7–10% of the population are "dismissives" who are ideologically committed to denial. Hayhoe advises against arguing with this group, as they are unlikely to change regardless of the evidence.
  • Rising Online Toxicity: Hayhoe reports a 10 to 15-fold increase in personal trolling and vitriol on platforms like X (formerly Twitter) over the last two years, attributed to changes in moderation and algorithmic amplification of "outrage" content.

The "Last-Ditch Effort" Against Transition

  • Economic Tipping Points: Clean energy has passed an economic point of no return. In many regions, solar energy plus storage is now the cheapest option available.
      • China: Installed more solar in a single year than the US has in its entire history.
      • Pakistan: Installed more solar in one year than the entire history of Canada.
  • The Source of Friction: Hayhoe characterizes the current surge in denialism as a "last-ditch effort" by those with the most to lose from the transition to a sustainable economy.
  • The Cost of Inaction: In the 1980s, the US experienced one "billion-dollar" weather disaster every four months; today, such disasters occur every two weeks.

Strategies for Constructive Engagement: Head, Heart, and Hands

To move people from "worry" to "action," Hayhoe utilizes a three-part communication framework:

  • The Head: Acknowledging the facts—it is real, it is us, and it is serious.
  • The Heart: Connecting the issue to personal values. This involves identifying what a person already cares about (e.g., family, local economy, faith, or personal health) and showing how climate change impacts those specific things.
  • The Hands: Moving toward agency. Instead of focusing solely on individual carbon footprints (like recycling), Hayhoe emphasizes using one's voice to advocate for systemic change in workplaces, schools, and places of worship.
  • The Identity Shift: Using a case study of her colleague John Cook, Hayhoe illustrates that people often adopt climate-friendly behaviors (like solar panels) for non-environmental reasons (thrift, independence). Once the solution becomes part of their identity, their perspective on the science often follows.

Reconciling Science and Faith

  • Faith as a Motivator: As an evangelical Christian, Hayhoe argues that faith-based values are "perfectly uniquely suited" to motivate climate action, centered on the concepts of stewardship and responsibility for the vulnerable.
  • The "Age of Earth" Non-Issue: Hayhoe notes that even for those who hold "Young Earth" views, the data remains urgent. Over the last 6,000 years, global temperatures were stable or cooling until the Industrial Revolution, at which point they spiked—a fact that should be alarming regardless of one's geological timeline.
  • Values vs. Data: While science provides the data on consequences, values (often informed by faith for 80% of the world) provide the framework for making the right choices.

The "Olympic Sport" of Practicing Hope

  • Hope vs. Optimism: Hayhoe distinguishes hope from optimism. Optimism is the passive belief that things will be fine; Hope is the recognition that things are bad, but a better future is possible if we act.
  • The Three Ingredients of Hope:

1. Acknowledging the dire nature of the current situation.

2. Recognizing that a better future is possible (though not guaranteed).

3. Identifying a clear pathway to that future and taking the first step.

  • Inoculation Against Misinformation: Research shows that "inoculating" people by explaining how and why they might be lied to (pre-debunking) is more effective than trying to correct false information after it has been accepted.
"I do not advocate for arguing with dismissives... my own personal definition of a dismissive is someone who if an angel from God with brand new tablets of stone appeared before them saying 'global warming is real' in foot-high letters of flame, they wouldn't change their mind." — Prof. Katharine Hayhoe
"What we're seeing today is the last-ditch effort to prevent the transition to a sustainable world." — Prof. Katharine Hayhoe
"I’m not sure I would say I am an optimistic person... but I’m definitely hopeful because I practice hope. I practice it like an Olympic sport." — Prof. Katharine Hayhoe
Today compared to two years ago, more people have been directly affected by extreme weather. So many more people are seeing the impacts and experiencing them personally today, and as a result, the number of people who are actually worried about climate change has increased. Welcome to Radio Davos, the podcast from the World Economic Forum that looks at the biggest challenges and how we might solve them. This week, is climate change denialism on the rise? We speak to an expert who says that the opposite is in fact true, but that social media is amplifying voices seeking to stop action on climate change. Compared to two years ago, we are facing a much more uphill battle online as algorithms have been changed. Whether deliberately or not, the ultimate result is that false information continues to be magnified even more than it was even two years ago. Katharine Hayhoe is a climate scientist and an evangelical Christian who lives in Texas and knows how to reach out to conservatives who might see climate change as a party political rather than a science-based issue. Faith-based values are actually uniquely suited, perfectly uniquely suited to motivate climate action. So why are even some of the most prominent people denying climate science that has been widely accepted for decades? What we're seeing today is the last-ditch effort to prevent the transition to a sustainable world. Follow Radio Davos wherever you get your podcasts, or visit wef.ch/podcasts. I'm Robin Pomeroy at the World Economic Forum, and with scientist and communicator Katharine Hayhoe, I practice hope. I practice it like an Olympic sport. This is Radio Davos. Two years ago on Radio Davos, we published an episode called "How to Talk to a Climate Change Skeptic." It was an interview with Texas-based climate scientist Katharine Hayhoe, who'd written a book called "Saving Us: A Climate Scientist's Case for Hope and Healing in a Divided World." As an evangelical Christian living in big oil country in the US, Katharine was used to talking about climate change with conservatives, many of whom were not convinced it was a real problem. In that interview from two years ago, which is still available on your podcast app, she had tips on how people can engage in constructive conversation on the issue, even if at first it seems like they don't agree. Two years later, just very recently, I met Katharine at the World Economic Forum's Sustainable Development Impact Meetings, which take place on the sidelines of the United Nations General Assembly and Climate Week in New York. I wanted to get her impression of whether climate denialism was really on the rise and why. Her answer was not what I was expecting. I started by asking Katharine Hayhoe why it was so important to her that we should all be talking about climate change. It's very important for me that we have these conversations because data shows that we aren't. Most people don't even hear someone else talk about climate change more than once in a blue moon. And so when I say have these conversations, though, I'm not advocating going after the people we know who are dismissive. I live in the US where about 10% of the population is dismissive. I'm from Canada where about 6 or 7% is dismissive. We have the same blocks in the EU, the UK, Australia, and more. People who are dismissive are absolutely convinced it cannot be true. In fact, my own personal definition of a dismissive is someone who if an angel from God with brand new tablets of stone appeared before them saying "global warming is real" in foot-high letters of flame, they wouldn't change their mind. So I do not advocate for arguing with dismissives. But for all of the people who are wondering, "Does it matter to me or not?" or even increasingly the people who say, "Well, I'm really worried but I don't know what to do," that's where having those conversations is so important. It is about connecting everything we know, which I think of as the head—you know, it's real, it's bad, we have to do something—to the heart, why it matters to me and the things that I care about, the places that I'm passionate about, the people that I love most, and then connecting our heart to our hands. What could we do together to make the world a better place? Head, heart, and hands is how we have those conversations. And in that interview from a couple of years ago, you said that you had to find common ground with people, connect with people. It's kind of a hopeful message. I wonder if over those two years we haven't seen things swing away from that, though. I mean everyone talks about being polarized. I don't see much advance in everyone agreeing the facts on climate change. Do you think kind of the pendulum is swinging away or is that just making more headlines? So, comparing where we were two years ago to now, there's even more rhetoric online, on social media, and in headlines of people claiming that climate is not changing, or the impacts aren't serious, or there are no solutions that work, or even that it's too late. But when we look at the polling data, it tells us a different story. And what's fascinating to me is the polling data shows the same story around the world. In the US we're seeing these trends, and in Canada, Australia, the UK, the EU, Asia and beyond, we are seeing the same trends. Today, compared to two years ago, more people have been or they know people who have been directly affected by extreme weather. And the most common way that climate change is affecting our lives is by supersizing typhoons, cyclones and hurricanes, increasing the frequency of devastating heatwaves, droughts, floods and more. So many more people are seeing the impacts and experiencing them personally today, and as a result, the number of people who are actually worried about climate change has increased. In almost every country around the world, the majority of people are already worried. So that begs the next question, right? The next question is, well, why aren't we doing more about it? And that's because worry is important, but it's not enough. We could have the whole world worried, but if we don't know what to do about it, we'll just be paralyzed. And that's the situation that we're actually heading towards today. I think of that as the head. We know that it's real, it's bad, and we need to fix it. But we still haven't made that heart connection. Whether I'm someone who works at a large corporation—how is climate change affecting our supply chain, our facilities, our bottom line? Whether I'm a homeowner paying my home insurance—it's gone up a lot the last year or two—how has climate change made that happen? If I'm a parent who cares about the air that my child is breathing that's filled with pollution or wildfire smoke, we haven't made that head-to-heart connection yet. Then we have to make the heart-to-hands connection. How can I as an individual do everything I can to make a difference? Around the world when you ask people what can I do to make a difference, you know what the number one answer is that we get whether we're talking about Singapore or New York? Recycle. Now don't get me wrong, I recycle with the best of them, but that isn't going to fix climate change. Neither is your personal carbon footprint reduction going to fix climate change, although that's also important too. The most important way each of us can make a difference is to use our voice to advocate for action wherever we live, wherever we work, wherever we worship, whatever organization we're part of. How does social change begin? It begins when someone uses their voice. And to use that voice effectively, it's not just about the head, we have to connect the heart and the hands. So that's where we are today that's different than where we were two years ago. I wasn't aware of that polling data. That's really interesting. And I think it probably shows once again there's more heat than light. From social media we always see the extremes of opinion. Well, if you don't mind, I have spent a lot of time on social media and I have seen some changes on social media that are amplifying this rhetoric. So I was an early adopter of Twitter back in 2009, a really long time ago, and for many years I was the most followed climate scientist on Twitter. I'm also very active on LinkedIn, on BlueSky, Threads, Instagram, YouTube, Substack, you name it. And so what I have noticed over the past two years is that the algorithms on many of these platforms have been changed. I have data on Twitter and X going back years of posting the same truths that everyone needs to know, for example: climate change is real, it's human caused, it's serious and there are solutions if we act now. And so I have data on how many people responded and how they responded to the same information over multiple years on Twitter and X. And what I've seen is that the amount of trolling, the amount of people calling me horrible names, telling me that I should just kill myself, it has increased by a factor of 10 to 15 times. In the last two days alone, now that we're talking today Robin, I have received hundreds of comments on X. These aren't even on posts that I myself created, but posts that others created of videos of me speaking this week, calling me absolutely the worst names in the world and saying absolutely horrible things, as well as that nothing I'm saying is true. On Threads, my Threads and my BlueSky account—both of those platforms came out about the same time—they were growing together equally at exactly the same rate until the US election. All of a sudden my Threads account froze and then dropped while my BlueSky account continued to grow. So compared to two years ago, we are facing a much more uphill battle online as algorithms have been changed, whether deliberately or not, such that the ultimate result is that false information continues to be magnified even more than it was even two years ago. About six years ago an MIT study already showed then that false information on Twitter spreads six times faster around the world than accurate information on Twitter. So it's not a level playing field; it's an uphill slope trying to get the truth out today. Have you ever asked the platforms about that? Like, have you changed your algorithm? Why is this happening? Are you going to do anything about it? I have certainly analyzed and posted my data online. And I have had conversations on the challenges of moderation on these platforms in the past with some folks at the old Twitter before it was purchased and turned into X, with some folks at TikTok, and with some folks at LinkedIn as well. I fully recognize that it is almost impossible to control for all disinformation because our human brain is wired such that we're more attracted to false information or information that makes us outraged than we are to truth. But at the same time, I do think that algorithms do play a role, and moderation plays a role as well. And there is a responsibility, I think, to at least ensure that we are doing more good than harm. And right now I think with some platforms the balance has tipped in the opposite direction. I suppose it's hard to say whether that's a genuine move of public opinion, although you cite poll data which would suggest not so much. But you know, has there been a move of kind of fury against climate action in a way? If you've spent your time on some of these social media channels, it looks like there has been. Do you think that is confined to those channels or does it actually express itself then in the real world? Another big difference between two years ago versus now is the fact that we are this much closer to the clean energy economy. Multiple countries have already passed the economic tipping point for electric vehicles, for solar energy plus storage. For the last few years, every year China has installed more solar energy in a single year than the United States has in its entire history. Last year the country of Pakistan installed more solar energy in one year than the entire country of Canada has in its entire history. The clean energy technology exports from China, taking into account also all the fossil fuels used to produce them, it's estimated they reduced global emissions 1% last year all by themselves. Wherever we turn, we are seeing evidence of new technology, clean energy, and more sustainable ways to use our water, our food, our infrastructure, our resources and more. We're closer than ever to the possibility of a tipping point for that better future. And so all the more reason for those who have everything to lose from that transition to fight even harder. And so I really believe that what we're seeing today is the last-ditch effort to prevent the transition to a sustainable world. Can I ask you about the psychology of some of these things? Because it strikes me that climate change denial or skepticism has been wrapped into the kind of woke issues—these lefty do-gooder causes—and that might be on gender or it might be on racism and on climate change. Whereas in fact, I would have thought climate change is not one of those issues, and certainly in some parts of the world it's never been a left-right thing. I think in Europe it was certainly, until recently, pretty bipartisan. You know, conservative parties, right-of-center parties were just as much in favor of climate action and climate treaties as the left-of-center parties. Do you have any insights as to why climate change is kind of lumped in with the more general culture war? Well, you're perfectly right. A thermometer does not give you a different answer depending on the way you vote. And a wildfire or a hurricane doesn't knock on the door of your house and say, "Excuse me, are you conservative or liberal or green party?" before it destroys your home and puts your family in danger. So there's nothing inherently political about the simple facts that climate is changing. Humans are responsible—we've really checked, it's not the sun or volcanoes, in fact according to natural factors we should be getting cooler right now, not warmer. And it's bad. Yes, there's a few places where there's some benefits from warming. I'm Canadian so I'm always looking for a little warmer conditions in the winter, but my goodness, even in a northern country like Canada or Greenland or you know, Mongolia, we see huge impacts. We see negative impacts from climate change on people's health, on our food production, on our water quality and availability, increasingly on our infrastructure and the economy, the insurance industry and more. So there's no question that this issue affects us all no matter who we are, no matter where we live, no matter how we vote. Back in the 1990s when Gallup first started polling Americans in the United States about global warming, as they called it then, back then Republicans and Democrats were neck and neck. Just under 50% said yes, I'm aware of it and I think it's a problem. There was no difference between them, and there shouldn't be. But then they started to separate. And within 10 years, the number one predictor of whether someone agreed with the simple facts I just shared—it's real, it's us, it's bad, and we need to fix it—it was not how educated they were, it was not how smart they were, it was not how much science they knew. It was simply where they fell on the political spectrum. Why is that? It's because there was a concerted investment in framing climate solutions as a threat to people with conservative values. There's an excellent book and documentary called "Merchants of Doubt" that explains how this was done in the US. There's one called "The Petroleum Papers" that explains how it was done in Canada. As a result, as the United States and increasingly the world has become more and more polarized, it's taking this toxic stew of issues with it, and climate change is one of those. But for myself, I mean, I'm a scientist. I just go with what the data says. If you had to place me on the political spectrum, I'm not even sure where I'd go. I'm an evangelical Christian and that's actually why I became a climate scientist—because it affects the poorest and most vulnerable among us. Yet I advocate for climate policy because I know it affects all of us, especially my neighbors in the deep red state of Texas where I live, which is also a leader in wind and solar energy. So my dream is that people and politicians across the spectrum would agree: it's real, it's us, it's bad, and we need to fix it. And then they would argue over who has the best solutions. I want all the best and the most politically savvy minds in the world to be arguing over "I want to do it this way," or "No, I could do it this way," or "This technology is better," or "No, this policy is better." We need those arguments, and instead we're wasting time arguing over the numbers the thermometer is giving us. How do we change that? So, the objections people have to climate change: it's not real, it's not us, it's not bad, we can't fix it. They sound different, but they all have the same goal. The goal of any objection, whether it's "wind turbines kill birds" or "scientists are making this up to line their pockets," both of which I've heard in the same breath from many people, the objection is to ensure we don't act. The point of any climate denial is to prevent action. Why is that? Because people are convinced that climate action poses a greater threat to whatever they hold dear than just letting it roll. That might have been true 50 or 60 years ago, but today it is not. Let me just give you one example from the US. Back in the 1980s, they had about one billion-dollar plus weather and climate disaster every four months. So three a year. Now, there's one every two weeks. And we know that the direct impact is only 10% of the long-term impact. So take any billion-dollar plus disaster, which could be 48 billion dollars, 96 billion dollars, 126 billion dollars for a big hurricane, and then multiply that by 10 to get the full knock-on impacts. And those are just the impacts you can put a dollar sign on. There's a lot more impacts in terms of health and loss of productivity that you can't. So when we look at these impacts, they're already happening today. And the solutions are starting to happen too. I live in Texas, which has more wind energy than any other state. It is neck and neck with California—one month Texas pulls ahead and then the other month California catches up—for the most utility-scale solar. When we introduce people to solutions, and when they see themselves in the solutions, and they understand how they can benefit from the solutions, whether materially or in terms of well-being, then that often erases all the denial. Can I tell you a story about my colleague John? Please do. So I have a colleague John Cook from Australia who every time he went home to have dinner with his father... his father lived in a rural part of Australia and was very politically conservative and heard from people he trusted that climate change wasn't real. So every time John went home to have dinner with his dad, his dad would be like, "Oh, well, I heard there's more polar bears now than there ever were, so what about that global warming, John?" So John, who was already a solar physicist, got so irritated by his dad pulling up all of these arguments, that he went and created the premier climate denial debunking website in the world. It's called Skeptical Science and it actually lists over 200 arguments about climate change that people often use and debunks them using science-based information. John didn't stop there. He went back to school and he ended up getting a PhD in cognitive psychology and became a global expert in misinformation. Now, do you think any of that changed his dad's mind? It did not. But the Australian government a number of years ago was offering a rebate on solar panels in the rural area where his dad lived. His dad prized being independent, being thrifty, and it doesn't hurt to stick it to the government either. So John brought this rebate to his dad's attention using those arguments, nothing to do with climate. His dad got the solar panels and pretty soon he was emailing or texting or telling John every month, "Look at how much I've saved on my solar panels." So about two years after his dad got his solar panels they were having dinner again, and in the course of conversation his father said, "Oh yes, global warming, that's such a serious issue and I've always thought so." John said he almost fell off his chair. With his degree in cognitive psychology he couldn't even conceptualize how his dad had not just changed his mind, but had forgotten that he ever had. So what happened was the solutions became part of his dad's identity. It made his father more thrifty than he already was, more independent than he already was, smarter than he already was. And the epilogue is his dad's since moved into an assisted living facility, and he was outraged that they were wasting his money by not putting solar on the roof. So now he's on a campaign to use his voice to make a difference because he sees himself in the solutions and the solutions help him be an even better and more genuine version of who he already is. We can take that lesson and we can apply it to almost anyone we know. Who are they? What values do they prize? What things do they care about? And then what climate solutions could help them be an even better parent, an even shrewder business person, an even more loving neighbor or family member or community member, an even more genuine member of their faith? Whoever we already are, I truly believe that we have every single reason we need to care. And those of us who do already care and want to use our voices to make a difference, all we have to do is to help them figure out how what they already care about makes them the perfect person to advocate for climate action. That's actually was kind of my next question: is a large part of this "follow the money," in that proponents of climate action will say green energy is cheaper? It's not only cleaner, it's cheaper. I honestly think if utility bills were coming down to someone... very often that doesn't happen. Do you think that's a policy failure, that if my energy electricity bills are going up, which they are in most of the places I know about, I say, "Well they keep installing all this green stuff," and if people are telling you well that's more expensive, well it's showing up on your utility bill? So that's a challenge for policy makers or maybe for the utility companies, do you think? I think it's a challenge for policy makers and for communication as well. So for example where I live in Texas, I can actually choose whether I want all wind or not from my power producer and they tell me how many cents per kilowatt hour it is. So I actually know if I'm paying a difference, and it turns out on the plan that I'm on I'm not, and I'm on 100% wind. But for some people it's not broken out. And electricity prices have been soaring at the same time as solar plus storage prices have been plummeting. In many countries around the world like Pakistan, for example, where again last year they installed more solar energy in one year than the entire country of Canada in its whole history, they did that because solar is affordable. But in the United States, for example, power prices have spiked and a lot of that is because of an increase in demand, especially those massive colossal data centers that are being built to run extremely inefficient AI models. We have much more efficient ones that use a lot less energy. In fact, already before the data center revolution, 67% of the energy produced in the United States was wasted. Other countries are a bit more efficient—Germany leads the list for most efficient—but we could still be so much more efficient with the way we use our energy. So it's not the fault of the green energy that the price is skyrocketing, but how is anyone supposed to know it if they're not told? And even worse, if they're told it is, and they're not inoculated in advance? John Cook's research actually shows that we can inoculate people against false information by telling them the true information and explaining it in advance. But if they're not inoculated and they don't hear the true information, what are they supposed to believe? Tell us something about your Christian faith then, because faith isn't science. Faith is belief; it's not based on data. And people who follow fundamentalist versions of Christianity or any other religion may say they believe things which can be disproven by science... Does that kind of trouble you or how have you come to peace with that? So for me there's a verse in the book of Hebrews in the Bible that says that faith is the evidence of what we do not see. And if I had been there 2000 years ago, sitting beside whoever wrote that, I would have jogged their elbow and I would have said, "You forgot the second half of that verse: science is the evidence of what we do see." So for me they're two different sides of the same coin. Another way to put it is science can tell us if we go in this certain direction, if we make this certain decision, here's the consequences of that decision. I'm a climate modeler and so that's exactly what I do. I say depending on the choices we make, here's what the future is going to look like and here's how it will impact our food, our water, our infrastructure, our health and more. But where our values come in is in helping us choose the right decision to make, the right direction to take. And for over 80% of people around the world, their values are informed by their faith. No matter what faith it is, almost every single faith has at its core ideas of being good stewards or caretakers of this incredible planet that we've been entrusted with, and of caring for those less fortunate than us. And that makes us the perfect person; that gives us the exact values we need to care about climate action. So where has this whole idea come from that climate is somehow in conflict with faith? People say, "Well if God's in control then why does it matter?" And I say, "Well, just open your Bible and read Genesis 1." I say that because it's typically Christians who say that to me. If they weren't Christian I wouldn't say that to them. But if you read Genesis 1 it says God gave humans responsibility over every living thing on this earth, which of course includes our brothers and sisters as well. And then they say, "Well if the world is going to end anyways why does it matter?" It turns out 2000 years ago people back then were saying the same thing, not about climate change, but just about doing good. And the Apostle Paul wrote to them and basically said: get out of bed, get a job, support your family, care for the widows, the orphans and the poor. You don't know what the future holds, but in the meantime we're called to love others as we ourselves have been loved by God. So faith-based values are actually uniquely suited, perfectly suited to motivate climate action. But over the years—and this has happened throughout human history—people build up cultural constructs around their theology, constructs like the age of the earth, for example. And so I often interact with people who believe the earth is 6000 years old. But you know what? When it comes to climate action, it doesn't really matter. As long as we believe the earth is at least 300 years old, because that's when the industrial revolution began, then we have all the information we need to take climate action. In fact if you take our ice core data which stretches back hundreds of thousands of years, our ice core data shows this regular pattern of glacial and interglacial cycles driven by changes in the shape of the earth's orbit around the sun. Right now where we are on those natural cycles is we should be gradually heading into a cooling period, but instead we're warming faster and faster. If you take that ice core record and you just plot the last 6000 years, you know what you see? You see that we were gradually cooling very, very, very slowly until about 200 years ago and all of a sudden, it goes straight up. So honestly, if someone believes the earth is young, they have even more reason to be extremely worried about what's happening because it is completely unprecedented in the history of the planet. Are you optimistic that things will get better? You've got very prominent people saying climate change is a hoax and people will be listening to that and taking it at face value and believing it. What gives you hope that that can either change or that action will happen anyway? You strike me as a very optimistic person. I'm not sure I would say I am an optimistic person because I'm not convinced it's going to turn out okay. I know that what happens in the future depends on the choices we make today. And so that's why I'm doing everything I can to use my voice and to encourage others to use their voice to help people understand what's at stake. It is nothing less than everything we love at stake. Imagine everything, every place, and most particularly every person you love, just kind of tied to the railroad tracks of life with climate change and the biodiversity crisis right behind it barreling down the track. Wouldn't you do everything you could to stop that train? I would, and I think if everybody knew that was the situation, they would too. So although I wouldn't say I'm optimistic, I'm definitely hopeful because I practice hope. I practice it like an Olympic sport. I practice it like I want to win my own personal competition of hope. Because for me hope is not, "Oh everything's going to be okay." Because it's not going to be okay unless we do something. When you look at what hope actually is, it turns out that hope begins with the recognition that things are bad. Think about London during the Blitz. Things were terrible. That's when you most need hope, when the situation looks most dire. If you look at the activists advocating for abolition back in the 1800s, or the civil rights activists in the US in the 50s, or those who were fighting apartheid in South Africa even more recently. It seems darkest before the dawn often, but by practicing hope, that's what keeps you going. So it begins by recognizing it's bad; that's the first ingredient to hope. The second ingredient recognizes that a better future is possible, although it's not guaranteed. But here's the third ingredient: the third ingredient is that there's a pathway to get from here to that better future, and that if we do everything we can to move each other along that pathway, it will make a difference. Now this is not just psychology, it's actually climate science, because what science says when it comes to climate change, when it comes to carbon pollution, it says every bit of warming we avoid matters. Of course we should do everything we can to strive for the 1.5 degree C goal. But if we strive for 1.5 degrees and we end up at 1.6, well that's better than 1.7. If we end up at 1.7, well that's better than if we had aimed for two. Every bit of warming matters, every choice matters, every action matters. And so for me hope is a practice where I go out and I look for good news about people, organizations, and more who are making a difference. When you go out and look, there's thousands of them all around the world. Whether it's a teacher, whether it's somebody working in industry, whether it's somebody in the faith community, or the sports and entertainment industry, or even just somebody living down the road from you. And then what I do is I share that. So a couple of years ago I started a newsletter called "Talking Climate," and every week I share good news about climate solutions, and not-so-good news about how climate change is affecting us—not the polar bears or the ice sheets, but how it's affecting fertility rates, the matcha tea harvest, our home insurance rates, our own health during heatwaves. It's not-so-good news, and then I always share something we can do. Head, heart, and hands. That's how we can build a better world. Katharine, thank you very much. Thank you for having me. Katharine Hayhoe. We have lots more episodes on climate change in our back catalog. Find those wherever you're listening to this or on our website wef.ch/podcasts, where you'll also find our two sister podcasts, both of them weekly like Radio Davos. They are Agenda Dialogues and Meet the Leader. And find out what the World Economic Forum is doing on climate change by visiting the Center for Nature and Climate online; link in the show notes. This episode of Radio Davos was written, presented and edited by me, Robin Pomeroy, with studio production by Taz Kelleher. Radio Davos will be back next week, but for now, thanks to you for listening, and goodbye.

Boston Fed President Collins on FOMC Dissent and Kevin Warsh’s Nomination | Big Take

Bloomberg Podcasts(479.0K)·20:39·324 views·2026-05-07
Susan CollinsPresident, Boston Fed Kevin WarshNominee, Federal Reserve
TL;DRBoston Fed President Susan Collins supports the recent shift toward policy dissent, arguing against an "easing bias" as the war in Iran and persistent supply shocks delay inflation's return to the 2% target.
As the Fed prepares for leadership under nominee Kevin Warsh,...

TL;DR

Boston Fed President Susan Collins supports the recent shift toward policy dissent, arguing against an "easing bias" as the war in Iran and persistent supply shocks delay inflation's return to the 2% target. As the Fed prepares for leadership under nominee Kevin Warsh, officials are bracing for potential "regime change" and structural reforms to the central bank’s communication and modeling.

FOMC Dissent and Policy Stance

  • Historical Dissent: The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) recently saw an 8-4 vote to hold rates steady, the highest level of disagreement since 1992.
      • The "Easing Bias" Conflict: While Collins (a non-voter this year) supported holding rates, she sided with dissenters Laurie Logan, Beth Hammack, and Neel Kashkari, who opposed statement language implying the next move would likely be a rate cut.
      • Diverse Perspectives: One member, Steven Myron, dissented in favor of a quarter-point cut, illustrating a widening range of views on the committee.
  • Consensus vs. Unanimity: Collins emphasized that "consensus" does not mean "unanimity." She views the current lack of a unified front as a healthy reflection of economic uncertainty rather than a breakdown in leadership.

Inflation Outlook and the Impact of Conflict

  • War in Iran: Collins identifies the conflict as a primary driver of renewed inflationary risk.
      • Price Surges: Baseline expectations for disinflation have been pushed further out due to surges in energy and food prices.
      • 1973 Comparison: Collins noted that while the current energy shock is of a similar magnitude to the 1973 oil shock, the U.S. is better positioned today as a net oil exporter.
  • Inflation Projections: Collins expects core inflation to remain elevated, likely ending the year around 3%.
      • Credibility Concerns: She expressed concern over the Fed’s credibility, noting the 2% inflation target has been missed for over five years.
      • Expectations: There is an increased risk that longer-term inflation expectations could become "unanchored" if the Fed does not demonstrate a firm commitment to its target.

State of the Labor Market and Economy

  • Resilient Growth: Despite global shocks, the U.S. economy remains surprisingly resilient with strong investment and a rebound in consumption.
  • The "Unusual Balance": Collins describes the labor market as stable but containing pockets of fragility.
      • Employment Data: The unemployment rate has held at approximately 4.3% for several months.
      • Warning Signs: Concerns remain regarding high duration of unemployment and elevated jobless rates among college-age and younger workers.
  • Supply Chain "New Normal": The Fed is monitoring a series of supply shocks (Pandemic, Russia-Ukraine, tariffs, and Iran). However, Collins noted that productivity gains have acted as a "positive supply shock" that supports growth while dampening inflation.

Leadership Transition: Jerome Powell to Kevin Warsh

  • The Warsh Nomination: Kevin Warsh is expected to be confirmed as the 17th Fed Chair by the June 16th meeting.
      • Regime Change: Warsh has signaled a desire for "big reforms," including potential changes to how the Fed communicates with markets, uses forward guidance, and models inflation.
      • Independence: Collins indicated she is taking Warsh’s public comments regarding Fed independence "at face value" but remains cautious about the specific details of his reform agenda.
  • Powell’s Continued Presence: In a rare move, Jerome Powell intends to remain on the Fed’s Board of Governors after his term as Chair ends, a decision that has drawn political scrutiny but may provide a counterweight during the transition.
"My preference also would have been to adjust the statement to... not be as closely aligned with language that has been associated with the presumption that the next move will be a cut." — Susan Collins
"I am concerned about... the credibility of having a 2% target that we have missed now for over five years... If inflation expectations were to become unanchored, that would complicate the task that Congress has set for us." — Susan Collins
"The labor market in my view does seem to be in balance, even though it's an unusual balance... The unemployment rate has been at around 4.3% now for a number of months." — Susan Collins
Bloomberg Audio Studios. Podcasts, radio, news. This is a difficult moment for central bankers trying to figure out how much lasting damage the war in Iran is likely to have on the global economy. In the United States, the Federal Reserve has been struggling to get inflation back to its 2% target, as it's weathered criticism from President Trump and scrutiny from the Department of Justice. Fed policymakers voted eight to four at their most recent meeting not to adjust interest rates. They held them steady. And that is the most dissent we've seen among members of the Federal Open Market Committee since 1992. No change in rates, but we have four, four dissents. Laurie Logan, Beth Hammack, and Neel Kashkari agreed rates should stay on hold, but they did not support including an easing bias in the statement at this time. Steven Myron wanted a quarter point cut. That lack of unanimity among policymakers highlights the difficult situation the Fed is in right now as its leadership is about to change. So let me back up for a little bit and tell you how I think about things. Susan Collins is the President of the Federal Reserve Bank of Boston. That's one of twelve regional Reserve Banks. First of all, I was strongly supportive of leaving the rate unchanged. I think that that is very appropriate and I can say more about why. And while she doesn't have a vote on the Fed committee that sets rates this year, Collins told my colleague Maria Eloisa Capurro she agreed with those three Fed colleagues who did vote against the decision because of what was in the statement that accompanied it. My preference also would have been to adjust the statement to, you know, not be as closely aligned with language that has been associated with the presumption that the next move will be a cut. This week, Susan Collins sat down with Maria Eloisa in Boston for a wide-ranging conversation about the economy and the Fed's future. Well, the main takeaway was that she agrees with the three people that dissented over the language of the statement. There's a lot of debate within the committee about what the next step for rates is going to be. Whether they're going to go up or down, and how much of that they should signal at this point. I'm David Gura and this is the Big Take from Bloomberg News. Today on the show, a conversation with Susan Collins, the President of the Boston Fed, about inflation, the economic fallout from the war in Iran, and how the US central bank could change under a Fed Chair Kevin Warsh. Maria Eloisa, Susan Collins, as I mentioned, President of the Boston Fed, is not a voting member of the Federal Open Market Committee this year. She doesn't vote on interest rates until 2026. Given that, why is she somebody that you wanted to talk to? So she doesn't have that amount of power this year. Why is hearing from her interesting? Well, first of all, we're always interested in what Fed officials say here at Bloomberg. But second of all, in her case in particular, Collins is one of these members of the Federal Reserve who's considered like a centrist. And we heard from Powell last week that there were a number of non-voting members at the FOMC who also disagreed with the easing bias. And we really wanted to know who these people were and whether Collins was one of them. And we felt that, you know, talking to Collins would give us also a good sense of view of what is the center thinking right now. The Fed has this dual mandate. It's focused on maximum employment and stable prices. And you asked her how she thinks the US economy is doing. So from my perspective, the economy still looks resilient, perhaps surprisingly so, given yet another shock. Consumption's rebounded a bit from January, and investment continues to be strong. The labor market in my view does seem to be in balance, even though it's an unusual balance. And what I mean by that is the unemployment rate has been at around 4.3% now for a number of months. And so that is evidence of a labor market that is in balance, that's relatively stable. The labor force participation rates for prime age are consistent. So there are some signs of fragility. You know, unemployment durations are high, college-age unemployment and younger unemployment is high, which is unusual, is higher. But at the same time, initial claims are relatively low. So there are signs of stability which just have actually grown since the prior meeting. At the same time, I see the inflation rate as something to really focus on. And I was wanting to ask you about that, you know, on the other side of the mandate as well. Like, how do you see the inflation outlook? I remember one of your latest speeches, you were still somewhat confident to return relatively quickly to the 2% target at some point, in the second half of this year, if I remember correctly. I'm just wondering what's your base case scenario in light of the war in Iran? So from my perspective, before the conflict, my baseline would be that we would have been likely to see the disinflationary process start to resume during the second half of this year. And that has really been moved further out because of the energy shock. We have seen certainly already a surge in energy prices. There is an expectation that higher food prices are likely to follow. There are other disruptions that we are seeing in a variety of different markets. And so some of those global spillovers might start to impact other prices as well. Contacts I talk to talk about shipping costs going up, about, you know, a range of different things that they see in train. And so my expectation is that we are likely to see inflation rise a bit further even if the conflict is resolved relatively soon before starting to come back down. And the impact on both the inflation side and the real side of the economy will certainly depend on how long it lasts and what the nature of the conflict develops to be. So I think that other scenarios that are somewhat less benign than my baseline scenario, their probability has increased as the conflict has continued. And I do think that that has increased the inflationary risks by more than it has increased the downside risks to the employment side of our mandate. President Collins, I'd like to return briefly to the war in Iran. One of the things that you mentioned that concerns you is the possible consequences of a more lasting supply chain disruption. And I wanted to ask you if the Fed is seeing this outlook in which supply chain disruptions are the new normal. We certainly have seen a series of supply changes, supply shocks, from the pandemic to Russia's invasion of the Ukraine to tariffs, but also positive ones. The productivity gains that have been ongoing is a different type of supply shock that has actually been helpful to bring down inflation and support growth resilience. So we have seen a number of supply shocks, and I think that that is a notable part of the past few years and something that the Fed needs to be increasing, you know, very continued attentive to. And supply shocks are complicated. I would say that the energy shock, how long it lasts will again be very important in terms of how extensive the impacts are both on inflation and on the real side of the economy, tending to push up inflation, increasing the risks of higher inflation, and to weigh on activity. At the same time, the US, even though it's a very large oil shock, it is a similar magnitude to the 1973 oil shock, it's quite large. The US is less dependent on imported oil in particular than it was, and is now a net oil exporter. And so that offsets the impact on the real economy in some ways. It certainly, you know, shifts who gains and who is impacted more negatively. But it makes it also somewhat more complicated to analyze the net effects because there are a variety of offsetting factors that relate. During your conversation, Susan Collins kind of gamed out where she thinks inflation is heading. She sees it rising over the next few months before it starts to come down, and she estimates we'll see core inflation at around 3% by the end of the year. Putting the war with Iran aside, what else does Susan Collins see as kind of the biggest drivers of inflation right now? I think she's still worried about tariffs and whether we're at the peak of that effect. And then on the war, she's still concerned about spillovers beyond energy prices and what the situation, like the global impact from the war, how is that gonna look like for the US? And then as a backdrop to all of that, she's still really concerned about the fact, and I feel like many other Fed officials are, about the fact that it's been over five years since they last hit 2%. There are a couple of things that I am concerned about. One of them is very much the credibility of having a 2% target that we have missed now for over five years. But importantly, longer-term expectations being anchored is really central to the Federal Reserve being able to achieve both sides of its mandate over time. If inflation expectations were to become unanchored, that would complicate the task that Congress has set for us and make it more challenging for us to restore price stability. And I don't think you can take inflation expectations for granted. The data are not showing longer-term inflation expectations that are currently consistent with the range of 2%. But energy prices and food prices are top of mind for consumers. And so there are reasons to be very attentive and to really continue to demonstrate the Federal Reserve's commitment to its 2% target, especially after five years of having missed that target. I want to come back to the sympathy Susan Collins has with those three members of the FOMC who did dissent. You asked her about what those dissents indicate about the committee as a whole. Let's play a bit of that exchange for our listeners. How are you seeing the three dissents that we had last week in terms of the language? So in the press conference, Chair Powell noted that there were good reasons for those who chose to dissent, who have similar views to mine, in terms of the reason for preferring to shift away from language that had been associated with an easing bias. And I think that speaks to the kind of widespread understanding of the range of views here. And so I think it's a context in which there's actually more common understanding even if people reached somewhat different conclusions than might be understood from just looking at counting how many people did or did not dissent. So I see the broader discussion as really important. I think we learned a lot from each other. Collins's interview comes at a pivotal moment for the economy and for the Federal Reserve. Coming up, what Boston Fed President Susan Collins had to say about Warsh's push for rate cuts this past year and what she thinks the Fed internal dynamics could look like under his leadership. The Federal Reserve's most recent two-day meeting, which took place late last month, was Jerome Powell's last one as Chair. I have served at the Federal Reserve under four administrations, Republicans and Democrats alike. In every case, I have carried out my duties without political fear or favor, focused solely on our mandate of price stability and maximum employment. And only a week before on April 21st, former Fed Governor Kevin Warsh had his confirmation hearing with the Senate Banking Committee. I believe a reform-oriented Fed can make a real difference to the American people and if confirmed, I will seek to create an environment in which the best people do their best work. The Senate is expected to confirm Warsh in time for the Fed's next meeting, which starts on June 16th. For his part, Powell has promised to stay on the Federal Reserve's Board of Governors after his term as Chair ends, a move that's drawn criticism from President Trump and many of his allies. Can you speak broadly to the perception surrounding Jerome Powell's decision to stay on the Board of Governors for a time to be determined after his term as Chair ends? How standard is it for someone like J Powell to do that and what kind of support or pushback are we seeing to his decision to do that? It is very rare for a Fed chair to remain on the board as governor. It is also a very particular set of conditions, right? Like I don't think the Fed has been under this political fire for a very long time, if at all. So it's an unusual set of conditions. We didn't hear from Collins much on that. I feel she's trying to give the benefit of the doubt to the new Chair, Kevin Warsh, coming in. Her and many others have said that their preference would be to take what Kevin Warsh has said regarding the Fed's independence at face value. So I feel like there's still an open mind at this stage with the new chair coming in. Yeah, speaking of this moment we're in, you asked Susan Collins about what she thinks of some of the proposals that Kevin Warsh has made. Now I want to play a bit of that exchange for listeners. President Collins, I wanted to ask you a little bit about the transition that the Fed is facing as we await for the confirmation of Kevin Warsh. He has often touted rate cuts, at least last year. Let's say that the Chair dissents from the majority's view on a rate decision, which would be very unusual by judging by the Fed's history. How would you navigate that situation and would you feel comfortable with the Chair speaking for the committee after a rate decision, if that were the case? So, you know, I think that every member of the committee assesses conditions in the way that they see appropriate based on the data. I would certainly continue to do that, and I do think that a range of views is very important and valuable as we all understand the economy better when there are different perspectives that are raised. You know, Kevin Warsh has lots of expertise in this space as a former governor. I look forward to working with him if he is confirmed as the next Chair. And he would be the 17th Chair, so there has been quite a bit of change over time. And I think that we should recognize that transition and be supportive of it. Have you talked to him recently? I have not spoken with him recently, no. I just wanted to ask you as well because Warsh has touted the need for big reforms at the Fed. And I'm just wondering what do you make of these calls for regime change at the Fed? So again, I think a new Chair needs to come in and be able to speak for themselves in terms of their priorities. And we certainly have seen in the history of the Federal Reserve that Chairs do have different priorities and those transitions unfold based on what those priorities were, are when they come. And so I don't want to get out ahead of that. Is that surprising to you that she would hold back, that she wouldn't have an opinion that she wanted to share on that right now? No. I think, as we were discussing, their preference at this moment, given that a new Chair is likely to come in relatively soon, is for most Fed officials to remain publicly speaking with an open mind. It's new leadership that you will have to face and work with for a number of years. And there's also still a lot of uncertainty as to what are the changes really that Warsh wants to do across the Fed. So, I think it makes sense for them to be cautious. So they're trying to figure out what a Kevin Warsh Fed might look like. I imagine you and your colleagues who cover the Fed are doing that as well. How much of a contrast is there do you think between what Kevin Warsh would prioritize and what J Powell has, or how he might run the committee? We don't really know out of all the changes that Warsh has touted what his priorities will be and we don't really know what the details of those changes mean. He has been saying that he wants this regime change at the Fed. He has touted possible changes to the way the Fed communicates with households, with markets, possible changes to the way the Fed thinks about forward guidance. He has touted changes to the way the Fed thinks about inflation and its models. But we don't really know the details behind all of that. So we'll just have to wait and see what his priorities are. One last question. I want to go back to your interview. You talked with Susan Collins about consensus among Fed policymakers. And I'm curious what she had to say about that and sort of what consensus among Fed officials looks like right now. I think that was very interesting because it appears that her views, and this goes also in line with what we've heard from other Fed officials, are very similar. It really seems that consensus for them is important, but they don't mean unanimity when they mean consensus. They really drill into the importance for them to have a range of different views and opinions within the committee and having those opinions being represented and being heard and debated. So I thought that was a very insightful answer actually. This is the Big Take from Bloomberg News, I'm David Gura. To get more from the Big Take and unlimited access to all of bloomberg.com, subscribe today at bloomberg.com/podcastoffer. If you liked this episode, make sure to follow and review the Big Take wherever you listen to podcasts. It helps people find the show. Thanks for listening. We'll be back tomorrow.

Fireside Chat with Day 1 Keynote Speakers

SANS Institute(68.6K)·45:35·127 views·2026-05-07
Rob T. LeeChief AI Officer and Chief of Research, SANS Institute
TL;DRThe integration of AI into cybersecurity shifts the focus from building "perfect" perimeters to managing system integrity within "dirty" networks, necessitating a move from traditional technical indicators (IOCs) toward understanding the "sources and methods" of autonomous agents.

TL;DR

The integration of AI into cybersecurity shifts the focus from building "perfect" perimeters to managing system integrity within "dirty" networks, necessitating a move from traditional technical indicators (IOCs) toward understanding the "sources and methods" of autonomous agents.

Redefining System Integrity in a "Dirty" Network

  • Living in Compromised Infrastructure: Anne Neuberger notes that the security community must embrace the reality of "living in a dirty network," where infrastructure like 5G routers may be inherently untrustworthy but must still be used to build secure systems.
  • Governance as a Scaling Mechanism: Sounil Yu compares AI governance to the fast-food industry—creating systems that ensure untrustworthy or low-skilled components produce consistent, safe results at a scale and speed previously unimaginable.
  • Ephemeral Software: Bruce Schneier predicts a shift toward "bespoke software" where AI writes, uses, and discards code instantly.
      • This makes traditional vulnerability hunting difficult because the software is temporary and "ephemeral," preventing attackers from studying it in advance.

Detecting and Mitigating Agentic Attacks

  • Speed as a Signal: Jacob Kaplan (Anthropic) argues that while agentic systems move at superhuman speeds, detection depends on understanding an organization's "median speed" to identify anomalies in lateral movement or access requests.
  • The "Accent" Analogy: Sounil Yu observes that the faster an AI-driven attacker moves, the more their "accent" (the specific patterns and fumbles of the underlying LLM) becomes visible to defenders.
  • Human-in-the-Loop Defense: For "crown jewel" assets, the most effective defense remains a human-led action.
      • Current agentic systems are not yet proficient at social engineering multiple humans to take concurrent actions.
  • Vulnerability Tolerance: Kaplan suggests that in an agent-led world, the risk tolerance for bugs in public-facing code should drop to zero, as AI will find every flaw.

The Evolution of the Cybersecurity Workforce

  • The "Last Mile" Gap: Kaplan highlights that while AI can handle bulk tasks, it currently struggles with "last mile" problem-solving. Human defenders are increasingly shifting from "doing" to "directing" swarms of agents.
  • Verification vs. Validation: Sounil Yu distinguishes between these two concepts:
      • Verification: "Did I build it right?" (AI-driven)
      • Validation: "Did I build the right thing?" (Human-driven)
  • The Apprenticeship Crisis: Schneier expresses concern that AI might eliminate entry-level roles (e.g., junior lawyers or SOC analysts), potentially destroying the apprenticeship pipeline needed to develop the senior-level "human intuition" required to direct AI agents effectively.

Information Sharing and Model Responsibility

  • Shift from IOCs to Methods: Traditional Indicators of Compromise (IP addresses, file hashes) are less useful when attackers use common API infrastructure. Sharing "sources and methods" is the new priority.
  • Models as a SOC: Anthropic acts as a de facto Security Operations Center (SOC) for its users.
      • They use "probes" to monitor how model "neurons" react to prompts to detect misuse without requiring massive compute for full text analysis.
  • The Challenge of Closed vs. Open Systems: While closed-loop providers like Anthropic can block malicious activity in real-time, attackers may simply shift to local, open-source models to become "invisible" to centralized detection.

Governance and Philosophical Risks

  • Under-Specified Instructions: Schneier warns of the "Aladdin’s Genie" problem—AIs will fulfill instructions literally but may cause harm because human language is inherently incomplete and lacks a "conception of the box."
  • Corporate vs. Public Interest: The panel debated whether private companies should decide what to report to law enforcement.
      • Anne Neuberger emphasized the need for a framework that protects civil liberties while ensuring platforms don't unknowingly enable mass-scale attacks.
"The price of freedom is a probability of crime. And as the effect of crime grows, that's a higher and higher price." — Bruce Schneier
"It is innately a human thing to ask the question and answer the question: Did I build the right thing?" — Sounil Yu
"We have to learn to live in a dirty network... We need to be able to build security even though they are untrustworthy infrastructure." — Anne Neuberger
"Your time to detect expectation, if you're running a DNR function, needs to go down... you're probably becoming just a director of your agents." — Jacob Kaplan
I'm going to first open it up to the other keynotes to talk to each other. There's got to be a question between one of you to the other one, so who would like to ask a question? I know Sounil, I think you mentioned to me there was a question that you wanted to ask about integrity of systems to Bruce and... Well, I think the—we clearly prepped this. No, I—I had the privilege of listening to the talks, and so listening to folks who preceded me and I really enjoyed all the talks. With respect to Bruce's talk, the premise of: can you build a system with integrity with components that have no integrity, or that for whom you shouldn't trust has anything of integrity? It's very similar to Ken Thompson's "Trusting Trust" sort of philosophy. But I think the premise, I think you said and what I introduced, was really around any organization. You hire a bunch of people for whom you can't necessarily trust that they're going to behave appropriately. And so the whole notion of governance factors into how we build more trustworthy organizations. And I think in the same way, we would have to do—we have a similar template when it comes to AI. It's just going to be at a scale that's much larger than we've ever imagined. And scale matters. I mean, like if you think about the fast-food industry, they have figured out how to turn teenagers into the front line against poisoning us. And it works, right? I mean, there are systems to ensure that they—that they behave properly at—at fast-food restaurants. And it happens at human speeds. Happening at computer speeds is going to be harder, and it's going to require AI. Remember Sue Gordon? So she said a really interesting talk, I forget when, and she was—what was her job? White House something... Deputy DNI. Okay, so she says something—she's talking about China and infrastructure, that we have to learn to live in a dirty network. It's a really interesting comment, right? That she's saying like we're not going to be able to secure these 5G routers. We're not. We need to be able to build security even though they are untrustworthy infrastructure. And I think so that kind of thinking, we really need to embrace. There's a similar—remind me the person that wrote "Surviving on a Diet of Poisoned Fruit"? Richard Danzig. Danzig, yes. Is he still alive? He is, he is doing well. I'll make sure to convey your warm regards. It's okay, it's recorded. We'll send it out online in a tweet here soon. What I think we'll discover with Mythos is that there's a lot of fruit that we didn't realize was poisoned. Are we—every single time we say Mythos, is that now a checkbox for later tonight? Everyone has to drink. True. All right, Jacob, you're kind of quiet right now. So everyone has eyes on Anthropic, but you know, this is Anthropic's way to ask something to the other folks up here, or we could go directly to the first question that's on, which I think is almost teed up for you, which is: How would you detect an agentic attack occurring on your network? Yeah, I actually think I saw some other question come up, which is do you just go back to the basics? And I think that's a big part of it, frankly, which is if you think about an agentic attack, you are not getting a signature that it's coming from Claude or one of our competitors. They're just clicking around as if, you know, somebody else is clicking around, frankly. One of the differences might be the speed that somebody's moving around your network, but I imagine with your own network at this point, you have employees who are also moving around at agentic speeds, so that's not necessarily a differentiator. But when I think about how you stop this sort of thing, one of the differences is good old-fashioned privileged access, like works. We're not yet seeing these systems socially engineer employees once they're inside of a system. They're just moving around laterally trying to gain access to internal networks, so I think that's a difference. So really, I think this is maybe my long-winded way of saying the basics matter a lot, and your risk tolerance has to be very different. And the only thing that I think is fundamentally changed is maybe two things. One is what is the risk tolerance of somebody finding your code and getting into your system? It should effectively be zero now. Somebody's going to find whatever bug you have. And then once they're inside of your system, what is your time to detect? Your time to detect before might be I have a week to conduct an investigation, I could have my SOC escalate something and have somebody do something else. That's—it's too slow of a time to detect. So your time to detect expectation, if you're running a DNR function, needs to go down. And then your expectation of what your like vulnerability coverage is needs to shift. And then I would say, if you have crown jewels that are incredibly important, like an Anthropic, that might be our model weights. Fortunately, those didn't go out the door though we had, you know, the embarrassing Claude code leak. But if you have something that is truly a crown jewel, then the way to protect it, I think, is something that requires a human being to take an action, which is very old-school, but it works right now. Agentic systems, again, are not—are not getting multiple human beings to take concurrent actions. So there were—at Unprompted, we had one of the talks from Sergey Ab... He mentioned how they observed an agentic attack, and at the time the attacker was kind of fumbling around a little bit. So to your progression that you mentioned earlier, it was in the earlier stages. And he had this really interesting observation that—so he's German, I think, and of course he was at Unprompted speaking English. And he said, if I try to speak really fast, my accent, my German accent comes out. It's very clear. If I speak really slow, it's hard to—you could still hear he's not a—a native English speaker. But the—the point that he was making is the faster that you move, the more—the louder you are in terms of your accent. And so he observed this notion that—that the nature of the tools and even the large language model that they were using became more evident as the attacker tried to move fast. Yeah, I think that's fair. You're probably right actually in that, I'm thinking at Anthropic we use so many agentic tools, it's not really a telltale sign. But if you are an entity that doesn't, there's going to be some differentiator there. So I'm like, "it's not differentiated." I'm—I'm probably a little biased there. If you are an old-school system and some actions are occurring that are beyond expected speeds, I think that is an interesting tell. It just depends on what the median speed or the median usage inside of your own company is when you're looking at whatever it is, lateral movement, like requests for actions, actions you expect an employee to take, whatever your logs look like. I think it's just—understand what your median speed is to understand if an attacker who's inside of your system who's likely using an agentic tool, if that is different. So there's like some, you know, difference comparison you're doing. I guess, I'm going to throw one more thing to you and we're going to have to get off Anthropic here in a second, otherwise it's just going to be all about you guys. So does Anthropic accidentally become its own security operations center? Because you can see all the API calls that are going across all these different companies at this point. Yeah, this is, you know, I think one of the differences, one of the other questions is: What's the difference between a—a closed source model and an open source model? A closed source model like Anthropic, and I don't want to get too controversial here, is we are seeing all of that activity. And so we have teams, like the team I'm a part of, it's a broader org called Safeguards, where we feel like we have a responsibility to build detections to stop this sort of thing, especially if we're at the cutting edge.
