Absolutely. You know, people usually think of a security operations center as trying to detect—trying to detect somebody who's trying to get into your network and then respond in some way. The entire premise of the organization I'm a part of is try to stop somebody in the first place misusing our model because it's attacking other systems that are not our own system. And then if they are getting around protections, to find it, stop it very quickly and disrupt it, and then when appropriate, conduct victim notifications. So I—I would say we do have a different responsibility because we're at the cutting edge. And the way I think about it is very similar to any team that is trying to protect against internal attacks, which is security in depth. You have—you need to jailbreak the model. That's step one. Every model can be jailbroken. After you jailbreak the model, then you get into account abuse protections. That's pretty standard. Then you get into what I think is unique that our team is doing, building things like not just classifiers but probes, which look at how the model, literally how the neurons in the model are reacting so we don't have to spend a bunch of compute looking at all the prompts and completions. And then we have other good old-fashioned things like YARA rules, which is kind of funny that we run on prompts and completions of usage, and then other indicators to try to find and stop misuse. So yes, I think we do have a responsibility to almost act in that way but on the misuse side. So I'll pick up on your—if we have questions for each other. I think to your point, there are kind of three places that companies operating closed models, like closed loop system models like Anthropic can... One is the anticipatory: know your customer, we know that this is an account that's done malicious things in the past. The second is oh, in line with the behavior, the behavior looks malicious, we'll block it. The third is oh, we missed that, but afterwards as we look at it, we now see that was a malicious type of behavior, we'll block it in category one or two in the future. And you know, I was really struck when you presented your excellent presentation on the slides you had a number. You said we've blocked or we've identified this many things. So I think, do you think Anthropic moving forward will be publishing numbers of the three? Right? Here's how we did in the first, the second, the third. Because I think folks' confidence, they want to see more and more of the third move to the second and the first. And I think in many ways you are becoming a SOC for others using those models because there isn't enough transparency or frankly enough cybersecurity standards for models for people to independently assess that who are using the models. It's funny, and this is where we can learn from the way we screwed up with social media. Everything Anne just said applies to Facebook. I mean, it—it's a different application, but it's the same problem. And Facebook is freaking terrible at all of those things because they don't care. And right, can it be different with AI? And I don't know the answer. I'm not optimistic right now, but I mean I think Anne's right, it has to be different. Yeah, I think one of the answers—this is, when you're describing what you were saying, this is like transparency centers that now many social media companies have. So exactly, I went to the exact same place. I used to work at Google back in the day, so I'm—I'm very familiar with this—this concept. And maybe I'd say a few things. One is, not to make, you know, get us off the hook. It is quite hard, just because the exact same thing, especially in the cyber space, can be good or bad depending on the context of doing it. You're doing vulnerability scanning, that's not inherently bad. Many people here are security researchers that want to use models to do vulnerability scanning. So, you know, when I internally we—we think very deeply around: here's a set of things that we want to always block. Here's a set of things that are dual use that we might only sometimes block, and we're actually rolling out some KYC programs right now that are, you know, block-dependent depending on who you are. And then there's some things that are so unsure that we're probably going to let go through, and you need a tapestry of data. You need to see a number of prompts and completions over a period of time to build context in if something is malicious. So I think that's sort of our top-level framework. I don't want to pretend that we're going to get perfect at this, but I will say that I think we are taking this very seriously. And the difference between what we're doing and what maybe social media is doing—and I spent some time in the social media space—is there's a lot of interesting freedom of speech questions that came up in social media. So it wasn't just how are you—you executing on this? It was also people had very strong philosophical differences of what to do. There are interesting philosophical differences in what to block and what not to block, but it's really based on pragmatism right now. I think it's what can we detect without getting in the way of good actors versus this deep-seated freedom of speech question because everybody wants to enable good actors and good folks to do good things while trying to stop bad folks. So I think this is, all day every day there's a large org at Anthropic that thinks about exactly this and we hold ourselves accountable to sort of in your framework: how many folks are we not letting get through the door to even do something bad, and then what's our time to detect when somebody does do something bad, and then of course how severe is the badness. And we're not perfect yet, but I—I doubt we're going to get to perfect, but I—I hope we can be better than the social media world. And I think unfortunately in the end none of it's going to matter. I mean, the two things that are changing, one is that models you're running locally are almost as good, or are as good. It really depends on how much of that was marketing, how much of that was real. The Apple replication matters a lot. So much marketing bullshit, oh my God. So anything that Anthropic does, at best is temporary. Like in months, where it's not going to matter. And the other thing that's changing in the other direction is that software's becoming bespoke. We might be moving to a world where if an AI needs a spreadsheet, it doesn't buy it from Microsoft; it writes one, uses it and throws it away because if it needs it 10 minutes later, it'll just write another one. And vulnerability hunting in that world is very different. The attacker doesn't see the software. The software is ephemeral. So there's a lot of changes both on the attack and defense that are going to make this very, very different in the next few years. Yeah, and to add on to that, the notion of software being ephemeral, I would—I've actually produced software—I've asked Claude code to produce software for me that has no external dependencies, no third-party, it's all first-party code. I—I say, use the template of—yes, I actually roll my own crypto, okay, as you know, that's great to hear. But the perspective of—of telling Claude code to use OpenSSL as a template, the implementation of it as a template and rewrite it in our first-party code, gives us more diversity in the ecosystem. And this is actually why in the—the medium to long run I'm optimistic about the world of cybersecurity for this exact reason. You rebuild everything from the ground up, bespoke, Rust, not with an external dependency with some open-source software, you're going to be pretty darn secure. So, you know, there's some period of time, this intermediary time—time period of time that is where I'm most concerned and I think why we're trying to talk to the world about this. But I hope that the world fundamentally changes for many users where all of a sudden you're not using some open-source library that somebody finds a vulnerability in and then you're—you have a supply chain problem. One thing that I would like to—add to some of the challenges that we've heard is the fact that how we're doing this is becoming—like when you see attackers creating these harnesses, you aren't wanting to share them because well, then the attackers will learn from them. I'm going to use a very touchy word here, which is sharing sources and methods. Okay? What we're trying to do is to avoid sharing sources and methods. And in the intelligence community, that's the crown jewels. And yet in the security community, we actually do share sources and methods pretty—I mean, that's—that's why we have these summits, we're sharing sources and methods effectively. And when it comes to even the attacker harnesses—when I used to run the Red Team at Bank of America, and I would have—we would be absolutely creating our own attacker harnesses. We may use Bedrock or some place where you don't have direct visibility into what we're doing. But at the end of the day, we're creating sources and methods that we then may want to share with our—with our close colleagues. But it's a fundamental question of: are we willing to share—to what degree are we willing to share sources and methods because that is what now the new—it's not IOCs anymore, it's not this very ephemeral indicator, it's how did you actually end up with that conclusion. And—this will be the last point. We're going to pull back in the audience to this discussion here in just a second. Yeah, I'd say when we were working on GGG1002, we had this debate extensively internally. Which is there is of course as I mentioned there's legal questions too, that's one part. The other part is this: which is there is, maybe to take a very simple example, I think most folks know at this point, I'm not revealing too much, that you can look at the MD file of Claude code and you can put all sorts of instructions in there, including jailbreaking instructions. Now, we could hypothetically say, "Hey everybody, this is exactly how they jailbroke it and this is exactly the sequence of, as you mentioned, the scaffold they built around it" which was actually somewhat complicated. It wasn't just an MD file because they were building—they were testing over and over again: can I get Claude to take these sequential tool calls without breaking down, which Claude's got better at with our newer models. Do we want to reveal all of that testing they did? And we thought, "No, like what's—what's the advantage here?" We're—we're making it easier, yes, maybe for some defenders to find these folks, but I don't think there's really going to be anything that actionable there. What is incredibly actionable is copying and pasting that so you could just do it all over again. So it was something that we were thoughtful about and I don't know if we struck the exact right balance, but there is a very real balance to be struck there. And I was just curious to your question: like what drove our sharing of IOCs was a goal to say an attack technique should be used once, infrastructure should be used once, and ideally if we share fast enough, you put some pressure on the attacker to have to rebuild infrastructure, rebuild capability. So is the difference now to your point about sharing sources and methods because of a concern that now the attacker is operating with the same infrastructure? Because if so, I would—if that's what I'm trying to understand is what's the distinction? That the sources and methods can now include, hey, you know what? Wipe out my entire infrastructure, start over, and have that all automated. So the IOCs are no longer as relevant. The sources and methods are entirely what's relevant. No, agreed. But what I'm trying to understand is the drivers of what drove cross-industry IOC sharing for years was really a sense that better together, if we share what we learn, well the same technique won't be able to be repeated. I would think that would be the same. So I'm trying to understand, what do you think is different about this era that would mean we wouldn't just take our prior sharing initiatives and just move them forward? Go ahead. I—I would jump in on this because right now Anthropic can see certain things because they are using AI—the API to connect back in using Claude code. If that is divulged and how they're exactly doing that, then they're going to shift to local models and we may—that may become invisible to us again. So some of these, even though you're right, we could shut down some of the infrastructure, it also is how we're currently detecting it and maybe the only way. Exactly. I was going to say the difference here is what is the infrastructure that's being used. If the infrastructure that's being used is an API call to Anthropic, us sharing that with somebody else, you can't do anything with it. An IP address is more—it's shared infra that everybody can do something with. You just can't do much because it's our infra that's fundamentally being used. So if we're revealing how we are finding it, you know, that's bad for us, makes it harder for us, or to your point you move to the—the open source model and they're by definition not going to do anything. I mean this speaks to that the advantage is going to go to whoever is sharing better. I mean, if—if the attackers have to reinvent their techniques again and again, that puts them at a disadvantage. If the defenders we can pool our knowledge to defend, that's advantage to us. So I think we're going to watch how sharing goes for attackers and defenders. Although I would say, just one thought, it also shows the difference between an open and a closed system. We had to share IOCs because we were all operating on the same core infrastructure we didn't control, telecom, right? And if the models now control that infrastructure, in an ideal world, they should be acting on it, so there's less of a need for independent entities to operate independently off that same... That is exactly the difference. Now I think it is key to share with OpenAI, with Google, with whoever else because you're just going to repeat these means and methods on those pieces of infrastructure. And this feels like spam, right? The more as—as the companies started sharing spam techniques, the defense got a lot better. Yeah, exactly. I think that is a very good comparison. All right, this has been an amazing conversation. Exactly the way I wanted this to roll too. But then we have a ton of questions up here. All right, so I'll ask you guys, do you want to do the integrity question or do you want to do the job market? Don't do that, it takes too long. Pick one. Oh, you're in charge, you do it. All right, I'm just going to ask you guys. well, just bumped up to the top: AI is reshaping the job market. How do you see this playing out within cybersecurity? Nobody knows. Next question. No, I was just going to say, I think cyber defenders have had a really tough job. Easier to attack: you need to find one way in, they were defending space they often didn't control. And in many organizations, let's be frank, the business operations didn't allow defenders to do the work they needed to do fast enough. So I think in—in that way, the greater threat, the speed with which the threat moves and the degree to which LLMs help make parts of the cyber mission far easier and more tractable, I actually think there'll be more of and a much more fulfilling role for cyber defenders. Can you tell I'm glass half-full little bit? We'll be the optimist. You know, I've been thinking about this question a lot because I manage a team and we use Claude a lot. So I'm like, okay, what does my team do? Like that is what I'm thinking. The people I'm hiring, what is the skill set I need and what's the skill set I'm going to need in six months and a year? And it—it—it's the thing that's actually stressing me out and keeping me up at night as I'm thinking about building out and hiring my team: how do I hire well and how do I make sure people who have jobs transition to what they need to do in the future. And I think there's a—a short-term view I see, which is there's a lot of last mile problems that LLMs currently can't finish. So the advantage of people right now is practically finishing last mile problems. That's like almost my framing for people on my team. you have people who are more junior that can just use the LLM or the LLM at this point can frankly do much of it itself. We have something called Claude Investigator we leverage internally. But it can't finish the last mile problems. Okay, now eventually the last mile problems are going to be solved. I don't know, maybe six months, maybe a year, maybe a year and a half, what then? That's where it gets very interesting and then I think it's—it's—you're probably becoming just a director of your agents. And fundamentally all these like investigative taste is something that I care a lot about: how do you know how to pivot and when to dependently pivot? Well, if we build RL models based off of investigations, you're going to have models that are very good at investigating and all of a sudden investigative taste becomes less important. Maybe what's more important is you have some like top-level domain expertise of cyber or in our org biology, chemistry, and you can direct your agents to go in the right area because what you're really doing is optimizing token usage in a thoughtful way. That might be where the world goes to, but I—I think it will shift. I don't know how it's going to shift, but I will tell you right now for my team, it is shifting in real time and I am stressing out about what this will look like in a year. I have a view. When's the last time you hired an entry-level person? That's a great question. I do not have any entry-level folks on my team because they can't solve last mile problems. So the way that I've looked at the problem space, like how do we as humans stay relevant—one of the ways I divide, I think about the nature of what our job is, there are two—there are two words that we oftentimes mix up. The word verification and the word validation. Verification and validation. Verification is: did I build it right? Validation is: did I build the right thing? When it comes to did I build it right, we now have systems that will build things right, we even have ways to verify that yes, it was built right. But it is innately a human thing to ask the question and answer the question: Did I build the right thing? And in the context of being able to answer that question, that largely comes with experience. So it's really hard to find junior-level folks that can answer the question: Is this the right thing to build? Which is why I think if you are a junior-level person, you should go build. Build as much as you possibly can. You have tools that enable you to build and you can build things for throwing away, absolute—just experiment and build things. Because as you build things, you understand: Was this the right thing to build? Not just "did it build it right," but "is this the right thing to build?" I'm—I'm going to recommend an essay for you all. So Daniel Miessler wrote a really good essay on an AI-centric organization and what it will look like. And he talks about where AI will replace humans, how it will. It's pretty easy to Google. If there's some way I can send a link to and get it out, I will. I—I think he has the most intelligent take on this question, and it's really worth reading his piece. I mean something, I thought about a lot here at the SANS Institute because we're asked this question all the time, even in our own workforce study. And so I'm going to kind of postulate this out to you all: Is the cybersecurity community kind of under the hypothesis that you need to start at the SOC junior level and kind of progress your way up? Or is it migrating over to the lawyers and doctors and highly skilled professions in which we do need to craft ways that they could skip that, moving into a high-level profession like a 27-year-old physician? What might that look like? Or is that impossible with cybersecurity? I mean, given where we're going, there's this assumption "mailroom to CEO" is the way you progress. Do we need to break that? I mean, this is—I think every apprentice-based profession is going to struggle with this. Like, you know, if nobody hires junior lawyers, where do the senior partners come from? So it's lawyers, doctors, architects, psychologists, financial investors, and—and, you know, cybersecurity professionals. I—there's—we don't have a good answer. I mean, because apprenticeship is how we've done it since we—since we started being human. And I don't know what is going to replace that. We need to figure that out. Like, you need to figure out how to hire entry-level people or in five years you're going to have a problem. And it—it—it's an unsolvable problem right now, so we need to—we need to do something. Well, I think it's a question of—whether they're entry-level or not, ultimately we're looking for a set of skills that they come in with. Now, if the skills that you come in with—which unfortunately our education system doesn't necessarily equip you with—is if the skills that you're looking for an AI company is you actually know how to manage a team of agents. If they are out of high school and they know how to manage a large team of agents, I would argue that they're—they're the kind of person that you actually would want to hire, that you would actually consider hiring despite the fact that they have zero years of actual corporate experience. Wait, so what I'm—I think that's like half the battle, and then the other half the battle is how—knowing how to direct this swarm of agents thoughtfully. And that requires some level of domain expertise and what I don't know is how you build that domain expertise. How do you know what really matters in the cyber space, what's a nothing-burger, what's something that is of interest? Same by the way for other spaces that I care about. Traditionally, it's this apprenticeship model: you work your way up, you're in the weeds, that's how you build human intuition and the skills so then you can direct, in this case, your agents. That—I'm like, how do you do that skill acquisition? I think I understand the skill acquisition on the AI side, I don't understand the skill acquisition on the domain side. I also want to point out what Sounil just said is that's his recommendation today. In three months, that actually might change to a different skill that you don't need. This is, you know, again it's hard. I'm sitting there thinking as a dad of 14-year-old twins. I'm like, oh, I have four years to get them to be able to manage 10 agents. Like no, that'd be in three months, you're going to want to hire them. But again, I—I mean, this is the problem with this. It's like our challenge as—as leaders is how do we recommend even to the high schoolers all the way to someone who is, you know, mid-50s, what skills you potentially need in the future and what's going to be able to drive that. It's crazy. I mean, this is incredibly disruptive technology in—in so many ways. I mean, we're seeing Google summarize webpages with AI to give to people who do search. Well, if they keep doing that, nobody's writing new webpages. I mean, they're going to kill their data source by not letting people go to the data source. And all over AI, we're seeing this dynamic play out. It's going to be incredibly disruptive in so many ways that I don't think we can even predict. All right, I'm going to throw the next question in there. Bruce and Sounil talked about two different angles around integrity. Most firms aren't thinking strategically about integrity yet, let alone budgeting for it. So what's next? I mean, I think they are, they're just not using the term. When they say, you know, "we need the AI not to hallucinate," that's an integrity question. And when they say "we need the driverless car like not to crash because someone put stickers on the road," that's an integrity question. They're not using the term, but I think they are. I think all of our AI problems right now are basically integrity problems. I want the one word because then we can start thinking about it holistically. But I think they are thinking about it, and I think us as an industry need—need to solve it. And—and there's—there's a lot, I mean, I was at RSA a couple months ago, there's a lot of companies on the show floor that are doing really cool things. So hopefully we're going to see some answers. I think that—the problem is fundamentally actually a process problem. And when we think about budgeting, we know how to budget for technology, we know how to budget for people, but we don't know how to budget for process. And fundamentally what we see is, what I made an argument about is: imagine your organization 10 times larger. You need to change your processes. You need to change your corporate workflows. How the—you need to do a reorg of your organization as it stands today with the number of employees you have today, but imagine now what that reorg looks like with a—a 10 times that number of people. And what you're doing is fundamentally changing your processes. You're making an investment in process. Agentic AI is not an investment in technology, it's not an investment in people. It's an investment of how you rethink your processes so that you can grow to handling what will be an organization with more parts that don't have individual integrity, but you need to make sure that the system as a whole is—is something that you know you can trust. And this may be—I may be wrong here to—to say this, but you feel that gives startups a clear advantage because they can org directly in this process now rather than trying to convince a bunch of executives and leaders this is what's going to happen if you don't reorg in the future? I—the answer is sorta. If you have a startup leader that knows how to run a larger organization because they've been part of a larger organization, they are already at an advantage because they have the experience of running of being part of a larger organization, they know what it looks like. But most of us again don't really know, we're looking into this deep void where we don't really know what it looks like to run an organization 10 times your current size. yet, again, there are templates already, we just most of us are just unfamiliar with it. But once we recognize that, "Oh, it just really looks like this" and we see an organization that looks like that and how they've reorged, we have an opportunity to learn from that and say, "Okay, how do I change my organization, my processes? How do I incorporate new governance functions and processes to ensure that my—that despite the fact that I've introduced that many more agents into my environment, that I can still have an—an integrous system as well." All right, it wouldn't be an AI adventure anywhere without the Terminator question. we're going to ask this question: Do you anticipate a future where rogue self-replicating AI will function as a threat actor with or without a human behind it? An AI worm effectively. Yes. Yes. I mean, this is—Anthropic exists to try to stop this, so yes. I mean, so it's a question—this is a question about autonomy. Like, how much autonomy will these systems get and you know, will we have a system where I can give it some instructions, push a button and say, you know, "Go to town"? And the answer is yes. And you know, will they escape people's control? I mean, that's no different than me like forgetting the password and how to turn it off, so yes. And you know, will they exist in—in our environment autonomously? You know, that's—that's harder to imagine. And so I think there are going to be barriers, but the more these systems can do autonomously, the less—you know, we're going to have direct control. Now, I do want there still to be liability flowing back to me. I mean, like you turned the thing on and what it does is your problem, right? So I think we're going to need that kind of legal protection. But these systems are, you know, pretty autonomous right now. And one way I think about this question is the time scale we're talking about where agents, there's a meter I think it is, has a—a graph that a lot of folks look at, which is how long can an agent or an AI system do tasks. Like it would take a human a week, a day, a week, two weeks, a month, three months to complete the task. Well, that graph is going up exponentially. So you're going to have AI systems that are running without breaking while understanding context doing very complex things that would take a human being months to do. They'd probably do it faster, but might still take them weeks. And when you have that kind of a long-running task that's taken, yeah, a human might initially direct it, but as that goes longer and longer and longer without the human being paying any attention to what's going on, that agent can start doing things that maybe the human didn't expect. Maybe the instruction following is not perfect. And I think that's like a very realistic scenario where there's going to be unexpected, and it might not be AI goes and does something Terminator-like. It might be AI goes and sells all of this asset that doesn't really make any sense for the human being to sell. All of a sudden, you know, maybe you're selling, I don't know, it could be oil, it could be some sort of good that you're shocked is being sold. You're like, "Oh, that doesn't make sense, that happened three weeks after I asked it to do this and how did the AI agent all of a sudden arrive at that conclusion of the thing to do?" So I think instruction following matters a lot if you're an AI company building these models. And then I hope whoever is directing that, you're probably going to have to think over—we're going to have to think of at what point in time does a human being need to look at the thing and say, "Oh, this is actually doing what I expected it to do, it didn't just go completely off rails." You know, I was struck that just about a decade ago, we had one of our early experiences with an uncontrolled worm during Russia's invasion of Ukraine. and we saw the impact when Russian capabilities got out, it impacted a Danish shipping company, hundreds of millions, billions of dollars actually in damages around the world. So I think we learned a lot in terms of principles around segmentation, sandboxing, and certainly in this case, whether it's controls at the LLM side or whether it's controls in how models can access highly sensitive data or frankly physical capability, physical AI. I think that's where building in those controls will really be needed because we know that this is possible. We've seen it even before the massive advances of LLMs. Let me add something philosophical. Is an essay I wrote way back in 2021 called "The Coming AI Hackers". One of the things I talk about is that—in wants and desires, in human speech are always under-specified. You cannot give all of the details. Like if I ask you for a cup of coffee, I mean, you're not going to rip it out of someone's hand and give it to me. But AIs can do stuff like that. They will think out of the box because they don't have conception of the box. This is the problem of Aladdin's genie. Right? Whatever you wish for, the genie will be able to grant it in a way you wish the genie hadn't. And AI is going to suffer from this. You'll ask—you'll ask a task and you won't specify like "and don't kill anybody in the process" because you don't have to specify that to humans. Or—or—or whatever, like whatever thing, it will come up with something that you haven't thought of. And you cannot specify all of the things not to do because language is incomplete that way. It's a really interesting way to think about it, and I wrote about this back then. I—I love—I got very excited because I thought, one, that's an incredibly thoughtful way of framing the problem right now, which is why I think two things came to my mind. One is I think you need to have, as much as people say we argue for regulatory capture, personal opinion, this is why you probably need to have some alignment within the model that stops it from going and killing somebody. So even if the human is super off-base and they—the model thinks that's the right thing to accomplish a goal to be helpful, it's still not going to do it. That's thing one. Thing two is you're not going to cover every edge case in your model training, which is why I think it gets very scary once you have these longer-running tasks. You will do something that's not as extreme as killing somebody but will have some very surprising negative effect because if you are just prompting an LLM right now, I bet everybody here has been prompting an LLM, it's gotten in response they didn't quite expect and they had to ask again. Well, that's a very quick turnaround time, you're waiting a few seconds or a minute. Imagine it's two to three months and then you're like, "Oh shoot, that was under-specified in some way that's unexpected. I want everything I touch to turn to gold. No wait, that was dumb." I love that. I—I'm going to throw one—one more question that's tied to this philosophical and also technical one. So I was asked about the Palantir situation, the individual lit all the fires. So they were able to get a lot of the evidence based off of his interaction with the LLM. So this walks down this path that was asked: Are the companies—and again this is tied to a couple of the questions here—what is the spy aspect of this on the general public who are using your models that are asking questions that are going on the line that says, "Hey, I could kill humans," or planning the next attack or "I'm going to burn a—you know, a—a part of a city down to the ground"? How—what is the responsibility of the Palantirs and the Anthropics and everyone else out there? And again it—it ties directly into this, you know, Terminator and there's some surveillance questions in here. Sorry, kind of all over the place but I hit like three of them at once. Scattershot. interested. So this—right, this is not new. I mean, this is the question of what do we do with super-empowered humans and how do we prevent them doing a really bad thing. This is the WMD discussion of a couple of decades ago. And there is a school of thought that as humans get so powerful that we cannot recover from them going rogue, the only solution is police-state like surveillance. That is not a great answer in my community. But the other option is if we give people the freedom, says the encryption guy, to—right—to—to do the bad thing, we have to be willing to suffer the consequences. Right? The price of freedom is a probability of crime. And as the—as the effect of crime grows, that's a higher and higher price. This is a really hard question. So having spent 15 years of my life in the intelligence community, I think we make a decision as a country, as a democracy, that the degree to which government can surveil foreigners and citizens alike is carefully controlled by law and policy, some of which is public, some of which is not. But as a country, we haven't actually put in place significant controls on surveillance—and I hesitate to use that term—on gathering of information that companies can do about individuals and what their responsibilities are to act on that. You know, so I recall a number of years ago during the height of the counter-ISIS fight, I was with a small group of government individuals. I was at Meta for a government-private sector discussion on the role of social media in detecting planning for terror attacks. And at the time there was a significant threat stream regarding a sports game overseas. I'm not remembering what it was, I think it was a World Cup game in Rome, I don't recall. But it was some sports game in Europe and we were all significantly concerned because all the elements of what makes it hard to detect a terror attack were there. You know, a large announced venue, large numbers of people where it's going to be really hard—you need to get people in quickly to really check. there was a significant presence of individuals with leanings towards—towards ISIS already in Europe, there was significant radicalization. And we knew that they were—the individuals planning were actually using US social media tools because they're free, they're ubiquitous and they work. And you know, during the—and we were having these discussions, and during the meeting we broke for a few minutes for restroom breaks, and as we walked out, the game was playing on the screen. And I remember I had like a pit in my stomach. I was so stressed out because I knew we had amazing teams working in countries around the world, but I also knew how much we were hamstrung. And as we came back into the room, I was struck that none of my private sector colleagues felt the same tension even though the threat was likely playing out on their platform. And—and I think that's a conversation we need to have because it's at the root of the comment Bruce made. We want a constrained intelligence community, we want a constrained law enforcement community because we live in a democracy. What brought my family here and probably many families here were those freedoms. And yet on the other hand, when these platforms potentially enable these kinds of activities, what is the responsibility of the platforms as partners, and then how do you ensure that there's careful, careful governance of that akin to what you have on the government to make sure that's only done with care and protection of civil liberties. Fully agree. I—I mean, you could go on and take the top two questions off because I think we got those. This goes directly into the regulation one, which is okay, so Anthropic, OpenAI, and Google, they all see a lot of these queries. Now, if they as a company like electively say, "Hey, I'm going to start reporting to law enforcement," there's a liability concern that's going to happen there. So is the solution that government comes in and gives them a little bit of an out saying you should report, you know, someone who's trying to plan, you know, whatever, you know, that is really bad. But you know, my question is again it's—it's literally down in the weeds of their policies which state that if it's bad enough, it's going to be forwarded, a human reviews it, and—and law enforcement will be told. But is it easier if regulation comes in and kind of blesses that? I mean, it—it is, but remember false alarms kill you here. So I—I've been—I've been at the University of Toronto for the past year and they've been struggling about the OpenAI query that—the killer, I forget which town, in right in Western Canada, and OpenAI should have told the government. Well, we don't know that yet. How often does that happen? If it's 10 times a day and then one in a year results in a killing, no, OpenAI should not tell the government because that's useless. So we need to know the data. We need to know how many false alarms there are, how many real attacks. We can't make that decision. And I think this is why I think a lesson about—sort of with Mythos and this—we're living in a world where for-profit companies get to make these decisions by themselves. And that is not a great world. I don't want Anthropic to be in charge of which models are released to the public. I don't want OpenAI to be in charge of what they decide to tell the police about a potential shooter who is on its platform. Right? Society needs to figure that out. And we can't figure that out without data. Like, we need to know a lot more about what's going on on these systems to make those choices. Might lead to AI swatting. Yeah, it definitely can happen. Do not do that. Has it happened? No, not that it's happened, but I mean I think this is why we have to be very thoughtful about when we are giving information to the US government or when we're not and ideally to your point, we're not making that decision. I mean, there's—I think there's a gap in regulation right now. The US—a democratically elected government's making that decision. Yeah, and I do applaud Anthropic for being a PBC, a public benefit corporation, unlike many other corporations that are just profit-maximizing. And I think that's fundamentally one of the big challenges that we just institutionally have. I think Bruce mentioned it earlier in terms of: we have a lot of these corporate entities that are really striving towards something that may not necessarily be in our best interest as humans. So it's really a more of a thoughtful question of, again, not "are we doing things right," but "are we building the right things to begin with?" And we just don't pay enough attention to that because our current corporate structures aren't geared towards asking that question because all we're trying to do is profit maximize. This has been amazing. The—we're heading out for lunch here right in a few minutes, but these conversations that we're having up here are the same conversations that I really want everyone in here to also have yourselves. It's not that we're up here and we're the experts, trust me. If I go down the line here and say, "Do you feel you have all these questions answered?", no. That's why we're up here trying to have these discussions. Your input into this, your thoughts behind the scenes, that helps drive what—what drives this—these conversations forward. So big round of applause for our keynotes from this morning.

What to Expect from the Trump-Xi Summit: A Conversation with Susan Thornton

Stanford APARC(10.6K)·33:52·198 views·2026-05-07
Susan ThorntonSenior Fellow, Stanford APARC Donald TrumpPresident of the United States Xi JinpingPresident of China
TL;DRThe upcoming May 2026 Trump-Xi summit in Beijing aims to stabilize bilateral relations amidst global volatility, though observers should expect symbolic de-escalation and leader-to-leader communication rather than a "grand bargain" or major substantive breakthroughs.

TL;DR

The upcoming May 2026 Trump-Xi summit in Beijing aims to stabilize bilateral relations amidst global volatility, though observers should expect symbolic de-escalation and leader-to-leader communication rather than a "grand bargain" or major substantive breakthroughs.

The May 2026 Summit and Diplomatic Outlook

  • Summit Context: Originally scheduled for earlier in the year, the Beijing meeting was postponed due to the war in Iran. Despite the delay, President Trump remains dedicated to the visit, recently announcing that his son will join the delegation.
  • Frequency of Interaction: While the original 2026 goal was four leader-to-leader meetings, Susan Thornton anticipates three will actually occur, likely on the margins of multilateral summits in September and November/December.
  • Low Expectations for "Grand Bargains":
      • Symbolism over Substance: Significant deliverables are unlikely. China has expressed little interest in a "G2" or grand bargain, specifically regarding Taiwan.
      • Stabilization: The primary value of the meeting is providing "guardrails" to prevent the relationship from deteriorating further.
  • U.S. Ally Concerns: Allies remain nervous about Trump’s unpredictability and the potential for him to "give away" concessions unexpectedly in the room with Xi Jinping.

Impact of Global Crises in Iran and Venezuela

  • China’s Strategic Surprise: Beijing was reportedly surprised by U.S. interventions in Iran and Venezuela, as they contradicted Trump’s stated campaign rhetoric against "forever wars."
  • Avoidance of Mediation: China has positioned itself as a defender of international law but has not stepped up as a mediator. They have offered peace proposals on paper but have made no material contributions to ceasefires.
  • The "Distraction" Benefit:
      • Prioritizing the Bilateral: China opted not to cancel the summit as a protest signal, viewing the U.S.-China relationship as too important to derail over third-party conflicts.
      • Shifting U.S. Energy: Thornton suggests China may welcome U.S. entanglement in the Middle East, as it distracts Washington from focusing its "negative energy" and resources on China.

Economic Policy and Technological Competition

  • U.S. Negotiation Weakness:
      • Revolving Priorities: U.S. negotiators often fail because they present a long list of changing priorities, allowing Chinese counterparts to "fob them off" without making commitments.
      • Managed Trade Skepticism: Thornton is skeptical of the administration's focus on the trade deficit and "managed trade," noting that governments struggle to keep up with the complexities of a global economy.
  • Technology Strategy:
      • "Run Faster" vs. "Tripping Up": Thornton argues that the U.S. should focus on out-innovating China rather than trying to contain China’s growth through export controls, which she views as largely ineffective when applied unilaterally.
      • Narrow Controls: Export controls should be limited to a "very narrow set" of high-end, exquisite technologies (like advanced chips) and must be implemented in coordination with global allies to be effective.
      • Market Realities: Attempting to wall off the U.S. economy from Chinese products like electric vehicles (EVs) or IoT devices is "not reality," as these products are already integrated into the global market.

Regional Security and the Global Order

  • Taiwan Status:
      • Strategic Patience: China appears to have readopted a posture of patience. Intelligence assessments no longer see 2027 as a hard target for a military takeover.
      • Election Monitoring: Beijing is focused on the 2028 Taiwan election, hoping for a KMT victory to ease their concerns about the current DPP leadership.
  • South China Sea: China is re-energizing reclamation projects and pressure in areas like Antelope Reef, potentially taking advantage of the U.S. being distracted by the war in Iran.
  • A Fragmenting Global Order:
      • Leadership Vacuum: As the U.S. steps back from global leadership, no other power (including China or Brazil) appears ready to pay for "global public goods."
      • UN Reform: There is a need for a "thinner," streamlined world order. Thornton suggests even China might support a reformed, more efficient UN Human Rights Council.

Notable Quotes

"Having been involved in some trade negotiations with China over the years, I think the biggest problem for US negotiators is trying to decide what it is that we want from China, and we tend to give them a long list of revolving priorities." — Susan Thornton
"I just think we need to mostly in the tech space try to run faster than China, not be constantly trying to trip China up and looking in the rearview mirror. I don't think that's going to bode well for the long-term development of the US tech sector." — Susan Thornton
"I think they see that the US being bogged down in the Middle East is maybe... a distraction for the US away from focusing its all its negative energy on China and they may welcome that." — Susan Thornton
"It's going to be a lot harder to be a diplomat when the US isn't just the 900-pound gorilla in the room... we'll need good people." — Susan Thornton
They didn't actually solve a lot of things in Busan, but there was some stabilization of the trade relationship among other things. What we've seen since is that keeping the US-China relationship on track is much more important than having some kind of a protest signal like that. So they're not going to do that and they have welcomed the visit of President Trump. They haven't really stepped up to put themselves out as a mediator or made any proposals about things they would contribute to a peace process or a ceasefire. Having been involved in some trade negotiations with China over the years, I think the biggest problem for US negotiators is trying to decide what it is that we want from China, and we tend to give them a long list of revolving priorities. On the tech piece, it's going to be very hard for the US to contain China's technological ambitions and growth. I just think we need to mostly in the tech space try to run faster than China, not be constantly trying to trip China up and looking in the rearview mirror. I don't think that's going to bode well for the long-term development of the US tech sector. Hello and welcome to APARC Briefing where we talk about Asia-Pacific affairs and US-Asia relations. I'm your host, Kiyoteru Tsutsui, the Director of Stanford University's Shorenstein Asia-Pacific Research Center. And today we are going to discuss probably the most important bilateral relationship in the world, the one between the United States and China. A face-to-face meeting between President Trump and President Xi took place on the margins of the APEC summit last year in Busan, Korea. That was almost around half a year ago, and that established a seeming detente between the two powers, but a lot has happened since then. And here we are half a year later, all kinds of developments have cast shadows over the global order. We are anticipating President Xi and President Trump meeting in mid-May, as the meeting that was scheduled earlier got postponed on account of the war in Iran. So what do we anticipate? How do we make sense of the situation today to digest and analyze all of that is happening in the world, and especially between the US and China? We have an excellent speaker, excellent guest for that analysis, Susan Thornton. She is a Senior Fellow at Yale Law School's Paul Tsai China Center, the Director of the Forum on Asia-Pacific Security at the National Committee on American Foreign Policy, and also a Non-Resident Fellow at the Brookings Institution. She's a retired Senior US diplomat who served in the US State Department for nearly three decades. Her career culminated in a very important position in the State Department: acting Assistant Secretary of State for East Asian and Pacific Affairs, where until July 2018 she led East Asian policymaking, and that was during the first Trump administration. Since then she is observing and offering analysis as a private citizen and a leading scholar of US-China relations. Earlier today, Susan gave an excellent public talk at APARC hosted by our China Program discussing the strategic choices facing the United States and China. And in this APARC Briefing conversation, we want to draw on her expertise and unique insights having been in the room where it happened. So thank you for joining us and welcome, Susan. Great. Great to be with you, Kiyo. Great. So let's start with the state of the US-China relationship and the upcoming summit between the two presidents. So last fall's meeting you portrayed that as a rather positive development that provided some kind of guardrail for the relations to not completely go south. So six months later, things have changed in many ways outside of the US-China relations. What's your assessment in light of what happened, especially in Iran? What's your assessment of the relationship today and what do you anticipate in the upcoming summit in mid-May? Yeah, I mean it's interesting that at the Busan summit the two leaders did try to set out this schedule of upcoming interactions that they would have. And I think most people who watch US-China relations were quite encouraged by that because it has been the case traditionally in US-China relations, and probably even more so now, that leader-to-leader level communication does kind of stabilize and bring down tension and resolve some issues in the relationship usually. And so the fact that the two leaders had set up this schedule of meetings this year, and there are supposed to be I think up to four meetings this year, was encouraging. They didn't actually solve a lot of things in, in Busan, but there was some stabilization of the trade relationship among other things. Now the first meeting that was set has been postponed till now upcoming in the middle of May. It's probably not the case that they'll manage to make four meetings this year but maybe three. You know, there's some question about with everything that's going on in Iran and the sort of on-again off-again ceasefire whether Trump will manage to make this trip to Beijing in mid-May, but it seems that he's very dedicated to doing this. He has announced that his son's going to be traveling with him recently, etc. So it seems it's still on at the moment. I don't think many people think that there will be big outcomes from that meeting, but at least that it happens is significant. There's a betting market on whether this May meeting will happen or not, and I think a lot of money is on it happening. And I'm sure there's a betting market for how many times the meeting between the two would happen this year. So your bet is on three, I take it? Yeah, I'm not in the cash or any of those markets, but yeah, my guess would be three. And the last two would be on the margins of another multilateral meeting. So at least out of Xi Jinping going to Washington in September, which I gather is being planned tentatively, or the follow-on two meetings in November and December which are both multilateral meetings. At least maybe two out of those three will happen is what I would guess. So it sounds like you do not anticipate a huge sort of G2 grand bargain happening at least this year. And could you comment, a lot of people in Asia, the US allies, are a little bit worried that something like that might happen and Taiwan might be the casualty. So can you share your assessment? Sure, yeah. I mean I can understand why people might be worried about that. Trump has talked in the past about this G2 relationship that he seeks with China. You know, he's somewhat unpredictable when he's in the room for a negotiation, so they might think he's going to give away something that was unanticipated. And he does see the relationship with Xi Jinping as very important. So all of those factors would make people nervous. But I think China's made very clear that they're not that interested in a G2 or a grand bargain. They've said in private conversations that they don't expect big movement on the Taiwan issue. And I think they generally have pretty low expectations for the list of substantive outcomes, what we call the deliverables in the US government, but they do have expectations about this symbolic signal that it will send that the two are meeting and having some good conversations. And that would be a positive development for even the neighborhood, I think they would probably welcome that. Yeah, some kind of stabilization in the relationship, yeah. About Iran, and also prior to that, Venezuela. Those are two things that happened in between the last meeting between the two presidents and the next one. How do these events factor into their thinking and the foreign policy makers on both sides? What did China take away from these events? I think China has been a little bit surprised by these two signal events in US foreign policy. Mainly because I think they probably thought that part of Trump's appeal to the American electorate was that he said he was not going to be embarking on these kind of so-called forever wars, right? So I think they were surprised by that. They of course would disapprove of both these issues, I mean on both these actions. They would see them both as violations of international law, which the Chinese have really staked a claim as being the great defenders of. And so on principle they would oppose it. On substance they would oppose it because of their interest in both of these countries first and foremost, but also because they probably are thinking that these kinds of interventions are not going over the long term to make for a more peaceful and stable environment for them or anyone else. So I think it's clear they would be opposed. There was some talk about whether or not they would cancel a meeting with Trump based on this, and I think what we've seen since is that clearly they're thinking keeping the US-China relationship on track is much more important than having some kind of a protest signal like that. So they're not going to do that and they have welcomed the visit of President Trump whenever he can make it there and whenever he can travel. I think they definitely still prioritize the US relationship and trying to get it on a better track. You know, they haven't really made a lot of efforts to get themselves involved in some kind of action to resolve any of these issues. They have made, you know, put forward a four-point peace proposal on Iran. They've made various statements about Venezuela, but they haven't really stepped up to put themselves out as a mediator or made any proposals about things they would contribute to a peace process or a ceasefire. And it seems like they are kind of hanging back and waiting to see what will happen. They do I'm sure see that this action is detrimental to sort of US influence in the world, in the Gulf maybe and in the rest of the certainly global south. They see that it's potentially damaging to their long-term interest in terms of energy. But I think they see that the US being bogged down in the Middle East is maybe, in a way, it's a distraction for the US away from focusing all its negative energy on China and they may welcome that. I see. So from what you said, China's looking at Iran for instance and seeing that the US is basically scoring its own goals there and damaging itself and China's stakes, interests there are not that high so they don't have to really step in and intervene in that situation. Right. I think they're probably busy securing alternative energy supplies and things like that and making gains from sort of the loss of influence of the US in certain countries due to this development. Moving on to other issues, the economic relationships, trade and technology, export controls, all those issues have been prominent, kind of overshadowed right now by other events but you've talked a lot about how the United States should make a distinction between the trade war and technology competition. How do you see what would be your suggestion in terms of foreign policy in this domain? What should the United States be focusing on in securing advantageous trade terms, but also at the same time making sure technological developments and collaborations with China will continue on? Having been involved in some trade negotiations with China over the years, I think the biggest problem for US negotiators is focusing in on two or three enduring and major asks of the Chinese in the trade and economic sort of market opening space. We've really had a hard time trying to decide what it is that we want from China, and we tend to give them a long list of revolving priorities, which, if you're on the other side of that negotiating table, it's very easy to just fob them off and not actually commit to anything over years of negotiations. And so I think we really need to settle on what are the major items in the Chinese economy that we are either want to get a market opening for or get them to stop using certain subsidies to foster their development plans, etc., and really focus in on that and concentrate on it. It seems like the Trump administration is moving to this model of managed trade where we try to focus mainly on the trade deficit and importing less from China. And I don't think that's probably going to work out that well for us in the long run just because it's very hard for our government to keep up with the changing makeup of a complex global economy like ours and in interaction with the Chinese complex economy. So I think that's going to be difficult. Maybe they can cite a few big interactions that would sort of make a kind of comparable trade piece and show that the trade deficit in this way can be somehow sustainable, but I'm a little bit skeptical of that play. On the tech piece, I just think that it's going to be very hard for the US to contain China's technological ambitions and growth. I don't think that we're exactly competing on the same metrics. I question how it is that we're going to be able to keep China from getting technologies that are dual-use but might be useful in some military application when these things are basically economy-wide products. And so I think it's just, you know, we look at the tools we have to do this, export controls, sanctions, etc. They haven't really been very effective, mainly because in a lot of cases they're unilateral from the US, not global, not UN directed. So they're incomplete and I just think we need to mostly in the tech space try to run faster than China, not be constantly trying to trip China up and looking in the rearview mirror. I don't think that's going to bode well for the long-term sort of development of the US tech sector. You know, there are certain things obviously we can do; curtailing the export of certain very high-end exquisite chips that we don't want the Chinese to get. We ought to be able to do a good job with all of our allies to stop that very narrow set of things from moving to China. So I'm not opposed to export controls but they need to be done in a much more effective way and in a very small narrow set of cases. I see. And this is an area in which China and the United States can have a very productive collaboration. You've made the case for climate change technology, sustainability kinds of technologies in which China has in some areas even has the edge, and AI governance. I think these two countries getting together, aligning their policies, that would do a lot for not those two countries and scientists in there, but and entrepreneurs in there, but also around the world. So do you have any suggestions as to how to make that happen? Well, I think the biggest problem lies in the area of sort of cyber-connected products, right? And the biggest thing that you hear people in government raising fears about is how will China use the potential access to various products that it produces for the US market in the event of some disaster or crisis or conflict between the US and China, to kind of hobble the US, either civilian infrastructure or even individual customers' kind of products in the case of like connected EVs. This seems to me to be a problem that can't really be addressed by kind of walling off each other's economies from one another. We really need to get to a better place in the relationship where we can have more confidence in and maybe talk about in a sort of arms control way, how, you know, what kinds of commitments we're willing to make to try to wall off certain things from happening in terms of the connectivity between our two economies. But you know, it just seems to me it's not really going to be practical to say that we're not going to have Chinese EVs be available in the US market. I mean, I personally believe they're coming here. I see, you know there are people producing Chinese products here already. It's already happening. And to think that we're going to sort of try to prevent Chinese refrigerators or connected IoT type products from entering the US market from China is just not, I just think it's not reality. Now Taiwan and geopolitics in the region. So you've identified Taiwan as the single most important issue that could trigger actual kinetic warfare between Washington and Beijing, US and China. What is your assessment today of the situation in Taiwan? Do things look better if preventing kinetic warfare is the ultimate goal? What's your evaluation? Yeah, so a lot of things have been happening obviously. I think right now the Chinese are watching very carefully the domestic political situation in Taiwan. They are making a calculation about how the leadership in Taiwan is viewing US reliability and backing. And I think the Chinese have determined based on both of those things they've been watching that they can afford to wait a bit longer, see what happens. They have been pursuing this policy of integration, trying to get more economic activity going after a kind of a drop in connectivity between Taiwan and the mainland. And you know, they seem to be willing to put more effort into that even though it's not always reciprocated and the feeling is not always positive from the Taiwan side. So I think all of that on the indicates that the Chinese side has readopted a bit of patience on this issue. This was kind of backed up by the recent US intelligence threat assessment that said that they don't any longer see 2027 as the target date for a Chinese takeover of Taiwan. But, in the longer term, a question is about US deterrence in the region, what kind of hand sort of the Taiwan side has to play in dealing with China. Are they going to is China going to force them to come to some kind of political negotiation over status? I mean I think that the Chinese side in Beijing will want to be pretty sure of itself before it makes a decision to do something dramatic, either some kind of military operation or try to insist on some kind of negotiation. And so I think it's not an immediate term problem but it is a problem that is going to develop over the coming decade or so. They'll be watching very carefully the 2028 election in Taiwan to see if the DPP current ruling party can muster another term in office, which would make them very nervous. Or if the KMT or some other party can come into power that would make them feel a bit more comfortable with the near term prospects. So but, you know, I think the US has to be steady on this issue and that's been a little bit worrisome recently. I hope that the Trump administration can pull itself together and get a firmer kind of statement and assertion of our policy if there is a meeting that comes up in the coming weeks. The recent visit by the KMT head to China, very warm reception it seems. Does that move the needle in Taiwan, do you think? Mm, that's a great question. I mean there has not been the visit of a KMT head for quite a long time and the KMT in Taiwan has been wrestling with how to deal with its posture on this question. So I think it certainly gave some comfort to the leaders in Beijing. Whether it gives comfort to the leaders of the KMT in Taiwan, I think is something they're going to be working on between now and the 2028 election. I'm sure Beijing will be watching that very carefully. It's probably put the DPP in a difficult position. I mean it's hard to win consecutive terms, what would be the fourth term, yeah. That will yeah, it is hard. I mean you become responsible for whatever anybody thinks has not gone exactly right, so and even this past election, of course Lai won with a minority vote. But you know, we'll see. A lot can happen between now and 2028, right? And now to South China Sea, there's some concerning development in around Antelope Reef and in that area where China's reclamation projects have been kind of on hold or at least we didn't hear a lot about that. And now it seems like China's reenergized to get going, and what do you make of that? That is a very good question. I know that they've been really putting a lot of pressure on the Philippines. It seemed like actually recently there'd been a little bit less attention on this, possibly because the US was not as focused on that part of the world. So I think that's certainly something that they could be trying to take advantage of in this respect. But I think right now, they're just kind of moving on areas where they don't think they can get a major reaction. So I think it's interesting that that does seem to be proving to be the case that people just haven't been paying a lot of attention to some of these issues that had previously been really high profile and that's partly because of the attention that's been absorbed by events elsewhere in the world. So one final question about geopolitics, zooming out to look at the global order. We have a president here who's proud of being a sort of bully in a China shop-like approach to breaking the status quo liberal international order. But the dust will at some point settle, and I don't know if it's 2030 or 2035 or 2040 or whatever, but when that happens, what do you anticipate in the sort of new global order in 2030 or 2035? It's a very good question and this is actually the question that's being asked at international meetings around the world now, what is going to happen to the global order? I mean it's very hard to see other countries that are going to step up and take a leadership role in the global order. I mean if you talk about like Brazil, I mean they care about South America but are they really going to be doing a lot outside their region? Turkey the same. I think of China and its role in the global order as being somewhat similar. You know, they care a lot about what happens in their region; are they really going to make a lot of effort to take leadership roles on issues outside their region? I tend to doubt it. So I think what we will see is this kind of fragmentation and if the US isn't going to play a global leadership role, it's very hard to see who else might step up and do that. People talk about Europe. Mark Carney was talking about Canada but I don't think any of those countries are going to be very it's not going to be very easy for them to step up and take a leadership role. And it would be smart for the US to try to assert its continued leadership in many of these areas with the help of others that want to support certain issues. But I don't really see us stepping out on the stage of wanting to continue the global leadership that we've seen in the past. I hope that this will evolve and change and I hope that others will come forward who have certain goals and want to devote resources. That's the big question, who's going to be paying for global public goods if it's not the US? And we seem to definitely be trying to step back from some of that responsibility. I don't see China stepping up to do it but maybe if we got ourselves and others together to insist that they do, that could make a difference in some areas. I think they have contributed a bit more money to the World Health Organization for example and there may be some other things that they care about that they would be willing to take on, probably none in the security area though. I've done a lot of research in the United Nations and its human rights regime. Do you see any hope for the United Nations to regain its status and authority? I mean I hope so. The United Nations needs a lot of reform and that is really hard to get a lot of support and consensus around. I know that I have a friend in the UN in New York and they've recently cut across the board 20% of their staff, so that's a start. But you really have to go, I think as in an audit situation, organization by organization, and it's going to be difficult because a lot of those organizations have a lot of interest and support from smaller countries that don't have representation elsewhere. So it's a real fight everything at the UN and it's easy to see how it has just grown inexorably in all of these different areas. But you know, the human rights area is one where I think it could use some reform but I certainly would not advocate getting rid of it. I think even China could be convinced to support like the Human Rights Council in a maybe a more stripped-down or streamlined configuration. I think a lot of the things that the UN has evolved to do probably need to be relooked at and streamlined and stripped down. And maybe a thinner world order with the UN at the center would have a lot of appeal for countries or at least find favor with those who can afford to pay for some of the things that would be required. All right, so our final question asking for sort of career advice for people who are interested in pursuing a career in foreign service or who are in the foreign service today. You've had a remarkable impressive nearly three-decade career in the US foreign service. In the world today, especially if you are in or you want to be in the US State Department, it might be difficult to stay positive in dealing with issues that the US is facing and the world is facing. What is your advice to aspiring foreign service officers? Yeah, I do talk to an awful lot of student groups and I try to encourage all of them to get involved somehow in foreign policy if that's what they're interested in and to take the foreign service exam, which is being offered again in May for the first time in a while. I think they should take the exam and think about a career in the foreign service or in the State Department or in whatever government they hail from and in various agencies in the US government to try to have an impact. I think the State Department and probably other foreign policy agencies in the US government also need reform and I don't at all deny the need for reform. But I think the fact is that all other countries have a foreign ministry, and so we are going to need to have a ministry and embassies around the world to act as counterparts for those people even if someone thinks that that's not necessary, it's going to be demanded by others. So I think we will have a future. The sort of foreign policymaking process in the US I hope will become a bit more reality-based. And I hope that at some point I think the US body politic will demand that the US government try to lean a little bit more on the diplomacy arm of foreign policymaking as opposed to some of the other tools at our disposal. I would like to think that those are more effective and cheaper and better for the US in the long run and I think we're starting to see the proof of that in recent policymaking. And so as the global order changes and more countries are coming into the mix of the councils of politics in the world, the US will I think have to lean back toward diplomacy more. I think that will be good for us and we're going to need very good diplomats because it's going to be a lot harder to be a diplomat when the US isn't just the 900-pound gorilla in the room. So we'll need good people and I hope people will still think to serve. Great. Thank you. On that positive note, thank you Susan for sharing your wisdom. Great. Thank you. Thanks for joining us. We'll see you next time on APARC Briefing. Susan: Great. Thanks Kiyo.

FULL REMARKS: Brazil's Lula Talks U.S. Relations, Trump Meeting, Trade, Tariff & Global Order | AC1N

DRM News(825.0K)·47:51·276 views·2026-05-07
Luiz Inácio Lula da SilvaPresident, Brazil
TL;DRPresident Lula characterized his three-hour meeting with Donald Trump as a breakthrough in consolidating Brazil-US relations, highlighted by a new 30-day mandate to resolve trade disputes and a proposed joint task force to combat the financial structures of organized crime.

TL;DR

President Lula characterized his three-hour meeting with Donald Trump as a breakthrough in consolidating Brazil-US relations, highlighted by a new 30-day mandate to resolve trade disputes and a proposed joint task force to combat the financial structures of organized crime.

Trade and Economic Re-engagement

  • Commercial Rebalancing: Lula addressed Brazil’s current $14 billion trade deficit with the U.S., urging a return to the 20th-century status where the U.S. was Brazil’s primary partner.
      • 30-Day Working Group: Following disagreements over tariff levels (Brazil cites a 2.7% average while the U.S. claims higher rates on specific goods), the leaders agreed to have their respective trade ministers present a resolution proposal within 30 days.
  • Countering Chinese Hegemony: Lula noted that the U.S. lost commercial dominance in Brazil around 2008. He specifically invited U.S. firms to compete in infrastructure auctions (railroads and highways) where Chinese companies currently dominate.
  • Technology and Energy:
      • Data Centers: Brazil is open to hosting international data centers provided they produce their own energy; Lula stated Brazil will not subsidize energy for data that is sent abroad.

Strategic Minerals and Sovereignty

  • Critical Minerals Legislation: Lula highlighted the recent passage of a law treating critical minerals as a matter of national sovereignty, establishing a council under the Presidency to coordinate these resources.
  • Value-Added Processing: While inviting investment from the U.S., China, Germany, and Japan, Lula emphasized that Brazil will no longer be a "mere exporter" of raw materials.
      • Strategic Goal: Partners must invest in internal transformation and processing within Brazil to share in the wealth of "rare earths."
  • Resource Mapping: Brazil currently only has detailed knowledge of 30% of its mineral territory; the administration aims to reach 100% through these new partnerships.

Security and Organized Crime

  • A Financial Approach: Lula proposed moving away from traditional military-base models to focus on destroying the financial potential of criminal factions, which he described as "multinational corporations" embedded in politics and business.
  • Shared Responsibility:
      • Arms and Money Laundering: Lula reminded Trump that many weapons reaching Brazil originate in the U.S. and that significant money laundering occurs within American states.
      • Economic Alternatives: Argued that drug production in Latin America can only be stopped if farmers are offered viable economic alternatives and guaranteed buyers for legal crops.
  • Regional Cooperation: Brazil invited the U.S. to participate in the intelligence base in Manaus, which coordinates with all South American countries to monitor borders.

Global Diplomacy and UN Reform

  • UN Security Council Reform: Lula urged the "P5" (U.S., China, Russia, France, UK) to take responsibility for expanding the Council to include Brazil, India, Germany, Japan, and African nations.
      • He argued that the 1945 geopolitical structure is obsolete and that the current veto power prevents the UN from stopping "slaughter" in places like Palestine.
  • Conflict Mediation:
      • Iran and Cuba: Lula offered Brazil’s services as a mediator, referencing his 2010 nuclear negotiations with Iran. He noted that the U.S. signaled it does not intend to invade Cuba.
      • Venezuela: Expressed hope for a democratic resolution under the current mandate while emphasizing his preference for dialogue over "bellicose" narratives.
  • Diplomatic Visas: Lula personally delivered a list to Trump of Brazilian officials and citizens (including a 10-year-old child) whose U.S. visas remain revoked, requesting a review of these sanctions.

Personal Relationship and Sovereignty

  • Relationship Dynamics: Lula described the rapport as "love at first sight" and "chemistry," noting that a "smiling Trump is better than a frowning one."
  • Electoral Non-Interference: Lula dismissed concerns about Trump’s influence on future Brazilian elections, stating that just as he respects U.S. sovereignty, he expects the U.S. to let the Brazilian people decide their own destiny.
"I believe much more in dialogue than in war... I don't have a bellicose vocation. My vocation is for dialogue, believing in the power of the narrative and the power of persuasion." — President Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva
"The public machine is eternal. The President has an expiration date... we have a four-year mandate, things have to happen. We must establish goal plans for people to fulfill." — President Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva
