Yes, indeed the situation in the Middle East is key for Maersk and at first it might sound like a negative for Maersk because of the higher fuel costs you mentioned, higher insurance cost, maybe lower volumes. But overall we think the situation in the Middle East is positive for Maersk. Why? Because of freight rates. Freight rates are up about 20% in the last couple of months which outweigh the negatives of the higher fuel costs for now. So if the situation normalizes quickly in the Middle East in the coming weeks, this should be positive for the broader market but actually negative for freight rates and negative for Maersk and other peers in the industry at least in the short term. So very interesting. Yeah, we've seen so much volatility when it comes to container freight rates. Yes, indeed container freight rates have been very volatile recently. And I think overall if you look forward, container freight rates are very much driven by two items: supply and demand. If you look at the demand side of the equation we have higher tariffs, we have inflation, we have geopolitical risks pushing demand lower, demand for container shipping services. And on the other hand we have the supply of new ships which is up 7% in the past year alone. So we have more supply of new ships, lower demand. So a very fragile environment for container shipping rates we believe for the rest of the year. Yeah, specifically if we isolate the tariff impact, how do you see that panning out through 2026? Lower freight rates and tariffs are a big swing factor as well. Because if you look at tariffs, they are clearly negative for container shipping. If you look at the fact that they bring prices up, tariffs bring demand down for container shipping services. But if we go back to Maersk, actually Maersk is very well positioned because they're a big player in a very fragmented industry. They're number two with a 15% market share and they also have a very small exposure to the US-China route specifically which is only about 5% of their total volumes. And lastly I think Maersk is very well positioned because they have a very strong balance sheet, they have a liquidity of about $26 billion. So they're very well positioned, especially compared to smaller peers, to weather some of the storms ahead. Okay, really helpful context. Thank you so much. That's Stephane Kovatchev, senior credit analyst at Bloomberg Intelligence ahead of those results from Maersk. And we'll be speaking to the CEO Vincent Clerc on the opening trade at AM London time. So plenty to discuss with him. Lots more to come, stay with us. This is Bloomberg. We have risks both on the employment side and on the inflation side. In my understanding that risks have been shifting towards more risks on the inflation side than the employment side. In terms of monetary policy, which I assume you want to hear, my own views are that there are very plausible scenarios under which the economy would require us to keep the policy rate at its current level for some time. The productivity growth miracle is happening in an unexpected way, then it will mean lower inflation and I think it means rates could go down. The Fed's Alberto Musalem and Austin Goolsbee speaking there. Of course the big question for the Fed is when the US-Iran war will end and when it will bring the oil price down further. We've already seen Brent crossing below $110 a barrel and indeed below $100 a barrel. That's the green line here but it's just hovering above that level at the moment. That's Brent. But if we turn over to WTI crude, it's really interesting as well to look at US inventories at the moment. So despite the record exports out of the US, the stockpile of crude is still well above the same time last year. And this comes as President Trump's making this big push for drilling, which all really shows just how much of an energy superpower the US has become. Now flip the board one more time and if we stay with commodities but turn to metals, you can see as we've seen these easing tensions in the Middle East, yes you've seen both copper and gold rallying. But actually gold is still well off its January peak. Gold is the yellow line of course. But for all these charts and all the charts we use on Bloomberg TV, do have a look at them on CHRT TV GO which terminal subscribers can find on the terminal. Well let's take a look at the risk events on the docket for today. AM London time and in four minutes polls will be opening in those UK local elections. We also have at the top of the hour German factory orders. We've got the right flag for you this time unlike at the top of the show. And at AM we've got a double header when it comes to rate decisions. The Riksbank and Norges Bank are both expected to hold, but a closer call perhaps when it comes to the Norges Bank. Lots of interviews on earnings coming up in the next hour. Maersk, Vestas and Pandora. The opening trade's up next. This is Bloomberg.

Brazil's Lula Reveals Key Details of Meeting With Trump – Trade Deals, Tariffs & Cooperation | AC1N

DRM News(825.0K)·27:45·408 views·2026-05-07
Luiz Inácio Lula da SilvaPresident, Brazil
TL;DRPresident Lula and President Trump have established a high-level working group with a 30-day deadline to resolve trade and tariff disputes, while signaling a renewed strategic partnership centered on critical minerals, infrastructure investment, and global security.

TL;DR

President Lula and President Trump have established a high-level working group with a 30-day deadline to resolve trade and tariff disputes, while signaling a renewed strategic partnership centered on critical minerals, infrastructure investment, and global security.

Trade and Economic Recalibration

  • Tariff Resolution: President Lula proposed a 30-day "hammer-down" deadline for trade officials to resolve disputes regarding import taxes and tariffs, aiming to bypass bureaucratic inertia.
      • Lula noted that despite perceptions of high Brazilian taxes, the average tax on U.S. goods is 2.7%, though some specific products hit 12%.
      • Brazil currently faces a $14 billion trade deficit with the U.S. and seeks to rebalance the relationship after decades of U.S. trade hegemony being eroded by China.
  • U.S. Reinvestment: Lula urged the U.S. to return its focus to Latin American infrastructure, noting that Chinese firms often dominate international tenders for Brazilian roads and railways because the U.S. fails to participate.
  • Data Centers: Brazil is open to hosting international data centers provided the companies finance their own energy production, ensuring Brazil’s power grid is not strained for foreign data storage.

Strategic Resources and National Sovereignty

  • Critical Minerals: Lula highlighted new Brazilian legislation that treats critical minerals as a matter of national sovereignty while inviting international partnerships.
      • Brazil has only mapped 30% of its territory for minerals and intends to reach 100% via partnerships with the U.S., China, Germany, and others.
      • The goal is to move beyond raw extraction toward domestic processing and wealth creation.
  • Energy Transition: Lula positioned Brazil as a global leader in energy transition, inviting U.S. entrepreneurship to participate in green energy projects.

Organized Crime and Public Security

  • Economic Alternatives: Lula argued that military bases alone cannot stop the drug trade; the international community must provide economic alternatives for farmers to replace illicit crops with profitable, legal agriculture.
  • Global Task Force: Brazil proposed a multilateral working group to share expertise in combating organized crime.
      • Focus Areas: Lula emphasized the need to tackle the financial infrastructure of gangs, weapon smuggling (noting many arms in Brazil originate in the U.S.), and money laundering within U.S. states.
  • Domestic Security: Brazil plans to launch a comprehensive national plan against organized crime next week, focusing on reclaiming territories from criminal factions.

Global Diplomacy and Geopolitics

  • UN Reform: Lula pressed Trump on the need to reform the UN Security Council, advocating for permanent seats for nations like Brazil, India, Mexico, and representatives from Africa to reflect the "2026 geopolitics" rather than the 1945 status quo.
  • Dialogue vs. Conflict: Lula reiterated his preference for narrative and dialogue over war, specifically mentioning concerns regarding the invasion of Iran and the protracted conflict between Russia and Ukraine.
  • Bilateral Rapport: Despite past political differences, Lula described a "chemistry" and "love at first sight" relationship with Trump, emphasizing that democratic stability in the two largest Western Hemisphere nations serves as a global example.
  • Non-Interference: Lula dismissed concerns about U.S. interference in Brazilian domestic politics or the 2026 election, stating that the Brazilian people—and only the Brazilian people—decide their destiny.

Notable Quotes

"The public machine is eternal, but the president has an expiration date. We have a four-year mandate; things have to happen. We must establish goal plans so people deliver what society expects of us." — President Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva
"I told President Trump that it is important for the United States to have interest in Brazil's things again. For example, we often hold international tenders for a highway or railroad, and the U.S. doesn't participate. The Chinese do." — President Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva
"I believe much more in dialogue than in war. We know how a war begins, but no one knows how it ends. That is why talking is much cheaper and more effective." — President Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva
"The only things we do not give up are our democracy and our sovereignty. Everything else is debatable." — President Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva
A palavra ao presidente, presidente Lula para suas considerações iniciais antes das perguntas. Eu não sei se tem que fazer consideração ou se poderia entrar direto nas perguntas. Depois da fala dos cinco ministros, eu penso... essa água é minha? É sua, presidente. Eu estava tomando conta. Eu vi você bebendo. Ninguém chega perto. Não, só para esquentar a pergunta de vocês é o seguinte: eu saio daqui com a ideia de que nós demos um passo importante na consolidação da relação democrática histórica que o Brasil tem com os Estados Unidos. Eu já tinha dito na Malásia para o presidente Trump de que a boa relação entre Brasil e Estados Unidos é uma demonstração ao mundo de que as duas maiores democracias, sabe, do continente, podem efetivamente servir de exemplo para o mundo. Nós somos as duas maiores democracias do hemisfério. Nós somos duas democracias muito importantes do ponto de vista, sabe, na América Latina, outro na América do Norte. E nós já tivemos com os Estados Unidos — é importante vocês não perderem de vista — durante todo o século XX, os Estados Unidos foi o maior parceiro comercial do Brasil. Os Estados Unidos começou a perder a hegemonia a partir de 2008, se não me falha a memória, porque a China entrou no espaço para comprar coisas brasileiras, sabe, que interessavam aos chineses e que outros países não tinham capacidade de produzir tanto quanto o Brasil. E o Brasil passou a ter na China o seu principal parceiro comercial. E hoje eu disse ao presidente Trump de que é importante que os Estados Unidos volte a ter interesse nas coisas do Brasil. Por exemplo, eu disse para ele que muitas vezes nós fazemos licitações internacionais para fazer uma rodovia ou uma ferrovia, e os Estados Unidos não participa da licitação. Quem participa são os chineses. Sabe, eu disse para ele que, durante um bom tempo, tanto os Estados Unidos deixou de olhar para a América Latina, só olhava com o olhar de combate ao narcotráfico, como a União Europeia deixou de olhar para a América Latina por conta da conquista do Leste Europeu. E deixou de olhar para a África também. E agora as pessoas perceberam a importância outra vez da América Latina nesse mundo conturbado. Por isso é que nós fechamos o acordo União Europeia e Mercosul, com o EFTA, com a Cingapura. Por isso é que nós queremos fechar o acordo do Mercosul com o Canadá, com o Japão, porque isso dá uma dimensão de defesa do multilateralismo contra o unilateralismo colocado em prática pelas taxações do presidente Trump. Eu penso que essa é uma coisa interessante. A outra coisa é que nós resolvemos discutir aqueles assuntos que pareciam tabus, ou seja, a questão do crime organizado. Sabe, eu disse ao presidente, muitas, muitas vezes os Estados Unidos falava em combater o crime organizado, a questão das drogas tentando ter base militar dentro dos outros países, quando na verdade, para você fazer com que os países deixem de plantar ou fabricar aquilo que a gente chama de droga, é preciso que a gente crie alternativa econômica para esses países. Como que você vai fazer um país deixar de produzir coca se você não oferece uma alternativa de algum produto para que alguém possa plantar e ganhar dinheiro? E nós temos que incentivar o plantio de outras coisas e sermos os compradores para que as pessoas possam sobreviver. Senão, senão, enquanto houver gente necessitada de recursos e houver consumidor, não vamos parar, sabe, de ter o mundo cheio de droga por tudo quanto é lado. É importante, eu disse para ele que nós estamos dispostos a construir, sabe, um grupo de trabalho com todos os países da América do Sul, com todos os países da América Latina e quiçá com todos os países do mundo para a gente criar um grupo forte de combate ao crime organizado. Não é hegemonia de um país ou de outro querer combater o crime organizado. É uma coisa que tem que ser compartilhada com todos, e o Brasil tem expertise. O Brasil tem uma extraordinária Polícia Federal. O Brasil tem uma experiência muito, muito, sabe, no combate às drogas, no combate ao tráfico de armas, e também é importante saber que parte das armas que chega no Brasil sai dos Estados Unidos. Sabe, é importante saber que tem lavagem de dinheiro que é feito em estados americanos. Então, se a gente souber isso e colocar a verdade em torno da mesa e criar um grupo de trabalho para trabalharmos juntos, a gente pode resolver em anos aquilo que não se resolveu em séculos. Ou a gente pode resolver em décadas aquilo que não resolveu em séculos. Também a questão dos minerais críticos. Ou seja, tudo que se fala hoje é dos minerais críticos porque a China, porque a China, porque a China, porque a China, porque a China. Eu disse ao presidente Trump que nós não só fizemos uma coisa extraordinária aprovando na Câmara ontem, sabe, a lei sobre a questão dos minerais críticos, como a aprovação de um conselho sob a coordenação da Presidência da República tratando a questão dos minerais críticos como uma questão de soberania nacional. Queremos que a gente possa compartilhar o potencial do Brasil que ainda é pouco conhecido. Nós só temos o conhecimento de 30% do nosso território e agora nós temos a obrigação de termos conhecimento de 100% do território para compartilhar com quem queira fazer investimento no Brasil. Nós não temos preferência. O que nós queremos é fazer, sabe, parceria, compartilhar com as empresas americanas, chinesas, alemãs, japonesas, francesas, ou seja, quem quiser participar conosco para ajudar a gente fazer a mineração, para fazer a separação e para produzir a riqueza que essas terras raras nos oferecem, estão sendo convidados para ir no Brasil. E isso é permitido pela regulamentação que foi feita na lei aprovada ontem que deve ser aprovada hoje no Senado. Além disso, uma outra coisa importante que nós discutimos é a questão do comércio. Sempre tem uma dúvida sobre a questão do comércio. Sabe, nós dizemos para os Estados Unidos: vocês tiveram 400 e não sei quantos bilhões de superavit comercial com o Brasil nos últimos 15 anos. No último ano vocês tiveram... o Brasil teve déficit de 14 bilhões com os Estados Unidos. Então, ele sempre acha que nós cobramos muito imposto. Eu disse para ele: não, porque a média do imposto que nós cobramos de vocês é 2.7%, apenas 2.7%. Mas ele continua: "mas tem produto que é 12%". Então eu falei assim: "Ô Trump, vamos fazer o seguinte, vamos colocar um grupo de trabalho e vamos permitir que esse moço da indústria e comércio do Brasil junto com teu moço do comércio sentem em 30 dias e apresentem para nós uma proposta para a gente poder bater o martelo." Sabe, quem tiver errado vai ceder. Se a gente tiver que ceder, nós vamos ceder. Se vocês tiverem que ceder, vocês vão ter que ceder. Porque senão a gente faz reunião e depois a máquina pública... vocês sabem o meu conceito de máquina pública, a máquina pública é eterna, o presidente tem prazo de validade. Ele tem data para entrar e data para sair. Então é o seguinte: nós temos 4 anos de mandato, as coisas têm que acontecer. Então é preciso que a gente estabeleça um plano de metas. Em cada reunião, estabeleça um plano de metas para que as pessoas cumpram e para que a gente possa colocar em execução aquilo que a sociedade espera de nós. Então eu saio muito, muito satisfeito da reunião. Acho que foi uma reunião importante para o Brasil e importante para os Estados Unidos. Eu sempre acho que a fotografia vale muito e vocês perceberam que o presidente Trump rindo é melhor do que ele de cara feia. E eu fiz questão de dizer para ele rir. É pouco importante, alivia, alivia a nossa alma se a gente rir um pouco. Bem, então é isso, eu acho que o Brasil está preparado. O Brasil está preparado para discutir com qualquer país do mundo qualquer assunto. Nós não temos veto. Não tem assunto proibido. A única coisa que nós não abrimos mão é da nossa democracia e da nossa soberania. O resto é tudo discutível. E ainda brinquei com o presidente Trump: ele perguntou da Copa do Mundo, sabe, se a seleção brasileira estava boa e eu falei: olha, eu espero que você não venha, sabe, anular o visto dos jogadores brasileiros para a seleção. Por favor, não faça isso porque nós vamos vir aqui para ganhar a Copa do Mundo. É isso. O que que ele respondeu? Hein? Ele riu. Ele riu porque agora ele vai rir sempre. Ele aprendeu que rir é muito bom. Vamos então abrir para as perguntas, começando com a Isabela Menon da Folha de São Paulo. Oi presidente, boa tarde. Presidente, foram três horas de reunião e nós da imprensa não pudemos acompanhar. Acabamos de viver aqui que a impressão é positiva de todos vocês. Mas o Trump postou sobre a reunião e comentou apenas sobre comércio e tarifas. Eu queria entender se segurança pública era uma questão central para o senhor, se teve alguma sinalização positiva sobre a proposta apresentada pelo Brasil e também a possibilidade de classificar o CV e PCC como organizações terroristas, se foi descartada pelos Estados Unidos. Não foi discutido isso. O que eu queria dizer, entreguei por escrito cada assunto que eu discuti com o presidente Trump. Além dos ministros falarem, eu terminei a reunião entregando para ele cada proposta nossa escrita em inglês, em inglês que é para ele saber o que que nós queremos. Para não ter dúvida daquilo que nós queremos. E porque nós estamos levando muito a sério essa questão do combate ao crime organizado. Sabe esse negócio de dizer que o crime organizado, que as facções, sabe, no caso do Brasil, tomaram os territórios nas cidades? Nós temos que dizer para o povo brasileiro que o território de uma cidade, de bairro, é do povo, não é de crime organizado, não é de facção criminosa. Isto é o que nós temos que dizer e no mundo também. Nós criamos uma base na cidade de Manaus para combater o crime organizado, o tráfico de armas e de droga na fronteira brasileira com a participação de delegados da polícia de todos os países da América do Sul. Se os Estados Unidos quiser compartilhar e participar conosco, estará convidado. O que nós estamos propondo é o seguinte: é muito sério, é muito sério. A partir da semana que vem vamos lançar um plano de combate ao crime organizado que é para valer. Quem escapou até a semana que vem, tudo bem, mas quem não escapou não vai escapar mais. E vamos fazer algumas frentes, vocês já ouviram aqui as quatro frentes de trabalho. Uma delas é a questão financeira. Nós precisamos destruir, sabe, o potencial financeiro do crime organizado e das facções. Se a gente não destruir, eles hoje viraram em alguns casos empresas multinacionais. Eles estão em vários países. Eles estão no futebol. Eles estão na política. Estão no meio empresarial. Estão, sabe, em tudo quanto é lugar, no poder judiciário. Então o que nós queremos é dizer: para trabalhar sério o Brasil está disposto a dar exemplo de país que vai levar muito a sério isso. Vamos para a próxima pergunta da Agência France-Presse. Yes, thank you Mr. President, in English if I may. Obrigado. Muy bien. Jordi Zamora, France-Presse. Two questions if I may. First, I would like to know if you talked with President Trump about Cuba, Venezuela, the situation in other countries in the region? And second question, you have qualified President Trump as imperialist in the past, you have said and again you said here that no country can rule the world or the region on its own. I wonder if you have change of mind after meeting him personally after three hours? How do you qualify him as a leader, as a political leader? Olha, o Trump não vai mudar o jeito dele de ser por causa de uma reunião que durou três horas comigo. O que eu fiz questão de dizer para ele é o que eu penso das coisas que eu acho que pode ser feito. Eu acredito muito mais no diálogo do que na guerra. Eu acho que a invasão do Irã, ela vai causar mais prejuízo do que ele está imaginando. Mas tem várias suposições. Ele acha que a guerra já acabou. Não é o real. Mas ele acha, eu não vou, sabe, ficar brigando com ele por causa da visão que ele tem da guerra. Sabe, ele acha que na Venezuela está tudo resolvido. Eu espero que esteja, porque eu lido com a Venezuela desde 2002. Mas eu acho que a Venezuela, sabe, eu espero que a Venezuela resolva os seus problemas porque o povo venezuelano precisa, sabe, ter uma chance na vida de viver bem. E Deus queira que a Delcy consiga cumprir, sabe, com o mandato de presidenta fazendo as coisas acontecer. Eu disse para ele que eu tenho interesse em discutir qualquer assunto que ele precise discutir. Se quiser discutir comigo sobre Cuba, sobre Venezuela, sobre Irã, sobre o que ele quiser, eu estou disposto a discutir. E porque para mim é mais simples. Eu não tenho vocação belicista. Sabe, a minha vocação é de diálogo, é acreditar no poder da narrativa, acreditar no poder do convencimento. É assim que eu acho que a gente deve fazer política. Falei muito com ele sobre a questão de mudança no conselho da ONU. É preciso reformar a ONU. E que ele, Trump, Xi Jinping, Putin, Macron e o primeiro-ministro da Inglaterra são, sabe, as pessoas que têm responsabilidade de propor a mudança porque eles são os membros permanentes do conselho de segurança. Só eles podem tudo. Eles têm direito de veto. Eles têm direito de indicar secretaria-geral da ONU. Nós somos coadjuvantes. Os outros países são coadjuvantes. Então eu falei para ele: "Você poderia convidar o conselho de segurança para discutir, sabe, as guerras que estão acontecendo no mundo, como é que vai encontrar e fazer parte disso. Vocês podem fazer isso. Por que que não aumenta o conselho de segurança da ONU?" O Brasil gostaria de participar, muito tempo o Brasil briga. O México tem tamanho para isso, a Índia tem tamanho para isso, a Alemanha, o Japão, países como Egito, países como África do Sul, países como a Argélia, países como... sabe, mais países africanos. A Etiópia tem 126 milhões de habitantes, a Indonésia tem 200 milhões de habitantes. Ou seja, então o que não falta é país para ajudar a que a ONU volte a funcionar em plenitude. Se a ONU funcionasse bem, poderia acabar metade dos conflitos que você tem armado na África. Ela precisaria ter autoridade política, autoridade moral e autoridade financeira para fazer intervenção com a ordem de quem? Dos cinco membros permanentes do conselho de segurança. Só eles podem fazer isso. Só eles. O Brasil só pode gritar. O Brasil só pode fazer discurso como o Mauro fez contra a chacina na Palestina. Mas os Estados Unidos veta, a China veta, a Rússia veta. Então é preciso que eles se coloquem de acordo para a gente mudar. A geopolítica de 2026 não é a geopolítica de 1945. O mundo é outro. O mundo é outro, a comunicação é outra. Então foi dito com a maior tranquilidade para ele essas coisas. Naquilo que quiserem que o Brasil colabore, o Brasil está disposto a colaborar. Vocês estão lembrados que a guerra da Rússia e da Ucrânia era para durar três meses. Já faz quatro anos. Ninguém sabe. Todo mundo sabe como é que começa uma guerra, como termina ninguém sabe. Ninguém sabe. Por isso que eu acho que dialogar, conversar, é muito mais barato, mais eficaz, não tem vítima, não tem destruição de casa, não tem morte de criança, não tem destruição de escola. Então isso eu falei ao presidente Trump porque não é a primeira vez. Sabe, quem sabe um dia a gente vai conseguir convencer. Eu já liguei para o Xi Jinping propondo convocar o conselho de segurança, já liguei para o Putin, já liguei para o Macron. Ô gente, convide vocês, são cinco pessoas, são cinco países. Convoque uma reunião, uma teleconferência. Ninguém tem que sair da comodidade do seu gabinete. Sabe, convoque uma conferência e decida. Vamos tomar uma decisão. Vamos mudar o estatuto da ONU, vamos convocar uma assembleia especial para discutir isso e vamos criar, sabe, paz no mundo. Isso foi dito. Espero que ele tenha ouvido. Próxima pergunta de Lisandra Paraguassu da Reuters. Boa tarde presidente. Uma das questões principais nessa visita era a questão justamente do risco dos Estados Unidos imporem novas tarifas ao Brasil, especialmente com o final agora da investigação da seção 301. O que foi conversado e o senhor sai daqui avaliando qual o risco que a gente tem hoje de ter realmente novas tarifas impostas ao Brasil? Olhe para a minha fisionomia. Você acha que eu estou otimista ou pessimista? Eu estou muito otimista. Veja porque tem uma divergência entre eles e nós que ficou explicitada na reunião. O ministro dele falou uma coisa, o nosso ministro falou outra. Ora, como a gente não podia ficar debatendo o dia inteiro sobre isso, eu propus ao Trump: vamos dar 30 dias. Vamos dar 30 dias para esses companheiros resolverem o problema. E nós voltamos a conversar. E ainda falei para o Trump: "A gente não precisa fazer reunião lá ou cá, a gente pode fazer teleconferência. É mais barato. Sabe, o que nós temos que fazer é resolver esses problemas. Porque vocês têm que entender o seguinte: o problema é que muitas vezes você faz uma decisão na frente dos presidentes e quando o presidente vira, que pensa que a coisa está acontecendo, a burocracia entra em campo." Como a burocracia não tem tempo de validade, sabe, a burocracia não tem tempo de validade, ela começa e somente quando Deus quer que ela vá embora. Então nós temos que acompanhar com mais precisão, sabe, essas coisas. Nós temos interesse, muito interesse que os Estados Unidos volte a investir no Brasil. Aliás, se tem uma coisa que nós queremos fazer é isso, que mais gente vá para investir no Brasil. Nós temos quem sabe, sabe, o maior investimento em transição energética de todos os países. Temos a maior possibilidade, vamos fazer as coisas acontecer a partir do Brasil. Alguém quer fazer data center no Brasil? Tem que produzir sua própria energia. Porque nós não vamos gastar dinheiro para criar data center para mandar dados para outros países. Sabe, nós queremos dados para nós. Agora, nós temos condições de oferecer aos outros países a oportunidade de construir data center desde que eles arquem com a produção de energia. É o mínimo que a gente pode exigir. E eu acho que vai ser bom. Eu acho que vai ser bom. Eu acho... o Trump ele repete muitas vezes que ele gosta do Brasil, que ele ama o Brasil, sabe? E eu acho que é possível que ele goste do Brasil, que ele falou que tem muitos amigos no Brasil. Aliás, eu peguei uma foto dele que eu esqueci de trazer para ele. Eu peguei a foto dele com o Vicente Matheus e a Marlene Matheus no tempo que era presidente do Corinthians no... no cassino, acho que era dele, não sei se era cassino que era dele em Los Angeles que quebrou o cassino. Sabe, eu trouxe pela foto. Então ele disse que tem muitos amigos no Brasil. É bem possível que ele goste mesmo do Brasil. Ora, como nós brasileiros gostamos também dos Estados Unidos, por que não fazer para as coisas darem certo? É isso que eu quero, fazer as coisas darem certo. Eu quero que os Estados Unidos volte a ver no Brasil um parceiro importante para as suas atividades de empreendedorismo. É isso. Cleve Watson do Washington Post. Thank you for taking questions, Mr. President. Tua voz não combina com o tamanho do jornalista. Thank you for taking questions and welcome to Washington D.C. You and President Trump have publicly traded insults. He has supported your political opponents and this meeting today ended without the traditional bilateral pressing engagement. How would you characterize your relationship with President Trump going forward and has he addressed your claims that he is violating or interfering with Brazil’s national sovereignty? Olha, se ele tentou interferir nas eleições brasileiras, ele perdeu. Porque eu ganhei as eleições. Eu acho que não é uma boa política presidente de outro país ficar interferindo nas eleições de outro país. É o princípio básico para que a gente não permita a ocupação cultural, política e a soberania de outro país. Tá? Eu penso que a nossa relação com o Trump é uma relação sincera. Eu penso que desde o primeiro encontro que nós tivemos de 29 segundos em Nova York, numa reunião da Assembleia Geral da ONU, até os telefonemas que nós fizemos, até o encontro da Malásia e até esse encontro de hoje, eu acho que evoluiu muito. Eu tenho razões para acreditar que o Trump gosta do Brasil. E por isso eu quero que ele saiba que nós brasileiros temos interesse em fazer os melhores acordos com os Estados Unidos. E eu acho que sinceramente ele sabe a importância dos Estados Unidos. Eu não acredito que ele vá ter qualquer influência nas eleições brasileiras ou ter... até porque quem vota é o povo brasileiro. Sabe, e eu acho que ele vai se comportar como presidente dos Estados Unidos deixando que o povo brasileiro decida o seu destino, como eu vou deixar que o povo americano decida o destino deles. É o que vai acontecer. Sabe, a nossa relação é muito boa, mas muito boa. Eu diria uma relação que pouca gente acreditava que pudesse acontecer com tanta rapidez. Sabe aquela história: amor à primeira vista? Aquele negócio da química? É o que aconteceu. E eu espero que continue assim. E eu espero que seja assim com qualquer presidente do Brasil e qualquer presidente do mundo. Vai... vai ser muito importante essa relação. E quem vai decidir a eleição brasileira é o povo brasileiro. Eu já tenho muita experiência, sabe, de eleição no Brasil. Não acredito em interferência de quem quer que seja de fora. Vamos à próxima pergunta, Eduardo Barão da Band. Boa tarde presidente. Eu queria complementar a pergunta do colega do Washington Post porque o senhor está tendo uma relação muito próxima com ele, terceira vez, estivemos juntos na Malásia, em Nova York. Com a eleição no final do ano, o senhor chegou a tocar nesse assunto com ele? O senhor vai pedir apoio pro presidente Trump como o senhor deu lá atrás para a Kamala Harris? Tem algum receio de pressão mesmo ou apoio do presidente Trump para a oposição, para o Pablo Marçal, por exemplo? Não existe... não existe nenhuma possibilidade de eu discutir esse assunto com qualquer presidente de qualquer país do mundo. Esse é assunto brasileiro. E eles sabem disso. Eles também são presidentes. Eles também disputam eleições. Então o meu respeito ao presidente Trump é porque ele foi eleito pelo povo americano. E só pelo fato de ele ter sido eleito pelo povo americano não cabe a mim questionar. Cabe a mim levar muito a sério que ele tem mandato no povo americano para ser presidente desse país. E é assim que o Brasil trata ele, como tratamos qualquer outro presidente que foi eleito pelo povo. Vamos à próxima pergunta, Eduardo Ribas da Agência EFE. Thank you very much Mr. President. Aí já é demais querer que eu entenda inglês. Excuse me, thank you very much Mr. President. I just wanted to ask you if you...

Jerome Powell is Fueled by Pure Righteous Anger—And We LOVE IT (w/ Andrew Weissmann) | Illegal News

The Bulwark(1.7M)·67:38·163.4K views·2026-05-07
Sarah LongwellHost, The Bulwark Andrew WeissmannLegal Analyst and Former Prosecutor Jerome PowellChair, Federal Reserve
TL;DRFormer DOJ official Andrew Weissmann and Sarah Longwell analyze the Supreme Court’s rapid intervention in Louisiana redistricting and the surge of "retributive" investigations targeting the Federal Reserve, election workers, and political adversaries.
They warn that the...

TL;DR

Former DOJ official Andrew Weissmann and Sarah Longwell analyze the Supreme Court’s rapid intervention in Louisiana redistricting and the surge of "retributive" investigations targeting the Federal Reserve, election workers, and political adversaries. They warn that the erosion of judicial and institutional guardrails is creating a "chilling effect" on civil servants and the democratic process.

The Supreme Court and Louisiana Redistricting

  • Accelerated Mandate: In the wake of *Louisiana v. Callais*, the Supreme Court fast-tracked its opinion to allow the state to redraw congressional maps immediately before the primaries.
      • The Partisan Shift: Weissmann notes the irony that states previously argued against changing rules close to elections (the "Purcell Principle"), but are now racing to redistrict to favor Republican outcomes.
      • Justice Jackson’s Dissent: Justice Ketanji Brown Jackson issued a scathing dissent, arguing there was no emergency requiring the Court to bypass the normal 30-day waiting period for a mandate.
  • Judicial Overreach: Weissmann criticizes the Court for "tinkering" with the Voting Rights Act without congressional findings, essentially declaring a "post-racial era" despite evidence of ongoing racial gerrymandering and rhetoric.
      • Institutional Loss of Faith: The speakers argue the Court has turned into a "Mickey Mouse court," where principles are replaced by partisan "newspeak."

Institutional Guardrails and Court Reform

  • The "Arms Race": Longwell expresses concern that expanding the Supreme Court (the "JVL proposal") could lead to an endless cycle of political retaliation and the total loss of institutional relevance.
  • Alternative Reforms: Weissmann suggests several non-expansion remedies to restore judicial credibility:
      • Term and Age Limits: Implementing a system for gradual, predictable replacement to prevent the "happenstance" of appointments based on longevity.
      • Professionalized Nominations: Moving toward a system used in other countries where non-partisan professionals vet candidates for quality and reputability rather than political "hackery."
      • The "Luttig/Wilkinson" Model: Weissmann points to conservative jurists like Judge Michael Luttig as examples of reputable, highly qualified judges who prioritize the law over partisanship.

FISA Section 702 Reauthorization

  • Indispensable but Dangerous Tools: Weissmann, self-described as "hawkish" on intelligence, argues Section 702 is essential for national security but requires stricter oversight.
      • The "Backdoor" Loophole: Section 702 allows the government to target non-US citizens overseas, but Weissmann highlights the problem of "incidental collection" where Americans’ data is scooped up without a warrant.
      • Proposed Reforms: Advocates are pushing for a warrant requirement when the government wants to specifically access the US-person side of an intercepted communication.
  • Political Volatility: While some MAGA-aligned figures previously opposed FISA due to the Carter Page investigation, Weissmann notes that once in power, they often realize the tools are indispensable for preventing terrorist attacks.

Retributive Prosecutions and Institutional Stalemates

  • The Jerome Powell Conflict: The administration is allegedly using criminal investigations to pressure Fed Chair Jerome Powell to resign.
      • Powell’s "Fuck You" Maneuver: Powell signaled he will stay on the Fed board even after his term as Chair ends, preventing the administration from easily replacing him.
      • Congressional Pushback: Republican Senator Thom Tillis and others have warned the administration that they will not confirm a new Chair while "BS investigations" are pending.
  • Targeting Civil Rights and Election Workers:
      • SPLC Case: Weissmann highlights the "baseless" prosecution of the Southern Poverty Law Center, noting that Todd Blanche (acting Attorney General) recently had to walk back claims about the group’s activities.
      • Fulton County Subpoenas: A grand jury is seeking the names of hundreds of low-level election volunteers. Weissmann characterizes this as a "fishing expedition" designed to harass and intimidate citizens from participating in election administration.
      • James Comey: The "86-47" prosecution threat (related to t-shirts/social media posts) is framed as a clear example of vindictive prosecution that ignores First Amendment protections.

Notable Quotes

"The Supreme Court has turned itself into just a Mickey Mouse court. It’s so hard to have any sort of faith in the court and in elections when people are just playing games and changing rules mid-game just to see who can win." — Andrew Weissmann
"One of the big pictures about what is going on is the Supreme Court, on its own with no congressional findings, says, 'We think that we need to tinker with and update a congressional statute.' Who are they? They’re not in Congress." — Andrew Weissmann
"Conspiracies crop up when people just don't know how things work. The idea that 'I don't know how it works, therefore it must all be rigged' is becoming a dominant mechanism, even though these are the most investigated elections in American history." — Sarah Longwell
"We have a one branch of government that's completely asleep—the legislative—and we have a Supreme Court that can take advantage of the fact that the legislative is asleep." — Andrew Weissmann
Hello everyone and welcome to The Illegal News. I’m Sarah Longwell, publisher of The Bulwark. And once again, I’m joined by one of my all-time favorites. Andrew Weissman is back. But Andrew, Kate Shaw was on last week. I don’t know if you know Kate Shaw, but she crushed it on this podcast. I was like, "I don’t know, man, Kate... Kate." Okay, those are big shoes to fill. For people who didn’t know, Kate Shaw is fantastic in like every way. I just like walked around later being like, "Does everybody know about this woman?" She was... but you know what she didn’t do? Here’s the thing. So Kate, she was so smart, so cogent, so crisp and clear in her delivery. She explained things so well. Andrew, you're saying, "Sarah, this is what I’m hearing. I’m hearing a 'but'." Yes, there is a but. It’s a kind but, which is she didn’t have any stories. There were no stories. And so it was just incredibly clear analysis. But I do, I do think that the audience comes in part for the Andrew Weissman story of it all. And so if you’ve got anything sitting around, I’m sure we’d love to hear it. Well, as you know, I have nothing but stories. So it’s interesting. When I teach, I actually am generally really careful about war stories because there’s so much material that you have to cover. I really try and pick a war story that’s going to like elucidate and illustrate a problem because I mean, I can just, as you, you know, Sarah, you probably don’t know this about me, but I could just tell war stories like for the entire podcast. It does appear that way. You don’t have to tell me that. I think I could, I could glean. I don’t have a law degree, but I could glean. Okay. So this is just to be fair. We were about... I was about to before we started, I said, "Oh, I have this funny story because we were talking about..." Oh, this is like a perfect segue. We were talking about signing books because guess what this is. Is that your... but you got them. That’s so exciting to have. Yes. It’s so, you know, it’s really funny for people should know there’s something just so cool when the physical thing arrives. So this is coming out two weeks from yesterday. So you can still preorder. Preorder it, man, put them up, put them up high on the list, on the New York Times list. Exactly. And so Sarah and I are busy signing cards and we were... and Sarah asked me, "So what are you writing in addition to signing?" And I’m like, "I’m sorry, you’re writing something?" Cause you know, you’re signing hundreds of these things. And Sarah’s like, "Of course, of course." So now of course, I have to go back and like write something. You just signed your name. That’s not... people want... you got to give them something like a catchphrase or something. Well, maybe I could write an anecdote in each one. Yeah, you’ve got all the time in the world. Yeah, pages, pages of anecdotes. A bespoke story for everyone. So I was like, maybe I could do it with an acronym. So I could just do like, you know, like NTOB or something like that, or OBE, which is like, you know, because Washington is nothing but acronyms. Okay. So I do have two acronym stories. Hit me. And by the way, since we last talked, you thought I only had one, but you know, it’s like that old Monty Python thing where you’re like, "Wait a second. I have... wait, Sarah, I have four." Let’s just go. Let’s just let it wash over. Come on. Okay. So, it... so you know, Washington is the land of acronyms. Like nobody speaks English. It’s like every single thing is turned into an acronym. So when I was first at the FBI, Matt Olsen’s wonderful, wonderful attorney had the position of Special Counsel. Before me, I come in, he spends a week training me and the last day he’s like, "Okay, well, we’re going to go to the NSA and we’re going to talk to the DIRNSA and he’s got a question about the DNI and we’re going to be doing this with FISA," and he goes on and on and on and on. He goes, "But, but first we’re going to go to ABP." And I was like, "Matt, you know what’s amazing? In one week, I understood absolutely every acronym you used except, what was that ABP? Cause I didn’t, I don’t think we covered that." And he goes, "ABP? That’s Au Bon Pain, which is the coffee shop." And I’m like, "Really? Like you can’t even say Au Bon Pain. You like have to have an acronym." And he’s like, "Get used to it." Which is true. Like everything is. And that led to my second one, which is Janet Napolitano, who was the head of the Department of Homeland Security, DHS. Mm-hmm. She bet her folks she would give them a case of beer if anyone could brief her on a subject and not use an acronym. And she never had to pay up. Oh, cause they just couldn’t do it. Yeah. They couldn’t do it. They could not do it. And they would like screw up with like, "And then, you know, we went to DHS." Ooh, you know, "Or then we went to the FBI." Ooh. So like there’s just acronyms constantly. You know who was not a big fan of that? Robert Mueller. Robert Mueller was really a big fan of like, "Speak English." Like, "I don’t want like all of this newspeak." Well, I’ll tell you part of my journey in Washington, DC has been learning how to absorb the acronyms and nod as though I know what people are talking about, but never bother to learn what any of them mean. Well, it’s also, you know, for what we do here, which is to translate what is happening and like translating the law, translating what’s happening in politics. You know, language that where you have acronyms and specialized words is a way of excluding people and keeping them in the dark and having this sense of like, "I’ve got special knowledge." And it can really be a bad crutch and it’s very, very useful to speak English, both so everyone understands and also so like people can weigh in and understand what’s going on and you’re not sort of hiding the ball. So, you know, I really understood where Robert Mueller was coming from. I could go on a whole Robert Mueller tangent, but I’m not going to. I’m not going to because... because I do that. No, I know. It’s just... Yeah. Well, you’re the storyteller. You’re the storyteller. All right. So you worked at DOJ for more than 20 years. You were General Counsel for the FBI. You have a podcast, *Main Justice* with Mary McCord. You obviously have your book coming out, *Liar’s Kingdom*. There are a few topics I want to cover today. I want to talk about what happens since the Supreme Court's Voting Rights Act decision last week, which Kate Shaw and I covered at length and she broke down beautifully, but there's been a bunch of new stuff now as a result of that that I want to get into. I also want to talk about what's happening with reauthorizing FISA, which I think is something that not a lot of people are following, but it's important. And so I want us to talk about it today. And also just I want to get into a little bit more of the... a lot of prosecuting of Trump's enemies out there. We talked a lot about about this last week with Comey, but there's more going on. So that's what we're going to do today. Let's start with the Voting Rights Act. Last week, when Kate Shaw was here and we did the breakdown of Louisiana versus Callais, she summarized that the court basically gutted the VRA's protection for minority voters. But less than 24 hours after the decision, the Louisiana governor, Landry, announced his intention to redraw the state's district before the primaries, which was set to start this month. Like early voting was happening. Crazy. And so they suspended the primaries until the maps are redrawn. And then this past Monday, May 4th, the Supreme Court agreed to fast track its opinion until the lower courts, allowing it to take effect immediately. Which I just as a layperson, tell me if I’m wrong, but this seems like downright partisan to me to be like, "Yeah, now that we’ve made our decision, we’re going to fast track it so you guys can redraw maps before the midterms," because the redrawing of the maps right now, this arms race is all about trying to squeeze out more Republican seats. People are kind of... Kate, I think rightly focused on the racial element and how disempowering black voters, but like it's really just a race for Trump to try to figure out can he get more congressional seats squeezed out by doing redistricting before the midterms. So like tell me about what's happening here and is... is my characterization correct? Sure. So... so first let’s focus on what the states are doing. So absolutely the decision comes down and they’re like, "How can we rush through redistricting before the midterms?" What is remarkable there is that when the states wanted to slow down democratic processes, this is like several years ago, they said, "No, no, no, you can’t possibly change things just, you know, six to eight months out from an election." You know this would... there would disrupt everything. Primaries are ongoing. No way, no how. And that was the state’s position. And now that the shoe’s on the other foot, it’s like we want to race to the finish. It, it in many ways reminds me of... of what happened with the Merrick Garland versus Amy Coney Barrett nominations, which is, "You know what, we need to take our sweet time when it’s a Democrat, but as soon as Ruth Bader Ginsburg, rest her soul, dies, we’re going to race through Amy Coney Barrett." It’s just very, very partisan. Now it’s one thing for there to be Congress, that’s partisan. Congress, you know, is partisan. They’re entitled to be—not that they should always be, but that is part of their... their... their raison d'etre. And the same thing is true for states in large measure. There’s certain things that really it shouldn’t... there should be areas where it is not a partisan issue. In the area that I’m, you know, steeped in, which is either the intelligence community or... or law enforcement, that really should be a bipartisan effort. Where you hit the nail on the head, Sarah, is the Supreme Court’s reaction to all this. The normal course, what normally happens is that there is about a 30-day period where the Supreme Court waits to issue like a mandate and to send the case back down to the lower court. And the reason for that waiting period, which is really normal, is that many things can happen. There can be requests to reconsider, to change things. And so there’s a... there is like this grace period. And there’s... there would have to be a real emergency and some reason to rush. And here, Justice Jackson issues a scathing dissent to what is happening, saying that there’s no reason to rush, that this is partisan, there’s no reason not to do this at normal course. You’re basically deciding that this can be done, that what the states are doing can be done before the midterms when there’s already a Supreme Court case on this issue about essentially not being able to change elections too close to the election. So this is really sort of... it’s basically sort of a pocket veto of any challenge because they’re sort of allowing the states to get a sort of a running start. Now Justice Alito said, "Yes, we are allowing them to do that because we have found that what is going on in the states is unconstitutional." And that there are these districts that... that is, he says, and this is now the law of the land, that there’s racial gerrymandering that’s going on. And that’s the *Callais* decision, which you and Kate talked about. So from Justice Alito’s point of view, which is the majority view, it’s like, "Oh, well, we’re sending this back quickly because we want to make sure there’s no racial gerrymandering that can interfere with the midterm elections." To say it though is it’s like a George Orwell has to be rolling in his grave because it is so much... to talking about newspeak that we started with. This is war is peace, freedom is slavery. The... the idea, I said this on my podcast. To me that one of the big pictures about what is going on is the Supreme Court on its own with no congressional findings says, "We think that we need to tinker with and update a congressional statute." Like, who are they? They’re not in Congress. "We need to update it." That’s Justice Alito’s line. And why? Because basically that 15th Amendment is sort of done with. We’re... we’re... we’re in a sort of post-racial era. The... the, you know, Voting Rights Act, we... we don’t really need that anymore. They’re saying it at a time that—and this is just my view—that we have the most overtly racist president that I... that I have been alive to witness. And that starts with the Obama birther conspiracy. It goes through decrying the... the ability of a quote "Mexican judge," who by the way, was American, to the fact that black and brown immigrants are eating cats and dogs. I mean, the decrying all sorts of... of immigrants and what they are capable of and doing. I mean, it’s at a time when we have the most racist policies and language coming out of the White House to have this Supreme Court say, "Oh, we’re past all that and we need to rush through the change in state election laws before the midterms" is... I mean, to me, the Supreme Court has turned itself into just a Mickey Mouse court and it’s so hard to have any sort of faith in the court and in elections. I mean, this is sort of now going into your area, but I would assume that your polling would show people are just like, "It’s all just partisan." It’s all just, you know, people just playing games and that votes don’t... there’s no principles and it’s just everyone’s going to change the rules mid-game just to see who can win. And so I just think it while it is obviously minority communities who are hurt by this decision and by the effects that are happening now, I think we’re all hurt by just how much it... it interferes with our faith in democracy and sort of the the right to vote. Yeah. I wish I could tell you I thought voters were going to be more bothered about this. I think it remains to be seen whether specifically minority voters sort of see this as an attack on themselves and... and push back, but like the thing that really is true of so many voters is I’m sure if we talked to some Democratic groups, some high information voters in those groups might bring it up. Vast majority of voters, these are process stories that they don’t often... like can’t get their hands around. Like they can on the abortion decision, but something like this, I actually think isn't going to break through. I haven't heard it come up a ton in what we've been talking about. I do have one quick question that you and I have never talked about. So JVL and I have been having a discussion where... look, I think a lot of us in the more political realm are thinking about, "What do you do if a Democrat is elected in 2028? What do you do to remedy some of the damage that's been done here?" And JVL has put forward and written a couple of times... right, this goes to your book, too... but JVL’s put forward like the... the idea of expanding the court. And I think that my... sometimes people are like, "Sarah’s such a conservative or whatever." And a lot of it comes down to like, do I... I have some conservative instincts around change, right? Like I just am... I am loathe to change important American institutions because we’ve gotten into this arms race around the court, right? And because there's been... you know, it's funny, I... I've heard... I... when we were talking about it, some people were responding in the comments saying, "Well, this is... this is Mitch McConnell's fault. Like we have to do this. Mitch McConnell started to wage this war like that's where the tit-for-tat began." Because my... I'll say my... like immediate concern is always about unintended consequences and getting into an arms race in the court. We expand it, it continues to expand, we lose the relevance and importance of the institution. But I’m also a big believer in we’ve learned a lot during this era about things we need to solidify, things we might need to change. So I’m like certainly open to a conversation about what we change, but I am desperate actually to hear what you think about the idea of... or as conservatives would tell you, it goes back to Bork, right? Like everybody can kind of reverse engineer themselves to a place about ways that the courts have been politicized. What do you think though about the idea of expanding the court now? So I think there... there are lots of ideas and... and the one thing, I’m going to answer your question, but the one thing—and it’s not just because I wrote about this—is in one respect, that, you know, my book is... is about what to do about political lies. But I think... and... and I’m writing a piece for the *New York Times* right now that has to do with what we’re going to talk about, which is retributive prosecutions and something that Congress can do. We have got to, in this 250th anniversary of this country, rethink our... our guardrails that are not functioning and not working. And I think it is really important to have these conversations and to be open to any and all ideas and to vet them and to really not be afraid to say that this is not... we now know from Trump 1.0 and 2.0 that the systems and checks we have are not functioning. We have a one branch of government that’s completely asleep, the legislative, and we have a Supreme Court that can take advantage of the fact that the legislative is asleep, which is how I view sort of what happened in the *Callais* decision. And we have the executive doing that as well. And so to answer your question, I don’t think that the answer, ultimately, is sort of increasing the number of justices on the Supreme Court. It’s not like they know we would be doing it because the caseload is so big, and that they need just more people. That would be... if that were the argument, I’m open to that. There are other suggestions though. So, one idea is—and there... you know there was a whole commission that Joe Biden had put together with various ideas. And that was, to me, it was sort of obvious that he did that as a way to kill anything. You know, there’s an old thing in Washington, which is like if you want to sort of not have something happen, you’re saying, "Let’s get a committee with all of the voices together and all of the stakeholders, let them come up with a report." Because it’s like, you know, by the time this gets resurrected and people focus on it, it’s gone. I mean, like no one... it’s no longer the issue du jour. But one of the issues is term limits essentially or... or... either a term limit or an age limit so that you don’t have the oddity of the way that people are getting on the court is just longevity in terms of who dies or who retires. And so you could have a system of sort of gradual replacement. And that sort of deals with, depending on the election, sort of, you know, not having the situation where you have a court where just one president by happenstance has appointed, you know, the vast majority of people. Presumably that... that... that’ll help deal with that. The other is something that other countries do. I’m a big believer in like looking at other countries, which is, you know, our system for nominating judges is a political one. And we’re seeing it right now where we’re seeing nominations where these... it’s appalling that Congress would approve these judges because they’re not willing to say, "Can someone run for a third term for president?" Hmm. That’s... let’s see. Hmm. Pretty tricky question there. Mm, no, the Constitution answers that. No, they can’t. Or was there fraud in the election? I mean, these are judges. So presumably, they're going to be judges. So presumably they can base that on evidence, which is there is no evidence that there was material fraud in the 2016, 2020, 2024 election. No, there’s... let’s make it sort of out of the partisanship. No presidential election has had that in... in my lifetime. So you could have a system where the appointments process and sort of the nomination process is through professionals where you have people who are skilled and knowledgeable and you can have a system which... and I hate to say it's like, "Oh, the halcyon days," but where the people being nominated may not be people you agree with politically, but they are qualified and they are not hacks. They are people who are smart and yes, they may have views that you disagree with, but they are reputable people. And so you could have a Judge Wilkinson in the Fourth Circuit who was, you know, who issued the scathing decision about what the Trump administration's doing with Mr. Abrego Garcia. You could have Judge Luttig, also an extremely conservative jurist, but by all accounts, both of them are, you know, at the... the cream of the crop in terms of who could serve on the Supreme Court. So there are other ways to deal with the sort of what I think is sort of the gross partisanship that you see in particularly in... in certain of the justices. Yeah. I mean, that’s always to me the idea and I don’t know which is easier. I mean, part of this then is because, for me, a lot of my thinking around what reforms is also what can you get done, right? So like if it’s going to take a constitutional amendment to turn us into a place where we don’t have an Electoral College, I’m like, we can debate this guys, but like, it’s just not going to happen. So it’s like not really worth spending energy on. Agreed. And we can do... all the things that I talked about though, they can be done. I totally agree that constitutional... if you get to the point where it’s a constitutional amendment, I mean, like look, the pardon power needs to be changed. But you know, we can talk about it, but it’s not going to happen. Because, you know, the reality is... and I know JVL talks about this, the reality is like, you know, we’re not flying anytime soon. So like we can all wish that we could fly, but it’s like that is not a particularly fruitful discussion to have. And so you got to start with what within the realm of what could be done, what are the ideas out there. So let’s move on and talk about the FISA surveillance. But before we do, we’re going to do an ad from our friends at Factor. *The Illegal News* is sponsored by Factor. Factor shops, preps, cooks and delivers straight to your door, so you have more time for everything you love this spring. You can see the website for more details. Go get your Factor meals. They’re so good. Thanks, Factor. Okay, Sarah, do you have... you have no dog ads? Because my... my dog is here waiting in the wings politely. He was so good on the last ad. He does not tell anecdotes. He cuts right to the chase. He’s just... comes. He’s adorable and leaves. I actually can’t believe they haven’t been banging down our door to do more ads like that. We rocked that ad with your dog. Rocked. Yeah. If you’re listening out there, come on. Come on, more... more animal stuff. I’ve got cats. I’ve got cats. This is... we should debate that some other time, but I’m a cat person. This is not going to go over well. People get really mad at me when I just say I’m a cat person. But I’m sorry guys, lesbians like cats. It’s just... it’s like we were born this way. We can’t help it. Okay? Okay. I’m... I’m going to just move right on. Sure. Sure. You’re right. FISA reauthorization is a thing that I think frequently comes up and it’s always... I’ve always heard libertarians in my world get very exercised about FISA. But it's up for reauthorization. And there are a lot of libertarians leaning Republicans who do not want FISA to be reauthorized. But Trump really wants it reauthorized. Can you just give us the overview of FISA and what's happening with the reauthorization this time around? Yes. This is something that I'm going to give a quick shout because *Just Security* did something that's sort of a pretty deep dive. It’s probably more than maybe people want to know, but Ryan Goodman and I did a pretty lengthy video that is out on the *Just Security* website about this reauthorization. Okay. So I am going to make this intelligible, I think, but you... you're going to interrupt me when it becomes like too in the weeds. Or you’re basically like, "Don’t need to know that." I will intervene on behalf of all normal people who do not understand. Love that. Okay. So there are people on the libertarians on the right and also even MAGA people on the right and liberal civil rights oriented people on the left who all have issues with various aspects of FISA. They are intelligence tools that are used by the government, whether it’s the... the National Security Agency, the FBI, certainly the Department of Justice, to gather information to protect this country. I need to give you just a disclosure with respect... not... I am in favor of certain reforms, but I generally have the view, and I think most people who have been inside the intelligence community have this view, which is that there are certain intelligence tools that are indispensable to keeping this country safe. This is an area where I’m pretty hawkish on why we need the tools. I am very, very open to the idea of certain reforms that could be put in place to protect rights, but absolutely dead set against the idea that... that these tools do not exist. That it would... it is not a safer world. I’m going to interrupt just to say the... the... the main sticking point, if I’m... if I’m right or wrong here, you can tell me, is that FISA can often be used to do like the warrantless wiretaps or like the surveillance of Americans. Yeah. So there are two parts of FISA. There’s... there’s a part that is really... you get a warrant, you go to court, you have to establish cause, a judge signs it and it’s... it’s similar to what would happen in a normal criminal case. That’s not what we’re talking about. We’re talking about something called 702. 702 is not a threat on the life of the president. Right? It’s not 86-47. Yeah. Right, 86-47, right, which we’re supposed to separate into 86 and 47. So this is 702. So more numbers and I just want to make sure in case people are listening to this in the government that by 702 I am not meaning to threaten anyone. And I have to make this joke because it is so ridiculously preposterous in case it’s not obvious what I would think about that case. 702 is a way of embodying how the Supreme Court has interpreted the Fourth Amendment. The Fourth Amendment protects the privacy of everyone in this country. If you noticed, I said everyone in this country. That means citizens and non-citizens. Anyone in this country is protected by the Fourth Amendment. And that means that you would have to get a warrant. You’d have to get judicial process to... to do certain things. But when you are trying to surveil and intercept people overseas, not in this country, for non-US citizens overseas, the Fourth Amendment, according to our current Supreme Court and prior Supreme Court decisions, does not apply. Okay. So you can see how this becomes an issue because the court says, as long as you, the Department of Justice, are targeting non-citizens who are overseas, you don’t have to worry about privacy under the Fourth Amendment because they’re not protected. But here’s the problem. When you do an interception of somebody overseas who is a non-US citizen, there are myriad questions. How do you know that the person is still overseas and they didn’t come into the United States? Because we’re dealing with... 702 deals with thousands and thousands of people. Like I mean, it’s not... we’re talking about like one-off. This is huge, huge scooping up of data. How do you know that the person’s not a US citizen? Or a US person? Like what... what are you doing to determine that? What presumptions, what data are you looking at? And then the really big thing, Sarah, if you are talking to a person who is overseas, who is a non-US person, your information is scooped up also. And so the concern is—and it’s true—that US person data, as well as everyone else in the United States who was covered by the Fourth Amendment... and those people talk about it in terms of US persons because they think it’s more... has more political salience to say, "Look, you’re spying on Americans, you’re getting this without a warrant," but it’s just as much a problem for anyone in the United States who is protected by the Fourth Amendment, that your information is getting scooped up under this idea that you can target someone overseas. It also puts a lot of pressure on the idea of what does it mean to target because you’re not supposed to reverse target. In other words, "Oh, I really want to focus on Sarah, but let’s see... does... is Sarah talking to somebody overseas? Oh, let’s say we’re going to target that person," but what they’re really doing is then scooping up your information, but they’re doing it without getting the warrant that they would need if it was happening in the United States. So, and so one of the proposals for reauthorization is that yes, you can have these sort of 702 programs. You can get all of this. But if you want to access the conversation with Sarah... like if you look through and you see, "Oh, the person overseas is talking to someone in the United States," and the government wants to see who that is and what is happening, they need to get a second warrant. They would actually in that case, it’s a first warrant. They would need to get a warrant so that a court says, "Yes, there is probable cause, yes, there is or reasonable suspicion." There’s some reason and it’s not just the government on its own doing it. Because it starts looking like a real backdoor way and especially this is one of the lessons from Trump 2.0, which is the presumption of good faith and good judgment on the part of the government is no longer something that I think is appropriate and warranted. And frankly, as somebody who worked in the government for 21 years, I would welcome the scrutiny. It’s like, "You know what? I will do my job, I will roll up my sleeves, I will get the evidence and I will make sure that I am not violating people’s rights. And if I am right, I can go to court and I can show a court that I’m right." And it’s not a way to run a country to say, "Trust us, don’t worry, we’re not going to do that," because that... that... that can be something that’s abused in any administration. And again, trying to make it not about Trump. It’s like that is something that I think we have to learn from the period that we’re in. So I hope that sort of explains sort of what the issue is. And... and to be clear, one of the reasons that I think people in the government though they want to see 702 reauthorized because the intelligence that you get from being able to monitor what people are doing overseas, if you have seen the daily PDB, another acronym, Presidential Daily Brief, your jaw drops. At least mine did the first time I read it and the second time and the third time. And you need that ability to know what’s happening. And I think one of the key things you need is an ability to do very quick triage on what are the... cause there's so much data. What is the... what are the things that need to be looked at first? What are the things that are so concerning? Robert Mueller used to say this, which is that it’s no longer the case that we will not have something in our holdings about some terrorist activity. It’s why did we not see it? How did we not see it? Because there’s so much data. And so having the tools, not just the judgment and the experience which we need—we need those people there, god knows we don’t have them now in senior leadership—but you need the tools to be able to say, "Oh, here’s reasons why we need to look at this." And that’s one of the advocates for for 702 say that is one of the key things that it provides the government. I see. Okay. So explain to me in the political context. I know Congress did like a short term reauthorization, but Trump wants a long term reauthorization. And why? Are they using it to target their enemies here the way he does? So the debate is, some people say, "I want a straight reauthorization," meaning no changes at all. Some people are like, "It should be killed." Just to be clear, Kash Patel when he was not in the government, he and many others were like, "Oh, look what happened to Carter Page. We can’t trust the government. All of this stuff needs to be eradicated." I’m sort of somewhat of hyperbole, but they were like we are against all of this. Now that they’re inside and they see just how indispensable it is and on their watch, you don’t want to see people being killed in terrorist attacks. At least presumably that’s what they’re thinking and I have no reason to think that they wouldn’t be thinking that. They’re thinking, "Well, okay, is it straight authorization or is there... or is... and as opposed to just getting rid of it or is there some interim measure that can be used such as this idea of getting a warrant based on at least some even if it’s not probable cause, maybe even a slightly lower standard." And so that is the debate. The last time this came up, it was authorized for two years. And that’s why it’s coming up now. I actually think it is appropriate to not have a particularly long-term reauthorization because it’s useful for the government to somewhat have a leash on it, so that if there are abuses that we see and that the court identifies that the government is not thinking, "Oh, well, we’re fine for the next 10 years." It’s like, this is going to be brought up in two years, which... and to answer your question about the abuses of intelligence products, who the hell knows. Yeah. If you had to ask me what is the number one thing that I am worried about in the sort of intelligence area for abuse, it is what we learn in a next administration about what this administration was doing. An example of that is post-9/11. The Bush administration engaged in warrantless wiretapping and all sorts of programs. Now admittedly, it was right after 9/11. A lot of times pendulum swings happen. And look, did I think it was too far even at the time? Yes. But it... but... but to be clear, there... the reason for it was not to go after political adversaries. It was to keep the country safe. And so there can be obviously honest debates about how to accomplish that and... and good debates about that. Here, because it is such a powerful tool and can be misused, does anyone have confidence that a powerful tool that can be misused is not going to be misused by this administration? And because it’s all secret, that’s why what people might be saying, "Well, why don’t you know, Andrew?" And the answer is it’s all under wraps. I do not have security clearance. You do not have security clearance. None of us outside at this point in normal circumstances has security clearance to... and a... and a reason to know this information. So we do not know if there is that abuse going on. Hey, just... this is also a slight aside, but related. Kash Patel and the FBI now, there was an *Atlantic* reporter who wrote that negative piece about Kash, and now they’re trying to go after who they say leaked, like they’re doing like an investigation, they’re going after people. Is that the FBI? Are they allowed to do that? What's happening there? So I'm just trying to... I'm just trying to calm myself. Center. Okay. People say I’ve gotten this comment, which is like, "How can you be so calm?" And I usually tell people, it’s like, "Oh, that’s because you don’t see me before I was calm." Where, you know, a lot of expletives that I still don’t... I try not to use. Although I will tell you, Sarah, a little inside tip that when you hear me say, "God’s green earth..." Mm-hmm. That’s your swear? Yeah. Well, yes, because let’s not... let’s just say in real life, that’s not what I used to say. Mm-hmm. So Kash Patel has... let’s see... brought a civil case against the *Atlantic* while also overseeing a criminal case into the reporter for... I’m sorry, what? First Amendment activity? I mean, are you kidding? I mean, like just to be clear, unless they have evidence that they manufactured the story and didn’t have any sources at all, that is protected First Amendment activity. So there’s obviously a huge conflict of interest to bring a civil case and then to conduct a criminal investigation. Two, where the hell is Congress on this? Because you know what, Congress sort of stepped in to really try and protect the Federal Reserve and Powell to basically say, "You know what? You want another chair? That ain’t happening if you’re conducting this, you know, BS investigation of Jerome Powell, Janine Pirro. So like back the God’s green earth off." Yeah. Exactly. Well said, Sarah. And so where are they on this? Because this is like a reputable journalistic organization where you don’t see that happening at all. And I think I mentioned the last time we were together that again, if I were the *Atlantic*, I’d be like, "You want a trial, Kash? You want a trial? Let’s go." Yes, I loved this idea. I, I have made sure they're listening. Yeah. Andrew Weissman has some good advice. Everybody go listen to it. I’m also ripshit on this, excuse me, and for... for a slightly different group. The extent to which there was an entire chorus of free speech absolutists, including one Elon Musk. But it was like the raison d'etre, we need Trump because we need a free speech administration. The extent to which these people have gone wildly quiet with the exception of a few really good people like my friends at FIRE, the Foundation for Individual Rights and Expression. But like most of the both... most of the advocates on the right who claimed that free speech was one of the essential reasons that we needed Donald Trump have gone deadly silent in the wake of his assault on the First Amendment, of which this is just another one, but an egregious one. Yeah. Absolutely. Absolutely. I mean, look, this is... I mean, this is free speech for me, not for thee. And yeah, that... that is an... I mean, I’m not big on always saying, "Oh, this is just like every authoritarian regime in every, you know, part of the world," but this part, this idea of squashing sort of media dissent is... is straight out of an authoritarian playbook. Okay. Well, not to make a surveillance pun, this seems like something we should keep a close watch on. Just issue. Nah, it's my producer. We'll just, it's fine. All right. Let’s hear from OneSkin and then we’re going to get to our next topic, which is prosecuting Trump’s enemies. I love OneSkin, but again, I mean, it’s great, but not... I don’t think... is that a dog product? It’s not. This one’s for me and for my face. Very much not for dogs. It would be messy. This show is sponsored by OneSkin. OneSkin’s my favorite. In fact, I keep it right here by my desk all the time. I put it on because I don’t really have a great skin care routine. I just have a... when I’m sitting at my desk and I remember, I put on my OneSkin, my face cream, my eye cream, all of it. Whether you’re someone who tries every new skin care product that hits the market, or you’ve been using the same one or two things for years, some people just realize that their favorite skin care product just isn’t keeping up for them anymore. But not me, thanks to OneSkin. It doesn’t just make my existing routine better. It actually works differently than anything else I’ve tried.
