Thanks, OneSkin. All right. Thanks, OneSkin. Let’s get to the prosecution of Trump’s enemies. Obviously, we talked about Comey’s indictment last week with Kate Shaw, and the seashell gate. You and I talked about the prosecution of SPLC, which I think you characterized as equally politically motivated and baseless. But I want to talk about what’s happening to Fed Chair Jerome Powell, who you just invoked, and the Fulton County election investigation. Can I just say one thing that is an update on the Southern Poverty Law Center, SPLC? Yes. Have you followed the Todd Blanche... what should we politely call it... retraction? I haven't. He is on air and says, "What do you mean? I’m some..." not direct quotes, but, "What do you mean that the Southern Poverty Law Center was... was giving us information and giving information to the FBI or to DOJ? I mean, there’s no evidence of that." And it’s like, hmm, really? Wasn't there like a formal working agreement? So it's... there's so much... because the Southern Poverty Law Center in writing their lawyers before there was an indictment said, "What are you talking about? We were giving you information which we got through this informant program and you are actually using it." And so even if you weren’t using it, you know, we were trying to get you to use it to thwart what was happening. So that’s like... it’s the direct contrary to the charge, which is that the Southern Poverty Law Center didn’t want to thwart hate groups. They actually wanted to help thwart hate groups so they could continue fundraising. That’s the theory. And so Todd Blanche is on air saying there’s no evidence of that and then he has to go, "A) we didn’t know that this was an informant program," which is sort of like, who cares? I mean, like whether he knew it was an informant program or not is irrelevant. But he then says, "You know, whether we got some of this information is not what they’re charged with. It’s irrelevant to the case." And I was like, "No, that actually is the case." I mean, I was just like... so he did this about-face and he did it because the Southern Poverty Law Center filed a motion in court that said, "He has said X and it is not true, and so there should be a retraction." And in fact, the government had to come in and say, "Oh, well, in light of what Todd Blanche has now said, there doesn’t need to be an additional retraction because he did a 180." This is the Acting Attorney General of the United States, who is overseeing a prosecution of a revered and esteemed civil rights group, whether you agree with all of their tactics or not. So it’s sort of a remarkable turnaround. It is one more reason that I think this case is dead on arrival. And I assume you agree the like the same thing about Comey, right? Because I was just thinking about Todd Blanche. I think in that same interview—cause I did not watch the whole thing or... or if I did I didn’t clock that part—the part that I was focused on was where he was like, "Oh yeah, it’s fine." He was being asked by Kristen Welker, "So are you going to prosecute people who are making the 86-47 t-shirts and you know, all the people who are saying it online?" He was like, "Well, of course not. Why would we prosecute them?" And it’s like, "Well, I’m sorry. Are these... is this not a death threat? Is this not your case against Comey? What do you mean that it's fine if they say it?" And it seemed to me, again, not a lawyer, but I know what vindictive prosecution is and it feels like Comey’s going to have a heck of a case to say that this was vindictive prosecution if they decline it for everybody else, but only pursue it for him. So Todd Blanche was intimating that there was more evidence, that the reason that James Comey was singled out from this panoply of people who are posting 86-47 is because there’s something else there. I mean, that falls into category of "give me a break." If there was something else there, you don’t think it would be in the indictment? And by the way, this is the other thing, which is all of this—and this is going to go to the SPLC case, it’s the Comey case—is this idea of the chilling effect of bringing these cases. And here’s like a really minor example. I was last week at a place in France called Mont Saint-Michel. It is an 8th-century monastery on a rock in the sort of northwest corner, in the middle, kind of in off the shore, built on a rock in the sand. And sort of this incredible spot. And... and I’m sitting there trying to... I’m trying to not think about my book, not to plug it. Not to think about The Bulwark. Sad. I know, sad. It’s like you’re like what? Not trying to think about *Main Justice*, my podcast. I’m trying to turn off and just have a few days of... On a monastery on a rock. I’ve got it. Exactly. And what I looked down from the top of the abbey down to this... cause it’s on this rock and it’s there’s a low sand, there’s the beach goes on for really long time cause then the water comes up and it’s just surrounds the entire abbey. But before the water came up, I’m looking at the sand and what do I see in the sand? 86-47. God, you’re... you’re good. I saw that one coming from a mile away. Exactly. And so I’m thinking, "I am going to take a picture of that and send it to Mary McCord," who I do a podcast with at *Main Justice*. And I think given who I am, like this is like manna from heaven for Todd Blanche. He’s like sitting there drooling at like, you know, "Oh, there’s another person I can prosecute, yet another person besides James Comey." So I don’t do it, but I am here to tell you the anecdote. I don't know, post it on Instagram, let them come, see what happens. Exactly. But now they’re going after Jerome Powell. That’s where we are. Okay. So talk to me about about this administration now going after Jerome Powell, who you just invoked, who by the way, is like the strangest folk hero of our time. I’m basically obsessed with Jerome Powell and how he’s handling everything, but them going after him, him rolling off and being like, "And I will stay, I will keep my seat on the board. I will have a new seat on the board." Sarah, he is brilliantly handling this. I know. I love watching the cage match between him and Janine Pirro and Donald Trump. So here’s... here’s... so first they’re like, "We want you off," and so we’re going to try and pressure you off by either making you look like a fool at this press conference where you actually do the reverse and make Donald Trump look like he sort of does a gotcha and Powell says, "You got your numbers wrong." So he’s not having any of it. So then they’re like, "We’re going to open a criminal investigation," and they... and they issue these two grand jury subpoenas to which Judge Boasberg goes, "Not so fast," and issues not one but two really rock-solid opinions. But there’s more because that’s where Congress stepped in and said, "You ain’t politicizing the Federal Reserve." And so here’s the deal. Jerome Powell is sort of time-restricted in terms of when he has to step down as chair, but he is not similarly time-restricted in terms of continuing as still being on the board. So he can go from being chair to being on the board. Most people though generally go from being chair to just leaving. But they don’t have to. And so Congress says, including some Republicans like... Tillis was good on this. Excellent. Excellent. Going, "You want a chair, you got to drop this. Like ain’t going to happen while you while you’re fump-... fumpfering around with this nonsense." And so Janine Pirro, tail between her legs, has to be like, "Okay, we’re going to try and work something out and we’re not pursuing the subpoenas and we’re not going to appeal Judge Boasberg’s decision." And so it seems like there's a bit of a truce, but Jerome Powell's smart because he's like, "You can take that in a—you know, it's like a token and that gets you like, not even on the New York subway anymore." And he's like, "Nothing protects me from you turning around and just reinstituting the criminal investigation because you're those kind of people." So he's like, "I—you know what, I have another card to play. I'm going to step down when it's time to step down and then I'm going to still be on the board." Mhmm. So see ya. And because—and so Janine Pirro is still going, "Well, we're not appealing, but depending on what happens with an inspector general's report, we may or may not resuscitate the criminal case." So she's trying to be like, "Okay, I didn't totally lose, I may resuscitate," and Jerome Powell's like, "You can resuscitate or not resuscitate because I'm going to be on that board." So he's playing his card. Congress is still very much backing him up. I do suspect that the Supreme Court is going to—which has this issue coming up about how much power the president has to remove people, including on the Federal Reserve—and is likely to say when it comes to the Federal Reserve that there has to be cause. In other words, it's not—you can't just say, "You're done, you know, off with your head." There has to be a reason that you're firing somebody. And so that obviously doesn't exist with Jerome Powell. It doesn't seem to exist with Lisa Cook. And so you're seeing this sort of stalemate where I think ultimately Janine Pirro is trying to save face and not make it seem like she just instituted a BS investigation, which of course it is. Yeah. Exactly. And so—but she does—you know, what she going to do? Turn around and say, "Yeah, okay, you're right. You caught my, you know, my hand in the cookie jar." So she's trying to save face and Jerome Powell's like, "Well, you know what, then I'm not leaving because if you're going to try and say this is still pending, screw you." And can I just curse for a moment? It's a free country. Still. Almost. So this is what I have learned after—and it's to my parents', probably deep, deep regret because it's like thousands and thousands of dollars of higher education to have this be sort of the rule of thumb of like how you operate in the world where one person goes, "Fuck you," and the response is, "Fuck me? No, fuck you." It's like that. That in many ways is like... you know, years at—I went to Princeton and I went to Columbia and all these like fancy places and that's the result. And you can just—my parents have to be like, "Really? Like did we really have to like do, do all of that for, for that, you know, level of analysis?" Yeah. Well, it was worth it. It was worth every penny, Mr. and Mrs. Weissman, because it's not our fault. This is where we found ourselves in the discourse and this is how we have to deal with it. But I just want—this is just a Jerome Powell appreciation segment because he's not the hero we deserve, but he is the hero we're getting. And God bless him. All right. Last topic. And then we got to get out of here. Fulton County. Just want to do an update on the Fulton County stuff cause there's been a little bit of movement there. Fulton County continues to be this Trump bugaboo. As people know, there was a search warrant. There's litigation that is still pending about efforts by Fulton County to say that the search warrant was improvidently granted. But the latest is that the administration is trying to obtain through a grand jury subpoena all of the names of everyone who essentially—it's like anyone who ever worked and put—touched the Fulton County election process. They want like hundreds and hundreds and hundreds of names. And again, just think about the chilling effect. There's obviously no factual basis to investigate hundreds and hundreds and hundreds of people. So this is like, you know, your classic fishing expedition, which is not what a grand jury subpoena is for. A grand jury subpoena is not supposed to be to harass. That is a Supreme Court case directly on point. Judge Boasberg used it in striking down the Chairman Powell subpoenas that we were just talking about. And this is one where the chilling effect on people—we have midterms coming up. These are people who are generally volunteers, low-level people who are just good citizens and bipartisan people. These are the people you want to be doing this, who believe in their country. And you're—just imagine what you're thinking now, which is, "Yes, I want to do this," but the chilling effect is, and then your name's going to be on the list and you're going to be investigated and you may need to get a lawyer and they may accuse you of, you know, posting something that has four numerals in it and saying that constitutes a threat. Meaning, if you can make this up out of whole cloth, the chilling effect on the running of our country and people doing things out of patriotism and principle is, I think, the point. Yeah, we're not angry enough at some of this. This is the extent to which—because you're exactly right and chilling, this is like... it's like a bunch of 75-year-olds. Like the people who just, you know, do the ballot collection and poll watchers, whatever. Yeah, they're just volunteers in election administration because they want to see us have a good clean election. And this is what's so crazy about it. So these are not people who get together as a group. This is not some isolated group that's like figuring out what to do. These are people from all sorts of walks of life. That you couldn't possibly have a conspiracy. Like, it's just too many people and too many witnesses and too many poll watchers. I mean, it's just not how things work. I mean, having investigated crime for decades and defended people accused of crime for a decade, that's just not how it works. It is not possible to have that kind of conspiracy. And so you really have to ask what is the purpose here, and the purpose unfortunately is really similar to everything we are seeing in these vindictive investigations and prosecutions. I talk about conspiracies a lot because I listen to a lot of voters and voters believe in a lot of conspiracies. In fact, it's like becoming a real dominant mechanism. But like, conspiracies crop up when people just don't know how things work. Like that idea of, "I don't know how it works and therefore it must all be rigged." No, actually there's a perfectly legitimate system and anybody who's worked in election administration will tell you that there's no way to do the things that people think were done. So wait, that's a good thing. Do you do that? I mean, of having somebody on to just talk about, "Here's what I do. Here's how a poll watcher works"? Just like sort of to just help dispel the mystery and also their background. Because so many of these people, it's like, "I'm a school teacher. I'm a retired cop. I'm, you know, it's like I'm a retired librarian." And this is what, you know, this is something that I do. I mean, these are sort of people that you meet every day in your life and you would never, ever think, "Oh, these are bad people." You'd think, "Oh, what a lovely thing to be doing." We have 100% had people write stories for us, especially in the wake of 2020. We really got people who did election stuff—the regular people—to say why it was implausible that these things would happen. Republicans who were like, "No, this is not how it works." And also Stephen Richer, who was the Maricopa County supervisor in 2020, is like a good pal of mine who's been on a bunch, just to be like, "Let me tell you how it—" because he was the guy who ran the election and was like, "No, none of this is rigged. None of this—none of these conspiracies are real. Let me tell you." And he's very good at laying out exactly how it works. That is not satisfying for people who want to believe that things were rigged. Like they just—because I listen to voters in the focus group say things like, "If—like why didn't they investigate the fraud? Why didn't anybody look at the voter fraud?" And you're like, "That was the most investigated election in American history. Those things were—they recounted, they did hand recounts, like they ran that thing into the ground." And people are still out there saying, "Well, why didn't they even bother to look?" And you're like, "I don't, you know, how do you talk to people around that?" Okay, we've gone long. Good show, long show. Andrew, you're the best. I love it when you're here with me. A reminder to listeners to go get your book, Liar's Kingdom. It's on pre-sale now, hits the shelves May 19th, which is soon and so exciting. There's a 15% discount code for Bulwark listeners that we're going to drop in the show notes. Go get his book, everyone, right now. We'll see you guys next time to talk about more illegal news. I love OneSkin, but again, I mean, it's great, but not—I don't think—is that a dog product? It's not. This one's for me and for my face. Very much not for dogs.

Hallie Jackson NOW - May 6 | NBC News NOW

NBC News(11.9M)·100:22·41.6K views·2026-05-07
Hallie JacksonAnchor, NBC News Howard LutnickU.S. Commerce Secretary
TL;DRWhile Wall Street rallies on potential peace progress with Iran, a New York Fed report highlights a widening K-shaped economy driven by soaring gas prices, as significant developments unfold in public health, political corruption, and AI-generated campaign advertising.

TL;DR

While Wall Street rallies on potential peace progress with Iran, a New York Fed report highlights a widening K-shaped economy driven by soaring gas prices, as significant developments unfold in public health, political corruption, and AI-generated campaign advertising.

Geopolitics, Financial Markets, and the K-Shaped Economy

  • Market Record Highs: Wall Street surged on unconfirmed reports of peace progress between the US and Iran, pushing the S&P, Nasdaq, and Dow to new all-time highs despite ongoing military friction.
  • Strait of Hormuz Conflict: Kinetic military actions continue in the region despite diplomatic optimism. Centcom confirmed the USS *Abraham Lincoln* enforced blockade measures, firing on and disabling an Iranian-flagged oil tanker attempting to run the blockade.
  • The K-Shaped Divide: A new analysis from the New York Fed reveals that rising gas prices are disproportionately impacting lower-income Americans.
      • Behavioral Shifts: Households earning under $40,000 annually reduced their real gas usage by 7%, whereas households earning more than $125,000 cut back by just 1%.
      • Economic Cushioning: Unlike the 2022 energy shock following Russia's invasion of Ukraine, lower-income consumers currently lack post-pandemic financial cushions, causing them to show signs of financial strain even as strong corporate earnings and housing equity keep wealthier consumers spending.

Public Health and Medical Breakthroughs

  • Hantavirus Outbreak on Cruise Ship: A cruise ship carrying approximately 150 people is bound for Tenerife in the Canary Islands after three passengers suspected of carrying Hantavirus (specifically the person-to-person transmissible Andes strain) were evacuated.
      • Current Case Toll: There are eight reported cases (three confirmed, five suspected) and three deaths associated with the vessel.
      • Treatment Limits: Dr. Akshay Syal noted that there is no targeted antiviral drug (such as Paxlovid for COVID-19 or Tamiflu for influenza) available for Hantavirus, meaning patients in critical condition must rely purely on supportive care, including ventilators and ECMO life support.
  • Pancreatic Cancer Drug Trial: Initial clinical trial results for the drug *durasertib* (developed by Revolution Medicines) showed a doubling of survival rates for patients with advanced pancreatic cancer when paired with chemotherapy compared to chemotherapy alone.
      • FDA Fast-Track: The FDA has fast-tracked the drug for approval and will allow its use outside of clinical trials.
      • Side Effects: Patients reported severe blistering rashes, described by former Nebraska Senator Ben Sasse (a trial patient) as "nuclear," which may require pausing treatment to resolve.

Political Oversight and Corruption Investigations

  • Howard Lutnick’s Epstein Testimony: Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick voluntarily testified behind closed doors before the House Oversight Committee regarding his past interactions with Jeffrey Epstein.
      • Correcting the Record: Lutnick, who previously stated on a podcast that he was never in a room with Epstein socially or for business, acknowledged three meetings, including a family lunch on Epstein's private island.
      • Partisan Divide: Committee Chair James Comer (R-KY) defended Lutnick’s transparency, while Democrats and attorneys for Epstein's survivors questioned his credibility and noted that misrepresenting relationships under congressional oath carries criminal penalties.
  • Federal Raid on Virginia State Senator: The FBI executed a search warrant at the Portsmouth offices and co-owned cannabis dispensary of Virginia State Senate Democratic Leader L. Louise Lucas. The raid is reportedly linked to a long-running public corruption investigation.

Technology and Media: AI-Generated Political Advertising

  • Spencer Pratt’s AI Campaign Video: Los Angeles mayoral candidate Spencer Pratt amplified a highly advanced, dystopian AI-generated campaign video depicting himself as a Batman-like figure, Democratic incumbent Mayor Karen Bass as the Joker, and Governor Gavin Newsom as royalty.
  • Gray Legal Areas: Though 15 campaign ads utilizing AI-generated content have run nationally since November and 24 states regulate political deepfakes, third-party content creators remain difficult to police.
      • Intellectual Property Issues: Legal experts note that while defamation claims are difficult to prove in political parodies, copyright owners (such as Warner Bros. for Batman imagery) can issue cease-and-desist orders.

Notable Quotes

"With Hantavirus, with Andes virus, there's no specific antiviral. So it's what we call supportive care... if the virus starts to affect your heart and lungs, you could be put on a ventilator, you could be put on ECMO, which is artificial life support essentially." — *Dr. Akshay Syal, Medical Contributor*
"I feel like that Lutnick has been very transparent. He came in voluntarily... the only thing that I had seen that Lutnick did wrong was wasn't 100% truthful on the, the brief visit to the island with his family. He corrected that." — *Rep. James Comer, Chairman of the House Oversight Committee*
"As far as the Pope is concerned, it's very simple. Whether I make him happy or I don't make him happy, Iran cannot have a nuclear weapon, and he seemed to be saying that they can, and I say they cannot." — *Donald Trump, Former U.S. President*
We are coming on the air with a record high on Wall Street, with the markets feeling what they think are good vibes between the US and Iran that the war could end soon. So why won't President Trump tip his hand on how things are going? Our team breaks down how the president sees this war and who these rising gas prices are hitting the hardest. Also tonight, we have new pictures of the evacuation of three people believed to be infected with that deadly rodent virus on board a cruise ship in the Atlantic. The whole ship is headed now to the Canary Islands, and we'll tell you what locals are saying about why they're not so thrilled about it. Then, a new development in a murder case cracked by a podcast: a search warrant is now out for the mother of the man convicted of killing college student Kristin Smart, who vanished 30 years ago. Plus, a controversial manisphere influencer just got, you could call it, brutally mogged by Florida police. We will look into why Clavicular is accused of killing an alligator tonight. And in tonight's breakdown, the former reality star turned candidate for LA mayor is pushing an AI video of himself dressed up almost like Batman, with the current mayor as the Joker, ahead of a big debate tonight. We've got the breakdown on why, just a little bit later in the show. Hey there, I'm Hallie. In just the last hour, we saw the markets closing, hitting record highs on hopes that the latest reports of potential peace progress are actually real this time between the US and Iran. I want to show you here where the stock markets closed. Look at this: a new all-time high for the S&P, the Nasdaq, and the Dow is up, too, after reports not confirmed by NBC News that the war could end soon. But the top official of all of them, President Trump, hasn't been super crystal clear. He says the war will end if Iran, in his words, "agrees to give what has been agreed to." Okay, so what is that? We are not quite sure, but Iran is processing whatever it is. It is a 14-point plan that a spokesperson says through state media will get a response through the Pakistanis at some point. All right, following all that? Here's what we do know. That whole Project Freedom that the Secretaries of State and Defense talked up big time 24 hours ago, that's over for now. Already, the US has fired on at least one ship in the Middle East. CENTCOM says the USS Abraham Lincoln enforced what they call "blockade measures"—you see it there—on an Iran-flagged oil tanker trying to dock at an Iranian port. Remember, the Strait of Hormuz has been effectively paralyzed. That's why gas prices have been so high. Allie Canal will have more on that in a minute. But I want to start with Garrett Haake at the White House who made a valiant effort today, Garrett, during this sort of unexpected moment where the president called in a bunch of reporters to try to get him to make some news on what is happening with Iran. There is a real question, I think, tonight as to where this progress, perhaps, toward peace really stands. Yeah, and I'm not sure we made much progress towards getting an answer to that, Hallie. The president, in a variety of interviews, has expressed his optimism. He suggested that a deal is close, even unwinding that Project Freedom last night. He said it was at the behest of Pakistan, and in light of this major progress. This event was MMA-themed, and so I tried to stay on theme in asking him about his opponent here, Iran, and why they don't seem to share his optimism that a deal is close. Listen to this: "You're facing an opponent right now in Iran that has refused to submit. You seem optimistic now that you may be closer to a deal. What's different about this moment now than in other moments where a deal has seemed close?" "Well, why do you say they refuse to submit? You don't know that. You don't know what's going on." "They fired on US ships a few days ago." "Yeah, well, a few days ago is a long time ago. You know, in the world of war, a few days ago. No, they want to make a deal badly, and we'll see if we get there. If we get there, they can't have nuclear weapons." So, the rhetoric is the same; the end goal of Iran with no nuclear weapons is the same. I think what is different is perhaps what happened in those 24 hours, Hallie. But what I can tell you is what didn't happen. We've gotten revised data from the global tracking firm that watches the Strait, and no ships—we thought it was one, but the answer is zero—went through the Strait yesterday when this Operation Freedom was still in effect. So, something's up here, but the end result is just as confusing as it has been for much of the last several weeks. I appreciate your daily checks on the number of ships going through the Strait of Hormuz. It is, I think, your current Garrett obsession, which is a franchise, as you know, we've decided to start on the show. The other piece of this, though, is the actual kinetic action in the region, right? Because the president has left the door open to more strikes. I mean, that's just on the table. Yeah, and all of this really, you know, challenges the definition of the English word "ceasefire" here, which we discussed yesterday. Normally, it means stopping shooting, but the shooting continues. It was Iran shooting at US ships two days ago, and today, we learned that the US fired on an Iranian tanker that attempted to run the blockade, just disabling it. But apparently, that doesn't run afoul of the ceasefire as it exists in the mind of the president. I think one thing is sort of notionally true through all of this, and that's that the president's threshold to restart this war is high. He has made and extended deadlines to Iran repeatedly that they have blown through. There have been shooting incidents back and forth several times that don't seem to have aroused his ire. It's pretty clear that he wants this to end in negotiation, but if and when that patience runs out, or the facts change substantively in the next few days or weeks, I think is another open question. Garrett Haake, live for us there on the North Lawn. Garrett, thank you very much for all that. Allie Canal, I want to bring you in now. We talked about markets at the top of the show. There's this interesting dichotomy that we're seeing here. While the markets are up on the word—and as Garrett has laid out, the potential that there could be progress toward peace—not everybody's got money in the market, right? Some four in ten Americans don't have retirement accounts. Look at gas prices, also an impact from this war. Nobody likes high gas prices, but they disproportionately affect less wealthy Americans, according to some of the new numbers we have out today. Yeah, and, Hallie, you're referring to something known as the K-shaped economy, in which the wealthier American is propping up the economy while those lower-income consumers continue to struggle financially. This latest oil shock is only deepening that divide. You mentioned some of that data that we got. A new analysis from the New York Fed shows that those lower-income households are immediately changing their behavior due to these higher gas prices. As you are seeing on your screen now, those making under $40,000 a year reduced their real gas usage by about 7%. Then you look at those higher-income households, those making more than $125,000, they cut back just 1%. Researchers say that this gap actually looks worse than what we saw during the 2022 energy shock when Russia invaded Ukraine, and there are a few reasons why. One, you have a stock market at record highs. You have those wealthier Americans enjoying home equity value. Then on the flip side, the lower-income consumers don't have those same post-pandemic supports that we saw back in 2022 that helped cushion a little bit of that blow. When you think about a stock market at record highs, it can feel a little bit confusing. Why are they able to look through all of these unknowns? It's because corporate earnings are still strong. They're beating expectations. Those wealthier Americans are still spending, so GDP growth is still solid. But while all that can be true, we are starting to see more signs that the lower-income consumer is just starting to crack a bit. Allie Canal, thank you very much for that update, appreciate that. One to watch, for sure. Let's get now to that cruise ship that is, we think, making its way to the Canary Islands. Right as we were coming on the air, we heard from health officials in Argentina who said they will start basically testing rats there to see if that is, in fact, the origin of that deadly virus now on that cruise ship where people have been sickened and some have died. I want to show you some of what we know at this moment here. Look at this. We're about to show you the moment that three passengers suspected of having Hantavirus—that's what it's called—were evacuated off the ship. You see somebody there being put in an ambulance, or you did see, being totally covered up. The ship's on its way to the Canary Islands. We don't know when they're going to be evacuated, with some 150 people still on board. The whole thing started unfolding April 12th, when the captain told passengers somebody on board died the day before. That moment was captured on camera. Here it is: "This is my sad duty to inform you that one of our passengers sadly passed away last night. I am told by the doctor not infectious. So the ship is safe when it comes to that." Let's make a point to say here that the cruise ship company, Oceanwide Expeditions, says that when this announcement was made, nobody knew why that passenger had died. Now the passenger who took the video said the atmosphere on board was pretty tense. "He said it was due to natural causes and they were not infectious. This was relaxing at the time, but it was also misleading us." So here's where the count stands now. There are eight cases of Hantavirus: three of them confirmed by lab testing, the other five are suspected cases. Three of the passengers who were on board have died. We've got Dr. Akshay Syal on standby for a medical breakdown. Camila Bernal is joining us now; she's been following some updates for us. I'm just looking over because it sounds like we're getting some new information, Camila, on where things stand with this cruise ship that is supposed to dock at a Spanish port in Tenerife at some point, right? Yeah, and it actually has already taken off, so we're in movement, I guess, is what you can say. There was definitely some pushback from the Canary Islands, but Spain is essentially saying that when these passengers arrive, they are equipped to handle them. They are saying that they're going to have medical evaluations, and that they're going to have specific places and transportation so that the people who are on the ship don't have any contact with anybody else that is essentially living in the Canary Islands. So, they're trying to handle this the best way possible. They say that they're going to continue the contact tracing when it comes to the Spanish nationals. They want to quarantine them in Madrid. They even spoke about that 45-day incubation period, so there's a real question about how many days these people will have to quarantine for. The WHO is saying maybe not necessarily 45 days, but it is possible that they're going to make many of these passengers quarantine. This is even though Spain right now is saying that they essentially will accept them because none of the passengers on the ship right now are experiencing any symptoms, Hallie. And we know that the first of two medicalized aircraft carrying two of the three people transferred from the ship has landed in the Netherlands. That is just coming in to us, too, Camila. All of this is happening after this kind of conflict, I guess you could call it, between the local government, Spanish health officials, etc., because this ship is very much in the spotlight now internationally. Absolutely. This is a territory of Spain, but the leaders there did not want to allow the passengers to get off at the Canary Islands, and they're saying once they get off, they're going to be sent to their home countries. There are a lot of residents there who clearly just did not want to see all of these people who've been in close contact with people who've died and people who have had symptoms of Hantavirus. Here is what some of the residents are saying: "The people are scared. The people are worried." "I think there is a little bit of disinformation, and that's what's making people a little unsure about the ship coming here." And I also spoke to that passenger that you played earlier, and he said, "You know, we do need more information on ships. We maybe need more testing because everybody is just confused about what's going on." The good thing, though, is that many experts say that despite the fact that this is a very deadly virus, you really do need very close contact to have it transmit to other people, Hallie. Camila Bernal, thank you very much. Dr. Akshay, let me bring you in on this because, listen, you and I have been talking about this. People have a lot of questions, and I think there was a level of concern. I think it's important to have you talk about how, if you're not on this ship, you don't need to freak out. This is not, as you have said, the next COVID pandemic from the information that we have here. But I do wonder. One of the things that we have learned today is more from this plane that one of the passengers had gotten on board—one of the passengers on the ship who ended up dying from the virus. KLM today, the airline, says that it was brief, and that the crew ended up not letting that person fly because of their medical condition. Health officials think they are dealing with a strain that can be transmitted from person to person. So, what does this mean if this passenger was at the airport, was around other people, etc.? What has to happen now? So, Hallie, I was on the phone with infectious disease doctors today, and they are all pointing to the same thing about the most concerning aspect of this whole story: it actually comes down to the air travel. Think about this, Hallie. If you are going to an airport and you're getting on a plane, you should feel good. If you felt really, really sick, you wouldn't do that. So, the fact that they felt good enough to go to an airport, get on a plane, and then subsequently pass away 24 hours later really speaks to how devastating of a disease this is. This is something that really can take hold pretty quickly in the body after several days or so. But, you know, Hallie, as far as that KLM flight goes, they may have actually been on a flight before that. We're still confirming that with Airlink, one of the airlines from St. Helena to Johannesburg. So while they may have been dismissed from that second flight, they may have completed the first flight, which had about 100 people or so. The WHO is undergoing contact tracing. They are looking to see who was in contact with that person because it can spread from human to human. If you have prolonged contact, like if you're sitting right next to somebody who had the Hantavirus or the Andes virus, you'd want to know that. To your point on how serious this can be, two of the three people evacuated from the ship, according to the cruise company, are in critical condition. What is the treatment like for them at this point? We've talked about how deadly Hantavirus can be. What do doctors need to be doing? So, Hallie, when you talk about some viruses like, for example, COVID, I can prescribe Paxlovid, or if you have the flu, we can prescribe Tamiflu. With Hantavirus or the Andes virus, there's no specific antiviral. So it's what we call supportive care, and all that means, Hallie, is that it depends on your symptoms. For example, if the virus starts to affect your heart and lungs, you could be put on a ventilator, or you could be put on ECMO, which is artificial life support essentially. If your blood pressure drops, we could give you medication to artificially boost that blood pressure up and make sure that your organs are getting enough oxygen. So, there is no specific antiviral, and there is no drug that we can give that is going to directly reduce the amount of Hantavirus in the body. But there is a whole host of other things that we can do to sort of fight the symptoms or fight the disease as it takes hold in the body. This is just another reason that, you know, this isn't an extremely common virus. We've had less than 1,000 cases in the US in the last decade or so, so it is something that hasn't really been prioritized in terms of developing treatments or developing vaccines. That is just another reason that we need all the funding we can get in terms of diseases like this, Hallie. Dr. Akshay Syal, glad to have you with us tonight again, appreciate you. Here in Washington now, we have a new readout after a top Trump administration official was questioned today about his ties to Jeffrey Epstein. We are talking about the man you see here, Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick. Here he is walking to this interview. Now, closed doors, no cameras inside. This is kind of like the other depositions we've seen, so we don't know specifically what he said, at least not yet. He did it voluntarily on his own as part of the House Oversight Committee's investigation into Epstein. But at least one Democrat says they're concerned by what they heard from Lutnick: "I think of all of the depositions we have done so far, I find Howard Lutnick to be the least credible." Lutnick has not been charged or accused of any crime, and while he has denied a relationship with Epstein and denied any wrongdoing, he has now acknowledged having three meetings with his then-neighbor Epstein, including once on his private island. This came after the DOJ's release of the Epstein documents over the course of the last six months, according to a source familiar with the testimony. That came, of course, as you probably remember, after Lutnick on a podcast last year said he decided to never be in the room with Epstein after a tour of his house in 2005 that disturbed Lutnick and his wife. Here is what he said about their ties ahead of today's testimony: "And I will never be in the room with that disgusting person ever again. So, I was never in the room with him socially, for business, or even for philanthropy." I want to bring in our Capitol Hill correspondent, Ryan Nobles, who is joining us here on set. We're glad to have you here, Ryan. So this is what people might remember; this is why Lutnick really came under fire weeks ago when the Epstein documents started being released, because he had those sort of strong comments like, "I was disgusted by him, I never want to..." and then he had interactions with Epstein even after that. Presumably, we think, obviously, this came up today behind closed doors. Yeah, that's the biggest reason that the Oversight Committee wanted to ask Lutnick questions, because there was a chasm between what he had claimed about his relationship with Jeffrey Epstein versus what we saw in the Epstein files. There was a whole back-and-forth of correspondence between him and the Epstein staff about a meeting in the Virgin Islands. His entire family met with Jeffrey Epstein and had lunch with him. Of course, the Virgin Islands was where Epstein had that infamous island. And so what this was, was an opportunity for Lutnick to clear the air. But obviously, as you saw from Democrats, they didn't necessarily believe him. Republicans had a different view of it. Here is what James Comer said after the deposition: "I feel like that Lutnick has been very transparent. He came in voluntarily. I said before this thing started, the only thing that I had seen that Lutnick did wrong was he wasn't 100% truthful on the brief visit to the island with his family. He corrected that." So as you see, that was what Republicans were looking for—for him to correct that and explain it. He said today that he had three different meetings with Jeffrey Epstein. One was that time they took the tour of the house and saw the massage table. The second was that lunch they had on the boat in the Virgin Islands, and then the third was a time he went in and checked on some scaffolding in the foyer because they were next-door neighbors for a short period of time. The frustration, I think, that Epstein survivors feel with all of this, Hallie, is that all of these people keep coming in, they keep answering questions, but we're making no progress toward getting anybody any level of justice. This is what an attorney for the Epstein survivors said about the Lutnick interview today: "The interview of Howard Lutnick, while a step towards potential clarity, does not provide any real substance for identifying the perpetrators of Epstein's network or pathways to justice for survivors. Lutnick has already misrepresented his relationship with Jeffrey Epstein, raising serious questions about if he was even truthful during the interview..." And I think what is important about the difference between him going on a podcast and making these bold claims to try to distance himself from Jeffrey Epstein versus sitting in a room like this taking questions from members of Congress, is that it is against the law to lie to Congress. So if he was lying today, which Democrats believe he may have been, that is much different than a podcast. You can say whatever you want on a podcast; the First Amendment protects you there. So, Lutnick was taking a huge risk if he wasn't completely telling the truth. Republicans believe that he was. "To be clear, Lutnick has not been accused of wrongdoing related to Epstein, correct?" "Right, no, no, no." "He's one that I had circled on the calendar as we've been reporting out all of these depositions. Pam Bondi is coming up; Bill Gates is another interesting one coming up, right?" "That's right, we're going to hear from Leon Black, we're going to hear from Leslie Groff, all of these folks. Kathy Ruemmler, who was, of course, the former Obama White House counsel who served as a personal attorney to Jeffrey Epstein, is also on the list." "With each one of these individuals, the survivors and their attorneys are hoping that this can piece together some sort of web that then could lead to an investigation and a subsequent prosecution. That's the most frustrating thing for the survivors. All of these interviews are taking place, all this information is being uncovered, but yet no one has been held accountable since this whole thing began, Ryan." "Ryan Nobles, thank you very much for that update. We think we might see at least the transcript at some point." "I think, more likely, we see the video first and then the transcript because the transcript has to be scrubbed by both sides' attorneys. So we could see the video. In the past, they've produced them within 24 hours, so it could happen as soon as tomorrow." "Come back if we get it. Thanks, Ryan, appreciate it." Also right now, we have an interesting situation in Virginia with the FBI searching the offices of a key Democratic official involved in the so-called redistricting wars. NBC News has learned that the Feds are at the Portsmouth office and business of the top Democrat in the Virginia State Senate, L. Louise Lucas. You're seeing that search right here. A source familiar with the matter tells NBC News it's connected to a long-running public corruption investigation into Lucas with roots in the Biden era. Now, Lucas is somebody who led the charge for her state to pass an amendment to let Virginia change its congressional maps to be, basically, more Democratic-leaning. That is happening as Republicans ramp up their own redistricting fight, like in Tennessee. The GOP there has unveiled a new map that would wipe out the state's lone Democratic seat. Republicans in South Carolina have taken some new steps to do the same. Gary Grumbach is joining us now. And to be clear here, Gary, we cannot necessarily draw a direct line between the Lucas investigation and the redistricting fight. But based on some of the statements you're getting, some top Democrats in Virginia are certainly connecting those dots. Yeah, on that point, federal agents today also executed a search warrant at a nearby cannabis dispensary called 'The Cannabis Outlet' that happens to be co-owned by State Senator Louise Lucas. So that's an important part here, too. We're told that drugs are not the primary focus of this investigation, but that this public corruption idea is. And it has, as you said, been long-running. Don Scott, however, the Virginia House Speaker and a very powerful Democrat in the state of Virginia, is questioning the legitimacy of this whole investigation, the warrant search, and this raid that happened this morning. He said in part, quote, 'Given the politicization of this administration, I think people should take this with a grain of salt and allow the facts to come out before jumping to conclusions.' Now, I will say it's notable that other statewide Democrats across the Commonwealth of Virginia—people like Governor Abigail Spanberger or the Attorney General J. Jones—have actually sort of held their fire and not said things like that. They have said, "You know what, I'm just going to wait until things come out, I'm not going to say anything whatsoever." This is because we don't know exactly how serious this could be. While the Eastern District of Virginia recently has been a hotspot for very political indictments and very political investigations, this one does seem, from our conversations with our sources, to be part of a more legitimate, long-running investigation, Hallie. Gary Grumbach, thank you very much for that update. Just some breaking news now on an experimental new drug that doctors say could change the way cancer is treated. In just the last 20 minutes or so, we are getting the results of the first major clinical trial of a pancreatic cancer drug. This drug has already been fast-tracked for approval by the FDA, and it's shown to double survival rates for the thousands of patients in the US with advanced stages of pancreatic cancer. And you know, pancreatic cancer is one of the deadliest ones out there. Last week, the FDA said that it will let the drug maker give this medicine to patients outside clinical trials. Let me bring in Erika Edwards now. Can you cut through the medical jargon here, Erika? Our team is looking at this, and we're able to report this big headline, it seems, right? That it could change the way cancer is treated. What does this breakthrough mean? Hey, Hallie. You know, every single cancer doctor I spoke to about this was so hopeful, calling it a game-changer. And as you said, pancreatic cancer is devastating because it's usually caught way too late, and doctors haven't had good options for patients other than some chemo. Even then, many people don't survive a year after their diagnosis. Now, patients who got this drug, which is called durasertib, as well as chemotherapy, had double the survival time compared to patients who got chemo alone. Now, some caution, of course: this is not a cure, and even with the drug, patients only survived up to about a year and a half. It can also come with side effects, especially a severe blistering rash that can look a lot like a sunburn. Former Nebraska Senator Ben Sasse was diagnosed with stage four pancreatic cancer last year. He's taken the drug, and he has called that rash "nuclear." Doctors say some patients may actually have to lay off the drug for a bit for that rash to clear up. Now, as for how long it could take for other patients to get it, the FDA, as you said, has already promised to fast-track that drug once the company, Revolution Medicines, submits an application for approval. Hallie, that process could take just a few months. Erika Edwards, certainly one to watch on that. Thank you very much, appreciate that. Also tonight, the most popular American is going to sit down with the Secretary of State tomorrow for what Marco Rubio says is not the start of some kind of apology tour with the Pope. It might be, actually, kind of the opposite after a new round of criticism aimed at Pope Leo from President Trump. The president hit the leader of the Catholic Church in just the last couple of hours, mischaracterizing his stance on the war with Iran: "As far as the Pope is concerned, it's very simple. Whether I make him happy or I don't make him happy, Iran cannot have a nuclear weapon. He seemed to be saying that they can, and I say they cannot." So here's the deal. Pope Leo has criticized the war, which the president has suggested is designed to eliminate Iran's nuclear program. The Pope has never said that it's, quote-unquote, "fine" for Iran to have a nuke. And he wasn't exactly a wallflower responding to the president's remarks: "If someone wants to criticize me for proclaiming the Gospel, that person should do so truthfully. The Church for years has spoken against all nuclear arms, there is no doubt." Monica Alba joins us now. So here we are, Monica, on round, I think it's two or three of the President versus the Pope. This time, it is through an intermediary, perhaps, in Marco Rubio, the Secretary of State, who says this is not like a smooth-things-over tour. They're going to talk about some of their differences when they meet tomorrow, on issues that Marco Rubio has talked about his very personal connection to. Absolutely, Hallie, though I think it would be really hard for there not to be some acknowledgment of these words that have been exchanged between members of the administration and Pope Leo. Certainly, Secretary Rubio said that when he goes and meets with him, they are going to talk about a lot of different things. This was a trip, we should point out, that was planned, according to Secretary Rubio, before some of this really kicked off in earnest between Pope Leo and President Trump. Here's a little more of what the Secretary had to say about what he believes he's going to be discussing with the Pontiff: "The Pope just returned from a trip to Africa, where the church is growing very vibrantly, and we have shared concerns about religious freedom in different parts of the world. We'd love to talk to them about that. On the topic of Cuba, you know, we gave Cuba $6 million of humanitarian aid, but obviously they won't let us distribute it. We distributed it through the church. We'd like to do more." So, there is plenty for them to discuss. But we do know, Hallie, that it does seem, at least according to Secretary Rubio when he was taking questions yesterday here at the White House, he did almost try to clean up some of the comments that the president has made. This includes that allegation that we just fact-checked about the Pope claiming that Iran can have a nuclear weapon, and even Secretary Rubio was trying to sort of walk that back. That signals the sort of approach he's going to take into this conversation and meeting, we expect. You do wonder, you know, if the president has an eye at all on the potential political backlash to this, because he's obviously sought the vote of Americans who are religious, Protestants and Catholics alike. The Pope is so popular, Monica, he really is. Let me look at some of these numbers here—he is well above President Trump if you're looking at approval ratings. He has had these moments, you know, the Pope being just like us, like the story his friend told about a customer service experience that the Pope had. He called his bank to try and change his name and address because now he is the Pope. He couldn't show up in person and tried to explain that to the teller on the phone. Let me play that: "Would it matter to you if I told you I'm Pope Leo?" "She hung up on him." Number one, who can blame her? But number two, even though he's the Pope, there are these relatable anecdotes that have emerged about him ever since he took the papacy here. How much is the White House thinking about that? Like, maybe he's not the guy to pick a fight with? And he is so popular, Hallie, that if you look at polling, he is definitely a more popular figure than a lot of the political figures that we end up talking about a lot day-to-day. So there is definitely a bit of a cultural divide there as well in terms of how Americans see Pope Leo and how they see people like President Trump, Vice President Vance, and others. I think there is an awareness that maybe engaging in this kind of so-called feud with the Pope is not the best idea or use of time for the president. But as you know very well, Hallie, whenever they advise the president to consider backing down from something, he is not one who typically is going to do that. I will say, in the last couple of days, when he does talk about the Pope, he likes to bring up the fact that Pope Leo's brother is someone who has spoken publicly that he appreciates the president and that he's a fan of President Trump's. That seems to be somewhere where at least they can have a little bit of agreement and common ground—at least on the Pope's sibling. We'll take it. Monica Alba, thank you very much, friend, live for us there on the North Lawn. Appreciate you. Also tonight, as we look at other parts of the country, there is a potentially dangerous night of weather ahead. You've got this winter storm warning in place for parts of Colorado. This late spring snow forced schools to close and created—look at that, it's a pretty picture, but kind of a mess if you live there. Some winter alerts are in place in Denver tonight. The metro area faces maybe 2 to 4 more inches of snow on top of what they've already got, and up to 6 inches in some spots. In the Rockies, more than 20 inches of snow blanketed Bear Lake in Colorado, with another 18 inches on Trail Lake. We're seeing fresh video of people's backyards. Look at this drone footage showing Estes Park, Colorado. In the Southeast, it is a very different story: 16 million people are at risk for severe storms today, with hail and maybe tornado watches. Those are in place from East Texas to Alabama for a little bit, yeah. Morgan Chesky is joining us now. Snow in Colorado is expected to wrap up tonight, but it's been really tricky for folks out there as we look to the potential risk in the Southeast. Yeah, Hallie, it has been. We're talking about one of the biggest snow events that Colorado has seen in the past decade or so. You mentioned Estes Park; they saw 28 recorded inches of snow. That is only about 35 miles or so outside of Boulder, with those communities right along the front range of the Rockies getting absolutely walloped by this late snowfall. At one point, there were 50,000 people without power, and we know that maintenance crews were working essentially through the night to keep roads clear there. The concern tonight is not so much snow, but a freeze and ice buildup, with temperatures expected to dip into the 20s. That is what they are facing there in Colorado. But, Hallie, as you mentioned, there is an entirely different risk throughout the Deep South, where we could be seeing a flood threat as well as some severe weather. We have millions of people—more than 14 million, in fact—who are at risk of severe weather that could include massive hail as well as tornadoes. The Texas Hill Country, including communities such as Burnet and Fredericksburg, has been suffering from a hailstorm within the past 24 hours, with folks trying to pick up the pieces there. It is certainly going to be 24 hours to watch very closely throughout those southern states as they are now really entering the threat of this severe weather. In parts of Atlanta and parts of Georgia, should they see just 4 or 5 inches of rain, Hallie, that's more than enough to create a flash flood that could create significant issues for people in the early morning hours. So, people there need to absolutely be keeping a close eye on the weather here. This is part of what comes with the spring storm season, but it certainly doesn't make things any easier, Hallie. No kidding, Morgan Chesky. Thank you very much for that. We've got a lot more to get to here on the show, including, coming up: will there be a Triple Crown winner in horse racing this year? We are just hearing about Golden Tempo's Preakness decision. Plus, the tomato fraud allegations against a pretty popular Italian brand. That's next. Let's stick to California, with police conducting a search at the home of the mother of a man convicted in the murder of a college student 30 years ago. If that sounds kind of confusing, this was a case that was, at the time, a total blockbuster: the disappearance of Kristin Smart. I want to show you here the sheriff's office, this is at the home of Susan Flores, in a case that captured headlines around the world. Smart was 19 years old when she vanished after going to an off-campus party; this was decades ago. She has since been legally declared dead, but nobody has ever found her body. A few years ago, Paul Flores, a fellow student, was convicted and sentenced to 25 years to life for her murder. When that happened, police cited a podcast about Smart's disappearance as helping them crack the case. Tom Winter joins us now. Tom, I mean, it really was just a bombshell disappearance, a story that was covered everywhere at the time. The Paul Flores development happened, and Paul Flores's father was tried but acquitted of being an accessory. Now, there is this search at the home of his mother, and you have some new info from the DA there. What's up? Well, that's right, Hallie. And yes, this case captured the nation's attention, not just in California, but with people coast-to-coast wondering what could have happened to this young woman who disappeared on March 25th in 1996. She was visiting a friend at a college, and campus police really did not advance the investigation very much. But when local authorities took it over, they realized that this was not somebody who ran off on a trip or went away with friends, but in fact, something much more nefarious. To that end, you mentioned the fact that the body has not been discovered in this case. When the initial charges came down against Paul Flores and his father, Ruben, there was a search conducted along with the FBI and archaeologists. They found a casket-sized, kind of disturbed soil in the ground. They used ground-penetrating radar to detect that, and they also conducted additional searches, all at Ruben Flores's home. They also found blood, but it was not suitable to conduct a DNA comparison. Now, we've heard from the District Attorney today, and we can show you that statement they put out. I think there is a key component of this, saying, "While those responsible for Kristin's death—and those with knowledge of her whereabouts—could provide answers at any time, we remain firmly committed to using every lawful tool available to locate Kristin's remains and to support her family until she is brought home." This is obviously coming up a little bit past the 30-year anniversary of her disappearance, so this is a significant case. Clearly, local authorities there want to see this case to its conclusion, certainly for her family. "And, again, the reverberations even now—modern-day college students in California, the laws have actually been changed after Smart's disappearance because of what had happened." "Well, that's right, Hallie. Back in 1998, passing the California State Legislature 61 to 0, it was the Kristin Smart Campus Safety Act of 1998." "It basically required—and I mentioned some of the issues with campus security early on—campus security at public institutions, including public colleges, to have some sort of an understanding with their local police departments as to what they would report and when. This is specifically as it spoke to potential violent crimes, kidnappings, abductions, and missing students." "So, when those types of cases came up, they were required under this law to get a hold of local law enforcement who might be in a better position to hopefully get a resolution. So the delays that impacted this case, Hallie, were not delays that would continue for other students." Let's get you over to the five things our team thinks you should know about tonight. Number one: Ted Turner has died at the age of 87. He was a media mogul who made history by launching CNN back in 1980 as the first 24-hour cable news channel in the country. I mean, he is the guy who basically founded 24-hour news, and that is only one piece of his work. Turner also pioneered other networks, he owned the Braves, and he was involved in philanthropy as well. Number two: an update to a story we've been following. US Marshals have confirmed that a retired Special Forces veteran accused of shooting his wife is dead. We told you yesterday, remember, Aaron Gilchrist was out there talking about this days-long manhunt in Tennessee for Craig Berry. The local sheriff's office says Berry died by suicide, apparently. Number three: dramatic new video is now out of Florida, with police rescuing six people from a sinking boat. Look at this—it was partially taking on water there in a pretty big lake. There had been a warning about a storm creating some rough conditions, but fortunately, everybody on board is okay. Number four: hey gang, no Triple Crown winner this year in horse racing. That is because we have just learned that Golden Tempo is not going to run in the Preakness next week. Of course, Golden Tempo won the Kentucky Derby incredibly memorably, going from last place into first. The team behind the horse says it is the best decision for him. This is actually the second year in a row that the Derby winner passed on the Preakness. Some trainers say that the two-week gap between the races is just not enough time for a horse to recover, which has raised questions about the future of the Triple Crown period. Number five: a new lawsuit is accusing a popular Italian food distributor of "tomato fraud." That's a quote, man. Tomato fraud. It is about these canned tomatoes here. The suit claims that the Cento Fine Foods label saying "Certified San Marzano" is misleading. Those tomatoes come from Italy, and they have a pretty unique flavor. The lawsuit even alleges Cento's tomatoes are inferior to the, quote, "Ferrari or Prada of tomatoes." Cento refutes the claims and calls the allegations meritless. Are we going to make a "tomato, tomato" remark here? Didn't that kind of scream for it, you know? All right, we have much more to get to here on the show, including when we come back. We are going to take you down to Texas and show you some really stunning video of the moment a father confronted somebody trying to steal his car with his family inside, and explain why police now say there won't be any charges. Plus, we'll look into why this interview with a Philly crossing guard is going so viral. To Texas now, where police say the dad who shot and killed somebody trying to break into his car is not going to face any charges. Take a look at this video. The suspected carjacker goes up—you see it in the bottom of your screen—to the white car. He's in the pink-colored shirt, trying to get into the driver's seat. There's the dad in the white t-shirt. He points his gun at the suspect and tackles him, as eight people inside the car, including two little kids, come running out. Here's a closer look, a little bit closer from different angles. This all went down in a parking lot outside Dallas, and police say it was self-defense. I want to bring in Ryan Chandler, who is joining us now. The expectation is that no charges will be filed against this dad, right? Well, that's right, and I think that video that you just played is going to be central to the police's determination that this shooting did not warrant any charges. We got some insight into what that investigation looked like after police arrived from a woman who saw it all happen—she saw the children run out and into one of these stores. She said police first made sure that everybody was unarmed, and then talked with witnesses and the shooter, and ultimately left without making any arrests or any immediate determination. Late tonight, we are learning that they are not planning to file charges at this time, although it is still an ongoing investigation. We are also learning, Hallie, that the suspect—the man who was killed—is 30-year-old Jose Ramirez. That's a new detail from Garland police tonight. I want you to take a listen to some of the reaction that we are hearing from residents here in the community. "It was traumatic for them, I could only imagine. Like, that's not what they signed up for. They were stopping to get snacks, they were stopping to get the baby snacks before going to the water park." That woman, Tatiana, manages one of the stores behind me, and she actually said she helped those kids rush into the Stop-N-Go to get them out of harm's way. You know, Hallie, as police say that they do not plan to pursue this any farther, at least criminally, that is the sense we are getting. People are happy for the actions that saved those kids tonight, Hallie. Ryan Chandler, live for us there in Garland, Texas. Ryan, thank you very much. NBC News covers hundreds of other stories every day. Because it can be tough to read, watch, or listen to them all, our bureau teams have done it for you. This is what they tell us is going down in their regions in a segment we call "The Local." Out of our Midwest Bureau, a North Dakota firefighter is recovering after what you just saw—a piece of that apartment building collapsing. Crews had been working for hours to try to get this thing under control before those walls came down. The good news is nobody was hurt. Out of our Western Bureau, take a look. This tractor-trailer tanker truck basically split open after being hit by a train in Colorado. Some of the train cars derailed, and some 6,000 gallons of road oil spilled. That is the kind of thing that you use to repair asphalt. The truck driver is expected to be okay, and nobody on the train was hurt. We don't know how the collision even happened in the first place, but investigators are working to figure that out. And out of our Northeast Bureau, we have a wild scene in Philadelphia. Police say an 18-year-old—look at this—stole a work truck, boom, crashed through an intersection and passing cars, and then—look at this—flipped. Yes, that is a playground, and it ended up overturned in the middle of that playground. So scary. The really good news here is that no staff and no students were hurt, according to the school district. Now, our Philly station ended up catching up with the crossing guard who says he actually pulled a child out of one of the cars that was involved. Now, that crossing guard's colorful recap is getting some attention, a lot of it, online. "He ran the light, t-boned the lady. I'm bobbing and weaving. I'm just thankful, thankful to be alive right now." Well, a lot of people are thankful for his heroic actions as well. Kudos to him. Coming up, we have some new legal trouble tonight for one controversial influencer. We'll look at how police say Clavicular disrupted what they call the peace and dignity of the Florida Everglades. Tonight, a controversial influencer known for his looksmaxing is facing a criminal charge in Florida for allegedly shooting a dead alligator during a live stream. We are talking about Clavicular. You probably recognize him here—he does this whole thing where you do this trend to, like, look better, and you do a lot of sort of extreme type things, like apparently this. According to documents, Clavicular, whose real name is Braden Eric Peters, is being charged with unlawfully discharging a gun at a wildlife area back in March. You see him firing a gun into the water in this stream that he posted there. We're also learning that he was apparently arrested on that same day for another incident, unrelated, this time for allegedly instigating a fight between his girlfriend and another woman and then posting a video of it. There he is leaving jail; he is facing battery charges in that case. Remember, last month Clavicular's reps said he'd been hospitalized after a suspected drug overdose. He posted on X at the time, "Just got home, that was brutal." Jesse Kirsch is following this one for us. Well, tell us what—I mean, for those who follow the looksmaxing community, Clavicular is the name, right? I mean, like, what are we hearing from his team on this? What else do we know about this? Fill us in. Yeah, so what we're told, Hallie, is that according to the court documents we've reviewed, this incident happened at the end of March. Our team has reached out to the prosecutor's office, but they wouldn't give us any further details on this. This apparently happened at a boat ramp area near a dock, about 40 miles from downtown Miami. So, this is something that, again, is apparently more than a month old, but now getting the attention of law enforcement, and it's not clear exactly why. But here's what we're hearing from this influencer's legal team, his attorneys writing in a statement that, quote, "Our client has been summoned to appear for a misdemeanor charge that stems from following the instructions of a licensed airboat guide. He relied on that guidance. No animals or people were harmed..." of the Everglades. And so, that was that. No other details on this, but we'll see if he goes to court in July. Jesse Kirsch. So, there is plenty for them to discuss. But we do know, Hallie, that it does seem, at least according to Secretary Rubio when he was taking questions yesterday here at the White House, he did almost try to clean up some of the comments that the President has made—again, including that allegation that we just fact-checked, the Pope claiming that Iran can have a nuclear weapon. Even Secretary Rubio was trying to sort of walk that back, and so that does signal to you the sort of approach he's going to take into this conversation and meeting we expect. You do wonder, you know, if the President has an eye, has an eye at all on the potential political backlash to this. He's obviously sought the vote of Americans who are religious, Protestants and Catholics alike. The Pope is so popular, Monica. He is. I mean, look at some of these numbers here, well above President Trump if you're looking at approval ratings here. And he's had these moments. You know, "American Pope," "the Pope is just like us"—like the story that his priest friend told about a customer service experience that the Pope had, that he went to go call his bank, tried to change his name and address because now he's the Pope, couldn't show up in person, and tried to explain that to the teller on the phone. Let me play that. "Would it matter to you if I told you I'm Pope Leo?" She hung up on him. Number one, who can blame her? But number two, like even though he's the Pope, there are these relatable anecdotes that have emerged about him ever since he took the papacy here. How much is the White House thinking about that? Like, maybe he's not the guy to pick the fight with. And he's so popular, Hallie, that if you look at polling, he is definitely a more popular figure than a lot of the political figures that we end up talking about a lot day to day. So, there is definitely a bit of a cultural divide there as well in terms of how Americans see Pope Leo and how they see people like President Trump, Vice President Vance, and others. So, I think there is an awareness that maybe engaging in this kind of a so-called feud with the Pope is not the best idea or or time spent for the President. But as you know very well, Hallie, whenever they even advise the President to consider backing down from something, he is not one who typically is going to do that. Though I will say, in the last couple of days, when he does talk about the Pope, he likes to bring up the fact that Pope Leo's brother is someone who has spoken publicly that he appreciates the President, that he's a fan of President Trump's. And that seems to be somewhere where at least they can have a little bit of agreement and and common ground ground, at least on the Pope's sibling. They'll take that. Yeah, so what we're told, Hallie, is according to the court documents we've reviewed, this incident happened at the end of March. He relied on that guidance. No animals or people were harmed. We are confident that once the full picture is understood, people will see this for what it is." And we'll obviously, Hallie, keep an eye on this to see what develops further. Okay, Jesse Kirsch, thank you very much for that, appreciate it. When we come back, the viral campaign tied to one former reality TV star and how it could be changing the future of political ads, at least in one state. We've got the Spencer Pratt of it all after the break. So, in just a couple of hours from now, the candidates hoping to be the next governor of California and mayor of LA are going to face off on the debate stage. A political double header you can catch on NBCLA at local time, kicking off with the Democratic incumbent in the mayor's race debating two challengers, including reality TV star Spencer Pratt, who's making some headlines after reposting an AI-generated video that's pushing the boundaries of what political content might look like. Steve Patterson has that story in The Breakdown. A dystopian scene playing out in this viral AI-generated video that's adding fuel to an already fiery race for the next mayor of Los Angeles. It depicts a burning Hollywood sign, and Republican mayoral candidate Spencer Pratt, a former reality TV star, as a Batman-like character. While his Democratic foes hosting a lavish dinner are portrayed in an unflattering light, to say the least. Democratic incumbent Mayor Karen Bass looks like the Joker. Democratic challenger Nithya Raman as a puppet. "Let's move the drug addicts closer." And California Governor Gavin Newsom as royalty. "Look, if you were a transgender migrant..." Other celebrities also making appearances. The political parody racking up millions of views online. And if you think it's a glimpse into the future of political messaging, welcome to 2026. It's now. Even former Florida Governor Jeb Bush calling it, "Maybe the best political ad of the year." But it's not clear this is exactly a political ad. It was made by Menace Studio, a production company that says it's pioneering synthetic images. The founder posting the evening of May 4th, quote, "I think I have to make a @spencerpratt ad." And then dropping this video the next morning, perhaps a sign of how quickly such content can be produced. Neither the company nor the Pratt campaign responded to our request for comment on who paid for or commissioned the work. Spencer Pratt retweeted the video. Bass, Raman, and Newsom did not immediately respond to our request for comment on use of their likenesses in the video. We also reached out to Warner Brothers for comment on the use of imagery associated with Batman and did not hear back. Using AI-generated videos to send a political message isn't new. Newsom himself posted a video in December responding to a White House post, showing the President, Pete Hegseth, and Stephen Miller in handcuffs. The White House is also not shy about using AI for shock factor. Just yesterday on Cinco de Mayo, posting this fake photo of minority leaders Hakeem Jeffries and Chuck Schumer in sombreros, with a sign that says, "I love illegal immigrants." At least 15 campaign ads featuring AI-generated content have run since November, stoking fears the new tech could mislead voters ahead of the midterm elections. "With AI, you more and more can put words in people's mouths, you can have all sorts of candidates say things that they never really said, positions that they never really took, and that is something that is really terrifying." At least two dozen states already have laws regulating the use of political deepfakes in elections, including California, according to the National Conference of State Legislatures. But when third parties create political content, the line is not so clear. This latest video ends with people throwing AI tomatoes at their elected leaders, a bit of a sloppy ending to a new form of political slop that could have a growing impact on elections and beyond. Steve is joining us now. Okay, so let's talk more about this decision by Pratt, because he's somebody who I think has promised protections to people who work in the entertainment industry about AI, right? Right. I mean, look, I think some might find it dystopian in and of itself to retweet an AI video when you are running for mayor for a town in which several organizations have taken to the streets to fight against AI. Of course, though, that is up to the voters. As for the legality of it, look, it's a gray area.
