Next Up with Mark Halperin(98.3K)·69:17·6.6K views·2026-05-07
Mark HalperinHost, Next Up Marco RubioUS Senator, Florida JD VanceUS Vice President-elect
TL;DRMarco Rubio is rapidly emerging as a 2028 Republican frontrunner, potentially eclipsing J.D.
Vance through high media likeability and cross-factional appeal, while critics warn that the media is failing to cover the geopolitical realities of the Iran conflict. The Rubio...

TL;DR

Marco Rubio is rapidly emerging as a 2028 Republican frontrunner, potentially eclipsing J.D. Vance through high media likeability and cross-factional appeal, while critics warn that the media is failing to cover the geopolitical realities of the Iran conflict.

The Republican 2028 Outlook: Rubio vs. Vance

  • The Rubio "Boomlet": Mark Halperin reports an "explosion of interest" among Republican and Democratic sources regarding Marco Rubio’s potential.
      • Strategic Advantage: Rubio is currently viewed as the "most likely next president" by some because he can distance himself from the administration's record more easily than a Vice President.
      • The Briefing Room Lovefest: Rubio’s recent performance at the White House briefing went viral, demonstrating a level of humor and media rapport that Halperin argues J.D. Vance cannot currently match.
  • Vance’s Challenges: Despite being the current "heir apparent," Vance faces internal GOP concerns regarding his likeability and "nasty tweet persona."
      • The "Shackled" Candidate: Vance will be tied to the administration’s performance on the economy and the Iran war, which could become liabilities by 2028.
  • Historical Caution: Halperin reminds viewers of Rubio’s 2016 failures, including:
      • Poor Grassroots Fundraising: Rubio raised a "paltry" $7 million online compared to the hundreds of millions raised by candidates like Trump and Harris.
      • Debate Vulnerability: The famous 2016 "canned response" incident with Chris Christie remains a cautionary tale about his performance under pressure.
  • Prediction: Halperin suggests Vance and Rubio are close friends and unlikely to run against each other; they may instead form a "pre-announced" ticket to consolidate donor support early.

Media Critique: The Iran Conflict and Strategic Blindness

  • Superficial Coverage: Leland Vittert (NewsNation) criticizes the U.S. media for reducing a major Middle East realignment to "what it costs to fill up your car."
      • Ignoring the Regime: Vittert argues the press is "awfully uninterested" in the Iranian people's oppression and the regime’s history of violence, focusing instead on domestic political optics.
  • The Debate Over "Platforming":
      • Vittert’s Stance: He is skeptical of interviewing Iranian officials because they are "known liars" who demand restrictive ground rules (e.g., forbidding specific B-roll footage of regime violence).
      • Halperin’s Stance: He argues for booking the Iranian Foreign Minister nightly to ask "tough questions" and publicly list the questions they refuse to answer.
  • Information Blackout: Vittert notes that Iran has effectively "flipped a switch" on the internet, using Chinese and Russian tech to target citizens with Starlink, making free reporting nearly impossible.

Democratic Strategy: The "Stop Rubio" Playbook

  • Driving Wedges: Democratic strategist Hyma Moore outlines a strategy to "slow down the Rubio Express" by fueling friction between Rubio and the MAGA base.
      • The "Flip-Flop" Narrative: Reminding Trump supporters of Rubio’s 2016 personal attacks on Trump and his past support for immigration reform.
      • Policy Inoculation: Painting Rubio as a "war hawk" if conflicts in Iran or Russia escalate during his tenure as Secretary of State.
  • Institutional Bandwidth: Moore admits the DNC is currently too focused on the midterms to execute a full-scale "Stop Rubio" campaign, which may allow Rubio to build unmolested momentum.

Democratic "8 for '28" Rankings

  • The Top Tier: Gavin Newsom holds the #1 spot, while Kamala Harris has moved up to #2.
  • Controversial Entries:
      • Bernie Sanders (#5): Halperin defends including Sanders, citing his enduring grassroots appeal and the possibility of "stranger things happening."
      • Jon Ossoff (#8): Debut on the list due to his ability to win in purple/red Georgia and his "outsider vibes" despite being a Senator.
  • Demographic Obstacles: Commenters noted the "Democratic penchant" for seeking white male candidates to avoid alienating specific demographics, leading to internal party tensions regarding electability.

Notable Quotes

"There's an argument to be made that Marco Rubio is the most likely next president of the United States... I don't know how to divide the pie for odds of being the Republican nominee, but [Vance and Rubio] would divide about 95% of it right now."

— Mark Halperin

"The U.S. media... has boiled this remaking of the Middle East, confronting America's single longest-running enemy the past 47 years, into what does it cost to fill up your car."

— Leland Vittert

"If you're a Republican, you ought to be looking at Marco Rubio... he does have some mass appeal and he does have an ability, as we saw this week with the levity he brought to the press room."

— Hyma Moore

"You cannot beat an incumbent vice president running for president unless you rip their face off. That's just the way our politics work. So I do not think they'll [Rubio and Vance] run against each other."

— Mark Halperin

And we vault into our second year. Next Up. One-year anniversary in the rearview mirror. Year two begins now. I'm Mark Halperin. Welcome to Next Up, you Nexters. I'm the editor-in-chief of 2Way and your host here for another year. I am grateful to you for being here. We have a great show for you. Two fine gentlemen are going to walk us through a bunch of stuff. Leland Vittert will be here, the NewsNation chief Washington anchor and host of "On Balance with Leland Vittert" at 7:00 p.m. on NewsNation. Leland and I are going to talk about how the media has covered the Iran conflict. We will discuss what things are going well, maybe not so well, and his perspective as a former Middle East correspondent and someone who understands the nuances. Would you book the Iranian foreign minister or the head of the Iranian parliament? Would you book them on your TV show or your podcast, if you had one? Leland and I will sort that out. And then Hyma Moore will be here. He's a principal at Cornerstone Government Affairs. He used to have a big job at the DNC and is a new member announced this week on the morning meeting on 2Way. We're going to talk about how things are going politically from the Democratic perspective. Also, we have our new segment, "8 for '28 Hate." We'll take a look at how many of you reacted, including some brutal feedback, to the May rankings that I put out. This includes my daring, shocking, maybe super risky inclusion of Bernie Sanders on the list of the most likely Democratic presidential nominees. What are you all saying about it, and where do people think I got things wrong? A few of you think I got some things right, so we'll take a look at that. But first, my reported monologue this episode is on two Republican heavyweights, J.D. Vance and Marco Rubio. They happen to be close personal friends. This week, because of how they spent their Tuesday, there has been an explosion of interest amongst my sources in the question of who will and who should Republicans pick as their presidential candidate in 2028. We haven't done "8 for '28" rankings for the Republican side because up until now it's been a one-person show with J.D. Vance, and the polls continue to show that. But let's get into this conversation that I've had with my sources. There are a lot of Republicans and some Democrats this week asking why is this happening? Why is there an explosion of interest in Marco Rubio? It didn't just start this week. There was his speech in Europe a few weeks ago, his general performance in media interviews, and some of the questions around J.D. Vance. These have really led to this really interesting question: why is it happening? Why is Marco Rubio up and Vance down in the estimation of some, at least? I talked to a lot of Republicans since Tuesday about what's driving this and what's propelling this. Again, Marco Rubio did the White House briefing on Tuesday. Karoline Leavitt is out having her baby. Congratulations, Karoline. Meanwhile, J.D. Vance went to Iowa for a political rally for a House Republican incumbent there who's embattled and being targeted by Democrats. This is a great moment if you like American history because it's certainly the case—cutting away all the things that I think are off-base—that as we sit here today, there's an argument to be made that Marco Rubio is the most likely next president of the United States. I say that because we don't know who the Democrats are going to nominate. At this point, if you take Vance and Rubio—and there's other people who are interested, including Ted Cruz—between the two of them, I don't know how to divide the pie for odds of being the Republican nominee, but the two of them would divide that pie. If you take 100%, I think they'd divide about 95% of it right now. And if you're the nominee of a major party, your chances of being president are maybe not 50-50, but they're pretty close to 50-50 given the way the Electoral College is currently stacked. Before, I thought Vance was a lock—a lock to be the nominee. And now I'm less sure. I'm less sure. I still think he's most likely, and I'll get to that, but no doubt Rubio is up. A lot of it was supercharged for my sources by the briefing room appearance. He went in and he talked to the media. Almost all the questions were about Iran. There were some on Cuba and a few other topics, but it was a lovefest. If you're a Republican running for president in the current age, having the press—the mainstream, the dominant media—love you is a double-edged sword. Rubio right now is in a moment where he's loved by MAGA, even though he used to support immigration reform that's anathema to a lot of folks in MAGA. He's also loved by everyone in the administration. They universally praise him for the job he's doing, this unorthodox thing where he's both National Security Advisor and Secretary of State. He is loved by a lot of the Republican establishment who supported him in 2016 when he ran for president, as well as the donor class and elected officials. He is beloved by the media. His poll numbers aren't that great in all honesty, so that's an interesting question and variable. But when he walked in the briefing room, from the moment he walked in and throughout the entire time he was there with reporters—some from conservative organizations but a lot from dominant media—it was a lovefest, ladies and gentlemen. There was laughter and respect. Here's some of Marco Rubio in the briefing: "Are you guys all the TV people? No. I'm learning. They gave me a little map, I don't know where I put it, of the people here. Some of you had like red X's. I'm kidding, no, that's not true. What happened? Why is everybody giggling? What happened? I have two questions on two separate issues that come under... Do they get two questions for these? Two questions. There's a lot of people in here. That falls under your umbrella. All right, well, you answer... you can ask me two questions, I'll give you one answer. Go ahead. The pinky is closer. No, you don't have black, you have blue on. I'm colorblind, but I know blue and black. Right there, yes. This is chaos, guys. Secretary, many people want to know what is your DJ name? My DJ name? Your DJ name. You're not ready for my DJ name." I mean, again, there are other Republicans who could have that kind of banter, but not for that length of time and not around a topic on which the press is pretty contentious with the administration. From that event, one of the last answers the Secretary of State gave was when he was asked about his vision for the country. That answer has gone viral. The State Department decided to take the audio of his answer, set it to music, and include images of Secretary Rubio and the president to make a bigger splash. It's gotten so many views. This is the video the State Department put together off of one of Secretary Rubio's answers in that White House briefing on Tuesday: "I gotta ask you, what is your hope for America at a time such as this? My hope for America? My hope for America is what it's always been. I think it's the hope I hope we all share. We want it to continue to be the place where anyone from anywhere can achieve anything. Where you're not limited by the circumstances of your birth, by the color of your skin, by your ethnicity, but frankly, it's a place where you are able to overcome challenges and achieve your full potential. I think that should be the goal of every country in the world. But I think in the U.S., we're not perfect. Our history is not one of perfection, but it's still better than anybody else's history. Ours is a story of perpetual improvement. Each generation has left the next generation of Americans freer, more prosperous, safer. And that is our goal as well. And as we come upon this 250-year anniversary, I think we have a lot to learn and be proud of in our history. It is one of perpetual and continuous improvement where each generation has done its part to bring us closer to fulfilling the vision that the founders of this country had upon its founding." In all honesty, it's a great answer and not everybody in politics could pull that thing off off the top of his head. Marco Rubio, since his earliest days in Florida politics when he rose up in the legislature, has been—like Barack Obama before him—tagged for greatness as someone who will someday run for president, and as his supporters would say, someday would be president. He ran in 2016 and didn't do all that well, but then stayed in the Senate and rose up. He really developed a lot more confidence. There's the famous moment when he did the State of the Union response where he reached for the water bottle. Not a highlight, but he was respected by his colleagues in the Senate and overwhelmingly supported when he was confirmed for Secretary of State. They've turned on him now, the Democrats have, but then he became Secretary of State. Secretary of State is always, for both parties, one of the most sought-after jobs in politics. There are many people—and I'd put Joe Biden in this category—who would rather be Secretary of State than Vice President, and would want to be Secretary of State more than anything but President. It's just a huge job. Now he's got both jobs, right? People look at him in the party and they say: here's the issue. Vance is not as impressive as Rubio in the minds of some. He's not going to be as tied to the administration as Vance is, and there's worry that the midterms could go poorly or the economy may not be in great shape by 2028. What these folks say is, if we want to win the White House, we have to take the guy who can quit the administration; he doesn't have to stay as Secretary of State. It is harder for Vance to quit. Rubio can run as more opposed to the Trump-Vance record, can win the Hispanic vote and remake the politics of America. His performance, his likeability, and his sense of humor are all perceived as superior to Vance's. That's what the pro-Rubio crowd says. I've heard from so many people this week, unsolicited, and over the last couple weeks, saying, "We need Rubio," as Republicans say. Democrats say we wouldn't be the least bit afraid of Vance if he were the nominee, but we'd be afraid of Rubio due to the Hispanic vote, likeability, etc. But I've also heard a lot of amateurish, just too over-the-top posture towards Rubio. I said this on 2Way and I got hammered. I'm not against Marco Rubio. I quite like the guy and I think he's a very nice guy. I've always been impressed by him. But you have to not just go overboard. You can't just say, oh, he's the greatest thing ever and the greatest thing since Jiffy Pop, as I like to say. You have to look at the reality. When and if Marco Rubio decides to run for president, there will be MAGA people who will be shocked at some of the positions he's taken in the past, including on immigration. There will be questions of whether he is really just as impressive and unbeatable as some of his supporters think. So let's be serious, folks. The guy's run for president before. Now, some people run for president and do poorly and they learn from it. There's no doubt that Marco Rubio's performance on the national stage has improved since then, but there were some pretty weak moments when he ran a decade ago. He has learned a lot, but let's look at that. Let's look at the question of his 2016 experience and whether there are cautionary notes there. Are there reasons to think maybe both in running for the nomination and in a general election if he's the nominee, maybe Marco Rubio has some issues? One of the things I was so fascinated by in 2016 when he ran—not just against Donald Trump but a big field including Ted Cruz, Jeb Bush, John Kasich, Rand Paul, and others—was why he didn't catch on more. This is on a couple of measures. One is as a communicator: why wasn't he seen as greater with voters and pundits? Then there is fundraising and small-dollar donors. Here's a video. He had a great team working with him in 2016 to make videos intended to get his name ID up and to raise money. Here's a video he made. The guy's a huge sports fan. And again, just like with Vance, these are two normal guys. These are suburban dads who can speak to people in a very natural way and who don't need to be called Mr. Secretary or Mr. Vice President. They're fine. If you called them J.D. and Marco if you met them at a restaurant, they'd be fine with that. They do not stand on ceremony. Here's an ad video Rubio made in 2016, very representative of the kinds of ways his team tried to shape his image. "Marco Rubio catches footballs and fields questions. Super Bowl pick this year? The same one I've made for the last 40 years, the Miami Dolphins. Super Bowl pick winning percentage? I have never gotten a Super Bowl right. Sorry. Fantasy football name? This year I think we're going to call it the Marco Polo. Best coaching advice you ever gave your youth team? Have fun." All right, a very likeable video intended to get his name out there and to raise money. Of all the things from 2016 that are most cautionary for me—and it goes back to what I said about his poll numbers not being that great with voters—it is fundraising. Marco Rubio in 2016 should have been, on paper, a spectacularly strong fundraiser at the grassroots level. He should have raised a ton, and he didn't. Now this is a question I ask in general about candidates as they have trouble raising money online. Some do, some don't. It's a little bit ineffable. We have talked about this many times. It's hard to say what makes a great low-dollar fundraiser. You all know the importance of being able to raise money at the grassroots. It's cheaper than having big events. You can go back to people and keep raising from them until they max out. People have skin in the game once they give you money, and the press sees it as a sign, correctly, of grassroots support. Look how much money this guy raised as a candidate in 2016 online. Comparing it to two other folks, Donald Trump in 2024 and Kamala Harris in 2024, Rubio raised about $7 million online as small dollars in 2016. Now, that's 10 years ago and there are more ways to raise money online. But look at this: Donald Trump raised over 300 million and Kamala Harris 400 million. Again, this is apples to oranges because they were the general election nominees. But that is a paltry sum for someone of Rubio's profile. I wonder—I know that elites like him, I know a lot of fat cat donors like him, I know a lot of his colleagues like him, and he is universally liked by a lot of elites as we saw in the media—but do regular people like him? Then there's a question of performance. There's that famous State of the Union response I mentioned. This also happened in a debate in New Hampshire in 2016 when Chris Christie decided that he had to take out Rubio. Now Chris Christie is a very good debater, a former prosecutor, and a very smart guy. Christie decided going into this debate at Saint Anselm College basically, "I'm going to try to take out Rubio." Rubio went in there having done extremely well in Iowa with an opportunity to become the alternative to Trump. Look what Chris Christie did to filet Marco Rubio: "Let's dispel with this fiction that Barack Obama doesn't know what he's doing. He knows exactly what he's doing. He is trying to change this country. He wants America to become more like the rest of the world. We don't want to be like the rest of the world. We want to be the United States of America. And when I'm elected president, this will become once again the single greatest nation in the history of the world. Not the disaster Barack Obama has imposed upon us. You see everybody, I want the people at home to think about this. That's what Washington D.C. does. The drive-by shot at the beginning with incorrect and incomplete information, and then the memorized 25-second speech that is exactly what his advisors gave him. See Marco, the thing is this: when you're President of the United States, when you're Governor of a state, the memorized 30-second speech where you talk about how great America is at the end of it doesn't solve one problem for one person. Chris, your state got hit by a massive snowstorm two weeks ago. You didn't even want to go back. They had to shame you into going back. And then you stayed there for 36 hours and then he left and came back to campaign. Those are the facts. Here's the bottom line: this notion that Barack Obama doesn't know what he's doing is just not true. He knows exactly what he's doing. There it is! There it is, everybody! The memorized 25-second speech." So if you didn't follow it there, the point is that at the time, and again this is a decade ago, it gob smacked people. Christie accuses Rubio of giving a pre-canned answer, a rehearsed line. In response, part of Rubio's response was to repeat the exact same line he just said. Would he do that today? Probably not. But it was cautionary for people to say, how could the guy fall right into that? The other thing that happened back then was when he thought he could be the Trump alternative. He decided rather than denigrate Donald Trump's technique of criticizing and mocking people in a very out-there way, Rubio decided for one brief moment—and never did it again—he decided, you know what, I'm going to fight fire with fire. I'm going to go after Donald Trump the way he's gone after me, Rubio, and everybody else in the race. This is cringey. It's cringey. This is Marco Rubio for a brief moment in the 2016 campaign deciding to fight fire with fire and go after Donald Trump in personal terms: "He says he's always calling me little Marco. And I'll admit it, the guy, he's taller than me, he's like 6'2", which is why I don't understand why his hands are the size of someone who's 5'2". Have you seen his hands? They're like this. And you know what they say about men with small hands. You can't trust them! You can't trust them! You can't trust them! All right!" So again, if I'm using the 2016 Marco Rubio presidential campaign as a template, as a sample space to try to understand how he would be as a presidential candidate, I don't know. I mean again, that wasn't great. That didn't go well, and he never did it again because I think he recognized it didn't go well. He acknowledged as much. So poor debate performance, poor grassroots fundraising, poor judgment about how to react to being attacked personally. Does that mean he wouldn't be a good candidate? No. And again, he's improved so much since then. But I just think people who are measuring the Oval Office drapes for him, or at least the Republican nomination drapes, need to think about how he was as a candidate last time. Then we get to what I think is driving a lot of this, besides people loving Marco Rubio. Many people in my sources do, but there are concerns that Vance is not handling himself well. It isn't just that he is shackled to the administration, gas prices, a potential bloodbath in the midterms, and the Iran war. There is a belief that the downside image of Vance is true: that he's a phony, that he's not good with people, and that he comes off as aloof or odd to some. He went to Iowa on Tuesday. That's a big moment if you're thinking about running for president; you're doing a big political event in Iowa. It's a big deal. He met with some of the big power brokers in the party, the kind of people you meet with if you're thinking of running for president someday, because the Iowa caucuses will be big for the Republicans. Then he gave a stump speech. Here's a little bit of Vance in Iowa: "Please, please, take a seat. We're going to be here. You gave a politician a microphone. We got about three hours to go. So I don't want anybody, no heat exhaustion. Let me think. Finding fraud in the federal government, it's kind of like fishing in a barrel with dynamite. The media asked me who I voted for. None of your business. I can't wait to tell my wife when we get home that we've watched about nine episodes of X-Men and my son has eaten four chocolate chip cookies. She's going to be very pleased, she's going to be very pleased with the vice president." Okay, I found that perfectly likeable. But I will tell you, I've reached a conclusion. People improve. Rubio's improved. People who want to be president can improve. I will say that in the next two years, as people in the party and the media are comparing Rubio and Vance side-by-side, I don't think Vance can win the performance competition. I don't think in the likeability. I may be wrong, but I just think Rubio has improved enough, and the perceptions are such that Vance is going to have a hard time with people looking at him in a press conference, in an interview, or on the stump, whether he drops his nasty tweet persona or not. I think he's going to have a hard time winning that, and that's a big part of how people think about presidential candidates and presidents: likeability, performance, etc. Here's how the Vance trip got covered by some of the local TV stations in Des Moines: "Now, of course, this stop isn't all about 2026. There is a lot of speculation that Vice President J.D. Vance may actually run for president in 2028. And because Republicans in Iowa kick off the presidential race right here, Vance visiting this early state gives him an opportunity to test out his message with voters and also lay the groundwork for a potential bid for the White House. Trump's vice president J.D. Vance's trip to Iowa can help re-engage with those MAGA followers on things like tax cuts. Born around gas prices was one of the biggest things that people outside the event wanted to hear about today. They didn't really hear much of anything about it. Vance also applauded the Trump administration's tariff policies and framed the GOP as being on the side of working-class voters. If the vice president decides to throw his hat into the 2028 presidential run, Iowa will host the first contest to select the Republican Party's next presidential nominee." Pretty good coverage, but again, not as good as Rubio would have gotten probably. Here's the bottom line for me. These two guys are genuine friends, and even though people tell me I'm naive, you cannot beat an incumbent vice president running for president unless you rip their face off. That's just the way our politics work. So I do not think they'll run against each other. I also am not sure that one or both of them will run because they're young dads. The Vances are about to have a baby, and Rubio's kids are still relatively young. They both know what scrutiny is like, and they're both smart enough to know that if they run, the level of scrutiny they get will be unlike anything they've ever gotten before. So if Vance chooses not to run, and I think that's a possibility probably because of his kids, I think Rubio will be in an extremely strong position. I think he'll have the support of the president and Vance his friend, and I think he'll be pretty close to a lock for the nomination. If Vance runs, I think they'll run together. I think they'll be a ticket, and they may even announce as a ticket from the beginning of the campaign potentially and raise $2 billion before the New Hampshire primary. Vance is a very competitive guy. They both are. But my sense of it is Vance is in some ways more competitive than Rubio. Rubio's a little bit more laid back, a little bit more let things come to him. Obviously, you don't get to be Secretary of State if you're not competitive. They're both competitive, but I think Vance is more so. And I still think, for all the current boomlet for Rubio, I still think in the end if he runs—and he's more likely to run than not—he'll be endorsed by the president, and Rubio will be for him, not run against him, and maybe run as his running mate. That's where I see things based on all my reporting. But I will tell you, this force for Rubio—this belief that amongst some in the Republican Party in both the establishment and the MAGA wing that the Trump administration is not going to succeed enough to make it possible for Vance to just waltz into the White House—and this love affair with Marco Rubio ain't going away. He's still going to be charming and funny and have the capacity demographically to revolutionize the party: young, with the air of an outsider even though he's got experience. It's not going away. Unless Rubio really messes stuff up, that's not going away. And so this dynamic will continue. It's going to continue until Vance decides whether he's running. But the minute he decides, if it's a yes, I continue to believe contrary to some of my sources and a lot of the conventional wisdom that Rubio will stand down. We'll never know if that happens in the context of 2028 whether Rubio could beat Vance. I don't think it's going to come to that. We'll see. But everybody needs to temper their Marcomania; that's basically my message. Marcomania is strong in my world, and I'm not saying he's not a contender because he is, but you need to temper the Marcomania in part because of Vance's hold on the vice presidency and his support from Eric of Kirk and others, like Don Jr. We need to temper it looking at the 2016 experience and we need to temper it because as great as Marco Rubio is currently seen, there's always questions about what happens when you actually get on the field. All right, that's my report for today. Let me know what you think. Am I undervaluing the strength of Rubio? Am I overstating the strength of Vance? Let me know what you hear, what you think, what you see. Send me an email at nextuphalperin@gmail.com. And let everybody know about the show also. Send it around to your friends, your family, and the neighbors. Let everybody know that if they're not a Nexter, they're missing out. If you're watching us on YouTube, make sure you subscribe. If you subscribe at youtube.com/@nextuphalperin, you'll get the full episodes, but also when we put bonus content up there. And if you're listening on the podcast version, always make sure your downloads are turned on. Please tell people about the show if you like it. Our ad budget's not as big as it should be, let me put it that way. We rely on you to spread the word. Thank you for that. All right, a quick break and then when we come back, Next Up my friend Leland Vittert of NewsNation. We'll talk about media coverage of the Iran conflict. Leland Vittert is next up. Did you know that high blood pressure is the number one risk factor for mortality? One in two adults has it. So that means there's a 50-50 chance that you are a walking time bomb. Here's some good news though: you can take control of your blood pressure naturally without relying on Big Pharma. 120Life is a blend of great-tasting superfruit juices that have been shown to help lower blood pressure. It's backed by hundreds of doctors and trusted by thousands of people who've seen measurable results. And here's the very best part: it's completely risk-free. Try 120Life for two weeks and if you don't see a difference in your numbers, you get your money back. Go to 120life.com. That's 1-2-0-life.com. Use my code NEXTUP to save 20% and get yourself free shipping. This is serious. This is your life we're talking about. 120Life can help. All right, Next Up and joining me in just a moment, Leland Vittert. He's NewsNation's chief Washington anchor, and every night he makes time to host "On Balance with Leland Vittert" on NewsNation every weeknight at 7:00 p.m. Eastern Time. One of my favorite Bill Clinton jokes—and let's be honest, Bill Clinton is not our funniest president—but one of his favorite jokes is he used to say growing up in Arkansas, "We were so poor that the only way anyone could make money was we took in each other's wash." When I tell that joke, I think most people don't even understand what I'm talking about because they don't understand what that means, to take in each other's wash. The point is, ladies and gentlemen, that everyone I know now practically has their own show and we just go on each other's shows. And it's hilarious. I remember once—I won't say who it is—but a very famous person asked me to be on his show, and then I went on his show, and then I said, well, come on my show. And he's like, "Oh no, I can't do that." That's not the kind of person I like. But the kind of person I like, ladies and gentlemen, is our next guest. Next up is Leland Vittert, on whose program I appear regularly, including as recently as last night, and who has graciously accepted the reciprocal obligation to come on Next Up. Sir, welcome in. "Not an obligation, an opportunity of the highest order." All right, I appreciate that. I want to talk about one of your favorite topics, which is to be clear-eyed about the media. If you could put everybody in the media in a time machine and go to a week before the U.S.-Iran conflict began, and put them in the Pepsi Center or some big arena and said, "Okay, I've seen the future. Here's what I think we've done... we will do poorly, but with this magical intercession, let's do better." What would you tell them, the media, collective media, about how to cover the war better than we've done? "Mark, I will say that journalists make lousy media critics. But with that caveat and disclaimer, what is shocking to me that has been lost in this conversation is anything about Iran, the evils of Iran, the victories against Iran, the successes of the U.S. military which have been extraordinarily significant, and the long-range effects of remaking the Middle East. Donald Trump has single-handedly remade the Middle East between term one and term two with the Abraham Accords. The Middle East is realigned behind Israel and the United States and away from Iran. And that's because Donald Trump understood the essential truth of the Middle East, which is that Iran is the root of all evil. The Palestinians and the Iranians have propagated this lie that the Palestinians were the root of all problems in the Middle East. Solve the Palestinian issue with Israel and somehow there would be figs, dates, olives, and happy camels in the Middle East. That was a complete farce, and that was meant to distract from the evil that the Iranians were doing. And the U.S. media that prides itself on being so learned and so understanding of world affairs has boiled this remaking of the Middle East, confronting America's single longest-running enemy the past 47 years, into what does it cost to fill up your car. And that's... yes, because the American people care about what it costs to fill up our car, but most people care because it is the lead story at 6:00 p.m. every night on the local news and the national news." I know you throat-cleared and said you wouldn't be good at this, but that was brilliant. It's just... it's spot on. And of course, Secretary Rubio made this point in the briefing room the other day and really tried to bring back the focus of the press to at least be part of the conversation to say this is—as the president says all the time—this has been the mission of every one of his media predecessors to try to deal with this, and he may fail but he's trying to deal with it. Iran is not some cute, adorable nation with happy camels. It has been the source of murder and slaughter of both its own people and others, and you're absolutely right, that doesn't imbue the coverage. That's one of my points, too. I'd literally love your perspective on this. To me, the challenge not just for the media but for the American government and anyone who wants to be involved in the conversation about this is what's going on in Iran? Are restaurants open? How much... how disrupted is business? And then of course what's going on inside the government. Here's the thing I'm confused about, I'd love your perspective on it. Every so often we see the Iranian foreign minister like on CNN or talking to Kristen Welker. Every so often there's this one New York Times reporter who's interviewing like today she interviewed like a guy who works for the Iran Chamber of Commerce. It's possible... every so often I've been able to interview like an academic, like a pro-regime academic from Iran. But these things happen very rarely. It seems like in the age in which we live, we should be talking to Iranians every night. I know there's a crackdown on talking to citizens, but why is that episodic and infrequent as opposed to every day? "Well, the problem for a lot of Iran is that they're cut off from the world, right? Having a Starlink or any kind of outside access, sort of like North Korea, is a death sentence now. The Iranians have cut off the internet basically with a big switch. Why the Israelis and the Americans didn't during the war take out Iran's ability to limit the internet and take out their firewall systems, I don't know. Maybe it wasn't possible but that certainly would help the flow of information out of Iran. And the Iranians are using Russian systems and Chinese systems to find anybody with a Starlink and kill them. To your point about why isn't the regime putting more people out? I don't really know. I think probably one of the biggest problems right now inside of Iran is that there is a power struggle going on, which by the way, is another feature, not a bug, of the U.S. military action. Anytime your enemy is fighting with each other, that's better than... it's a lot better than them fighting with you or being able to reconstitute and rearm. So I think your point's well made that there's not a lot of information about what's coming out of Iran. To that point, I think that should make the American media all the more skeptical of the Iranian regime and of what they say and all the more thoughtful about the evils of Iran. I'll just put a little finer point on what you said about killing millions. I think if you add it up, Iran is responsible for the killing of more people in the past 47 years since the Islamic Revolution than any other country that still exists. For a media that prides itself on looking after women's rights and gay rights and protecting those that are oppressed, they are awfully uninterested in the people that are being oppressed in Iran and awfully loving of the oppressors inside Iran, which I think tells you so much about not only the media but also so many Democrats who are... I don't want to say rooting for Iran, but certainly aren't doing anything to help the American cause because they view it's bad for Donald Trump." Yeah, I couldn't agree with you more. Again, there's still this question to me of like, are you trying to book the Iranian foreign minister every night and getting a "no"? Or... "We've reached out. It's very opaque of how you get there. I also have... and I spent a lot of time in the Middle East and I've interviewed a lot of terrorists and everything else. I am very suspect of giving the Iranian regime unfettered access to American airways, insofar as I think that they are known liars, they're known propagandists, and they are professionals at not answering the question. Now, sometimes you can say, well, by doing that interview you're showing America who they are. Fine. There's not much the Iranian foreign minister has said that has been true in the past eight weeks. I have a hard time agreeing to often their ground rules that are required before any kind of interview to be willing to let them just sort of spew whatever it is that they want to spew." I wanted to draw... that was on my list of things I wanted to talk to you about because I think I'm sure you and I mostly agree about this, but based on the way you just framed it, I disagree. I'll put people on Next Up or on 2Way who people say you're platforming them. I platform anybody as long as they're interesting and/or they have a following. For instance, we had Laura Loomer on 2Way several months ago, and people criticized that. I'd put on anybody. The example I always give has been Bin Laden. When Bin Laden was the chief terrorist in the world, I was at ABC, and John Miller—who's had a bunch of other jobs—did a big interview with Bin Laden. I'd put the Iranian foreign minister on every night. I'd ask him tough questions, but even if he ignored them, I just think there's... again, I'm not trying to have a fight with you, I'm just trying to tease out this topic. I just think if, you know, the Iranian foreign minister, I'd want to hear him every night and I'd ask him hard questions and if he didn't answer them, at the end I'd say, "Here are the questions you didn't answer." But what's... what's the objection to putting him on every night? Just say... say it again, the argument against it. "I think there's a nuance here because when I was at Fox and overseas I interviewed Muhammad al-Zawahiri, Ayman al-Zawahiri's brother who was the leader of the Benghazi attacks and one of the original founders of the militant Muslim Brotherhood. And I had no problem putting him on because there were direct questions to ask and his obfuscations made a real point. My bigger issue with the Iranian foreign minister is what are the ground rules that they are requiring, number one. And number two..." Sorry, before you leave number one, what's an example of a ground rule that they would require? "Whatever it is. I don't want to get into the inner discussions that we've had and how..." Well, but not in specific, just give me... is it taking topics off limits? "Topics may be off limits or you can't put up video. I've had that. I'm not going to speak to the Iranian foreign minister but I have had discussions where 'Oh, you can't put up video of X while talking to me.' Right? Because the most obvious thing to the Iranian foreign minister is to put up video of them killing their own people and all of the body bags and say, 'How can you claim to be the legitimate government of Iran? How many more of your own people are you going to slaughter?' Right? There's all these sort of obvious things. Again, I don't want to get into our internal negotiations. That's just an example. But my point being, I don't have a problem with platforming people. My bigger question about the Iranian foreign minister for my viewers is if you say you'll put them on every night, at some point if somebody's just going to sit there and deny that the sky is blue, or tell you, 'Well, we've agreed to all these things that we haven't.' That to me doesn't really serve our viewer in terms of enhancing their knowledge, right? Or understanding of the situation. I think it's pretty obvious to anybody who knows the Iranians, number one they're lying, number two they'll tell you kind of whatever you want to hear in the moment and then do something else. I'd a lot rather take a hard look at what the Iranians are doing than what they're saying." All very sensible. You're a fancy anchor now, you almost never leave the air-conditioned studio. But back when you were a Middle East correspondent and you traveled around, if you could... if your safety could be guaranteed—and that's a crazy hypothetical—but if you could be in Tehran now, would that be a good assignment? And like, what would you do? "Well, it's a great question. You say if your safety could be guaranteed. If you say what the regime wants you to say, your safety's guaranteed. That's fairly fair. I don't... again, I don't know what the value in going would be if you couldn't report freely. And you saw CNN get a lot of flak for just puppeting the regime's talking points in return for being allowed to go in. So that I would not do, no. I think if you were there, obviously you'd want to report on what are the Iranian resistance groups doing. They exist, we know they exist. Why have they not risen up? Is there a resistance or possible secondary government, government-in-waiting forming? Is there fractures inside the regime? What do those look like? These are all things that if you were there and had kind of fairy-godmother-like protection, you could go report on. That's not something you could do right now." It's interesting because again, the AP like a few weeks ago, they had some reporters drive from, I think it was north of Tehran several like miles and miles, and they drove to Tehran and on the route they did reporting just like you're describing. And again, I just don't quite understand why they were able to do that once. I understand American organizations not being in, but shouldn't the Dutch be in there? Shouldn't the British be in there? "You know, Mark, the one thing I learned when I was in the Middle East and I was there for the transition between when journalists were a protected class to when journalists became targets. And it happened very quickly. Basically from Egypt, the Arab Spring, through Libya, and then into Syria when ISIS started beheading people. That was only a couple of years. And I think there's a lot of journalists now, rightfully so in a lot of organizations, that realize that we are no longer a protected class. In fact, we're a targeted class. We are now legitimate targets to then be traded or tortured or held or anything like that. So it doesn't surprise me, especially with the cruelty of the Iranians, that no one is sending people in. The flip side of that is for media organizations, especially Americans, that seem so concerned with defense of democracy, the freedom of the press, anyone who dares attack the press or criticize them, they are awfully accepting of everything the Iranians do in the name of brutality and regime survival that they ignore. And it's perplexing to me. The only obvious answer is anything that could be good for Donald Trump, they won't report on. And that sort of seems to be where we're at." If you could have any two bookings on your show tonight related to the Iran war broadly, not counting President Trump—any two bookings—who would you take? "And into the sense that they would tell me what? Is it that they're going to actually answer the questions or they just... I can't guarantee. You can have any two. It doesn't have to be Iranians. It could be anybody in the world besides President Trump to talk about the Iran war and you take what you get, they may not tell you a thing. Who would your two choices be?" "MBS of Saudi Arabia. Yeah. Either MBS or MBZ of the United Arab Emirates. Yeah. I think one of those two would be really interesting. I think you'd have to say, and I don't have a... you'd have to say like the head of the Iranian Islamic Revolutionary Guard, of the IRGC, whoever is the head of that right now. That would probably be the answer. But you'd have to say that... I would put it opaquely, right? Whoever is actually in charge of Iran right now, or who speaks for that person, we just don't know who it is." God help your booker if that were your instructions to them: "Go find out who's in charge of Iran and then book them." I'll tell you mine. Mine would be that person, same thing, and President Xi of China. That'd be a good booking, right? To get him? "Yeah, that would be a very good booking, especially ahead of the summit. Putin right now would be a very good booking." Yeah, I was thinking of Putin except we know his answers would be ridiculous and your show's only an hour so you'd probably get a couple questions... "Well, yeah, and you'd get a long history of all the ways Russia has been aggrieved. But Xi is the same way. I think those guys who are so scripted like that, it's a great booking and it's an uninteresting interview." I would break Xi's discipline. I would wear him down and he'd be confessing to everything and giving the inside story of his efforts to make peace. Leland, thank you, man. Grateful to you for making time. "Always fun. Thanks, Mark." Ladies and gentlemen, if you enjoyed this moment, this opportunity, this brief interlude with Leland, believe it or not, you can catch him every night for a full hour minus the commercials, at 7:00 p.m. Eastern Time on NewsNation, Leland Vittert. Leland, thank you, man. All right, up next, or as we say here Next Up, "8 for '28 Hate." We take a look at your reactions to my May rankings of the eight most likely Democratic presidential nominees in 2028. The hate will be on full display right back with that. That's Next Up. Are you being lied to? They tell you to defer paying your taxes by saving, say in a 401k or an IRA, because you'll retire in a lower tax bracket. But if that were true, why are so many retirees now in the highest tax brackets of their lives? It's time now to get the truth and discover a better way to grow and protect your money. Bank On Yourself is the proven retirement plan alternative that big banks and Wall Street desperately hope you never hear about. It gives you guaranteed predictable growth that doesn't go backward when the market drops. It can provide you tax-free retirement income under current tax law, putting you in control of your future tax rate. You also have control of your money. Access it when you need it with no government penalties or any restrictions, and your money keeps growing even when you use it. You can get a free report that reveals how you can Bank On Yourself and enjoy tax-free retirement income, guaranteed growth, and the control of your own money. Just go to bankonyourself.com/mark and get that free report. That's bankonyourself.com/mark. Again, go now to bankonyourself.com/mark. All right, Next Up, we're looking at the reaction, questions, comments, concerns, and a little bit of hate from what we had on our last episode, my May rankings, "8 for '28," most likely Democratic presidential nominees. As always, people have some strong feelings. Let's look at the latest rankings for this month just to remind everybody: Gavin Newsom 1, I moved Harris from further down to number 2, Josh Shapiro from 2 down to 3, Pete Buttigieg number 4, debuting Bernie Sanders at number 5, which I knew would cause some controversy, Rahm Emanuel 6, Andy Beshear at 7, and Jon Ossoff at 8, first time Ossoff on the list. Again, the ones I knew would be controversial. Harris is always going to be controversial. But I knew the Sanders one would and maybe Ossoff. Sure enough, that's what the reaction has been like. Lots of emails and lots of comments on both Instagram and on X. So let's start with the Sanders one. I made my best case and I still have people, both political professionals and some people outside politics who are interested, questioning it. Lots of people basically just saying I'm nuts. But here's one I liked from Kenny. Kenny wrote this, Kenny said: "Mark, I thought you were crazy to include Bernie Sanders, but I think you made a strong case for him. Stranger things have happened." I appreciate that. Kenny goes on to say that he thinks that both Beshear and Rahm Emanuel should be higher because conservatives will like them. We'll get back to that theme in a second. But Kenny, I appreciate you listening closely and hearing me out on Sanders. I've heard a few other people say that, but most people still saying I'm nuts. Here's another one and is an issue I raised in the monologue about the rankings. This is on X from MF Hussey CFA CMT: "You've said it yourself, Mark. There is no way today's D Party will nominate a Jew. So no Bernie, no Rahm, no Josh. I'll add that a party disproportionately reliant on Black support won't nominate a gay man. There, fixed it for you and now you're at four." So what he says is if the party's not going to nominate a gay person or Jew, my list gets cut in half and of course as we've discussed here before, some people say they won't nominate a woman or a person of color. So if we do all that, we're basically just down to Gavin Newsom and Andy Beshear. They can arm wrestle for it. All right, another big theme that came up was this question of adding Ossoff to the list. And our friend Andrew Kolvet at Turning Point USA wrote this on X: "What has Ossoff done for Dem insiders to mention him? Or is it just the classic Democrat penchant to want to find white males to run because they know how much they've alienated the larger demo?" Andrew, thank you for that. When I do my reporting on this, it's not just quote-unquote "Dem insiders." In addition to talking to some Republicans, I'm constantly sampling, thanks to 2Way and Next Up Nexters here, grassroots opinion. What justifies Ossoff being on there is, first of all, he's on track to win reelection in a pretty red-slash-purple state, but state certainly that's more red than blue. He's just demonstrating a lot of the things Democrats want—grassroots Democrats want. He is very tough on Trump, but also talking about the real lives of real people and issues that matter to folks. So Ossoff has said he's not interested in running, but I'm going to expect if he does win reelection handily—and in Georgia for a Democrat that means a couple points—I suspect he's going to be under a lot of pressure to look at it. He's young, and even though he's a senator he's got the vibes of an outsider, and the party definitely wants that. Lastly, again this issue of whether the Democrats will vote for a centrist. This goes back to one of these tensions which we talked about in the monologue. This is an email from William or Billy. Billy says: "Hi, Mark. I enjoy your take on this. As a former centrist Democrat now Independent, I'm personally pulling for those centrist Democratic candidates like Emanuel, Beshear (I'm from Kentucky), and Ossoff. What about Jared Polis? I think he's fantastic." "He'd be a great candidate, but I'd enjoy hearing your take." So again, here's the tension as we talked about last episode. The party tends to nominate people who are more moderate, but there's also this issue of, in some ways, electability in the general. But this is not about who can win a general election, and we know that the party is more progressive in terms of the grassroots and the influence of the grassroots than it's been really in a generation. And so maybe a moderate will win—that's still my gut—but the progressives like Sanders, or whoever runs instead of Sanders, they're going to have a moment. They're going to have their moment to try to win. So Jared Polis is interesting, Governor of Colorado. He certainly would like to be president; there's no doubt about that. But my sense from talking to folks is he's not thinking of running this cycle. But let's see. Somebody like that who can raise a decent amount of money, not as much as others, let's see if he decides to jump in if some of these other people on the list now take a pass. Always grateful to get feedback on 8 for '28, and thank you for whether you sent an email or went on social media. Love to hear the comments and what people are thinking. It helps me work through as I think about the June edition. So thank you for that. Always like to hear what I got wrong and what you agree with. All right, Next Up, after a quick break, Hyma Moore, Democratic strategist and one of the new members of the 2Way Morning Meeting community contributors, will be here. Hyma Moore is next up. Think about the last 30 bucks that you spent, maybe on a streaming subscription you don't watch or a lunch you've already forgotten about. That's 30 dollars that's gone forever. Acre Gold lets you turn that lost money into physical 24-karat Swiss gold. You pick a plan you like, your balance builds, and once you hit the price of a bar, they ship it straight to your front door. We're talking real gold here, in your hand, at your house. And over time you're sitting on something that's been valuable since the dawn of civilization. And for you collectors out there, they've just dropped the limited edition Hot Wheels collection. These are officially licensed by Mattel, really cool, strictly capped, and once they're gone, they are history. While you're checking those out, claim your free entry to the Speed Club sweepstakes. They're giving away a 1-gram Hot Wheels gold bar plus a massive grand prize, the 10-gram 24-karat gold Hot Wheels bar. Both come in official collector packaging and they're up for grabs right now. Start stacking for just 30 dollars at getacregold.com/mark. Again, that's getacregold.com/mark. Go on, subscribe today. All right, Next Up and joining me now, Hyma Moore, principal at Cornerstone Government Affairs and a former director in the office of the Democratic National Committee's Chair's office. And this week he joined the 2Way family officially. He's been a regular on the Morning Meeting and 2Way tonight, but as one of our contributors now on the Morning Meeting. Hyma, welcome back to Next Up. "Good to see you. Happy to be a part of the family." Yeah, really happy to have you here and grateful to you for making time. We talked, I talked in my monologue about Marco Rubio and how he's having this moment. I remember in 1998, George W. Bush was seen as a big threat to win the White House: successful governor in Texas, appealed to Hispanic voters, Black voters, young voters. And the Democratic Party knew we got to slow this guy down. We got to slow him down. So, you're too young. You ever heard of Tony Mauro? Do you know who that is? "Yeah, I do actually." All right, so Tony was the person nominated to run against Bush, and Al Gore went down to Texas and campaigned with Tony Mauro and they did their best to prop him up. But Bush destroyed him and built up momentum to become the Republican nominee and a formidable general election candidate. Here's my question to you: if you were at the DNC and you were in charge of the "Stop Rubio" war room to say, "Look guys, if this guy continues unmolested politically and becomes the Republican nominee in 2028, he's going to be hard to beat." And right now the media is just a slobber lovefest. So if you were in charge of that war room—Oppo research, social media, etc.—what would you do to try to slow Marco Rubio down? "Well, one, Mark, we can't be surprised. I think the DNC, we did this a few years ago where you would do the book on all the prospective candidates. And so I think they probably have one on Marco Rubio already because we've seen this coming for a while. So you start with the book and then look, Marco Rubio, who's been a senator and been in politics for so long, he's said so many things, he's made so many policy decisions, and so you go back and you have to comb through all those things. You either remind people when he was absolutely in opposition to Donald Trump to just sort of draw some distinction between the two of them and sort of play them against each other. And then two, you have to remind people of things that he's done and said that are antithetical to what he's doing right now, what he's saying right now. So make him seem like he is a flip-flopper or someone who's wishy-washy. And then you look at all his social media. He's said things about Donald Trump in 2016, he said things about other Republicans in 2016; you remind people what those things were." So you're absolutely right about phase one, which is you got to collect all that stuff and, as you suggested, you can make him seem unappealing to the general election electorate, but you can also drive wedges between him and MAGA and the president. But how do you execute that? Because they had a book on Bush too in '98. Where does it happen? On social? Do you need a surrogate? How do you get that out in the bloodstream to start taking... slow him down? "Well, you need all the above. And you know, right now, what I imagine—I don't know, I haven't heard this because I don't work there anymore—but what I imagine Democrats are doing is they're... all these reporters who you're talking about who are feening over Marco Rubio, the Democratic machine is sending them Oppo research, sending them quotes, sending them things to just sprinkle into the coverage of Marco. They'll say, 'Oh, we love him, but in 2016 he said this.' So that's one part. The second thing, you know we're in the world of online influence right now, and so you have to figure out a way to get to the MAGA influencers, to get to the super liberal influencers, and sort of feed them a message that—and you mentioned this a second ago—on the MAGA side, he's not MAGA enough. He is not a Donald Trump acolyte, and so how do we feed that flame? And then the second piece on the liberal side, just to sort of inoculate on the general election, start feeding that 'This is why Marco is too conservative' or 'This is why he'll take it too far, too much of a hawk' or something like that. So that's a big piece of it. But a lot of this right now, Mark, you have to sort of ride the wave. Marco's having a moment and I think we knew he would have a moment. And so you don't expect to undercut him at this exact moment. This is going to be something that they're going to build on for months and months to come. And so you'll see some friendly reporters, some liberal reporters, who are going to start writing these stories that, 'Here's the real biography of Marco Rubio from a liberal perspective.' And so you'll see those things happen over the next couple months. It won't all happen right now." What would be, let's say between now and the midterms, what would be a realistic but dream hit where if you worked in that DNC war room, you say the National Review attacked him or the Wall Street Journal editorial page? What would be something that could happen this year to slow him down that would be realistic? "Well, I think—and we'll talk about this at some point—but look, the Iran war, the continued relationship with Russia or lack thereof, and then obviously what happens in China over the next couple weeks, those are going to be big pieces of him telling his story because you gotta think about this: one of the things that J.D. Vance is going to have an advantage over Marco is he'll be in the news all the time during the primary season and during the general election because he can do that. Secretary of State can't do domestic politicking. And so if the war goes poorly, if the China summit goes poorly, if Russia continues to become aggressive, then we can start drawing a picture around him being a war hawk and things just not going well and not being America First. But he will get some distance from the domestic policy stuff because J.D. will be the face of that over the next couple months. So it's going to be tricky sort of telling the big 'Marco isn't the right guy' story right now. That's probably more of a later fall story than it is pre-midterm." We talked about your mom and her sensibility sometimes. Could you see your mom voting for Marco Rubio in a general election against the Democrat? "Look, I'll be honest with you. I had said this six, seven months ago. I said if you're a Republican, you ought to be looking at Marco Rubio and you have to give him a really close look because he does have some mass appeal and he does have an ability, as we saw this week with the levity he brought to the press room... we haven't had that amount of levity in the press room in quite some time. And the way he commanded the press and the way he commanded such a different, diverse audience, so I think he will have some appeal to Black southern women who are religious and he can build from that." So if you were at dinner with a bunch of your friends who were not professional Democrats, but people who've voted for Biden and Harris and Obama, and they said, "You know, Marco Rubio seems like a nice guy, young dad, and he's funny and multicultural," what would you say to them to keep the Rubio flame from burning brighter? "I think the only anecdote to that right now is having a viable Democratic candidate that Democrats and Independents can coalesce around and start seeing some of those same attributes in that person. But as it stands, you know, there's something to say about compassionate conservatism as you and I both know, and that was one of Bush's big components. And so if Marco can continue to show that level of compassion and exude that through social media and through the media, I think it's going to be really hard to make a case that he won't be a viable nominee for the Republicans." Yeah, I just think so much about the Bush experience in '98. You've laid out in this conversation probably more thought than's actually been given to it at the DNC because they're so busy. They've got so much to do there. And the thought of saying we're going to put serious bandwidth, we're going to develop a whole ad campaign, a social media campaign, and brief surrogates on this when they're trying to win the midterms, it's just not realistic. It's unfortunate for the party's point of view if we fast forward to January of 2029 and Marco Rubio's taking the oath of office for president because it's a little bit of a paradox. They know they need to do it but they just don't have the time or the bandwidth or the resources. And Rubio, he's not even doing that much. Like there's such a love affair for him now that he does one stint in the briefing room and all of a sudden people are just so gaga for him. And again I'll say, I've heard from so many Democrats this week, either "This guy's really going to be a challenge if he's the nominee" or "Hey, I kind of like him,"—again, not professional Democrats—saying, "I like him. What's wrong with Rubio? Why wouldn't I support Rubio if it were whoever for the Democrats?" "No, you're going to be hearing that a lot more. But look, there's a lot of talk about the DNC right now and about the leadership. And I know Chairman Ken Martin very well; I've worked with him for years. And I think two things that I think he's really good at: he's really good at walking and chewing gum, because you know he had to do that when he was in Minnesota, and Minnesota, as we all know, has just been a mess for quite some time and he's had to wade through that. But the second piece is he's really good at elections, and you've seen... and while he can't articulate the story right now, but he's been at the center of winning a lot of these Democratic elections over the last couple months. And so he's looking toward 2028 because he understands that Jeffries and Schumer have to put more stake in the midterms than he does, and so he does have the ability to look forward a little bit more than the other committees." Next time you see him, ask him what his plan is to slow down the Rubio Express. "No, I will; I'm going to text him after this." All right, text me, forward the text. Hyma Moore, grateful to you, love having you on and like I said, I knew you'd have such a clear-eyed view about how to get this done. Every day I'm just going to be checking, is anybody listening to Hyma's advice on how to get this done. That's what I'll be looking for. "I love that. Let's play that clip. I love it." Good to see you. All right, that's it for today's program. We'll be back on Tuesday, a whole brand new episode. Hope everybody has a great weekend. A reminder: please share the program around, subscribe yourself on YouTube and where you get your podcasts, but also tell all your friends to become Nexters. Thank you. Join us always so you know what's coming next up.

Why Elon Musk is even more powerful than you think | The Global Story

BBC News(19.7M)·27:20·59.0K views·2026-05-07
Elon MuskBillionaire Tech Entrepreneur Donald TrumpPresident of the United States
TL;DRElon Musk has transitioned from a political advisor to a "global sovereign," wielding more power than many heads of state through his near-monopoly on satellite internet (Starlink) and his aggressive expansion into space-based AI infrastructure.
The Lawsuit: Musk is...

TL;DR

Elon Musk has transitioned from a political advisor to a "global sovereign," wielding more power than many heads of state through his near-monopoly on satellite internet (Starlink) and his aggressive expansion into space-based AI infrastructure.

The OpenAI Legal Battle: Vision vs. Profit

  • The Lawsuit: Musk is currently suing OpenAI CEO Sam Altman and President Greg Brockman in a high-profile trial in Oakland, California.
      • Breach of Mission: Musk alleges the founders breached their original nonprofit contract by transitioning to a for-profit model to enrich themselves and investor Microsoft.
      • Core Demands: Musk is seeking the removal of Sam Altman as CEO, $150 billion in damages (to be returned to the nonprofit), and an unwinding of OpenAI’s for-profit entities.
  • Defense Counter-Arguments: OpenAI’s legal team labels the suit "sour grapes," noting that Musk left the company in 2018 before its massive success with ChatGPT.
      • Competitive Hypocrisy: Lawyers highlighted that Musk’s own AI venture, xAI, has used OpenAI’s models for training despite Musk’s public criticisms of the company.

Starlink and Geopolitical Influence

  • Unrivaled Infrastructure: Starlink, a division of SpaceX, currently operates over 10,000 satellites in low Earth orbit (LEO), serving 10 million users across 150 countries.
  • Geopolitical Gatekeeping: Musk has demonstrated the power to personally dictate the flow of information in war and crisis zones:
      • Ukraine/Russia: Musk reportedly refused Ukraine's request to use Starlink for an attack on Crimea due to concerns over nuclear escalation, while also facing pressure to block Russian forces from the network.
      • State Interventions: He has selectively provided or restricted access in Iran (during blackouts) and Venezuela (following political meetings with Donald Trump).
  • Raw Power: Analyst Lily Jamali notes that because Musk controls a "life-or-death" resource like internet connectivity, he operates with the influence of a head of state but without the constraints of term limits or democratic oversight.

The "Space Grab" and Data Centers

  • The 1-Million Satellite Ambition: Musk has requested US government permission to launch 1 million satellites, a move critics describe as a "land grab" in space to prevent competitors like Jeff Bezos (Amazon) from securing orbital slots.
  • Space-Based Data Centers: Musk aims to move AI data centers into orbit to solve two terrestrial constraints:
      • Power Access: Space provides 24/7 solar energy, sidestepping the energy-intensive demands of AI on Earth's power grids.
      • Technical Hurdles: Experts question the feasibility of this plan, citing difficulties in cooling hardware in a vacuum, maintenance logistics (robots vs. humans), and slow data transmission speeds.

Legal and Regulatory Headwinds

  • Judicial Reprimand: Judge Yvonne Gonzalez Rogers, presiding over the OpenAI case, ordered Musk to stop disparaging Altman on his platform X, describing his social media activity as "distractions" and "playground" behavior.
  • European Probes: French authorities are investigating X’s algorithm for political interference, while the UK and EU are scrutinizing the AI chatbot "Grok" for generating Holocaust denial content and non-consensual deepfakes.
  • US State Action: Several dozen Attorneys General in the United States are taking action against xAI regarding the generation of sexualized deepfakes.
"If you can provide or withhold internet service, that is raw power because it's something that supersedes government. It is a global resource in a way." — Lily Jamali, BBC Correspondent
"I know that in Elon Musk's case in particular... he is not well-liked... as long as you can stay fair, you can stay on this jury." — Judge Yvonne Gonzalez Rogers, Federal Courthouse in Oakland
"He is probably the most important figure in the tech industry and is making all kinds of choices that affect every single one of us." — Lily Jamali, BBC Correspondent
Is Elon Musk now even more powerful than he was when he was at the center of politics in Washington? In May of last year, Musk left the White House and the Department of Government Efficiency that he was running. And sure, in the year since, he has remained an extremely high-profile figure. But he's also sort of been out of the limelight until the last few weeks, when he's been back in the headlines for a lawsuit against his former colleague, the CEO of OpenAI, Sam Altman. And that got us looking into what exactly the world's richest man has been doing in the year since he left the Trump administration. Turns out, he has quietly been shoring up his control of space and internet around the globe. From the BBC, I'm Asma Khalid in Washington DC. And welcome to the global story on YouTube. Our episode today, we want to take the long view on what exactly Elon Musk has been up to since he left the White House about a year ago in May of 2025. And at the outset here, I will say I don't think that we can have this conversation in this moment about Elon Musk without mentioning that he is currently suing the CEO of OpenAI, Sam Altman. That trial of those two tech titans is underway as we tape, and I know you have been in the courtroom in California covering it all. What is this lawsuit actually about? This case is taking place at the Federal Courthouse in Oakland, California, so just down the road from me. Elon Musk and OpenAI CEO Sam Altman actually started OpenAI together with some others back in 2015. And it was a nonprofit; that was the vision for it, that was supposed to develop AI technology safely. And Musk gave a lot of the money for it starting, is that right? That's exactly right. He ended up donating up to somewhere on the order of $38 million. And, you know, the idea behind this was to develop AI safely and to develop it for the benefit of humanity—a very lofty goal, but one that Musk thought was really important to have in sort of the founding rubric for the company. So now, he says that Altman and his colleague Greg Brockman, who's the President of OpenAI, breached this nonprofit deal that they had, this nonprofit contract, and ended up enriching themselves along the way. So at a certain point, they decided to become a for-profit. It's a lot easier to collect money from investors that way, and boy is it expensive to develop AI technology. And so in doing that, what Musk is saying is that the for-profit entity kind of superseded at a certain point the nonprofit that they had created together, the nonprofit that he had donated all this money to. And that's his argument. On the other side, you have OpenAI and Altman and Brockman saying that this is really just sour grapes on Musk's part, that he is upset that he left OpenAI when he did in 2018. You know, just about four years later they launched ChatGPT which was this massive overnight success. And it's worth noting here that Musk is actually working on his own AI startup xAI which is a competitor to OpenAI. And so that's another part of the company's argument is that he's just trying to kind of derail one of the top players while he's trying to get his own thing off the ground. What exactly does Elon Musk want? What is his end goal here? A couple things. For one thing, he wants $150 billion. That money would not go to Elon Musk himself. It would go towards OpenAI's nonprofit entity. He wants Sam Altman out of the company as CEO. Oh, okay. And he also is really pushing for an unwinding of all of these for-profit entities that have become a really key part of the company's ability to fund itself. So those are the main three things he's pushing for. He has to endear himself to this judge, Yvonne Gonzalez Rogers, who ultimately decides the fate of this case. There is a jury that's been impaneled of nine Northern California citizens, but they are in an advisory role. They will issue a verdict, but it is considered an advisory verdict that the judge will take into consideration if she has a problem with it. She will ultimately make the call. And, you know, there were a lot of memorable moments but, I mean, just to start before Musk even took the stand, the judge pretty much dressed Musk down and said, "Listen, you've got to stop posting on social media about this case. You're just making this complicated for all of us." Going into the trial, Musk had been posting to his social media platform X about the case, disparaging Sam Altman and Greg Brockman, calling Sam Altman "Scam Altman" and trying to make his case in the court of public opinion, so to speak, on his social media platform X. And she said, "We just don't need those distractions outside of the courtroom." And she actually turned to Sam Altman at one point and said, "Would you also agree to stop posting about this case?" Because Musk was saying, "I'm just doing this because OpenAI is posting about this and it's me trying to defend myself." So you kind of had this little bit of a playground situation with these two boys who are a little trigger-happy with the social media and the judge saying, "This has got to stop." Both of them agreed to do so and that was how we started the case. That's when we started with opening statements shortly thereafter. And Lily, you've been there in the courtroom. What's it been like? It is a packed courtroom. It's a media circus. And so there's just a lot of energy. It's an electric energy. You see high-profile people, not just the parties to the case but Ari Emanuel, the super agent, the Hollywood super agent who is working with Musk's team, was sitting right in front of me on the second day of Musk's testimony. It is really interesting hearing Musk articulate himself. I haven't actually been in a room with him in probably 10 years and his public image has really changed during that time. Back then, I think he was truly revered as a technologist and somebody who just had a much less polarizing public image. Seeing him there having to answer questions in a trial that he himself initiated—but he had to answer some questions that I think were pretty uncomfortable. I think, you know, also really important is hearing the details about his AI venture, xAI, which is now part of SpaceX, which developed that controversial AI chatbot Grok that's made a lot of negative headlines over the last few months for different reasons. He admitted on the stand under prodding from OpenAI's lawyer that he has used OpenAI's models to basically train his product. And I think that was an uncomfortable admission for him to make. So we're getting a little bit of a look under the hood at how he's doing his own startup, which is not a nonprofit and which is not known for safety guardrails.
