Now what happens is you get a long list of well we don't know about legal liabilities, we don't know about information security, we don't know about hallucinations, we don't know about any of this. And you're like well but what you do know is that AI will be a more and more significant part of the future such that every company that is not fundamentally using AI will start arcing off. So don't use excuses to not take small risks, small challenges, small problems, to start the learning curve within your own corporation. And you know that takes a little bit of more boldness than most managers or executives have because the classic thing is we keep doing it the way we have, no one ever questions me. If I change it, I get questioned. But you should seek to be questioned in this time. Yeah. I agree. So what you're saying is one of the key differentiators is creating a culture where people actually embrace AI, use it, experiment with it. And doesn't mean it'll be perfect on it right now. It'll have hallucinations, it'll have some problems etc. Learning what those are now and how to integrate it in like for example for me in investing. Due diligence, competitive analysis, etc. Great. If I said should I make the investment or not, it's not currently useful at that, right? It doesn't really get to the like if I proxied all of my decisioning over to ChatGPT or Copilot or anything else, it wouldn't work, right? And I tried that with all of them just to see every so often. But and it doesn't mean that it won't get there in ways that will be much more helpful than they are, but because it's not useful there doesn't mean I stop using it in an investing context. Mhm. So you talk about investing. You're in—you make investment decisions every single day, especially in technology and AI companies. How do you decide which companies to invest in? Where do you see the real opportunities today and especially in a world where everyone can use AI, everyone has access to super powerful foundation models? Where do you see lasting competitive advantage coming from? So there's kind of maybe two primary kind of answers to this. One is some of the areas are the old is new again in various ways, which is there's classic things that you get to tech investing like network effects or a large fleet of like when you get integrated into a number of enterprises, because enterprises tend to adopt and then hold in terms of where adoption is. You know this kind of thing. And part of the where the new is the old is new again is that when you have a new technology and a disruptive thing, that opens up markets for competitors. When you had mobile phones, all of a sudden it's like oh well we'll have mobile native various forms of apps and so forth. When you had the cloud, okay well we'll have SaaS and we'll have cloud. Now AI is kind of the next version of that. So there's a bunch of different kind of classic business 101 how do you create moats. Now like with the SaaS what changes is well I very few companies can hire a 100 engineers and do code with that. That expands with the amount of coding agents, Cloud Code, CodeX, etc. but so there's some changes there, but it's still the principles of what creates moats around businesses, what creates margin structure in our businesses is still there. Now the second one is AI specific, right? So part of the reason why like I co-founded Manus AI is because well most Silicon Valley people will approach biopharmaceutical the wrong way, namely they'll go, it's all software. It's either we put it all in silico and we do all of our drug discovery reasoning within silico, or we'll build drug researchers or we'll just analyze drug research papers. And actually in fact what you want to do is high-quality AI with high-quality bio, right? And you want to kind of put those two together. And that then means that you have different surfaces where you could create something because where traditionally like there's a whole bunch of software competition say well why isn't the frontier model just going to go on this? And the answer is because they don't have the focus on doing the bio the right way; they're focused on frontier models even if they have a group doing this. This is their primary focus. Then the question around the bio the classic drug discovery companies go well buy some AI, but that isn't restructuring your process for something that's AI native. That's essentially what you need, you bring those two things together and that's essentially the like is one instance of when new kinds of things become possible because of AI. So whenever you say oh that's now changed, great, change. Change opens up opportunity, change opens up a new structure what you're doing. To say well it used to be software economics where it was expensive to make the software but cheap to run it, so you seek business models for that. By the way that's still true, right? Just different how the expenses on making the software and everything else is now changed. But like now you have new business models of like being token factories. That's part of what all the hyper-scalers like Microsoft and everyone else are doing because you know OpenAI and everyone else because then it's like oh that I'm buying a set of tokens. Like there's almost like one of the things we talk about in Silicon Valley is what is people token versus what is compute token and how do those tokens come together in the production of useful work. Well now there's a whole bunch of business models around that too. Interesting. So how will AI change the nature of work then? We talked about the strategic change, but it will profoundly change what people will be doing, the skills they need, what organizational structure will look like in the future. Paint me a picture what you see there. So a couple things. So one is I think every single as it were cognitive task, anything with information or language will have AI agents. Some of those cases the AI agents will completely take over what was a job or set of tasks on this because it's just more efficient and effective to do that. Customer service is one of the examples I use at any place where a human being is following a script, the AI will do that much much better. And that's you know companies like Sierra, Parloa, etc. in terms of doing this. The then there will be a bunch of jobs that'll be transformed, namely the work tasks that you were doing before, now that the AI is much better at those tasks, the work tasks you were doing before, now you plus the AI will be better than that. And then there will be kind of new and evolving set of tasks that come out from there now being an AI-rich universe. One of the ways that I tend to put this is I don't think we'll have individual human contributors anymore. You'll always be essentially managing and leveraging a set of agents. So like agent management will now be a basic skill that everyone kind of needs, how you compose them, how you make the teams work, etc. etc. and part of that is because like even if you look at some of the most advanced frontier models today, they're very good at tactics equivalent—not all tactics, but a lot of them. But they're not as good at strategy. That's the reason why like like hey what's the due diligence on company X, should I invest or not, kind of is tactic strategy. So if you apply that lens across a whole lot of things, that's essentially what the human job evolves to with managing the sets and fleets of AI in terms of how you operate. So a good analysis is you know which tasks are done by AI, which tasks we transformed by AI, and which tasks will stay in people and also which new tasks for people in the whole set. And that will be I think the bulk of a lot of things with a lot of job transformation. Now then we get to what is the nature and the transformation of the firm. Because part of the nature of the transformation of the firm is that the like for example there's this classic Coase paper on the theory of the firm and actually the flows of information, the flows of coordination, the flows of how it's all put together will change. I still think you'll have managers and a CEO and you might have the same department structure that we have now—maybe that will change, that's an interesting question. But like for example as the example I was mentioning earlier that every company should start having AI agents in every meeting, that changes the efficiency of how your flow of information works in a firm. And that then creates different decisioning methodologies, that creates different depth of research and risk analysis on certain kinds of decisions. A lot of what work firms do is build work processes like this is how you use wikis or this is how you use knowledge management. All of that will get transformed and that will transform the nature of like how the company works together, right? Not just the individuals managing agents and all that but that will also happen in terms of this. What do you see the impact of junior employees will be? Because there's a big fear among young people that actually the junior jobs where people train on the job those are very easily automatable. I have three kids at the stage where they are starting to think about their future careers. What advice would you give young people? So right now and immediately, one of the advantages you'd bring as young people is "I'm AI native." You really want me in your firm because you want me helping you with the AI transition. And I'm very—so there's a thing right now to take what people worry about as this disadvantage and turn it into a very strong advantage, and playing into that I think is very important across nearly all young people. Now the question is what happens as this matures and firms get more AI native and everything else and they go, "Okay well a lot of those like we used to have you do the simple rote boring jobs as a way of proving yourself into this." I suspect we will also create two forms of pathways. One is there will be new apprenticeship ways of doing it. Sure. Like say you're a paralegal, it wasn't go down and pull out all the books and photocopy the articles relevant to it because we have all that. But it would be the do all the pre-processing on working with the AI agent to create the summary by which the senior lawyer will be going into to doing that. And maybe that will be fewer paralegals than there were before, but by the way there also may be more law firms too in terms of how this is operating. So I think that I think there will still be that transformation. Now the second one is all firms will want to be will need to have, unless we get to a Star Trek universe where all companies are run by AIs and everything else which you know some people talk about. I think we're far much further away from that than most Silicon Valley discussion. But before we get there, firms do need to have an onboarding path. Now part of what they do with the onboarding path because they want people like new talent coming on to be able to be like in law the partner, etc. They want a path that actually produces useful work, that produces high-quality outputs on it. Like I wouldn't be surprised if now you know takes a little bit of a good containment of your own ego, but like use the legal thing. Say I was a law partner and I'm trying to figure out how to bring in younger partners you know younger like associates to come up as partners. I'll say okay I want you to take all the work that I'm doing and all the work that I'm producing getting produced and with AI and all the rest and I want you to go use AI and red team it. I want you to come back and analyze it and bring back the three things that we should also consider, the weaknesses, the strengths, the things we could do different and go use AI 80 hours, 100 hours this week to bring back the best possible red teaming. And part of what I'll learn is are you doing that the right way because that's the kind of expertise that you'll need to know at high quality it is. And so I think you'll we'll get new patterns of what apprenticeship looks like in this. Now of course it's scary because people are like well I'd like to know that now. This is always the case of work transformation. But that's the and and you know there's always in every single this is where I wrote Superagency, in every single time "Oh this time is different and the world's coming to an end." It's the printing press, is electricity, is the car, is the mobile phone, like all of the stuff is what tends that is the computer. But actually in fact part we have a collective reason to inventing a good society that has good economic pathways, has good ways that we can make progress in our lives, contribute, etc. We will get there, right? And that's why we are always creating the right kinds of interesting new jobs. And so you know for example before internet services existed, there were no data scientists. Now we have lots of data scientists. Now you say well AI is changing data science. Yep. And like knowledge as SQL doesn't really matter; they can do a bunch of interesting analysis. Now the data scientist is to be the strategist for the data science. Anyway, and on and on. Very good. Yeah. I like that. And I completely agree again. Yeah. AI is moving super fast and companies we talked about the fact that companies struggle to really embrace it properly. How do you balance speed and adoption and experimentation with the responsible and ethical use of AI? So part of the to have to take risk to do innovation, you always have some errors, some faults. So the key thing you have to do is say we acknowledge and allow there to be some errors. Zero error is like again like say we we're going to plan to drive from here to Oxford and we're going to have zero error. You're like well you're never going to get in the car. You can't go to zero error, you go to reasonable amount of error, right? Just like autonomous vehicles shouldn't be zero accidents, should just be a lot less than the human average and making the road safer which is part of like what Waymos do as people experience them and so forth. So the question around innovation and safety is not big errors. And what I mean by big errors is like I think Anthropic has done a good thing by paying attention to cybersecurity and paying attention to the use of AI by criminals or rogue states, bioterrorism, etc. So that's one stack of things. The second is the transition in society is challenging. It was challenging for the printing press, it was challenging for the industrial revolution. This will be new and difficultly challenging because it's faster than the industrial revolution and the printing press. It is a cognitive industrial revolution; it might be even bigger than the earlier industrial revolution. So the civil disruption, right, where people as opposed to working together to kind of help the transition get through, get very intensely internally conflictatory, right? Whether it's civil disorder, whether it's having kind of society be much more in internal conflict in these things. Those kinds of things also worry me. And then you know like in the science fiction case where you go, well maybe we are creating this superintelligence. Obviously I'm a deep humanist and maintaining a role for human beings as very important here matters to me. Now I don't know—I think it's weird that it's above 0 percent that this is an operative question. It's not nearly as high as most of the people talk about this as. Like for example I'm not sure that this kind of superintelligence question you or I or your children will confront in our lifetimes, right? Because they say well but look at the exponential AI progress. I say well it's a jagged edge, right? Like it's like an amazing savant. A savant that's great at a number of different things, but an amazing savant is not a reordering of the cosmos. And so anyway so those kinds of things are the things that I would say I pay attention to and probably in roughly that order. Yeah. I like that. If you were to give leaders watching this a top tip when it comes to AI and implementing it successfully in their organization, what tip or tips would you give them? Well so one thing that we've already talked about today is get your people engaged. Yeah. Have individuals using it, trying it, reporting it. By the way this is also true of government. So there was recently here as you know some minister's like "I don't use AI." It's like everyone who has any care and any responsibility for any size of organization should be using it some personally, but people should be using it. Two is integrate it into meetings, integrate it into kind of this kind of information flow. And third is be thinking well what are the areas where we have some unique capabilities, knowledge, expertise, etc. that putting together with AI could help us be creating some of the industries of the future. Like those three areas I think would be the beginning. Excellent. I could literally carry on for days. That is so enjoyable. Thank you so much for your time today. Thank you. My pleasure. Thank you. And for anyone who ever wants to rewatch this conversation, you can simply watch my YouTube channel where you can find this conversation and many others like it. Thank you. Pleasure. Thank you. Ciao.

The View Full Broadcast – May 7, 2026

The View(1.7M)·36:19·165.3K views·2026-05-07
Lisa KudrowActress Howard LutnickCEO, Cantor Fitzgerald Katie PorterUS Representative
TL;DRThe investigation into Jeffrey Epstein intensifies as Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick faces allegations of lying to Congress regarding his ties to the financier, while a deadly hantavirus outbreak on a cruise ship triggers global health concerns and comparisons to the COVID-19 pandemic.

TL;DR

The investigation into Jeffrey Epstein intensifies as Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick faces allegations of lying to Congress regarding his ties to the financier, while a deadly hantavirus outbreak on a cruise ship triggers global health concerns and comparisons to the COVID-19 pandemic.

The Epstein Investigation & Howard Lutnick’s Testimony

  • Congressional Scrutiny: House committee members questioned Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick in a closed-door session regarding his relationship with Jeffrey Epstein.
      • Perjury Allegations: Critics and committee members labeled Lutnick a "pathological liar," noting discrepancies between his past public statements and his recent admission of visiting Epstein’s island with his family and a nanny.
      • Legal Jeopardy: Sunny Hostin noted that while the interview was voluntary, lying to Congress remains a felony (perjury).
  • Trump Administration Fallout: Alyssa Farah Griffin suggested Lutnick’s tenure in the cabinet may be nearing an end as the administration seeks to distance itself from the "stink" of the Epstein scandal.
      • Presidential Communication: Lutnick refused to confirm if he spoke with Donald Trump prior to his testimony, a move Hostin interpreted as a likely "yes."
  • New Evidence: A newly unsealed 2019 suicide note from Epstein has surfaced, which the Department of Justice claims it had never seen before, raising further questions about a potential cover-up.

Katie Porter’s Gubernatorial Bid and the "Temperament" Debate

  • California Governor Race: Former Congresswoman Katie Porter defended her temperament during a recent debate following criticisms of her treatment of staff.
  • Gender Double Standards: The panel debated whether Porter is being unfairly judged.
      • The "Likability" Trap: Joy Behar argued that female politicians like Hillary Clinton and Kamala Harris are often labeled "abrasive" while men like Donald Trump are praised for the same volatility.
      • Character Concerns: Alyssa Farah Griffin argued that video evidence of Porter berating staff points to a character issue rather than a gendered double standard, noting that "how somebody treats staff... says so much about their character."
  • Personal Scandals: The panel discussed allegations of personal volatility, including a disputed incident involving Porter allegedly throwing mashed potatoes at her husband.

Global Health Crisis: Hantavirus Outbreak

  • Cruise Ship Quarantine: The *MV Hondius* is currently under quarantine following a hantavirus outbreak that has claimed three lives.
  • Transmission Concerns: While hantavirus is typically spread via rodent droppings, this specific strain appears to have a higher rate of human-to-human transmission through bodily fluids.
      • Public Panic: There are reports of the virus appearing in three U.S. states, leading to "COVID flashbacks" and concerns over the speed of contact tracing.
  • Institutional Trust: Sunny Hostin expressed concern over U.S. preparedness, citing the administration’s withdrawal from the World Health Organization and the appointment of RFK Jr. to lead Health and Human Services.

Entertainment and Social Conduct

  • Broadway Etiquette: Whoopi Goldberg issued a stern rebuke to theatergoers who have been heckling actress Isa Camille Briones by yelling lines from her television series, *The Pitt*, during live performances.
      • "Ill-Raised" Behavior: Goldberg characterized the interruptions as a sign of "no class," asserting that the theater is not the place for "circus" behavior.
  • Jewish American Heritage Month: The show featured journalist Bianna Golodryga and her new book, *Don't Feed the Lion*, which aims to provide the younger generation with tools to combat antisemitism.
  • Media Returns: Lisa Kudrow joined the program to discuss the final season of *The Comeback*, highlighting the show's commentary on the changing landscape of the entertainment industry.

***

Notable Quotes

"Howard Lutnick is a pathological liar who is enabling the most egregious cover-up in American history. There are real victims here... potentially over a thousand women who were abused." — Sunny Hostin (quoting committee sentiment)
"I do suspect that Howard Lutnick's days in the Trump cabinet may be numbered... the President does not want to see [Epstein] front and center again, and I could see a world in which he says, 'politely move on.'" — Alyssa Farah Griffin
"If you feel like you need to yell in the theater, do it at home... It shows you're ill-raised. When you don't pay attention to the other people in your space, it means you have no class." — Whoopi Goldberg
"Donald Trump has a freaking fit every day... his people worship him and say 'he's strong-willed.' But if a woman does it, she's out. Two things can be true at once: temperament matters, but there is a double standard." — Joy Behar
Notes on a scandal? As questions mount over why the Justice Department claims they didn't know about a newly released, purported suicide note from Jeffrey Epstein, House committee members speak out after Trump Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick's closed-door grilling about his relationship with Epstein. Howard Lutnick is a pathological liar who is enabling the most egregious cover-up in American history. Will this bring us any closer to finding out the truth? Plus, how a cruise ship on quarantine over a rodent-borne hantavirus outbreak that's claimed three lives is causing global panic and COVID flashbacks. Then, Emmy winner Lisa Kudrow talks about the final season of The Comeback. Welcome to my new chapter. Come on in. You coming or just taking it all in? And rediscovering Friends. Here come hot topics! Whoopi, Sara Haines, Joy Behar, Ana Navarro, Sunny Hostin, and Alyssa Farah Griffin. Now let's get things started! Hello, hello, hello! Welcome to The View, y'all. Welcome to The View. Welcome to The View. Welcome to The View. Welcome to The View. My god, I feel like I have seen these people many times. We have! Yes. I mean, it's nice to see you again. So let's tell you what's going around or going on. The mystery surrounding the life and death of Jeffrey Epstein just got deeper because apparently yesterday, a suicide note supposedly written by him in 2019 was unsealed by a judge that a DOJ spokesperson said they've never seen before. Now, you know who's Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick was also in the hot seat in a closed-door meeting with house committee members to clarify his relationship with Epstein. Take a look. If Donald Trump had seen the video transcript, he would have fired Howard Lutnick. It was really embarrassing. He was evasive, nervous, and he was dishonest. He would not admit to lying, which he clearly did in the podcast. Howard Lutnick is a pathological liar who is enabling the most egregious cover-up in American history. There are real victims here. There are potentially over a thousand women who were abused and raped when they were children. The only thing that I had seen that Lutnick did wrong was he wasn't 100% truthful on the brief visit to the island with his family. He corrected that in his opening statement. If we find that there were any misstatements by Lutnick, it's a felony to lie to Congress and he'll be held accountable. Sir! Sir, you just said the only thing you thought Howard Lutnick did wrong was lie. That's what the Democrats said too! They said he was lying. I don't get it! But you know, he's not accused of any wrongdoing in connection with Epstein's crimes. But given the way the investigation is going, I mean, thank goodness we're still talking about it. Because the only way anyone is going to get any kind of justice is if we never stop talking about it until someone is held accountable. So, do you think—I mean, do you think it will happen? This will never go away because people do not take kindly to child abuse. And that is an understatement. You know? And they just don't. I think it's never going to go away and eventually, I think it'll all come out. But this Lutnick guy, I was reading—correct me if I'm wrong—didn't he go on the island with his kids? Yes, he went on the island with a 7-year-old, a 12-year-old, a 14-year-old, and a 16-year-old. Wait a second. So who takes their kids—"Come on kids, let's go to Epstein island!" It's like, "No, we want to go to Legoland!" "No, you were going to Epstein island!" I mean, what kind of a psycho takes his children to something like that? And his nanny, and he lied about it. He said during a podcast that he met him one time because he was his next-door neighbor for years, and when he met him, he got creeped out by him and he would never be in his company again. He says that now. And then somebody showed up—and then now, then he takes all of his children and his nanny to the island. The thing is, he wasn't under oath, and he hadn't been subpoenaed. It was sort of a voluntary type of interview, but it's still illegal to lie to Congress. That is perjury. And it was closed door also. And that, and it was closed and not on camera. And so, what he didn't—I think what he didn't say is most important. He refused to confirm whether or not he spoke to President Trump before his testimony before Congress. In the law we say, "no answer is an answer." And so it's very clear to me that he likely met with the President, allegedly, and I think that is very troubling because Donald Trump seems to have been mentioned in the files what, over a thousand times, maybe several thousand times. Correct me in my ear if I'm wrong. And the fact that he is speaking with a witness potentially before testimony before Congress should be concerning to every single American. Well, and listen, this table has praised the fact that this committee has been somewhat bipartisan, that they're going to likely subpoena Pam Bondi if they've not issued it already. This is another Trump—a Trump cabinet official who's now testifying. But there feels like there's this uneven treatment. So you had the Clintons testify. It was under oath, on camera, yes, we have seen footage from it, we have read transcripts. They volunteered. Yeah, and that was under subpoena so that's the distinction they're making. But I think for the public trust and the fact that this is actually a nonpartisan investigation, it would have been helpful to have him in his own words on camera answering these questions. And I do suspect that Howard Lutnick's days in the Trump cabinet may be numbered because the news cycle has largely moved on from Epstein. We're talking about the war in Iran, we're talking about high gas prices; it's been kind of on the back burner for a bit. People have not forgotten about it, but it's not in the headlines. Then he shows up to testify and it's front and center again. That is something that the President does not want to see, and I could see a world in which he says, you know, "politely move on." Maybe he's not formally fired, but that they would want him to move on to something else to just kind of get the stink away from the White House. Well, the arrogance of it all, because normally if you lie and you have any conscience, you think, "Oh god, could they find out otherwise?" As recently as the fall, the arrogance in which he knew, "No, they'd never release that part," and he doubled down in that interview. He was asked a simple question. If you're dodging and you know the truth, you just say, "No, I didn't know him." He said, "My wife and I decided we'll never be in the room, he's disgusting, if that guy was there we're leaving because he's gross." Then you proceed not to tell anyone, but we find out you not only went to visit—it's in the emails! You took your children to a pedophile island. Then you continued business. He went out of his way in that same podcast to say, "I would never work with him professionally, personally, or for philanthropy." Looking back, he was telling us too much, which is also a tell for a liar. But the problem around the entire Epstein file is there's little trickles of breadcrumbs which when they say, "You gotta just forget about it, there's nothing here," then stop lying about it over and over and over and over again. Well, I worry about the idea that we have now AI things going on, and even if we see them in compromising positions, let's say photos—you don't know who it is. You don't know if it's real. So that bothers me a lot. That the public won't believe it? Yeah. Yeah, because the press has been good about debunking things that are fake images, because there's a lot circulating online. There's lists that our names have been on—there's falsehoods. Aren't they teaching—I think they're teaching kids in Norway, I think, how to discern between what is fake and real. They should do that here because this is going to be a catastrophe. Listen, we can't teach kids how to cross the street without a problem. Then we don't teach—you know, we don't teach our kids anything that they need to get through life. Geometry! Geometry. I have not used a lot of geometry. I mean, math really comes in handy at the table. But I do think in terms of Howard Lutnick, you know, we've got to reiterate that he hasn't been—he hasn't been accused of any wrongdoing and said that he had no personal or professional relationship with Epstein. But we can still question that. The fact that—well, but you know, listen, if we're going to take what we see and take it all apart and people get accused and lose their livelihoods because their name appears, you know, he has to be held to the same standard. If you—if you did nothing wrong, tell the truth. But you keep saying there's nothing here and you keep lighting the fire. You guys keep relighting the fire, which is what your point is. Stop relighting the fire! Just deal with it because it's not going away. You can slow it down, but it ain't going away because every time we talk about the war, or the fact that people can't afford fuel, and no one is really releasing all the information with the Epstein files, it's a threesome or a foursome when we're talking about it. It's in a package, man. It's in a package. So, tell us—tell us the truth, give us some truth. And have some—some semblance of humanity when we're talking about thousands, possibly, of—of child sex crime victims. For the survivors. Well, you—you want them to—you're asking them a lot. Yeah. You're asking for a lot because if we—we really want them to start paying attention to what people need, they would pay attention to what the people need. They'd pay attention to what our elderly need, they'd pay attention to what our children need, but we're not getting any of that from that. You know, we're getting continuous talk about ballrooms. Yeah. I don't want to pay for it. We'll be right back. Welcome back former congresswoman Katie Porter, who's been criticized over the past for outbursts to staff members, is now running for governor of California. And she defended herself at a recent debate. Take a look. "I can't believe that on a stage with 30 minutes of interrupting and bickering and name-calling and shouting and disrespect for everyone up here who's stepping into public service, that anyone wants to talk about my temperament." "You were actually interrupting them too. I don't know why you want to act like you weren't." Oh, cowboy Steve. "Californians can decide for themselves about my temperament based on what they've seen here tonight. And if these bullies, these boys' bullying and bickering hasn't been enough to raise questions about their temperament, I would really challenge that." I—I like the—I like the fact that she said, "Wait a minute, me? Yeah. Let's talk about y'all." Because it was like 45 of y'all and one of me. And maybe she—you know, maybe she does yell at people. I yell at people! You yell at people, you yell at people, and sometimes it's—I only yell at my husband. I don't think that's—but he's a person though! Yeah, that's true. There's no question that women are held to a different standard in the public sphere—likability, all these issues we talk about in politics. But I don't think she's the best spokesperson for this. I think how somebody treats staff under them says so much about their character. And we should note, we saw the video where she berated a staffer in a way I've never in any job in politics been talked to. That staffer then did accept the apology and continue to work with her, but that to me is a sign of very questionable character. She's had abuse allegations against her from her husband, she's had counter abuse allegations—her against him. I think there's a volatility there that needs to be taken seriously. If it was a man to a woman, I think that we would take it seriously. Yeah, but you know what? Hillary Clinton was called unlikable, nasty and abrasive. Michelle Obama was subject to endless sexist and racist attacks. Kamala Harris, remember, "Oh I don't like her laugh." But they've never been on camera berating people. They all treat their staffers with respect. I know. I mean, haven't—look, Katie—she's high speed, maybe she had PMS, I don't know what her problem was. Don't—but the fact remains that she threw hot mashed potatoes at her husband's head, apparently. I read that. Allegedly. So she has anger issues, which—well, maybe she has bad taste in men. Or potatoes! Or mashed potatoes. So, but—but I think that we can basically, any level-headed person can see that there is a double standard. Donald Trump has a freaking fit every day, a temper tantrum. But two things can be true at once. And his—his people worship him, "Oh he's strong-willed." I ran into a woman in a supermarket, I said, "Why are you a Trump supporter?" "Because he's the best thing that ever happened to this country." I said, "I was on the set of The View when he said about his daughter, 'If she wasn't my daughter I'd date her.' What do you think about that?" And she said, "Oh he says a lot of crazy things all the time, it doesn't mean anything." Yeah, but I think the temperament matters. And yeah, if that was a woman, she'd be out on her—but too, even grouping her in with Kamala Harris, Michelle Obama, Hillary Clinton is not the same as watching with your own two eyes what happened. And that temperament does bother me because one of the biggest complaints, and we talk about it at the show all the time, is the abuse of power we're witnessing where there's no accountability. If you're already berating people in videos—and she also got mad at an interviewer; the very job of an interviewer was about something she had said about Trump voters, and she snapped, quit the interview, walked out. You can't unsee that with your eyes, and you are now going to be in a very tense position. I feel the same way about men. I saw the Bill O'Reilly clips of "Save it for live," the Laura Ingraham, "Don't come in my ear." We'll go live! We'll go live! You've seen them all flip out. To me, it's not male or female, it's not black or white, it's not old or young. It is a character—he's not running. I want to know why she's still in the race though. She's like number six in the race when a Republican's leading in California. You would think she would have gotten out and endorsed another Democrat. Because a lot of people like her. I like her. Yeah, I think a lot of people like her. Clearly, it made it over 90%. We know that there's a double standard and I've got to tell you, the bar is so very low when you look at J.D. Vance, the way he behaves, you look at Pete Hegseth, you look at Donald Trump—it trickles down from the top. We have seen the worst—the worst behavior in the Republican Party. I don't think—and I watched some of that debate, and there was terrible bickering, terrible cross-talking. Those men were disrespectful and I think she rose above the fray, and I give her credit for it. I thought that it was smart politically to point out what was happening. And also I—I have to say, you get caught in a moment, and you know, everybody's got a camera. So, I'm—I'm sorry, I have yelled at people who worked for me. I have yelled at people. But I've never seen you treat someone like that to us. Well, because you—you can't always... yeah. Do it Whoopi, do it Whoopi, doing it. The reason you don't see it is because I'm very careful when I yell at people now. Because what you don't know is oftentimes people are, you know, messing with you. I get it all—I used to get it all the time and I used to get really angry at people because they come up and they go, "So what are you going to do when your girl loses because Trump is our—" and I used to get right back in people's faces. I realized, that's what you want! You want me to do that. So you learn to do it. I'm not mad at anybody who steps in poo. I'm not. Now, I don't know what she did to her husband, I don't know anything about the potatoes, I don't know about any of that, but—she threw hot mashed potatoes but the gravy she left out. I mean, I'm just saying. She does deny the mashed potato incident. He does. She. She does. She denies it, you know. Well, she denies it. I—I don't know. All I know is as a human being, I am subject to the same things that she's subject to. I'm subject to screwing up and messing up and being not the person people want me to be. But if—if I'm the right person to run that state, and I know what I'm doing, I want you to say, "Hey listen, I want you to be better, but I want you to run the state." I have a lot of faith in her. I don't know if I have so much faith in some of the other folks. Not only her, but there's a couple. Anyway, we live in New York, so what do we care? We'll be right back. The Dominick Hotel, proud hotel provider for The View, offers spacious luxury accommodations and sweeping city views. Just steps from the studio, savor the day a little longer with relaxed treatments at the Sisley Spa or light bites at El Taco. Last chance. View your deal. The View staff is giving you the inside track on their favorite finds from past View Your Deals. We partnered with vendors for at least half off, so get shopping at ViewYourDeal.com now. So welcome back. There is a growing concern about an outbreak of hantavirus on a cruise ship that's claimed three lives. Passengers from at least 12 different countries left the ship nearly two weeks ago after the first passenger died. And this video was released by an American travel blogger who's still quarantined on board. Take a look. "I am currently on board the MV Hondius, and what's happening right now is very real for all of us here. We're not just a story. We're not just headlines. We're people. People with families, with lives, with people waiting for us at home." Yeah. And so the question is that people are asking is, you know, how—how worried about this are you? Because usually the virus spreads by inhaling contaminated rodent droppings. But this strain is transmissible between people by a significant degree of contact with bodily fluids. Ew. What were they doing on that ship? Well—well it's a—it's a trip I think that has a lot of excursions to places that you normally don't get to go to and natural—Yeah, but if there's all that much rodent dropping, what are you doing on that ship? I—I don't think it was on the ship, necessarily. They're really remote parts of the world, but I don't think they knew about the rodents. I am concerned about it for several reasons. One, that RFK Jr. is the head of our Health and Human Services agency. He'll bring roadkill onto the boat. Well, it just concerns me because of what happened during the COVID pandemic and the fact that Donald Trump and the administration withdrew from the World Health Organization. Within hours of this happening, the World Health Organization released this information but the United States didn't get it. And that concerns me. That concerns me. Well, and when Bill Gates was on our show, we asked him what keeps him up at night, what scares him, and he says another pandemic. And he thinks that in the next decade we would see one. The one word of caution, just talking to some doctors and experts, is that it's a very close degree of human contact allegedly for this to spread, that it's something like intimate relationships. In one case it was a spouse that both came into contact. But I mean, my hearts are with the people on that ship being quarantined. That's absolutely terrifying. And this also comes at a moment when there's so much disinformation, so much lack of trust in public health, and still some lingering trauma from the COVID pandemic. It's kind of a perfect storm for a very bad potential response if this gets—I've been on about 10 cruises in my life. I loved all of them. I never had any rodents near me. Yeah. I love a cruise. I love—I'm like—I'm Italian, I'm like descended from Christopher Columbus or something because I like to be on a boat in the high seas. I like it. And I would not—not go now. I would go right now on a cruise. Doesn't bother me. Well, the only thing that concerns—the only thing, one of the things that concerns me is that they say it has to be—it's transmissible between people by significant degree of contact. We have a significant degree of contact! What is that? Well—well I thought there were exchange of fluids involved. Well, they say that too, but that—that's what freaked me out. There are reports that a flight attendant contracted it by being on the same plane as somebody. So I think that sparks a little more fear, but there's just a lot we don't know at this point. And didn't people leave after the first person contracted it? And they've seen this virus now in three states? Yeah, the contact tracing was a little bit delayed, which is scary. So well, you know, we'll—we'll get it together, but it is—it's something to know about. Don't be caught out by not knowing. It's out there. It's one of many things that's out there. So we—we know now, but ooh. Oh, we going to be right back? Yeah. Okay, we'll be right back. Welcome back. Emmy winner Lisa Kudrow returns as Valerie Cherish for the final season of The Comeback. And she's helping her boss navigate the changing landscape of the entertainment industry. Take a look. "That much anger and rage, that's not about you. It's about them. It is. So don't waste your time with it. You've got a network to run, and you are doing a wonderful job. You are. Thank you. I just could really use a hug. Oh. Okay. But this is an open concept working environment. Can't get a hug? Right. Inappropriate. Yeah." Well, consider yourself hugged. Self-care. Please welcome back Lisa Kudrow! So welcome back. May is Jewish American Heritage Month, and today we are featuring a refugee and an Emmy award-winning journalist using her platform to stand up to antisemitism. Sara. Yes, at just 18 months old, Bianna Golodryga and her family fled antisemitism in the Soviet Union, arriving in the U.S. as political refugees. When they arrived, Bianna was the only Jewish student at her elementary school, an experience that taught her early on the importance of holding tight to her identity as a proud Jew. Bianna's television career began in 2001 at CNBC as a bureau producer at the New York Stock Exchange. She conducted countless interviews with top business leaders and produced live coverage following the 9/11 terrorist attacks. Today, Bianna is the senior global affairs analyst for CNN and mom to two children. She's also giving the younger generation tools to face and fight back against antisemitism with her new book, Don't Feed the Lion, co-authored with Israeli journalist Yonit Levi. We have Bianna in our audience today! Yes! And you can scan the QR code on your screen to get yourself a copy of Don't Feed the Lion. And members of our studio audience, you are all going home with a copy of Bianna's book. Thank you. Thank you for writing it. The more we can tell young people about what we've seen and what we know, the better we can make the world. So thank you for writing it. Yes! Now, here's something incredibly annoying. Like incredibly annoying. Star of the series The Pitt, Isa Camille Briones, is currently performing in the Broadway musical Just in Time. But fans in the audience, they're yelling lines from The Pitt at her while she's on stage. So she posted a message on Instagram telling them Broadway's not a circus. She told them that they're being disrespectful to the performers and to the audience. You know, listen. I don't care how you're raised. If you feel like you need to yell in the theater, do it at home. People pay big bucks to go see people on Broadway. When you interrupt the actors, when you interrupt the play, when you interrupt the flow to yell some dumb lines from a show that she's not doing at the moment—she's live on stage! Don't be disrespectful. Grow up! This is not the movies. You go and yell at movies? Fine. Do it at home but do not do this in the theater. It's beyond—forget it's disrespectful. It shows you're ill-raised. When you don't pay attention to the other people in your space and you feel like you can interrupt them, it means you have no class. Don't do that anymore. You know how when you go to the movie theater there's that Nicole Kidman message about turn off your cell phones? We need that in the theater, but it needs to be Patti LuPone yelling at everyone about not interrupting before the show. Put the fear of God into them! That's a good idea. I thought the same thing because I just went to see Joe Turner's Come and Gone, and Debbie Allen is directing it. Right before the play starts, she announces—her voice, yes—"Everyone please behave. Everyone don't—you know, put your—take your cell phones—turn your phones off." You understand. And it was a great way of doing it, and she did it in sort of this gentle but very funny and firm way. I thought to myself when I read this story, you know what, maybe they need to implement that in this particular show. No! People need to learn how to behave when they're out in public! Well, because it's not about you. That's right. It's not about you. It's about the audience members, it's about the artists that are up there. Asking people to turn off the phone? Basic, because we all carry that around with us. But now that they know they have this 27-year-old star and she's perhaps bringing people in to the audience that haven't experienced Broadway, maybe—No! Learn! But I'm sorry, we don't have to tell you not to yell at people on stage. That's—I'm sorry, that—I will refuse to believe that that's how low we are. You know better, don't do it anymore! And if you feel like you want to yell, yell in your mind. We'll be right back. Yes! I can use all the help I can get at home, and our sponsor Lowe's is making that even easier with HomeCare+, a new home maintenance subscription for just $99 per year. The subscription includes two in-home visits annually with up to seven services to be completed each time by trained Lowe's associates. Services include cleaning electric dryer vents, garage door lubrication, even replacing HVAC air filters and light bulbs, plus more. So with Mother's Day around the corner, give mom the gift of less to-do's with a HomeCare+ gift card that can be applied toward a HomeCare+ subscription, purchasing products needed for your annual service appointments, or shopping in-store and online at lowes.com. And thanks to Lowe's, members of our audience are going home with a $100 HomeCare+ gift card! And we are too! Have a great day everyone. Take a little time to enjoy the view, and remember, we're in it together. We'll get through it together. We'll see you tomorrow.

AMD Revenue Jumps 38%, Anthropic’s $200B Google Deal, & OpenAI President Greg Brockman on Elon Musk

The Information(13.3K)·32:20·754 views·2026-05-07
Ben BajarinCEO, Creative Strategies Akash PasrichaHost, TITV Greg BrockmanPresident, OpenAI
TL;DRAMD is capitalizing on a massive expansion in the data center CPU market driven by agentic AI inference, while new data reveals a "circular financing" loop where major AI labs like Anthropic commit hundreds of billions in future revenue back to their cloud-provider investors.

TL;DR

AMD is capitalizing on a massive expansion in the data center CPU market driven by agentic AI inference, while new data reveals a "circular financing" loop where major AI labs like Anthropic commit hundreds of billions in future revenue back to their cloud-provider investors.

AMD: Shifting from "Share-Taking" to "Share-Grabbing"

  • Financial Performance: AMD reported a 38% revenue jump to $10.3 billion in Q1, primarily fueled by massive growth in its data center segment.
  • TAM Expansion: According to Ben Bajarin (CEO of Creative Strategies), the Total Addressable Market (TAM) for CPUs is shifting from a stagnant $25 billion market to a projected $125 billion.
      • Agentic Inference: Unlike traditional cloud workflows, the rise of agentic AI requires significantly more CPU infrastructure, creating "greenfield" growth rather than just stealing market share from Intel.
  • Supply Constraints: The market is currently in a "compute deficit" where demand outstrips supply so significantly that any viable compute—from Nvidia, AMD, or custom silicon—will find a buyer.
  • Strategic Roadmap: Investors are looking toward the July "Advancing AI" event for details on the Helios (MI455) system to see if AMD can meaningfully challenge Nvidia’s full-rack dominance.

The Intertwined Economics of AI Labs and Hyperscalers

  • Anthropic’s $200B Commitment: New reporting reveals Anthropic has committed approximately $200 billion over five years to Google for computing power and chips.
  • Circular Financing: A massive portion of hyperscaler revenue backlogs is now tied to the very AI labs they fund.
      • Google: Anthropic represents roughly 40% of Google’s $460 billion revenue backlog.
      • Amazon: OpenAI and Anthropic are significant contributors to Amazon’s $364 billion backlog.
  • Bubble Concerns: Industry analysts question if AI is a bubble, noting that much of the reported growth at Google and Microsoft is currently derived from a small handful of AI labs (OpenAI and Anthropic) rather than broad enterprise adoption.
  • Oracle’s Unique Position: Unlike the other hyperscalers who resell models, Oracle is focused on building physical data center infrastructure specifically for OpenAI, a deal valued at roughly $300 billion.

Musk v. OpenAI: Testimony from Greg Brockman

  • Elon Musk’s AI Intuition: OpenAI President Greg Brockman testified that Musk was initially unimpressed by the technology that became ChatGPT, calling it "stupid" and suggesting kids on the internet could do a better job.
  • The "Morally Bankrupt" Memo: Brockman addressed a digital journal entry where he contemplated a "morally bankrupt" path; he clarified this referred to a plan to fire Musk and start a for-profit without him—a path the company ultimately did not take.
  • Tesla Collaboration: In a contrasting anecdote, Brockman noted that co-founder Ilya Sutskever previously assisted Tesla’s self-driving efforts, providing the insight that specific data collection (10,000 images of poor road conditions) would solve their AI hurdles.

Enterprise Adoption and the Chatbot Race

  • Tessera Labs Funding: The company raised $60 million in a round led by Andreessen Horowitz.
      • Focus: CEO Kabir Nagrecha noted the firm uses AI to automate "heavy legacy" IT work, such as ERP migrations and on-prem-to-cloud transitions, which often act as bottlenecks to AI adoption.
  • Subscriber Survey Results: A survey of 574 *The Information* subscribers indicates a tightening race among AI chat services:
      • Claude: 60% usage (taking the lead).
      • ChatGPT: 57% usage.
      • Gemini: 56% usage.
  • Tool Trends: While ChatGPT's early lead is eroding, usage of advanced tools like coding assistants is rising, though established names like GitHub Copilot saw slight declines in this specific cohort.
"We’re at such a compute deficit for AI that the reality is almost any viable compute will get adopted... You need two, you need three, you may even need four vendors to have enough to meet this demand." — Ben Bajarin, CEO of Creative Strategies
"Oftentimes this AI transition and moving to AI usage is gatekept and bottlenecked behind making that first digital transformation... our view is bringing AI into that journey to solve the prerequisites for AI." — Kabir Nagrecha, CEO of Tessera Labs
"Brockman said he doesn't think Elon is very good at AI. He said he knows cars, he knows rockets, sure, but he doesn't know too much about AI." — Rocket Drew, Reporter at The Information
The Information. The biggest tech stories. Sharp conversations. This is TITV. The rest is just commentary. TITV. Welcome, everyone, to The Information's TITV. My name is Akash Pasricha. It is Wednesday, May 6th. First up today, AMD shares are rising after their quarterly results yesterday. The company reported a 38% jump in revenue, bolstered by its data center segment. We'll get into the results in just a moment. Plus, The Information published exclusive reporting with new numbers that highlight the growing link between Anthropic and Google's businesses. We'll bring on the reporters who broke that story. We'll also get our daily check-in from Rocket Drew, who is covering the Elon Musk-OpenAI trial. I'll then talk with the CEO of Tessera Labs, which just announced a new multimillion-dollar funding round. And we'll close out the show with some insights from The Information's subscriber survey, highlighting who is winning in the three-way race between ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini. It's going to be a fun show, so let's get right on into it. AMD posted another big quarter. Revenue jumped 38% to $10.3 billion in Q1, driven by its data center segment. I want to bring on the CEO of Creative Strategies, Ben Bajarin, to help us break it all down. Ben, welcome back to the show. It's great to have you here. Yeah, good to be back. Thanks for having me. Okay, so AMD is up. What stood out to you from the results? Yeah, I mean, we weren't terribly shocked. I think, you know, there'd been enough signal around what we'll just say like data center CPU as a demand trend, that I think everybody sort of assumed they would be in a similar spot as we saw from Intel in terms of just outpacing supply with demand. And part of that's really just being driven obviously by changes in infrastructure and agentic inference that's happening, which was not a dynamic we had a year ago. And so there's a number of new things coming to the industry where, obviously, AMD is a huge beneficiary of that. And so really, the spotlight was on CPU growth. You know, they updated their TAM, which sort of aligns with some of our forecasts around the CPU TAM because of agentic. And really, I think overall, this is the bulk of the story right now. Not that GPUs aren't, you know, sexy. They and accelerators are still relevant, but a big spotlight on CPUs and that story alone seems to be enough to be driving this part of the market. So when you talk about the total addressable market expanding here, the idea here is what? That agentic inference, I mean, that requires more CPUs in this environment, right? Yeah, and I think the best way to look at it, prior to agentic—so if we were just talking about the framing that we use, which is cloud-native CPUs—prior to this moment, AMD, even Arm, or Intel, would talk about how they're mostly just selling these to hyperscalers. They're running enterprise software, they're running their e-commerce, they're running their email, like they're hosting cloud workflows. And that market wasn't growing. It was roughly a $25 billion market a year and staying relatively flat. And CPUs were not super interesting, right? Now people are coming out and saying that number is drastically changing as of even, you know, Lisa Su said last year. They changed their CPU TAM to going up to $60 billion. So from 25 to 60, which alone at the time was like, that's a pretty big jump, right? And so now saying, well, agentic drives another 40-plus billion, or in their case, more than that because they're saying $125 billion. You know, we were around 110 at our first shot with this. This is again a drastic increase from what was typically a $25 billion market and not growing. And so that's why I think in the market context, people are seeing that and realizing that the TAM's expanding. It used to be a share-taking environment when we talked about AMD encroaching on Intel in data centers or even Arm. It's not share-taking anymore. It's share-grabbing in what is essentially, as Lisa said, a 35% year-over-year growth. And just for agentic, we have that growing 65% year-over-year for just agentic CPUs. Why is it share-grabbing now instead of share-taking? And what is the difference there, in your mind? Yeah, I mean, when it was a $25 billion market and growing, and we know how many units that moved every year, it was their ability to grow for AMD or Intel, or even when you thought about like Arm's potential prior to this, purely on taking share. Because it wasn't growing. Now that the market is moving meaningfully to a drastically larger size, both in unit volume and ASP as a dollar amount, it's really just greenfield growth for CPUs in agentic and for new data centers. So in a lot of ways, like we thought about the GPU expansion story, Nvidia while being dominant, moving to a trillion-dollar market for just AI accelerators—I'm not even including CPUs in this point—was an "everybody benefits," right? It's not just dollars there for Nvidia; there's dollars for AMD, there's dollars for other competitors, there's dollars for the guys making their own custom silicon. So in TAM expansion like we're talking about, it's brand-new dollars to go after. It's not "I have to take money from my competitor." Right. I guess my question is there, so, in that story though, I mean, how confident are you in AMD's prospect to then grow its market share? The market's expanding, I'm with you, but in terms of growing the market share, you know, how sustainable is the current strategy in terms of trying to grab a higher percentage of the market? Very. I mean, I think, you know, again, and I'll make this point for everyone in CPUs that's making a claim that they're doing CPUs in the data center, this is a massive expansion that they're going after. From AMD's standpoint, what's interesting is they have typically done a lot of customization and collaboration with their hyperscale customers. So, you know, the Googles, the Amazons, the Microsofts of the world. And this will have to extend to neoclouds, to be honest with you. Like this is something I've been arguing with the neocloud folks in those communities, is that most of their infrastructure has been in the training era. If you're just installing Nvidia solutions, Blackwells and whatnot, those were training and some inference. Everybody's agreeing that inference infrastructure looks drastically different than training, and that's where CPU racks come in. So there's a ton of data center CPUs that have to get played out. And AMD has always had a strategy of saying, "We can change our SKUs. We have a one architecture that scales, and we can do agentic tweaks to our CPU or we can keep them for cloud-native workloads within their pipeline." And again, they are deep customers with the hyperscalers and right now there's so much CPU demand. Like, really more than we ever thought a year ago would was going to come up. And that alone, it just means if you're relevant in data center CPU, like you're going to see market expansion. Right. What did we get from the results around AMD's full rack system and how that competes with Nvidia? Where is that at and how confident are you that that will actually pose a challenge? Yeah, I mean, not a lot yet, right? They have an event coming up in July called Advancing AI where, you know, we're going to learn a whole lot about Helios as well as the successor to Helios, which they call MI455. You know, people tried to prod this on the call, right? Everybody wants to know what their prior, so MI 355, has looked like. Again, it hasn't been gigantic, right? The role of the market has moved, again, largely to Nvidia's accelerators as well as custom ASICs, which continue to dominate this conversation. But I think the second half of the year is where this ramp is. And I'll just sort of lump this as a broad statement, but I think this is relevant for AMD too. We're at such a compute deficit for AI that the reality is almost any viable compute will get adopted. And that's why you saw them do deals with, you know, OpenAI, right, and others. And I expect you'll see them do a whole lot more because you just can't get enough compute for one vendor. You need two, you need three, you may even need four vendors to have enough to meet this demand that everybody's seeing. And so there's a lot of opportunity and growth side. And I think the second half of the year is what, you know, Lisa and management has said this starts to ramp. And I think once we see what MI455 looks like, so Helios, at the Advancing AI day and beyond, people will have a little bit more confidence about where that goes and how much they can grow that business into '27 and '28. Right. Last question for you, so what do you think it would take for investors to fall out of love with AMD? This is obviously—you know, this is boom times for the company, but now we've seen a couple chip companies go through their boom period and then keep booming, but, you know, when does the multiple start to contract, in your opinion? Yeah, I think the debate really is how much capacity, right, can you grow and scale? Like if you're just constantly in a supply-constrained environment, then most people will model in what they believe, right, your forward projections can be in terms of growth. And so if that's your constraint, then the only upside is can you get more capacity? Like where are you going to get more wafers? Where are you going to get more, you know, manufacturing capacity? And that's, to be honest with you, a dynamic that I think is hitting Nvidia is that most people just have said, "We know how much you can grow, or we think we know how much you can grow based on your supply chain, and that's what we're pricing in." And that's really, I think, the dynamic that everybody's working on in their models. So the downside is, right, that they've capped. They're capped and everybody knows it and they've modeled that, and can they, you know, defeat that narrative? But that would be the stale point, you know, if there's anything. The momentum in CPUs, right, remains an upward trajectory. Their business will grow, but how much can they grow really becomes then, you know, where the model gets focused on. Right. Great. Well, Ben, I want to thank you for coming on. That is Ben Bajarin from Creative Strategies here on TITV. It is no secret that Anthropic and Google's businesses have grown in tandem over the years, but new exclusive reporting from my colleagues Sri Muppidi, Erin Woo, and Amir Efrati reveals the great extent to which that is now the case. I want to bring on Sri and Erin to talk more about what they found. Sri, I want to start with you. What did we find out about Anthropic's reliance on Google? We found out that Anthropic committed more than, or roughly, 200 billion in over five years to Google for computing power and chips. And so that's particularly important just because Anthropic has been scaling its revenue really quickly. For example, the company crossed more than 30 billion in annualized revenue last month, up from 9 billion at the end of the year. And so as the company continues to scale and add on more customers, it needs computing power desperately. And so Google is an excellent cloud provider that can serve that need for Anthropic. So that's Anthropic's reliance on Google. The other part of your story that was really interesting is you actually looked at the reliance that both OpenAI and Anthropic have on all the hyperscalers. Tell us a little bit about what you learned there. Totally. So, along with Anthropic signing that deal with Google, we also had written about how Google had signed a $100 billion computing deal with Amazon. Amazon also has a deal with OpenAI where OpenAI committed about 100 billion. And so that results in about a 364 revenue backlog for Amazon. And similarly with the Google backlog, which basically means the revenue contracted for future customer revenue, Google has about a 460 billion revenue backlog. And so Anthropic for them represents about 40% of that backlog. And so you just essentially see how intertwined a lot of these cloud providers and AI labs are because as Google, Amazon, and Microsoft invest money into these labs, the labs then turn back and actually spend money on them as well. The other one to mention of course is Microsoft, which has a longtime relationship with OpenAI and now also has one with Anthropic as well. Right. And there's a great chart in the story that I would encourage people to go look at, but it basically shows, if I'm recalling correctly, you know, of the revenue backlogs that all of these companies have told us that they have, the cloud companies, roughly half of it—you know, give or take, for I think most of these hyperscalers—is coming from OpenAI and Anthropic, right? Do I have that right, Sri? Totally. So, so much of that revenue that these cloud providers are projecting will actually come from these AI labs. And so, I think that's quite important for investors to know as the AI boom continues on. Okay, so Erin, let's look at it from the other side now. So how much is Google spending on Anthropic? Yeah, so Google has most recently said that it's investing $10 billion into Anthropic and there are plans to invest another $30 billion if certain milestones are met, and that's just the most recent amount of investment. Okay. And so, Erin, I mean, taking a step back here, what do you make of the interconnectedness here that Sri pointed out? We've talked about this circular financing in all sorts of different circles. This is one of said circles. And I'm, you know, sort of imagining drawing all these overlapping circles on a piece of paper. But, you know, when you talk to people, is this—was this inevitable? Is it something to be concerned about? You know, what's your reaction to it? Yeah, I mean, I don't necessarily think it's inevitable. I think this gets back to the big question that everyone's asking, which is: is AI a bubble? Like is the bubble going to burst? And so I think you have a week of pretty good earnings for all of these four major hyperscalers—like Google, for example, did very well. And then you dig into the numbers a little bit and you see that a lot of this growth is coming from Anthropic, a lot of this growth is coming from OpenAI for some of the other providers. And even beyond the specific numbers that we have in this that represent like the cloud deals that companies have signed with these providers, like there's also additional revenue because some of the growth in Google Cloud's revenue that's not about compute, like comes from the resale of Anthropic models. A lot of this is like bound up in these same two companies. And so that's what's stoking fears of a bubble and the question is obviously like: is anyone actually using this AI? Sri, Oracle was in this equation, but they have sort of a slightly different case here. What is different about Oracle's relationship with these companies? So Oracle is actually working with OpenAI to build out these data centers on behalf of OpenAI. And so OpenAI has committed about 300 billion towards that relationship. And so I think a lot of investors are also sort of seeing how the data center build-out on Oracle will actually like play out because it definitely impacts OpenAI in terms of like having that compute available. And so, I think that Oracle is perhaps in a different position from Microsoft, Google, and Amazon because it's reinventing itself as a major cloud provider to actually take on some of these huge contracts. And so I think that it's quite interesting in that more interconnected relationship that Oracle has with OpenAI because Oracle definitely relies on OpenAI to be able to get on that business. Right. But Erin, correct me if I'm wrong, I mean, Oracle's slightly different in that they're not reselling the models the way that Google and Microsoft are, right? Yeah, so Google and Microsoft and Amazon, they all make additional revenue reselling the models to customers. So like Google, for example, has this thing called the Model Garden, so they offer Google's proprietary models but they also offer Anthropic's models and a lot of different open-source models. So there's that additional revenue stream. And Erin, as you look at the reporting questions that you have for this story going forward, what comes to mind? Like I said, I think one of the big questions is how companies are actually using AI. And I mean, certainly to some extent companies are—like we've seen like the huge revenue growth that Anthropic has, that comes from enterprise AI. Another story that I reported yesterday was that Google and private equity firms are in talks to form a new partnership. And so this is Blackstone, KKR, EQT. And this would help distribute AI to the portfolio companies. I mean, like one of the trends that I've written about recently is the difficulties that some companies are having adopting AI. And so this would be beneficial for Google, for example, because all of these portfolio companies would start using it. And as long as you believe that using AI is also beneficial for the portfolio companies, like it's also good for them and private equity firms in that way. Right. Yeah, it certainly seems like a steady drumbeat of news or announcements or stories from you and Sri, mind you, talking about how the private equity firms are really leaning into this to get their portfolios in check. It's one to watch. I want to thank you both for coming on. That is Sri Muppidi and Erin Woo from our newsroom here at The Information. The Information's Rocket Drew is covering the Elon Musk-OpenAI trial from the courtroom all week long. Here is his latest update for us on OpenAI president Greg Brockman's second day of testimony in the court on Tuesday. Greg Brockman had a slightly better day in court. After previously facing some really rough questioning from a lawyer for Elon Musk, who asked about Brockman's personal finances and his stake in OpenAI, Brockman had the chance to answer some friendly questioning from a lawyer for OpenAI. That gave Brockman the opportunity to explain some of the context around his personal journal. This is the digital diary that Elon Musk's side has presented as though it contains a smoking gun, an admission of guilt from Brockman that he was intending to lie to Elon Musk or deceive him about his plans for OpenAI's future and for its corporate structure. In particular, there was one passage where Brockman contemplated a path forward that he said would be morally bankrupt for him to pursue. Under questioning from OpenAI's lawyer, he explained that that was a path they never ended up taking. That was a path that would have involved firing Elon Musk from OpenAI's board and then creating a for-profit subsidiary without him. In fact, they didn't fire Elon and when they did create a for-profit subsidiary, the questioning revealed that Brockman and others were looking for ways to include Elon in that plan or looking for ways to offer Elon equity. Under Brockman's testimony, it was only once Elon was unable to have control over that for-profit entity that Elon gave up on the project and left OpenAI. Elon's side says that he would have had control initially, but that control would have been intended to dissolve or reduce over time as further board members were added and new investors were brought on board. One of the most interesting exchanges during Brockman's testimony today was that Brockman said he doesn't think Elon is very good at AI. He said he knows cars, he knows rockets, sure, but he doesn't know too much about AI. And to illustrate that, he gave an example of when Elon was given a demo of an early iteration of the technology that would later power ChatGPT and Brockman says while the rest of the team understood the promise of the technology, Elon thought it seemed kind of stupid, he thought kids talking to you on the internet could do a better job, he was not impressed, and the researchers who worked on that technology ended up feeling disheartened. So Brockman said for him, this was an early illustration that he didn't really trust Elon's judgment on matters of AI as far as the technology goes. That was kind of contrasted with an anecdote that Brockman gave earlier in his testimony of a time when Elon asked a bunch of OpenAI researchers to come visit Tesla and improve some of the technology and the processes at Tesla. In Brockman's telling, Ilya Sutskever, who was also a co-founder of OpenAI and is regarded as a brilliant AI scientist, went to Tesla and gave them some encouraging words of wisdom saying that if you can collect, say, 10,000 images of roads with poor conditions where Tesla cars currently struggle with self-driving, then I guarantee you that AI will be able to handle the problem. So that was kind of an illustration of what maybe it does look like to have a natural intuition or a familiarity with AI and a sense of the promise that it has. So that was what we heard from Brockman. Up next in Musk v. Altman, we're expected to hear from Shivon Zilis, who is a mother of four of Elon Musk's children, but was also on the OpenAI board. The question for her is whether she was, sort of observing and honoring her commitments on the board, or whether she was also funneling information back to Musk in a personal capacity during that time. So that's what's up next in the Musk v. Altman trial. AI platform Tessera Labs announced $60 million in a new funding round led by Andreessen Horowitz. I'm joined now by the company's CEO, Kabir Nagrecha. Kabir, welcome to the show. It's great to have you here. Great to be here. Thanks so much, Akash. Okay, so I am excited to talk about the company, but I have to ask you the question: You started college at age 13? How does one even do that? It's a long story, long journey, but, in... I mean, it wasn't that long, it was four—you did the same four years, just you were 13 to 17 years old. That's right. Four years of college, three years of PhD, ended up wrapping it all up around 20. Okay, and what—you just—like you just—you just applied at age 12? Ended up skipping high school with some programs for it out in California. So went down that route. Okay, well that's a route I would like to hear more about later on. Let's talk about your news this week. So Tessera Labs is raising $60 million. What does the company do and what do you plan to do with the money? Yeah, so what we do is really AI around IT transformation work. All of the heavy legacy work around ERP migrations activities, mergers and acquisitions, consolidations, revamps of portfolio companies for private equity firms, IT transformation work all around the world, currently done primarily by services activity. We instead try to drive via AI. The money, it's really going to help drive our expansion. We've been on a good rip so far, getting our first few blue-chip Fortune 500s and now pushing out to that broader market. We really appreciate the support and confidence from Andreessen to help lead our round and drive this activity. So on a good rip so far, how much revenue are you guys generating? I don't know if I can mention specific numbers, but what I can say is that the programs we're going after are multibillion-dollar programs. I think the lowest one I've ever seen was $200 million. These are the heavyweight services engagements of the world and we're doing what we can to cut down those costs by orders of magnitude. And, what was the valuation that you guys raised at? So the valuation we raised at, I'm not sure if I'm able to disclose, but it reflects the substantial value we've been able to bring to those enterprises so far. Great. So I just want to understand this a little bit. So, you know, when you talk about, these enterprise—these software transformations going on at enterprises, I mean, are we talking like, you know, the old-school digital transformations like—you know, companies trying to get their IT systems from on-prem to the cloud? You know, because there was certainly that transition. Now there's the whole transition of using AI, which is a whole separate story. But am I correct in understanding that you are actually focused on sort of the more traditional, digital transformations that we have been doing for the last 20 years? Very much so. You know, I think we tend to believe that a lot of these digital transformations have already come to fruition and already ended. What I can say is even the on-prem to cloud transition is very much still alive. We still see it in many—many customers out there that they're running on-prem data centers, their entire businesses are still being run through mainframes through systems from the '80s, the '90s. And the reality is we talk about this AI transition and moving to AI usage, oftentimes this is gatekept and bottlenecked behind making that first digital transformation. So our view is not just focusing on the AI at the end of the journey, but actually bringing AI into that digital transformation journey to solve the prerequisites for AI. And so how—how are you using AI then tactically to help with that on-prem to cloud transformation?
