We're essentially helping to solve out all of those traditionally long-pole items, the ones that would have taken six, eight months, multiple years to drive: code transformation, data cleanup activities, configuration of new systems, identification of business process gaps. If I'm running a Fortune 500, how do I ensure my supply chain is never disrupted? How do I ensure resiliency of my manufacturing operations? IT is the foundation for these businesses and if the IT systems aren't there to support that, if your code isn't right, if your data isn't right, nothing else can be done. That's where we're trying to bring the value in. And why did you get so addicted to this particular problem? Of all the problems that a 17-year-old college grad—you're not 17 now, but, you know, you—you're one of the younger founders out there. So why this problem? This is like—this is like an ancient problem. I mean, you could have solved anything. Well, my first exposure to the space actually came pretty early. My father used to work in the IT transformation digital transformation world for many—many years and I grew up listening to the steering committee meetings and seeing what it's like firsthand essentially. Later on, once I got into the space hands-on myself, I mean it is—it is addictive in some ways to see the value you can bring to real companies, is what I would say. When I see a can of Coca-Cola let's say or a shipping truck out there, I like to think, "Hey, you know that shipping truck, its routes were configured by some kind of IT application we helped set. That Coca-Cola can flowed through a manufacturing system we helped optimize." Those are the ideas I like to have in mind. By the way, those are all just examples there, not necessarily any customer names I'm listing. Right. And so—so you are—you have—you have your own proprietary models in-house that you are using to help with those—all those tasks that you talked about. You are—you are applying your models to those enterprise software tasks. Our custom models, our proprietary harnesses, our knowledge bases, all of this we deploy on-premise in our customer environments to drive these transformations. Great. Well, Kabir, I want to thank you for coming on. That is Kabir Nagrecha, the CEO of Tessera Labs here on TITV. The Information's latest subscriber survey points to a tightening three-way race in AI as ChatGPT loses its early lead among our readers. We asked our subscribers which AI chat services they use. 574 people responded. 60% said Claude, 57% said ChatGPT, and 56% use Google Gemini. We first started tracking AI usage back in February 2023, just months after ChatGPT launched and before competitors like Google and Anthropic rolled out their own tools. Since then, both Claude and Gemini have steadily gained ground. Meanwhile, other chatbots like Grok have lost share compared to our previous survey. We're also seeing readers go deeper with AI, using more advanced tools like coding assistants and agents at higher rates than what we saw in September. There are gains for products like Claude Code and OpenAI Codex, while tools like Cursor and GitHub Copilot have declined. For more insights, head to theinformation.com to read the full survey. It is posted on our website. That does it for today's show. A reminder we are on this stream Monday through Friday at 10 a.m. Pacific, 1 p.m. Eastern. If you can't make it then, episodes are available on theinformation.com, our YouTube channel, or wherever you get your podcasts. Make sure to follow us on social media on X, Instagram, and TikTok. I'm already excited for our next show tomorrow. Have a great rest of your Wednesday. Bye-bye for now.

Musk vs Altman: ‘This isn’t going the way Elon Musk wanted’ | Will Guyatt

The Tech Report(56.0K)·23:03·27.0K views·2026-05-07
Will GuyattTech Journalist Isaac PoundHost, The Tech Report Elon MuskCEO, Tesla and xAI
TL;DRElon Musk’s lawsuit against OpenAI is facing significant hurdles as recent testimony reveals his own past intentions to commercialize the entity, undermining his legal argument that the company must remain a non-profit.
Despite Musk's attempts to disrupt OpenAI's momentum...

TL;DR

Elon Musk’s lawsuit against OpenAI is facing significant hurdles as recent testimony reveals his own past intentions to commercialize the entity, undermining his legal argument that the company must remain a non-profit. Despite Musk's attempts to disrupt OpenAI's momentum and damage Sam Altman’s reputation, the legal proceedings have so far highlighted Musk's own inconsistencies and personal friction rather than providing a "slam dunk" against OpenAI leadership.

The Core Legal Conflict and Musk’s Motivation

  • Contradictory Objectives: While Musk’s lawsuit argues OpenAI breached its "non-profit" founding mission, testimony suggests Musk previously explored turning the organization into a for-profit subsidiary of Tesla to fund Mars colonization.
      • Will Guyatt’s Analysis: Guyatt argues the case will likely fail because Musk is complaining about a for-profit shift while simultaneously attempting to turn his own AI business (xAI) into a for-profit enterprise.
  • Pre-Trial Intimidation: Musk reportedly wrote to the OpenAI chair days before the case began, suggesting a settlement and threatening that Altman and Greg Brockman would become the "most hated men in America" by the following week.
  • Strategic Disruption: The lawsuit is viewed by analysts as an attempt to "take the shine off" a potential OpenAI IPO and distract a major competitor.

Key Witness Testimony: Shivon Zilis

  • Loyalty and Influence: Zilis, a former OpenAI board member and mother to several of Musk’s children, provided testimony that illustrated intense loyalty to Musk.
      • Information Flow: Internal communications suggest Zilis may have acted as an informal bridge or informant for Musk even after his departure from the OpenAI board.
  • Altman’s Leadership Style: Zilis painted Sam Altman as deceptive, specifically citing his decision to launch ChatGPT without what she deemed "proper board communication."
  • Personal Complexity: Testimony revealed a high degree of personal entanglement, including NDAs between Zilis and Musk regarding the paternity of their children—a situation Guyatt described as "unusual" for corporate directors who have transparency obligations.

Financial Entanglements and Internal Conduct

  • The "Evil Eye" Incident: OpenAI President Greg Brockman testified that Musk's behavior was often volatile. During a dispute over equity, Musk reportedly sat in silence before storming out, leaving Brockman fearing he might be physically struck.
  • Secret Stakes: Evidence emerged that Sam Altman secretly granted Brockman a $10 million stake in his personal family office/finance fund.
      • Conflict of Interest: Brockman also held shares in a chip company that OpenAI was considering for a partnership, raising questions about whether OpenAI’s leadership was truly acting independently during its transition to a for-profit model.
  • Silicon Valley Standards: Guyatt noted that while these financial ties look "fishy" in court, they are often viewed in the industry as the "cost of doing business" to retain high-demand talent.

Courtroom Performance and Future Outlook

  • Musk’s Difficulty with Authority: Musk has struggled under cross-examination, with the judge repeatedly instructing him to provide "yes or no" answers.
      • Irrelevant Arguments: The judge reportedly rebuked Musk for attempting to pivot the testimony toward his concerns about AI being an "extinction event," ruling it irrelevant to the specific legal breach of contract claims.
  • Predicted Outcome: Despite the "nine-dimensional chess" nature of the legal battle, Guyatt predicts the lawsuit may ultimately be withdrawn or fizzle out.
      • Minimal Impact: Even if Musk succeeds in forcing a leadership change, it is unlikely to stop Altman, who remains highly backed by venture capital and tech giants like Microsoft.

Notable Quotes

"I feel quite strongly that's where his case will fall down... because you find yourself in a weird situation where somebody is complaining about business being turned into a for-profit enterprise at exactly the same moment as you are just about to turn an AI business into a for-profit enterprise."

— Will Guyatt, Tech Journalist

"It shows you the complex relationship between these people and the recognition that to be really powerful now in Silicon Valley, you've got to be a bit of an ass."

— Will Guyatt, Tech Journalist

"Elon Musk thought more dirt would stick on Sam Altman than has stuck on Elon Musk. And I would go as far as saying it hasn't quite landed to that extent yet."

— Will Guyatt, Tech Journalist

"Deception is seen as a cost of doing business now at the highest level... many of these most powerful and successful people... are ultimately no different to carnival show people, talking up the latest and greatest next thing to ensure that money continues flowing."

— Will Guyatt, Tech Journalist

I feel quite strongly that's where his case will fall down, because you find yourself in a weird situation where somebody is complaining about business being turned into a for-profit enterprise at exactly the same moment as you are just about to turn an AI business into a for-profit enterprise. It shows you the complex relationship between these people and the recognition that to be really powerful now in Silicon Valley, you've got to be a bit of an ass. On the Tech Report with me today is the tech journalist, Will Guyatt. Thanks for coming on. My pleasure. So I wanted to talk about the Elon Musk lawsuit against Sam Altman. I did do a video on this last week sort of going over exactly what the trial is about and what it could mean for the future of tech and AI. But, just briefly, could you explain what Elon Musk is suing OpenAI over and what it would mean win or lose? For those watching in the UK, do you remember Peggy Mitchell versus Pat Butcher in EastEnders? They were fighting over the Queen Vic pub and they were fighting over Frank Butcher. And in this instance, Frank Butcher is OpenAI because you found yourself in almost some sort of strange nine-dimensional chess legal battle over whether or not OpenAI can allow to remain as a not-for-profit. It would challenge the existence of the company if Musk wins here. But, if you look at this from a helicopter looking down, Elon Musk slapped or tried to throw up I think at least 30 different lawsuits at OpenAI and Sam Altman and made various disruptive practices through the last year, like publicly suggesting he was going to make an offer to buy it and all sorts of other stuff that has been thrown around. So, this was the one that landed, this was the legal case that stuck and got heard in a court. But is Elon Musk really committed to it? Because we've now since heard that several days before the court case began, he wrote to the chair of OpenAI and said, "Do you really want to go through with this? We could settle now. Because by the end of the next week, you and Sam will be the most hated men in America." Now, if we go several days on from the end of that week, I don't think it worked out quite how Elon Musk was threatening and suggesting because this is one of those cases where both the two key characters are complex individuals. They're not on many people's Christmas card lists and we're not entirely sure who is screwing who, but somebody is getting screwed in this case. And, it's been, from an outsider looking in, it has had all of the storyline marks of the fight between Pat and Peggy in EastEnders. There's been many, many ups and downs over the last week and a half. So let's get into some of the testimony that we've been hearing. Shivon Zilis, former OpenAI board member and mother to four of Elon Musk's children, I think is the most recent person to have given testimony. She was a witness for Musk's team, but what have we learned from her? What have we learned from what she said and the emails and texts that have come with her? Well, I think there's one thing we can say, and it is fair to say that Elon Musk has good days and bad days because she was talking about when it was a good time to contact Elon Musk. Now, I'm not at all suggesting that is related to his own well-documented use of ketamine—not suggesting that at all. But you do find yourself in a situation where those close to Musk are advising people on when best to tackle him or meet him or discuss a situation with him. And, there's sort of this interesting loyalty to Elon through this process. You know, when things are beginning to break down between Elon and the OpenAI team, there's the suggestion of "how close do you want me to stay, do you want me to keep communicating, like what do you want me to do? Give me guidance." So, it's clear, whatever you and I personally think about Elon Musk, he meant a lot to her. And, you know, that's one of the sort of big underlying parts of this—that we sort of find ourselves in a situation where the lives of really rich, powerful people are just as weird and just as confusing as the lives of the rest of us in the fact that she had to go public with her existence and the fact that she'd had a consensual relationship with Musk, but also he'd donated sperm for children. So, Elon Musk might be rivaling Boris Johnson for the number of undocumented children to a senior figure. I think the exact words that were referenced between Zilis and Musk was asking if she should stay close and friendly to OpenAI, which obviously bolsters OpenAI's case that maybe she was funneling information to Musk during—well, after he had been— Yeah, loyalty. Immense loyalty towards Elon. Yeah, definitely. I mean, further to that point, it was specifically revealed that the board members had no idea and that there was an NDA between her and Musk about the paternity of her children as well. It is a bizarre thing 'cause it's not common that somebody has sex or consents to having children and then makes somebody sign an NDA. So that's fairly—we can safely say without fear of reprisal that that is quite an unusual circumstance for anybody to find themselves in. Whether or not you need to tell fellow board members that you're in a relationship, arguably probably yes, because of the duties and the responsibilities of directors of businesses and organizations. But the reality of this case and the reality of it happening and going through the courts, Elon Musk thought more dirt would stick on Sam Altman than has stuck on Elon Musk. And I would go as far as saying it hasn't quite landed to that extent yet. Yes, and I'm sure we'll talk about in a minute the chaos of Sam Altman's life and lots of questions about where he is on the level of various things. But, I don't think Elon Musk has come out of this, you know, with the Nobel Peace Prize in any way, state, or form. Yeah, to your point, she painted Altman out to be deceptive, which is maybe not the wildest of accusations. But in particular, she pointed to the pushing ChatGPT out sort of without the proper board communications and kind of tried to make it seem like Altman was an obstacle to the proper running of the early days of OpenAI. Yeah, and we have that mysterious 72 hours several years after this where he disappeared from the leadership of the company and seemingly went and found a job at Microsoft, and then seemingly found himself back at the top of OpenAI without any of this being fully clarified. But the rumor and tittle-tattle at the time, if we're looking purely at that, was that members of the board felt that he had cut corners, had rushed to make certain decisions, and hadn't been fully transparent in how the business was operating. And that, if you read the recent New Yorker piece, the kind of whole story about the last number of years in his life, that would tally with what Roman Farrow had written in that great piece. So, I don't think it's a surprise to anybody. But, like I said at the very start, it shows you the complex relationship between these people and the recognition that to be really powerful now in Silicon Valley, you've got to be a bit of an ass. Musk's case is based around the idea that OpenAI was to be kept a non-profit company free of corporate control. This kind of brings me also onto the OpenAI president Greg Brockman's testimony as well, who combined with Zilis's emails revealed Musk didn't just want OpenAI to be a for-profit vehicle for Mars city building, but was also considering turning OpenAI into a subsidiary of Tesla as well. I mean, how damning is this kind of revelation to Musk's case against Sam Altman? I feel quite strongly that's where his case will fall down, because you find yourself in a weird situation where somebody is complaining about business being turned into a for-profit enterprise at exactly the same moment as you are just about to turn an AI business into a for-profit enterprise. You know, rolling xAI into SpaceX and making xAI more attractive because you're linking it to a load of space rockets. Those two things are not dependent of each other. There's a very clear plan that Elon Musk wants noise, he wants success, he wants the AI part of his business to potentially not have a rival fighting it. And one of the kind of the bigger questions here, let's say he does win, Sam Altman pitches up at Microsoft, Google, sets up another company with billions of dollars of VC money. It doesn't stop his push to do all of these things. It's purely a beef between two people that at one stage got on and are now trying to take lumps out of each other in a court. How successfully do you think it paints this portrait of Musk as somebody who wasn't against OpenAI changing but was just angry he wasn't in control of it? I find it hard. I've covered the Elon Musk story for almost 20 years. I did a weekly feature on Times Radio called MuskWatch which was purely about everything that he did every week and we did that for several years. And I still find him the most complex individual I've ever seen. There's no doubt he is hugely successful and has created some incredible businesses. But his mood changes with the wind. There doesn't seem to be any kind of reality. The fact that he's been repeatedly questioned in a court—he can't deal with it. He cannot deal with questioning. He has been told by the judge multiple times to answer with a yes or no answer. He finds it almost impossible. But then he says, "You're not giving me a chance to explain my question." And the judge is saying, "I want simple yes or no answers." There was a moment in the court where he got really angry and quite tetchy that the judge said, "Will you stop talking about AI being an extinction event for humans? It's not relevant to this case." And the judge was right to do that. But Elon Musk, I think, struggles to deal with people having any ability to question or challenge him. That's been one of the very, very interesting things that's come out of this case, not by him saying, "I can't deal with this," but just seeing how he's behaving to these kind of repeated responses to get him to follow someone else's process. Getting back to Greg Brockman, as I said, he revealed that Elon Musk was keen to make OpenAI a vehicle for city building on Mars. We kind of covered if that kind of puts a hole in Musk's narrative, but kind of is it just more indication that billionaires always have ulterior motives? Well, I think to get to be a billionaire you probably do have to have them. And something in Roman Farrow's investigation was that deception is seen as a cost of doing business now at the highest level, and that's something that we're sort of seemingly seeing from many of these most powerful and successful people. And depending on who you believe, the whole AI boom is a deception game. There's some value to be had in it, but at the same time it's being hyped up by people who are ultimately no different to carnival show people, talking up the latest and greatest next thing to ensure that money continues flowing into those ventures and vehicles. So, what's the story behind him thinking Elon Musk was going to hit him? Well, this was a discussion over who had what equity in the business and the proposal from the board was equal equity. Elon Musk apparently sat through this part of the conversation very silently before storming out, clearly giving Greg Brockman the evils, the evil eye, because he said in testimony that he felt like he was going to be hit. And that kind of passion, enthusiasm—some people would see that as being really passionate about your subject, other people would see that as bullying, inappropriate behavior in the workplace. We also learned that Sam Altman secretly gave Brockman a $10 million stake in his personal family office, or his sort of personal finance fund. And Brockman owned shares in a chip company OpenAI was looking to buy from as well. Does this level of financial entanglement kind of undermine OpenAI's defense that its leadership was acting independently when it decided to become a for-profit company? It's all really complicated. There are two—you know, I expect if you looked at businesses that Elon Musk has money in, has invested in, has backed, has supported, there would be contradictions there. The 10 million I don't think is massively unusual. I think he's obviously wanted a really in-demand leader of a business to stay with a company that was looking to, you know, make money, generate money. We sort of talk about OpenAI, the success of OpenAI, and it took years for it to get to a position where it is not making any profit but is certainly turning over money. So, yeah, I don't think it's hugely unusual. I don't think if they are a decent chip company and they were going to provide you with what you needed, I don't know why that's a problem, you know? You're going to find in these sorts of court cases that anything that the opposite side can use to make it sound fishy will be pulled out for the courtroom punch-up. It does go some way though to really—especially the fact it was a secret—implying that there was some compromised sort of decision-making around going into a for-profit company if some people are being given money. Whether it's connected or not we don't know, but the financial ties and benefits that would be gained from going public then surely call into question some of Brockman's testimony and stance in the lawsuit. Oh yeah, but with these cases you find yourself questioning things on both sides. Like I'm not defending—as I said at the very start of this, neither of these people and neither of these organizations are at the top of people's Christmas card lists. Elon Musk and Sam Altman lead complicated lives and are aggressively fighting for the future of one of the world's largest artificial intelligence organizations, businesses, companies. So I think you're going to find contradictions to and fro on both sides. I think it's obviously who makes that most convincingly. And there's a judge involved in this process. So no amount of Elon Musk attempting to post Scam Altman memes to X or making various accusations and throwing his largesse around is not going to make a blind bit of difference. It's a court. This is a decision to be heard and a decision to be made in a court. I think the judge as well told Elon Musk off for that Scam Altman post as well, didn't she? Yeah, well, you would. And, you know, this comes in the very same week as Elon Musk has settled a dispute with the SEC over various tweets he made when he had buyer's remorse and buyer's regret around buying Twitter, now X. And he was attempting, depending on who you're viewing, these tweets could have potentially forced the value of the company down. And, we didn't get to decide or hear that in court 'cause Elon Musk decided to settle for several million dollars. Going back to the lawsuit between Sam Altman and Elon Musk, what did we learn from Greg Brockman's diary when he was testifying? I think Elon Musk's team certainly tried to paint him out as greedy, but that's not a massive jump for a billionaire. No, and it's tricky, right? There's accusations thrown around about every party in these cases. And I expect if I found myself in court, there'd be various things that I've done today or things I've said to people or documented in emails that could be interpreted in a negative fashion. So, I just think going back to where we find ourselves, I don't think any of the people involved in this are going to be neighbor of the year, are going to be winning the local community prize or the Nobel Peace Prize. I think they're all really desperately trying to make their businesses succeed. Musk is obviously doing very well, and OpenAI feels quite aggressively looking to build. The relationship between the team at OpenAI would appear pretty tight. There are close relationships there. I don't see similar—and I know Elon Musk has investors and lots of support, but I don't see within his own business, I don't see the same level of backing. The leaders frequently change. This lawsuit was always going to be a bit of an uphill battle for Elon Musk. But now we're just shy of a couple of weeks in. Do you think it's looking any better for him or is it looking worse? I think it's not going quite how he expected it to go based on the fact that he told Greg that him and Altman would be the most hated men in America. That definitely hasn't happened. I don't think any of the evidence or information that's been given from the sort of Musk side is being seen by people as a slam dunk. I think many people are just wondering how this conversation found its way into court. And people who love Elon Musk will suggest that he's trying to save humanity and the future of AI. People who routinely study Elon Musk will understand that this is all part of Elon Musk's MO: disruption, try and take your competitor's eyes off the ball, throw money at a problem, try and back out of it at the last minute. The attempts to settle kind of points to that for me. And, yeah, I don't think it shows him in the greatest state either. And like I said, I don't even know what's achieved other than marginal disruption if he forces a new boss at OpenAI. It's not like Sam Altman won't get a role in a similarly high-powered position somewhere else, you know? I just can't see it. One of the possible motives for Elon Musk doing this lawsuit is that he's essentially trying to do as much damage to OpenAI before it goes to IPO. To make it as difficult as possible for the company and specifically Sam Altman, I suppose. Do you think it could still be a problem for that IPO that Sam Altman's so keen for? Yeah, it could potentially take some of the kind of the sparkle off of it. But let's not forget all the stuff that went on with Elon Musk over the last few years. Sales of Tesla tanking in Europe because people didn't—the number of stickers on cars in Europe with "I bought this Tesla before Elon went mad," for example. There's a real backlash against the man, but it hasn't impacted the excitement in his businesses. It hasn't, other than declining Tesla sales, it hasn't really taken the shine off of Elon Musk being seen by parts of society as the real-life Tony Stark. The real-life Iron Man superhero character. So, yeah, it's a really tricky one to kind of really get to grips with. I can't see what this does to benefit either person. Like I said, if it disrupts OpenAI for a moment, it's not like Anthropic isn't doing amazing work with Claude. It isn't like Google hasn't managed to make some really, really huge seismic shifts in energy efficiency and stuff in their AI models. So, yeah, it's taking one competitor out in a big group of people. And it does appear that this is, you know, the beef between the two leaders, so he's not seeing it in the normal clear way that he might see other business transactions. Just finally then, if you were a betting man, would you say not on who's going to win, but do you think it's going to come to a ruling or just fizzle out into some kind of either a settlement or withdrawal of the lawsuit? I think we'll probably end with a withdrawal, but let's not forget I also said at the time I'd eat my hat if Elon Musk ever actually purchased X and I famously ended up having to eat a hat. So, I can't see this going the way he wants it to go. I can't see Altman being pushed out from the top of this company. I can just see it making lawyers a ton of money. Well, Will Guyatt, thanks for taking the time. Thank you. If you enjoyed today's episode and you want to hear more of the Tech Report, please consider liking and subscribing. Also you can get episodes of the Tech Report wherever you get your podcasts.

Ray Dalio’s Theory of American Decline | Interesting Times with Ross Douthat

Interesting Times with Ross Douthat(99.6K)·49:13·66.2K views·2026-05-07
Ray DalioFounder, Bridgewater Associates Ross DouthatHost and Columnist
TL;DRRay Dalio warns that the United States is currently in the late stages of a predictable historical cycle defined by unsustainable debt, domestic social fracturing, and a crumbling international order.
He predicts a period of extreme disorder and a "time warp" of rapid...

TL;DR

Ray Dalio warns that the United States is currently in the late stages of a predictable historical cycle defined by unsustainable debt, domestic social fracturing, and a crumbling international order. He predicts a period of extreme disorder and a "time warp" of rapid change over the next five years, necessitating a return to fundamental national "health" and defensive financial positioning.

The "Big Cycle" and the Three Orders of Decline

Ray Dalio, Founder of Bridgewater Associates, explains that his historical research into the last 500 years reveals a recurring "Big Cycle" consisting of three evolving forces:

  • The Monetary Order: This is defined by a long-term debt cycle.
      • Fiscal Imbalance: The U.S. currently spends ~$7 trillion while only taking in ~$5 trillion, running a deficit of roughly 40%.
      • Debt-to-Income Ratio: National debt is approximately six times the government's annual income, a level that historically precedes major financial corrections.
      • Currency Devaluation: Unlike individuals, governments can print money to service debt, but this inevitably devalues the currency and reduces its efficacy as a "store of wealth."
  • The Domestic Political Order: Social stability is threatened when wealth and value gaps become "irreconcilable."
      • Political Stalemate: Dalio notes that neither party can realistically cut spending or raise taxes without losing elections, leading to a "heart attack" scenario where change only occurs after a crisis.
  • The International World Order: The post-1945 multilateral, rule-based system has broken down.
      • Shift to Power Dynamics: Disagreements are no longer settled in international courts but by raw power.
      • New Alignments: A growing coalition of Russia, China, and Iran is actively challenging U.S. dominance.

Geopolitics and the "Suez Moment"

Dalio compares the current U.S. situation—specifically the tensions with Iran and the potential closure of the Strait of Hormuz—to the 1956 Suez Crisis.

  • Perception of Power: Just as the Suez Crisis signaled the end of the British Empire, a perceived U.S. failure in the Middle East could trigger a global loss of confidence in American military and financial reliability.
  • The Reserve Currency Question: While the U.S. Dollar remains the primary medium of exchange, its role as a store of wealth is weakening.
      • Gold as Default: Gold is currently the second-largest reserve asset for central banks because it is the only "non-fiat" alternative that has survived thousands of years of imperial cycles.
      • China’s Limitations: While China’s currency is increasing as a transactional medium, Dalio doubts it will become a primary store of wealth due to China's history of not protecting private wealth.

The Five-Year "Time Warp" and AI

Dalio predicts that the next five years will be a period of "great turbulence" where several forces converge:

  • Political Conflict: Dalio anticipates that the period between the 2024 midterms and the 2028 presidential election will see an intensification of domestic conflict, with a non-zero risk of "broader-based violence."
  • Technological Disruption: AI acts as a "double-edged sword" in this cycle.
      • The Upside: AI could provide a massive productivity boost that helps mitigate debt problems by increasing national income.
      • The Downside: It will likely widen wealth gaps, replace jobs, and be weaponized by bad actors or rival states.
  • Acts of Nature: Climate change and pandemics are "acts of nature" that Dalio includes as a fifth force that historically accelerates the decline of empires.

Path to Recovery: Restoring National Health

When challenged by Ross Douthat on whether the U.S. can "bounce back," Dalio argues that the country must return to three fundamental basics:

1. Quality Education: Focusing on the capabilities, productivity, and "civility" of the next generation (Dalio notes 60% of Americans have below a sixth-grade reading level).

2. Internal Order: Creating a society where people work together productively rather than fighting over wealth distribution.

3. Avoidance of War: Steering clear of both civil and major international conflicts.

4. Leadership: Dalio suggests the country needs a "strong leader of the middle"—similar to an FDR or Reagan—who has the strength to ignore partisan subversion and implement structural changes to debt and education.

Investment Implications

Given the high level of uncertainty, Dalio recommends a "well-diversified" defensive posture:

  • Diversification: Portfolios should be balanced across different countries and asset classes.
  • Gold Allocation: He suggests a 5% to 15% allocation in gold as a hedge against the "really bad times" when fiat currencies and traditional bonds underperform.

***

"I think everybody should be most worried about what they don't know about the future... What we don't know is much greater than anything we know." — Ray Dalio
"It is like the plaque building up... you haven't had a heart attack yet. I understand you haven't had a heart attack yet. Can I show you the MRI of this plaque building up in your system?" — Ray Dalio (on the national debt)
"We no longer have a multilateral rule-based system. We have what existed prior to 1945... It's power that rules." — Ray Dalio
"Over the next five years it'll be like going through a time warp. It'll be huge changes... it'll be almost unrecognizable." — Ray Dalio
“I think everybody should be most worried about what they don't know about the future.” “Okay. I am—I am worried about that. That’s why—that’s why I'm asking you to tell me.” “So what I'm saying is, I think we know that we are in increasingly disorderly times.” “Lately I feel like we're having kind of an end of the American empire moment. In part, it's our stalemated war in Iran. The Strait of Hormuz is closed. The nuclear material still in their hands. Americans are being crushed. In part it's just the strain that Donald Trump is putting on American alliances and the whole post-World War II order, or whatever remains of it. And in part it's the sense that our biggest rival, China, is sort of patiently biding their time, watching us flail around, waiting for us to just collapse. My guest this week has been on this beat for a while now, and he has a grand theory of history that predicts that America is headed for a fall. He's kind of an unlikely Cassandra. Ray Dalio built one of the world's largest hedge funds, Bridgewater Associates, from the ground up. But these days, he mostly wants to talk about our imperial decadence and whether there is anything we can do to pull the American empire back from the brink. Ray Dalio, welcome to Interesting Times.” “Thank you. It's interesting to be in interesting times.” “So people say. So, you're someone who's spent your career making bets, and a substantial number of them have paid off over the last few decades. And lately you have been arguing that the United States of America is maybe not such a good bet at the moment. So if someone is looking at America right now, trying to decide, let's say, whether to bet on the American empire as a dominant force in the 21st century, what are the big forces or factors that they should be looking at?” “Yeah, I'd—I'd correct that. I'm not saying that America is a bad bet or a good bet. I'm just describing what's going on and what I learned through my roughly 50 years of investing is that many things that are important that happened to me didn't happen in my lifetime before, but happened many times in history. And so I learned to study the last really 500 years of history to find what caused the rises and declines of reserve currencies, their empires, and so on. And you see a pattern over and over again, and there is such a thing as a big cycle. And the big cycle starts when there are new orders. There are three types of orders. There's a monetary order, there is a domestic political order, and there is an international world order. And so these are three big forces that evolve. So on the first force, as we look at that monetary order, there's a debt cycle. Okay? When debts rise relative to incomes and debt service payments rise relative to incomes, for countries, for individuals, for anybody, that squeezes out spending. That's a problem. For example, the United States now spends about $7 trillion. It takes in about $5 trillion, so it spends 40% more than it takes in. It's been running those deficits for a while, so it has a debt that's about six times its income, the amount that it takes in. And you can see throughout history that that produces problems. And it's a very simple thing. The debts for a country work the same as the debts for an individual or a company, except the government can print money.” “Right, which is—which is an important difference.” “An important difference, okay.” “Just speaking personally, so I can't print money to pay my mortgage, right.” “Right. But what that does is it also devalues money. Okay? So that's the mechanics. That's why there is a long-term debt cycle as well as shorter-term debt cycles and money cycles and economic cycles that take us from one recession to an overheating to another recession. Related to that is the domestic political and social cycle that relates to the money part. And when you have very large wealth and values differences, big gaps in those...” “Meaning between rich and poor? Between...” “Between rich and poor and those with different values. And you get to the point where there are irreconcilable differences, then you have political conflicts that are such that the system is at risk. Okay. I—I think we have the first that I talked about, first cycle going on. I think we have the second cycle going on. Okay. The political left and right and their irreconcilable differences. We can get into those.” “And then—so then how does the international aspect factor in?” “And then the international is the same thing. Okay. Internationally, there is, always, following a war, there is a dominant power and that dominant power creates the new world order. Okay. The order means the system. And so that began in 1945.” “For us. The United States was the dominant power establishing that system.” “That's right. Okay. And it established a system which was largely modeled after the United States system in that it was meant to be representative. The United Nations, for example. This was the multilateral world order they call it. And so all different countries would operate and there was supposed to be a rule-based system. But the—the problem with that is that without enforcement, it is not going to be an effective system. It was an idealistic system and it was a beautiful system while it lasted, but we no longer have a multilateral rule-based system. We have what existed prior to 1945 through most of history. And now you're going to have geopolitical disagreements such as even what is existing with Iran. How are those disagreements resolved? You don't take it to the world court and get a verdict and get it enforced. It's power that rules.” “Right. But even—but just—just on that point, even at the height of what we think of as the rule-based international order, first for most of that history, the US was in conflict with the Soviet Union, right? So there was an ongoing Cold War. So there was a—it was a relatively narrow window of just that system existing independent of Great Power conflict. And even then, right, American power was in the end sort of the decisive force, right.” “Of course. Because the Soviets did not have real power. They had military power. But at the end of World War II, the United States had 80% of the world's money, it had half the world's GDP, and it also had the dominant military power. So as a result, we could give away money and those who received the money appreciated the money, and then they had the Soviet system which was a very limited part and financially almost broke or certainly insignificant.” “Okay. So the military balance of power was real, but the financial balance of power just put America in charge basically.” “That's right. And when fortunately there was mutually assured destruction, we didn't use that military power. Although I remember when the Cuban Missile Crisis as a kid I watched and we didn't know whether there would be, you know, a nuclear exchange. But there never came to that and then the Soviet Union collapsed.” “And then what role do just contingent events play in this kind of cyclical view of history?” “Well, they're—all the events that come along, I guess the question is do they lead to a dispute and how is the dispute resolved in a world where there's not the court system that you go to to resolve it either domestically or internationally. For example, what's happening in the Middle East, particularly what's happening with Iran. There's a conflict and then there's a war because there's no other resolution. What the world is looking at right now is will this war be able to be won by the United States or will it be lost? When we look at that, it'll be measured in almost black and white terms of who will control the Strait of Hormuz and who will control the nuclear materials. Will the United States win a war? And we should also recognize that there are alignments here so that China and Russia and Iran tend to be more supportive of each other—just as there are supports on the other side.” “Right. And that again just to sort of emphasize what's distinctive about this moment relative to the past few decades certainly, right, it's the strength of the alignment on the other side? Like what are you...” “It's the relative—it's the relative strength and the breakdown of that order. In addition, there are big debtor-creditor relationships that enter into it. For example, when the United States runs large deficits, it has to borrow money. That is very risky during periods of conflict. And so are interdependencies. In other words, in this world of greater risk, you have to have self-sufficiency because history has taught us that you can be cut off. Either side can be cut off.” “Yeah, I'm—I'm very interested in how the pieces fit together, right. So suppose the endgame in Iran is that we are perceived to have lost the war or at the very least failed in our objectives. Maybe the Strait of Hormuz is open but the Iranian regime is still in power. There's just sort of a perception that America tried, you know, tried this thing and it didn't work. You think that that then bleeds back into people's perceptions of are we trustworthy to pay our debts?” “I—I was just through Asia. I just spent about a month in Asia going and meeting different leaders and others. It has a very big implication. Very much like the implication that happened when the British lost the Suez Canal because Egypt took control of the Suez Canal. And that was perceived to be the end of the British Empire. In other words, very significant.” “Right. This was in the 1950s.” “That's right. Okay. And that's also when there was not a willingness to hold the debt and so on. What's happening now in different countries is the question of will the United States defend us? Or is the United States not in a position to defend us because the population does not want to fight a war that lasts long? So the war has to be quick and not expensive and popular.” “Popular, right. Which our wars don't tend to be these days. Okay. So just—just to stay with—with the Suez analogy just for a minute though because I think it's interesting and we've heard—I've heard a lot of people offer that analogy. This was a case where Britain and the French and the Israelis basically tried to retake the Suez Canal after it was nationalized by Egypt. So obviously there's parallels to Iran. You have a choke point in global trade. You have a conflict over it between Western powers and a regional power. But in that case, the key element of Suez it seems to me was that Dwight Eisenhower and the United States basically told the British no, you're not going to do that, right? And so part of the crisis in confidence for the British Empire and the British pound and everything else was connected to this realization that, you know, this is as you said before, like it's the post-World War II order and America is in charge. Do you need that to happen with China, right? Do you—do you need to have a similar moment for people to really lose confidence in America? How much do you need a new hegemon to emerge I guess is what I'm asking for people to abandon the old one?” “By the way, I don't think China will end up being the classic hegemon which we can get into for—for a few minutes. But what I would say is, there was the combination of the British debts and the fact that it clearly lost power. The decline began before the Suez. There was a recognition that the United States was in a financially better position as well as a world power. So being my main point...” “But so how does that—but then how—if there is value in that analogy, what is the equivalent now? If people decide the US is no longer as trustworthy as we thought, it's less likely to pay off its debts and so on, and maybe this goes to your point about China and whether they're a new hegemon, do people go to China? Do people abandon the dollar as a reserve currency? Where—where does the money go if people lose confidence in America?” “I'll give you my thoughts on that but I also want to say this is typical of every cycle. So when the British took over from the Dutch, it happened in the same way. Okay. The British were financially strong, capably strong, they lost, and it caused the shift from the Dutch Empire which had the reserve currency at the time and the debt. And so it's happened repeatedly the same way. So you don't need the particular of let's say, President Eisenhower doing it.” “No, but you need a successor power. That's—that's what I'm asking about.” “Okay. Then I think what happens is in answer to your question of where the money goes or where the wealth is. Okay, you could be a dominant power, you could still have a dominant power, and you could still have financial problems. Like the breakdown of the monetary system in 1971. Okay. United States was still a dominant power. In 1971 you had too much debt and you couldn't back up your promise to deliver gold and you had a breakdown of the monetary system and we had the 70s stagflation. Okay. You could still have that.” “So you could have a crisis—so that is a situation where you have a crisis, you don't have a successor power yet, right. The Soviet Union didn't take over in the 70s. We just went through a really bad 10 years.” “That's right. You still have lousy finances and what that means is holding the bonds is not a good storehold of wealth. So money, to answer your question, there are two purposes of money: medium of exchange and a storehold of wealth. I think that you're seeing right now, China's currency increasingly become a medium of exchange for a number of reasons. But I very much doubt that China will—Chinese debt or whatever will be a serious storehold of wealth because of their history in not protecting wealth. Right. And I don't think any of the fiat currencies will be effective storeholds of wealth.” “A fiat currency just for our listeners is just a currency issued by a nation-state that is not backed in gold or anything else.” “Where they can print the money.” “Where they can print the money.” “So when we look at history, we see that in all such periods all the fiat currencies go down. Okay. And gold goes up. Gold right now is the second largest reserve currency of central banks. In other words, the dollar's first, then there's gold, then there's euros, then there's yen. So why—I think the question is what is money as a storehold of wealth? Right? But gold is now—has been the leading candidate for that out of default because it's been the winner over thousands of years.” “So alternatives—so alternatives to the dollar become more attractive without there being this shift to just, you know, we're just buying Chinese debt instead. That—that would be the prediction. How—so then for Americans, right...” “So I think from a transactions point of view, the way it works traditionally is, countries as they start to transact in a currency will build reserves in that currency. It's like their cash account so that they expect that when they pay for those things they just bought, they have enough cash on hand to do that. So I would expect that that would increase the reserves. The issue of saving in that—in that debt is—is a problem. So we are in a new world of saying what is a safe storehold of wealth?” “Right. How does this then for the average American sort of looking at your cycle and saying okay, yeah, this has happened before, it's happening again, we're going through a period when we've spent beyond our means and there's going to be a correction. What do you expect that correction to look like? There's the 1970s which is a period of inflation and slow growth, a stagflation, right. There's the Great Depression model, which is a financial crash and crisis that leads to poverty and deflation. Which—which one should we be most worried about in this environment?” “I think everybody should be most worried about what they don't know about the future.” “Okay. I am—I am worried about that. That's why—that's why I'm asking you to tell me.” “So what I'm saying—we do not know a lot about what the world will look like in three to five years. What we don't know is much greater than anything we know. I think we know that we are in increasingly disorderly times. And these are the greater risks. And so what do I think that answer should be? I think that answer should be to know how to have a well-diversified portfolio that is largely balanced for these kinds of uncertainties. I would say one element to give a simplicity. If you're saying what is my typical portfolio? There's stocks, there's bonds, there's investments in other countries. Diversification is good. I'm not going to be able to go through all the things about how to structure that. But I think any portfolio should have between 5 and 15% in gold. Because when you get into the really bad times for the rest of it, that is when gold does best. Okay. So whatever it is, it's one of the reasons it's been such a great investment in a sense over the last few years because there's a movement in that direction. So I would say, balanced. To know how to have good diversification as a hedge against the other stuff.” “But so just—and I do, as an investor myself, I do want the investment advice. But as a pundit, a columnist, whatever I am, right, who's trying to describe or anticipate reality, even accepting that we can't know for sure, if there are these lessons from history, if there are these sort of cycles that repeat and we're headed for a kind of—we can call it a bottoming out, we can call it a reset, you know, maybe we bounce back from it. I'm just trying to get a sense of what you think life looks like at the bottom of the cycle. And whether it is a sort of stagnation and—and persistent unhappiness or is it more like crisis and, you know, clashes in the streets kind of thing? Because the 70s versus the 30s seem like different examples, that's all.” “I'll give you—I'll give you my concerns. I think we have these big issues. The money issue, the political social issue domestically and the international geopolitical issue, okay. I think as I look at the clock, we're going to come into the midterm elections and I think that probably the Republicans will lose the House. And I think from that point on you're going to see an intensification of political conflict, political and social conflict that will take place in that period particularly there between that election and the presidential election in 2028. I worry that those can be irreconcilable differences. I don't know how they will go down. I don't know how the respect for rules and law and order and whatever will keep law and order. I am concerned about, but I'm not predicting, broader-based violence. You could have broader-based violence. There are more guns in the United States than people. And so I'm not—I'm not predicting any—I—let me complete my thought if I may. I've seen the possibilities. I think that everybody around them can look at these things and judge for themselves. My general reaction is that we are entering a period of greater disorder to answer your question. I think greater risk than existed and it is following that arc. And I'm—now we talk with words but I plot things on charts in terms of what the patterns are and these things are following those kinds of patterns. So for that reason, I think—you asked me the question, I'm giving you my answer, and I think for those reasons, a good diversification of a portfolio and to be alert to those types of things.” “Tell me how—how you think the debt picture and the political and social picture interact? Because it seems like if you ask people what they're divided about right now, they don't say interest payments on the national debt, right? And they—you know, they have a—a much longer list of things they're divided about. And I'm just curious how you think, so interest payments go up, they crowd out other forms of investment. What is the economic force that interacts with social disarray here?” “They're divided about who has what money and—and who gets it, okay. So which is very much related to the deficit. I wrote my most recent book to explain how it works with 35 examples; it was called 'How Countries Go Broke'. And I've been speaking to top levels of both the Democratic and Republican party and everybody agrees on those mechanics. And then when I go down, I say you've got to get to 3% of GDP deficit through some mix of raising taxes, cutting spending, and controlling interest rates. That's how you have to do it mechanically and so on. But they say, 'Ray, you don't understand. In order for us to be elected I have to make at least one of two promises: I will not raise your taxes and I will not cut your benefits.' Okay, so what the country's divided of is, you know, let's say the multibillionaire class and those who are not—those who are struggling financially, the left and the right and populism and so on. And that has a money component. So the deficits and the money part is a very big part of the social conflict part.” “But so—but so when you're talking to politicians about and they give you this spiel about how we can't raise taxes and we can't cut spending, right. I think the follow-up that they would say is that people experience those things as threats to opportunity or equality, right? People who rely on Medicare and Social Security think this is the guarantee of equality, people who rely on low taxes to build a business think this is the guarantee of opportunity. So if you are trying to sell those people on cutting deficits to 3% of GDP, what do you tell them you're saving them from?” “You're saving them from a financial crisis. Okay. Okay.” “What happens in a financial crisis? In the US? What does that look like?” “The financial crisis will mean that the capacity to spend will be very limited. In other words you can't afford military expenses and social expenses and so on so that you'll be very constrained. And you'll have, because the demand won't meet up with the supply, you'll have interest rates going up which will curtail borrowing, will hurt markets and so on. And that will lead to central banks trying to balance that by printing of money which will also devalue the money and create a stagflation kind of environment.” “Okay. So it sounds like in the worst case it's a combination of—it's 2008 financial crisis yielding 1970s style stagflation. I'm sorry to try and demand...” “No—no, I'm happy to try to give. I'm saying...” “But because—just—just to put it in perspective for you, I am 46 years old. I have lived my entire life in the shadow of predictions about the US deficit being unsustainable. The first presidential election I really remember is Ross Perot's campaign in 1992, which was run in part on those themes. But like a lot of Americans, that means that I tend to tune out the deficit argument. The first time I feel like that deficits, overspending became a really big issue in—for people's pocketbooks since the 1990s was the wave of inflation in the first couple of years of the Biden administration. So I just think it's—it's useful for me and for listeners to understand concretely why are the 2030s or the late 2020s different from the last 20 years when we've also had these deficits?” “Thank you for your curiosity, okay. And I—I feel compelled to give you that answer, okay. So it is like the plaque building up. And so it's like you saying, like, 'I haven't had a heart attack yet,' okay. And I can say, like, okay, you haven't had a heart attack yet. I understand you haven't had a heart attack yet. Can I show you the MRI of this plaque building up in your system and can you understand what I'm saying about what that plaque—that you will have a heart attack if that plaque then starts to get that? Can you understand that? Can you understand where the numbers are and where you are? Look, it's your life. It's your choices, okay. And—and ask yourself is that right or is that wrong? Okay, that's what you need to do for your own well-being.” “And so—and so in your story it sounds like if you combine that diagnosis with your sense and my sense of how the American political system currently works, that you're going to get at least a mild version of the heart attack before you get change. You said at the outset you weren't really betting against America in spite of—like my podcaster's framing. Are you optimistic that we could have—I guess you could call it a minor heart attack and recover?” “I—I think we're going to come into a period of greater disorder as there's a confluence between the monetary part, the domestic social and political part where there's irreconcilable differences and the international world order part. And I would say then I should bring in two other factors. One of them is acts of nature through history.” “And pandemics? Droughts, floods?” “Droughts, floods and pandemics, okay. And if you take what is, most people think about what's happening to climate, it's not a movement toward improvement, it's a movement toward worsening. And then technology and AI. I think that we have to talk about technology and AI as it enters into this picture because it plays a role. And the way it plays the roles, I think, is three ways: it can be a tremendous productivity-enhancing result that can help to mitigate—maybe a lot of the debt problems and so on and so forth. Perhaps. We can get into this. I don't think it's going to come across with that speed and so on. But...” “Well, that's—just—I can—I hear this from AI people. They—they will say that in a best case AI, if it just adds X percent to GDP growth, X percent to productivity growth, that it reduces your original problem.” “That's right. That's right. That's what I'm saying. Because it can produce the incomes let's say and the incomes can help debt service payments and the like. Okay. So I just want to say that's one of the three effects of the AI. The second effect of that AI, it is now creating enormous wealth gaps. Okay. Those who are the beneficiaries of it—we're approaching who will be the first trillionaire? Okay. The wealth gap thing is increased at great amounts and so on and it will replace a lot of jobs. And so that's number two as a factor, right? So those gaps are an issue, however we deal with them. They will have to be dealt with. And that's going to become probably a political question but that's an issue. And then number three is the technologies themselves can be used for harm. A lot of power. It could be used by other countries. It can be used by those who want to inflict harm. It can be used by those who want to steal money. It can be used for harm.” “Right. But in—and in your pattern, in your—in your cycles, right, you can see it as in that last sense it increases geopolitical tensions potentially, it heightens Cold War dynamics, it increases domestic tensions. But it could ease fiscal tensions.” “Right, it could produce the productivity. So...” “But if it—but if it has some of the bad effects it will probably have some of the good effects too, right? Like...” “And—and how that balances. Yeah. You know, and we won't know what it is like in the future because it's too—our human capacity to anticipate what it'll be like in three to five years. So I think that for all these forces, these five forces, over the next five years it'll be like going through a time warp. Okay? It'll be huge changes over the next five years in all of these forces coming together. And at the other side of that, it'll be almost unrecognizable. It'll be very different and it'll be a period of great change, a great turbulence. And then back to how okay so what does one do knowing that one is not going to know what that's going to be like. Then my own approach to this and my recommendation is knowing how to balance positions.” “But for—for politicians though I feel like listening to that account maybe makes them say well I know Ray Dalio wants us to cut deficits to 3% but he also thinks we're going through a five-year time warp unlike anything in human history. So maybe we'll just wait and see what the world looks like in five years before we painfully restructure Medicare and Social Security.” “I don't think they're going to think about where Ray Dalio thinks. Well no, but—I think they're going to think about like what does the ballot box think? Yes, no—No, no, absolutely. But there are—you know, I've talked to people in Washington D.C. who have there's always legitimate concern about the deficit and there are actual attempts to do something about it. I guess what I'm interested in is in your account of the rise and fall of empires: Spanish Empire, British Empire, the Dutch mini empire and so on. You don't have these case studies of a Great Power going through this cycle hitting what you think of as the bottom and then bouncing back and sort of having another run. Or do you? Because that's I mean look as—as Americans, right, that's our goal. If someone buys into your narrative they would say okay, but history isn't deterministic, we can make choices and we can have ourselves another cycle, right?” “Yes, I—I think that's possible, but here's what has to happen I think. And history would suggest that. Plato talked about this cycle in the Republic, okay? And he talked about the democracy and the problems with the democracy because the people don't vote for what is good for them and the strength. 60% of the American people have below a sixth-grade reading level. And there's a problem with productivity and so on. And they vote and they determine a lot. And so the question is how in a democracy can that happen? And—you know, his view is that's when you have ideally the benevolent despot, somebody who is going to take control, be strong, and give for the country in a sense bring people together. However that happens, what you need is a strong leader of the middle who recognizes essentially that the partisanship and the conflict is going to be a problem but has the strength to get people and everything working in a way that it needs to work. So that there can be a debt restructuring of some form, there can be an improvement in our education system, there can be the structural changes and efficiency. It's difficult to run a big company. Imagine what it's like to run this country and to run it well. So a lot has—you have to have a remarkable person with great strength and you have to have strong leadership that is then followed rather than subverted by either of those sides.” “So you're looking for the Franklin Roosevelt, maybe the Ronald Reagan figure of this particular crisis.” “Well, I think it's tougher now than it has ever been.” “Because we're further—we're further down.” “Right. We're—we have the—everybody's got an opinion. Do you know how difficult it is to lead? I mean—I mean can you imagine and everybody's wants—you know, so can you lead people down the middle, bring people together and get them to do difficult things?” “Right. But we're also—I mean this is something that I think about with these debates. We're also extremely rich. Like the United States is much richer than it was even in the 1980s. It's certainly much richer than it was during the Great Depression, right? And that, you know, as much as people feel the bite of inflation or feel the struggle of a spike in the unemployment rate, that itself is a kind of stabilizer. And so it—it seems like you can also see scenarios where—like take the example of Japan, right? Japan is a country that has carried a tremendous debt burden for a long time and it has done so—I wouldn't say it has done so with great success. It has become less economically dynamic, it's more stagnant, it's not where it was in the 1980s or 1990s when people were talking about Japan taking over the world. But it also has this kind of wealthy older society stability. Do you think that's a plausible scenario for the US?” “I—I think you—you raised two questions and I want to treat them separately even though they're related. The first is about the higher living standards and us being richer. That has been true through all history. So all of these times before World War II, that has been true. And the big issue is how people deal with each other.” “Meaning that at the peak of debt the empire is richer than ever before?” “We—yes, all—if you take per capita income, any measures, life expectancy, any measures of well-being, and you do a chart for really from the 1400s—in the Dark Ages it was relatively flat and so at every moment in time we as a world, as a society, have been richer than before. Making your point. Okay. That didn't prevent World War II, didn't prevent the debt problems, didn't prevent any of those things. Because the most important thing is how people deal with each other. Can they together deal with those problems? Because realistically so what if we had 10% decline in our living standards as part of a healthy adjustment? So I'm almost finished, but I want to get this out. So—so that's—that is the first thing. Doesn't alleviate the debt problem, doesn't alleviate the fighting for who control—and—so...” “But—but maybe it does a little, right? Like the 30s...” “I didn't—I didn't interrupt you. Please don't.” “Okay, I'll finish. Okay, I'll finish and then you'll reply. So, and on the Japanese case, or do you want me to answer this one and then we'll go to the Japanese case?” “Just—wait—wait on the Japanese. Just one question on that. Don't you think though that there is some—like if you take the 30s, the 70s and the aftermath of the financial crisis in 2008 as, you know, each a period of some kind of economic crisis. It was better, right? The 30s—the 30s were worse than the 70s and the 70s were worse than the 2010s. So maybe things can stabilize a little, a little bit because we've gotten richer, right?” “Yes, if you were to look at per capita income, life expectancy, any measures of standard of living or whatever it is and you would see a graph...” “But even—but just—I'm just saying even the fighting, right, was not as bad in the 70s as it was in the 30s. That's all I'm saying.” “Well, I wouldn't make too much of that. Okay. In other words, I would say this is much more like the 30s. Okay. For a variety of reasons having to do with the measures. If you were to say the severity of the debt, the severity of the internal conflict. I mean I live through these and I would...” “So you think we're worse off than the 70s?” “Our conflicts—our debt is worse off. That's true. Okay. And the United States' dominance in the world order and the conflict is worse off. Okay. So I would say that's objectively the case. I'm not trying to make a bad case. I'm just trying to be analytical because I—you know, my job is to bet correctly. All right. So—so how about—how about our Japanese future? Could we have a Japanese future?” “The Japanese situation is two main things, very interesting. The Japanese debt is an internal debt. In that particular case, the way it was dealt with is that the central bank printed a lot of money and bought the debt. Okay, that's how they did it. And as a result of that, the Japanese yen declined. And so they had a tremendous depreciation in the value of the wealth because of the depreciation in the value of the money and the debt. So yes we can see something happening like that. Okay, but we also have a foreign one-third of our debt is held by foreigners. Okay, so that's a different thing. And so and we as a country, you know, owe money to other countries. But yes and if you think that that's a good outcome...” “No, I don't—I think it's—I think—I mean it's like the decline of the British Empire. You can have the decline of the British Empire, the same sort of thing.” “No, I don't think it's a good outcome. I'm interested in it because in the case of Japan it's sort of a sustainable stagnation rather than crisis and collapse. But I think where I would agree with you is that there—for various reasons that are beyond the scope of this conversation, Japanese society seems more likely to accept a depreciation in living standards than American society. And in that sense it probably isn't a model. Let me ask you a last question in the form of a comment about my own optimism that you can respond to, right. Which is that, I mentioned earlier that, you know, I've lived my entire life in a world of people worrying about deficits and deficit spending. I think it is completely reasonable to say as you've said that, you know, just because the crisis hasn't arrived doesn't mean that you aren't going to have a heart attack tomorrow. And so I totally expect everything you're describing to have significant negative impacts on the United States. At the same time, I do think it's a weird moment where the US looks weak if you look at certain indicators, but we also can look very, very strong in many ways, right? Just if you look at the last 10 or 15 years, our GDP growth has substantially outpaced Western Europe, Canada, peer economies, right? We still have the world's most profitable, cutting-edge technology companies. We still have the world's most capable military. And then socially—we have a lot of problems, but are there other big countries in the world that are better at assimilating immigrants, that have higher birth rates, that are geographically isolated from major wars and refugee streams and so on? I'm not sure there is a better bet. So I guess if I'm looking forward 50 years, right, isn't America still in the context of the whole world order a place to have a certain kind of confidence in. What do you make of that?” “I think we can't frame it as—like in the beginning of what I objected to was, 'is the United States going to win or lose' or any of those things. I think we know what healthy is. There are only three things any country has to do in order to be healthy. And this is throughout history. First, educate your children well in terms of their capabilities, the quality of their ability to be productive and their civility. Number two, have them come out to a country in which there's order and that people work together to be productive so that there's broad-based productivity and prosperity. And don't get into a war. Don't get into a civil war or don't get into an international war. That's all you have to do. Then you can look at the fundamentals of that. Are we educating our children well so that they can be productive and capable and they're civil with each other? That we have a civil population? Do we have an environment where there is productivity and we can get along? I think we have terrible circumstances. I live in Connecticut, and my wife helps kids trying to get through high school. The gaps in education, the gaps in these things and civility are real problems. Okay. So I think that it really comes down—you know it's basics. Do you earn more than you spend? What's your income like, what is your balance sheet like? These are basics. You know those basics. So if we can have those basics. Yes, I lived, I thank God that I grew up in the United States because oh my god, it was unbelievable. It was the place that anybody from anywhere in the world could come and truly be a citizen. So it had that real meritocracy. And I grew up in a lower middle class family, my dad was a jazz musician, I could go to a good school, and I can—I don't know, make my way. Okay. And I think of that creativity and all the wonderful things: broad-based education, a middle class, that we had a middle class and we had those things. And so I've seen the difference. Right? And so I know what the fundamentals are and I look at measures and I'm worried about that.” “All right. Ray Dalio, thanks for joining me.” “Thank you for having me.”

Surviving Twitter's Growing Pains: Ex-CEO Dick Costolo

Sequoia Capital(182.0K)·66:45·3.3K views·2026-05-07
Dick CostoloFormer CEO, Twitter
TL;DRScaling a hyper-growth organization requires shifting from group consensus to a "Bias to Yes" culture, where decision-making is pushed down the stack and managers prioritize providing radical context over implementing rigid processes.
The "Bias to Yes" Principle: To...

TL;DR

Scaling a hyper-growth organization requires shifting from group consensus to a "Bias to Yes" culture, where decision-making is pushed down the stack and managers prioritize providing radical context over implementing rigid processes.

Engineering Velocity and Decision Architecture

  • The "Bias to Yes" Principle: To prevent organizational "barnacles," Costolo implemented a rule where only a direct manager can say "no" to an initiative.
      • Other departments (Legal, Marketing, Security) cannot stall projects; they can only advise.
      • This prevents the "ask your mom, then ask 14 other people" culture that slows down innovation.
  • Eliminating Group Decision-Making: Costolo moved Twitter away from being a "collective" where decisions were reached methodically through diplomacy.
      • Authority was pushed "down the stack" to engineers and individual contributors.
      • Kiling the "CEO Said" Justification: Costolo discouraged managers from using "Dick said" to end debates, requiring them instead to defend decisions based on merit and data.
  • DRIs Over Process: Costolo argued against solving problems with checklists (which grew to 17 pages at Google).
      • Instead of adding steps to prevent mistakes, he focused on Operating Control and Directly Responsible Individuals (DRIs).
      • Leadership's job is not to prevent every mistake, but to "correct mistakes quickly when they happen."

The "Scale-Up CEO" Management Playbook

  • Management by Walking Around: Costolo would return to the office at 9:30 PM to walk the engineering floors.
      • He used these informal check-ins to find out what was actually being built and would publicly prioritize those projects in all-hands meetings to shift the culture toward late-stage execution.
  • The Steve Jobs "Pixar Trick": Costolo would meet with junior teams (e.g., illustrators or front-end devs) without their managers present.
      • He asked two questions: "What is working?" and "What is not working?"
      • This allowed him to bypass middle-management filters and hear the ground-truth reality of the organization.
  • Radical Context (The Ben Horowitz Rule): A CEO’s primary job is to ensure "everybody understands what you understand."
      • Priorities are useless without context. Employees should understand not just the "what," but the "why" and how it impacts them personally/professionally.

Performance and Talent Management

  • The Struggle to "Fire Fast": Despite the Silicon Valley adage, Costolo admitted he often fired too slowly, usually knowing someone wasn't a fit six weeks before taking action.
  • Eliminating "Trans-firing": To prevent managers from passing poor performers to other teams, Costolo implemented a rule: employees with low performance ratings were prohibited from switching teams until their rating improved.
  • The Art of Tough Feedback: Costolo advised writing down the exact feedback the night before and sticking to it without "fluff."
      • Sitting in Silence: He noted that the best managers deliver the news and then sit in silence, allowing the employee to process and respond rather than filling the air with excuses or apologies.
  • Authenticity and Anxiety: Referencing Hakeem Olajuwon, Costolo argued that being a "role model" is only stressful if your public persona differs from your private one. Effective leadership requires being the same person in all settings.

Strategic Regrets and Reflections

  • The Instagram Miss: Twitter attempted to acquire Instagram for roughly $700 million (mostly stock) shortly before Facebook’s $1 billion deal.
      • Costolo views this as a "checkmate" move by Facebook that boxed Twitter out of the future of rich-media social feeds.
  • The Burden of Public Markets: Costolo expressed that if he could do it over, he would have kept Twitter private longer.
      • The "daily ticker" creates unnecessary employee anxiety and forces a constant trade-off between long-term health and quarterly revenue targets.
  • Content Moderation Realities: He described moderation as a series of impossible judgment calls rather than a set of hard rules, citing the difficulty of distinguishing between "terrorist content" and "newsworthy reporting" (e.g., ISIS videos vs. the New York Post reporting on those videos).

***

Notable Quotes

"It’s not the job of leadership to prevent mistakes from happening, it’s the job of leadership to help correct them quickly when they happen." — Dick Costolo
"I hate hearing 'Dick said' in the company as a reason for things... It became like this reason for doing things [without context]." — Dick Costolo
"There's nothing more miserable than trying to be someone you're not... If you try to be someone you're not, nobody is fooled." — Dick Costolo
"Make sure everybody understands what you understand." — Dick Costolo (quoting Ben Horowitz)
