I just felt like Twitter was such, such a drama. Twitter is like the drama queen of hyper-growth companies. You know, it was like, when I took over as CEO, that year the Twitter bird had hanged itself and exploded on two different magazine covers. So I went up to our head of communications, Gabriel Stricker, and I was like, "Gabriel, great news. Next year's goal: no dead birds on magazine covers." You know, low bar. Hey everybody, we have Dick Costolo on the show. He's a rare bird in Silicon Valley these days. He was CEO of Twitter, but he was the third CEO. The first one was Jack Dorsey, who you probably met on the pod a couple weeks ago. The second was Ev Williams, the other founder, and Dick took over as COO and then ultimately as CEO of Twitter. He ran it for five years. It was a really extremely fast-growing organization. They created a category, didn't have a revenue machine, and was famously dysfunctional. I think Dick did a really nice job and he was sort of the adult in the room, and he kind of ran this scale-up CEO masterclass. He talks all about it in this podcast. He talks about increasing velocity of decisions and execution, which is tricky as you scale. He talked about how he managed by walking around. This is something I did; I think it's a really good idea. He talked about how do you give very tough feedback to very tough people? There are some gems in there. Dealing with the press, particularly press that doesn't like you. He talks about focus, and how much focus you really need in the era of AI when you can build stuff so quickly. He talks about how do you manage the forest instead of fighting all the fires. He's got a lot of wisdom in here. He also talks about his regrets, including a whopper: his regret not acquiring Instagram. That one hurt. If you're scaling your 100 employees, you're ripping, growing fast, you've got a lot of growing pains, maybe you feel a little out of control, this episode is for you. Welcome to the show. Thanks for having me. Appreciate it. I want to wind the clock back to Twitter, of course. Of course. I watched a documentary recently. And? Was it the one I was in? Yes. I thought you were really good in it. Oh, thank you. Great. You're great. You were great in the documentary of yourself. You were really, actually you came off as rational and knowing what the hell you were doing. But you're in a long line of interesting CEOs: Jack Dorsey and then Ev Williams and yourself and Jack Dorsey and Parag and Elon. And don't forget, you know, Elon's hired hand, Linda Yaccarino. Okay, of course, of course. You joined as COO. What was the pitch? What was the pitch to me? Yeah, Ev pitched you. I mean, yeah, Ev pitched me. The pitch was, the initial pitch was, "I'm taking paternity leave for a couple weeks. I, Ev Williams, am taking paternity leave. I need someone to come in and like just hold down the fort for a couple weeks while I'm, you know, out." Interim? Yeah, interim. "Would you, would you do that?" But then while we were having that discussion, he's like, "Well, what if it was permanent? You know, what if you were COO?" And I was going to go start another company. So, rewinding the clock a little bit further, I had sold my previous company FeedBurner to Google. It's where I rebumped into the Twitter guys 'cause they were all there from Blogger and getting ready to leave and start Odeo, which became Twitter. Anyway, I'm leaving now. I'm leaving Google a couple years later, and Ev texts me that. I was like, "Nah, I'm gonna go start a company. I'm gonna go start another company again with one of my FeedBurner, one or more of my FeedBurner co-founders." And then Ev said, "What if it was, what if it was permanent? What if you were COO?" And Twitter was already—so it's summer 2009—it had already hit the Ashton Kutcher and Oprah Winfrey thing, you know, fighting for who's going to get a million followers. It was ripping. It was ripping. I can see why you wanted to do it. But zero, zero revenue model. Zero business model. All the articles were sure Twitter's interesting, but they'll never—they don't have a business model and they'll never make money. And I just kind of had the thought, "Man, you don't get an opportunity to go into these, to get on these rocket ships, and when you get the opportunity you should do it." So I did it. So it was famously dysfunctional and chaotic. I assume you knew that going in. Was it much more so than you thought or was it about what the press was saying? It's always both, you know? It was, the things that were different were it was very much a collective. By which I mean, like, decisions were kind of made as a group. It wasn't, "Oh you're responsible for engineering, I don't know, you make it, figure it out and you know, figure out what the right answer is." Or, "Should we, you know, have, should we make retweet a native thing or should it be, you know, just you had to write RT at the time and like rewrite the tweet." I remember that. And there was a discussion about, well, should we just have retweet be a thing you can do in Twitter? And it would be kind of sit around the table and talk about it and decide. The group would decide, you know? So, things moved methodically, as a diplomatic way of putting it. I see. And decisions were reached over a period of time. It was chaotic and then it was also just, we just didn't, it just didn't move very quickly. It didn't move quickly at all. In the documentary it went through the, you know, pushing Ev out and giving you the CEO job. It sounded awful, the whole process. It was awful. What's your side of it? What was going on? What's your side? I don't really have a side of it other than it was awful. Like it's, you know, these things in hindsight are—it happened very, very quickly. It happened super slowly and painfully and you know, I was informed like in the driving rain on the road home by, you know, Ev and Jason that the board had decided that you should be the CEO. But I hadn't been told. And then Bill Campbell called me and was like, "You should just quit," you know? And then I was like, "Why am I quitting?" You know? And anyway it went, and then like the board decided, "Actually no, Ev is going to remain CEO, but you've been great and we're going to vest a bunch of your options and so long." And then, I mean no kidding, over many, many days and weeks. Finally I was like, "Listen, just write up the separation agreement and you know, I'm good. I'm good, like whatever. I'll go find something, I'll go do something else." And then, you know, to make a long story short, the board got back together and was like, "Oh no, no no no no." I remember Bijan Sabet from Spark texted me, "Don't quit." Like, I was like, "I'm not quitting, like okay." You guys told me you wanted me to leave 'cause Ev's gonna stay. Anyway, it was super crazy and there was obviously a ton of stuff going on. Anyway, long story short, they were finally like, "Look, you gotta do it." Anyway, and you know, really long story short, I was like, "All right, I'll stay and do it." Okay. You take over, you're the adult. What did you do initially? It's very tricky, the situation. And I think the current, the existing founders are hanging around, probably looking over your shoulder if not breathing down your neck. Well, Jack was the chairman of the board still. Ev had made Jack chairman of the board when he made himself CEO. Ev's still on the board. So two of the founders, Ev and Jack, are still on the board. And Biz was in the company. And Biz is in the company. And Jason Goldman's in the company running product and, you know, and a bunch of other people. The first thing that I felt, and I sort of foreshadowed this, the first thing that I just felt we had to do is like velocity, velocity, velocity. And that remained a concern of mine throughout my time there: like faster, faster, faster, cadence, cadence, cadence. Like, how can we go faster? Why do things take so long? If this takes six weeks, what would have to be true for it to be two weeks? We did stuff like that constantly 'cause I was always like, "Man, this is like dental surgery." But it was starting from a position of, "We're not going to make—we're not going to sit around the table and vote on things anymore." I remember vividly the status, like, the status line changed from like "What are you doing?" or "What's happening?" to something else. Or maybe it changed to "What's happening?" I don't even remember. I wasn't even involved in it. And one of the front-end engineers, Britt Selvitelle, came into my office and was like, "Hey man what's going on?" I was like, "What are you talking about?" He's like, "The status bar changed to 'What's happening.'" I was like, "Okay." He's like, "I didn't—no one asked me, you know, what I thought about that." And I was like, "Oh man, yeah, we're not doing that anymore. Like, it's gonna start being—there's gonna start being a lot more of that." So that was thing number one. Thing number two was there were a set of operating principles that were written on a whiteboard that Biz had been involved in, either on his own or with other people. But they were like, you know, like sometimes you'll get on a United Airlines flight or whatever and they'll say, "Here at United Airlines we believe in integrity and honesty." I'm like, "Okay, who cares?" Like, that doesn't help me, that tells me nothing about me, it doesn't help me make any decisions, and these things are meaningless. And they're usually weaponized against management by like, "Well, it says right here, anyway." So I was like, "All right, we gotta get rid of these things." They're like, they're not useful, they're costly. Did the founders push back? No, they didn't really. Okay. Good for them. So got rid of those. I remember there were just, like, everything was just kind of like messy. You know, I remember there was this kitchen and there was—it was just kind of a mess and there were like dirty dishes in the sink and everything, and I think I sent out an email. I was like, "This is why the—this is why the fucking site crashes. Like, you may—you may not get it, but like, this—this is why the site crashes 'cause everything's just kind of fucking like, 'Oh well, someone else will clean it up.'" And I was like, "This is ridiculous." And it was just trying—I just tried to get this sort of, "We're not going to do this like, 'Oh well, we'll get 'em next year' stuff anymore." There was a poster on one of the floors that was like, "We'll make better mistakes next time," or something like that, you know? And I was like, "Get that poster out of here." It's not—I don't really care about it, but it's just sort of indicative of, "Oh well, you know," and then the site would crash again and people would go like, "Oh well we'll figure it out, you know, hopefully we won't happen again next year whatever." So it was a lot of cleaning that up and a lot of velocity, velocity, velocity. All any CEO wants to talk about now is speed. In particular, like, companies seem to slow down as they are in hyper-growth. Yeah. Because you get bigger and get more and get less done. Yeah, because barnacles build up on the—or you get like organizational barnacles. You know, so a couple things. Initially when we were smaller, this is sub-200 people, what I did to speed things up was one: we're not going to make decisions as a group. We're going to push decisions down the stack. By the way, I don't have to be like—don't come to me and ask me what you should do about this unless you have no idea and want my opinion. Like, figure it out and don't tell your team what to do, tell them to figure it out and push as many decisions down the stack as you can. And I don't want to hear—you know, I remember I would talk to Jack Dorsey at these dinners we would have from week to week when he was chairman and was CEO of Block down the street from us, and we would get together on Tuesday nights and have dinner. I remember telling him, "God, I hate hearing 'Dick said' in the company as a reason for things." 'Cause what would happen was—let's, I'll give you an example. I remember this example really well. The Android app started taking longer and longer and longer to boot. And so we would have a metrics meeting. I was like, "Hey man, you can see the usage numbers going down almost linearly with the Android boot time going up. People are firing up Twitter and it's taking too long and they're moving on to something else. Like over here on iOS, that doesn't happen. It's gotta take just as long to boot Android as it does iOS." Okay. So two, three nights later, a week later, I'm walking around like one of the engineering floors after hours and this Android engineer comes up to me and he goes, "Hey, can I ask you a question?" I'm like, "Yeah." He goes, "Hey, did you say remove the JSON file from the Android boot system?" I was like, "I didn't. I know what a JSON file is, but I couldn't tell you what's in the Android boot JSON." And he's like, "Okay, 'cause I was told Dick said remove the—" and I was like, "Aha, I think I know what's going on here." Right? And this would happen throughout the company. Some manager or director decides, "Here's a way to solve this problem," and the engineer pushes back against them and they just go, "You know what? Dick said do it." And it became like this reason for doing things. So part of the speed was: no, we're not going to have "so-and-so said." It's you push the decisions down the stack and by the way, if your team makes a decision and you can't defend it to your manager, like, that's—don't go, "Well, so-and-so said." Like, have the engineer talk to the director, whatever. The second thing I did as we got bigger and bigger is I started realizing, "Oh, the organization is slowing down because there are all these fiefdoms in the organization that want—that want like ball control over their stuff." Very common. Very common. So here was how it surfaced. We ran an experiment and it was like, "Oh this is actually really super interesting, what are the hypotheticals about what this experiment would do in production?" The engineer runs through it in an experimentation meeting: "Great, let's go ahead and launch this to, you know, X percent of users and see what happens." One week, two weeks, three weeks go by and like, "Hey where is—" I'm like looking at the app, I'm like, "Where is, you know, where's this engagement experiment we're running?" And I find the engineer and he's like, "Oh, I'm waiting for, you know, Trust and Safety to sign off on the—" I'm like, "What?" He's like, "Oh yeah well, I asked a so-and-so if we were ready to launch and they didn't say no, but they said you have to go get approval from User Security." And I started realizing that when people would try to go do something, they wouldn't be told, "No, you can't do that." They would be told something much, much worse and slower than that: "You need approval from security. You got to get permission from security, you got to ask your mom, go ask these 14 people and if they all say yes..." Okay, everyone's got this problem. How do you fix it? So what I did was, it's very similar to what Bezos used to do at Amazon with Bias to Action. I had—it was from my improv background—I was like, "We're going to have a 'Bias to Yes.'" And Bias to Yes is: only your direct, the person you report to, is allowed to tell you you're not allowed to do that. Or legal can tell you if you do that you'll be fired 'cause it's illegal and a violation of the privacy policy. But no other organization, period, is allowed to tell you you can't do that or you need to go ask these people for permission. Just your direct manager. And that means if you have an idea for an experiment and your manager signs off on it and you have time to do it 'cause you're done with whatever else you're working on, no one can tell you you can't do that. Or, you know, the marketing team's not allowed to tell you you can't use the Mailchimp software we license 'cause that's the marketing team's, right? And so Bias to Yes was: I don't want to hear about something being stalled because organization X said you have to go get approval from organization Y. And that was great. Experiments started flying out. In fact, engineers started going like, "Wait, I don't have to necessarily get Design to build all these wireframes for something. I can just do it myself since we're only launching it to 1% and see if it works." And so that was just great. It worked throughout the organization and I would repeatedly get on stage at all-hands meetings and talk about Bias to Yes and what it means and what it doesn't mean. Doesn't mean you can go stop doing what you're supposed to do 'cause it's priority and do whatever you want to do, 'cause your manager is going to go like, "If you do that I'm gonna fire you 'cause you know, you're being measured on getting things done." Okay. No group decisions, DRIs, and Bias to Action. Anything else that all these CEOs that I work with are like, "We're slowing down, how do we speed up?" Don't solve problems with processes. So, it happens all the time in organizations. Like, some customer problem happens, the customer churns out or the advertiser quits and the CEO goes, "That can never happen again." And so what happens? Some step gets added to some process that's like, "Before you do..." I remember at Google, famously, the launch checklist got to be like 17 pages long or something and Larry was like, "This is insane." I was like, "Yeah, it's 'cause you know, it's like the FDA—never another thalidomide—or the TSA: 'Make sure there's no water bottle on the plane.'" And pretty soon you're not managing to outcomes, you're managing to the processes and you gotta go through nine steps before you—you know, instead of just managing to the outcome of "make sure there's not a bomb on the plane." So the way we did that, the way you do that is you solve problems with DRIs, operating control, and not processes. Like, you should probably only really have a couple organizational processes and everything else should be span of control and decision making. When you do that, you can't—and this was something I had to come to understand and adapt to—when you do that, you can't punish people when things go wrong. You want them to take risks, you want them to be bold, you want them to have this Bias to Action and do things and not go around asking for, tiptoeing around asking for permission. But one of the implications is you can't punish people for making mistakes. And I got to the point where I would tell the team, "It's not the job of leadership to prevent mistakes from happening, it's the job of leadership to correct mistakes quickly when they happen." So if you screw something up, like, by all means tell us so we can help you fix it, but no one should get in trouble for screwing something up or making a mistake. And I didn't come to that immediately, but I came to realize that that was an important artifact, if you will, of pushing decisions down the stack and Bias to Yes and we're not going to have lots of processes. Love it. So many of the CEOs I work with—I just was at a—I won't say the company, but here in New York City and spoke at their company meeting. Here's how I'd describe every company: when it's, call it under 50 employees, everybody knows what's going on all the time. When it's over 150 employees, nobody knows what's going on anytime. How did you fix, how did you scale communication, keep people on page, and tell me a little bit about the management by walking around? Yeah, well, I did two things. First of all, I taught this management class at Twitter. It was actually based on Ben Horowitz teaching a management class at Loudcloud. Fascinating. And Ben had in his outline from his management class a statement that I highlighted and underlined and circled three times: "Make sure everybody understands what you understand." So I would always tell my managers that. Like, your job is to make sure everybody understands what the priorities are and what it means for them and their team, how they fit into those priorities, and more importantly for your team, what it means for them personally and professionally if we achieve what we have to achieve. Those kinds of things are great. I remember somebody telling me a story of taking a factory tour at a Tesla shop and seeing some guy who's like, screwing a widget into the car and asking the guy, like, "What are you doing?" And the guy didn't just say "screwing the widget into the car," he said, "Well, if I do this and we're able to build these kinds of cars, and these kinds of cars can become more popular in America..." I mean, the guy understood the story and I thought that was great. And so it's like the analogy: someone asks the bricklayer, "What are you doing?" and the bricklayer says, "Oh, I'm laying bricks," and the other bricklayer says, "I'm building a beautiful cathedral." I love it. Yeah, like you want people to under—I mean, how much more motivating is it to work in a company where you know, "If I'm successful in my work, I understand what the implications are for both me personally and the company." Okay. Priorities and context. Priorities and context. Good news travels fast, bad news travels slow, and as the org gets bigger, it slows down and the signal gets distorted. How do CEOs deal with that? Yeah, I said earlier, it's not the job of leadership to prevent mistakes from happening, it's the job of leadership to help correct them quickly when they happen. So I would always communicate: "You gotta tell leadership if something's screwed up." If your situation is screwed up, or your team or some decision is a mess, you have to communicate that constantly. And then secondly, I would regularly communicate the priorities to the team over and over and over at all-hands meetings. "Here's what the priorities are that we're working on, this is how we're going to measure them, this is why they're important, and this is what they mean for us as a company if we're successful." You always wanted—I always wanted people to understand not just what we had to do, but why it was important to do it. And I think that scaling communication architecture is like one of the most important things in any company. Part of it is never saying "because so-and-so said." That's not context. That doesn't help your team understand context for why some decision was made, or why, or what they should do or how they should go do it. So that combination of pushing decisions down the stack, making sure that everybody understands what you understand—and a lot of that is context—and always communicating problems or issues up the stack as a means of helping get it resolved quickly, were the ways we dealt with that. One last thing that I picked up from Steve, a Steve Jobs trick. I love a Steve Jobs trick. Yeah, so when Steve was managing both Pixar and Apple—so at the time he's CEO of Pixar still, but now he's back in the seat at Apple. They're making like Toy Story 2, and what Steve would do—so he's got one day to come up to speed on what the heck's going on at Pixar—he'd go into the office on Friday and he would say, "All right, today I want to sit down with like, the illustrators." You know, without Lasseter, the director of the movie, John, without Lasseter in the room. "I just want to sit down, me and the illustrators." And he would go, there's like eight or nine or whatever of 'em: "Janet, tell me something that's not working well at Pixar." And then, "Bob, do you agree with that?" You know, taking notes. Like, "Okay, Phyllis, tell me something that's working well at Pixar—not on the movie, not about your job, you know." And at the end of the meeting—45 minute, one hour meeting—he would say, "Okay, here's what I heard. You know, people are having these kinds of issues, this is going really well. I'm going to go talk to John about it and he'll deal with it on Monday. You know, you can follow up with him." So he's not saying, "Oh yeah, that sucks," or "You know, no you're wrong," or whatever. He's taking notes, making sure he hears everything that was said, and then goes and talks to Lasseter about it. I started doing that with teams. You quickly find out if that team knows what the priorities are, is in sync with what you're hearing from the leadership of that team. Some of the leaders of the company hated it and I was like, "Well, now I'm concerned about why you hate it 'cause yeah." But you're not in that meeting doing things like throwing the director under the bus. You're going, "Okay, here's what I heard. I'm going to go talk to my management team about it on Monday and you know, Chris or whoever will follow up with you guys." Let me switch to you on priorities. So all these CEOs I coach, they have their list of priorities—it's January 1st—but oh my God, there's so much opportunity, so much interesting stuff, and this employee has this thing going on. How often did you rejigger those priorities? Were you disciplined about it over the course of whatever that period was? Yeah. You can rejigger them once in a while. What's once in a while? I don't know, like, you know, if you've got four huge priorities for the first four to five months of the year, you can't be—you can't get to February and go, "These two are stupid and we're changing," you know? Once or twice a year you can go, "Actually, let's change that one to this other thing." If you start doing it regularly, people are like, "Well, we can get to work on the direct messaging architecture, but Dick's probably going to change, you know, 50/50 he's going to change." You just can't do stuff like that. By the way, that's another way companies slow down, because people are just like, "Look, I'm not going to bust my ass on this thing when there's a 50% chance Dick's going to throw it out the window in a month." Yes. Let me test something on you. A lot of these hyper-growers—it used to be that there were a lot of one-way doors in the software industry. Stuff took a long freaking time to get out the door even to test, and you had to kind of do things serially. Now you can do so much so fast, and so they're just doing more and it's more in parallel. And a lot of those one-way doors are two-way doors. Is focus overrated now? No. Focus is—I don't think focus is ever overrated. However, the fact that you can do more quickly, and since they're two-way doors, you're like, "Great." I mean, this is the old Jeff Bezos thing: only take a long time to make decisions that are existential, like "we might die as a company if we make the wrong decision." There's fewer and fewer of those. Decisions that can be—like, if we screwed it up, we can go back and do the other one—do those right away. So focus is still important, but it's also true that you should feel like you can do a lot more now with less because you can. There was a point in 2011 when we wanted to try to get Jeff Bezos to join the board of the company. Like, we have an independent seat open, who would be the best possible independent director? Jeff Bezos. So Jack and I go meet with Jeff Bezos. I think it was like at the Allen & Company conference or something like that. And Jeff asks a question about strategy. And Steve Jobs was still alive at the time and Jack said, "Well, Steve says the most important part about being a CEO is focus." "And you should say no to almost everything and it's, in fact, it's the CEO's job to be the editor-in-chief and you should say no to almost everything and do very, very few things." And Bezos looks at Jack and goes, "Oh yeah? Well, I like to do everything!" And he does the big Bezos laugh and he goes, "You know, my team has to talk me out of stuff!" He goes, "You know, there's lots of ways to be successful!" And I remember that like it was yesterday, and he's right. There's many ways to climb the mountain. And I think it's true that today he would say, "Great, I can do even more now. We can still be focused but I can do even more things." I think those things can both be true. Okay, just want to get back to some of your management techniques 'cause I think they were great. The management by walking around, which is a very old school Hewlett Packard concept—it sounds like you didn't do it the same time every week. No. I did it at all hours, and mostly late night. It started because Bill Campbell—for people who don't know who are listening to the podcast, Bill was on the board of Apple and famously coached Larry, Sergey, and Eric at Google. They wrote a book about him called "Trillion Dollar Coach." The Benchmark folks had also brought him into Twitter before I got there. And Bill famously said when I took over as CEO, "You could throw a grenade into Twitter at 6 p.m. and only take out the cleaning people." And he was right. So I was like, okay, how do you—you can't just tell people "work harder," you know? They're like, "Okay, you work harder." So what I did was—it was such a stupid thing—but I was like, "All right, I'm just going to go out to get dinner and I'm going to come back at like 9 p.m. or 10 p.m." I'm going to walk around, and whoever is here, I'm going to talk about the stuff they're doing at the next all-hands meeting and I'm gonna like, bump up the prioritization of that. I remember like Marcel Molina at @noradio on Twitter—that's a little promotion for Marcel there. Marcel was always working late on stuff and I would be like, "What are you working on?" and "Oh, I'm working on native retweet" or whatever. And I would get in front of the company and go, "Look at the work Marcel is doing." And people were like, "How come—how does he always know what Marcel is doing?" And other people would go like, "Oh, Dick comes back after dinner at night and walks around." And people slowly, slowly started to change the culture. Okay, the people who are around when Dick's walking around are getting their stuff prioritized, etc. I super duplicated you; I walked around Friday afternoons and I learned a lot. Yeah. And you—yeah, so I would just go up to engineers and go, "What's going on? What are you working on?" Yeah. You had an interesting expression that I don't understand about "forest fires" and "forestry management." Oh yeah. Yeah, I was trying to help a director in the company be more strategic. They were super tactical. And I was finally like, "Your job as a director as you grow in this company is you can't just say, 'Well, I put out this forest fire.' Your job is to map the territory and start to do forestry management." "Like, what are we going to do so that next year, if there's a fire over here, that highway is not going to be in trouble? Like, you can only work so many hours a day and stamping out fires isn't going to work as we scale. You need to push those down the stack." "Your team should be fighting the fire that's over there because it might, you know, burn a house down. You need to be focused more on thinking about this whole pie and what has to be true 24 months from now so this whole territory is in better shape." And that was the metaphor I had for helping people try to think strategically versus like, "Well, I'm chopping down the trees. Look at all the trees." Well, that's not leadership and strategy; that's tactics. I like that. Okay, now I get it. One of the people I connected with before interviewing you said something interesting about you. Oh boy, I can hardly wait. I'm sure most people did, but anyway. Both sides of this are interesting. He said, "You know, Silicon Valley, San Francisco, everyone's passive-aggressive." So I'm curious your take on that 'cause I don't live in Silicon Valley. And the second thing he said: "Dick's not passive-aggressive. Dick's aggressive." Yeah. Give me both sides of that. It's just like—I am super—I know this is a personal flaw, but I'm super impatient. Like, people would come in and go, "Here's why I think..." or whatever. "I'm sad." You know? And they would start talking to me like, "Okay, stop. It seems like you don't like working here. You should quit." "No, I want to tell you why I'm sad." You know? Like, "Okay, I don't—I don't care. Who cares? I'm sorry, I'm not trying to be a jerk, but we have a million things to do." I remember, and it was just like—I was just direct and blunt with people. Not a jerk. You don't have to be—people think a lot of times, especially engineering leaders who have come up in the organization coding and programming and doing stuff on their own and now they have to manage people—they think when you tell them you have to manage performance, you have to be a jerk. You don't have to be a jerk about it. You just have to be direct about it and forthright and not, "No one's sadder about this than me," you know? Don't say stupid stuff like that. You can be empathetic and still be straightforward. Okay. A lot of these CEOs I coach are in their mid-to-late 20s and in their whole life, they haven't given—they don't go to their mom and say, "I got some feedback on dinner," or like, "You're hogging the basketball." You've just never given a Homo sapien feedback. It's unnatural. Advice for that CEO? Yeah, my advice for that CEO—Bill Campbell actually gave me this advice early on. He's like, "Write down what you want to say, and then say that and don't say anything else." So the night before, if you write down the honest feedback that you would give Brian, especially if you have to give a bad performance review, I guarantee you 99 out of 100 people, if they go into the meeting the next day, they don't say that. They're like, "Listen, Brian..." instead of, "You know, it's a really hard time here for everybody, we're all working..." you know, shut the fuck up. Like, the person knows you're about to hit them over the head or something. And you also don't have to say, "Hey Brian, that's just the way it is, you know, too bad if you don't like it." You just write down what you want to say and be forthright and honest. You can say, "I understand. If you don't want to deal with it anymore, I get it." Say those things and don't add a bunch of fluff around it. I thought that was great feedback and I would do that. Sit with the silence. Yeah, sit with the silence. Sitting with silence is hard. Yeah, it is unnatural. It's unnatural. I used to do a thing in my management class where it was a six-hour course, there was a ton of stuff in it, but one of the things I would do was about giving people feedback. And like, "Okay Brian, you're going to give Dick feedback." "And the feedback is: you've been working on this thing for six weeks and busting your ass and you've been here till midnight every night, and we have to cancel the project 'cause it's no longer a priority," or some horrible thing that no one wants to hear. The only person who did the role-play who was ever good at sitting in silence was Elad Gil, current venture capitalist. Elad sat in the chair and gave the person the feedback. And I would pull the employee aside before the role-play and say, "This is bullshit, and you're going to tell Dick it shouldn't be cancelled, they need to look at the data." And Elad was great; he just sat there. It's hard. And then eventually, the other person runs out of gas and stops talking. And Elad was great at that, and the only person who ever did it. Okay. One of the things I want to ask you about is you had some very talented folks, some of which you brought in, like Adam Bain and Kevin Weil, Anthony Noto, Elad. How did you guys recruit? What did you look for? Are you looking for slope or experience? It's a great question. I always actually tried to promote from within as much as possible because you want people inside the company to feel like, "We're not going to get capped out where I can only get to this level because anything above that, Dick just goes out and finds someone." So Kevin Weil, for example, was promoted from within, from an IC when I got there in 2009—well, there were only 40 people in the company then—to eventually running all of product after running all of ads product. He was promoted from within. Noto was—I just felt like Twitter was such, such a drama. Twitter is like the drama queen of hyper-growth companies. You know, it was constantly in the news. When I took over as CEO, that year the Twitter bird had hanged itself and exploded on two magazine covers. So I went up to our head of communications, Gabriel Stricker, and I was like, "Gabriel, great news. Next year's goal: no dead birds on magazine covers." Anyway, it was just constant drama. And Noto was so good at Goldman Sachs, where he ran technology and media, at dealing with investors. I had been trying for a while to bring him into Twitter, and I was finally able to. And he was, of course, he's fantastic. How about Adam? Adam was funny. Adam was what you'd call a "non-consensus hire" when I first recruited him. It was while I was COO, and I went to the board and Ev and said, "I got the perfect person to run revenue at Twitter. It's this guy Adam Bain, he's in LA and he works for Fox Interactive." And everyone was like, "You're going to bring in someone from Fox in LA to San Francisco to run our revenue?" But he was the perfect person to do it. I must have flown down to LA for five or six months before I could convince him to do it. But I'd met him while we were in the process of selling FeedBurner. His employer, News Corp, was one of the interested parties. I got to know him and was just like, "Man, this guy is tireless." And we were going to be in a constant dogfight, fighting from behind against Facebook for social ad dollars. And Adam was just—he's just one of those people who's got a motor that doesn't stop. There is an old adage: "fire fast, hire slow." Yeah, I always said that out loud. I didn't do that. I fired slow, and almost every time I was like, "Man, I knew six weeks ago." Yeah, we tried to hold ourselves accountable to that. It's horrible. Like, there's nothing worse than having to do something like that. I remember one of my board members, David Rosenblatt—you know, I always am a little bit like, "Come on," when people are like, "Oh, I like firing people because it's the right thing for them." That just sounds like you're an asshole 'cause it's horrible. But you have to do it. And I remember asking David Rosenblatt 'cause I had to terminate someone with global operating responsibility. I was like, "Wait a minute. If I fire them in the morning, the people in Japan are going to be in bed..." "But if I fire them in the afternoon then the people..." you know what I mean. I had to figure out how to do it. And I was talking to my board about it, and David Rosenblatt, who had been the CEO of DoubleClick, was like, "Oh, I had to do that." "I actually saved the calendar date from that 'cause I had a whole series of communications I had to roll out." This is where now we're a couple-thousand-person company with 30 offices around the world. So I get his day of communications. The only thing I remember from it is: "6 a.m. Wake up in a cold sweat." You know, it's just horrible. You don't—it's not fun, you can't wait till it's over, and you just have to do it. Jeff Weiner, the LinkedIn CEO, had an analogy for baseball fans. He was like, "You know, sometimes you see the manager walk out to the mound and the pitcher's like, 'No no no don't worry coach, I still got it.' And the manager walks back and the next guy cranks one out of the park." And Jeff was like, "I always tell myself: don't be the manager having the first conversation with the person when you know what you should be doing is having the second conversation with the person." As a Red Sox fan, that really hits home. I think his example was actually Pedro Martinez and Grady Little didn't take him out. Yes. I work with all these hyper-growth companies, I think you do too. What are the pitfalls when you're in hyper-growth and what are your tips for these CEOs? One of the real pitfalls, especially for these hyper-growth AI companies that are going from single-digit millions up to 100 million, is it's very easy to take your eye off the ball when you raise too much money. By taking your eye off the ball, I mean, we're not thinking about operating leverage and efficiency. Once the money's there, you're like, "Don't have to worry about that. I'll go worry about this thing over here." Next thing you know, the burn is crazy. "What are you worried about? We have money." It happens all the time. And Ryan wrote this great post saying, "Look, I thought I was like Mr. Efficiency, but when you don't have to worry about it at all, you stop worrying about it." Are people raising too much money? I think so, in many cases. The models probably need all the money for compute. But for an app company? I think people are raising too much money because raising a hundred million Series B is the new twenty million dollar Series B. What else in hyper-growth breaks? Hiring. Oh, almost—I mean, my favorite thing is you talk to a CEO and they're like, "We're only going to hire A-players." You know? I'm like, "Yeah, PS, everyone says that." Come back to me when your customer success team has to hire twice as many people next quarter because you just turned a bunch of customers because no one checked in with them last quarter. And then tell me. You just end up not doing that. So, one of the things you have to do when you realize you don't only hire A-plus players is you have to get religious about managing out low performers. But as you move down the stack and you've got inexperienced managers, there's nothing they want to do less than let someone go. They'll do anything not to do it. How do you fix that? You prevent them from trans-firing people. Trans-firing is like, "Janet would be great on the ads team. She loves ads." You need people? Yes. And then you realize, "No, no, no." So I had—we had performance reviews. I was like, if your review is below an X number, you can't switch teams. Yep. That's a good idea. No, you know, a whatever—let's say it's one through four. Four is amazing, one is you gotta go. If you're a two, you can't move teams until you're no longer a two. PS, if you go two to three to two to three, I'm going to talk to your manager and say, "Don't tell me this person is a three again just so you don't have to put them on a performance plan." So we would always go through as a senior team and say, "Okay, I can see these eight people are bouncing from two to three to back to two. You're just trying to not fire people. I can see that your curve is inflated." We would just be super hardcore about that. Let's talk about competition for a second. You were competing with Facebook. They were the juggernaut that we had the hardest time dealing with. And like, tomorrow I'm speaking at another company meeting, they're going to be competing with OpenAI and Anthropic. Almost every boardroom I'm on is talking about that. Everyone's anxious about that. Advice about that? First of all, you have to acknowledge it and then you just have to—I mean, one of the things I would always do is say, "They're huge and Mark is the most competitive person on the planet." Trust me when I tell you, they don't say to themselves, "Oh whatever, Twitter won that thing, that's fine." They're like, "That can never happen again." So we have to think about them, we have to talk about them, and we have to try to figure out how to outhustle them. And Adam Bain on the revenue team was great at that. He would go to one of the first big customer meetings—let's say it's Unilever—and he would sit down with the CMO from Unilever before the meeting and say, "How'd the Facebook meeting go?" "Oh, great, blah blah, what are they psyched about showing you this year?" You know? And then he would learn all that and he would go into the meeting with Unilever and say, "Even if we had a bunch of things to show them," he'd say, "We have some things we want to show you, but we really want to spend the next two hours just understanding your business." He knew that she just told him that the Facebook people came in and showed a bunch of things and weren't asking those kinds of questions. Now, Facebook wised up to that over time, but Adam had just come up with a great way of thinking about how to compete with this juggernaut. And the juggernaut's rolling out new things every quarter and they're microtargeting, so I'm going to totally come at it from a different angle. It was just smart about learning how to do that by talking to the customer beforehand. You guys picked the advertising model to make money. As you look back at that, good call? Yes. Advertising is undefeated. ARPU is higher, it just is, versus subscription revenue etc. I think we would do the same thing over again. Any other do-overs? Oh yeah. We tried to buy Instagram the month before Facebook bought it. And by the way, for a much higher percentage of the company. Facebook's offer was famously a billion dollars, and I think it was 700 of stock and 300 million of cash. They only had eight or nine employees at the time: Mike Krieger and Kevin Systrom. But we knew well before that, like, these guys are—Kevin is an extraordinary product manager. Kevin and Evan Spiegel I think are probably two of the best product thinkers in Silicon Valley. So I knew that, and well before Mark made his offer, I went out to dinner with Kevin and Mike and told them we wanted to buy the company. We would end up giving them well over 10% of Twitter, which was something equivalent to 700 million in stock. And I would have gone and—I could go back there and talk to Kevin—I would say, "Like, you can run the company when I leave, congrats, you'll get to be CEO." I mean, they still might not have done it. And I would also say, "Also, I'll go borrow 300 million dollars from JP Morgan and give it to you and you can have it all and we'll pay them back later." That, because that was a game changer. Once they had Instagram, I was like, "Oh man, we're boxed out." That is the future of rich media social media feeds. We have Vine, but it's much smaller and they're now going to come at us on Instagram with video, which of course they did and that—and that was sort of checkmate. Other regrets? Any regrets on man the content moderation stuff has been in the news over the last five years. It wasn't as hot while you were there. Any as you look back at that? It was always like it was the thing I'll say about content moderation that people get wrong all the time is like, "This is really easy, just prevent people from doing that." You're like, "Okay, well that is a lyric in a Kanye song, can he not tweet his lyrics?" Or whatever. Or it's easy just—I'll give you a specific example that I always think highlights why it was such a pain in the ass and it's so hard. You can't just come up with a set... these companies try to come up with a set of rules and like, "Well if it violates the rule you have to kick it off and if it doesn't violate the rule you can't kick it off." Like that's... you just can't think about it that way. These things are so subjective. So ISIS, the terrorist organization, captures three pilots from some Air Force and puts them in cages basically on a beach and lights them on fire and kills them, basically burns them to death. And they post these to Twitter, photos and videos. And my general counsel comes to me and goes, "Hey, these terrorist accounts are..." I'm like, "Great." She's like, "Suspend the accounts, right?" Like yeah, of course, like right away. Anyone that posts the media of these pilots being lit on fire, like suspend the account. Well, the freaking New York Post an hour later posts the huge photo of this pilot being lit on fire. And then I get this like, "Hey, what... you suspend... you know Kara or someone... like why'd you suspend the New York Post?" Like, what? We didn't suspend the New York Post. "Yeah you did." And you're like, "Okay, wait." I guess the New York Post can do it, but now who can't do it? I mean, and that was one tiny example. It was just so so infinitely hard and you just can't... my big if I could go back again is you just can't have these hard and fast rules. There has to be, again, pushing... having operating control and somebody with an understanding of the situation like our GC. You're going to have just a bunch of judgment calls and you're going to get a bunch of them wrong and that's life. In like after your tenure and like, I don't know what to believe, but it looks like there was a lot of conversation between the Biden administration and that group. And then a lot of accusations from Elon that that group was just very left of center in their orientation. Any reaction to that? Was that the case when you were there? I mean yeah, probably it was left of center, but I don't think I don't think when I was there that I don't think it impacted the way we thought about abuse. I mean, it wasn't like you know we didn't have any like, for example, we didn't have any like, "Oh, you're not allowed to dead name trans people." I mean, like that stuff was all post me being there. At the time it was mostly what we were dealing with... the Culture War kind of happened after you left. The yeah, the stuff we were dealing with when I was there were ridiculous amounts of Russian bots, which at the time were not election stuff. Maybe they were preparing for it, preparing the groundwork for 2016, but it was mostly like phishing attacks and just massive bot farms that were doing phishing attacks on people and trying to hack into their accounts or get credit card information, etc. Most of the CEOs who have been around a while have got a shit ton of bad press. You got a metric ton of bad press. It must... I got a bunch of bad press. It impacted me. I tried to show a face like it didn't, but it it was soul crushing to me. How did you handle it? I don't know, like I had been... I had had the fortunate opportunity to be a performer and do improv comedy and stuff and audition for SNL a couple times before I went back into business. And I had been like, you know, I was at... I remember I was at the Adelaide Comedy Festival with my improv group and we had this midnight show at the Opera House, the Adelaide Opera House. Adelaide in Australia? Yeah. Okay. And the crowd is hammered, drunk, maybe a thousand people, and it's a like an hour and ten minute show completely improvised. We get on stage and like five minutes in the crowd starts yelling like, "You suck! Get off!" Something like that. And like, "Man we got 80 more minutes or whatever." And so I don't know, I just became resilient to like, "Oh well, I gotta like have a booed on." Did it hurt inside? I don't know, like we got good press, I remember my daughter called me. The good press never hit for me, the bad press did. No no no, I'll tell you... I'll tell you my daughter called me, this is at the end of 2014. So 2013 we go public, IPO's successful, everyone's super excited, go yay us. And there's some you know the these stupid awards that come out come out and like "CEO of the Year: Dick Costolo." End of 2014 my daughter texts me, she's like, "I have bad news and good news." And was like, "What's the bad news?" She's like, "Yahoo Finance says you're one of the five worst CEOs of 2014." And I was like, "What's the good news?" She goes, "Nobody reads Yahoo Finance." Okay. Got it. Okay. I just felt like I don't know, it's like you're like okay. And I'll say one last thing on it, I've said this a bunch but but I think is important. I got invited to go to like the Vanity Fair Oscars party one of the years. By the way that must have been cool. Well I got invited to go. I didn't go. Why? Good question. So I told my daughter I don't want to start going to a bunch of stuff that I get invited to because I'm the CEO of Twitter, because then I'll be like, "Oh, I don't want to not be the CEO of Twitter 'cause I won't get invited to the." So I went to the NBA All-Star Game because I knew Adam Silver and he invited... I felt like it was a Dick Costolo invitation. Yes. I was like, "I want I'll go to things I get invited to 'cause I'm Dick Costolo." I don't want to go to things that the CEO of Twitter gets invited to because then I'll be like... wasn't it part of your job? No. I mean I mean I mean going to the White House, sure. Like, "Hey, the president wants to talk to you about you know Edward Snowden and abuse on the platform and what are we going to do about terrorists using these services." Yeah, you got to go to that as part of the job. But going to the like going to these things, you know, like the Oscars or whatever... like I don't know I don't know any of these people, they don't care about me. I totally would have done it. I totally would have done it. Well you would have been standing over in the corner going like, "No one's talking... Tom Cruise won't talk... Tom Cruise isn't talking to me." It's exceptionally rare these days that somebody bring a founder-led company is replaced or just they don't hire the adult external CEOs. Is that... should it be happening a lot more? I think it goes in waves. And it's not not doesn't happen at all these days. Yeah I know. I think it goes in waves and I think it will continue to be... there'll something will happen and people will start going, "You know we really need for these companies that are making you know four billion dollars in revenue after three years and the 26-year-old founder," they'll just something will happen and there will be a wave of you know operating executives coming back in and run some of the companies. It happens a little bit like Slootman at Snowflake. Pretty rare. Yeah yeah rare. Agreed. Even the public companies like MongoDB but Workday recently, but like maybe one a year. Yeah, if it's true I do think it goes in waves, we'll see if it we'll see if it happens. Okay you're a VP of blah-di-blah company and you want to be a Dick Costolo. What should you do? You want to be that adult. I want to be you when I grow up. I'm a VP at whatever. I'm Anthony Noto... did you know when he was CFO of Twitter he started like telling making it clear to you know executive recruiters like, "I want a CEO role. Like start you know when you start seeing CEO roles I I might not get one right away but like I want I want to talk to you about CEO roles when you get them." I got it. Get your name out there and just... I never... and Noto would tell you this too about his own career. I never took like a risk that I didn't you know that I wasn't super psyched I took. Even when I was like trying to get on SNL and wasn't successful. It ended up benefiting me down the road 'cause it was like, "All right, like like live TV on CNBC, are you okay doing that?" I'm like, "Look at the camera!" like I've got booed at midnight in Australia by a thousand people. I can look in the camera and talk about... should all CEOs take a comedy... my co-founder took a comedy class in business school and it really helped him. Yeah being on stage and you know being uncomfortable being on stage makes... I mean you go you stand up at an all-hands meeting you're like, "I know exactly what I'm going to say at the all-hands meeting I'm just going to talk about the priorities." That's easy after doing that. Okay. Let me give you a fun one. Hit me. Is San Francisco passive-aggressive? Is everyone passive-aggressive? Are CEOs passive-aggressive? I think there's a lot of passive-aggressiveness. I think I know who I know the person you talked to and I think and I know the other companies he's been at. I think he's generally right but there are some great exceptions. I think the Collison brothers at Stripe are both the rare combination of really really widely read and and and polymaths and understand have a broad perspective on humanity and biology and the human condition and and are and are forthright communicators and are de you know neither passive-aggressive nor hyper-aggressive. They're just they're forthright and straightforward and blunt. And I think those guys are great. And I and I feel the same way about Reed Hastings, a totally different executive at Netsky at Netflix. Reed is always like, "This is just how it is. You know I don't like I'm not I'm not trying to be mean about it this is just the way it is." And I think it I think it you rhyme with Reed in my head. Yeah yeah oh I take that as a huge compliment. I love that guy. I always thought those three people, John and Patrick at Stripe and Reed at Netflix, are extremely self-aware. Like I remember was at a table with Reed at a dinner once and he went around the table and said, "I want to hear everybody's opinion on this. How much of your success do you think is based on your DNA and how much of it is based on like I just learned how to kick ass and be amazing?" And I kind of I was like, "I think I know where he's going with this." And of course when it got around to himself he was like, "I think it's basically like 80% DNA." Interesting. How would you answer that? Yeah I would similarly and I think that's a result of him being... how much of is luck versus skill? Yeah I think there's certainly a bunch of luck. By I don't think he was saying luck versus skill. I think he was sort of saying like you're not like some you know exceptional person who came out and like I'm going to fight against the out... like it's basically encoded in you to be this way and you know it's ended up helping you. And sure there's like 20% luck along the way that put you in the position to get this role that you might not have otherwise had but you were always going to do something like this. Okay you brought up some good CEOs. The CEO rulebook feels to me to be changing. Your take on how Jensen Huang runs that with like 60 direct reports, no one-on-one, openly criticizing. He's got a whole different playbook. The thing that surprises me is I work with all these CEOs, nobody's copying him which I think's interesting. Your take on that. I go back to the Jeff Bezos comment to Jack Dorsey. "Jack, there are a lot of different ways to be successful." I think that I think that the mistake that CEOs make is they read the Walter Isaacson book on Elon Musk or they read the one on Steve Jobs and they think this is the archetype. Yeah. And Walter's just left out a whole bunch of stuff you know and that's not... did you have an archetype? No, I didn't have an archetype. Steve Jobs was mine. I tried to copy everything he did, quite poorly. I did it quite poorly. I mean it goes again to the like well Steve says you have to be an editor and you said and and you know and Jeff saying like, "Oh yeah well there's lots of different ways to do it." I think the and the reason I say that about the books is not just because they're more mythology than they are what really happened. Some of it's of course this happened but it leaves out a bunch of stuff. But more importantly you just if you try to be someone you're not, nobody is fooled. Like you think people are fucking fooled? Nobody is fooled. Everyone knows you're spinning them or you're playing some role and you're not yourself. And nothing is more miserable than trying to be someone you're not. One of the things I used to I used to do this in my management class actually during one of the NBA Finals in back in the like the 90s. This reporter's in the Houston Rockets locker room. And it was at the time where Charles Barkley did this ad for Nike where Charles dunks a basketball and he looks at the camera and goes, "I ain't no role model," something like that. Yeah. And this reporter's in the Houston Rockets locker room and asks Hakeem Olajuwon, "You know Charles Barkley says that kids shouldn't you know idolize look up to NBA players role models, what do you think about that?" And Hakeem Olajuwon goes, "I'm happy to be a role model, I love being a role model for kids." He's like, "Well Charles what he"—He wasn't wrong. He wasn't he wasn't he wasn't wrong. Although he appears to be on Ozempic or something, he's got to have his suit re-tailored. The this reporter's in the Houston Rockets locker room and goes up to Hakeem Olajuwon and says, "You know Charles Barkley says that kids shouldn't you know idolize look up to NBA players role models, what do you think about that?" And Hakeem Olajuwon goes, "I'm happy to be a role model, I love being a role model for kids." He's like, "Well Charles what" and and Hakeem says, "Well here's the deal. Charles is a different person in public than he is in private. And so he's got this anxiety and tension in his mind about whether he's supposed to be public Charles in this moment or private Charles in this moment. I'm always the same person so if people want to look up to me as a role model that's fine with me." And I just think that it's nothing but misery if you're trying to you know look at some written down playbook of how Steve Jobs did things and I gotta go do that when it's all but it was impossible for Steve to do it almost and you're certainly not going to be able to do it and you'll be miserable trying. Okay last question I have is like the Twitter board, man there's a lot of freaking drama there. Tons of it. So much. So much. You're building that, you're a 100 million dollar company going on 400 million. You're building a board, advice like should you bring in independents early? Should you wait? I had two board members that were immensely helpful to me. Peter Chernin who had been brought in from News Corp as an independent director 'cause he'd managed tens of thousands of people and I needed someone with operating experience, global operating experience. And Peter was great at always like always did the sort of the Jeff Bezos: "Let's talk about this from the user's perspective." Instead of like "How are we going to do XYZ" like he would always back up and say, "You know wait, if I'm the chief if I'm the chief user officer at this company and I'm thinking about this from the user" and so that was immensely helpful in lots and lots of cases. And then secondly Peter Fenton from Benchmark would always help me interview candidates and either close them or help me with "Hey how should I think about this candidate?" And that having someone on your board who's not in the day-to-day at the company and who can also give the candidate sort of a third-party perspective on why I think this is going to be a monster and why I think you should do this was like crazy helpful and helped convince numerous execs like Adam Bain and Anthony Noto to come to the company. Do you bring in okay you're at a hundred million, you're doing your series B, do you bring in an independent or you what happens now is they wait right before the IPO? No, I you I think it's helpful to have operating people with operating expertise in there much sooner than that. Okay and just while we're on IPO you took Twitter public, what's your... it's been in very hard to take companies public, it's become very unfashionable to take companies public. I would have kept it private longer if I could go back and do anything over again. I would have too. It's just so much easier running a private company and not having to like okay well that's going to mean we're going to miss the revenue number by a percent. It's the right thing to do but you know the employees are going to be running around like, "What's wrong?" when the stock goes down 8% after hours. You know and as you know you don't have a daily ticker that the employees are freaking out about when you're private company and it's just a lot more you can be it's so much easier and stress-free to focus on the long term as opposed to, "Okay now we have to make a long term versus a short term to have a discussion about this because you know everything's going to go sideways if we do this in Q1 instead of" blah blah blah. You get it. It was just a lot easier to run private company. I wanted to talk to you for a long time because you are kind of a rare species these days, the adult CEO brought into a hypergrowth company and you delivered. Thanks for coming on the pod. Thanks for having me. Okay I thought Dick was great. It's a masterclass in how do you scale and be a terrific scale-up CEO. There's a few things he talked about that I think are worth reviewing. Some of which I did, some of which I wish I did when I was CEO of HubSpot. One thing he did that I totally did was management by walking around. I'd wait until like seven o'clock at night and I'd walk the halls of HubSpot through the dev organization, support, sales, see who was around, and really hear what was happening. That was super useful for me. It was great context and it was also good to see who was burning the midnight oil. I liked his feedback around giving tough people tough feedback. I was never very good at this and his advice is good. It's you know the night before you're going to give feedback for that tough person, you gotta write it down the night before, practice it in the mirror, it gives good advice. And when you deliver it, you deliver it and you sit in silence. No fluff, you sit in silence. Really hard to do, but I think that's great advice. He talked about killing committees and using DRI's. A lot of the CEOs I've interviewed are talking about similar things, particularly important as you've got silos in your organization and the important stuff cuts across boundaries of those silos. He gave a great quote from another pod guest, Ben Horowitz. He said, "Make sure everybody understands what you understand." Not just the what, the why, and what success means for them personally. Priorities without context are useless. I thought that was a gem from Ben and I thought it was really cool that he repeated it. He accuses Silicon Valley of being passive-aggressive. He said he's not, he's aggressive. I think that's good advice. Like a lot of CEOs he says you should hire fast and fire slow. He didn't fire slow. Almost no other CEOs don't fire slow, I did not fire slow, and so he wishes he did that. Some gems in there. Hope you enjoyed it and looking forward to having you in the next pod.

Meet The CEO Behind This $90 Million Plan Expanding HBCUs In Private Finance

Forbes(2.1M)·49:20·540 views·2026-05-07
Marcus ShawCEO, AltFinance Jabari YoungSenior Writer, Forbes
TL;DRAltFinance is a $90 million, 10-year nonprofit initiative backed by Apollo, Ares, and Oaktree to diversify the private investment industry by providing HBCU students with specialized training, mentorship, and career pathways.
CEO Marcus Shaw emphasizes that while...

TL;DR

AltFinance is a $90 million, 10-year nonprofit initiative backed by Apollo, Ares, and Oaktree to diversify the private investment industry by providing HBCU students with specialized training, mentorship, and career pathways. CEO Marcus Shaw emphasizes that while artificial intelligence will transform the sector, the core of future success remains human talent, grit, and intentional wealth-building through early investing.

The AltFinance Mission: Diversifying Private Capital

  • Program Structure: AltFinance operates as a co-curricular apprenticeship model designed to prepare students for "alternative" investment roles, including private equity, venture capital, and private credit.
      • Fellowship Reach: In its first five years, the program has supported over 250 fellows, placing them in major global investment firms and transactional banks.
      • HBCU Integration: The organization provides capacity-building grants and works directly with faculty at Historically Black Colleges and Universities to modernize finance curricula.
  • Shifting Student Awareness: Initially, only about 1 in 30 students understood the "alternative" investment landscape. Five years later, awareness has surged to 20-30% of students entering the program with specific industry knowledge and peer networks.
  • The "Flywheel Effect": Success is driven by a growing alumni network. Graduates currently working in the industry "reach back" to mentor underclassmen, providing realistic insights into the high-intensity requirements of financial modeling and Excel-based work.

Artificial Intelligence: Productivity vs. Replacement

  • The Tools vs. Talent Thesis: Shaw asserts that AI will not replace human professionals, but rather that professionals who fail to utilize AI will be replaced by those who do.
  • Increasing Efficiency: AI is viewed as a tool to increase the "total addressable market" for investments by allowing firms to analyze more deals and perspectives with higher efficiency.
  • Educational Strategy: AltFinance integrates AI training into its finance curriculum. Teaching students to use AI tools alongside foundational finance allows them to enter the workforce with a modernized skill set that avoids the need for retraining.

Investment Strategy and Wealth Building

  • Wealth Accumulation: Shaw notes that in the U.S., individuals cannot simply "work" their way to significant wealth; participation in the investment market is a mandatory requirement for wealth-building.
  • Market Outlook:
      • Volatility as Opportunity: Shaw maintains a "constructive" view of current market volatility, preferring to "buy on sale" when uncertainty creates discounts in tech, media, and telecom infrastructure.
      • Core Assets: He highlights early real estate acquisition as a primary vehicle for long-term family wealth, regardless of market timing or "palatial" scale.
  • Sector Interest: Shaw expresses interest in modernizing U.S. infrastructure, specifically electrical grids and energy systems, identifying them as critical areas for human and physical capital investment.

Leadership and Career Philosophy

  • The "Burden of Optimism": Shaw views his career through the lens of purpose, acknowledging that leading change is often "torturous" or burdensome but necessary for creating new pathways for others.
  • Coaching Mentality: Drawing on his background as a Morehouse College football kicker and a youth coach, Shaw employs a "hard coaching" style.
      • Grit over Pedigree: He prioritizes resilience and the ability to "suit back up" after failure—citing a missed game-winning field goal in college as a foundational lesson in persistence.
  • Stakeholder Management: Shaw treats the nonprofit like a for-profit entity, focusing on "maximizing stakeholder value" across four groups: donors/partners, students, HBCU administrators, and the broader financial industry.
"In America, you cannot work your way to wealth. Investing has to be a part of your wealth-building strategy." — Marcus Shaw
"AI is not going to replace people... but people that don't use AI will be replaced." — Marcus Shaw
"If you ask for money you get advice, and if you ask for advice you get money. People want to be a part of your story." — Marcus Shaw
In America, you cannot work your way to wealth. Investing has to be a part of your wealth-building strategy. Those are the words of one CEO who is trying to level the playing field when it comes to the investment space through the lens of an apprenticeship program. We'll be talking to Marcus Shaw. You're in the Enterprise Zone from the Milken Institute in Beverly Hills. Hello everyone, it's Jabari Young, senior writer at Forbes, and we are at the Milken Institute in Beverly Hills, the 29th annual Milken Institute Global Conference. I am joined by Marcus Shaw, the President and CEO of AltFinance. AltFinance is a nonprofit organization and their goal is to bring more HBCUs into the space and diversify the private investment world. We're talking about private equity, private credit, and venture capital. Marcus, I appreciate the time, man. We were supposed to do this a long time ago at the NASDAQ. That's right. Yeah, how you doing? I'm doing well, Jabari. I appreciate it. Things have gone well. There's been a lot of change, both personally but in the larger environment over the past few years, but we're still here and doing great work. Yeah, I love it, man. I love it. Well, listen, man, again, we were supposed to do that at the NASDAQ, but here we are at Milken Institute. You've come to these conferences so many times over the years, man. What do you look to learn at these conferences? I'll call it the Super Bowl of the business world. For me, it's the Super Bowl of the business world. But when you come here, there's obviously the network and all that good stuff, but what do you look forward to learning when you come to these type of big conferences? I think what Milken is doing with the global conference is one of the most incredible things not only in the country but in the world. It's really finding what are the themes that some of the most influential and some of the brightest people in the world are thinking about. How are people thinking about talent? How are people thinking about growth? Artificial intelligence is something that I'm keen on, and I'm listening to the way that different leaders in both politics and in finance and in the social impact sector are thinking about how AI is impacting their outcomes and getting really a perspective. It's a great opportunity as a leader of an organization to step back and take an assessment of what other incredible leaders are thinking about. Biggest thing you've learned so far? Again, we're still early here, right? But biggest thing you've learned so far, what's the big takeaway? One of the big takeaways that I've heard thus far is that AI is not going to replace people. That's a good thing. That's a good thing. That's a good thing. But what I think is really interesting is that people that don't use AI will be replaced, right? And so, seeing artificial intelligence as a tool to increase human productivity as opposed to being something that will replace human efficiency, I think is an incredible takeaway. I've heard that from several different sector leaders and I think for the work that we're doing preparing young people for the world of finance, learning how to use AI as a tool is something that you've got to be keen to. Yeah, 100%, man. I definitely agree with that. And you know, there is a little bit of scare though when it comes to the world of finance and how AI is kind of going in. You have analysts and programs that could replace. Is there any fear, right, that programs like yours and you're trying to bring new people into the alt investment space—is there any fear that those jobs may not be widely available with AI? Look, I think there's two takes on these things. One, that you will have rapid efficiency due to the tool, that tool being AI, and that that's going to lead to a reduction in workforce. The other is that you're going to have an introduction of a tool which will increase the total addressable market. And so you'll have more opportunities to invest, different perspectives to invest with, and different tools with which to invest. And I think the latter is going to take place. And I believe that now you've got new people, like the students that we're working with, that are coming into the market, that are learning AI at the same time that they're learning finance. And so it's not having to reteach yourself finance; it's that you're learning it for the first time with these tools in place. I'm excited about where that goes and I think it will create a new generation of professionals that will take the industry to a new level. Yeah, I love that, man. I love it. Well, listen, before we dive in and talk AltFinance and update it, you gotta give me your take, right? Stock market is here, right? You've been a trader on Wall Street for years, right? And so you gotta tell me, when you look at the stock market as we sit here in May 2026, what does Marcus Shaw see? Look, I come from the world of "I like to buy things that are on sale." Whether it's shoes, whether it's homes, whether it's cars, I like to buy things that are at a discount. I think that there's a lot of volatility in the market that has created an opportunity. Although we've had a recovery over the past few weeks, I think there's still a lot of latent uncertainty in the market and a lot of volatility that allows you to buy things at a discount. And so I'm constructive on opportunities where people are doing the hard work and figuring out what is going to be able to be sustainable as we move forward and buying those things. And I think that that's a real opportunity. Yeah. Well, what type of investor are you? How do you build your portfolio? So I've had a couple of things. I covered tech, media, and telecom as a sell-side analyst and then as a buy-side analyst. So I've always had interest in technology and infrastructure plays on the public equity side. I've invested in some venture deals as a young man. I covered, or where I built an entrepreneurial ecosystem down in Tennessee and in Alabama, and so had the opportunity to meet a network of startup founders and an entrepreneurship and investment venture capital ecosystem that allowed me to get access to some deals. And so those are things that allowed me to build up my community. I think one of the incredible things that I did, and I recommend this to all young people, is buy real estate early. I'm an old school person. Can they afford it, though? Well, it's difficult. There is real estate that everybody can afford, but you might not always be able to afford the piece of real estate that you want. And so one of the things that benefited us was early on—and again, this is almost 30 years ago—I came out of college, I got married young, we bought a home. We lived in that home for several years and then we were able to rent it out pretty consistently. It wasn't a great palatial mansion, right? But it was a humble home in a good community with good schools, and we were able to rent that out and that has created an opportunity for us not only to hold that real estate but to also build up some wealth for our family. Yeah, I wish I was talking to you in 2019 when I sold my house. I know, I know. I cry, my stomach hurts all the time when I talk about that, because I had a house, it was about $80,000. That's when Obama was giving out money to buy houses back in the day. I took advantage of that program. I still can't believe I sold that house. And then look what happens in 2020. You get the pandemic and real estate goes way up. I mean, imagine that, right? Imagine a situation in 2019, 2020, where all the market indicators are pointing that there may be a recession on the come. You get a global pandemic, but the key is, it forced people to be in their home, right? So it's really a perfect storm to lift real estate. Normally you would think, oh man, I'm selling in 2019, this is an opportunity for me to take gains and you know, it's a great place for me to exit the market and maybe I rent or maybe I buy something a little bit cheaper. But the confluence of all of those things happening at one time, right? We got a lot of fiscal stimulus that led the market to a higher point, we got some monetary relief, and then we got a shelter in place which said, everybody said, "I want to have a really nice home." Right? And so that caused real estate prices not only to stabilize but to increase from that point on. And you know, I hate the fact that you had to sell in 2019—I do too—but you got a great job here. I appreciate it, man. Hopefully I can go back and get the house, but I'm sure it's not going to be the same price. That's right. Well listen, man, you walk into a third-grade class and they say, "What do you do, Mr. Shaw?" How do you explain your role to these third-grade students? And remember, you're talking about third-grade students. Yeah. So I've got an 11th grader and I've got a college student, but I remember when they were in third grade, right?
