And some of the things that I think about is how do you simplify this world? Right? And so the work that we do at AltFinance is rooted in educating students and preparing them for opportunities to think like investors and think like owners. Right? And so, one of the games I used to play with my kids when they were little was, they'd say, "Daddy, I want to stop by the store" or "Can we stop over there?" and "Just something I want to pick up." And I would play the game "Who owns that?". Right. So if you want to stop at that store, you got to tell me who owns the store. You got to tell me how that store makes money. If there's something that you want to buy from the store, you got to tell me what's the utility that that's going to bring to your life. If it's a game or a toy or something like that, you enjoy it because it makes you happy. If it's a piece of clothing, why is it that you want that piece of clothing versus one that's less expensive? What is that going to do for you? Right? And so I think building in third graders a sense of utility, a sense of ownership, a sense of making investments, a sense of financing—like all of these terms that make up capitalism—is important for young people to understand early on. Yeah. And you would explain your role at AltFinance as CEO, how would you tell them what you do for a living? Yeah, I mean, what we're doing at AltFinance is giving people an opportunity to participate in the investment ecosystem that drives the type of capitalism that we operate in the United States. And so whether you're—you know, we've had incredible college presidents that are here at this conference, we have nonprofit leaders that are here at this conference, we have fund managers, we have politicians. All of those things in the United States operate on capital. Right? If you're running for office, you've got to raise money, right, to finance your campaign. If you're the president of a college or the chancellor of a university system, that school is funded by dollars—both philanthropic dollars, dollars from tuition, and dollars that come from state and other funds, right? And so you've got to understand how the flow of capitalism works. Oftentimes for young people, they don't understand that those things are often connected by investment firms. And so students that have an interest in investing, students that have an interest in economics and finance that want to own, that want to be decision-makers and influencers in this economy, I think it's imperative that they understand the alternative investment market. Yeah, absolutely. Again, private investments, you know, including private capital, venture capital, real estate, all of it, and private credit as well. But let's dive into AltFinance, right? 2026 here. It was a nonprofit that was started in 2021, right? It's a 10-year commitment that is dedicated to diversifying the alt investment space, right? You want to take students, especially those in underserved areas, HBCUs and all those who have a love, as you said, for finance and introduce them, right, to the world and to the people, right? Because this is an apprenticeship program and backed by some major people, right? Apollo, Ares, Oaktree, Howard Marks—one of my favorite people to listen to. Tell me, update AltFinance in 2026 and how would you explain the organization for someone who's never heard of it? So five years later, we've done incredible work. Our mission is really to enhance the talent pipeline in the alternative space by connecting Historically Black Colleges and Universities and the students and faculty that attend those colleges and universities with industry-driven education to help people work through that apprentice model. Right? I mean, no matter how smart you are, you come into this business as an analyst. And you're going to work for an associate, and that associate will work for a VP, and so forth and such. But knowing what that path looks like is something that you've got to understand right when you come into college. Nowadays things have become more advanced. You used to could wait until you graduated and figure it out during your investment banking rotation. Today, students really need to have an understanding of what that process looks like as early as their freshman semester or their freshman year in college, so that they know which classes to take, so they know which internships are going to position them for the types of roles that they want to pursue in their first job out of school. And so what we're doing at AltFinance is working with the academic community at those HBCUs to provide co-curricular education, to provide access to mentors and industry players, and to also provide some support for schools and capacity building so that they can do it on their own. And it's been an incredible five years. In our flagship fellowship program, we've had over 250 young men and young women that have participated in the program. They're going to some of the largest investment firms in the world. They're going to some of the most transactional investment banks in the world, and they're building opportunities for their career early on that may have been unimaginable to them just five years ago. And so that's a positive for the industry because now the industry has more well-trained talent that's ready to take it to the next level. And we talked about the AI component, teaching students how to use that early on, something that we believe inevitably will be a large part of the training program in financial services at the largest banks and the largest alternative money managers. Teaching them how to utilize and build those skills in college will have absolutely strong returns on their performance once they move into the market. Yeah. Well, listen, again, 10-year, $90 million commitment with the partners. I read a story in the Financial Times and it said, "Hey listen, these partners are still committed," right? How are you facing what the atmosphere is? Right? You have DEI rollbacks, a new administration, the rules have changed. Right? How is a program like AltFinance continuing that momentum because the job and the work that you guys are doing is just truly important. What's it like in 2026? I mean, the reality is that our focus is and is always going to be on talent, and that our theory of change is that there's incredible talent at HBCUs. That's been indicated by the success of HBCU graduates in other fields like law and education and STEM, and we believe that we can transfer those same type of gains to the world of financial services. And so we're focused really on building great talent from the universities and HBCUs that we're working with. We're focused on making sure that they are moving with the industry as it moves, that they are building relationships and that they're creating a flywheel effect such that the students that have graduated and are having success in the market are now reaching back to the juniors and seniors and sophomores behind them and giving them some insight on what it looks like in the real world. Yeah. Since the program has been initiated or the inception of AltFinance, you going around these schools, have you sensed that there is more excitement and more interest in finance and private investment or are you still kind of— Absolutely. I mean, that's one of the earliest signals that we received was that in our first cohort, we had about one out of 30 students that had any relationship or really any deep understanding with alternative investments as an industry. They understood investment banking and they understood that there were sponsors and the buy-side that existed out there. But in terms of really having relationships with people and an understanding of how those firms operate, the awareness was fairly low. In just five years, that number is significant. I mean, we've got 20 to 30 percent of students that are coming in with people that have gone ahead of them, that are telling them about what their roles look like, they're telling them about the type of work that they're doing. They're telling them about the challenges that they should expect to see, that "Yeah, you're going to work a lot of hours. You're going to have to do a lot of work. You've got to be strong in Excel. You've got to be strong in financial modeling" and that there's no concession to that. And that's allowing the newer students to say, "Look, I'm going to put the gauntlet down. I'm going to work a little bit more. I know that the reward at the other side is significant as long as I put the work in." And that's what we're seeing at AltFinance. So as we would expect, our subsequent cohorts have been stronger than our first. Our first cohort was very strong, but the subsequent cohorts are building upon the lessons and experiences of those that went before them and we're seeing that in a very short time. We're seeing results that are going beyond even our wildest expectations. Yeah. Well, listen, it's a who-you-know world, right? And this apprenticeship program is meant—and you let me know if I'm wrong—and designed to help these young kids not only learn the world of alt investment but to have those mentors to call from time to time. I was listening to a podcast that you were saying and how a student called a mentor of his to get some advice, he called someone he knew, and all of a sudden that individual is at the top of the chain because again, it's a who-you-know world, right? For a student who may be interested in AltFinance, how would they go about applying? Do you guys choose a certain amount? Do students just come to you and y'all give them a whole bunch of money? Because I believe over $6 million in financial scholarships that you guys have helped put out. And capacity building grants for the universities themselves. So for students that want to get involved, altfinance.com is our website. Our applications are now closed for our sixth cohort, but they open up again in January. So every January we open up our applications. The only prerequisite is that you have to be a student at an HBCU. It doesn't matter what your sex is, it doesn't matter what your racial background is, but if you attend an HBCU, this is an opportunity for you to learn. Ironically, HBCUs are one of the more diverse schools in the country. In fact, there are a number of students that go to HBCUs that aren't Historically Black. The schools intentionally were designed to be inclusive and the work that we're doing is intentionally designed to be inclusive. What we believe is that there is a treasure trove of value at HBCUs, that other industries have recognized that value over time and we're helping bring that value to the world of finance. And so that really is where our mission is: how can we enhance that pipeline of talent for an industry that is suffering? Right? I mean, that industry is growing fast and needs more talent and we're bringing that talent from a community that we're very familiar with and we can identify and develop great talent at HBCUs to meet the demands of the industry. Yeah. Everyone's always saying DEI, DEI. I say, no, it's diversity of thought. That's it. That's what it is, right? Because you know, we were in meetings today here at Milken where you had partners and company who said "If you don't show us a diverse team, we're not even talking to you. We're not even giving you any money." So although that word may be a taboo today, people still want to see that diversity. Is that the sense that you're getting from major institutions? Look, I use this analogy. I love basketball, I love the NBA. The NBA's been around—But you a football player! I'm a football player, you know, I didn't have the height or the talent to play basketball. And you kicked a 50-yard field goal! We'll get into that. At Morehouse, right? We'll get into that. You know, I didn't have the skill set necessarily to play basketball, although I love it. But if you look at the history of basketball, right? A history that has been dominated over time by a bunch of different ethnic groups. Right? Irish, Italian, Jewish basketball players in the thirties and forties, Black basketball players come in and dominate in the fifties and sixties. What you see now is really interesting and when you think about diversity, this is why I say I don't focus on the DEI, I focus on the talent. Eighty so years we've had 70 MVPs, or 75 MVPs or so in the NBA. For the first 70 years, three foreign-born MVPs. Right? You've got Dirk Nowitzki, you got Steve Nash, and you got Hakeem Olajuwon. Over the past—I believe it's eight years now—every MVP in the NBA has been foreign-born. Yeah. Shai Gilgeous-Alexander from Canada, yeah. Is that because the NBA's putting this focus on DEI and they're giving out MVPs? No! It's because you have incredibly talented players that have been developed through incredible basketball systems in their home countries. In Serbia, in Canada, in Congo, in Greece, right? And thus, you are now building incredible basketball players that are playing basketball in the best conference in the world in the NBA that are coming from other countries that have authentically developed their talent because people put a focus on building them where they are. And that's what we're doing at AltFinance. We're building incredible talent at HBCUs, a community that we know is capable of building incredible talent as evidenced through other sectors, and we're doing it in the world of finance. Love it. What's it like being the CEO of a nonprofit like this in 2026? Again, you know, you hear stories about nonprofits facing a lot of issues right now. There's fundraising issues, bodies, right—they can't find more people to volunteer. What's it like for you being CEO of a nonprofit? You know, I think when you're running a nonprofit, you know, I still—I think about it like a for-profit in that, you know, maximizing shareholder value is important. But I change one keyword, right? Because our shareholders are really our stakeholders. And we have a multiple set of stakeholders. We have the folks that are funding us, we have the students that are in our program, we have the colleges and universities that are housing the students in our program, that are incredible partners of ours. So for me, it makes the job easier in some ways in that there's several different people that we can look to and we can say, "Are we doing a good job? Are we meeting your needs?" But there's also several people whose needs we cannot meet. Right? And that's what keeps me up at night, that's where we put in the hard work is I wake up every day saying, "I've got three or four different stakeholders and how can I make sure that their objectives are aligned?" And the places where they're aligned, that's where I want to dedicate my time. I'm not worried about if, you know, one person thinks we should be doing A and the other thinks we should be doing B. If they both think we should be doing C and C is consistent with our mission, that's where we want to put the focus. Yeah. Well, how do you lead? I was talking to Cathy Novak down in Miami—she's President of the Miami Grand Prix—and she doesn't like layers. She does not like layers when she leads. She likes to have direct people she can call, get the job done, but she hates all those layers in between, right? How does Marcus Shaw lead his organization? I think Cathy and Marcus are well-aligned on that. I come from a coaching background too. Coach basketball, coach football, coach soccer—anything that's competitive, I've coached and I've played. Mike Tomlin is one of my favorite coaches of all time, although I'm a Raiders fan and he was a great Steelers coach. I love a comment that he made about coaching. He said, "I like coaches that like to coach." Right? So some coaches put a ton of time going out there and trying to find the best pedigree and then they just believe that that pedigree will go and win them a championship. But I think that strong coaches and my leadership style—both for my team but also working with my partners and working with students—is based on hard coaching. I'm with you there in the tough times, but we got to get through that. And sometimes that requires us to find a little bit of grit, find a little bit of perseverance and a little bit of determination. In order to do that, we got to be willing to go through the tough times together. I'm always accessible to any of my stakeholders and I believe that that's the key to success for what we're doing at AltFinance, also the key to success for what it takes to be successful in any facet of life. Yeah. Well, you joined 2021, right? Right at inception, right? And so you've been on the job for a few years now. You can answer this one: what's been the biggest positive surprise? You know, I got a couple of compliments from both some of our financial service partners, but also from some of our college leaders. And one thing that really resonated with me was there was a lot of question "Could this work?", right? Could we find great and talented students that could be successful in this industry? And after a few years, I had a pretty esteemed president say, "Marcus, you've proven the thesis." Right? And that to me was probably the biggest positive surprise: was that other people were watching our success and were assessing the work that we were doing and were cheering for us, right? They wanted us to be successful because again, we have multiple stakeholders and everybody was aligned on what success could look like. Everybody was aligned on what they needed to contribute to that, and that they were with us on that path. And so that's been a very positive surprise despite anything that's happening in the larger macro environment. I believe that I've got a dedicated group of stakeholders that are committed to the work that we're doing to increase talent in the pipeline for the industry. Yeah. With positives, Marcus, comes negatives, right? What's been the biggest negative surprise at the job? Unless it's sleep, what's been the negative? You know what keeps me up at night—and I'll say this, I've got two young children myself. Not young children, a 20-year-old and a 16-year-old. And Jabari, you know, if you can remember back to these times, we tend to change our minds sometimes. We tend to change our minds. This is a tough industry. Everybody's not going to elect to go into this, but it doesn't mean that they shouldn't have the opportunity to learn about the industry and have access and exposure to it. And if people decide, "Look, I want to be a lawyer instead of being a private equity investor," I think that's a decision they should make. They are better off for it though, understanding the dynamic of the private equity industry so that they can apply that to their legal studies. We are better off because we now have a connection in the legal community, you know, God willing, if we ever need to have a credit amendment done for a deal, that we have people that we can go to and people that are, I believe, empathetic to the industry. You know, years ago I remember this, Wendy Kopp who started Teach For America, and that there was a young man that was in a fellowship program with me and went through the program and he was a fairly senior investment banker. And I said, "Man, all this time I thought the goal of Teach For America was just to develop a new generation of teachers." But what he said and I thought was really interesting was that that might have been the original theory of change, but it evolved to something bigger and obviously something more important, which was how can you create citizens in this country who, through their experience in education, now have a greater sense of empathy for public education and for education of students? How can you have bankers that will also sit on the school board for their kids' school district? How can you have, you know, military men and women who, when they're home, will make sure that they make it to a PTA meeting and are involved in their education? And so I believe that one of the negatives that I've seen, or what I would perceive as a negative, is actually not a negative in the grand scheme. Everybody's not going to decide to go into this industry, but it doesn't mean that their training in this industry is for naught. They will actually be a benefit to themselves and society by having a greater understanding of what it takes to be in this industry and what success looks like. Yeah, listen, I started off the intro like that, you know, those are words you said years ago and you said, "Listen, you're not going to build wealth by working a nine-to-five. You're not. Investing has to be a part of your strategy to wealth building." Right? And I guess if you were to enter a program like this, even if you don't go all the way through, you will have a great understanding of the world of private investment and how to move about that. That's right. Listen, man, you know, you go into how you lead, but let's take a step back into your background, right? Born in Mississippi but grew up in Maryland—Well, my dad's from Mississippi—my dad's from Mississippi, but you grew up in Maryland, right? In Maryland, grew up in Maryland. What was that like for you? Look, for me, Maryland, my youth was awesome. You know, I grew up in the eighties and nineties and you know, that time has some challenges in and of itself, but I grew up in Prince George's County in Maryland. That was a great hip-hop era though. It was a great hip-hop era. But you know when you grow up in the DC area, you know, there's a genre of music called go-go. Yeah, yeah. And for those outside of the area, they may not know about it, but I actually, you know, listened to a lot of that music growing up. It was a very nuanced regional music and actually when I went to Morehouse is when I really fell in love with hip-hop and with rap music and ironically a lot of other types of music. But growing up in that area in Maryland was incredibly powerful for me. I went to a great high school, Sidwell Friends, and had an opportunity to meet, you know, folks from all over the world, folks that are still incredible friends of mine. What I will say is that it is a little bit of a bubble. And this is what I think is important for young people—I think this is why things like the Milken conference are so important. That you know, growing up in that area where many of the people's parents were attorneys or civil servants and worked for the government, you don't get to see some of the private sector success and malaise that you see in the rest of the country. So when I went to Atlanta for college, when I went to North Carolina after I graduated college and went to grad school, we spent time in New York City, we spent time in Chattanooga, Tennessee, I felt that I got a broader perspective of the world to add to the perspective that I had from growing up in Maryland. And I think it's formed a pretty healthy worldview for the work that I do, but also everything I aspire to be as a father, as a husband, you know, maybe one day as a grandfather. I think that I saw that in my parents. My dad was in the Navy. You mentioned, you know, Mississippi—my dad was born in Mississippi in 1929. Not a great place. You know, he went into the military, he got a chance to see the world. And so one of the things that I think has drawn me to this world of finance is I believe for people that are coming from disparate parts of the country, that the world of finance can do a lot of the things that the military did for my father. It can take you to places, expose you to opportunities that you may not have otherwise. Right? Folks are investing in companies all over the world. When they're investing in companies, they're not doing it necessarily from behind a computer on a spreadsheet. People are going, they're making calls, they're having conversations with people in different regions and different languages to understand the business. But to understand the business, you got to understand the people. You got to understand the region. That's an incredible opportunity for young people. There's a young man in our program, a phenomenal young man—he's actually working at a consulting firm but he's, you know, thinking about what that career could look like in investing. This man was Phi Beta Kappa, didn't have a passport and never traveled outside of Tennessee or Georgia. Okay? That opportunity to move into the world of consulting, to have this exposure to finance—he just sent me a picture of his passport, he was headed down to Latin America for a little bit, a mix of business and pleasure. But he sent me that because for two years we talked about the importance of having a passport, right? And how this can change your world and this can change your worldview. And so I felt that that was incredibly important. Yeah. Well, listen, you said your dad was in the Navy, he was also—you also worked at the Library of Congress, right? And your mom was a school teacher. What'd you learn from her? You know, my mom passed away last year and you know, we talked even when she was going through some of her illness. And there's a couple of things that my mom taught me and I, you know, even as she was dealing with dementia, I kind of got a chance to ask her this. And I said, "Well, what did you want from me? You know, did you want me to be a teacher like you? Did you want me to go into the military like my dad?" She made it clear that that was not something she wanted her only son to do. But she said she never had a career that she wanted me to follow. There were three things that she really wanted me to follow in life. Number one, she wanted me to be respectful. Right? That doesn't mean that you're a sycophant, right? It doesn't mean you're kissing people's butt, right? But it does mean that you're treating every human being with a decency and respect in the same way that you would want them to treat you. It means that you're commanding the respect from them that you're treating them with. That's incredibly important to somebody who was 30 years old before integration. Right? That's a survival instinct, that you've got to demand respect but you've also got to give respect. So number one: to be respectful. You know, number two was that she wanted me to be smart. My mother's a school teacher. I spent hours and hours on end studying when I was little. I was also a pretty good athlete, so she said, "Look, if you're going to compete, you got to go out there and win, but you got to be smart, you got to study." Right? And so that created, I believe, this intellectual drive, this intellectual curiosity to understand things. It's also incredibly important to being an investor. Do you care about how things work? Right? You've got that tablet in front of you. Do you understand how it works? Do you understand how the reflective display works? Do you understand how the processor works? Do you care? No, I don't, me personally. I just want to pay for it and let it do the job. You got to care! That's what the investors will say. You got to care. And you're a journalist; it's the same thing. I think, you know, journalists and investors are very, very similar in their intellectual curiosity. They want to understand how things work, they want to understand and make sense out of the world and then share that with people. Right? Journalists want to share it with the public for free, investors want to share it with a smaller group of people for a fee. Right? That's the difference. Well, at Forbes, we're going to start charging for it. All this free information—the paywall is coming, absolutely. But I would say the last thing that she said—so number one be respectful, number two be smart, and the last thing, which was critically important especially in these times: to be tough. Yes. You got to be tough, right? Because the world is going to change and what was easy for you today is going to be difficult for you tomorrow. And you've got to have resilience and perseverance and fortitude to be able to get through those tough times. And I saw a lot of that in my parents. Life was significantly easier for me than it was for them, and significantly easier for them than it was for their parents. Right? And so those are the things that my mom taught me: the importance of being respectful, the importance of being smart, and the importance of being tough. Well, man, you're smart! Okay, I see a Morehouse College degree, right, in mathematics. I see an electrical engineering degree from Georgia Tech. And then you get your business degree from Duke. Right? So I would say you're a pretty smart brother there. Look, I've studied a lot. I've studied a lot. Well, tell me, how did you study on kicking a 50-yard field goal when you were a player at Morehouse? Let me tell you, it's one of my all-time great stories. If you've ever seen the movie Rudy, right? I like to say like I was Rudy of Morehouse College, right? In the sense that when I went to school, I thought I was good enough to play basketball. We talked about that. I wasn't a very good basketball player, but I loved basketball. I loved basketball like people love art and ballet. Who did you want to be in basketball? I wanted to be Jordan. Jordan's the default. Jordan's the default. I would say my second favorite all-time player though was Kenny Anderson. And you know, that's a deep cut for the New York guys, but you know, I was a Georgia Tech fan back in '89, '90 when Lethal Weapon 3 was doing their thing and you know—Dennis Scott, Nick Anderson, Kenny Anderson—those guys were hooping. And you know, although I was a big time Duke fan later—so you see there's a pattern here. The schools that I liked and the players that I liked, I ended up going to those schools, right? I was a big Duke fan, I love the way that Coach K coached basketball. You know, a lot of my buddies gave me a lot of grief for liking Duke back then when I was in the backyard of John Thompson and you know, the incredible Georgetown Hoyas and Len Bias, God rest his soul, right, and Maryland Terrapins. But I love the way that Duke played basketball. And so for me, that was like the way I thought about sports and I thought I could go play basketball at Morehouse College. I played soccer in high school, played for a pretty good soccer team—So that's where you got your leg! That's right, I got the leg. The leg was a soccer leg. I bet some buddies freshman year during new student orientation, which is the first two weeks of school, that I could kick a 50-yard field goal. And they did not believe it and we snuck onto the field and I kicked the field goal and we were about to get in a bunch of trouble for trespassing. And at the time, the special teams coach said, "Did you kick that field goal?" and I said, "I did sir, I apologize, I know we shouldn't have trespassed on the field." He said, "Well, I need you to come out to walk-ons at the end of the week." And the rest was history. Yeah. So you were a field goal kicker! Field goal kicker. Oh wow. Did you ever think that that could turn into field goal kicking in the NFL? Like, were you recruited? I had a strong leg. The guys that are good at field goal kicking—I went to a Ray Guy camp, a select camp that he had. Ray Guy the famous punter from the Oakland Raiders and the Los Angeles Raiders had a camp up in Suwanee, Georgia. I went out there and there were some really good kickers. I think Jeff Hall who was previously at Tennessee I think was at that camp as well. The guys that are the NFL kickers that can kick, like they don't miss in practice. Yeah, they're unreal. They're just like golfers. Like if you ever watch a practice round with Tiger or any of these guys, you know, they hit everything right. And I had a strong leg but I don't think I was dedicating the time needed to be in the NFL. And you know, look, I got the nerves to pick stocks; I don't know if I have the nerves to hit a 60-yard field goal with the time running off the clock. I mean, listen, that might be a good title for this video: "How a Field Goal Kicker Fell in Love with Finance". I like it. I like it too; I'm going to publish it. Well listen, when you think back, Marcus, and you had such a deep career, but you know, you go to IBM, right, Bank of America, you're a Wall Street trader for a very long time, Management Leadership of Tomorrow... Right, you were with that organization, and then the CEO of the business consultancy, the startup accelerator, the Company Lab. When you had to think of your career, and here you are right now, CEO of AltFinance, you think of your career journey in one word, what word would that be? It'll be purpose. Purpose. Why purpose? For me, purpose is something that roots you and motivates you to get up every morning and do what you're doing. And I think I've always been purpose-oriented. A good buddy of mine, we were recently on a text and we were talking about the difference between happiness and purpose and how people often search for happiness. They search for happiness in life, but happiness is fleeting. Today's your birthday, you get a great present, you're happy. Tomorrow you gotta go back to work and it's not such—your happiness is fleeting. But purpose is something that really can motivate us to do all the things that we do. And sometimes purpose can actually be a little torturous, right? Purpose does not always feel good. I talk about this concept around the burden of optimism, right? And that to be optimistic, to believe that you can make a better thing out of something, sometimes feels burdensome. But I relish in that burden, right? I feel that makes me feel good. Maybe it's, you know, the field goal kicker in me, that am I the guy that can win the game with the time running off the clock? And I might not be the strongest, I might not be the fastest, I might not be the biggest guy on the team, but in that moment I've got to be purposeful. Right? I look at the same work that I've done as an engineer. That was really fueled by intellectual curiosity, but can I create better network systems to help connect men and women in this country to each other? Can we evolve the technology? The work in finance: can I find better companies to invest on behalf of firefighters and school teachers and public workers that are working every day in order to put food on their tables for themselves and their family after retirement? And the work we're doing obviously now with AltFinance... Is can I create a pipeline of incredibly talented young men and young women who have an interest in operating in this world of investment but did not necessarily have the pathway to get there? And so purpose is important to me, it's something that I'm incredibly intentional about and it's what fuels me. I love that, man. I love it. Hey, but you must have been the most hated man on campus that one time. Well listen, get you out of here on some fun stuff, right? But before I do, I always like to ask this question: what's Marcus Shaw's biggest failure and what'd you learn from it? If you ask any great athlete—Michael Jordan talks about this all the time—you know, he's like "I've made X amount of game-winning free throws or game-winning shots," he's like "but I do remember the ones I've missed." You know, 1997, I missed a field goal against Kentucky State that would have won homecoming with time running off the clock. And you know, where I kind of think about that, in my senior year we had a real tough season. Here's the lesson that I take away from that: you really don't lose until you quit. So subsequently, I mean, I've been a big supporter and continue to be a huge fan of Morehouse football, all things Morehouse College... But the contributions that I continue to make both personally and professionally to that school are fueled by the fact that I'll never forget that field goal. Right? And so sometimes a little bit of failure in life, as long as it motivates greater success and greater contribution, it's worth it. Right? Had I made the field goal, I might have thought I could go to the NFL. I might have done something else with my life that was less purposeful. And so I think that—and I point this out to a lot of the young people—we got to use those failures in life as an opportunity to drive forward the future that we want to see, and that if you just kind of stew in the failure, it's of no virtue to anybody. Yeah, love that, man. I love it. Hey, but you must have been the most hated man on campus that one time. Let me tell you, it was not a fun day, man. Of course not. Not a fun day. You couldn't get a date, nothing, right? At all. It was—like I'll tell you, it was a visceral feeling, man. And even thinking about it, I got to go do something purposeful today because that feeling of, you know, you got a hundred guys out there on your team and you got one job to do. You know, it was a little bit of wind, laces might have been to the face of me or something like that, but there's no excuses, right? You got one job to do, you didn't do it, and your team paid the consequence for it. You got to suit back up the next week. You got to suit back up the next week, you got to tie your boots up, and you got to go back out there and get to work. And that's kind of the approach that I take to life, right? Sometimes you're going to get down, sometimes you're going to miss the opportunities that you should have made, but until you quit, you haven't lost the game. Yeah. Well listen, I'm glad those students now that you're helping out, I'm sure that they're glad you missed that field goal, right? 'Cause you coming back to help there, you know? But get you out of here on some fun stuff, right? Some Milken fun questions, right? If I give you 10 grand right now, what's the first thing you invest in? There's an infrastructure play out there that I would be interested in. I you know, I haven't done full due diligence, but I think that the infrastructure strategies that you hear a lot of... I think broadly in the United States that we have infrastructure that is largely depleted and antiquated and that if I look at some of the other emerging—Elaborate, what part of the infrastructure you talking? So I think there's general road and building infrastructure, I think the energy infrastructure in this country needs modernization—especially electrical grids—the electrical grid needs modernization. And I say that in that we are okay today, right? I don't think that we're headed in the direction of a third world country without the modernization, but I don't think we're maximizing the value and the opportunity of this country without it. Right? And so I think when I look at the 350 million people that are in this country... That have incredibly aspirational—like nobody else in the world, right? I think that Americans are among the most aspirational human beings to ever grace the face of the earth, that if we want to do that, we've got to invest in infrastructure. And that's human capital, that's physical capital, that's the energy and the electric grid. So I think that's a place where if I had 10,000 today, I'd do some time sharpening my pen around what type of opportunities exist in infrastructure. Yeah, I mean, listen, railroads as well, we're behind on fast—high speed rail. I don't know if we'll ever get to be a high speed rail nation the way that I think we should be. We'll get there. I hope so. It may not be in our lifetime though. Too much red tape. Listen, we just missed a field goal. Remember there's other players that are going to come. I love that. I love that, too. I love it, man. I appreciate it. Marcus Shaw superpower, what is it? I have a deep—and my father, I don't know, we didn't talk about this, you know, after his time in the military, he went and got his PhD in psychology and my mother is a teacher. I believe all teachers have a PhD in psychology—an earned PhD in psychology. I think I'm a very good read of people and I spend a lot of time both with my team but with students and my own family. Although people will say I can be a little tough, I spend a lot of time trying to understand people's motivations in life, what they want to accomplish and trying to find their superpowers. Right? And so you know, I'm a big Marvel fan, I love Marvel. If I were to think of kind of the archetype that I really enjoy, I mean it's Professor X, right? Professor X can't walk, he can't shoot lasers out of his eyes or do anything, but he has this mental telepathy to be able to not only get in the minds of the people that he's leading, but to help lead them to where they're trying to get to. He can also find them. Right? And so I think that that's my superpower. I'm not saying that I have actual mutant powers, but I do believe the ability to understand people and their motivation is important. Yeah. Favorite AI app? Spent a lot of time with Claude now, man. Yeah, I love Claude. Claude has done some incredible things in the past six months. You know, I was an early adopter, I think, on ChatGPT; Perplexity I also like a lot. But Claude—I have seen an improvement in Claude in the past honestly three months that has been incredible. I think you know, as an engineer, Moore's Law governed a lot of the development and the enhancement of technology during my lifetime. I think we're going to approach some type of Moore's Law type pathway for AI where the way we use AI today, I think in 18 months, will be exponentially different. Yeah, 'cause we're heading into that agent phase, right? This was the software phase and now you're going to have the agents that are rolling out. It's going to be really, really interesting. It's going to be powerful. But remember, and I said this at the top, it's not going to replace us. It's going to replace us if we don't use it. Biggest money management advice, right? What would you tell someone? So look, one of the best pieces of advice I've ever heard, and this was during my time working in the entrepreneurial community, was that if you ask for money you get advice, and if you ask for advice you get money. People want to be a part of your story, right? And so I'm speaking specifically to first-time managers, emerging managers that are going out there and running the gauntlet of raising capital. People want to be a part of your success. Especially, you know, those early LPs, they don't want to be transactional. They really want to understand who you are, they want to understand your motivation, they want to understand the team you're building together and your strategy. So engaging people in being a part of your story often is asking for advice. "Jabari, what do you think I should think about, you know, this concept we're thinking about AltFinance?" The more that people are investing their advice and their intellectual capital in your idea, the more likely they are to invest their financial capital. And so that's one of the things that I've seen again in some of the early-stage companies that were going out there and pitching for dollars. And, you know, look, your pitch has got to be tight, but people are more likely to invest in you and invest with you if they feel like they're a part of that process. Yeah, I love that. And ask for advice, that's a psychology thing too, right? When people feel a part of it, they give you their advice and now they want to invest in it because you know, you took in their ideas. And so it is a psychology. The reality is everybody really wants to cheer. Right? I mean, look, we're in the middle of the NBA finals, and everybody wants to really cheer for a team. People rarely like to cheer against a team. Right? And so the ability to bring people into your narrative to get them to cheer for you, right? Give them a reason to cheer. Don't miss the field goal, right? But people's psychology is built that they want to cheer, right? And what they'd rather do than cheer against you is cheer louder for who they want to win. They don't really want to cheer against you, right? It doesn't—it doesn't comport with kind of human nature in my mind. And so I think that for people that are looking to engage supporters, whether that's financial support or strategic support or something else, how do you get people to buy in and want to cheer for you? Yeah, I love that. Well, we'll get you out of here on this, man. Good to Great, right? Jim Collins, my favorite business book. What's the difference between a good apprenticeship program and a great one? In my belief, if you want to build a strong practitioner in any model, you've got to coach them. Right? You've got to coach them, you've got to train them, you've got to, you know, invest in them in a way that makes them see themselves in the position, right? So again, I talk a lot about this, but you know, Mike Tomlin. The reason why he likes coaches that like to coach is because the players that are coached will end up playing harder, longer and ultimately for less risk than those that are just really good but don't know if they want to be there. Right? So if you get people that are coached well, they'll buy into the system. So if you have an apprentice program where you're not only teaching the folks the technical aspects of doing the job, but you're teaching them how to be a leader, you're teaching them how to deal with disappointment and failure, right? You're teaching them how to come back after they miss the field goal and saying, "Listen, you can't miss a field goal like that again. If you do these things, you need to practice more." If you're running that type of an apprenticeship program, you will have a stickier, more resilient outcome, right? And that outcome is people. And that outcome for people in the investment world, particularly in the world that we're operating in, is better deals, better deal discipline, better exit discipline, better returns. Right? How to be patient in the face of adversity, right? How to be urgent in scarcity, right? How to operate in those type of modes. And so I think apprenticeship programs and training programs that are dedicated to growing the individuals that they have brought onto their team are the organizations that are going to be long-term successful. I love it, man. Marcus Shaw, CEO of AltFinance, man, thank you so much for the time. Really appreciate it, man. And listen, good luck for the rest of the work that you guys are doing. It's again, it's very, very important. 10-year commitment, 90 million, but those students that you're getting, the bringing them to the world, I can only imagine it's going to have a domino effect, right, and continue. And hopefully we come to the Milken Institute, man, and all those students, they're here. Right? And we can look at them and say, "Oh, those are Marcus Shaw's students." Man, it's about 500 of them there, you know? I love it. Managed a few trillion dollars. There you go. And none of them miss field goals. That's right. Love it. Marcus Shaw at the Enterprise Zone here at the Milken Institute in Beverly Hills. I'm Jabari Young, thank you for watching, thank you for listening.

$250M Founder Joins Naval Ravikant to Disrupt Venture Capital | ft Ankur Nagpal

Life of Luba(101.0K)·48:53·1.8K views·2026-05-07
Ankur NagpalFounder, Teachable and Carry Naval RavikantEntrepreneur and Investor LubaHost, Life of Luba
TL;DRAfter exits with Teachable and Carry, Ankur Nagpal has joined AngelList to lead USVC—a pioneering public venture fund that utilizes recent SEC approvals to allow non-accredited investors to access private startup markets with as little as $500.
The Carry Acquisition:...

TL;DR

After exits with Teachable and Carry, Ankur Nagpal has joined AngelList to lead USVC—a pioneering public venture fund that utilizes recent SEC approvals to allow non-accredited investors to access private startup markets with as little as $500.

The Shift from Founder to "Employee"

  • The Carry Acquisition: Nagpal recently sold his second company, Carry, to AngelList. While the business was successful ($4M ARR in three years), he recognized it was a "solid business" rather than a massive venture outlier.
  • Working with Naval Ravikant: Nagpal accepted a GP role at AngelList to run USVC, a project Naval Ravikant has envisioned for a decade. This marks Nagpal's first time being an employee since an internship at age 18.
  • Radical Honesty: Nagpal rejects the "false bravado" common in Silicon Valley. He attributes his success to a mix of luck and persistence rather than being "crushing it" at all times, noting that building a startup is a humbling experience where one often feels like they are "getting their ass kicked."

Disrupting Venture Capital: The USVC Model

  • Democratizing Access: Historically, the SEC’s "accredited investor" laws restricted startup investing to those with a $1M+ net worth. USVC has secured approval to allow anyone to invest in private technology companies with a $500 minimum.
  • The Goal of Public Ownership: Nagpal argues that the public narrative around tech and AI is often negative because the average person has no stake in their success. USVC aims to change this by making the "common person" a stakeholder.
  • Fund Mechanics:
      • Investment Strategy: The fund invests in direct startup stakes, other venture funds, and SPVs.
      • Liquidity: While venture capital is inherently illiquid, the fund aims to provide quarterly liquidity for up to 5% of the total fund value.
      • Net Asset Value (NAV): Shares are purchased at a current NAV (approximately $20/share), fluctuating based on the valuations of the underlying private holdings.

Investment Philosophy and Founder Evaluation

  • Three Pillars of a Great Founder:

1. Speed of Execution: High-velocity thinking and acting.

2. Horsepower: Raw intelligence and a tireless work ethic.

3. Persuasiveness: The "controversial" ability to convince investors, employees, and customers to believe in a vision.

  • The "Venture Bargain": Nagpal warns that most people should *not* raise venture capital. It requires an "irrational" commitment for 10-20 years with a low statistical probability of success. For those seeking a high quality of life, a lifestyle business is often the better path.
  • Emotional Runway: Nagpal observed that in the 2021 market downturn, more founders failed because they ran out of "emotional runway" (the will to continue) rather than actual cash in the bank.

Personal Wealth and Financial Optimizations

  • The "Happy Number": For Nagpal, the ideal net worth for lifestyle freedom is roughly $10 million—the point at which one can fly first class, support a family, and eliminate financial stress without needing to be a billionaire.
  • Portfolio Allocation: Despite his wealth, Nagpal remains aggressive, with approximately 50% of his portfolio allocated to private startups. He acknowledges this is high-risk and not standard advice for most people.
  • The Power of Tax Compounding: He emphasizes that saving $20k in taxes through smart optimization isn't just about the cash—it’s about the millions that those dollars become when invested in the market over a lifetime.
"Once you’re lucky, twice you’re good. Except I still don’t think I’m very good... running a startup just ends up being a very humbling experience." — Ankur Nagpal
"Think of venture capital as adventure capital. Do not do this with money you need in any kind of short-term basis." — Ankur Nagpal
"For almost everyone, you should not raise venture capital... You are effectively signing up to work super hard for a long period of your life with very uncertain reward." — Ankur Nagpal
Hi everyone, welcome to Flatiron New York. I'm drinking my favorite, or one of my favorites, Blue Bottle Coffee. I'm here near the AngelList office, and I'm here because I am having a conversation with Ankur Nagpal, the founder of Teachable and the founder of Carry. Carry recently sold to AngelList and Ankur became a GP at a public markets fund that is run by AngelList. Ankur and I can't wait to get into his second exit, how he's feeling, and the decision to be an employee, which is kind of wild because he's pretty much never been an employee at a company. We'll discuss his favorite books, the mindset of a founder, how he invests, and so much more. So tune in for my conversation with Ankur. I'm really excited to chat with him. Ankur, congratulations. How does it feel to have sold two companies? It feels like a little bit of a relief, to be honest. But no, building startups is something I've been fortunate to spend the last 10 years doing, and exits are always a fun part of it. But yeah, it feels pretty good. Yeah, that's amazing that, you know, you weren't like a one-startup wonder. You were a two-startup wonder. Well, that's what they say. Once you're lucky, twice you're good. Except I still don't think I'm very good. But yeah, it's rewarding to get to this place. Okay, so tell me more about this "not very good" part. Why do you say you still think you're not very good? I think running a startup just ends up being a very humbling experience. People are watching us from the outside, but as you and I talked about privately, yes, the exit feels good, but right up until that point, you feel like you're getting your ass kicked, kind of. So much of running a startup is just realizing you're not very good at a lot of different things. So yeah, I sold the business, but in my mind, I'm still like, "Man, we could have only been better, or grown faster, or done other things." My mind is still more fresh on all the things we couldn't do. So, did you feel like that when you were about to sell Teachable? Selling Teachable was interesting. Even then, our growth had slowed down. When you sell a company, typically as a founder, you're calculating the exit path. If you think you're better off taking the deal, it typically means, at some level, you've lost some level of optimism or love for the game. Even with Teachable, while the offer was very exciting, part of it was that the work ahead to get our revenue growing really fast again seemed very daunting. Exactly. Ironically, right after we sold Teachable, COVID happened and revenue grew faster than ever before. But we couldn't have predicted that. Yeah, that's very true. So, were the things that you feel like you were not good at running Carry the same as you were reminiscing on when you were running Teachable, or were they completely different? I think you just make a whole separate set of mistakes every single time. With Teachable, look, I was 24 when I started it. With Carry, I was 31 or 32 or something. You're a different person at that age and the company kind of reflects your personality. At Teachable, a lot of things worked from the outset. With Carry, because I had a relatively large audience from day one, the market signal was not the strongest in that people always said nice things no matter what. Ultimately, with Carry, I think we built a very, very good product for quite a few people, but it never crossed into the mainstream. You know, we were at four million in ARR after about three years, which is just good. It's a good business, but it's not fantastic. Yeah. So I love the fact that you're always so honest. You're like, "You know, this was a solid business." It's disproving Indian parents, you know, it just drills it into you. Which is honestly a lie; my parents could not be more supportive. But yeah, look, you know, I want to call a spade a spade. But why not, you know, put it up as like this triumph? "Oh my god, we sold Carry, we're now part of AngelList." You know, a lot of founders do that; they hype up their exit no matter what the exit is. Why are you not doing that? I think a lot of people who do that are suffering from like a deeper form of insecurity where they don't feel comfortable telling it how it is. I think I feel very good about myself and I think you get a lot of credibility by being honest. Both in personal relationships around me, but also when people listen to me or see something I write, I think those who know, know. The most legit founders are the ones who appreciate it the most, versus sort of this culture of like false bravado. Yeah, I agree with that. That always got to me in the Valley where you go to a founder and everyone's crushing it. Yeah, exactly, everyone's crushing it. Everyone raised all these rounds, and that's the most vanity metric that people talk about. Even though I think I was kind of looking up to those people when I was working before I was a startup founder myself, after I started being in those circles, I realized that building a business that works is so freaking hard. It's much harder than, let's say, raising money, even though it's a different skill. I just hated that vibe of people always hyping themselves up. I also think, by the way, as an immigrant, some of this is cultural. In a lot of countries, self-deprecating humor is a thing. But in America, Americans don't really self-deprecate that much. If you listen to British TV or British humor, everyone makes fun of themselves. They kind of intentionally talk down on themselves as a joke. America is the opposite; everyone has this sense of bravado, and we're crushing it, and things are amazing, and awesome, and incredible. Understand. Yeah, that's part of sales. That's why America is so good at sales, and why maybe... I mean, I think you're really good at sales. I think I'm still very much learning how to do the hype up in a balanced way. I think I'm okay at sales. It depends on what it is. If it's something I have conviction in, yes. But yeah, it's all part of the game. Okay, so you said that you feel really good about yourself and people who do this hype-up probably have some sort of insecurity. Did you always feel really good about yourself, or was it a learned journey of "love yourself"? I think, firstly, baseline, of course everyone has some level of insecurity. But I do think the more you actually see good results happen, the more confident you get. I also think age is a big factor. I think everyone feels more like you truly become who you are around 30, late 20s or 30, somewhere around there. In your early 20s, you pretend to like stuff because other people like it and you kind of mimic behavior. By the time you become 30, you're like, "I am who I am, you know, I can't be changed at this point," and you kind of settle into that. As a result, as you get more assured in who you are, I think it leads you to being more honest. So you never had to go through some sort of love-yourself journey, or meditating a lot, or going through any sort of... okay, just a happy guy. No, I think it's more like... maybe it's being an immigrant, but the whole self-love journey feels like a culture that I see in the US where it just doesn't exist the same way in other places. I remember even at work, someone was telling me their pet is on antidepressants, and that just seemed insane to me. I was like, "Wait, what?" Yeah, yeah, yeah, like your cat can have anxiety apparently, and I was like, "This sounds insane to me." Like I think there's probably, like we were joking, there's probably more people on anxiety—there's more pets on anxiety meds in Brooklyn than like people in India, right? It's just a different culture. Well, I think part of the problem is that we talk about it so much. So once we start talking about something so much, then people start putting labels on the things. Of course, but I think that's sort of the culture you have. You can give vocabulary to anything, and a lot of things are sort of... I'm not saying suffering is part of human nature or whatever, but you can always come up with fancy words for it. But I think part of it is just kind of getting on with it. Yeah, that makes sense. So after you sold Teachable, would you say that you were leaning into the same strengths that you figured out you had at Teachable when running Carry, or were you a completely different type of founder? When I first sold Teachable, I was very burnt out at the time. Prior to selling, I was not sleeping super well. I started getting into like, "Oh, all these people online are like, 'Oh, you need to have no alcohol, you need to have a dark room, you need to take all these supplements,'" and I was doing all of that stuff and I still wasn't sleeping great. But when I sold Teachable, I still wasn't sleeping amazing, but then I stopped running the company and I slept fantastically. Clearly, there was a baseline level of... that's the other thing in all of this health hacking and longevity hacking. I think the single most impactful thing is stress, just mentally being in a really good place. After Teachable, I was like, "I want to spend none of my time doing this again." I wanted to travel the world and take some time off, and I did that for almost two years. It was super fun and super rewarding, but I eventually started to miss the idea of building something with other people, and that's what drew me back to Carry. That ended up still being one of the most valuable parts of this. Okay, so now that you've sold Carry, you're not pissing off to go travel. You're actually becoming an employee, which is almost the first time in your adult life. I have a job. Yeah, I had an internship when I was 18 at Amazon. I interned for a summer, but since then, this is the closest—this is the only time I've worked for an organization that is not my company. Yeah, so what the heck? You know, is it like adulting, going to corporate? It's not a bad life. You come in, you get paid no matter what happens, and you're not the one writing the checks. But no, I'm kidding. The opportunity here is very interesting, and that is what drew me to this, where we get to build a public venture fund, which is an insane concept. It just combines a lot of the things I'm interested in, so it felt like a very fun thing to do. Okay, so I want to talk about what USVC actually is and how it works. But before that, at which point did you realize that, okay, Carry is a solid business but not a venture outcome? What was that gap or distance between realizing and actually acting on it? Again, I was very fortunate in that I was not looking to sell the business. However, Naval—oh, you were not? Okay. I was not looking to sell the business. I was like, "Look, this is our destiny. We're going to keep growing, keep compounding, keep figuring this out." And what was driving that? Why did you want to? Ego. Like, this is a battle that I've signed up for. We have a good team, things are working well enough, and we backed ourselves to figure it out. However, I've known Naval from AngelList for a while, and I know Jeff, who's also on the AngelList board. They reached out a couple times for me to run USVC. At every stage, I was intrigued because the opportunity was cool. They then suggested, they're like, "Hey look, if you're running Carry, maybe there's a world where we can acquire the business so you can actually..." They liked a lot of what we had built at Carry in terms of growth marketing and being able to explain complicated financial products. They're like, "Maybe we'll absorb this into AngelList and you can then kind of still do financial education." And how long were they courting you? Pretty quick. So they courted me for, I want to say, about four to six weeks before we ended up deciding to. Oh wow, so not that long. And at that point, my understanding was that they had to jump through a lot of hoops to actually set up the entity and deal with regulation, right? So they must have been dealing with it for like a year. USVC has been a project that, according to Naval, who was the original founder of AngelList, was his vision for what venture investing should be for the last 10 years. So for forever. They spent a few years getting the legal structure in place and they'd spent at least the last six months looking for the person to run it. So this has been something they've been working on for a very long time. But then when they came to me, my approach was like, "Look, this sounds intriguing, but if we can find a way to make this a good outcome for my team, for my investors, for all the company shareholders, we'll consider it." And that's sort of what kicked off the conversation. Got it, that makes a lot of sense. And so why is it so complicated to set up a public markets fund? Assume that I don't know anything about public markets funds. I also recently knew very little about it. But the biggest reason is in the US, there are accredited investors laws which say you cannot invest in a startup unless you're an accredited investor. What that means is you need to have a net worth of a million dollars, or make two hundred thousand dollars for multiple years in a row, to invest in a startup. Which to me seems a bit insane because you can invest in any crypto scheme, you can buy a lottery ticket, you can sign up on Polymarket and bet on how long Trump shakes someone's hand. You have all these insane things for people to lose money, but you can't invest in a startup. However, with USVC, AngelList was able to get SEC approval to offer venture investing to anyone with as little as five hundred dollars. This was pretty big from a legislative perspective because, historically, this wasn't allowed. So this was a bill that passed recently? This was... I don't want to misspeak, especially on the record, but this is something that we got approval for after many years of back and forth to find the right structure to enable this. Got it. Okay, so how does it work for the startup's cap table? So let's say I'm an OpenAI, or maybe I'm a much smaller startup, because my understanding is that USVC can invest in very small startups, too. How do I give that allocation to a public fund? Do you guys approach the startup? How does it work? Yeah, so right now it's basically my job as a portfolio manager to decide what it is we want to invest in. And we can invest in almost any way. We can go buy stakes in a startup directly. We can invest in funds. We can invest in SPVs, which are one-off vehicles to invest in a company. We can buy out fund investors or fund managers; we're very innovative in the structure. However, for the positions that the portfolio already has, most of them are through funds for now. But my goal with time is to start doing much more direct allocations and keep it a lot more efficient. Either way, it's us typically picking what we think to be the most important technology companies of the next 100 years and investing in them before it becomes obvious to everyone else. And so why would I as a startup want to have USVC on my cap table? That's a great question and one we're trying to find good answers to. I believe for a lot of startups right now there's this narrative amongst a lot of people, especially mainstream America, that technology is bad. AI is evil. I think part of that is because the common person has no ownership; they don't benefit from this at all. So I believe by creating a vehicle to give regular people an actual stake in the business, that can be very meaningful in changing public perception. Right, cause at the end of the day it's all about incentives. If people make money, then obviously they're going to be a lot more positive. And right now what you're seeing is in private markets, companies are staying private much longer. Apple went public at like a billion dollar market cap. Shopify, even recently—recently being 10 or 15 years ago—went public at a billion dollar market cap. But now you have these companies worth a trillion dollars and you still can't buy them. So the goal with this vehicle is to kind of give ownership, and my hypothesis is companies will want this because they can now have regular people be stakeholders in this. Yeah, that makes sense. So they are, by allowing regular people to participate, they will lift up the narrative and change the narrative. That makes a lot of sense. If I'm investing five hundred dollars, the minimum on USVC, what can I actually expect? How long is the money locked up? I know that you're making a gamble because it's private markets, but yeah, like will I make a ton of money? I have to make all the typical disclosures and disclaimers. Venture capital is an illiquid asset class. It is risk capital; think of venture capital as "adventure capital." Do not do this with money you need on any kind of short-term basis. The whole idea is that this is where a small, speculative growth part of your portfolio can live. With those disclaimers out of the way, the way it works is when you buy into the fund, you effectively get shares at what we call NAV, but it's effectively a share price. So right now the NAV is about 20 bucks, for instance. So if you spend five hundred dollars, you get whatever, 25 shares. Every time there's a change in the valuation of the underlying company, the value of the fund goes up or down. Every quarter, our goal is to provide liquidity where up to five percent of the fund is made available for anyone to sell out of. I see, so like five percent of the holdings? No, so it's five percent of the overall fund. So as an example, let's say the fund is a hundred million dollars; there'll be five million of liquidity available based on who wants it. So you can potentially liquidate much more than five percent of your holdings if no one else is liquidating. Gotcha, gotcha. And so how would you think about the percentage of your portfolio that you need to allocate to private markets? Do you think about it the same way as if someone had access to angel investments? I think it's very similar. Numbers vary by person and I legally can't give investment advice, but I tend to think for a lot of people it's small, right? It's like five percent, ten percent, whatever. For me personally, it's insanely high, like I have maybe 30 percent? Or something like that? I think right now it's about 50 percent. Oh wow. I wouldn't recommend this. But wait, why did you allocate such a big percentage of your portfolio? To me, I believe look, we all are in startups; it's such a core part of what we do. I believe I'm more bullish than the average person. I also am fortunate where I have more information than the average person. I'm also fortunate where I have multiple exits, so even the half allocated in public equities, it's enough that even if all the startups go to zero, I'll be okay. Right, that's true. So I love the fact that you shared your fund performances. How many startups have you seen now and how many startups have you invested in? I've invested in a little over a hundred, which means I've probably seen like a thousand. And then I saw that you had Spearhead one and two. So were you a part of that Spearhead program? That was your personal fund that they gave you liquidity for. That's how I started investing, with this program called Spearhead, for anyone who doesn't know, where they gave a bunch of founders a million dollars. They said, "Go pick companies and see how you do," and that's what got me started with the investing. And you did pretty well in those funds one and two. Again, it's venture capital. The difference between a very successful fund and a not very successful fund is one outlier company. Yeah, so who were your outliers? I can't reveal. You can't name... okay, you can't reveal. I can't name, I don't want to name because I have a lot of good companies and I don't want the other ones to feel bad. Makes sense, okay. So based on everything that you've seen, now that you're seeing founders, what would you say makes a great founder? Can you tell right away? So, three things that I look for in founders—and this is my personal decision-making process, everyone has their own. One, I look for someone who moves very fast. I'm a very fast thinker, fast talker, fast everything. So speed of execution matters a lot, because a lot of times it's very hard to tell what's going to be successful, so the faster you try different things, the more likely you are to find something. The second thing I look for is something I just call horsepower; this is just raw ability. It can either be intelligence, work ethic, whatever. Some people are just really, really smart and that's super important. The third thing I look for, and this is a bit controversial, is how good are you at convincing people to do things? Now, I see this as controversial because I don't think that's controversial; that's like resourcefulness or magnetism. But even if you think about, okay, all of the founders that end up going to jail, guess what? They're very good at convincing people to do stuff. Right, yeah, it's like a thin line. But running a company, you have to convince so many people all the time. You have to convince investors to give you money, you have to convince people to join you, and customers to try your product. So people that are convincing, those are sort of the three things I look for. So I was going to say, what that in turn means is if you're a founder trying to fundraise, how can you apply that? I think as a founder, being scrappy, being fast, and one of the things I found very useful—I did this myself as a founder—is being very good at sending updates. Not just to your actual investors but even prospective investors, because it'll demonstrate that you can do all these things. Yeah, for sure. So let's say someone's coming to you and they are pretty early on in their journey. How can you judge the speed of execution because you're about to put in a pre-seed check? Yep, and that's exactly why the updates help, because typically you can't really tell that much about speed of execution when you get a single data point. Maybe you can ask them what they've done in the last month, last two months, last three months. But this is where seeing someone develop over a period of time, even a couple of months, you can tell, "Wow, these people move really fast." Yeah, they're making a lot of progress. Would you say that that... but I guess I'll give you one easy negative signal. If a founder is fundraising and they ask for an intro and you want to talk to them, and they then suggest chatting in like four or five days, that's already such a bad negative signal. Cause they should be like, "Let's chat tonight, let's chat tomorrow, let's chat right now, pick up the phone." I think another thing is also desire—like how bad do you want this? Which I think is really hard to measure because again, if you're really convincing, you are going to be perceived as someone who really, really wants it. Does that count? Is that a part of your equation, or not really? It should be, because I misread this. Some of my biggest investment mistakes were in the 2021 sort of hype when everyone was raising money. I was surprised how many founders gave up the second things became hard, which clearly meant I didn't calibrate very well for how much pain they're willing to undertake. Interestingly enough, if I look at all the people who fundraised during 2021-2022, there's almost no correlation with how easy it was for them to fundraise then and if they're still good or successful or whatever. Some of the companies that had the easiest fundraising rounds, the hottest deals, are completely dead in the water. Some founders, for whom every round was a grind and they barely survived, are doing great now. Yeah, for sure. I feel like you have to endure a lot of pain to be able to endure more pain. And if things just come really easy, you've never eaten enough crap to keep going. Did a lot of companies like fold within that one year? Yeah, so when things got hard, I was surprised by how many more people ran out of emotional runway than actual cash in the bank. A ton of companies ended up just being like, "Hey, this is hard. I didn't think it would be this hard, and we're returning the money, we're done." Well, I'm a good example of that. You know, I've been out for three and a half... You? No, you embraced a lot of pain. I did, but I did eventually run out of emotional runway and other things, and I did end up returning the capital, so that definitely happens. But okay, wait, I wanted to ask something on emotional... so on emotional runway, how do you develop that aptitude for being able to endure a lot of pain and for being able to really, you know, run through the walls? Is there a way to do that? One, again, if I were to be self-reflective, I don't know if I fully am that good at it. Like, what do you mean? When things got tough, I sold my business two times now. Well, you were at Teachable for 10 years, you endured 7... okay, 7. That's a really long time. That's a really long time. Yeah. And then Carry, what, like 3 and a half. I think it's tough. I don't have the answer. I don't think I'm particularly good at it. I know some founders that are far more resilient than I am. However, I think a lot of times you're a product of the environment. Back in 2021, everyone raised money so easily they kind of forgot the gravity of how difficult it is to get a random person to give you a lot of money. I remember when I was fundraising for my first company, it took me three months before someone actually said yes. Along the way, there were people who said yes but never followed up. There were investors who basically did grocery shopping while I was pitching them, just the worst, most disrespectful experiences. But I remember a founder in 2021 would ask me like, "How do you decide which investors to take money from?" And I was like, "Whoever says yes." It's so counter to the classic advice of, "Oh, you need to like them as a person." I was like, "Dude, fundraising was tough and it sucks, so I don't care if you live in Iowa and are randomly rich for four generations. Like, sure." Wait, have you changed your opinion about that? Because I think it is still important to work with people that you genuinely like working with first. Totally, but it depends on the factor of the market, right? What are you looking for in founders right now in, again, kind of a bubble—a market that's really, really hyped up? I think... who knows if it's a bubble? It's hard to tell. And I guess that's what your public markets company is going to... Yeah, so right now with a public fund, we're not investing in anything early-stage ourselves just because the universe of exciting early-stage companies is so broad. There's no way I have time to meet with them or keep up with them, so in early-stage, we're investing in all the emerging fund managers, and they are effectively our eyes into that universe. That makes sense. And then with later-stage, it depends. Sometimes it's a case of: you know this is going to be a good company, how do you get access? That's where I think being a public fund can be helpful. Part of it is being part of AngelList; there's such an advantage in that we see all of this powerful information where we believe we can predict fund managers that are going to be good and companies that are likely to break out, and we apply that to pick. So for a person who wants to build today, would you recommend them to build a solid lifestyle business or go raise venture? For almost everyone, you should not raise venture capital. I don't think people understand the effective bargain you make when you're raising venture capital. You're implicitly saying, "I'm going to give up making any kind of personal wealth until or unless this business succeeds. I'm going to work irrationally hard for 5, 10, or 20 years. I'm going to underpay myself for this, and I'm only going to do this because I believe the company I'm building has a chance of being worth many billions, maybe tens of billions of dollars."
