For almost everyone, that's a terrible deal. That's not what you want. It's glamorized, but it's just not the reality. So almost everyone should not do it. Is there some sort of quiz or test you can do on yourself to determine whether you should do it or not? I think if what you want to do is just a very hard problem—you want to build a nuclear reactor, you want to make people live to 200, you want to do something truly very difficult that takes a lot of capital but is very rewarding—it makes a ton of sense. But I think it's worth auditing if that is the type of funding you want to take, because you are effectively signing up to work super hard for a long period of your life with very uncertain rewards. Yeah, 100%. I think obviously the fact that founding a company is so glamorized and it's so easy to raise money, everyone wants to have the accolades of raising a big round, but most people should probably not do it. Is there like one question someone can ask themselves to determine whether it's the right thing? Because, you know, there are a lot of businesses that are not a nuclear reactor that still could get... Yep, I think it's scale of ambition, right? How much do you want to build something really, really big, even if it entails a lot of downside to get there? The answer for a lot of people is that it's not that important. If what you want to do is build a cool product and have it exist, especially now, there are so many different ways of doing it. Also, just candidly, do you want to be a billionaire? In which case, yeah, you probably have to build a company worth, you know, tens of billions of dollars. But for most people who want a high quality of life, that's not necessary. Okay, so then on high quality of life, your "happy number" is 10 million, right? I think the number varies. I would say it's somewhere around there. Basically, the point at which you're not stressed about anything, you can buy a first-class flight anywhere, and just not worry about stuff. Okay, so that's your advice to someone who wants to determine their happy number: think about the things that you really care about that you would want to spend up on. Think about... for everyone it varies a lot. For me, being able to take a long flight and lie down was one of those things where I'm like, "Okay, that actually kind of matters." Being able to have a house, if you have kids, and not worry about them. The number varies. I've seen people go as low as three, four, five million, or as high as 20 or 25 million; it's somewhere in that range. It's very personal. It depends on where you live, who your friends are, a lot of different things. But for some people, that's just not enough. They're very driven by wanting to be a billionaire, one of the wealthiest people in the world, and that's a fully different personality type. Do you feel like money has changed you in any way? I think it's allowed me to lean more into who I am and do what I want to do—and not do what I don't want to do. To me, the most valuable thing with money is just having freedom and deciding, "Yeah, I actually'd rather not do that," and that's been really nice. Why are you so open about your net worth and all your financials? I mean, on the one hand, obviously it's really cool, but on the other hand, it creates, I'm sure, a lot of security risks and paranoia, maybe. Yeah, I mean, I'm open in how I think about these things. I don't share a lot of specifics intentionally, and with time we'll keep what's important from a privacy and security perspective in mind. But things like, "Okay, I allocate my portfolio this way," I think doesn't present security risk. And it's interesting information that I wish more people talked about, and I think more people are starting to talk about it too. I guess I was just referring to, you know, being on Moneywise; you shared about your expenses and costs, and that is a lot more detail than people usually share. Of course, those are details that you can deduce if someone is a public persona, but still, were you scared of sharing so much information? I wasn't scared. On Moneywise, I basically was talking more about the spending side of things and I've been pretty open about that. I tend to think that more people should share that. The security side, I think, is real, but the damage there happens well before. The damage there happens when people realize you sold a business and things are harder to control. The other thing that is just the inevitability of being in media is any time you do an interview, the interviewer or the social media people or the graphic people, whoever writes the headlines, focuses a lot on it. A lot of times it's even out of my direct control. How did you feel when you got the money in your bank after selling Teachable? At this point, it was a very long time ago, but I remember it was a very strange time because it was April 2020. Full lockdown. Like, I was in New York, I was in my apartment, I was living alone at the time, people were wiping down packages, everyone was washing their groceries outside. It was very... you know, you have all this vision that you're going to sell a business, you're going to have a massive acquisition party, and it's going to be a blast. But in reality, I was locked in my studio. I eventually bought a car so I could drive to Rockaway Beach and go surf, because that was one of the only activities you could do at the time, and that felt nice. But yeah, nothing major changed or felt very different. The thing that really did feel super different was the day I stopped doing my job. The day I stopped being CEO, that improved the quality of my life. So that was a very clear example of how money creates freedom and opportunity for freedom. Exactly, yeah. For someone who, say, makes 200 to 500k and still very much feels like they're on a treadmill, what would be one financial decision they need to make that would really change life for them? I think at that point it's lifestyle; it's where you live and who you hang out with. That ends up being such a big concern because a lot of people try and keep up with the people they're around, so just potentially changing where you live, unless that place is suiting you quite a bit. As an example, I've talked a lot about how I think people should never move just to save money on taxes, but at the same time, you should audit what city really resonates with who you are. Beyond that, the personal finance nerd in me will be like: make sure you're paying off all your tax-advantaged accounts, be smart about taxes. Realistically, that will save tens of thousands every year. But you're not just saving tens of thousands, because when you save that, it means more dollars are getting invested, which means more dollars are growing at five to ten percent a year. The impact of those thousands of dollars a year is actually millions of dollars over your lifetime. What people don't realize is when you do something and it saves you 20k in taxes, people are like, "Oh cool, I saved twenty thousand dollars," but in reality, you've saved hundreds of thousands or millions because that 20k is hopefully being invested in the market. Every saving is not just a saving it gives you today, but it is what it compounds to over the course of your life. Yeah, that makes sense. And I know, I believe on one of the podcasts you mentioned that for founders, you don't really need to care about all of these ultra-optimizations because you just want to work hard and have a big exit. Do you think that they should do any optimization? So, this is why being a venture-backed startup founder is so difficult: your net worth is 99 percent determined by how successful your company is. It's actually sure, you can do a little bit of optimization, but it's not worth optimizing anything if it distracts you from the main thing, because your main job is to make your company successful. As a founder, sure, do the basics—don't go into debt, don't do stupid stuff—but focus your energy on actually increasing the likelihood that you will be successful with your company. For everyone else who isn't a venture-backed founder, I think all of this applies. How would you factor this math in with the fact that most startups do fail? It goes back to: most people should not be a venture-backed founder. It is a bad expected value trade. In the outlier case, you end up doing very well, but the average person who ends up starting a company will not do very well, which is why it's one of those "only do it if you're sure." It's also why, ironically, sometimes being young and kind of dumb is helpful. When you're young, your earning years don't feel as precious. Imagine you're 37 years old, you're making four or five hundred thousand dollars, and you have two kids. For you to start a company now is incredibly expensive because you're stressed. "What if this doesn't work out? What am I doing for my family?" all of that. Got it. How did you figure out what your zone of genius was? One, it's always evolving. But I don't know, I thought back to like being a kid. What was I good at in school? What was I not good at in school? I always found my superpower was being able to do a little bit of everything. Do you have ADHD? Probably. Again, I grew up outside America, we never looked at that, we never diagnosed it, but yeah, I'm sure I have a little bit of ADHD. But yeah, I have a computer science degree but I was never the most technical person. I've always been a marketer, but I don't have the traditional sort of brand background. It is combining those universes that I always felt to be my strength, which is why being a founder was good—it's an interesting cross-section of a little bit of everything. And would you say you were the same type of founder at Carry that you were at Teachable? Like, were you very much leaning into the same strengths? You change and you evolve. I think at Teachable, I had no idea what I was doing. At Carry, I think I knew much more of what I was doing, but the market is the market and the product is the product. I think I evolved a little bit, but also while we were building Carry, the whole AI thing happened, and you also have to evolve as everything keeps going on. What business would you start today? I want to buy a sports team. Oh cool. Enough, enough, enough. A cricket team? Yeah, I bought a small stake in a cricket team. Maybe eventually I'll buy a larger stake. But I think everyone is getting into AI and building all these businesses; I think it'd be really fun to lean into something with real-world experiences. Yeah, owning a sports team is pretty much... well, actually, when I was a kid I wanted to play on the sports team, but that ship has sadly sailed, so maybe we buy the whole team now. And what do you think your life is going to be like when you buy a team? Like, you just go to a bunch of... I'm going to be the most annoying owner ever. You'll be at the practices, you'll be like coach-side coaching. I remember there was this article about this European billionaire rich dude or whatever. He bought a soccer team, maybe this was in Norway or something. The first thing he did is he put himself in the team. He's old, not as athletic, and all the comments were like, "That's pathetic, that's so sad," and I'm like, "That's awesome, this is incredible!" He's living his childhood dream. I was like, I would totally do that. So that was great. But I bet I would still be the annoying owner where, you know, a lot of owners typically stick to the business decisions, but I would be the one in the draft deciding who we pick and who we bench. And also on the bench as a replacement, potentially. What are you worried about in this chapter as an employee? Do you think you'll be able to stay for a long time? Be focused? Yeah, I think this is a bit atypical than being an employee because I have a very clear mandate to build something. It's enjoyable. To be honest, for now, it's kind of nice to not have a large team and stuff and be in a very specific role. As of now, I think it's a really, really fun product, so I don't have any bigger concerns over there. The question is, in three to five years, will I be like, "Oh man, I miss building a company again?" I hope not. Every time I tell myself never again, but we'll see. Wait, why are you being pulled by building so much if there's so much pain in it? Maybe it's the ADHD, I don't know. I also think the brain is interesting; the way nostalgia works, your brain only focuses on the positives. You forget all the pain. I have a lot of friends who alternate between building a company, having five or seven years doing it, coming out of it being like "never again," then spending three years being like "my life has no meaning, my life has no purpose," and then jumping back into it. But we'll see. Yeah, that's totally fair. We'll be watching what happens. Okay, I want to talk about like AI and personal life. Are you using AI to run any of your life right now? And what are you doing? What are you using? Not a whole lot. I use it for business-adjacent things. Like as an example, I think I was telling you about this, but I use it to run my LinkedIn; I use it to run my Threads. So it's business-adjacent—it is still my personal social media, but there's still some business-type end goal for it. Otherwise, personal life is very, very limited. Small things, like I've had a list of every book I've read since 2016, and now I'll have AI go in and create smart recommendations. Oh, that's cool. And you actually follow the recommendations and they're good? Yeah, they're pretty good, because my spreadsheet has got great information. It's got the book name, title, and my rating, and the rating is very helpful for it to calibrate what I'm likely to enjoy reading. What are the three books that changed your life? Oh, this is a tough one off the top of my head. One, when I was 13 or 14, I read a book called Losing My Virginity. It was Richard Branson's autobiography. Yes, yes, yes, it's really great. I remember that was the first time I was like, "Man, it'd be nice to build a company," because he looks like someone that's just having a blast, right? It was less about optimization and it was just like kind of doing it for the love of the game. Okay, I'm just going to pause here. I love Richard Branson. I also read... it didn't ring a bell immediately, but I read Losing My Virginity and another book of his which I totally am not remembering. I was like, "Wow, he's the type of entrepreneur that I want to look up to and I want to be," because he seems so balanced in the way that he had a family and multiple companies. As you said, he had so much fun. I think we need more examples like that, who are like, "I'm just having a blast, I'm playing a game, I'm a kid on the playground." Do you feel like company building has to be pain and suffering, or can you do this through love and joy? I think the only way to make it sustainable is to do it in a way... you look like you're having fun. My personal hot take... Look like having fun or actually be having fun? Well, actually having fun. But looking like you're having fun is important. I think a lot of times startup founders get bad raps. I was actually looking at this, I was like, "Why are some startup billionaire founders well-liked and why are others disliked?" How much you look like you're having fun, I think, is how people judge how likable you are. That's my hot take. Think about it: there are a lot of Silicon Valley rich people that are honestly disliked. But the people that are liked, like rappers and celebrities and even Richard Branson is a great example—he's a bit silly. Yeah, they're silly, they're goofballs, they're always just having a good time. Even Larry Ellison is a great example. Who are your role models when it comes to entrepreneurs? Like, whom did you want to imitate? It's tough because I don't have like the traditional, "Here is my idol." I think everyone sort of has their pros and cons. I will say a different but related question: the person I'd most like to be friends with is The Weeknd. Oh, that's interesting. Why? I became the biggest Weeknd fanboy back in 2015 and it's something... I just put this out there, hopefully one day someone will help connect us. Okay, The Weeknd, if you're listening, Ankur here wants to connect with you. Okay, let's move on to book number two. This isn't even a... I'm trying... it's not even about a specific book, but I remember... so I read a lot growing up, and then there were dark years of four or five years where I didn't really read much at all. While you were building? No, not even. It was from college to my mid-20s, I barely read a book. I just went through a period where I fell out of love with reading. But then, I don't remember which book I picked up first, but I started with a Murakami stage, then I slowly went to every other Japanese fiction author. That was just an important time because it reminded me how to love reading again in a way where you're reading for the joy of it, and not because someone on Twitter said "this business book you have to read." Basically since then, for the last 10 years, reading has become an activity I do almost every night. It's almost always fiction, just for enjoyment, not trying to optimize anything. What's in it for you? Why are you reading? Are you transported into a different world? Yeah, transported to a different world. It helps reset. Imagine you're working all day; how do you switch that off? Reading is the perfect off-switch for me. I just feel like it's the closest you have to being able to experience what it's like to live multiple lives. Yeah, I was recently talking to my mom about national identity and she doesn't have a strong sense of national identity. I never grew up with my mom being like, "I'm proud to be Belarusian." And I was like, "Yeah, why is that?" And she's like, "Well, I loved reading as a kid, and every time I read something about Spain, I was like a ballerina in Spain, or if I read something about France, I was a French person." Fascinating. It's also like different books for different time periods in your life. I remember I read a lot of Bukowski after a breakup and it was very helpful at the time. Yeah, it just transports you to a different place. Okay, what's your advice for someone who's still looking for their thing? Try a lot of stuff. Honestly, the more you can maximize your surface area, the better this is going to be. How do you maximize your surface area? Live in a big city, meet a lot of people, try a lot of different things. Just the more collisions you can possibly have, the more likely you are to get lucky. Great advice. Ankur, thank you so much for your time. Yeah, thanks for having me. This was fun. Awesome.

Is Marco Rubio the FUTURE President of the United States I EP709

Sean Spicer(241.0K)·46:02·2.4K views·2026-05-07
Sean SpicerHost Marco RubioUS Senator
TL;DRMarco Rubio is emerging as a top-tier presidential prospect following a viral White House briefing performance, while J.D.
Vance strategically builds groundwork in Iowa and shifts focus toward aggressive federal fraud investigations. The "Acting" Press Secretary: Rubio’s...

TL;DR

Marco Rubio is emerging as a top-tier presidential prospect following a viral White House briefing performance, while J.D. Vance strategically builds groundwork in Iowa and shifts focus toward aggressive federal fraud investigations.

Marco Rubio’s Rising Political Stock

  • The "Acting" Press Secretary: Rubio’s recent performance at the White House podium, where he handled reporters with charm and fluency in both English and Spanish, has sparked speculation about a future presidential run.
      • "Happy Warrior" Persona: Erika Donalds described Rubio as the "pride of Florida," noting his ability to handle multiple roles (Secretary of State, NSC Advisor, and Press Secretary) while remaining relatable and humorous.
  • The American Dream Viral Moment: Rubio received significant praise for a patriotic response regarding the U.S. being a story of "perpetual improvement" and a place where birth circumstances do not limit potential.
  • The Florida "All-Star" Pipeline: Panelists noted that Florida is currently the center of Republican talent, producing leaders like Trump, DeSantis, Rubio, and Byron Donalds.

J.D. Vance: Strategic Groundwork and Federal Oversight

  • Iowa Engagement: Vance has been quietly meeting with influential Iowa leaders, including evangelical leader Bob Vander Plaats and State Party Chair Jeff Kaufmann, signaling a "slow and steady" long-term political strategy.
  • Aggressive Fraud Prevention: Vance is leading a push to treat taxpayer dollars with "respect" by targeting federal fraud.
      • The Entitlement Debate: Adam Guillette argued that while fraud is a "drop in the bucket," the administration must eventually tackle entitlement reform to address the national debt, which he called the greatest threat to national security.
  • Transparency Initiatives: Erika Donalds highlighted efforts by Byron Donalds to make Florida the most financially transparent government by putting all local and state contracts and spending data in a searchable online format.

Geopolitical Tension: Iran and the Strait of Hormuz

  • Strategic Blockade: Rubio asserted that if Iran attempts to close the Strait of Hormuz to international shipping while allowing their own ships through, the U.S. must respond with a defensive blockade of Iranian vessels.
  • "Might vs. Might": Sean Spicer argued that Iran only respects strength and that the current "stalemate" requires a return to more aggressive military deterrence rather than diplomatic "exchanging of paper."
  • Weakened Proxies: Adam Guillette noted that Iran’s regional position is at a historic low following the degradation of Hezbollah and Hamas, and the fall of Al-Assad in Syria.

Domestic Politics and the Culture War

  • Indiana Primary as a Referendum: The ousting of several Indiana state senators who opposed Trump-backed redistricting and legislative priorities was cited as proof of Trump’s continued "grip" on the GOP.
  • DEI and the New York Times: The panel discussed a recent EEOC lawsuit against the *New York Times* alleging discrimination against a white male staffer in favor of a less experienced candidate to satisfy diversity quotas.
      • Meritocracy: Erika Donalds criticized "identity politics" in both corporate hiring and redistricting, arguing that voters and employees should be judged on merit rather than skin color.
  • Judicial Concerns: The panel expressed outrage over a D.C. judge apologizing to an alleged would-be assassin (Cole Allen) for jail conditions, citing it as an example of a "radicalized" judicial world view.

Notable Quotes

"Our history is not one of perfection, but it's still better than anybody else's history... It is a story of perpetual improvement." — Marco Rubio
"It’s actually the most racist thing to say, 'You can only represent people that match your skin color.' We’ve got to get away from that level of identity politics and focus on issues." — Erika Donalds
"Anytime I hear a Republican elected official walking around talking about waste, fraud, and abuse, my eyes roll... The only way to balance our budget is serious entitlement reform." — Adam Guillette
"Marco Rubio was charming the pants off of the reporters... I’ve actually never seen them be so warm to a Republican in this type of setting ever." — Stephanie Hamill
Marco Rubio and J.D. Vance both have big weeks. What's the latest on Iran? The panel's going to weigh in on it all. Welcome to the Sean Spicer Show. Let's get into it. All right, guys, it is Thursday, one of my favorite days of the week because we get to break it all down with the panel, have some fun, and I actually have a really fun winner and loser this week, which I hope that no one takes because that's why I always go last, but you never know. Anyway, some really great news to start the show. I was so excited last night. I got a text and an email around 10 o'clock which generally I'm in bed, so I was like, what's going on? And it is now official: Trump 2.0 has made the New York Times bestseller list. Thank you to everybody who's made this possible. I appreciate all the support, all the purchases, all the reviews on Amazon. Making the bestseller list is tough. Making the New York Times bestseller list when your book has Trump's face on the cover is an entirely new feat of being. Even the publisher said to me, "You do know that you put Donald Trump's face on this book? It's going to make it very difficult for the New York Times to put it on the bestseller list," but sure enough, they even got that. I had a great time at the Heritage Foundation yesterday talking about Trump 2.0 and the work up and the lead up. Take a quick look at that. On MAHA, for example, we're making tremendous strides. We're making America healthier, more conscious of what they're putting in their body. I use myself as the prime example on this, right? So, I've yo-yoed; I've been in better shape and not as good shape, right? When you go see a nutritionist, they used to be like, "Okay, so we're going to put a daily calorie intake together for you. This is what you need to lose some weight and, okay, and by the way, so maybe here are your snacks. So we're going to give you this fruit bar or something like that." It's always a disappointing list to me. Of course it is. It's like, I don't like that, I don't like that, that tastes horrible. But, and even the second that I like something, it's always like, "Yeah, that's not really that good for you." But so I'd be like, "Okay, well here's this bar, protein bar or something, and it's only got 100 calories, great." What Bobby Kennedy got us to do is to say, "Flip that bar over the back and how many of those, how many of those ingredients can you pronounce?" All right, you get the gist, but we had a great conversation there about MAHA and so much of the other stuff. A big reminder of the Trump Kennedy Center event that I'm doing in Washington D.C. this March 15th. Tickets are absolutely free. One of the last times, even if you don't want to see me and you just want to see the Trump Kennedy Center before it closes for a couple years, go to SeanSpicer.com. You'll see a picture of the book there. Click on it, get your free tickets. We're doing a VIP reception on the roof afterwards. I'm scared of heights and I'm even excited. By the way, tomorrow, a great show with Jan Jekielek on this best-selling book that he has on China and body parts. It sounds disgusting, but it's unbelievable what's going on and I'm excited to have that conversation with him. You're not going to want to miss it. He's over at the Epoch Times doing amazing work, and I'm really excited about that. Before I bring in the panel, I gotta tell you, after I got my great news about the book, I had already taken my Beam Dream Powder. I had a great night's sleep knowing that I had some great news, but this is what I do every single night. I literally take one scoop of the Beam Dream Powder, put it in some water, put it in the microwave for 30 seconds and boom, I'm out like a light. I wake up feeling refreshed, ready to go. I track my fitness, by the way. I got an Oura ring, a Fitbit, I got all this stuff, or a Whoop. I always wake up looking at my sleep score, and because of Beam's Dream Powder, boom, it says I'm ready to go, I had a great night's sleep. All five natural ingredients: Reishi, Apigenin, L-Theanine, Magnesium, Melatonin. I love this stuff. I get asked all the time, "Do you actually take it?" Of course I do, every night. And you can too, because Beam right now has a 40% off offer. I don't know when it'll end, trust me it will, so go to ShopBeam.com/Spicer, use code Spicer for your best discount that you've ever gotten on Beam. You will love this stuff. ShopBeam.com/Spicer, code word Spicer for 40% off. And then by the way, I'm so excited to show this off. I've talked a lot about how much I love Patriot Mobile and the products and the service, and guess what? I now have—this is a hotspot that I travel with. This is my Patriot Mobile. It really looks pretty cool, like America love, America patriotic. So whenever I travel now, I've actually got a Patriot Mobile hotspot that I travel with. So you got great wireless service and it's on all those great networks: AT&T, Verizon, T-Mobile. But the cool part is they're an activist company. And oh by the way, I forgot to even tell you, when I set up my Patriot hotspot, I call, a dude answers in America! They're in Texas and in California; they speak English. You gotta love that. You don't have to like sit there and be like, "No, this is what I mean." Patriot Mobile, 100% US, great service, but they're an activist company that gives back to pro-life, pro-veteran, free speech causes. That's why I really love them. You're getting the best of everything, you feel comfortable in every dollar you spend. So you can go to PatriotMobile.com/Spicer or give them a call at 972-PATRIOT. But if you go to PatriotMobile.com/Spicer or give them a call at 972-PATRIOT, just say Sean Spicer sent you and you get one free month. So now I'm actually saving you money in this economy. Hello? Thank you very much. PatriotMobile.com/Spicer. Without further ado, I want to bring in today's amazing panel: Commentator Stephanie Hamill, the future first lady of Florida Erika Donalds, and Adam Guillette of Accuracy in Media. Guys, good to see you all. Erika, I'm going to start with you. I don't know what's up with you guys in Florida. You all have to have like 18 jobs. So Marco Rubio, Secretary of State, National Archivist, National Security Advisor, DJ, and now White House Press Secretary. Take a look. All right, can I—there's no way I can figure out who to call on. I'm just going to like press right in the middle, right there. You, right there. Yeah, in the back row. Thank you, thank you Mr. President. I'll go to you next, I can't get everyone at once. All right, go ahead. Thank you, Secretary Rubio. Going right, welcome to the White House. What's that? Are you talking to me sir? Yeah, to you. Yes sir. Thank you. I have two questions on two separate issues that come under your umbrella. Do they get two questions for these? At that tempo? Two questions, there's a lot of people in here. That didn't hit under your umbrella. All right, you answer, you can ask me two questions, I'll give you one answer. Thank you very much. All right. I mean look, I gotta be honest with you folks. Erika, I was a little jealous. I'm like, how come I didn't start like this? But Marco Rubio is really, I mean he's getting accolades all over the place. He's DJing, he's press conferencing, he's flying to meet the Pope. I mean what's the vibe in Florida? Are you guys super excited? Absolutely. Marco Rubio is the pride of Florida. He's doing an incredible job as the best Secretary of State in history. And now he's in on the joke. I mean it's our favorite joke about this White House that Marco Rubio is doing so many different jobs, our favorite meme. And he truly is a great Press Secretary. He's so knowledgeable, he gets to showcase, you know, he was Speaker of the House in Florida before he went to the United States Senate. He's multi-talented, he's so impressive. You know, that's why a lot of people are saying he might have a run at the next presidency. But if not, he could take your old job, Sean. I don't know that he'd want that one. Adam, I was unbelievably impressed with—the media just ate it all up. I mean he was, I was like what's going on? How did this happen? I'm like, they hated me. They came after me like spider monkeys. And here's Marco Rubio charming the pants off of them and they're like, "Haha, great one Mr. Secretary!" Like I was like, what did I do wrong? Adam, I mean I just think that we gotta be careful that we don't believe that they actually like any of us. They'll rent us for a moment, but they don't really like him. He just, I think, played the job well temporarily. Yeah, that's exactly it. It's kind of like substitute teacher day. You're not taking it as seriously, you're not going to go as hard on them, maybe if you're lucky you get to watch a movie instead. But to her point, you know, I'm a Florida evangelist. I think ours is the greatest state in American history. And there's so much talk about this being China's century. This is Florida's century. We're putting out all-stars left and right, like DeSantis and Rubio. I worked with him when he was a House Speaker, and he was a rock star then, putting forth great policies. He's got a great future in front of him, he's incredibly charming, and it was exciting to see him on display and it's exciting to see the rest of the country get to appreciate the talent that we've been seeing for some time in the Sunshine State. Stephanie, I mean he definitely—his stock is rising. I mean Erika mentioned that there's a lot of people now that are saying maybe this guy should run for president too. He's made it very clear he's deferring to J.D. Vance, but I mean I get asked this, like what do you think? And I hope they run as a ticket to be blunt, but I get a lot of questions about what his future looks like. I think you're spot on, Sean, and I would love to see him run. And we keep seeing the headlines, we keep seeing the chatter on social media that Marco Rubio is a presidential favorite in the future. And not only that, but as in regards to this press conference, they were saying that this was sort of like a presidential tryout. And Marco Rubio has impressed so many people, especially a lot of us who were skeptical of him. He's coming off as clean, polished, confident, intelligent, and he has this way of being like serious but relatable. And as you pointed out, he was charming the pants off of the reporters in the newsroom. I've actually never seen them be so warm to a Republican in this type of setting ever. I mean they were eating it up and it was incredible to watch. And there were so many, you know, just kind of interesting moments with Rubio where he was able to joke about his DJ name. He said, "you guys aren't ready for that." They were talking about some video that was going viral on social media of him DJing at like a family wedding or something. And then another reporter asked him a question in Spanish and he was able to break into Spanish fluently. So there are very few people that can do what Rubio does, and this is just generating more memes, right? The memes of him wearing every single hat, now he's in the Press Secretary dress. It is interesting, and I wanted to show you people, they took this video. So he gets asked this question by Kelly Wright, used to be with Fox, now with CBN. And I'm going to be honest with you because I don't, like I like Kelly as a person and I'm a big fan of CBN. But Kelly asked a question about what he thought of America, right? And I was like, oh my god, this is a throwaway question. Okay. Here's the question, here's the answer, just so everyone sees it. Take a look. I hope for America's what it's always been. I think it's the hope I hope we all share. We want it to continue to be the place where anyone from anywhere can achieve anything, where you're not limited by the circumstances of your birth, by the color of your skin, by your ethnicity, but frankly, it's a place where you are able to overcome challenges and achieve your full potential. I think that should be the goal of every country in the world, frankly. But I think in the US, we're not perfect, our history is not one of perfection, but it's still better than anybody else's history. And ours is a story of perpetual improvement. Each generation has left the next generation of Americans freer, more prosperous, safer, and that is our goal as well. But it is a unique and exceptional country, and as we come upon this 250-year anniversary, I think we have a lot to learn and be proud of in our history. It is one of perpetual and continuous improvement, where each generation has done its part to bring us closer to fulfilling the vision that the founders of this country had upon its founding. So, thank you very much. So Erika, he took that answer. And like I said, I don't want to BS anybody, when Kelly Wright, who is a, like I said, he's a friend, I like him, he asked the question, "What do you think, what's your message about America?" And I thought, "Oh my god, like you got the Secretary of State / National Security Advisor / Archivist / DJ / Defensive Coordinator for the Baltimore Ravens," I mean like, and that—and I thought, okay, that clip has now gone viral. Like, they've now changed it, put some music behind it. I don't want to play that because I'll get copyright violations, but like that—that has become—like people are sending that all over and saying this is what America's about, like it's inspiring, it's patriotic. I mean Marco took what should have just been a normal question and been like, "I love the country," and now everybody's like watching this clip and people who probably aren't political are like, "Wow, who is this Marco Rubio guy?" He blew it out of the water, and it's because he knows what achieving the American dream is all about. His family came from Cuba with nothing and was able to work hard, achieve the American dream, which is to allow your children to have it better than you did. And look at him, you know, the son of Cuban immigrants, the son of working-class people who's now not just gone through the United States Senate, but now serving as Secretary of State and truly the embodiment of a happy warrior, which is what we all want, right? Whether you're left or right, Democrat or Republican, you want people who are fighting for the good of this country and for the good of its people, for the good of our future, and he just embodies that in such an amazing way that truly is inspiring. It has to be to anyone, no matter whether you're a Republican or not. I was like I said, he hit this thing out of the park. And now he's off meeting with the Pope. But it's you know, while Marco's stock is going up, I think J.D. Vance is doing the slow and steady to win the race part of this. And I mean that by he was in Iowa, he talked to a bunch of the key leaders in Iowa, which again, is going to be an early state on the Republican primary, right? So you had he met with Bob Vander Plaats, a huge evangelical leader in Iowa and frankly nationally as well, but like huge player there. He met with Steve Deace, who's a talk show host but very influential in Iowa. He met with Jeff Kaufmann, the party chair, longtime Republican party chair, doing the smart things. And then here's the thing, Adam: he's been doing a lot on fraud. Take a look. Whatever your views on a specific policy, there is maybe no more important issue than how about when you pay your taxes to the federal government. We just had tax day just a couple of weeks ago. How about you have a government that actually takes those resources as what they are: your sweat, your tears, your time away from your family, and protects every single of those dollars. And that's what we're doing by going after fraud finally in the federal government. Adam, I can't tell you how long I've been waiting to hear that message. I don't mind paying taxes, I mean when I say I don't mind, I love this country and it costs money to be a part of—to have all of the great things that we have here from the roads and bridges to our military, I get that. But I don't want to pay a penny more than I have to. And when J.D. Vance said that, that was the respect that I want to hear from government officials as a taxpayer. I was like, "Boom." And as I said, like Marco's doing his stuff, I thought—but he also J.D.'s doing the slow and steady to win the race, which is go out, meet with the key leaders, talk about issues. I feel like, you know, he got a little overshadowed this week, but he also had a great week. Well, I'll be honest with you. Anytime I hear a Republican elected official walking around talking about waste, fraud and abuse, my eyes roll and my stomach churns, because there is absolutely massive fraud in any big government program, unquestionably. But those are merely a drop in the bucket compared to the actual entitlement programs on their own. The only way to balance our budget, the only way to pay down our ridiculous national debt, is serious entitlement reform. It's easy to run around claiming, "Well, if we just get rid of the fraud, we're going to be fine." No, we're not. The fraud is absolutely terrible, but the entitlement programs will bankrupt us regardless. The greatest threat to our national security is our national debt, and we will not get there by, you know, offering platitudes that we're going to catch more fraud in Minnesota. We're going to get there by actually reining in entitlements. Stephanie, Adam's got a point, right? And I agree with him wholeheartedly. I mean that's the real, that's where the real money is. But the reality is that I also feel like if you don't respect the small stuff, you'll never get to the big stuff, right? Sarah, my old colleague, Sarah Huckabee Sanders, was telling me this story that when she became Governor of Arkansas, she realized there were all these fax lines in state government. Like throughout Arkansas they had fax numbers. Now I can't even tell you the last time I saw a fax machine, never mind faxed something, never mind like anyone faxed anything. And yet you think about—let's say you had 500 fax lines at $20 a pop a month—I mean that starts to add up. But while I agree with Adam, I also feel that we should have a more and deeper respect of every dollar that's spent and not just dismiss it and say, "Oh well that's only $20,000, and that's only $200,000, and that's only a million." Like to me, okay, great. I think every dollar should be treated with respect that a taxpayer works for and sends to, whether it's Washington or Tallahassee in the case of the Donalds. I think every Republican that I know does care about the dollars—like the little dollars, the big dollars. It's our party that talks about fraud, the excessive amount of taxes that we pay. It's never the Democrats who talk about this. They will literally tax you for every single penny that is in your account if they could without giving you any reason of where the money is going. And we've seen this play out with a lot of the fraud that was going on in Minnesota, some of the fraud we're learning about in California. And the Democrats' reaction to it is just insane, right? They're like, "actually we should punish those who are investigating fraud at these levels, there's nothing to see here." It's like they're angry at the people who are looking into it versus angry at the fraud that is in the hundreds of millions and billions of dollars in some situations. Erika, do you hear that when you and your husband Byron are out on the campaign trail in Florida? Does that come up as an issue? Do people say, "I don't like spending money, I don't like Tallahassee, the state capital there, spending money that they don't have when we know that there's a lot of inefficiencies and waste." Is that or is it just a pet peeve of some of us? Yeah, absolutely. And actually, Byron just announced this week making Florida the most transparent government in the country by putting all of the financial information from local government to state government online, making it searchable, making all of the contracts, which are already public record, but you have to request it and wait a month or two or more for them to get back to you. Why not put all of that and make it available—full transparency for the taxpayers who are the ones who are digging through these things and asking the right questions? Actually though, it's not just Republicans that care about fraud. If you're looking at polling data, especially in California, although Democrats and especially independents want government services, they want those taxes to benefit the disadvantaged, they don't want that money wasted, because they do want it to go to the purposes that they are voting for—that they think they are voting for. And so this is really a bipartisan issue and I think it's very smart on J.D.'s part to lean into this and make it an issue that he is likely to run on. You know, one of the other things that Marco was asked when he was in the briefing room was about what's going on in Iran. And I want to just play for you a second because I am truly perplexed where we go now in Iran. Like I feel like they're trying to get a deal, they understand the domestic politics, the blockade is kind of working, but Iran doesn't seem to want to capitulate. This is what Marco had to say about the blockade in the briefing room. These guys have shut down the straits of Hormuz. This is what Iran is saying: we will shut down the straits, no one can go through, no country in the world can go through unless we allow you to go through and you have to pay us. But our ships can go through, meaning the Iranian ships, as much as they want. That's crazy, who would agree to that? So how are we going to have a situation where they get to close the straits to everybody and the only people who are allowed to go through there 100% without paying anything are the Iranians? If you do that, they'll keep the straits closed forever. They're trying to make this some new normal. Okay, under no circumstances can we ever allow them to normalize the fact that they get to blow up commercial ships and put mines in the water. So the response to that is, we're going to blockade your ships. If everyone's ships are not getting out, your ships are not getting out either. That's not an act of war, that's a defensive measure. It's a counter to what they have decided to do. You know what is an act of war? Putting mines in the water. Why don't the members of Congress or whoever it is is complaining about it—they should be all over that. These guys put mines in the water. That alone, you mining in the water is illegal, period, under any circumstances, and they've done it. But this is crazy stuff, but this is what they've done. Okay, Adam, I honest to god, I mean my answer—and I've said this now for 10 days or whatever it's been—the only thing that Iran respects is might, strength. We need to start bombing again. And they are going to try to weasel out of everything; they are not going to give us a deal where their uranium goes bye-bye. I mean I guess the question that I'm asking you is, tell where do you think the puck goes from here? Because we're not meeting with them, we're exchanging paper, but I feel like we're not just at a blockade, we're at a full-on stalemate. Yeah, watching Iran debate with these negotiators is like watching that scene in Monty Python where the guy's got his limbs chopped off. He can't even do anything and he's threatening as if he's winning the fight and the other guy's like, "What are you going to do, bleed on me?" We have weakened Iran so dramatically in the past three years. Their proxies are nearly gone, in Hezbollah and in Hamas. Al-Assad is out of Syria. They are so much weaker than they were just a couple of years ago. Their ballistic missile production? Gone. Their nuclear production? Gone. And conversely, Israel and the West, we've built all these allies, the US now working with the UAE, Israel working with UAE, in ways never imaginable before. And the sad fact is that the media in America buys into the ridiculous narrative of the knight who has all of his limbs and arms and legs and whatnot chopped off as if he still has anything to stand on. Now, worst-case scenario, we end up having to mow the lawn like Israel has done every couple of years and going back and striking Iran again whenever they act up. But I think we're in a much stronger position than ever imaginable, perhaps in my lifetime, relative to Iran. And I think we merely need to keep up the pressure because we've weakened our greatest geopolitical ally while at the same time weakening China, I mean, because their oil is crushing them right now in an already weakened economy. We need to act like we're in the position of strength that we're actually in. Stephanie Hamill, real quick, we had on the Huddle, the morning show I do, Mike Sommers of the American Petroleum Institute. And he basically said, you know, if everything kind of plays out like we hope—that we come to some deal, blah blah blah blah blah—it's still going to be a while before we see cheap gas, okay? Because they have to assess the infrastructure in Iran, blah blah blah blah blah. Do you think that the White House is effectively messaging, ahead of the midterms, what we're doing in Iran and why we're doing it, because gas is going to be higher, right? I mean, if it goes down, it's going to be really close to the election, but probably not in time for the election. Yeah, I'm not going to lie, the timing isn't great. But I do think that the White House and President Trump are doing a good job messaging. The problem is the mainstream media and their hysteria and the way that they twist this conversation. It's almost like they want the US to lose, they want President Trump and our military to fail, which is absolutely disgusting. Think about over the past few decades every single Republican and Democrat president has talked tough on Iran and has made that a priority. And all of these same people that are spinning this situation, they were all for it. But all of a sudden because President Trump is in the seat here, leading this effort against Iran, now it's a problem because everything is a problem in their eyes when it's coming from the Trump administration. And so you know, this needed to be tackled head on. Iran, we cannot trust them. These are the regime that chants "Death to America." They are a threat to not only the US but the entire world. These people shouldn't have nuclear weapons and we know that they were on the way to that very mission. And so, yeah, I think the White House is doing a great job, but the mainstream media is spinning it and so the American people aren't really learning and understanding everything that's going on. Hey Erika, I have a question that I've been talking about all week about redistricting and the Voting Rights Act, but you're the perfect person to ask about. The Voting Rights Act—a lot of Republicans are trying to figure out how many seats we're going to pick up, right? So we've got Louisiana—we'll get a seat out of there—probably one out of Alabama. I think there's a potential to get the Memphis seat out of Tennessee; the Clyburn seat I'm not so sure about in South Carolina. But the Democrats keep talking about like how this is such a huge issue. Your husband, Byron Donalds, represents a majority white district. In fact, every black Republican represents that: Burgess Owens, Wesley Hunt, John James. It's interesting how the Democrats as a party have to carve a seat up for a constituency they claim is important, but for Republicans, we don't seem to have that problem. Oh that's exactly right. It's the DEI in the voting, right? And putting these gerrymandered districts together because white Democrats don't want black Democrats running against them in their own districts, so they want to set them aside where they're only representing other black voters and not crossing over to white Democrat voters. It's actually the most racist thing to say, "You can only represent people that match your skin color." So black Democrats should be offended by being put in that corner, if you will, to only represent people of their skin color and not a wider population. I'm glad that the Supreme Court made this decision, because we should not have black districts and white districts, no matter which party that you're talking about—it happens to be Democrats. We've got to get away from that level of identity politics and focus on issues. It doesn't matter which party you are in, it should not be based on skin color. I'm glad that we were able to get that out of the way. It's high time that it happened and get back to meritocracy, right? Who is the best person for the job and to represent this constituency based on their values, their beliefs, and what they need from the government. You know Adam, the media never wants to tell you that part that Erika just alluded to, right? They'll be like, "Oh, they're diluting this," and it's like, no, I've said this before: Democrats want to make race a priority in their party, then say so. Just say, "Great, we'll give you more money to run, we'll give you more resources, we'll make it a priority in our primaries." But it's funny, like the media never tells you that all of this currently only benefits one party. They're not getting blacks elected, they're getting black Democrats elected, and that's very different. But you won't ever hear that in the media narrative because they want to make it seem like this was an attack on race as opposed to undermining what was clearly a racist outcome, which is we're going to guarantee black Democrats get X amount of seats. Well, the most invisible person to the mainstream media is an African-American Republican. You know, look at how they celebrate African-American history month every year. Do we see the celebration of Clarence Thomas? I don't think so. That stuff doesn't happen. Do we see people talking about Tim Scott, other great leaders? Doesn't happen at all. It's as if those people don't exist. And it's amazing because these people love to hyper-focus on skin color. They love to reject Martin Luther King's vision of a color-blind society. Skin color is supposed to be everything to them, except political party matters even more than skin color, I guess, at the end of the day, because black Republicans, they don't even exist, they get no attention. It's as if these evil white racist Republicans would never elect a black man. That's what they want people to think, rather than actually understanding reality. Yeah. Stephanie, you know I talked about all the potential additions that we could get out of redistricting. All these states that are now acting because the Supreme Court acted. One state that's not acting: Indiana, where we could have picked up some seats, but a bunch of stick-in-the-mud elitists said we don't want to do that despite the fact that the Democrats are doing it all over the place. So President Trump said, "Fine, I'm going to target you guys." And his team had a huge night on Tuesday. They literally sent a lot of these Indiana state senators packing. And one of the guys lost by like 50 points. My takeaway from Indiana was that this was a test of President Trump's—for all the media questioning of President Trump, "Does he still have a grip on the party?" It was, "Oh yes he does." Like he made it very clear, if you cross him, they will take you out, and they showed it on Tuesday night in Indiana. And I think that is foreshadowing of a lot of the power that he still has for the Republican Congress to maintain their foot on the gas when it comes to, you know, Save America and the other legislative priorities. Absolutely. And if you tune into social media, you would have thought that MAGA was dead, it didn't exist anymore because some prominent people who were Trump supporters who called themselves MAGA are no longer MAGA, if you will, because they didn't like a few issues. But if you think about it, there was no other better candidate than President Trump and in my opinion, I don't think there's any president in my lifetime that has accomplished as much as this administration and President Trump. So, you know, they don't like a couple things so they want to throw out the entire Republican party. I mean that's just insane to me. Think about what our option was in the last election, that was Vice President Kamala Harris. I mean, could you imagine the direction this country would be in if she was leading the charge? I mean it is a nightmare to think about it. So again, I am thankful for President Trump and everything that he's been put through and his family put through so that he could stand up for the American people. And in regards to these elections and the MAGA candidates coming ahead, I think it is a referendum on President Trump and the American people are still standing with him because we know that President Trump is always right, and things might be a little bit painful but in the end, he's going to find solutions and he's going to make it right. It's funny, Erika, you mentioned DEI when we're talking about voting rights. The Trump administration sued the New York Times because it discriminated against a white man, and this is what the report—this is literally I'm reading from the Wall Street Journal, it says: "The New York Times is sued by the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission, rather it's EEOC, over allegations it passed over a white man for promotion in pursuit of what the agency called unlawful corporate diversity policies. In a complaint filed in Manhattan Federal Court Tuesday, the US watchdog said that the Times chose not to promote the staffer to an editor role in early 2025 because of his race and gender. Instead, it hired a non-white woman who the agency alleged had less experience." One of the things that I love about Trump 2.0 is there's nothing sacred anymore. They're going after culture, they're going after, you know, education and academics and that's obviously your wheelhouse. But like they're not leaving anything off the table. They're like, if you played to the woke DEI policies of the Biden years, we're coming after you. Yeah, it's a celebration to dismantle this anti-American, anti-meritocracy that has taken place especially in corporate America. You know I was in the investment management business for 20 years. That ESG where they were giving us quotas of how many women or minorities that we had to have on our investment team—so you had to select someone based on their gender in order to qualify to manage some of these public pensions. That has—it's infiltrated the entirety of corporate America and throughout government, and I love just making an example out of especially the New York Times, we love to hate them. Right, to sue them, because we've seen people win, and especially white men who've been discriminated against with these types of policies, and they've really gone by the wayside, but it's great to showcase why this is not okay. It's exactly the same as the lack of meritocracy in elections with the Voting Rights Act that was also just overturned. So hopefully by the end of this Trump term, we'll be rid of all the DEI and ESG across the board. You know Adam, as a white guy to another white guy, I'm actually picking up on what Erika was saying. For you don't remember a few years ago the idea was—people that I would talk to at least in my world would say, "I'm not even going to apply for that, I'm not going to do it because there's no way I'll get it," right? The bend of the Biden years was if you didn't fit the mold of like a gay Indian that had three hands, you were never going to get, you know, a job or promotion or accepted to whatever. And at least this idea of meritocracy is it rooting its head again? The idea of who's the most qualified person for the job or for the admissions? And I think we're getting back to that. Well, I'd like to see that, but I worry that in the public sector we're definitely not going that direction, certainly not fast enough. You know, as you know, we do hidden camera investigations at Accuracy in Media and every week all year we've been releasing multiple hidden camera investigations from universities and K-through-12 school districts in red states that are still pushing DEI in defiance of the Trump executive order, in defiance of state law. And in a lot of instances, people get fired—one of our investigators, everywhere she shows up, we call her the angel of unemployment, these people get fired. But these are institutional problems, and at times it seems like the Trump administration is quick to declare victory, move on to something else, while the radicals in education, unlike the New York Times, funded by our tax dollars, continue doing DEI each and every day. Harmeet Dhillon has done some great work investigating a number of these universities, including ones that we've exposed. Ken Paxton in Texas seems to investigate every university that we expose, which is a ton of fun, but I would like to see a far more proactive Trump administration actually staying on top of these universities and ensuring their reform. Is there—for all of you—I mean is there an issue that you wish—like Save America, I think is huge, right? But I also wonder—and I say this because I know where I stand in the political spectrum, right? And I go, okay, Save America to me is huge, integrity is huge, like if we don't know about what's going on in our elections. But I do wonder if that's what's going to pull enough people to our side. Erika, you're out there on the campaign trail, you and Byron talk to people every day. What is it that you think we're not talking enough about? I think we are talking about what people care most about, which is the affordability crisis. Unfortunately, these things take time, right? It takes time to unravel four years of terrible policies under the Biden administration and what they did really totally against the economy and what would help the American people. And so trying to message that it's going to continue to get better is just difficult when people are feeling things in their pocketbooks. But that is what people care about. People do care about Save America because in—and it's not for the midterms and you know how this is—you have to balance great policy that truly is good for America and good for the long-term future of this country with what's going to help you message to win the next election. I commend President Trump and the Trump administration because they are trying to focus on both. Save America is truly to save America and to make sure that our elections are secure and that we are not taken over by illegal immigrants who are not supposed to be voting in our elections. I think people do care about that. Is it going to motivate people to get out and vote in the midterms? Perhaps, and definitely the base. But when it comes to independents, what we are hearing is the pocketbook, right, those economic issues. It's the economy, stupid. So we just have to continue to hammer on that and make sure that they are actually feeling the results of the good policies that are in place. You know Adam, it's funny, there's this balance between promoting what you've done and the policies that are making people's lives better and pointing out how ridiculous the other side is. Like how crazy some of their left-wing progressive policies are. There—and I know this isn't an elected official, but it reminds me of what's going on. There's this guy, Cole Allen, who tried to assassinate the president and kill other people at the White House Correspondents' Dinner, right? They lock him up, okay, they arrest the dude. And this is not a joke, this is literally the manifestation in my opinion of liberal policies: the judge, the magistrate judge in Washington D.C., apologized to Cole Allen for the conditions of the D.C. jail. I mean, this is not made up. "Whatever you've been through, I apologize for the prior week," the judge told him. He's sorry. I mean, dude, you just tried to kill some people, what's the big deal? I'm sorry that the jail isn't up to your standards, and we'll put you in the Marriott next time. I mean that's who these people are, Adam. It really is, and I was looking into this judge. When he was appointed, the letter written in support of him proudly proclaimed how devoted he was—there it is again—to diversity, equity and inclusion. My gosh, the priorities of these people, their world view, is so far removed from mainstream society. Now, I recognize that this isn't going to be the issue that gets the people to the polls—I think affordability is number one, two and three. But it's still important for Americans to understand this is who they are. These are their priorities. This is what matters to them most: making sure this guy has the proper mattress after attempting to kill the President of the United States, the leader of the free world. You lead with an apology—that's the kind of judge. You wonder why your streets are full of crime in major cities? Because these prosecutors, these DAs, drop charges against violent criminals, send them out on the street, and this guy gets that apology after an assassination attempt. Their world view is insane to me. Stephanie Hamill, I never thought that I would think trying to assassinate a group of people would be a partisan issue. Like the guy literally—everybody should be outraged because here's the thing: even if you're that liberal judge, all the people in the room were a bunch of woke leftist journalists. You should be at least hoping that he didn't take them out. But they all know they weren't the targets, right? Because they're the ones that are on TV calling President Trump Hitler and completely just slandering the entire administration and everyone else that was there. So they're not worried about themselves. I mean maybe there could have been an accident and maybe they could have been targets, but they were not the targets and they know that. That's why we're seeing the reaction that we're seeing. And as for this federal judge, I mean it's absolutely despicable. His comments—I mean that's disqualifying if you ask me. And apparently he has a history of this; he has a history of activism, he has said some other questionable things in regards to these types of matters, like sympathizing and feeling bad for the alleged violent criminals, if you will. And when it comes to Cole Allen, there's actual videos. Like did the judge not see the actual video? I mean everyone's innocent until proven guilty and obviously there will—this will go through the court system, there will be a trial and everything—but did he not see the video? I mean we all saw it. Not only that, but when no one cared about all those people from January 6th that were kept without their due process in those same jails, no one cared about their conditions or their rights. And suddenly now a guy who literally tried to assassinate people, we worry about him. Anyway, unreal. By the way, I want to start—I want to go to winners and losers. Always, I love this. I think I have two that you guys are not going to guess. Erika Donalds, I'm going to start with you, give me your winner of the week. I've got to go with the ultimate Florida man, Marco Rubio, because he got to say they better check themselves before they wreck themselves at the press podium in the White House. So, definitely a winner, 100%. Adam, who do you have? Every Florida property owner. As Governor DeSantis pointed out this week, Mayor Adams—he's the realtor of the year for the Sunshine State. The only mistake DeSantis ever made was selling his home. He makes $141,000 a year as Governor; his property that he sold down the street from me makes a lot more. All right, Erika, you got to remember you're not selling your house now because when Byron becomes governor and you're first lady, you're going to keep making it. More people from New York are going to come down there, you know that. So just FYI, pro tip there. And you guys are investment people, I didn't need to tell you that. Stephanie, who do you think won the week? Is anyone watching this LA mayor's race? It is incredible. I am just in love with Spencer Pratt, mostly because I'm a millennial and I grew up watching his reality TV shows that he was part of. But he's, I mean, really stunning and he's intelligent and if you—there was a debate yesterday and he completely owned everyone on that stage. I mean he's talking a lot of sense, like I never would have thought that would actually come from his mouth. But he's doing an incredible job and I honestly think that he has a chance of winning this. I—good call on that. I do wonder—he's getting a lot of national attention—that debate last night was pretty epic. But I will tell you I do wonder if he's doing the blocking and tackling necessary in a campaign. I mean that's why Erika and Byron are on the campaign, I mean like there's things you do on a campaign, you can't just have a bunch of viral clips. You gotta go talk to voters like they're doing. All right. My winner, I'm glad no one got it, is photo ID. And I'll tell you why. We talked about Save America Act. Barack Obama announced that the Obama library monstrosity center—that's officially the name—will require photo ID. Now, I gotta show—they put out this video with Mark Hamill by the way, which I just want to show you too because this shows you when I call it a monstrosity this is what I'm talking about. Take a look. Happy Star Wars day from the Obama Presidential Center. "I have a very good feeling about this." Mark, I am glad you are here. I want to tell you about someone. "Okay." A young person born into ordinary circumstances, but restless. "Unsatisfied." Mmhmm, a kid with big dreams. "A bit of a rebel." I like where this is going. They join a scrappy group of underdogs and set out to change things. "By blowing up a giant space laser?" Mark, this is not about you. "Oh." All right. I mean look, they are the—like this is how cheesy this Obama—and it literally is disgusting. I mean it's a monstrosity. People are actually calling it the Death Star. He wanted to do that thing with Mark Hamill of Luke—of Star Wars. The Obama center is disgusting. It's gross. It's an eyesore. And I mean maybe they should use that laser Mark Hamill was talking about to blow it up—and I mean that, don't—don't play this, I'm not inciting violence and I'm not—just grow up everybody on the internet. I'm just saying like Mark Hamill brought it up. Maybe they do destroy this thing. But I'm telling you, it was disgusting. All right, I want to do losers real quick. Erika, you were ready to go, so who do you got? Indiana state senators and every state elected official that goes against the MAGA agenda, right? Be on warning, right? We did it in Texas when they went against school choice, we did it in Indiana, now they went against redistricting. So who is up next? All right. That's right. Watch out lawmakers. All right Adam, who do you have? Well, now I have to say it's Mark Hamill; that's his worst role since the Last Jedi. Before that I was going to say Californians because they had a debate and people were praising Villaraigosa, who's still in favor of their insanely corrupt train to nowhere, so there you go, two of them. Stephanie, who do you have as a loser? There's a video going viral of a woman who hung herself upside down naked in a bronze bell to bring awareness to climate change. I don't know if you guys saw this, but it is laugh-out-loud funny that these climate change activists are so insane that they think that this is going to do something for climate change. Instead we're just laughing at them and we're actually concerned for their mental health. There's some dude that hung out on a bridge in the D.C. area and apparently I was like, "How do you hang—how do you go to the bathroom if you're up on a bridge?" Anyway, that's crazy. But I have—I was hoping you weren't going to go here. I have this woman I just gotta play the clip for you because it's epic. This is who my loser of the week is. Take a look. He's a very humble guy. Two months in, he calls his bank in South Chicago to change his phone number and I think his address. And so he gets a lady, says, "Yes ma'am, I'm Robert Prevost, I'd like to change." She asks all the security questions and then—oh, "I'm sorry sir, it says here you have to come in person." And he said, "Well that's not going to be able to do that. Can't we change..." "No sir, I gave you all the security questions." "I'm sorry sir." "Would it matter to you if I told you I'm Pope Leo?" She hung up on him. Could you imagine being known as the woman who hung up on the Pope? All right, that's my loser: you hung up on the Pope when he tried to change his bank account address. Anyway, I love that. US NEWS LIVE: Pentagon DROPS BOMBSHELL | Troop Surge in Iran COMING? | Pete Hegseth LIVE

MIRROR NOW(2.2M)·241:06·1.6K views·2026-05-07
Pete HegsethU.S. Secretary of Defense
TL;DRFifty-five days into major combat operations against Iran, the United States is enforcing an ironclad global naval blockade, successfully turning back 34 vessels and seizing three major ships (the *Tuska*, *Tiffany*, and *Majestic X*).
). Under the campaign "Epic Fury," US forces...

TL;DR

Fifty-five days into major combat operations against Iran, the United States is enforcing an ironclad global naval blockade, successfully turning back 34 vessels and seizing three major ships (the *Tuska*, *Tiffany*, and *Majestic X*). Under the campaign "Epic Fury," US forces have struck over 7,000 targets, decimating Iran's air defenses and reducing its ballistic missile and drone capabilities by 90%.

Global Blockade and High-Seas Interdictions

  • Enforcement of the Blockade: Enforced by US Central Command (CENTCOM), the blockade targets any vessel of any nationality transiting to or from Iranian ports. To date, 34 ships have voluntarily turned around.
  • The Seizure of the MV Tuska: On Sunday, April 19, the 965-foot container ship *Tuska* attempted to breach the blockade line.
      • After the crew ignored six hours of warnings and five warning shots, a US destroyer fired nine inert rounds from its Mark 45 5-inch guns to disable the ship's engine room.
      • US Marines subsequently boarded the disabled vessel via helicopter fast-ropes and took custody of the ship and crew.
  • Global Oil and "Dark Fleet" Seizures: Operating in coordination with the Department of Justice, US INDOPACOM forces executed major maritime interdictions in the Indo-Pacific and Indian Oceans:
      • MT Tiffany: On April 20, US military and law enforcement forces boarded and seized this Very Large Crude Carrier (VLCC), which was carrying approximately 2 million barrels of sanctioned Iranian oil.
      • MT Majestic X (Ponics): On April 22, US forces interdicted and boarded this stateless VLCC in the Indian Ocean, securing the bridge and taking custody of the vessel.

Combat Milestones of Operation "Epic Fury"

  • Unchanged Strategic Objectives: Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth reconfirmed that the campaign’s core mandates are to permanently prevent Iran from acquiring a nuclear weapon, destroy its military-industrial base, and eliminate its offensive naval capabilities.
  • Severe Degradation of Iranian Forces: The joint force has systematically dismantled Iran's military infrastructure, yielding key metrics:
      • Infrastructure Targets: Over 7,000 targets have been struck across Iran, flattening air defenses and defense industrial production lines.
      • Weapon Capability Reductions: Launch capabilities for both ballistic missiles and one-way attack UAVs (kamikaze drones) have plummeted by 90%.
      • Naval Losses: US forces have damaged or sunk over 120 Iranian navy ships and 44 mine-laying vessels. Iran's entire submarine fleet of 11 vessels has been destroyed, reducing their navy to "bottom half of the ocean" status.
  • Tactical Transitions: US forces have transitioned from standoff weapons to stand-in weapons. B-1, B-2, and B-52 bomber crews are deploying 5,000-pound penetrator bombs into hardened underground missile and mine storage facilities, while A-10 Warthogs and AH-64 Apaches hunt fast-attack watercraft on the southern flank.

Geopolitical Dynamics and Allied Cooperation

  • Regional Partnerships: Israel remains a highly capable frontline partner. Reckless Iranian strikes on regional civilian infrastructure have successfully pushed Gulf States—including the UAE, Qatar, Bahrain, Kuwait, and Saudi Arabia—squarely into the US defensive orbit.
  • European "Free Riding" Criticized: Secretary Hegseth sharply criticized European nations for failing to commit naval assets to secure the Strait of Hormuz, calling their recent security summits "silly conferences." Hegseth emphasized that because the US is energy independent, the security of the Strait is primarily a European and Asian energy crisis, not an American one.
  • Rules of Engagement: President Trump has authorized the US Navy to use lethal force to "shoot and kill" any Iranian fast boats attempting to lay mines or disrupt commercial shipping in the Strait of Hormuz.

Internal Security, Leaks, and the Afghanistan Review

  • Termination of Nuclear Chief Andrew Hug: Hegseth confirmed that Department of the Army Nuclear Chief Andrew Hug has been summarily terminated and faces potential prosecution after an undercover investigation revealed he leaked top-secret national security information to a stranger on a dating app.
  • Condemning Press Leaks: The Pentagon issued a stern warning to domestic media outlets, criticizing publications like the *New York Times* for publishing classified leaks that actively jeopardize the lives of active-duty service members.
  • Abbey Gate and the Afghanistan Review: Senior Adviser Sean Parnell is leading a comprehensive review of the 2021 US withdrawal from Afghanistan, scheduled for public release later this summer.
      • Hegseth attributed global deterrence failures—including actions by Vladimir Putin, Hamas, and the Houthis—to the "feckless and weak" withdrawal under the Biden administration.
      • As an immediate result of the review, members of Golf Company 2/1 Marines who served at Abbey Gate will receive upgraded awards and proper recognition for their heroism.

Notable Quotes

"To the regime in Tehran, the blockade is tightening by the hour. We are in control: nothing in, nothing out. Iran's battered military, the IRGC specifically, has been reduced to a gang of pirates with a flag." — Pete Hegseth, Secretary of Defense
"This is not a fake blockade, this is a real, full blockade. We'll use up to and including lethal force if necessary, and [the attempts] stopped, and other ships have taken note of that." — Pete Hegseth, Secretary of Defense
"There's never actually been a full accounting in this department of the decisions that were made... the cascading effect of what Joe Biden did in abandoning that mission the way that he did and that disastrous withdrawal had ripple effects for our military, for our country, and for the world." — Pete Hegseth, Secretary of Defense
Intelligence requirement to that piece of intelligence being collected in the field by a military or interagency case officer to the rigorous analysis that goes into reporting on that finding. Those team members spend their careers answering the incredibly important questions that we must attempt to answer before, during, and after operations. They live in the enemy's mind. Normally located behind the green door or behind the vault door in dark, windowless rooms across the world, 24 hours a day, 7 days a week, 365 days a year, our world-class intel members from DIA, our service intelligence components, NSA, NGA, CIA, NRO, and in the case of the operations that I've just described, the Office of Naval Intelligence, all work tirelessly to sift through the mountains of collected information, using tools, tactics, techniques, and procedures that I'm not going to discuss today.
