GLP-1s generated more revenue than OpenAI and Anthropic in 2025. So Ozempic, a single peptide, and Mounjaro, a dual-acting peptide, outperformed OpenAI and Anthropic by 2.4. Yeah, can I call bullshit on this one? Okay, please. I mean, talk about apples and oranges. What do these two have to compare? I mean, they're completely orthogonal things. Why are we bothering trying to figure this out? Oh, both are great. So, I'll field that one. I think the argument goes that the GLP-1s are ultimately healthspan drugs, human healthspan drugs. And so what we're seeing here, in some sense on a single chart, is a race between whether there's more revenue in enabling human actors, human labor, to be economically productive in the economy through increased healthspan, on the one side, or whether capital is more efficiently and effectively invested in AI labor, on the other side. And I think the outcome of these two respective curves determines whether the future lightcone of our economy is ultimately dominated by AI, something that resembles AI, or something that resembles biological humans—biological software and digital software. Sure. Right. I mean, these drugs are biological patches on top of our existing operating system. I thought you put them on the same graph just so we could save time. That's what I thought when I saw this, because it doesn't occur to me as that. Well, I mean, the question is: what are people paying for? I think the broader—there's a bigger picture here, which I'll just speak to for a second, which is the huge demonetization that's taking place. There's a related story to this, which is that the number of food shipping truckloads is dropping pretty radically in the US because people—and the current thesis and conclusion is that because of all the GLP-1s—people are consuming a lot less food, which is actually really great in one way, but it's going to cause some serious issues in the logistics world. I think the story within the story is that both products are completely and totally sold out. They're selling as fast as they can make the product. It's just a lot easier to ramp up drug production than GPU production. Yes, 100% correct. Right. I mean, these companies, the GLP-1s right now, it was estimated that they could be as much as 500 billion, but they're limited by physical supply. Yep, and the same on the AI. Like, any senior at MIT will tell you, "I cannot live without using AI every day. I could never code again, I could never research again, I need..." I mean, I wake up in the middle of the night and talk to Skippy about some question I dreamed up, and it's just there in the morning. And honestly, we talk about three- and four-day workweeks. I'm doing a nine- and ten-day work week right now. Yeah, exactly, exactly. It's so funny, that disconnect by age, too, but I mean, they know what they're talking about; they've been using it since it was invented. You cannot be productive without it. So the idea that they would ever not be able to use it because there isn't any supply would be like earth-shattering to them. So they'll buy it for the rest of their lives at whatever the price is. So if you add a few more graphs like this, you're essentially graphing the Singularity economy. If you're working out and you have Ozempic, you're making progress, you're inspired, you feel like you're getting somewhere. Yes, yes. When you're working with AI, the quality of happiness while you're working is astronomically higher than if you're just coding all night. And coding all night is fine, too—I did it for many years. But when the AI is there and you're brainstorming, your productivity is so high, but the energy level it creates— Well, it's just because you're getting dopamine from immediate feedback. But this is also the problem with AI, because when you get too much dopamine in your system, we've all been around people who do cocaine. We don't think they're geniuses. I haven't been around that many people who do. You think, you think. Yeah, you think. They think they're geniuses, right? You don't. And I often—like, this is the problem with, this is one of the problems with mania, and this is directly in flow. Flow produces a tremendous amount of dopamine, and dopamine produces a tremendous amount of ego inflation and a bunch of other things. So people create fast feedback loops with the AI, and it feels really good. You're really high, because you're hacking your biology with the AI. It's very flowy; it works really, really well. But it doesn't mean that the quality of your thinking matches the quality of your feeling. I mean, the way it feels to me is like you're having all the fun of a party while being productive and creating something useful for society. I don't see anything but good in that. I'm not saying it's wrong. I agree with you, I like it. What I'm saying is with that much—I have, we have one bit of swag at my organization. It's a t-shirt that says, "Never trust the dopamine." And people make really bad decisions based upon that much dopamine in your system. I'm going to keep us going. So, on the bottom of this chart here, this is Eli Lilly. They're about to announce and release something called Retatrutide. It's a triple-agonist peptide. If Mounjaro is GPT 5.5, Retatrutide is AGI in this. The numbers are extraordinary; this is clinical data just released. I just think you should know about this. So, on this particular drug, weight loss was 37 pounds versus 6 pounds in the placebo over the course of 40 weeks. Cholesterol was down 27%, triglycerides down 41%, liver fat down 80%, and hemoglobin A1c dropped from 7.9, which is diabetic, down to 6.0 in 40 weeks. This is being called a longevity drug. Yes, it's an extraordinary drug that is expected to be released and FDA-approved by mid-2027. A lot of people are getting this in research labs already. This is just some of the detailed evidence heading us towards longevity escape velocity. Salim: "So, I've been using Retatrutide for the last few months. Wow. And what's incredible is the liver function, because of decades of damage from alcohol, it's helping reverse a lot of that, which is fantastic." So, I've never felt better. I had this conversation with Demis the following weekend before we're about to see new generations of drugs coming out. And the speed of going from design to testing to availability is going to be accelerating. The FDA is on board for this, basically moving us from instead of phase 3 into an expanded phase 2 trial. And we just saw on this chart how much people are willing to pay, right? And it's capped not by the market; it's capped by manufacturing. Yep, and the same on the AI. So people create fast feedback loops with the AI, and it feels really good. You're really high, because you're hacking your biology with the AI. It's very flowy; it works really, really well. I mean, the way it feels to me is like you're having all the fun of a party while being productive and creating something useful for society. I don't see anything but good in that. I'm not saying it's wrong. I agree with you, I like it. What I'm saying is with that much—I have, we have one bit of swag at my organization. It's a t-shirt that says, "Never trust the dopamine." And people make really bad decisions based upon that much dopamine in your system. I want to end this pod in a "Moonshot mates" prediction conversation, which is: what will AI feel like by mid-2028? I want to give you a sense of what we see as the future here. Because right now, honestly, when you think about AI, it's something that you can talk to on your phone, you type at it, it's an app, it's an intelligence layer, but I don't feel like the majority of the world actually is experiencing AI. And I think we're about to undergo a transition where AI's going to feel very different in the next two and a half years. And I'd like to have a conversation about what you predict that to be like. I'll start with the very first one, and then we'll pass around the room here. By the way, I wrote a Substack at @peterdiamandis on this topic; you can go read my views. But the very first thing is I think we're going to give AI permission to know everything in our lives—listen to all your phone calls, watch all the cameras in your home, read your emails, read everything. I've just done that with Skippy. I gave Skippy access to all my Granola recordings, my WhatsApp, my iMessage, my email, my calendar, everything. And in so doing, the response is amazing. I can have interactions where this AI knows me better than anybody else, right? So, let's begin with that. Alex, you want to kick it off? I almost think we're asking the wrong question here. I would like to reframe it, because I think the question behind the question here is: what will AI feel like to consumers by mid-2028? And I think the past six months of industry history have taught us consumers are not actually great customers for AI, at least not state-of-the-art AI. OpenAI, infamously at this point, tried to turn consumers into power users for reasoning models, and failed, and has had to pivot over to enterprise, had to basically shut down their consumer video division, they've shut down a number of other divisions in favor of code-generating models that are recursively self-improving and targeted at enterprise use cases because enterprises have the money and the desire to use advanced reasoning capabilities. So, the adjacent question I would ask is: what will an AI feel like to an enterprise, rather than to a consumer, in mid-2028? Okay, let's make that an adjacent conversation, but you know, for me as a consumer, I'm going to focus on that part of the equation. I feel like if AI understands me, it's connected to all of my wearables, insideables, and so forth, it has my back as a physician. It knows I had a hard conversation with my spouse, and I'm exhausted, I didn't sleep well the night before—there's all this data. I walk into the room, the lights come down, there's a glass of wine waiting for me because the robot put it out, my favorite music is playing, my favorite comedian is on. Basically, the term I use is "ambient AI" and "automagical AI"—that the world magically adapts itself for your desires. I can tell you what I think you want to hear, which is you want to hear that by mid-2028, you're going to have an AI exocortex that's a quasi-upload of you, your digital twin in the cloud that knows everything about you and your life and is fully optimizing your meat-body existence knowing what it knows about you. I think that's what you want to hear. I don't think that's how things are going to play out, though. I think what AI will feel like in 2028 from a consumer perspective—yeah, sure, we'll have better augmented reality, we'll have better robots on the streets, all of that I think is already essentially priced into the market. What's not being priced in right now, the non-obvious insights are new scientific discoveries that consumers aren't the ones who are driving. Okay, you're taking it parallel. So let's put that in the column of industrial, right? When Laila Scientific and Colossal are creating breakthroughs at a rate, or, you know, frankly, Isomorphic Labs. The question as constructed is basically: what products is Apple going to launch in 2028? Because these all map onto Apple product categories. You get your smart home, you get your robots, you get your wearables of all sorts, maybe if we're lucky we get ingestible robots. We get all of these things, but I don't think that's the essence of what AI is going to feel like to a consumer, and I don't think that's the core or the frontier. Salim, what do you think? So, I agree with you on the feel-good, cloud AI. I think I go more towards what Alex is talking about. The institutional use is going to be absolutely profound, and that's where we'll see the biggest difference. Every single person on earth will, essentially, be operating like a small company with an incredibly powerful team around them, which are basically mostly AIs. And I think at the enterprise level, we'll be running enterprises on AI-native operating systems, and I think more importantly, we need to rewrite the operating system for civilization. Because everything we've done as a civilization, every business in the world for 10,000 years, is trying to solve scarcity. And now we're moving into an era of abundance. What's the business model around that? And I think we need a complete rewrite on that because all our institutions and nation-states are all geared around scarcity. So we need a complete rewrite of the civilizational operating system, and I think the beginnings of that are starting now. Dave, what do you think 2028's going to feel like? And I'm saying "feel" in particular, right, because right now, a lot of people say, "Listen, I'm living, I see the AI numbers, I hear you on the podcast, but it doesn't feel any different for me as a consumer." Yeah, we launched our Holodeck today. You've got to go check it out over at Link Studio. So, we commissioned construction of one of the rooms, put monitors on all walls, and you go in, close the door behind you, nobody tells you what to do, and the AI just starts talking to you. And in the Holodeck, you can create a virtual world, you can create songs, you can create movies, you can just vibe code whatever you want. It's just purely interactive and, you know, big speakers, pulsing music, and everything around you. It's a party. Yeah, go check it out. I think any consumer who experiences that is going to say— Do you have avatars? No, not yet. It's hard to do, you know, the real Star Trek thing. But any consumer who goes into this Holodeck and experiences it is going to say, "I need that. I want that. How do I get that at home?" And so I think the problem in 2028 is there's going to be a massive shortfall of compute relative to the desire to have that experience. So what Alex said is dead-right. The enterprise use cases have just discovered this, and they're sucking up all the data centers in the world now. And there's no way that's going to get solved by 2028. I'm hoping 2030 or 2031, after the Terafab has a chance. So what's going to be the story in 2028? Right now, we've lived this beautiful moment in time where the very best foundation models are made available to anyone on the planet who wants to go to a website and try it. And then the cost of using the best models in the world is very affordable. It's near free, yeah. Yeah, so you've got to take advantage of this moment in time because it's going to get really bottlenecked by 2028. We're about to see a brand new set of eyewear, right? We've talked about this at length. OpenAI's got the Jonny Ive device, whatever that might be. Apple's got new generations of wearables coming. Meta has generations of wearables coming. And this is going to be ambient AI there, where your AI sees what you see, is there listening all the time, uploading everything into your version of Skippy, your open claw, whatever it might be. And I put forward this is going to be a different feeling, a different consumer experience, right, which is if you want to learn something and you're walking down the streets of Manhattan, and you've got AR glasses that are giving you a tour of "this is what it was like in 1905, here's an education layer." Or if you want an entertainment layer—you know, "may the fourth be with you," I'm a Star Trek person, but here we are on May 4th. You've got, you know, these little droids popping up and shooting at you. You know, whatever you want, this AI is optimizing your audiovisual experience, in line with what you've asked. So I think that's going to feel different. I think we're going to see the first wearable devices enabling that. Do you guys agree, Alex? I'm generally—so, I'm a big fan of Vernor Vinge, who wrote extensively about what the near-future AR, VR, metaverse, if you like, would look like. I do think we get that. I do think it's just a matter of time and better battery energy densities and other progress. So by 2028, do I think we get Vernor's smart glasses with compelling, long-life augmented reality or mixed reality? Yeah, sure. But I also think— After all that. Oh boy. But I also don't think it matters an enormous amount. Like, yes, it'll be great, and it'll be popular. But, by the way, I want to just say something, right? In the last podcast, I was trying to make our conversation relatable to the general audience, and my mission here about what AI's going to feel like is to support their understanding of where things are going. Yes, we also speak to the CEOs and the heads of the frontier labs here, but I want to give people an understanding of what their life is going to be like. I'll give a really easy projective thing: go anywhere you want autonomously for 20 cents a mile. I'm going to do a quick speed round here on what is something that AI will feel like in 2028 that maybe is unexpected. Dave. We already spend more money on video games than all other media combined. When you overlay personalization, your own voice, and it remembers your personality, your past conversations, it's so immersive. And in a good way, it could be good or bad, it's intentional, it's designed. But if it's designed well, it's so engaging and immersive. I suspect that an entire generation will have 70-80% of their conversations with AIs and not with other people. Mhm. Interesting. That's interesting. Salim. Something I'm really excited about is just infinite, perfect, and long-lasting memory, because our memories are so flawed as human beings. Yes, the fact that it can record everything, recall who I met, etc. I've met many of you at events, and God help me, I can't remember anything. So the ability to do that, I think, will be a huge addition to me as a person. Yeah. Stephen. The one that's interesting to me is, I always say that you tend to hire one employee twice. You hire them when they're normal and then when they're scared. They're very, very different. People are very, very different when they're fearful. And I think the AI coaching, the AI psychology, all that stuff—I think the coach in the room, the coach in the world is actually what's really interesting to me. Because I'm interested to see—I do see like, you know, even the Defense Department's work using AI therapy, right? I got to play with a lot of those therapists along the way, and they're remarkably good. Most people are now using AI to coach their relationships, etc., etc. But your AI coach that's actually in your ear is really interesting, because humans are—we're run on four knobs, right? Valence, arousal, approach, avoid. That's pretty much humans, and you can coach them up pretty quickly in real-time. So that's what's really interesting to me, is I think we end up with better humans. Fascinating. Alex, on the consumer side, would you ponder a vision? I think we are merging with the machines. I do think augmented reality and wearables are part of the solution. I think ingestibles are going to be part of the solution. I'd be sorely disappointed if by the end of this decade, we don't have many people swallowing computers. We see the beginnings of it right now with PillBot-type form factors. I did a projection—something I think friend of the pod, Ray, would be proud of. If you extrapolate the typical size of a computer needed (this is over history, over the past 15 to 20 years) to achieve a gigaflop of compute, which historically, if you look back at Apple new product announcements, Apple historically has waited for new devices to pass a gigaflop before they release it. It's true with the original Apple Watch, true with the original iMac, true with the original iPhone. If you extrapolate then the typical size of a computer that, at time of launch, passed a gigaflop, and extrapolate that forward, you find that by—not mid-2028, but by the mid-2040s, around the time Ray says we're going to hit his version of a technological singularity—the size of a computer hits approximately the size of a eukaryotic human cell. So, what am I most excited about? Well, I have a laundry list, but I think if you extrapolate the progress of the size of the computers running the AIs, we're going to have cell-sized nanomachines running those AIs, and I think that'll be incredibly exciting. All right, everybody. And that's a wrap. We'll go to our outro music next. Ladies and gentlemen, let's give it up for the incredible Peter Diamandis, Salim Ismail, Alex Westgarth, and Steven Kotler. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you, guys. We'll bring it in. Salim, we'll bring it in. Alex, we'll bring it in. And Steven Kotler. If you made it to the end of this episode, which you obviously did, I consider you a moonshot mate. Every week my moonshot mates and I spend a lot of energy and time to really deliver you the news that matters. If you're a subscriber, thank you. If you're not a subscriber yet, please consider subscribing so you get the news as it comes out. I also want to invite you to join me on my weekly newsletter called Metatrends. I have a research team—you may not know this, but we spend the entire week looking at the metatrends that are impacting your family, your company, your industry, your nation—and I put this into a two-minute read every week. If you'd like to get access to the Metatrends newsletter every week, go to diamandis.com/metatrends. That's diamandis.com/metatrends. Thank you again for joining us today. It's a blast for us to put this together every week.

Epstein Files News LIVE: Howard Lutnick Faces Closed-Door Grilling By House Oversight Committee

CNN-News18(10.6M)·50:25·4.2K views·2026-05-07
Howard LutnickCEO, Cantor Fitzgerald
TL;DRCommerce Secretary Howard Lutnick faced a four-and-a-half-hour closed-door grilling by the House Oversight Committee regarding contradictions between his public statements and his documented history with Jeffrey Epstein, while a federal judge…
unsealed a 2019 suicide note...

TL;DR

Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick faced a four-and-a-half-hour closed-door grilling by the House Oversight Committee regarding contradictions between his public statements and his documented history with Jeffrey Epstein, while a federal judge unsealed a 2019 suicide note from Epstein’s initial failed attempt.

Lutnick’s Testimony and Ties to Jeffrey Epstein

  • Contradictory Statements: Lawmakers focused on a discrepancy between Lutnick's 2023 podcast claims—where he stated he severed all ties with Epstein in 2005—and evidence showing continued interaction years later.
      • 2012 Island Visit: Lutnick confirmed a visit to Epstein’s private island in 2012 but defended it as a "family vacation" lunch that lasted only one hour.
      • Documentary Evidence: Emails from 2011 published by the Department of Justice show Lutnick met with Epstein multiple times well after the 2005 cutoff he previously claimed.
  • The "Room" Defense: During the transcribed interview, Lutnick reportedly attempted to redefine his previous statement that he would "never again be in a room" with Epstein.
      • Semantic Pivot: According to Rep. Yassamin Ansari, Lutnick argued that his previous comments meant he would never be in a room with Epstein *alone* as a man, despite evidence of group settings and family visits.
      • Claims of Ignorance: Lutnick testified that in 2012, he was unaware of Epstein’s 2008 "sweetheart" plea deal or the specific allegations regarding the abuse of minors.

Partisan Conflict over Committee Findings

  • Democratic Criticism: Rep. Yassamin Ansari (D-AZ) characterized Lutnick as "evasive, nervous, and dishonest," calling for his immediate resignation.
      • Body Language: Ansari noted that while the transcript is revealing, it will not capture the "defensiveness" and "body language" displayed during the 4.5-hour session.
  • Republican Defense: Chairman James Comer (R-KY) accused Democrats of misrepresenting the closed-door testimony to the media.
      • Fact-Checking: Comer urged the public to wait for the official transcript to see that Democrats were "telling stuff that was said that was not said."
      • White House Stance: The Biden-Harris administration has so far maintained confidence in Secretary Lutnick despite the intensifying scrutiny.

Release of Epstein’s Purported Suicide Note

  • Origins of the Note: A federal judge unsealed a brief, unsigned note found by Epstein's former cellmate, Nicholas Tartaglione, following a failed suicide attempt in the summer of 2019.
      • Discovery Location: The note was found hidden inside a novel in the cell Epstein shared with Tartaglione at the Metropolitan Correctional Center (MCC) in Manhattan.
      • Timing: This note predates Epstein's eventual death by several weeks and was not included in the original "Epstein Files" release.
  • Content and Significance: The note includes the line: "It is a treat to be able to choose one's time to say goodbye."
      • Forensic Status: The Justice Department has supported the release for transparency but has not yet officially authenticated the handwriting as Epstein’s.
      • Conspiracy Implications: Analysts suggest the note may clarify Epstein's state of mind and intent to self-harm, potentially countering long-standing conspiracy theories regarding his death.

Notable Quotes

"Howard Lutnick is a pathological liar who is enabling the most egregious cover-up in American history... he doubled down and tried to say that he was not lying in that podcast." — Rep. Yassamin Ansari (D-AZ)
"I will never be in the room with that disgusting person ever again. So I was never in the room with him socially, for business, or even for philanthropy. If that guy was there, I wasn't going, because he's gross." — Howard Lutnick (Commerce Secretary), *referring to his October 2023 public statement*
"This is a habit of the Democrats on this committee coming out, telling you all stuff that was said that was not said. I hope that when you get the transcripts, you fact check them." — Rep. James Comer (R-KY)
"They investigated me for months, found nothing. It is a treat to be able to choose one’s time to say goodbye. No fun, not worth it." — Jeffrey Epstein (attributed), *from the unsealed 2019 note*
Here on Jeffrey Epstein's private island, a 2012 visit that Lutnick defended later on Capitol Hill. We had lunch on the island. That is true for an hour and we left with all of my children with my nannies and my wife all together. We run family vacation. We were not a part. To suggest there was anything untoward about that in 2012, I don't—I don't recall why we did it. But as for what happened behind closed doors during Howard Lutnick's interview with the Oversight Committee today, which was transcribed but not recorded on video, it depends on who you ask and really what side of the aisle they sit on. If Donald Trump had seen the video transcript, he would have fired Howard Lutnick. It was really embarrassing. I feel very comfortable saying that Howard Lutnick is a pathological liar who is enabling the most egregious cover-up in American history. I feel compelled to have to come out and clean up and correct some of the statements that the Democrats made. I couldn't believe as I was getting updates to what they were saying, and I hope that when you get the transcripts, you fact check them and what they say. This is a habit of the Democrats on this committee coming out, telling you all stuff that was said that was not said. My source tonight was among the lawmakers in the room today, Democratic Congresswoman Yassamin Ansari of Arizona. And thank you for being here. I mean, you heard Chairman Comer there. You said Howard Lutnick is a pathological liar, why? Thanks, Caitlyn. You just showed a clip of Howard Lutnick from the podcast last year where he very forcefully says that he thought Jeffrey Epstein was disgusting and that him and his wife agreed that he would never see him again or have a personal or professional relationship. We grilled him today on that. We went line by line over the transcript from that podcast, and what was so jarring was that the secretary doubled down and tried to say that he was not lying in that podcast. He really was trying to redefine what he meant when he said, you know, I would never have this relationship. He kept saying, "I, as a man, would never again be in a room with Howard Lutnick." So trying to say that he meant by himself. Then, of course, we know that in 2012, he took his entire family to Jeffrey Epstein's island. I then asked him about whether or not he knew about the sweetheart deal in 2008 and the many allegations against Jeffrey Epstein, saying that he was a child abuser. Howard Lutnick tried to tell me that he had never heard of those things back in 2012. He had no idea. To me, as a woman, I was the only female member of Congress who took part in the transcribed interviews today. I found that appalling. I mean, I was a child at the time when this happened and I remember hearing about Jeffrey Epstein and what he was accused of and then, of course, what he got the sweetheart plea deal for, which was still solicitation of a minor. So, I do hope—the only thing I would agree with Chairman Comer on there is that I do hope Americans read the interview and read what happened today. It's very disappointing that they won't be able to see the video like they saw with the Clintons. Did Howard Lutnick say anything else about that visit to Jeffrey Epstein's island? He just repeated the same lines—that it was, you know, very short, that his family was there, his staff was there. The line that he actually repeated—I will have to count, it felt to me like 20 or 30 times—was that the interactions he's ever had with Epstein, the several that he's had, were meaningless and inconsequential. That's what he said repeatedly. But to me, I mean, this all just comes back to the fact that he did lie to the American people, very blatantly. And then today, to try to change the definition of words—I mean, it is so black and white. You said you never saw this person again, you were so disgusted by him in 2005, your wife was so uncomfortable with him, but then you took your wife to his island just seven years later. None of it makes sense. You know, this will be a transcript that we'll see because, or we'll be able to read, Chairman Comer said that's because it was a voluntary interview from Howard Lutnick. But you know, when you're reading something and seeing something, it often sometimes has a different interpretation. Do you think there's anything that people won't be able to glean from the transcript that happened in the room? Yeah, that's absolutely true. I mean, I think the defensiveness, the body language, all of that won't be seen. But I still actually do think that reading the back and forth will be very revealing in and of itself, because it's just so baffling. Like the way he tries to get out—if I were him and all of this had come to light, I don't understand why you don't just admit that you were lying. But he refused to do that and he tried to say he didn't lie. So, I still highly recommend every American who cares about justice for the victims here reads this. I think that the secretary should resign and that we need to actually bring him back again in the future. Some House Democrats are calling for US Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick to resign following his testimony about his ties with Jeffrey Epstein. Howard Lutnick should resign. That was absolutely mind-boggling what we just heard in the room. He was evasive, nervous, and he was dishonest. He would not admit to lying, which he clearly did in the podcast. He said he was never in the room with Jeffrey Epstein ever again after the first time he met him, yet he then admitted that he was in the room with Jeffrey Epstein. I had to ask him whether he and I were in the same room just now, because I couldn't understand his meaning of, "We were in the same room together." Lutnick is currently the highest-ranking Trump administration official prominently named in the Epstein files, outside of the President himself. Lawmakers grilled Lutnick for more than 4 and a half hours behind closed doors on Wednesday about his multiple interactions with Epstein. And that includes a 2012 visit to the convicted sex offender's infamous Caribbean island, just years after he was said to have cut ties with Epstein in 2005. A Commerce spokesperson did say that the secretary did explain repeatedly that he did not have a relationship with Epstein. The White House has so far expressed confidence in Lutnick. Breaking news: a purported suicide note written by Jeffrey Epstein was just released. So here's what we have, you're seeing it on the screen. This note had been kept sealed for years. It was just released by a federal judge in a different case. And what the note says in part, as you see it there, is, "It is a treat to be able to choose one's time to say goodbye." Now, the New York Times reports that Epstein's cellmate says that he discovered this letter after Epstein was found unresponsive with a strip of cloth wrapped around his neck. Now, that was not the incident in which Epstein died. It was an incident that Epstein survived weeks before he was eventually found dead in his cell—a death that was ultimately ruled suicide but has been the focus of endless conspiracy theories. John Miller is out front. And John, what else do we know about this note, which is very eerie to see it in his hand, and why are we finding out about it now? It doesn't seem that it was part of the Epstein files. Okay, so to sort this out for people who aren't students of the case because a lot of this happened a long time ago: he was in the Metropolitan Detention Center, Metro Correctional Center in lower Manhattan, which is now closed, and he was paired with a cellmate named Nick Tartaglione. Tartaglione was a former New York City police officer who was serving time in a corruption case, and he says he found Epstein in the cell with a strip of cloth tied around his neck, and that he found this note inside a novel. Now, why isn't this in the Epstein files? We don't know, but it is in the criminal discovery evidence that was part of Tartaglione's trial, and it was discovered by lawyers in there, and then there was the petition to the judge to release it. Of course, the note says: "They investigated me for months, found nothing. It's a treat to be able to choose one's time to say goodbye. What you want me to do, bust out crying?" So it doesn't particularly say it's a suicide note, but it has that tone. Absolutely does. And as I say, it's fascinating that it was not part of the files, and also as we point out, it was an attempt of suicide that was about a month before when he was actually found dead. But it is very significant to see it in his handwriting and all of that; as I said, eerie. So, the questions that emerge from this are: number one, how do we validate that note? Is it in his handwriting? And if so, who says? Is there DNA or fingerprints or other forensics that can be matched to him to prove that? Let's assume that it is authentic. Does that give us an indication that he was trying to kill himself and waiting for another opportunity? Does that wash away many of the conspiracy theories about how he was found in his cell dead later? There were glitches in videos and stories about people coming and going, all of which have caused controversy but none of which have been proven. Right, right, but all of which are out there and have cast a lot of doubt on it. All right, John Miller, thank you very much. There's breaking news in the Jeffrey Epstein case that has waited nearly 7 years to come out. A purported suicide note has now been found—not found, I should point out, in the millions of pages of documents released to the public today. A federal judge unsealed it. It's brief, it's unsigned, and it reads in part: "They investigated me for months, found nothing. It is a treat to be able to choose one times—one's time—to say goodbye. No fun, not worth it." CNN's Katelyn Polantz is working the story and joins us now. What more do we know about the origins of this note and why it's just now been unsealed? Anderson, this is a note that was found by the cellmate of Jeffrey Epstein in the summer of 2019 after his arrest for sex trafficking as he was awaiting trial. What happened is that he was housed with the cellmate in the MCC in Manhattan and attempted suicide. At that point in time, Epstein survived, but the cellmate, his name is Nicholas Tartaglione, said he found this note in a book in the cell, opened it, saw it, and said Epstein was trying to kill himself. All of that was something we didn't know about widely until about a couple weeks ago, because at some point in time, this cellmate started talking more publicly about finding this note and it got tied up in his own criminal case, something different from Epstein's. And so the New York Times went to court in April and they asked a judge in the Tartaglione criminal case—and this is a man who is serving life sentences for quadruple murder—they asked for more information about Nicholas Tartaglione and to release this note. It came together very quickly. The Justice Department spoke up just two days ago in court saying, "We support transparency around Epstein. We don't have this note authenticated, but if it's in this case, we have no issue releasing it." And a judge ruled today that this note should be released. So it was in the possession of Nicholas Tartaglione, the cellmate of Jeffrey Epstein, in July of 2019, but is purportedly written by Jeffrey Epstein in a suicide attempt before he actually committed suicide and died. A Commerce spokesperson did say that the secretary did explain repeatedly that he did not have a relationship with Epstein. The White House has so far expressed confidence in Lutnick. Breaking news: a purported suicide note written by Jeffrey Epstein just released. So here's what we have, you're seeing it on the screen. This note had been kept sealed for years. It was just released by a federal judge in a different case. And what the note says in part, as you see it there, is "It is a treat to be able to choose one's time to say goodbye." Now, the New York Times reports that Epstein's cellmate says that he discovered this letter after Epstein was found unresponsive with a strip of cloth wrapped around his neck. Now, that was not the incident in which Epstein died. It was an incident that Epstein survived weeks before he was eventually found dead in his cell—a death that was ultimately ruled suicide but has been the focus of endless conspiracy theories. John Miller is out front. And John, what else do we know about this note, which is very eerie to see it in his hand, and why are we finding out about it now? It doesn't seem that it was part of the Epstein files. Okay, so to sort this out for people who aren't students of the case because a lot of this happened a long time ago: he was in the Metropolitan Detention Center, Metro Correctional Center in lower Manhattan, which is now closed. He was paired with a cellmate named Nick Tartaglione, who was a former New York City police officer serving time in a corruption case. He says he found Epstein in the cell with a strip of cloth tied around his neck, and that he found this note inside a novel. Now, why isn't this in the Epstein files? We don't know, but it is in the criminal discovery evidence that was part of Tartaglione's trial, and it was discovered by lawyers in there, and then there was the petition to the judge to release it. Of course, the note says: "They investigated me for months, found nothing. It's a treat to be able to choose one's time to say goodbye. What you want me to do, bust out crying?" So it doesn't particularly say it's a suicide note, but it has that tone. Absolutely does. And as I say, it's fascinating that it was not part of the files, and also as we point out, it was an attempt of suicide that was about a month before when he was actually found dead. But it is very significant to see it in his handwriting and all of that; as I said, eerie. So, the questions that emerge from this are: number one, how do we validate that note? Is it in his handwriting? And if so, who says? Is there DNA or fingerprints or other forensics that can be matched to him to prove that? Let's assume that it is authentic. Does that give us an indication that he was trying to kill himself and waiting for another opportunity? Does that wash away many of the conspiracy theories about how he was found in his cell dead later? There were glitches in videos and stories about people coming and going, all of which have caused controversy but none of which have been proven. Right, right, but all of which are out there and have cast a lot of doubt on it. All right, John Miller, thank you very much. There's breaking news in the Jeffrey Epstein case that has waited nearly 7 years to come out. A purported suicide note now found—not found, I should point out, in the millions of pages of documents released to the public today. A federal judge unsealed it. It's brief, it's unsigned and it reads in part: "They investigated me for months, found nothing. It is a treat to be able to choose one's time to say goodbye. No fun, not worth it." CNN's Katelyn Polantz is working the story and joins us now. What more do we know about the origins of this note and why it's just been now unsealed? Anderson, this is a note that was found by the cellmate of Jeffrey Epstein in the summer of 2019 after his arrest for sex trafficking as he was awaiting trial. What happened is that he was housed with the cellmate in the MCC in Manhattan and attempted suicide. At that point in time, Epstein survived, but the cellmate, his name is Nicholas Tartaglione, said he found this note in a book in the cell, opened it, saw it, and said Epstein was trying to kill himself. All of that was something we didn't know about widely until about a couple weeks ago, because at some point in time, this cellmate started talking more publicly about finding this note and it got tied up in his own criminal case, something different from Epstein's. And so the New York Times went to court in April and they asked a judge in the Tartaglione criminal case—and this is a man who is serving life sentences for quadruple murder—they asked for more information about Nicholas Tartaglione and to release this note. It came together very quickly. The Justice Department spoke up just two days ago in court saying, "We support transparency around Epstein. We don't have this note authenticated, but if it's in this case, we have no issue releasing it." And a judge ruled today that this note should be released. So it was in the possession of Nicholas Tartaglione, the cellmate of Jeffrey Epstein in July of 2019, but is purportedly written by Jeffrey Epstein in a suicide attempt before he actually committed suicide and died. Yeah, Katelyn, thanks very much.

Q&A: Trump's plunging ratings, Vance vs Rubio & Israel without Bibi? | Christiane Amanpour Presents

Christiane Amanpour Presents: The Ex Files(131.0K)·28:25·44.4K views·2026-05-07
Christiane AmanpourHost Jamie RubinCo-host
TL;DRThe Trump administration is attempting to pivot from military escalation in the Middle East toward a face-saving diplomatic "victory" before high-level talks with China, even as the conflict’s ripple effects threaten to push 30 million people into poverty across Africa.

TL;DR

The Trump administration is attempting to pivot from military escalation in the Middle East toward a face-saving diplomatic "victory" before high-level talks with China, even as the conflict’s ripple effects threaten to push 30 million people into poverty across Africa.

U.S.-Iran De-escalation and the China Factor

  • Shift in Strategy: Jamie Rubin notes that the White House is pausing "Operation Freedom" in the Strait of Hormuz to avoid a return to full-scale war. The administration is reportedly seeking a "one-pager" nuclear agreement—a minimum solution that allows President Trump to declare victory and reopen the Straits.
  • The Xi Jinping Meeting: Amanpour suggests the timing is tied to Trump’s upcoming trip to China. Trump likely wants to resolve the Iran "checkmate" to avoid appearing weakened or distracted while negotiating with a "smug" Xi Jinping, who currently positions himself as the "adult in the room."
  • Tactical Deadlock: Despite U.S. efforts to escort ships, the Iranians have returned to "Tanker War" tactics from 40 years ago. Both sides are now looking for a way to exit the conflict while claiming success.

Global Economic Fallout: The "West’s Fault"

  • Impact on Africa: Amanpour highlights a "reputable international NGO" statistic suggesting the war could push 30 million people into poverty. While the West focuses on the military conflict, sub-Saharan Africa faces a "food shock" due to the scarcity of fertilizer and rising fuel prices.
  • U.S. Isolationism: Rubin admits that the knock-on effects in Asia and Africa—such as airline cutbacks and small business failures—are not a high priority for the current administration.
  • Migration and Conflict: The speakers warn that these "push factors" (hunger and economic dislocation) will inevitably lead to mass displacement and resource-driven conflicts, yet the U.S. has significantly weakened USAID and its role as a primary humanitarian donor.

The Future of "Trumpism" and the GOP

  • Radicalized Leadership: Amanpour references analysis by Simon Kuper, categorizing Trump and Putin as "radicalized leaders" who operate in echo chambers, use religious iconography, and rely on violence/war rather than traditional committee-based diplomacy.
  • Rubio vs. Vance: A proxy war is emerging for the 2028 GOP nomination:
      • Marco Rubio (National Security Advisor): Represents the "classic Neocon" interventionist approach and values traditional alliances.
      • JD Vance: Represents isolationism, favoring a scaled-back U.S. global role and skepticism toward allies like Ukraine.
  • Political Vulnerability: Despite Trump’s sway over his base, his economic approval rating has plummeted to approximately 21%, a "catastrophic" number for a president who campaigned on economic strength.

Democratic Prospects and Israeli Leadership

  • Democratic Strategy: Rubin notes that Democrats are increasingly looking toward governors (e.g., Gavin Newsom, Josh Shapiro) for 2028, as they possess executive experience and are perceived as "winners" who can "fight fire with fire."
  • The October Israeli Elections: There is a growing coalition (Lapid, Bennett, Eisenkot) determined to unseat Benjamin Netanyahu. While this opposition is united against Netanyahu, they remain hesitant to explicitly endorse a two-state solution, focusing instead on internal stability and security.
  • The "Pollyanna" Peace View: Amanpour defends her belief in a negotiated settlement with the Palestinian Authority, arguing that the alternative is a "permanent state of war."

Notable Quotes

"This is squarely the West's fault and it's squarely in the hands of the United States and Israel, and they need to fix it for the good of the rest of the world." — Christiane Amanpour
"The administration is trying to basically do what people have suggested they may do for a long time—try to find a way to declare victory, to say they've achieved their objectives, and to try to find a short-form of negotiation, a one-pager." — Jamie Rubin
"People call me Pollyanna for still believing in a negotiated end settlement for peace, and I'm saying, dudes, if it isn't that, you're going to be in a terrible state of war and attack for a long, long time to come." — Christiane Amanpour
This is a Global Player original podcast. Well, this is squarely the West's fault and it's squarely in the hands of the United States and Israel, and they need to fix it for the good of the rest of the world. People call me Pollyanna for still believing in a negotiated end settlement for peace, and I'm saying, dudes, if it isn't that, you're going to be in a terrible state of war and attack for a long, long time to come. The Democrats wanted a winner, and I think that's what they're going to want this time, a winner. We're good on policy, but domestic US politics, i.e., the election of Trump over and over again, has befuddled us. Hello everybody and welcome to the bonus Q&A episode of The Ex Files with me, Christiane Amanpour in London, and Jamie Rubin in New York. Okay, great, so let's get started. This is where we answer your questions and we love to hear from you, so let's get right into it. First question, Greg on email: "Do you think we will reach a point with the Iran War where the rest of the world—particularly European nations and/or China—start to directly apply major pressure on Trump and his administration to reach some kind of more lasting resolution?" Well, I think you should have a stab at it given that it's very bizarre. The last 48 hours, as we're recording, went from escalation—including some hot missile firing or drone firing—to now Trump saying he's pausing his "Operation Freedom" in the Straits of Hormuz. Take it away. I do believe that both sides don't want to go back to war. I do believe that the signals coming out of Washington in the last 48 hours are quite encouraging if you want this war to end. I do believe that the rest of the world is putting pressure on the administration in the subtle ways that they can without infuriating the White House. And I think that, if I understand it correctly, at the request of Pakistan, he stood down on some of the enforcement of the ships that were trying to get out. I'm under the impression that the administration is trying to basically do what people have suggested they may do for a long time: try to find a way to declare victory, to say they've achieved their objectives, and to try to find a short-form of negotiation. They are talking about a one-pager now, according to one of the reports, that would essentially find a minimum solution for the nuclear issue, which would then open up the Strait of Hormuz. I'm not saying this is going to happen tomorrow, but I'm saying that all the signals are that the President doesn't want to go back to war and the Iranians don't want to go back to war. The Strait of Hormuz is their power. They don't want to negotiate the nuclear issue under threat and having gone through this war and survived to an extent. And so they will negotiate the nuclear issue. They may give some concessions, diluting some of the enrichment, perhaps moving some of it, or perhaps repeating that they don't want to make nuclear weapons. That will open the door for the President to declare victory—that he's solved the nuclear problem. He's always been able to do that based on the bombing that he did several months ago, where he said that he eliminated the threat. You mean the June 12-day war? He could have done it right there and then. He could have, but he didn't. And he got led down a path that I don't think the administration is now comfortable with. They can say they've reduced Iran's capabilities; they can say they've reduced their navy, their conventional forces, and their missiles. And then the trick is that the rest of the world is suffering from the Strait of Hormuz being closed. The rest of the world is going to place pressure on the administration. He is going to go to China. China wants the Strait of Hormuz open, but they don't want to be seen as pressuring Iran. They want this thing resolved, I suspect, without seeming like they're pulling America's chestnuts out of the fire. I actually think that this is very linked to the trip to China. I don't think Trump wanted to go and sit opposite Xi Jinping, a major world leader right now who seems to be holding a lot of cards and sitting there in the sort of smug satisfaction of being the adult in the room. Trump obviously needs to make deals with him. Why would Xi give anything—and we still don't know if he will—when this is going on? Because Xi sees this as a big mistake and sees that the United States, in his view, has been somewhat checkmated. So I do think that this is timed to that. Even this latest—I don't know what it's called—Operation Epic Fury? Somebody asked if it is actually "Operation Colossal Blunder." And then Operation Freedom, which was meant to be trying to lead and escort ships out of the Straits, has achieved very, very little; maybe a couple of ships while there are thousands in play. So I think it's an impossible job anyway. The Iranians are going back to their tactics of 40 years ago when the Gulf and the Straits of Hormuz were in play during the Tanker War during the Iran-Iraq war. And both sides, I agree with you, need to declare some kind of victory and move on. So I think definitely that's what's happening. And you can even see from the White House, they're beginning to majorly spin that they're done and they've won. Right. Mandis on YouTube: "I'm South African and I've heard agriculture economists predict massive increases in food prices in, for example, Zambia and other African nations. This is likely to lead to the movement of millions of people within sub-Saharan Africa as a result, all because of the war in the Middle East. Is anybody talking about the potential for displacement, conflict and other major consequences for the world as a result of this war?" Well, it is a good question because as ever, unfortunately, Africa is undercovered by mainstream media in the West. The last quite frightening statistic that we heard—and I can't pull it out of my head but it's from a very reputable international NGO, either the ICRC or some UN body—said that this war, even if it ends now, is going to turf 30 million people into poverty. That was two weeks ago. So this is having very unpredicted and long-term results, not just in the rising fuel prices but in food prices because of the scarcity of much-needed fertilizer. And that's needed right now. The season is now to actually make sure that crops and therefore food are available in a few months. So this is a big issue, particularly for Africa as Mandis points out. I agree that not enough people are talking about it publicly, but I do hope that this will be taken into account. The last time we saw this kind of food shock was during the initial days of the invasion by Russia of Ukraine, Ukraine being the breadbasket of the world. At that time, Putin was able to spin it as Ukraine's fault or the West's fault. Well, this is squarely the West's fault and it's squarely in the hands of the United States and Israel and they need to fix it for the good of the rest of the world. I think you're absolutely right. The knock-on effects of this war across the world have not been given enough attention, nor, I'm sorry to say, is it a matter of high concern to my administration. Normally, as America as a leader of the world, we would take into account the views of Asians and Africans and Europeans who were suffering from this war, whether it's fuel prices or fertilizer prices. Airlines are cutting back so workers can't travel around to get jobs done. The fuel has affected small businesses all over the world, so there have been amazing knock-on effects. It doesn't get the attention it deserves. I've read some about it, and I'm sure you've read some about it. It's a tragedy that America, as a country that used to care and was the largest donor of food, medicine, and assistance to African countries, is no longer seeing itself in that role. That's a shame, but that's just a reality right now. But also, that compounded with the destruction of USAID is just adding injury upon injury. The question was whether it would lead to mass movement and displacement or even conflict. Honestly, no option is off the table. It is very possible that if it gets really bad, there will always be some kind of conflict over scarce resources, particularly when they are food—the basic elements of survival. So we'll see. Displacement is already a massive issue in the West because of climate change and other such things. Not enough emphasis, even before this war, was put on the push factors—trying to keep people in their own countries and give them a decent quality of life. And their quality of life is being seriously compromised now with this war. All right, Jamie, do you want to read the next one? Yes, I do. Bari-Ann, I live in the UK, asking this on email: "And what worries me about Trump is not so much him as an individual, but that so many millions of Americans voted him in. Do you worry that even when Trump eventually goes, those people will remain with the same attitudes and that they will elect more candidates like him?" Let me start on that as an American. Yes, you should. I've said this before; I'll say it again. I've been surprised at so many Americans being willing to vote for a president given all the obvious facts about him that we know. It's been a matter of trouble to me. In the first term at least, he had a series of advisors that were considered serious men who tried to ameliorate or modify some of his instincts. In this administration—the second Trump 2.0—that hasn't been true. I do think his coalition is not what it was, and I do think that Trump is a unique figure in American politics. For whatever reason, American politics has always had a soft spot for populists, for people who gather a populist mentality. And there was a lot of anger in our country after the long years of the 2008 economic crisis and the feeling that the elites were getting an advantage. Why they ended up voting for an elitist, I don't know, considering that elites are the biggest beneficiaries of this administration in large respects so far. So I don't believe that Trump is a phenomenon that will easily translate down the line. He will have changed the Republican Party, though. He will have changed who the Republican Party is because he's forced them to change views on everything from free trade to the use of military power—although now he seems to have switched back a little bit to the use of military power. But Vance and Rubio, his two potential successors, are going to fight this out. I don't think anyone will have the same charisma as Donald Trump has, for better or worse—the same ability to change the subject, to keep the attention on himself, to be inoculated from any criticism or mistakes. I don't believe that other candidates will have that same power. I think we'll know a lot more about what the questioner asks after these midterm elections when we see what happens to the Republican Party despite Trump leading it. There've been a lot of signs that the Democratic Party is set for some wins around the country, but I don't think we can really answer it well until after November. I agree. I mean, look, people are pointing out that this primary situation in Indiana shows that Trump still holds huge sway over his base. All the Republican candidates for the primaries that he backed are winning, so that's important. And I think you're right; what does that mean in the midterm elections which are incredibly important? Trump's popularity is really sinking. I think we discussed this week that on the economy, his popularity or approval is somewhere around 21%. Now, that is catastrophic for a man who actually won based on what people think about his approach to the economy. So they are hurting. In America, as the famous James Carville, Democratic strategist, said all those decades ago: "It is the economy, stupid," and it remains the economy, stupid. There will be people who really dislike his breaking of a promise of non-intervention, no more forever wars, etc., which is probably why he's hurrying to try to wrap this one up. I still think the jury is out. I think that he's under a huge amount of influence from the Israeli government, from certain donors who believe that now is the time to cripple Iran, and who knows whether he will declare victory and go home. So we'll see. But I think the people of the United States have shown, certainly in the last round of off-year elections, that they are not pleased with what his 2.0 has done for them. They voted for Democratic candidates in the off-year elections just in the last November. So we'll see. And the question really is: will there be another one like him? You say, and you're right, he's a unique character. I've just been reading Simon Kuper in the FT talking about radicalized leaders. He names Trump and Putin as radicalized leaders—people who operate in their own echo chamber based on the support they get from their base. These are leaders who use religious iconography, like Trump does and Putin has for a long time, and who use violence. I mean, Putin uses violence, i.e., wars. Trump is now using violence, i.e., wars. And for him, it's a breach of the trust that he had with the people when he ran. So we will see. It's going to be interesting to see how these individuals, who are single-handedly making decisions on war and peace which affect the whole global economy, move forward. It used to be, and Jamie, you know this, it was more of a committee, a consensus. It wasn't just the president deciding to go to war; he talked to stakeholders and allies. This president does not do that. So I do think we will have to see what happens and who will be able to follow him in a 2028 election and who will be chosen. The money now is on Rubio, but hey, two years is a long time to go from now, and we will see. So this is Christiane, very similar to me: "Would you please talk about the competition between JD Vance and Marco Rubio to be the next Republican Presidential candidate? And how confident are you that California Governor Gavin Newsom will be the Democratic candidate?" So I'm going to take it first because we just were talking about it. It was not unnoticed that Marco Rubio took to the podium in the White House. He is obviously National Security Advisor as well, and he has an office apparently—spends more time in the White House than at the State Department. Nonetheless, he went in front of the cameras to talk about President Trump moving away from Operation Freedom and saying that we have some new offers from the Iranians that we want to consider. Everybody has been saying that Marco Rubio is much more overtly with President Trump on this issue, and also on Venezuela and Cuba. He is the classic Neocon, whereas JD Vance is not. However, I think the jury is still out. If this thing goes completely pear-shaped, JD Vance will be able to say, "See, I told you I was never for this war." So I don't know; I think it's way too early. As for Gavin Newsom, I honestly can't weigh in on that because again, it's way too early. He definitely wants it; he's meeting the Fox News Republicans where they live and fighting back. But others are saying even Kamala Harris hasn't ruled out a run for president, whereas some Democrats say she should be considering a run for the governor of California. What do you think, Jamie? Well, political analysis has never been my strongest suit, as you know. I've got it wrong before; I'll get it wrong again. We're different than policy analysis people—we're good on policy, but domestic US politics, i.e., the election of Trump over and over again, has befuddled us. It has. And the only thing I can really say about the Democratic side where I know it better is that governors tend to make better presidents. There are governors from Illinois, from Maryland, from Pennsylvania, from California. Governors make great presidents because they understand the executive function. They understand how to negotiate with legislatures. And they understand in our system the importance of getting things done because governors have to get things done. And so I tend as a rule to find governors to be attractive candidates, whether that's Governor Newsom or one of these many other governors that people are talking about. I don't know. Governor Newsom, as you said, clearly wants it. He's clearly showing it; he's written a book, he's trying to mix it up with the President and the Fox voters, and he has his own podcast. So, you know, we'll see. It's very, very early. People didn't think that Joe Biden was going to be the nominee at the end, and he was chosen because the voters decided they wanted someone to beat Donald Trump back in 2020. I think the Democrats have shown through this Virginia redistricting that they really, really want to play hardball this time. They want to win. But you know the courts—one of the courts has suspended that vote for the moment. All of that redistricting is going to be played out in the courts. The point I'm only making is that it was an unusual thing for the Democrats to be as aggressive as they were to say, "We're going to fight fire with fire." That's what I believe will happen in the fall and in the 2028 election. Democrats, much like back when I started in politics in 1992 after 12 years of Republican rule, wanted a winner. And I think that's what they're going to want this time—a winner. Yeah, who is a winner? So look, Jamie, this is kind of related obviously. Andrew on BlueSky: "What do you think U.S. foreign policy under JD Vance would look like?" Well, if you believe everything he's said up till now until this war, you know, it's going to be a scaling back of America's role in the world. It would be an unwillingness to use force to solve problems and a desire to see Europeans change and take care of their own problems. I'm not entirely clear about what he thinks about China. I'd like to think that any president would understand the threat from China that we all face because China is zooming ahead. They are becoming a peer competitor, as it's called. They are really spending enormous amounts of money to do that. I'd like to think that any president would realize that our advantage over China is to have allies. Unfortunately, Mr. Vance—Vice President Vance—has not seen the building of alliances as part of his job. Neither has the President. I hope that changes. I think Marco Rubio seems to understand the value of alliances more. And I hope and expect that by the time this is all played out, we will understand that our alliances are what make us strong and that we won't have done too much damage to them. I hope so too. And one can never forget JD Vance coming to Europe and berating his allies at the Munich Security Conference a couple of years ago, and then taking part in the deriding of Volodymyr Zelensky—an ally and President of Ukraine under illegal aggression—in the White House. I'm hoping that, as you say, the value of alliances and some kind of world order will translate to anybody seeking the presidency of the United States. Otherwise, I do think, Jamie, it will be a moment of the United States losing its footing as the dominant power in the world. I really do. And I think Trump will be treated to quite a spectacle when he goes to China. I'm sure Xi will greet him with all the flourishes of a rising superpower, and that will be quite revealing and sobering. Let us ask the final question, Sylvie on Instagram. Do you want to ask that? I think I asked Andrew. Yes, and I'll give it a whirl: "What are your predictions for what will happen in Israel's elections this October?" Well, this is a subject of interest to me. I've been talking to a lot of people about it. Look, I think the leading politicians in Israel outside of the so-called base of Bibi Netanyahu really want to see a change. That's why there's a coalition developing between Lapid and Bennett—two prominent politicians determined to see a coalition develop. Also, Gadi Eisenkot, a former general, may eventually join their coalition. I think there's a strong feeling that after October 7th and all the internal wrangling, it's time for new leadership. I think there's obviously a strong percentage who want that. Whether these three can get together, or whether some other formulation will come together, the Israeli political system is so complicated it's very hard to see how it all works out. But I have to say I'm hopeful that it's time for new leadership. Israel needs leadership that can help the region come to grips with the chaos that has been wrought. Israel is so good at its military intelligence operations but not so good at putting those military intelligence successes together with diplomacy that achieves results that are of long-term benefit to Israel. And yes, talking about Naftali Bennett and Yair Lapid, they've both put out statements—or at least Bennett did—basically saying, and maybe this can be just put down to election campaigning, but they don't talk about a Palestinian state. Quite the reverse. You know what's going on on the West Bank: stealth annexation. They are not willing to come out right now before the election and say they agree with a Palestinian state. And the others on Bibi's side are basically still talking about wanting to occupy great tranches of other countries' territory in order to make a security zone. So this battle for Israel's projection of power is very strong. Will it be a constant "mowing the lawn" group of leaders who are constantly committed to permanent war in the name of security? And then as you say, inside, many people are fighting, like Holocaust survivor Colette Avital, who's a human rights and democracy activist. They are committed to a two-state solution, believing that this is the only way Israel gets a much more permanent and sure future of security. So we'll see. I think it's very important, and don't forget it's very difficult to know who's going to win. Last time Netanyahu did not win, but he was able to cobble together this coalition of extremists. Okay, and by the way, when I say "two-state solution," I mean—if anybody had any doubts—that I am not talking about Hamas being the Palestinian interlocutor. I'm talking about the recognized Palestinian Authority who recognizes Israel and who's in the Oslo peace process. I think we all knew that. I hope so too, because you never know. People get all bent out of shape. People call me Pollyanna for still believing in a negotiated end settlement for peace, and I'm saying, dudes, if it isn't that, you're going to be in a terrible state of war and attack for a long, long time to come. People have said that about Ireland as well; until it happened, it doesn't happen. Exactly, exactly. Right, on that very happy and hopeful note, thank you all for listening to this Q&A bonus episode of The Ex Files. If you have questions for us that you want us to answer, don't forget you can contact us via email or on our socials. Find us; the handle is @amanpourpod or email us at amanpourpod@global.com. And our next episode is out on Tuesday. Remember you can always listen for free on Global Player and don't forget to subscribe to our YouTube channel so that you can see everything. That is at "Christiane Amanpour Presents The Ex Files." Sorry, had to read where it is, but that's where it is and you can see all our episodes there, okay? Bye-bye from London for now. Bye from New York. This has been a Global Player original production.

Tech-Elite und Trump: An diesen Stellen bröckelt das Zweckbündnis – mit Felix Holtermann

Handelsblatt(164.0K)·47:41·4.7K views·2026-05-07
Felix HoltermannJournalist, Handelsblatt
TL;DRThe strategic alliance between Donald Trump and the Silicon Valley elite is fracturing as the administration moves to centralize AI regulation and control, signaling a shift where the President may soon need the tech industry more than they need him.
From Social Media to...

TL;DR

The strategic alliance between Donald Trump and the Silicon Valley elite is fracturing as the administration moves to centralize AI regulation and control, signaling a shift where the President may soon need the tech industry more than they need him.

The New Hierarchy of Tech Influence

  • From Social Media to AI: The "Tech Oligarchy"—a group of roughly 20 powerful men—has shifted its focus from social media platforms to AI and data analytics firms like Palantir and OpenAI.
  • The Peter Thiel Pipeline: Peter Thiel remains the most influential tech figure in the White House due to his mentorship of Vice President JD Vance.
      • Direct Access: Insiders claim that "if Peter calls, JD answers," giving Thiel an unprecedented direct line to the administration.
  • Political Financing: The newly formed "Leading the Future" Super-PAC, backed by figures like Greg Brockman (OpenAI) and Mark Andreesen, has secured over $100 million to support candidates favoring light-touch AI regulation.

Rising Frictions in the "Zweckbündnis" (Marriage of Convenience)

  • The AI Regulation U-Turn: Despite early promises of total deregulation, the Trump administration is now planning a "Center for AI Standards and Innovation."
      • Centralized Power: Trump aims to prevent individual states (like Colorado) or governors from setting AI rules, insisting that he alone should dictate the future of the technology.
      • Pre-Market Testing: This new body mirrors the Biden-era institute that Trump initially abolished, requiring models to be vetted before public release.
  • The Anthropic Conflict: Tensions peaked when Trump labeled Anthropic (creators of the Claude chatbot) as "radically left and woke" on Truth Social.
      • Military Usage: Friction arose after Anthropic reportedly restricted AI models from certain self-sustaining weapon systems, leading to threats from Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth to "outlist" the company as an unreliable supplier.
  • Labor and Immigration: Tech leaders remain frustrated by strict visa regulations for high-skilled workers, which conflicts with Silicon Valley's need for global talent.

Strategic Synergies and Shared Enemies

  • Anti-EU Alignment: Both the Trump administration and Silicon Valley are united against European "over-regulation."
      • Defensive Stance: Trump views EU digital regulations as an attack on American exports and has signaled he will shield U.S. tech giants from Brussels' oversight.
  • Domestic Economic Wins: The tech sector continues to benefit from Trump’s corporate tax cuts and federal support for massive data center expansion.
  • Global Competition: The "China Threat" remains the primary glue holding the alliance together, with both parties viewing US-led AI as a national security imperative.

Ideological Extremes and the 2028 Outlook

  • Radical Governance Models: Within elite circles, there is active discussion of extreme political shifts, including "Tech-Fascism" or a "Late-Roman Military Dictatorship."
      • Efficiency over Democracy: Some tech thinkers argue the state should be run like a corporation by a powerful CEO-manager, utilizing AI to monitor citizens' needs and bypass traditional elections.
  • JD Vance as the Tech Heir: While Trump’s personal relationship with tech is volatile, the industry is increasingly looking toward 2028, viewing JD Vance as their primary vessel for long-term influence.
  • Power Dynamics: Experts suggest a shift in leverage; as AI becomes indispensable for modern warfare and administration, Trump is becoming more reliant on the very "Oligarchs" he seeks to control.

Notable Quotes

"If Peter [Thiel] calls, JD [Vance] answers." — Felix Holtermann (Quoting an industry insider)
"When it comes to it, exactly one person in the USA should make the rules for the AI future, and that man is Donald Trump—not individual entrepreneurs or governors." — Felix Holtermann
"I believe a problem for Trump will eventually be that he needs the tech industry more than the tech industry needs him." — Christian Lemmer
Wenn, dann soll genau ein einziger in den USA die Regeln für die KI-Zukunft machen und der Mann, der ist Donald Trump. Das sind nicht einzelne Unternehmer und das sind nicht einzelne Gouverneure. Und ich glaube, dass eventuell ein Problem für Trump sein wird, dass die Tech-Industrie langsam mehr braucht als die Tech-Industrie ihn. KI scheint zu sein, sehr hart. Es scheint zu sein das Ding, dass eine lot of smart people are looking at very strongly. Unsere Country will be prospering like never before. I think that’s true and it's going to be the Golden Age of America. Das hat Donald Trump zum Amtsantritt im Januar 2025 gesagt. Ein goldenes Zeitalter in den USA, dank der Tech-Branche. Drei der Technologiemilliardäre, die standen da direkt neben ihm. Kein Präsident hat sich so oft und so nahe mit Tech-Unternehmensführern gezeigt wie Donald Trump. Und während in der ersten Amtszeit viele gegen ihn waren, hat sich das Bild mittlerweile komplett gedreht. Und deshalb wollen wir in dieser Woche schauen, wie weit der politische Einfluss der Tech-Unternehmen geht und wie sehr sie von der Trump-Regierung profitieren. Und wir analysieren aber auch, wie sich in diesem Bündnis zunehmend Konflikte aufgetan haben und auftun. Dazu sprechen wir gleich mit Felix Holtermann, der für das Handelsblatt aus dem Silicon Valley berichtet. Und damit willkommen bei Trump Watch, dem Analyse-Podcast des Handelsblatts zur zweiten Amtszeit des US-Präsidenten. Ich bin Nicole Bastian, ich leite beim Handelsblatt das Ressort Podcast und Live-Journalismus. Und ich bin Christian Lemmer, Politikwissenschaftler vom John F. Kennedy-Institut der FU Berlin. Heute ist Donnerstag, der 7. Mai, Tag 473 der Trump-Administration. Schön, dass ihr uns zuhört. Die Trump News der Woche. Ja, Christian, wir fangen wieder mit dem Thema an, über das wir seit mehr als zwei Monaten wöchentlich sprechen, nämlich dem Krieg der USA im Iran. Wir nehmen auf, das muss man jetzt mal sagen, Donnerstag, 10 Uhr, weil wir nicht wissen, was sich danach in den nächsten Stunden noch alles so verändern wird. Stand derzeit ist, dass Donald Trump sagt, dass eine Einigung zwischen den USA und dem Iran wahrscheinlich ist. Wir haben das allerdings in den letzten Tagen immer mal wieder gehört. Man muss jetzt aber sehen, dass die Finanzmärkte auf diese Ankündigung reagiert haben. Die Ölpreise, sowohl der Sorte WTI als auch der Sorte Brent, die sind auf unter 100 Dollar gefallen. Das heißt, es ist schon, man sieht an den Finanzmärkten, es ist eine klare Hoffnung darauf, dass dieser Konflikt auf die eine oder andere Art und Weise endet oder zumindest ruht und damit eventuell die Straße von Hormus wieder frei wird. Ja, das zumindest die Hoffnung. Ich bin nicht ganz so optimistisch, weil wir hören, Iran hat einen Plan vorgelegt, die USA hat auch einen Plan vorgelegt. Das sind viele Punkte. Man weiß gar nicht, wer eigentlich momentan miteinander spricht, wie die Gespräche genau verlaufen. Wir haben ja auch wieder gesehen, Trump hat dann vor zwei Tagen, glaube ich, angefangen Schiffen Geleitschutz zu geben, damit die durch die Straße von Hormus fahren können. Das hat er jetzt gestern schon wieder zurückgenommen. Das passiert momentan nicht. Also, es gibt immer noch kein klares Bild, was da eigentlich im Iran passiert und wie stark auch das US-Militär vom iranischen Militär getroffen worden ist. Auch da gibt es ganz unterschiedliche Aussagen. Ja, die Washington Post hat einen Bericht veröffentlicht, wonach deutlich mehr US-Ziele getroffen worden sein sollen, als die USA bisher offengelegt haben. Und das sollen wohl Satellitenbilder klar darlegen. Es ist noch immer viel Unsicherheit drin. Die Folgen allerdings, die haben wir nicht nur auf der wirtschaftlichen Ebene, sondern die haben wir im Moment auch ganz klar in der Außenpolitik. Nachdem nämlich Friedrich Merz, der deutsche Bundeskanzler, dem US-Präsidenten vorgeworfen habe, er habe ja gar keine Strategie im Krieg mit dem Iran, hat Donald Trump reagiert.
