Instead, it's creating custom emails, bespoke automations, personalized insights, and more for you and your business. The true power of Active Intelligence lies in that it gets to know you, your goals, and your ways of working. In our evolution from marketing automation to autonomous marketing, we are building Active Intelligence to be a trusted strategic advisor and an extension of your team. You'll hear more about our AI personalization shortly from Mamie in our roadmap demo and discussion. Now, let's hear directly from two of our customers how Active Intelligence has made their marketing unstoppable. Hi everyone, I'm Jackie Palmer. I'm here today with two of our amazing customers, and you're going to hear directly from them on how Active Intelligence has made their marketing unstoppable, from speeding up workflows to creating campaigns and automations to surfacing helpful insights. We've got some amazing customers in the house with us today: Chris Clark from Toss and Spin, and Gary Spagnoli from Analytics Mates. We're so happy to have you both here for this discussion. Alright, Chris is the founder and CEO of Toss and Spin, which is the racket sports brand experiences agency of the future and a turnkey partner for brands entering racket sports culture. They've also recently expanded into physical spaces built to drive participation, community, and brand integration under one roof, namely a brand new indoor facility featuring tennis, pickleball, turf fields, and multi-sport courts. Chris has been an ActiveCampaign customer for about a year now. Welcome, Chris. Excited to be here. Thanks for having me. And then Gary is the founder and CEO of both Zen Anchor and Analytics Mates. With Zen Anchor, he and his team provide digital marketing services to support SEO, pay-per-click, and performance-driven strategies for businesses that rely on consistent high-quality demand generation. His years of experience at Zen Anchor have led him to start Analytics Mates, where his focus is on building AI-driven marketing analytics systems that help businesses understand their data and make better decisions. The Helm, his AI analytics co-pilot product, combines analytics expertise with AI automation to turn chaotic Google Analytics 4 data into clear insights, faster implementation, and better outcomes. Gary has been an ActiveCampaign customer and partner for over 10 years. Welcome, Gary. Hey, thank you for having me. Alright, let's get into our discussion. I'm gonna start with an easy one. What was the first thing that you asked Active Intelligence? How do we make more money through our campaigns? That was the funny joke, but then we drove down. But did it actually answer you? It did. It did. It did. But I think the cool thing about Active Intelligence is making sure you drill down on a specific ask to get the result that you really want. What we really asked it was to pull out three things that can make our campaign stand out, but the biggest thing we picked out was relevance versus sending large database email blasts. The reason being is because super targeted emails are specific to our customers. We have higher click-through rate on those emails and ultimately we're going to sell more. Large database blasts that are not relevant do not do as well from a click-through perspective, from open rate, you name it. So our team is really focused on the time of day we send campaigns, as well as being super targeted with our messaging to get the best bang for our buck. Excellent, excellent. Gary, how about you? What was the first thing that you asked ActiveCampaign or Active Intelligence? I remember the first thing was similar to you. I didn't directly ask for revenue. I had asked for what is the best performing campaign over the last year. And we started with analyzing the top performers, at least at the open and click rate level, based off of the ActiveCampaign data. We have been running campaigns for years, and so there was quite a bit to do analysis around. I remember it gave me some campaigns, and the very next follow-up was literally just "why?" Why are these the top performers in your view? At that point, it gave me recommendations around the messaging types, the subject lines, time of day, and the target segments. Once you start to get that type of analysis, it really helped in the follow-up questions of, "Okay, well which of these target segments do you think make the most sense for my campaigns coming up this month? Which of these messaging positions may be the best subject that my content team should focus on for the rest of this month?" So I remember doing the analysis for a full year worth of data, at least initially. Since then we've evolved in very different ways, but the value really came from the "why." It started to understand what messaging and which audiences do well. That actually informed a lot of marketing strategies that even live outside of email marketing. Yeah. I mean I've been an ActiveCampaign customer for about a year. This project, what we're doing now, was an internship project last year. And I would say the biggest thing is by the time they finished the project, nothing was really relevant because that's how fast things move. Now we're able to make real-time decisions with real-time data. So I can go fast, I can challenge our team to go fast, but they can go fast without guessing. They have the data, they know what to do, and it helps everything operate very, very efficiently based on relevance and the metrics. We're looking at 71% of the emails that are targeted; those emails get opened as opposed to sending large database blasts, which has a 46% open rate. So we're able to pull that data and really think about what messaging we want to send. I know Gary lives in a neighborhood in Chicago, so we're gonna target that neighborhood and target that park and use trigger words that Active Intelligence tells us to use to hopefully get him out onto the tennis or pickleball court. Chris is gonna have us both playing pickleball. I would love to, let's go. Yeah. So, time save we talk about, but you now just brought up a different result that you're seeing: the increase in open rates with the AI-driven campaigns. Gary, are you seeing an increase in open rates? Or what other kinds of results—revenue, number of campaigns out the door—what are some of the other results that you're seeing? Yeah, so I mean our open rate has improved. We were originally at around 33% for our newsletter, now we're floating between 40 to 45% consistently. And this is a result of the time-of-day optimization, but also subject line optimization. With our product, The Helm, which is our AI agentic platform, the newsletter is used for education. We're trying to educate and just provide value. So we monitor the blog post traffic and how many clicks we're getting, and our click rate has also improved now from the blog post to actual signup to register for the product. That has all improved since the beginning of this year. Every week we're sending out new campaigns and new communications that continue to get better and better. I've seen not just from our newsletter but all of our email marketing communications, we are getting smarter from the analysis and we're seeing the results on our open rate and our click rate. It's lifting the site and the brand as a whole, but also driving more users for our product. Yeah. And Chris, I know you had something just recently where you drove a lot of people coming to your event, right? So you were actually driving an increase in attendance as well. Is that right? Yeah, so Hyde Park is a new area for us. When we opened on November 1st, we had a database of less than a hundred people. Now we have a database of 10,000 people with over a hundred automations coming directly from ActiveCampaign. I always say the more we do, the smarter it gets, the more data it can analyze, and the faster we can go. We've had a couple instances, and I always make this joke: whatever you do on the back end, don't touch Active Intelligence, don't touch ActiveCampaign, because it's getting better and better. Now we're expanding back outside. So we're doing automations for indoors and outdoors, we're doing upsells, downsells, what's working, and what's not. Then we're doubling down on those campaigns that are working, and it's happening in real time. We can move very, very quickly. As a startup, it's so important that you're able to have these tools and move very, very quickly. The longer you take, I mean, time will pass you by. So. Yeah. So unfortunately we are coming up on our time. I've got one last question that I always like to ask customers, and we've got really good customers here because we've got a long-standing 10-year plus customer and a somewhat newer customer. New. Right. What advice would you give to somebody just starting out with Active Intelligence and their ActiveCampaign instance? I would first start by segmenting your data on the front end and then just ask Active Intelligence, almost like it's a person, what I can do to grow my business. If you start with those questions, it will give you the blueprint on what to do. Like I said, a year ago we were hiring two to three people to help us figure this out. Now we have one person running the system to inform the team on what to do and what's working. Quite honestly, if we look at this year versus last year, February on record was our best month we've had. It's a 10x increase from the previous February. So there is a lot of—I attribute that a lot to the good people we have on our team, but how they're running and using our systems and how, like I said, the car is, we're building it together. It's not just me or someone else. Yeah, I think one of the biggest pieces of value that Active Intelligence offers today is that contextual history from your own internal data. I think there's three steps that I would go through for any sort of email platform. It aligns with some of the segmentation that Chris is describing. The first would be research, the second would be design, the third would be test and deploy, especially if you're early because it's less risky if you're first testing things out. On the research front, what I would do is look at both internal and external data sources. On the external side, you could use other AI tools or you could do your own market research to understand, "Okay, well what are the types of email messaging that I should be generating? What type of marketing communications do I need?" On the internal side, you have to look at your own existing data. The way I would incorporate Active Intelligence into this is you ask the questions of, "Well, what are the benchmarks here?" If you go to Active Intelligence then and say, "Okay, here's some data I have, here's some research I've put together. What do you recommend?" Then how do you structure this? What type of standard email campaigns should I have in place? What type of standard automations just to have the foundation in place? The second piece then would be to start designing, and I would ask Active Intelligence to actually develop at least the initial templates. What's the basic automation? What's the first campaign that I should have in place? Once you start designing and testing things out, the third is deploy. You actually have to run the campaign and start to evaluate how effective it can ultimately be. I think that as you use the product more, the better it gets. I think it's a critical component of ActiveCampaign and Active Intelligence. Anything that has direct first-party access to the data will always perform better than you going to any tool and asking questions, because those just give you external research. You really want to be using your own data to be making decisions. Absolutely. Absolutely. That was amazing. Thank you Chris and Gary for your thoughtful comments and your helpful advice. I hope all of you watching enjoyed that conversation as much as I did. Let's keep it going. In our newly updated ActiveCampaign community, once you sign up, you'll get access to some amazing resources, including our new prompt library to help inspire you with new ways to use Active Intelligence. You'll get exclusive access to autonomous marketing expertise, peer networks, and cutting-edge tools to help you drive measurable business results. Marketing is changing faster than ever. New tools, new tactics, new wins to unlock, but you don't have to figure it out on your own. Let's build the ActiveCampaign community together. See how other teams are using Active Intelligence to do more with less. Get real results from real marketers in the insights that actually move the needle. Get answers from people who've already solved your problem. No more searching for hours. Every answer is in one place. Tell us what features would make your job easier. Help build the tools you use and see what's actually possible with AI right now. Practical examples, not just hype. Marketing's moving fast, but the ActiveCampaign community is here to keep you ahead—your go-to hub for big ideas and real answers. community.activecampaign.com. Hi, I'm Mamie and I work on the product here at ActiveCampaign. Let's take a look at the next chapter of autonomous marketing: deeper intelligence, smarter decisions, faster results. We'll start on the ActiveCampaign homepage. Every person on a team has a different job, a different set of decisions to make every morning, and a different definition of what matters. So, the dashboard doesn't show everyone the same thing. A marketing director sees campaign performance across a full portfolio and where revenue is trending against a goal. An email specialist sees deliverability, open rates, and which sends need attention before the day starts. A sales rep sees which leads just engaged, which deals have gone quiet, and where to focus first. This is Maya. She is the marketing manager at Compass Creative, a boutique creative agency scaling their referral program from 30% of new business to 50%. Maya logs in Monday morning, and this is what Active Intelligence has been building for her overnight. Three outcomes: a campaign was optimized and relaunched, a new high-value segment was discovered, and performance patterns were identified with some changes already applied. These are autonomous interactions: AI working in the background and surfacing outcomes when they're ready for you. Not alerts, not tasks, but outcomes. Let me show you how Maya taught Active Intelligence to work for Compass Creative, because quality outcomes start with context. Before Maya configures anything, Active Intelligence already knows something about Compass Creative. Not because it was told, but because it was watching. It noticed that every time Maya reviewed a campaign, she softened the call-to-action. "Refer a friend" became "introduce them." "Schedule a call" became "let's talk." Invitation language over directive commands every time without exception. It noticed that when AI recommended expanding SMS to 89 contacts, Maya actually narrowed it to 67. Her note: "we want deeper relationships, not broader reach." Depth over scale, relationship history over engagement metrics. It noticed that she changed brand trends to "where brand strategy is heading." That strategic framing: direction over evolution every time. This is memory—AI learning, not from what Maya says, but from how she decides—and it compounds. The more Maya works with Active Intelligence, the more it understands not just Compass Creative's business, but how Maya thinks. Memory captures what AI observes; custom instructions are what Maya taught it directly. There are four areas: how they work, who they serve, how they communicate, and what makes them unique. So the working preferences, like "always confirm details before creating anything," "ask clarifying questions," "understand the creative challenge first," and "collaboration over assumptions." This is how AI works with every person at Compass Creative, and it solves the intent gap. Even when prompts are vague, AI knows enough to ask the right questions. Target audience: small businesses seeking creative partnership. Many have been burned by vendor relationships before, and they value that collaborative process and strategic thinking. Every recommendation that AI makes now considers who Compass Creative serves. Then there's the content guidelines, like "inspiring yet approachable tone," "use creative partnership, never vendor," "keep emails under 200 words," and "always lead with creative process and thinking before mentioning services or pricing." That is core to how Compass Creative differentiates. And additional context: like being a boutique agency of eight people. They compete against larger agencies by offering strategic partnership. Their differentiator is a collaborative creative process, and their current priority is scaling their referral program from 30 to 50% of new business. Now AI knows the business challenge that they're solving. So there are two inputs: what AI learned from behavior and what Maya chose to share directly. Together, they define how Active Intelligence works for Compass Creative: what it looks for, how it communicates, and what it prioritizes when it has to make a call. Maya set this up once, and AI has been building on it ever since. Now let's see what this looks like in practice. Here's what happened. The client showcase campaign performance dropped Sunday evening. Engagement was down from 38% to 22%. AI analyzed 247 client responses and found that they were asking about their own creative challenges, not engaging with the client showcase message. So AI revised the approach. Instead of "our latest work," it was repositioned as "see what we've been making." It set up an AB test scheduled for Monday morning. Look at the before and after. And here's where personalization really matters. AI remembered Maya's content guideline. It led with creative process before services, it used transformation language, invitation tone, "introduce" instead of "refer," and partnership language that wasn't transactional. This isn't generic AI writing. And notice AI didn't need explicit instructions on this specific email. It understood Compass Creative's context from the custom instructions, which means that even when a prompt is vague, AI knows enough about the business to make intelligent decisions. This solves the intent gap that makes most AI tools frustrating. Maya reviews it and makes one small change to the subject line, shifting "see what we've been making" to "our latest showreel is live." It's more aligned with their positioning. Three minutes of review deployed at 8 AM, and by noon engagement recovered to 41%. 23 introductions are already coming in, and AI will monitor the AB test and report back Wednesday. What used to take until Wednesday to diagnose and fix happened overnight. Maya spent three minutes reviewing instead of three hours rewriting. Now watch what happens. AI optimized that campaign, but it's also monitoring the broader referral program. Overnight, while Maya was reviewing that email, AI was analyzing 18 months of data across all 50,000 contacts looking for patterns. It found something valuable. Here's what Active Intelligence discovered: a segment of 284 contacts who are your highest value advocates. These are past clients who have each made two or more introductions to other brands. Average value per introduction: $47,000. But you're treating them like cold leads. Active Intelligence analyzed 18 months of behavior and found that they're not responding to the promotional outreach. They engage with craft and process content. They want a creative relationship, not a campaign. Look at what this segment is actually worth. Each introduction averages $47,000, but here's what makes them different: 68% of their introductions convert to clients. That's not a referral program; that's a revenue channel. And it compounds. The average lifetime value of a single creative advocate is $94,000, and there are 284 contacts. Active Intelligence profiled exactly what they engage with: case studies, behind-the-scenes process, and strategic perspectives. There is 62% engagement on thought leadership, 31% on creative process content, and 18% on promotional emails. They don't want referral asks. They want content that's worth sharing with peers. That right Nurture Track—Monthly High-value Craft-led—is what turns a past client into a $94,000 partner. First, Active Intelligence finds the segment. And then, more importantly, it tells Maya what to do with it. One recommendation: send a one-time campaign to creative advocates this week, a behind-the-scenes look at a recent project. There's no referral, no promotional angle, just craft content to a group that's proven that they engage with it. This is the highest leverage move available right now. It re-establishes a creative relationship before anything else and it costs Maya 20 minutes. So there are three ways to act: you can save the segment and come back to it, you can add a tag to pull them in permanently out of the general audience, or you can send a one-time campaign right now. Maya does both. Creative advocates are tagged and separated from the general audience. They'll never receive a campaign email again. Then she kicks off that one-time campaign. Active Intelligence drafted the email already. Maya makes one adjustment and she approves. That is the next best action. Not a six-month program, but one well-timed send to the right 284 people. One send, one tag, 20 minutes. That is what acting on the right segment looks like when AI has already done the analysis. But while Maya was reviewing creative advocates, Active Intelligence wasn't waiting. It's been running in the background across every automation, every send, and every timing pattern in Compass Creative's account. It's looking for what's working, what isn't, and what needs attention before it becomes a problem. Some of what it found it handled automatically. Some of it needs Maya to make the call. While Maya was reviewing creative advocates, Active Intelligence was watching something else. Compass Creative's client showcase automation has been misfiring. The date-based wait step is skipped early. Early contacts are receiving a personal milestone note before the milestone arrives. Not after, but before. This happened to 14 contacts in two weeks, and nothing on the surface looked wrong. The automation showed active. Since we're completing without AI watching contact-level timing, this stays invisible. But AI traced it. The wait step is resolving against enrollment date instead of milestone date. For some contacts, that's a difference of weeks. A message that arrives too early doesn't feel personal; it feels automated. And for a referral program built entirely on relationship timing, that is the wrong moment to get wrong. AI isn't just flagging it; it's bringing Maya a choice. Option one: you could remap the wait step to the milestone date field. This is a clean fix that scales with the program. Option two: convert to a fixed-duration wait with a manual review checkpoint before each send. AI recommends option one, but it flags that 14 contacts currently in the flow need to be reevaluated before the fix goes live. Maya recognizes most of them. 11 get re-enrolled at the corrected timing, and three she removes as their milestone windows have already passed. Two minutes and it's fixed. AI caught what a dashboard couldn't show. Maya decided what only she could decide, and that is the collaboration. Three outcomes: a campaign recovered overnight, 284 strategic partners identified, and an automation issue resolved before it quietly damaged 14 relationships. All this happened while Maya was working on other things. But wait, there's more. The more Maya works with Active Intelligence, the more it understands how she thinks—her language, her instincts, and her priorities. This is the platform that we are building. Active Intelligence works across your entire business, understands natural conversation, discovers opportunities proactively, and adapts to every person on your team. It starts with what you saw today: outcomes through autonomous interactions, personalization through custom instructions, and learning through memory. These autonomous actions multiply your team and grow from there. Throughout our session, we have seen how Active Intelligence is changing the way you're working and making your marketing unstoppable. It's your trusted strategic advisor and extension of your team, serving you insights, recommendations, and actions proactively to fine-tune your marketing. And now with personalization, it helps you customize your experience of Active Intelligence and what it creates for your business. The successes you have seen so far, like increasing your marketing performance by 20% with Active Intelligence and driving 40% higher revenue, those are just the beginning. As you saw in our roadmap demos, there's even more coming to ActiveCampaign and Active Intelligence to launch you way ahead of your competition. Thank you for going on this journey with us into the next chapter of autonomous marketing.

AI Rally Powers Wall Street Earnings | Open Interest 5/7/2026

Bloomberg Television(3.1M)·87:11·2.1K views·2026-05-07
Matt MillerCo-host, Bloomberg Television Dani BurgerCo-host, Bloomberg Television
TL;DRWall Street is flirting with all-time highs fueled by strong corporate earnings and optimism surrounding a proposed deal to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, even as mixed reports from tech and banking sector guidance flash warning signs about the labor market and consumer sentiment.

TL;DR

Wall Street is flirting with all-time highs fueled by strong corporate earnings and optimism surrounding a proposed deal to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, even as mixed reports from tech and banking sector guidance flash warning signs about the labor market and consumer sentiment.

Geopolitical Shifts and Energy Market Impact

  • Strait of Hormuz Negotiations: The US is awaiting Iran's response via Pakistani mediators regarding a one-page memorandum of understanding. The proposal seeks to gradually reopen the Strait of Hormuz in exchange for lifting the US naval blockade.
  • Oil Prices Retreat: Brent crude fell below $100 a barrel (down nearly 4%), which helped bonds rally for a second day and fueled equity market optimism.
  • Oil Trading Investigation: A series of highly lucrative, well-timed trades executed between March 23 and April 21 has drawn scrutiny. Traders made over $2.6 billion betting that oil prices would drop right before major policy announcements from the US White House and Iran's foreign minister.
  • Military Stance Remains Active: Despite active negotiations, the US naval blockade remains firmly in place. CENTCOM confirmed that the US military recently disabled an Iranian-flagged oil tanker attempting to traverse the Gulf of Oman.

Mixed Corporate Earnings and Growing Consumer Pressures

  • Arm Tumbles on Smartphone Slowdown: Despite beating fiscal fourth-quarter expectations, Arm shares fell over 8% due to a decline in smartphone shipments.
      • The AI Memory Squeeze: Chipmakers are shifting production away from lower-end consumer electronics (smartphones and PCs) to meet massive demand for memory in AI data centers, inadvertently choking off cheaper consumer hardware segments.
  • Consumer Sector Divergence:
      • McDonald's: EPS topped expectations, but overall sales growth fell short of estimates. Management warned of a meaningful decline in lower-income consumer sentiment heading into Q2, prompting the company to rely heavily on promotional value menus to sustain foot traffic.
      • Shake Shack and Papa John's: Both missed revenue estimates as rising food costs (specifically beef prices) squeezed margins. Companies are absorbing these costs internally to prevent a drop in customer volume.
      • Whirlpool: Warned of a "recession-level decline" in the appliance sector, indicating that wobbly consumer sentiment is beginning to translate into deferred purchases for big-ticket household goods.
  • IPO Market Resiliency: Capital markets showed renewed life with two successful, albeit modest, initial public offerings: Hawkeye (satellite location services) and Suja Life (organic beverage brand).

Wall Street Bonuses, Citigroup's Targets, and Private Capital

  • Citigroup’s Disappointing Outlook: Citigroup shares fluctuated after the bank set a return on tangible common equity (ROTC) target of 14% to 15% extending all the way out to 2031. Analysts noted this long-term guidance lags significantly behind competitors like Bank of America (16% to 18%) and JPMorgan Chase (which is already printing returns in the 20% range), overshadowing Citi's strong Q1 results and the authorization of a stock buyback program.
  • Rising Banker Compensation: Wall Street bonuses are projected to rise for a third consecutive year. According to the latest Johnson Associates report, investment bankers in equity underwriting and advisory are poised for the largest payouts on the back of a rebounding deal environment.
  • Private Equity Bottlenecks and Carlyle’s Miss: Carlyle Group missed quarterly profit estimates despite rising asset sales. The miss was attributed to a technical delay in cash realizations flowing through to shareholders as carried interest.
  • Private Credit Language Shift: BlackRock reported further markdowns on legacy TCP funds. Due to liquidity concerns, private market players are moving away from the term "semi-liquid" for retail-oriented funds to better align investor expectations with the reality of illiquid assets.

The AI Capex Boom vs. Labor Market Risks

  • Accelerating S&P Earnings Growth: S&P 500 earnings growth estimates for the year have surged from an initial 10%–12% up to 20%–27.5%. Bernstein Private Wealth CIO Alex Chaloff noted this growth is highly concentrated in tech and the broader AI ecosystem.
  • Trillion-Dollar Capex Commitments: Major hyperscalers are implementing five-year infrastructure plans that will deploy trillions of dollars to power AI data centers, which are expected to yield massive long-term productivity gains across the broader economic grid.
  • Labor Market Displacement: The tech sector is experiencing a persistent trickle of job cuts (e.g., Coinbase, PayPal), with AI increasingly cited as the driver. Chaloff identified the labor market as the "canary in the coal mine" for this economic cycle, as companies seek to replace human overhead with AI systems.
  • Looming Inference Costs: While companies currently tout massive productivity gains from AI integration, they face a future wave of margin pressure when hyperscalers eventually pass down the currently subsidized, high cost of model inference.

Notable Quotes

"She's [Jane Fraser] gone from F to C, but the challenge to go from a C grade to an A grade student like some of their peers right now, is a much harder journey, and perhaps their more muted targets that they're setting today is a reflection of that." — *Sridhar Natarajan, on Citigroup's turnaround efforts*
"This is not a late-cycle corporate environment. The combination of high capex, strong shareholder returns, improving margins, AI-driven productivity, and durable demand is inconsistent with a typical late-cycle slowdown narrative." — *Alex Chaloff*
"Everyone wants memory in their AI data centers, so chipmakers aren't making as much memory for the cheaper goods, like smartphones, like PCs. And so that is hitting a little bit on where they, of course, have huge exposure." — *Caroline Hyde, on why chipmaker Arm slid despite record AI demand*
Just 30 minutes until we start trading, we are still at all-time highs. It is a rally built on optimism and strong earnings. I'm Dani Burger; Matt Miller is off for the rest of the week. Bloomberg Open Interest starts right now. Coming up on the show, stocks extend record gains on strong earnings as the US awaits a response from Iran. And Citi is under pressure to prove its turnaround is real, as CEO Jane Fraser makes her pitch to shareholders this morning. And Johnson Associates says that it's the year of the bank, with Wall Street bonuses set to jump for a third year in a row. All right, let's look at some of those strong earnings that we've been tracking. McDonald's is one of them. It was gaining in the pre-market but actually just turned around and is now down about two-tenths of 1%. The initial optimism was about the fact that they topped expectations. Sales growth was just shy of Wall Street estimates, and maybe that's why we're little changed right now. But it did say that value meals—including that big Arch Burger that had that cringey video from the CEO—are doing well. Then there's Arm; shares are down eight and a third percent. It said a slowdown in the smartphone industry is weighing on revenue, while its AI efforts are helping to offset some of those losses. And then there is Citigroup, whose shares are falling. The bank's new guidance showed that it would take time to catch up with Wall Street, so those shares are falling about 1.1% in the pre-market. Let's get some of the details of what we learned from Citi this morning in their investor day. Joining me now is Bloomberg financial reporter Katherine Doherty. Katherine, so I guess it was those ROTC targets that missed. What did Citi say? Yes, so today they gave their ROTC guidance of 14% to 15%. That is up until 2031, so we've got a few years looking ahead. But other banks that have reported similar guidance are much higher. So Bank of America, when they had their big investor day in the fall of last year, their guidance is between 16% to 18%. JP Morgan posted a return in the 20%s, and that was back in 2025. So really, what you're seeing is these analysts and investors comparing Citi's projected guidance to either, for JP Morgan's sake, what they've already been printing, or for Bank of America and others, what they're projecting in a similar timeframe. I thought that the turnaround had been achieved. I mean, their results in the first quarter were so strong. Is there a disconnect between the two of having something like the best, best return in five years for Citi versus an outlook that disappoints? I think that if you're talking about an outlook that goes to 2031, and then you're looking at a quarter like the one that you're referencing—the first quarter of this year being so strong—things can change, and especially when you have so many geopolitical questions that need to be answered. We're still looking at what's going to happen with Fed rate decisions moving forward. All of this is going to play into the strength of the economy, which is going to impact Citi and, at least, their consumer business, and, and, for their commercial banking and investment banking, what their clients are doing. Yes, we did see a very, very strong first quarter, but they're likely to be giving this guidance as a conservative outlook given that there are still so many questions that need to be answered. There are so many questions, but it's a pay-now, ask-those-questions-later environment. I know you've been reporting on the latest pay figures for Wall Street. I mean, they're looking really strong. Right, this is a projection that comes out of the Johnson Associates report, and they update this throughout the year. This is kind of the earliest look that we get at what 2026 could be in terms of bonuses at year-end. But right now, it's looking really strong for investment bankers in particular, in equity underwriting and advisory. This is really on the back of a stronger deal environment. What everyone was waiting for in terms of the uptick in what we would see at some of the biggest banks, you've started to see that come through now. Whether that remains is, is still a question. So there are some caveats. While the future does look pretty bright right now, there are a lot of clouds that could change how the end of the year actually pays out. Sounding like Jamie Dimon with those clouds, K-Do. Thank you so much for joining, it is always great to hear from you. That's, of course, Katherine Doherty. And don't miss our exclusive interview in the next hour with the man behind the report that Katherine was just talking about, Chris Connors, managing director of Johnson Associates. Elsewhere in earnings and share news, corporate news this morning, Arm shares are down sharply after revenue took a hit from the slowdown in the smartphone market. Let's bring in Bloomberg Tech co-host Caroline Hyde. Caroline, eight and a half percent is a pretty big decline, but how much of this is just based on these chipmakers having a huge run this year? I think Arm is up something like 110 or 115 percent. Well said. I mean, there could be a lot of profit-taking going on here, when actually the company beat expectations on the fiscal fourth quarter. The year is at a record in terms of revenue, and they're pointing towards guidance that beat expectations. But he was pretty down on the whole smartphone shipment market. Look, he's actually saying you're turning into negative growth a little bit in terms of unit growth for phones. But he says it's at the lower end of the phones, the cheaper phones. This is all about the memory crisis—a memory crisis sparked by AI. Everyone wants memory in their AI data centers, so chipmakers aren't making as much memory for the cheaper goods, like smartphones and PCs. And so that is hitting a little bit on where they, of course, have huge exposure, which is the smartphone. Basically every smartphone has Arm architecture in it. But this is a company that makes money through licensing. You're allowed to use the technology, but then the royalties are per chip actually used and shipped, and that's increasingly going into AI data centers. They've got this new AGI CPU, and they're saying growth is going big there. Meta's a client, and they're going to see 2 billion of orders by 2026, 2027. So there's a lot to be excited about, it's just, look, we are seeing the stock perhaps roll over from some pretty significant gains. You talk about that huge rally we've had. I think the SOX index, the Philly Semiconductor Index, is up something like 64 percent. It's all at new highs. This seems like the outlier in earnings that hasn't helped fuel that. I mean, like, AMD was such a big contributor just this week. Extraordinary. And remember, we are seeing this just galvanization around AI and euphoria once again. You're seeing talks of whether Anthropic is so in need of compute right now they're signing deals with SpaceX, and we know that there's been talk about how much they could be reliant on Google. We're thinking about how much people are behind the curve when it comes to AI infrastructure, and how much this is more of a supply-side issue than it is a demand-side issue. Everyone's been running back into these stocks. Look, you got Samsung now worth more than a trillion dollars. We're getting more and more huge market capitalizations. It's the only thing that matters for this market. Who cares about war and oil and energy? And job cuts. And job cuts, in tech, in tech too, there's been a lot of job cuts. Yeah, look, look at the Challenger data out today. You're seeing predominantly the job cuts coming from the tech sector. Why? For the second month in a row, AI is being blamed. Look, we can say whether it's AI washing more broadly, and look, since 2023, since they started looking at whether AI is an issue, it's only about 3.5% of all job cuts that have come in those few years, but it's ticking up higher. And you are seeing tech really—look what we got from Coinbase, look what we've just got from PayPal—it's just adding this trickle, trickle, trickle. And they're all getting rewarded for doing it, too. Yeah, because instead of filling it with people, you might not be losing your job to AI, but probably the money that they used to pay you with is getting redeployed on AI. Yeah, and it's getting increasingly expensive. Caroline, thank you so much for joining, always great to have you. That's Bloomberg Tech co-host, Caroline Hyde. When it comes to the picture for oil that we mentioned, Brent is below $100 a barrel this morning. Yes, that is helping a rally in stocks. The US is now awaiting Iran's response to, to a proposal to reopen the Strait of Hormuz and end the war, President Trump speaking yesterday. "You know, they want to make a deal badly, and we'll see if we get there. If we get there, they can't have nuclear weapons. You know, it's so simple, but what's not to submit? Their missiles are mostly decimated. They have some, they have probably 18-19%, but not a lot by comparison to what they had. And their leaders are all dead. So, I think we won." Bloomberg Washington correspondent Tyler Kendall joins us now. Tyler, there is this hope that there'll be an agreement on this one-page memo. Does it seem like we're moving in that direction? Well, Dani, at this point, we're going to have to wait and see what Iran's response is, as people familiar with the matter tell us that Iran is expected to give a response via Pakistani mediators within the next day, and this White House maintains that a deal will get done. But at least publicly, the two sides still feel very far apart. Our reporting does indicate that there are active discussions about a potential moratorium on Iran's uranium enrichment capabilities, but Iranian officials so far have maintained that they are not making any concessions regarding the country's nuclear program, at least for now, because before we can get to, to those discussions, it is our understanding that this one-page memorandum of understanding would first seek to gradually reopen the Strait of Hormuz in exchange for the US lifting its naval blockade, which at this point, to be sure, is still very much in place. In fact, CENTCOM yesterday confirmed that the US military had disabled an Iranian-flagged oil tanker that was trying to make its way through the Gulf of Oman. And Dani, this White House has really been maintaining that negotiations have been complicated by fractured leadership in Iran. But we should point out some headlines crossing the Bloomberg Terminal just moments ago that Iran's president says that he had a meeting with Iran's supreme leader, detailing a rare public account of interactions with the supreme leader, who has been in hiding since the war began. So we'll see if that yields any sort of progress or results in this, because again, at least publicly, we haven't gotten indications that the sides are getting closer to making a deal when it comes to those nuclear capabilities. Tyler, separately, we also got some headlines this morning, this from ABC, on probes over oil trades. They had gotten a lot of attention in the war in Iran about trades that seemed very well-timed and well-placed before action that benefited from what the President said he was doing in the country. What exactly do we know about this probe? So, ABC News putting a little bit more of a finer point on some previous reporting, including from Bloomberg News, that there was a series of well-timed trades between March 23rd and April 21st around announcements made by both President Trump, as well as Iran's foreign minister, related to President Trump holding off on those infrastructure attacks and, and his threats there, the announcement of the ceasefire, and then when that, when Iran's foreign minister had announced that there would be a reopening of the Strait of Hormuz, which, of course, didn't end up happening. Now, we should say that the data obtained by ABC News does not indicate any identities behind these trades, nor that anybody did participate in insider trading, but it is adding this heightened scrutiny, particularly after we had reported that back in March, the White House circulated a memo to staff urging them not to use private information to make these trades, including when it comes, in particular, to betting platforms. But this reporting showing that traders made a total of more than $2.6 billion betting that oil prices would drop right before they did, Dani. Tyler, thank you very much, as always, for your coverage, that's Tyler Kendall in Washington. Let's get a check on these markets that are still riding high on the optimism that Tyler was laying out, that we will get an agreement on this one-page memo. Oil dropping three and a half percent, that's helping bonds rally for a second day, down two basis points. We're little changed on both the S&P and Nasdaq, but we are flirting yet again with all-time highs after closing at an all-time high yesterday, also thanks to a stellar set of earnings results. Let's look at some of those individual stocks that are on our radar. Snap, that is moving to the downside, down nine and a half percent. It said that the war in the Middle East is having a negative impact on ad sales. We're going to talk with the MNTN CEO Mark Douglas in the next hour, so we'll ask him about that. Peloton raised its outlook as commercial sales climb, and CEO Peter Stern sees progress with his turnaround strategy. Celsius earnings, those showed strong demand despite more competition in energy drink markets and wobbly consumer sentiment. I love me a Celsius, those shares up two and a half percent. Coming up, more retail earnings, McDonald's sales growth doesn't meet estimates, but it sees growth outside the US. We're going to have more on the sector coming up next, this is Bloomberg Open Interest. Let's get a look at your high interest stories this morning, what's making headlines around the world. US Secretary of State Marco Rubio is visiting Italy where he met with Pope Leo XIV amid a renewed clash between President Trump and the holy leader. The Pope is reaffirming the Church's, Church's anti-nuclear stance, a focus on peace, adding tensions to a delicate diplomatic visit. Rubio is expected to meet with Italian Prime Minister Meloni tomorrow. And GameStop CEO Ryan Cohen says eBay suspended his account after he tried raising buyout cash by selling personal items, everything from used socks to a square of a carpet from GameStop. The stunt pulled in tens of thousands, but critics say it's nowhere near, near enough to fund his $56 billion bid for eBay, which remains highly unlikely. Federal prosecutors charged 30 people in an insider-trading ring tied to confidential M&A tips from major law firms. Authorities say a former corporate lawyer helped steal deal information and pass it through a network that made tens of millions in illegal profits. Prosecutors say the group used burner phones, encrypted apps, and code words like 'rabbi' to hide the scheme. In earnings, McDonald's earnings per share topped estimates, but sales growth fell short of expectations. Shake Shack and Papa John's revenue missed estimates as consumers turned cautious and food costs rise. Bloomberg's Redd Brown covers consumer stocks and joins me now. McDonald's is back higher again, and the earnings were solid, but it seems like there's some nervousness around the commentary we've been getting from them. Yeah, the CEO on the, or excuse me, I don't know if it was CEO, but one of the managers, someone in the C-suite on the call, said that they're seeing a meaningful consumer sentiment decline in the second quarter, so early indications that consumers are starting to see, feel some of the effects that we've been talking about for a few weeks but haven't really seen show up in earnings necessarily. But you have to remember with McDonald's, you get a really great insight into a lower-income consumer who's going to feel these pressures most acutely. And I think that's what's starting to show up, and probably what's, what's, we're seeing the reaction to. And that's the thing. There's been a lot said that the consumer's really strong, now the macro data has been really good, and yes, some of the earnings have been really good. But then you also have a Shake Shack, for example, that earnings were not strong, and they're not necessarily the value consumer. Does that not portend that it's not smooth sailing, that there are some weakness in pockets for this consumer? Yeah, I think, well, Shake Shack's a great example of like how companies are figuring ways to, to manage this. They didn't pass on the higher cost to their consumers. They took the hit on their earnings, and now that, you're seeing how that's impacting their, their results, right? So, you know, companies are in this weird position of, we've been raising prices for years, we can't raise them anymore, we can't sacrifice our foot traffic, so we're going to have to take the pain lower down the income statement, and that's kind of what, what happened with Shake Shack today. So the companies that are doing well, is it fair to say, are just finding a way to squeeze value out? Is, is that essentially what makes the winner? Yeah, well, like McDonald's, again to go back to them, is a great example of squeezing value out. They rejiggered their value menu again to continue to pull people in, again this strategy being like, we need foot traffic, and hopefully we'll get enough foot traffic that it offsets the pain that we're feeling internally on higher costs. But not every company has the scale of McDonald's and is able to kind of do that like Shake Shack as the example. One of the ones I thought was really interesting too, was Whirlpool. And I think specifically because they're talking about a recession-level decline because of consumer sentiment, because there's been a lot of noise made that although consumer sentiment surveys that are at record lows, it doesn't actually translate into consumer spending. Is Whirlpool an example that actually it does, it does translate into people pulling back, at least, I guess, in big purchases like something you'd get from Whirlpool? Yeah, I mean, I think that might be one of the first places you really see it. Like, that's not a high-volume business in the, in the way that some of these other, the restaurants are, right? So if someone says, 'You know, I need a new appliance. It's not totally broken, I can maybe push it for another few months,' that might be one of the first places you start to see some of that changes in consumer behavior start to show up. And I mean, it must be tough for Whirlpool because it's a lot of materials that they need that go into their things that are expensive. Producer Will Shaker also points out that beef prices are higher for restaurants. So how much of this is really just about a, a, a period of margins getting squeezed that we're about to enter? Yeah, I think there's going to be quite a bit of that. And I think what we are going to continue to watch for this earning season is what McDonald's is talking about right now, which is the consumer side of it, because that has been where the question marks have been, right? We understand that costs are going to be immediately rising, companies can do a lot to mitigate those costs, but there's not a ton that they can do to mitigate if consumers are just not showing up. Yeah, a really good point. Redd, thank you so much for joining. A lot of earnings to cover, that's Bloomberg's Redd Brown. Coming up, Bernstein's Alex Chaloff sees more upside for stocks as we continue to see record after record for the S&P 500. He's going to join the show coming up next, you're watching Bloomberg Open Interest. Stocks are at an all-time high, and Bernstein's Alex Chaloff sees more room to run. He writes, '...this is not a late-cycle corporate environment. The combination of high capex, strong shareholder returns, improving margins, AI-driven productivity, and durable demand is inconsistent with a typical late-cycle slowdown narrative. Further support to move higher.' That's important, 'inconsistent', not 'consistent'. Joining me now is Bernstein Private Wealth CIO, Alex Chaloff. Alex, thank you so much for joining. And it is remarkable to look at the type of earnings we've seen, the, the uptick and the estimates going forward are something that are consistent with coming out of a downturn into the upside. How, how did we get to this place? It's, it's, it's wild this year. We came into the year thinking we'd have 10% to 12% earnings growth on the S&P. Now we're 20, 25, I saw 27.5, it continues to move higher. It's all really focused in this AI ecosystem. If you look at the breakdown of what sectors are producing the biggest earnings growth, it still is the tech trade. Can we keep that up? I mean, there's something from Hussman Strategic Advisors that basically said the current forecast, especially for next year, implies sales growth at roughly two times the expected pace of GDP, and as I mentioned, historically that happens when we're coming out of a recession. So can we continue at that kind of pace, at a pace that just based on history we rarely are in this type of cycle, in this place in the cycle? That's right. I think the last time we saw earnings growth like this was coming off the bottom in '20, and then you have to go back to the GFC and post-GFC to see this type of growth. But this is, as I note, a re-acceleration. This is, we, we had a little of a, a mild pause and then we've taken off from there. I think we can continue. If you look at the AI spend amongst the massive hyperscalers, there's real dollars being put to work, and they're not saying it's just a '26 phenomenon. They're putting out a five-year plan where they're going to spend trillions of dollars on this. How much does that mean that it is a concentrated earnings, that it will be on the AI spend, especially because if you look, okay, for example, a data center, in order to construct it, you get thousands of people coming in. But then once it's constructed, you have like a couple of people around just running the HVACs or, or whatever it is. So is it something where it's like what's good for AI isn't necessarily good for the entirety of the labor market, and thus things like consumer stocks maybe don't look as attractive? I think if you're just looking at isolate, looking at data centers specifically or something like that, where there's a one-time spend, then it's hard to see that recurring over time, you will, there, there's some pessimism around that. But if you think about the productivity gains that we'll get from that AI spend, it's not just the three guys and an HVAC. It's the fact that that data center is powering a massive part of the grid that's then providing productivity gains to companies of all types. Yeah, but aren't productivity gains also fewer employees? Less people working, less people getting good wages? Absolutely. I mean, that, that's the canary in the, the coal mine. This cycle could be the labor market. You know, we've seen some wage growth pullback, we've seen net new job creation is, has come down. We're, we're going to get excited tomorrow if we have 75,000 net new jobs. So, it, it, it definitely is making corporate America pause and say, 'What can we replace with AI?' But, but as we saw with the internet, it, it, there is that type of pause where people think, okay, there's, there's going to be some replacement, and then the next re-acceleration is when you can use that power to actually create new jobs. Okay, Alex, you're going to stick around with us through the opening bell, which is coming up in just under four minutes time. And a check on futures ahead of that, we still trade at all-time highs. We're up just one-tenth of a percent for both the S&P and Nasdaq. A decline in oil prices, that's also helping to boost us higher this morning, down by nearly 4% as 10-year yields move lower by two basis points. We're waiting to see whether a one-page memorandum can be agreed upon between the US and Israel. On the other side of this break, we'll have your opening bell. Just seconds away until we start our cash equity trade. You are watching Bloomberg Open Interest, I'm Dani Burger. We closed yesterday at an all-time high, and we are on track to do that again, with optimism over the war in Iran and earnings powering this thing higher. So we're up about a tenth for both the S&P and Nasdaq. Dow Jones doing even better, that's up two-tenths of 1%. We've also been on this huge run for semis, the SOX index up 64% year-to-date. Arm earnings disappointed, so that's maybe going to drag us down a little bit lower. In terms of opening bells, we got two IPOs on our hands, and they're not huge IPOs, but that's telling in itself, capital markets are alive and well, not just for open AIs, for the Anthropics of the world. You have Hawkeye that does satellite location services, from right next to my hometown, they're from Herndon, Virginia. I'm a Northern Virginia girl, so great to see a company from that area doing well. Here at the Nasdaq we have Suja Life, Suja Life. They make a drink, you've probably seen them in grocery stores, they're like very colorful, they tend to have like little chia seeds and fun things in them, so they're IPOing. So there you go, you got like a tech company and a consumer company, all is right with the world that we're getting these types of IPOs. Some of the stocks that we're watching this morning. McDonald's, it fell briefly during the pre-market trade after some of the commentary from the C-suite on their earnings call saying that it might look a little bit softer in the second quarter. But for the most part, their earnings were strong and was just shy of Wall Street estimates. It's doing well because it's leaning into value menus and it's seeing an uptick in sales. Citi also able to defy the gloom. It was declining pre-market because its new guidance said basically would take more time to catch up with Wall Street peers, but it did authorize a $30 billion stock buyback program, so that helped, 30 rather. $300, that's much bigger. And then Arm, those shares I mentioned they're falling, down by seven and a third of 1% saying that a slowdown in the smartphone industry is weighing on revenue, but AI efforts are helping to offset those losses. Let's go back over to the desk. Patiently still waiting there is Bernstein Private Wealth CIO, Alex Chaloff. Alex, you know, we, we talked about just this huge AI boom and how it's helping to lift the market. Are you surprised at all that we didn't see more of a blip given energy prices continued to stay around $100? I think we just dipped below that around Brent. But that the energy issues we've seen haven't led to a bigger sell-off in the equity market? Yeah, it's a, it's a great question, it's one that we are struggling as a market to respond to. It seems as though bad news, good news, okay news, we're still going one direction, up. And, and I think it's going to take a while before the fuel inflation pushes its way through. You're seeing some, some moderation in consumer spending. It's a tax on the lower-income consumer. It's a, a bigger portion of their paycheck is going to, to pay for gas than, than before, but so far, the, the upper end of the consumer spending spectrum has driven a continuation in higher spending pattern. It, it, it feels like every day it's optimism that we're getting closer to a deal. That's what we're trading on today on this one-page memo. And then inevitably, something happens where, you know, the US decides to escort ships, people fire at each other, maybe we move backwards. Has this market become numb to the daily different price, to the daily different developments that are happening in the war in Iran? I think that's exactly right. We, over the last couple of years, we've gotten numb to the 'Taco Trade' where there was some ambitious, bold statement, markets fell, and then there was some walking back of said statement and market rallied even more. You're starting to see that same playbook play out in the Middle East where, we, the US engages, Iran engages, we back off, they back off, market goes up. It's like the same playbook over and over again. Now there's a one-page memo, next week it'll be a two-page memo. It's the, I think investors are getting used to it. Is it, is it just investor psychology that we have had these V-shaped recoveries, that maybe something like Liberation Day or even the rebound from COVID is just seared into our psyche that when we rebound, we rebound hard? Is that just a change in maybe market structure going forward, that the declines look less severe because of that propensity to rebound out of them? I think there's some of that. There is also this, we used to call it TINA, 'there is no alternative'. Stocks have become, for most of the investing public, the preferred avenue to allocate capital to. And because there's a shrinking pool of public companies today, even, you know, the IPOs are coming back, but coming back ever so slowly, there's just less names and so the market moves higher. Just on that, I'm really curious what these mega IPOs do to the market. If we get SpaceX, OpenAI, Anthropic, that's a lot to absorb, and these exchanges, or, or, rather the indexes are trying to move things along to get them incorporated sooner. What, what are conversations with clients like, and how to own these stocks, how to approach them when they do come to market? I think there's, there's a demand for them post-IPO, there's a demand for them pre-IPO for a bunch of these names at any price, by the way. So I think post-IPO, for some of the big names you mentioned and a handful of others, it will be constructive to the market, it's going to add for continued capital flow. Does that make the market more or less concentrated? I guess less because you have more of these big attractive companies, or less concentrated because of that, but more because they're now bigger companies in this market that maybe look, attractive and crowd everything else out? I think it's more diversified on a name-by-name basis, adding these big names that will start with trillion-dollar market caps, that, that will help from a diversification standpoint. But it's still the same trade, it's an AI trade, and that's probably going to be the rest of this year and into '27. So on, on sort of the, the contrast the AI trade and everything else that's happening in this market with higher energy prices, you know, I look at earnings from Whirlpool where the CEO said that things are at a recession level for this industry because consumer sentiment has turned. Shake Shack can't raise their prices, that consumers have stopped accepting it. But then on the other hand, you heard from airlines that are saying, 'Yeah, we have higher fuel prices and we can pass that on to the consumer.' Is this a consumer right now that's willing to accept higher prices, or is it just only in specific industries? I think that they're I go back to the high-end consumer and the low-end consumer. The high-end consumer continues to spend and whether that plane ticket to Europe is 900 or 1,900, they're going to Europe. The, the, the difference in the Shake Shacks and the, the McDonalds and some of the quick-service restaurants, they're, they're serving, mostly serving a different type of consumer that is constrained today, and further constrained as gas prices remain elevated. I guess all of this kind of also points to whether or not this period will, will see any sort of inflation or sustained inflation, whether this does translate to companies raising prices because of fuel cost and staying there. Is that your expectation or do some of the pushback, especially from those value-oriented consumers, means that we, we won't have any sort of inflation? I think we will have inflation. If you go back and look at the '22 cycle, it was higher rates and higher inflation, and while the markets were in tough shape as a result of that, you saw companies finally after 10 years of not touching prices, move them higher. They haven't moved them back, they didn't move them down in '23, '24, and '25, so there's no retrenchment, and I think this will be another leg higher in pricing. By the way, speaking of higher prices, I had a really interesting conversation at Milken with someone who basically said inference costs are really high, and the big language models aren't passing them on yet. And an example of that is Uber last week who ran through its entire year's budget in just a couple months because of all of the AI coding that its engineers were doing. Have we fully realized for these companies that are saying, 'Oh, I have huge productivity gains, my costs are coming down because I'm using AI instead of people.' Are they about to face this wave of higher inference costs when these models start to finally pass it along? Yeah, I think it's just going to be a slow burn. It's not all of a sudden that all the hyperscalers are going to come out and say, 'Prices are now up 20%,' but, but I do think the dial will be turned over, over the next couple of years. I mean, if you think about, the, the, the way that some of these companies have operated, and the, the Mag 7 specifically, they have market share, right now they're competing on price, they're, they're competing on somewhat of a product sale basis, but they're also competing on price. And as soon as that trend is over, yeah, they're going to look to raise prices. Does this also kind of emphasize the whole, you know, halo trade, hard asset low obsolescence, it feels like because of those nerves even if you say, 'Hey, this company is doing a great job in adding AI,' there's still a lot of left tail risk there. So does this just push us more into industrial-type stocks? I think, it, it, I think we're moving to industrials, capital equipment, infrastructure anyways, that, that put AI aside. I, I look at the small caps, small caps are rallying hard this year, and if you think about their asset base, that, that, that would put a little bit of a, a cold water on that halo trade. I think that there's, there's real growth expectations and the consumer's, the, the a big part of it, but also corporate spending is the other part. Yeah, brings it back around to where we started this conversation, just how strong earnings have been. Alex, thank you so much for joining, really great to get time with you. That's Bernstein's Alex Chaloff. Let's get a check on your equity market. We're about nine minutes into your trading day, and we are little changed. We're pretty even between the gainers and the losers this morning. But little change to the upside, so we could probably get to another all-time high though we still have a lot to play for in today's trade. A lot of the big Mag 7 stocks leading us this morning, Microsoft, Tesla, Apple, Meta, Nvidia, all there in the top gainers. Datadog, some good estimates coming forwards for them. There's also some of the cyber stocks like Palo Alto, Oracle is doing well, too. To the downside, you have a lot of the SOX index, the Philly Semiconductor Index, that is falling this morning after a huge run, gains of 60-plus percent so far this year, trading at all-time highs. But instead this morning, Broadcom falls, Micron falls, Lam Research, and do remember AMD had huge number for earnings but it takes a breather from those results. Seagate, Western Digital, so again, it's the chip stocks that are moving to the downside this morning. Also moving to the downside, energy stocks as the price of oil falls with optimism that there will be some sort of agreement between the US and Iran. Utility shortly under them, they're down six-tenths of 1%. On the top of the pile, consumer discretionary, results from McDonald's that look strong helping to lift those stocks up, up six-tenths of 1%. So we're again little changed, just slightly, slightly higher on the S&P 500. Coming up, we talk Citi, their new return target falls short of expectation, shares still up a third of, of a percent. We're going to have the details for you next on Bloomberg Open Interest. Just under 15 minutes into your trading day, here's how markets stand. Despite the fact Brent crude moving lower by 4%, it's an S&P that's little changed this morning as chip stocks give back some of their rally, even so the Nasdaq is outperforming, up one-tenth of 1%, the Mag 7 stocks having a very good day. Let's get to some of the top calls, the analyst action that we are watching this morning. First up, Wells Fargo, they upgrade Alcoa. The firm says that aluminum prices will stay strong through next year because supply is tight and inventories are low. The war in Iran adds more pressure to the aluminum surge. Next up, JP Morgan, they upgrade Freshpet, arguing its sales and profit growth will beat peers and that is not fully priced in. Shares of the pet company fell following the results that flagged higher fuel costs, but shares are up this morning 2%. And finally, Wall Street weighing in on Warner Brothers' earnings. Benchmark says it looks solid, but mostly matters for the upcoming Paramount Skydance deal. TD Cowen flags stronger-than-expected streaming growth, while Guggenheim says streaming is improving but old-school TV remains a drag. Let's turn to Wall Street. Citi issuing a disappointing return target at its investor day, showing that it will take more time to catch up with Wall Street peers. Let's bring in Bloomberg's Chief Wall Street correspondent, Sridhar Natarajan. Sridhar, I mean what, what went wrong because their first-quarter results were so strong? It seemed like that ushered in this new period of optimism that they have turned things around, that they are catching up. So, let's do some tone-setting. I don't think anything went horribly wrong, as evidenced by the fact as the markets have opened, the stock is pretty much flat. But there was a bit of an expectation mismatch precisely for the reason you just mentioned. When you look at the Q1 numbers, when you look at the fact that they posted a 13% return on tangible equity, when you come into an important Investor Day, after the CEO has said that they're through the nine, 90% of the work tied to their regulatory challenges, the back-office issues, really the toughest part of the transformation work that led to Jane Fraser becoming CEO of Citigroup in March 2021. They are through most of that. So, the hope was now that you've given us a print of a 13% return on tangible equity, you can lift your return expectations, and that's what investors were looking for, that's what analysts were looking for. But the fact that they're saying that they'll get to this 14% to 15% target by the end of the decade, by 2031, that doesn't feel, that doesn't set an exuberant tone, if nothing else, because look at their immediate peers. JP Morgan's already sitting at that figure, Morgan Stanley's already sitting at that figure. And if you look at one of the more recent examples, when you go back to Goldman Sachs to the '21-'22-'23 time period, they were in a bit of a difficult spot, a lot of their investments on balance sheets were not performing well, they had that consumer banking misadventure, and at that time, the bank said it does have a medium-term target of mid-teens returns and it has already achieved that. The hope was that Citigroup can keep that same pace. Perhaps it's a way to underpromise and overdeliver, but the market would much rather that you overpromise and overdeliver. That's a, maybe a tall order, I guess, especially for Citi. So if they've done the bulk of the transformation, they've done the back office, all of the various IT issues they have, what, what is left? What is left that they're, the kinks that they're still working through? It, it shows you the steep grid of the transformation. When you look at a Bank of America report that just came out before this Investor Day, they talked about the fact that when Jane Fraser became CEO, Citi had an F grade. Her transformation and the work she's put in and the team behind her, the work that they have put in, has meant that now you can give Citi perhaps a C grade. So, she's gone from F to C, but the challenge to go from a C grade to an A grade student like some of their peers right now, is a much harder journey, and perhaps their more, muted targets that they're setting today is a reflection of that fact. By the way, for, for Wall Street as a whole, how much of this also sort of flags that there's just a lot of uncertainty going forward? That yes, the first quarter was really strong, M&A results were strong, trading was, was robust. But there's so much volatility right now that things could turn on a dime, that the geopolitical situation could turn on a dime, too. A, yes, 100% true, B, that's what we've been saying for the last five years. But quarter after quarter, the prints keep getting better and better for the banks. They seem to be in a good place. But no, that is a very important point, you can't expect that mood and momentum to last forever, and when the cycle turns, you better be in a very robust and strong position. And to be fair, most of these banks seem to be in a good, healthy position right now. But this is the time that they have to make the most of the favorable conditions in front of them because the underlying consumer economy still feels okay and good, the investment banking industry, when you talk about the trading division, when you talk about dealmaking, this is perhaps as good as it's going to get for some while. So, this is the time that they should be firing on all cylinders. Well, it also seems like the pay reports, the estimates of what people are going to get paid suggest that, that they're going to pay people like they're continuing to fire on all cylinders. I mean, the estimates are very strong for, for what Wall Street and investment bankers are going to get paid. Yes, relative to what you were paid at your old banking seat, you are going to get paid a lot more. But then when you look at the competitive landscape, when you look at some of these bigger hedge funds, and when you look at other market-makers like Citadel Securities or even Jane Street, the fact that a firm like Jane Street can pay its 3,500 employees, what was it, $9.4 billion, about $2.7 million on average, no bank can match that figure. So... Yeah, and I guess if you're trying to hire AI experts, you're like, 'What do I want to go to? Like an old, staid bank or like a, you know, flashy Jane Street or Citadel?' Right, Citigroup is not going to be your first bet on that front. I, I'd assume so. Sridhar, thank you so much for joining, always a pleasure to have you. That's Sridhar Natarajan. And coming up, Carlyle earnings miss as profit lags, despite strong asset sales. We're going to get into that next, this is Bloomberg Open Interest. Some of the power players in private capital that I spoke with at the Milken conference in Beverly Hills, that, of course, was just the Carlyle co-president. Carlyle did report earnings this morning, they missed estimates even as asset sales picked up, shares down just about under 1%. Joining us now is Bloomberg private capital reporter, Allison McNeely. Allison, down about 1%. So what went wrong because they did have a lot of asset sales, they did have a lot of realization. So why did they miss some of their targets? So, they didn't see those asset sales lead to flow-throughs on carried interest to shareholders. It's a, sort of a technical nuance, but essentially the, funds need to pay out to fund investors before they can pay out to shareholders and they haven't hit that level yet. And for a, current, firm like Carlyle, which is very much a buyout-focused firm, you know, you need to see a lot of asset sales to start paying, paying out that carried interest, which flows through to shareholders and then flows through to the, bottom line profit numbers. Is, is the direction that they're going in one, one in which gets us there? Because again, John Redett, who joined me, was basically like, 'Our asset sales look so strong.' And, of course, there's always a little of book-talking. I feel like everyone that has joined, joined us has said that the asset sales look really strong. But there has been a lot of clogging up in this industry of private equity assets that have just been sitting and unable to get sold. Does it feel like some, some of that is starting to free up now that we are starting to see movement? I think Carlyle folks would tell you that, as well as others. We saw sort of similar language around, with the KKR executives this week, too. They also reported earnings. For those firms, again, that are very focused on private equity, they're sort of saying, 'We've got a great pipeline, we're starting to sell assets, you know, better days are ahead.' And then that sort of contrasts to like a more credit-focused firm like Apollo, which, you know, is getting a lot of questions about credit quality and they're taking in massive amounts of cash and sort of reassuring investors that the book is still performing. So, depending on kind of what you, the orientation of your firm is, we're sort of seeing these two different story lines coming out of earnings. By the way, just in where, you know, private credit woes are, are still showing up. Because a lot has been made of the argument that it is just about liquidity, that it's about headline risk, but then you see something like BlackRock, them continuing to mark down some of the TCP funds, some of their, you know, the kind of legacy, not the new HP stuff. So does that show that there still are further markdowns to come? Yes, I think some people would say there are potentially further markdowns to come. I mean, I wouldn't know that, but certainly TCP has been a story for BlackRock. That is a fund that has had some challenges with the portfolio. We saw them move further markdowns this morning. And I think there are potentially markdowns coming in other BDCs as well, or at least that's what some folks I speak to in the market say, but, you know, it very much remains to be seen. By the way, how, has the language changed? You know, Per Franzen spoke to us at Milken and basically said we're probably going to get rid of that word 'semi-liquid', that that's going to disappear for this industry. Does it feel like people are trying to move away from maybe some of the old language they were using around retail products? Absolutely, yes, we are definitely seeing a movement away from 'semi-liquid'. You know, Harvey Schwartz has this line that says, you know, 'Maybe we should have called them not liquid at all,' that he likes to trot out, which I think is, you know, a, a good one. I think, I think illiquid means the same thing. Yeah, it's, it's, yes, there is definitely, I think, some shifting of the language around these funds and moving away from liquid because that sets certain expectations that some of these funds maybe, you know, whether or not it's a miscommunication or whether or not investors don't understand, that's a subject of fierce debate, but setting, changing expectations, I guess. Allison, thank you so much for joining, great to have you. That's Bloomberg's Allison McNeely. Now, coming up in the next hour, Julia Hermann of New York Life joins us, plus Chris Connors from Johnson Associates after a strong forecast for where his firm sees Wall Street bonuses going, and the CEO of Genco joins, and Mark Douglas, the president and CEO of MNTN after their earnings and strong ad sales powered by AI, all that coming up in hour two of Open Interest. And we are 30 minutes into your trading day, just turning negative on the S&P 500. I'm, I'm Dani Burger, you are watching Bloomberg Open Interest. Matt Miller is off today and tomorrow.
