Moving the market this morning, coming up, the US waits on Iran's response, while investors lock in on strong earnings and a tech rally. Johnson Associates says that it's the year of the bank with Wall Street bonuses set to jump for a third straight year. And the Fed says a global insider trading ring didn't just tap bankers, it allegedly reached deep into the elite world of M&A law. We're going to have details on that later this hour. But first let's start with one of our top stories. Brent falls below $100 a barrel this morning. The US now waits on Iran's response to a proposal to reopen the Strait of Hormuz. President Trump speaking yesterday. "You know, they want to make a deal badly, and we'll see if we get there. If we get there, they can't have nuclear weapons. You know, it's so simple, but what's not to submit? Their missiles are mostly decimated. They have some, they have probably 18-19%, but not a lot by comparison to what they had. And their leaders are all dead. So, I think we won." Bloomberg Washington correspondent Tyler Kendall joins us now.

Are UFOs Actually Demons in Disguise?

The Free Press(452.0K)·44:12·7.4K views·2026-05-07
J.D. VanceVice President of the United States
TL;DRModern UFO phenomena are increasingly viewed through a religious and "preternatural" lens by both scholars and high-level government officials, bridging the gap between physical evidence and metaphysical experience.
The "Preternatural" Category: Dr. Diana Pasulka...

TL;DR

Modern UFO phenomena are increasingly viewed through a religious and "preternatural" lens by both scholars and high-level government officials, bridging the gap between physical evidence and metaphysical experience.

The Intersection of UFOs and Religious Theology

  • The "Preternatural" Category: Dr. Diana Pasulka explains that Catholicism maintains a specific category for non-human intelligence—the "preternatural"—which encompasses entities like angels or fallen angels that are distinct from both the natural world and the divine supernatural.
  • A New Form of Religiosity: Pasulka views modern UFO reports as a continuation of historical supernatural encounters, such as the Virgin Mary apparitions or Irish folklore regarding fairies.
  • Jacques Vallée’s Influence: The discussion highlights the work of computer scientist Jacques Vallée, who theorized that UFO sightings are part of a persistent human tradition of interacting with "other" beings that do not appear fully human.
  • Religious Frameworks: Pasulka argues that if society is on the verge of acknowledging non-human intelligence, traditional religion provides the most established "organic framework" for humans to interface with such entities.

Government Interest and the "Disclosure" Movement

  • High-Level Political Engagement: Ross Douthat notes that traditionally sober, "headline-avoidant" politicians like Senator Mike Rounds and Senator Chuck Schumer have shown a strange, persistent interest in UFO data collection and disclosure.
  • The AATIP Catalyst: The 2017 "Tic Tac" video release was the result of a $22 million defense budget provision secured by the late Senator Harry Reid.
      • Douthat notes the skepticism surrounding this: some argue the government "psy-oped" itself into belief because Reid funded a specific group of enthusiasts within the bureaucracy.
  • Presidential Rhetoric: The speakers discuss the "weird" language used by Donald Trump and Barack Obama regarding classified information on UFOs, suggesting the government may be "tap dancing" around a reality they don't fully understand.
  • Skepticism of Full Disclosure: Both guests express doubt that any upcoming government "disclosure" will provide a "smoking gun," with Douthat predicting it will likely follow the pattern of the JFK files—lots of "light" but little "heat."

Physical Evidence and Crash Retrievals

  • The "Metallic Frog Skin": Pasulka recounts a 2012 expedition to a New Mexico "crash site" with Stanford scientist Garry Nolan (referred to as "Tyler" in her book *American Cosmic*).
      • They recovered debris that Nolan later claimed was "engineered" at a level impossible for the era it was allegedly found (the 1940s).
  • Crash Retrieval Programs: While "crash retrieval" is a standard military function for fallen satellites or foreign drones, Pasulka asserts that a subset of these programs specifically focuses on unidentified objects.
  • The Disinformation Theory: A competing narrative (recently highlighted by the *Wall Street Journal*) suggests that the entire UFO movement is a government-run disinformation campaign designed to hide advanced U.S. aerospace technology.
      • Douthat finds this theory plausible for government interest but notes it fails to explain historical "encounter narratives," such as the 1994 Ariel School sighting in Zimbabwe.

Expert Perspectives on Key Figures

  • David Grusch: Both guests describe the high-profile whistleblower as "frustrating," largely due to the extraordinary nature of his claims (such as the Vatican being involved in a 1930s UFO recovery) coupled with a lack of public evidence.
  • Sean Kirkpatrick: Former head of AARO (All-domain Anomaly Resolution Office), Kirkpatrick has criticized the UFO movement as a "faith-based" system that has "seeped into the highest levels of government" without empirical proof.
  • Leslie Kean: Pasulka cites the investigative journalist who co-wrote the 2017 *New York Times* UFO expose as the researcher she respects the most in the field.

***

Notable Quotes

"I think there are ways in which elements of the UFO phenomena look like sort of characteristic but at the same time extremely bizarre forms of spiritual and supernatural experience... that would fit pretty comfortably inside a kind of William James style account of the varieties of religious experience." — Ross Douthat
"The Catholic Church... has a well-formed category for understanding non-human intelligence, be it extraterrestrial or interdimensional. And this is called the preternatural... things that are not necessarily from God, but are in between." — Dr. Diana Pasulka
"The strange thing about the last five or ten years is that a lot of people inside the government... have acted like there is something a little bit more objective associated with this, that it’s not just something you would analyze in terms of people’s subjective experiences." — Ross Douthat
"If indeed it's the case that we are on the dawn of learning about non-human intelligence, the organic framework that we already have is religion. Traditional religions have already helped us learn to interface with non-human intelligence." — Dr. Diana Pasulka
From The Free Press, I’m Will Rahn. And in this series, I’m here to ask you a question. What should smart people, everyday Americans, think about UFOs? Today we are joined by two of the great contemporary thinkers on the subject. One they both say has a lot to do with religion. This is, for now, our final episode, so it's only right that we concern ourselves with final things. After all, when we grapple with questions that take us beyond the limits of human understanding, it’s only a matter of time before we bump up against God. And one of the people we’ll be talking about isn’t a theologian at all, but a French computer scientist named Jacques Vallée. If you’ve ever seen Close Encounters of the Third Kind, you already have a sense of who he is. The French UFO researcher played by François Truffaut was based on him. Back in the 1960s, Vallée started making an argument that still interests people. The basic gist is that long before we ever started talking about flying saucers, people have been seeing things. Things that cannot be explained. Every culture has them. In Ireland, for example, there is still a persistent belief in fairies. Other cultures have trickster ghosts, beings in black garb that do not appear fully human, monsters, angels. A tradition of supernatural belief that stretches back to the beginning of humanity. And Jacques Vallée theorized that perhaps these things had something in common with UFO sightings. He did not dismiss the idea that beings from other worlds might be visiting us today. In fact, now in his 80s, he’s still on the hunt for conclusive physical evidence. But he opened a door to something beyond a nuts and bolts belief in UFOs. And today we’re going to walk through that door. Joining us on that adventure are two of the most thoughtful and provocative writers in America. Ross Douthat is a New York Times columnist. He is conservative, Catholic, and someone unafraid to weigh in on this issue. We are also joined by Dr. Diana Pasulka, a professor at the University of North Carolina. In 2019, she wrote a profoundly influential book called American Cosmic that has in many ways shaped the debate on UFOs. She is perhaps the only scholar of religion who has been taken to see the possible physical remains of an alien starship. And yes, we will talk about that. Her new book, out in July, is The Others: UFOs, AI, and the Secret Forces Guiding Human Destiny. Ross and Diana, joining us today. Hello from the Free Press, I’m Will Rahn. Today we are joined by two of the most thoughtful writers in America. Ross Douthat, thank you so much. After that introduction, how can I not thank you? Yeah, well, you know, I deserve it. We are also joined today by Dr. Diana Walsh Pasulka, a professor at the University of North Carolina. Diana, I'm a huge fan. Thank you so much. Yes, absolutely. Ross, Diana, thank you for joining me today. We're going to get into these things in some detail, but first I want to lay some groundwork. Ross, let's start with you. You're not exactly on the UFO beat at the Times, but this is a subject that interests you. How did that start? Were you like me, an X-Files obsessive as a kid? I did watch the X-Files as a kid, I will cop to that, but I would say that I had never had really strong interest in, you know, what gets called UFOlogy, or anything, anything associated with the landscape of sort of theories, abduction narratives, any of that stuff, really at all until the late 2010s when my own newspaper, The New York Times, published a series of stories. But sort of the core thing that got published was footage from Navy pilots and sort of national security footage basically showing what now people are supposed to call unidentified aerial phenomena, but... We’re going to call them UFOs. We’re going to call them UFOs. We’re calling them UFOs. So UFOs made—they made the New York Times. And I had, you know, to the extent, I guess I'd put it this way: to the extent that I had an interest in the subject, it was primarily along some of the same lines as I think where Diana started out as an academic researcher on this subject. I'm a Catholic, I’m interested in religion, I'm interested in religious experience. I think there are ways in which elements of the UFO phenomena look like sort of characteristic but at the same time extremely bizarre forms of spiritual and supernatural experience of the sort that would fit pretty comfortably inside a kind of William James style account of the varieties of religious experience. So that was sort of my baseline. And the reason I have become more interested in it, without achieving the level of expertise that Diana enjoys, is that the strange thing about the last five or ten years is that a lot of people inside the government and around the U.S. government have acted like there is something a little bit more objective associated with this. It’s not just something that you would analyze in terms of people’s subjective experiences, their mystical experiences, their weird encounters, but that there might be something more concrete. And I don’t have a coherent theory of what that concrete thing is, but it’s a very strange world where my sense is that people as diverse as Chuck Schumer and Marco Rubio think that there might be something sort of hidden inside the vastness of the U.S. national security state that is related to this phenomena. So that’s, that's basically the background of my own interest. I’m interested in it more than I used to be because I feel like people in the government who are themselves not conspiracy theorists or anything like that are also very strangely interested in it as well. Yeah, and we’re going to get to that. I do want to say, as we lead into that really, I remember when Donald Trump—he was asked the question on Air Force One about Obama’s comments when Obama said they’re real about aliens, and then of course walked it back and said, well, I’m talking about the entirety of the universe, there’s probably intelligent life somewhere out there. And Trump, when he was asked about it, his face changed. He looked a little bit odd and he said, he released confidential information. And I remember you tweeting, "that's a weird statement." What struck you as so weird about it? Yeah, it was strange. I mean, I think it was, I think Trump's comments—Trump's comments and the follow-up where, you know, he posted a longer post promising some kind of disclosure of, I think he called it this very complex subject, right? Something like that. He was, he was literally... he was, he was vague and he was like, not promising a lot and there was no timeline for this disclosure. No, exactly. But the language was strange, odd language to use. It’s very odd for a President of the United States to say, well, the declassification of UFO material, that’s a very complex subject. And it just tracks with both things that have been said in public by other figures, again, across party lines. Early in the Trump administration when Trump was ordering the declassification of files related to JFK and other figures, Chuck Schumer tweeted, "now do UFOs." Again, a weird thing to say given, like, just a weird thing for the Minority Leader of the United States Senate to say. So there’s that. Yeah, there’s sort of a running—a running set of public interventions by prominent figures. And this extends to the way that Schumer and Mike Rounds, the Republican, the Republican senator who was sort of his co-sponsor on an essentially a UFO data collection and disclosure provision that the Senate keeps getting put forward in the Senate. The way they, the way these guys talk about this issue is very weird. It sounds like someone tap dancing around something that maybe they aren't themselves sure about, but that they’re more interested in than you would expect presidents and minority leaders and others to be if this was, as I assumed it to be ten or fifteen years ago, essentially a story about kind of personal experiences and conspiracy theories. And I want to get back to that, but it is curious that someone as kind of sober and hardworking and headline-avoidant as Mike Rounds would be taking the lead, at least on the Republican side of this issue, particularly now that Marco Rubio has joined the cabinet. I want to ask you about one line in your piece following up on that. So, Ross, I'm quoting you here: 'How does all this relate to the accounts of Nephilim in the book of Genesis, the Watchers in the apocryphal book of Enoch?' I’m going to acknowledge that the book of Enoch was something that I had to look up after I read that. The Nephilim have been discussed by biblical scholars such as Diana. There is an idea that they are perhaps fallen angels that existed before the Great Deluge, the Great Flood. Were you playing around there or do you think that there actually might be references to alien life in the Bible? Or by alien, I mean, I’m not saying extraterrestrial, I’m not saying, like, I’m saying something... something... So one of the... you know, one of the backgrounds to this conversation is that I had Diana on my own podcast and I think, I think, made her, you know, very annoyed by asking consistently for sort of extremely concrete and certain answers. But so I’m going to do the same thing that Diana did to me and say I’m not sure what that line, you know... I put it in there because there’s a lot of weird, a lot of weird conversations about this stuff and I wanted to have a nod to those weird conversations. And I’m not going to take a formal position at this moment on the relationship of any of this stuff to, you know, to the Bible, sacred scripture or anything like that. I think that's a safe thing for you to do, and I understand the reasoning behind it because I'm doing the same thing. Diana, let's bring you in here. You're a scholar of religion, you've combed the Vatican archives. Tell us about this intersection of religious belief and UFO lore. Are religious people more open to these ideas broadly speaking in your experience? And I mean mainstream religious folks, in other words, not the Nation of Islam, which believes in UFOs and as a group is rejected as heretical by mainstream Islam. Yeah, so I think that mainstream people in the United States, religious people, are about as apt as secular people, or atheists, or just agnostics, to believe in UFOs. Although they do metaphysically have a category of understanding non-human intelligence because obviously throughout the Bible, non-human intelligence is referenced: angels, fallen angels, that would be the Nephilim. The Book of Enoch is an apocryphal text that a lot that, you know, sustains basically our belief in angels comes from this book, the Book of Enoch, which Ross mentions and so. So also I'm a scholar of religious studies, which means that I'm not a dedicated advocate of one particular religious tradition, and my field is Catholic history. And I just also want to mention that like Ross, I have not been a person who was obsessed with the X-Files, although I did see episodes, I think lots of people did, it's an excellent show, but I never thought of UFOs until around 2012. And it was through working in the Catholic tradition, particularly in angel contact events, that my mind then turned to see modern-day reports of UFOs as a continuation and a new form of religiosity. So that’s, you know, so I wasn’t... I never saw myself as being the scholar of UFOs or being an expert on UFOs, which now I've written three books about them, so I guess I am. I want to ask you about some of the comments made by Sean Kirkpatrick, who was the head of the All-domain Anomaly Resolution Office, which began in 2022 to find whatever hidden evidence there was of UFOs or aliens within the government. He says he found no such evidence and has complained repeatedly that the, quote, 'UFO belief system has long ago morphed into a faith requiring no evidence.' He’s further complained that the disclosure movement has, quote, 'elements closer to religion with the same core group of advocates pushing the non-human intelligence narrative of hidden aliens or interdimensional beings.' And he says this UFO religion has seeped into the highest levels of government. Ross, what did you think about Kirkpatrick's comments? Well, I thought the suggestion that UFO belief has seeped into the highest levels of government, whatever exactly the quote was, I think it points to a theory that you would have to hold and believe if you think that there is absolutely nothing concrete to any of this story, right? You would have to believe basically that for whatever reason inside the government there are people who have become, you know, erroneously convinced that there is evidence of non-human intelligence and maybe they've been convinced of this because of some kind of strange psy-op, right? Some kind of disinformation campaign. And there is extensive evidence of the Pentagon and the national security state using disinformation campaigns that employ UFOs across the last 50 or 60 years. So that's certainly not an implausible scenario. But what makes it unusual is that in this instance it would have to be a disinformation campaign that didn’t just sort of, you know, it wasn't just there to trick people in New Mexico or Arizona into thinking they'd seen a flying saucer when they'd really seen a B-2 bomber or something, right? It would have to be a disinformation campaign that somehow had ensnared, again, United States congressmen, congresswomen, United States senators, and depending on how you interpret Donald Trump's comments, even the President of the United States. So it is quite possible, and maybe entirely rational, to construct a narrative in which everything that we're talking about with the government and non-human intelligence is some sort of weird Hall of Mirrors, sort of Cold War disinformation campaign, whatever. But again, to explain the level of interest in this, you have to basically say that it has sort of broken containment in some radical way and engulfed many more people than any kind of, you know, understandable psy-op would, I guess. Yeah, I would say it’s, you know, the government... you could make an argument like Steve Coll has that essentially the U.S. government psy-oped itself into believing that there were weapons of mass destruction in Iraq. I can't think of an example of the government psy-oping itself into believing something that isn't true. That I think would make this somewhat unique. But Diana, moving on a bit, you are both observant Catholics, same here. You may have noticed that every so often a Vatican official will come forward and say something to the effect of, you know, if we find intelligent life tomorrow, that in no way conflicts with Catholic dogma. I asked Cardinal Dolan about this recently and all he would say about it is, 'makes you think, doesn't it?' What do you make of that, that the Vatican, that the chief astronomer, that people there will say from time to time, it's like, well, you know, if there are aliens out there, that doesn't conflict with anything that we've said? Does that hint at some sort of secret knowledge, or is that just kind of in keeping with dogma and orthodoxy? Okay, so Catholicism already has the ability to talk about... in fact, they have a category, they call it the preternatural. And so when you look at, you know, non-human intelligence, they do look at non-human intelligence all the time. There are apparitions of the Virgin Mary, you know, people have experiences that they would consider to be angel events. There are saints, you know, saints bilocate, levitate. And so the Catholic Church assesses these on a case-by-case basis. And they have a well-formed category for understanding non-human intelligence, be it extraterrestrial or interdimensional. This is called the preternatural. It’s Karl Ratzinger—the Pope Benedict XVI—has actually written about this. And so his categories are natural, supernatural, or preternatural. And so the supernatural is of God and things that are of divine origin, of which Catholics believe. Natural is just natural what we see as the world, science, things like that. But then there's a category that's called the preternatural, and the preternatural has to do with things that are not necessarily from God, but are in between. And that would include Virgin Mary apparitions that are not yet approved by the church. And when you start to look into the people who have these experiences, their testimonies, to me, who's been well-trained in assessing what's preternatural, the preternatural has to do with angels and fallen angels, both of which the church believes in, right? And this gets back to Karl Nel's point that a lot of American Catholics today would say, 'Yeah sure they exist, but it's not like we'll ever encounter an angel or see an angel.' But this category of UFOs then opens up this idea of perhaps people are having experiences that are preternatural. This falls directly within Catholic theology, yeah. Inside Catholic teaching, I think it would be unusual... the church is pretty comfortable, most Catholics are pretty comfortable with a set of categories that are, you know, real but invisible. The real but invisible supernatural. And it would be... it would be a shift, let's say, if, you know, the church was like, 'And by the way, some of these preternatural beings can show up on Air Force cameras,' right? Like that would be... you know, not impossible, I mean, but it would be a different... it would be a different mode of thinking about these things than most Catholics have right now. We don’t know what would have happened if an F-16 flew above Fatima. We don’t know what it would have captured on film there. We just have to... yeah, we have to embrace the mystery. But I want to talk a little bit about how we got here. And Ross, it was your newspaper, 2017—you revealed, not you personally, but your paper revealed the existence of what we now call the Tic Tac video that was... it was flying by, it was buzzing a naval exercise off the coast of San Diego. And they had no idea what this thing was. And it was 2004. Today you might be able to say, oh that's Chinese technology, we were trying out some kind of technology. This thing seemed to defy the laws of physics, the jets could not capture it. People have come up with various scientific rationalizations, and we're going to talk about that. But I want to talk about the origin story of that article, and a fellow you're both familiar with named Robert Bigelow. He had a lot of money, he was a believer that aliens had come to Earth, and he was friends with Harry Reid, to whom he gave a lot of money. And then in 2017, Reid slipped $22 million into the defense budget to study UFOs. That program was called the Advanced Aerospace Threat Identification Program, or AATIP. A lot of that money went to Bigelow's aerospace company, and out of that we got the release of the Tic Tac video. That video is really what led us to today's UFO craze. And I want to ask you, Ross, as someone who primarily covers politics, basically, did this all start with a bribe? Robert Bigelow is this UFO enthusiast, he gives a lot of money to Harry Reid, Harry Reid slips something in the defense appropriations budget, it's pennies as far as the Pentagon is concerned, and boom, we get this video out of it? I would never... I think Harry Reid's interest in UFOs was entirely sincere. I think what you could say about that story, and Diana may have more of a sense of this than I do, is one of the arguments that gets made to dismiss this stuff is to basically say, 'Look, you know, it seems like there’s people who believe in UFOs inside the government, but that's just because Harry Reid put them there.' Right? So that every indicator of UFO belief inside the national security bureaucracy only exists because those millions of dollars that Reid put in the budget led to a bunch of people who love the X-Files or believed in aliens getting hired inside the government, and then they sort of laundered their pre-existing theories through... I don’t think that suffices to explain everything, but that is a narrative that you'll hear. Well, yeah, I mean it's differently, it's not that... I mean I don’t think anybody disputes that the Tic Tac video is authentic. Again, people have said it's like, look at like the cameras and stuff like that, this could be some, you know, technical trick, some malfunction of some computer somewhere, something like that. But it did emerge from... I do think it's kind of a funny way of looking at how the government works. We got this video, kind of the clearest, most concrete evidence of UFOs in my lifetime, and it came because Harry Reid—you said was sincere and is a UFO enthusiast—he snuck in just a little bit, $22 million. You know, nobody really noticed this, $22 million into the government, and out of that we are confronted by this discovery and now we have to talk about this. Diana, turning back to you, I love American Cosmic, as you know. You have a story in there towards the front of the book where you were blindfolded and driven out to the New Mexico desert. You were taken there by a man named Tyler, as you call him. You were accompanied by a man named James. They are anonymous in your book but they are men of distinction. Why did Tyler bring you there? Yes, this is a great question. And it actually answers the question you posed to Ross, which is, you know, did this all come from a bribe? But you know, I was studying this prior to knowing that this story would come out in 2017. In fact, my book was finished around that time period, and it was in press. So the research, of course, that takes many years to research a book. I started this in late 2011, early 2012. And what I found—I thought it would be fairly easy, by the way, to hypothesize that, you know, digital technology was creating this type of belief system because now when we see things in the sky, drones and whatnot, we take photos and videos and we put them on social media and that, you know, informs belief and belief rises through these communities that we see on social media. All right, that is not what happened, that's not what happened. My hypothesis was shifted when people from aerospace industry started to reach out to me and ask me about my former research into Catholic angel contact events. And these were people of incredible status, often affiliated with the Department of Defense, United States Department of Defense. And so I was shocked because they were interested in historical accounts that they said were very similar to what they were seeing, the fighter pilots were seeing, the mission controllers were seeing when they put satellites up into space. And that shocked me because I was a disbeliever in UFOs. And so I started to work with this community of people and again, bringing up Colonel Karl Nel's talk, which is public, anybody can watch it, where he talks about unelected people in government, unelected, you know, government people who have been affiliated and working on these programs. Deep State. Yeah, well I guess you could call it that, that's what he's saying. He calls them unelected. And you know, these are most likely the people who were showing up and asking me about my research. I can't say that for a fact, but it seems likely if that's the case. So my work predated any instance of the recognition of the Harry Reid program, okay? So this... and some of the people who had been working in this program had been doing so for more than 20 years, okay? So this program, I ascertained, had been around for a long time. I didn't have the language for it then. Now I can say that that was a crash retrieval program. And the person, Tyler, who took me and James—that's his name in the book—he has since come out as himself, Garry Nolan. So I can actually say his name now. He’s a Stanford microbiologist. But you would call this today—this is a UFO crash retrieval program, these exist within the government, UFO crash retrieval programs. I believe that first, crash retrieval programs exist because things crash and we want to know, you know, what’s landing in our United States soil. It’s a... crash retrievals are crash retrieval programs, not necessarily UFO crash retrieval programs. When we have rockets land or satellites fall... Sure, if an F-16 crashes we pick it up. Yeah, so these can be people whose specialty are crash retrievals. Alongside those would be things that they would examine that are unidentified. So this is where the origin of the crash retrieval program comes from. So I happened to be taken to a place where a person told me—and by the way, in American Cosmic, I just want to be clear, I was quite aware that, you know, I'd done my research, and in the history of UFOlogy, people like academics, naive academics, and journalists are shown things and told 'this is a UFO' or 'this is UFO debris'. So I put that with... I wanted to be honest with my audience and let them know that this could possibly be that. And so yes, we were taken to a place that was in a no-fly zone, we were blindfolded and brought into this alleged crash retrieval of a craft that supposedly crashed in the 1940s. It wasn't Roswell. Did I believe it at the time? No, I did not believe it, but I went anyway because to me, this was the establishment of a religion, a new form of religion. So I was basically documenting the believers and even maybe ground zero of this religion, which would be New Mexico. And you found something there? In a strict materialist sense, what can you say that you and Garry Nolan found there on the ground and was of interest to you and Garry Nolan? Yes, so it was of interest to me because I was looking at it within the framework of: they found relics, like religious relics, and now what are they going to do with them, the two scientists that I was with. So Tyler told us that... I first I said, 'I don't want to bring this home.' And we did find debris. So we were there for two days, and we found debris. And it looked like—this is by the way, I've said this on several podcasts and the internet seems to go crazy when I say it—but the material looked like metallic frog skin. And I just reported that, that's what it looked like to me, okay? So Garry did put it in his backpack and we went through the airport. Now Tyler goes through the airport without having to go through TSA. All right? Not me, and not Garry. So we go through the airport and Tyler had already told us, 'Garry is going to get stopped and he's going to have this, you know, his backpack looked at and so forth, and don't worry about it, it'll be fine, right?' And so indeed that happens to Garry, we—he and I—are both very, very, you know, concerned, we have anxiety. But he passes through. He brings it back to his lab and he studies it. And what he finds is that it’s engineered. And it's engineered in a way in which he says that it was impossible to be engineered, especially at that time. And if it's engineered like that today, it would be at great expense, millions and millions of dollars. This is what he said at that time, and he and I reported it in American Cosmic. Since that time, he's gone on record talking about other debris that people have presented him with, and also a re-examination of that debris. So, so yeah, we did find something. From my perspective, this is an important... this is important, the physical evidence of UFOs. Now, do I believe that that’s physical evidence of UFOs? I don’t know, but I can tell you that many people believe that. And many people in the government, like Colonel Karl Nel, like Admiral Tim Gallaudet, Lou Elizondo, right? They believe, they absolutely believe that this is of non-human origin. That doesn't mean it is, that just means that that’s what they believe. There are people in the government that do believe... I mean, it’s a no-fly area, you picked up something, you and Garry Nolan differ about some of the specifics about what was picked up, but you both picked up strange materials, let's, we can say that. And I want to talk to you both—this is something that you’ve talked about before, but we were referencing earlier the Wall Street Journal report that came out some months ago about a vast government disinformation campaign. The idea being that all of UFOlogy, all of the study of this stuff, it’s bullshit, in other words. There’s nothing to it. This was a program invented by the U.S. government to cover up our real planes, our SR-71s, our F-117s, all of these things. The idea that we wanted, or the government rather wanted, people to believe that these things were UFOs. And it spiraled out of control at some point, it became a hazing ritual and people with serious security clearances were being told, 'Oh, by the way, we have a UFO we captured from aliens, no we can't show it to you,' but this spread and all of these people knew this thing. And Diana, I know you're very suspicious of what the government says. Ross, I think you're more in the camp of, like, there’s some real evidence here that points to, well yeah, maybe some of this is made up. Would that be fair to say? I mean, I think there is plenty of evidence across multiple generations that the government has used UFO narratives as a form of disinformation. I think that that is clear. Whether that means those disinformation campaigns explain the weirdness of the situation now, I’m pretty agnostic about. Well, first of all, the disinformation campaign narrative doesn’t explain the weird footage that we were talking about earlier, right? It doesn’t explain the Tic Tac video. It doesn’t explain a lot of things. It doesn't explain those things, unless you... well, unless you think it’s like a really vast disinformation campaign... You know, in 1994 all of these kids see a UFO land at their school, the Ariel School in Zimbabwe. Dr. John Mack from Harvard goes out and talks to them, believes them, does tremendous damage to his career in the process. But you know, these are people who are now adults, they stick by their stories. No, it doesn't explain any of the UFO experience narratives, no. What it could explain is why you have, again, this thing that sort of is like the pebble in my shoe and I can’t get rid of it, right? This like high-level government interest in the phenomenon. You could say, okay look, the government, yeah, it like memed itself into belief in UFOs in a way that eventually reached the level of Chuck Schumer and Marco Rubio, but in the end, that interest is based on something that has its roots in disinformation. That is what could be the case. And the reason to think that it could be the case is mostly just that the alternative seems, you know, even crazier, right? And that’s, so that’s... that’s all I would say. I think, again, I think Diana has gone deeper, much deeper than I have into like the world of people who see themselves as investigating this stuff, working on this stuff inside the government, right? And so she has a more direct sense that no, there’s enough people here who are sure there’s something there that it can’t all be disinformation. The only thing I’ll say is that just in my limited experience, there is a tendency for these stories to sort of dissolve a bit or kind of retreat from you the more you reach for them, right? That people will sort of offer kind of big claims and then there is kind of a retreat. And I've seen congressmen—I think it was Eric Burlison, one of the congressmen who’s interested in this—doing an interview recently and he said something like this: like, you know, there’s more promise than is delivered. And that is something that I have obviously observed as I’ve become interested in this. Yeah, Diana, what do you think about that? I mean, do you wonder at all that we've now had numerous whistleblowers come before Congress, do the podcast circuit, talk about these things? Do you think any of them perhaps were victims of a disinformation campaign? Or is the disinfo thing that's being pushed in outlets like the Wall Street Journal—do you think that’s just nonsense through and through? Yeah, so I just question why... what objective would we have the Wall Street Journal to call out the U.S. government for something that they’d like, you know, are they not patriots? Like, if it is indeed true that this is the UFO is a cover for some really amazing technology that we have, why would they out our government like that? I think that that’s strange. But I do agree that the history of UFOlogy has been infiltrated by misinformation, very specifically from the government. So absolutely. So it’s a really difficult field to get into and to have something concrete to say because the story shifts all the time. And especially the government story. Right now you have people in the government saying one thing and then very credible people in the government contradicting that. So that’s confusing for anyone. What I tend to do is I’ve written about the government, you know, and I didn't mean to write about it by the way, it just happened in American Cosmic. I wrote about that. Then my next book covered just people who had experiences. And you know, we're talking about credible people who would rather have not had UFO experiences but did have them, and I described what the character of those experiences are. In the next book I did, which was, you know, going to be released soon, The Others, in this book I'm doing more of an analysis of it comparatively with religion. And I'm basically saying that if indeed it's the case that we are on the dawn of learning about non-human intelligence, the organic framework that we already have is religion. So traditional religions have already helped us learn to interface with non-human intelligence, and it seems like we can learn something from them. And so that’s my position on it today. And I feel like this is good news for Ross, who just wants people to accept religion and go to their mainstream religion, go to their houses of worship. And the ontological shock of an extraterrestrial rival might be just what you're looking for. Let's just go to the lightning round, okay? All right. Going to start off with this. Now this is just very quickly, and try to go just yes or no, or as short an answer as you can. Are either of you expecting anything remarkable from this disclosure that Donald Trump has promised the American public? Ross, let's start with you. I would say I think we'll get something that is interesting, but I would be skeptical that you would get something that actually resolves the questions that I have about the issue. And I say that just in part from observing what’s happened so far with things around the JFK assassination and everything else, right? I think the administration has a pattern of overpromising and under-delivering. And I think it’s possible to do a kind of disclosure on this subject where you are, you know, doing an Epstein-style document dump in which there’s lots of like every unverifiable claim ever made about the subject that doesn’t get at the question that Diana is talking about, like is there something hidden inside the government that even senators and people in the White House don’t know about? More light than heat. Diana, are you pretty much on the same page there? Not necessarily. I mean, generally I would say that the government, what the government is going to tell us is going to be disappointing and disappointing for people who are interested in UFOlogy and belief in UFOs. But I have a different feeling about what’s happening now. When American Cosmic came out in 2019, I didn't imagine that within 2021 the government—the Department of Intelligence—would release the document that talks about non-human intelligence and the small... you know, the small percentage of unexplained that they are very interested in. I didn't expect that. I believe that probably if the President talks about UFOs, I think that’s going to be big. I do. I don’t think he’s going to give us the smoking gun, but the very fact that he’s going to talk about it publicly to all Americans, yes, I think something like that is on the horizon. I could be wrong, but I do think that. Do you think that he is going to say that non-human intelligence exists? Yeah, that would be pretty amazing. I think that he’s going to say that we have a program in place that is determining whether or not non-human intelligence exists. That’s what I think he’s going to say, something to that effect. All right. Are presidents told the whole truth of what the government knows about UFOs? Ross, go first. I have absolutely no idea, I’m totally agnostic. If we, say, find some alien fish on Europa, will the government be honest about it? Ross. A fish? Yeah, just an alien fish, you know they got that big ice and it’s like, okay, we find an alien but it’s just non-human. I think the government doesn’t have strong incentives to lie about the discovery of forms of life in the solar system. So I think there’s a fear of committing to it for scientific reasons because there’s debate about like, you know, is this actually proof of life, but I don’t think there would be a government cover-up about non-intelligent life in the solar system. Diana, did a balloon of some kind crash in Roswell or was it something else? I'm agnostic. I'm going to do a Ross. Okay, all right, a lot of agnosticism. Three Catholics walk into a flying saucer and we're all agnostic about this stuff. All right, one of the most important whistleblowers here is a fellow named David Grusch. His credentials are impeccable, military intelligence. In as few words as possible, one word if you can, David Grusch. Ross, what do you make of him? Frustrating. Diana. I second that. All right. We're going to stay on Grusch for a second. Grusch claimed that Mussolini’s government recovered a UFO that was then transferred from the Vatican to the U.S. military. True or false? Ross. I’m going to go with unlikely. Okay. Diana. I don't believe that. Okay, one more, last question guys. Who is the researcher of this phenomenon you respect the most? You cannot say Jacques Vallée, and Ross you cannot say Diana. So Diana, you go first. Leslie Kean. Leslie Kean. The great Leslie Kean who co-wrote the Tic Tac story for the New York Times. I think that's a good way to just wrap this up, make it circular. Guys, I cannot thank you enough. Thank you, Will. Thank you, Ross. Good to see you again.

Michael O’Leary - Group CEO at Ryanair | Investment Conference 2026

Norges Bank Investment Management(107.0K)·15:07·18.8K views·2026-05-07
Michael O'LearyGroup CEO, Ryanair
TL;DRRyanair maintains its position as Europe’s most profitable airline through a ruthless, singular focus on cost-efficiency, a flat management structure, and the strategic use of controversial publicity to drive bookings.
Eliminating Intermediaries: Ryanair was the first...

TL;DR

Ryanair maintains its position as Europe’s most profitable airline through a ruthless, singular focus on cost-efficiency, a flat management structure, and the strategic use of controversial publicity to drive bookings.

Radical Cost Efficiency and Behavioral Engineering

  • Eliminating Intermediaries: Ryanair was the first airline to remove travel agents from its distribution model, cutting 20% in distribution costs for an industry that typically operates on 1% margins.
  • Customer Behavioral Shifts: The implementation of baggage fees was designed to change behavior rather than just generate revenue.
      • Baggage usage dropped from 80% of passengers to 20%, significantly reducing labor costs for check-in and handling.
      • Fuel Savings: Reducing weight via fewer bags significantly lowers the company's $5 billion annual fuel bill.
  • Automation of Labor: O'Leary aims to use technology to eventually replace as much human labor as possible, including check-in staff and baggage handlers.
      • Pilot Productivity: He views current pilot labor laws (limiting flight time to 18 hours a week) as unproductive and expressed a desire for future drone technology to further reduce pilot costs.

Management Philosophy and "Anti-Culture"

  • Skepticism of Corporate "Culture": O'Leary dismisses traditional "culture-smulture" initiatives, focusing instead on profitability over the next 5–10 years.
  • Flat Organizational Structure: The company maintains only three layers of management between the front line and the CEO to ensure agility and speed in decision-making.
  • Strategic Simplicity: Ryanair avoids complexity by shunning M&A, hotels, or airport ownership, focusing exclusively on selling seats.
  • Fleet Strategy: The airline is transitioning to Boeing Max 10 aircraft, which provide 20% more seats while burning 20% less fuel, fundamentally transforming the company's unit economics for the next decade.

Marketing via Controversy and "Cheap Publicity"

  • The Power of Adverse Publicity: O'Leary notes that bookings often increase following negative press. He prioritizes being the cheapest and most on-time over being "well-loved" by the public or travel agents.
  • The Elon Musk Spat: A public feud with Elon Musk over Starlink technology and fuel drag generated an estimated $30 million in free publicity.
      • This led to a 3% increase in bookings during a typically quiet period in January.
  • Brand Reputation: Ryanair consistently ranks last in passenger satisfaction surveys, a metric O'Leary ignores in favor of price dominance. He famously stated he would quit if the airline ever won a "Travel Agents Airline of the Year" award.

Competitive Outlook and Technological Threats

  • Inefficient Competition: O'Leary views the European airline industry as "modestly competitive" at best, characterizing legacy carriers as inefficient, government-subsidized, and poorly regulated.
  • Existential Risks: He identifies "Star Trek" technology (teleportation/beaming) as the only true threat to his low-cost model. Until such technology exists, he believes Ryanair will continue to "blow up" the competition by widening the price gap.
  • Regulatory Environment: He expressed disdain for EU environmental taxes and regulations, labeling them as "bullshit" hurdles that the airline must manage through extreme efficiency.

Notable Quotes

"I get very nervous at these culture-smulture meetings, yada yada yada. I am a skeptic when we do all the cultural stuff... We want to make money for the next 5, 10 years, and if we do that, there's a reasonable prospect we'll survive 15, 20 years." — Michael O'Leary
"What keeps me awake at night? Star Trek. Because it doesn't matter how bloody cheap my fares are going to be... somebody, sometime is going to invent some way of beaming people across Europe." — Michael O'Leary
"The advantage of not being overly sensitized about my reputation or our image or being culturally popular is we're ruthlessly low cost and that gives us huge freedom to chase those kind of cost-saving measures." — Michael O'Leary
"As long as it doesn't revolve around safety... we're up for any bit of cheap publicity we can get." — Michael O'Leary
Our next speaker brings a distinctive approach to culture. Michael O'Leary is the group CEO of Ryanair and previously served as CEO of the airline. He transformed a struggling airline through a low-cost model. Today, it is Europe's largest and one of the most profitable airlines. His approach is a relentless focus on cost and execution. The result is a culture of extreme efficiency and an airline that has made travel affordable for millions. Daniel Balthasar, co-chief investment officer of Active Strategies of Norges Bank Investment Management, will explore how he did this. Please welcome Michael and Daniel. Michael, a warm welcome to our conference. Thank you very much. It is a great pleasure to be here. We met in this office a couple of years ago for an investor meeting. We did. After that, I received a letter from you thanking me for the meeting, which made me feel really, really special. That has never happened to me in my 25 years of doing this. What does that tell me about you and Ryanair? Well, firstly it tells me you didn't buy enough of our stock after that meeting, which is why I had to do the follow-up letter to encourage you to buy more. We have a cost focus as well; we like to buy low. Oh, there's never been a better opportunity. The stock is down about 25%. Just don't tell anybody because we're buying it as fast as we can at the moment, and I don't want to be competing with anybody else in the room. Very good. So if the culture is set at the top, how does that translate into how 25,000 people behave every single day? I get very nervous at these culture-smulture meetings, yada yada yada. I am a skeptic when we do all the cultural stuff. I respect David, but you know, I don't want to be running a company that survives 300 years and for 55 generations. We want to make money for the next 5 or 10 years, and if we do that, there's a reasonable prospect we'll survive 15 or 20 years. But ultimately, somebody's going to come along and reinvent the wheel. I don't think Nokia failed, by the way, because the management were arrogant or there was a culture of fear in Nokia. Nokia failed because fundamentally the iPhone came along and totally transformed the technology. And that bedevils all of us. What keeps me awake at night? Star Trek. Because it doesn't matter how bloody cheap my fares are going to be; when it comes to flying in Europe, no one's going to beat Ryanair on price ever. But somebody, sometime is going to invent some way of beaming people across Europe. Although the European Commission will probably find some way to tax it for some bullshit environmental cause, yada yada yada. But ultimately, some sort of transformative technology will come along, and it doesn't matter how good our culture was or how good our cost base was, we're going to get blown out of the water. Until that time comes, our culture is we kind of... I kind of shout and rant and rave at everybody. We're not interested in explaining our culture. Our culture is we want to deliver the lowest fare and most on-time flights, and we'll do it by having the lowest costs bar nobody in the industry. And we will keep transforming our cost base. We lead the airline industry across the world. We were the first people to get rid of travel agents when the internet came along because we'd been paying 20% for distribution for 15 years in an industry that had a 1% margin. Why the hell were we paying 20% to mom and pop travel agents? Internet came along, we were out the door. Ban the travel agents; they were gone. We were the first people 15 years ago to charge for bags. And as you know, as it says in the good Bible, Moses came down from the mountain top and the fourth commandment is "thou shalt be entitled to bring free bags on board aircraft forevermore." But by charging for bags, we went from 80% of passengers on our flights bringing a bag to 20% of passengers today now bringing a bag. Which means not alone have we got rid of huge amounts of labor at our airports—and again I slightly disagree with David, I want to treat people well, but ultimately I want to get rid of as many people as I can within the business. And the ones who remain I'll treat well. Pilots, for example, are egregiously expensive employees. By law they can't fly more than 800 hours a year, which is 18 hours a week, which I think is ridiculously fucking unproductive. And for that I pay them about a quarter million each. So, technology will in time come along. You know, if the US military can fly drones or fly aircraft across with... pilots will ultimately get competed out of the system. I want to treat my pilots very well, I want to treat my cabin crew very well, but at the back I also want to find some way of finding a technology that will replace them all. And then the cost of flying across Europe, even with the EU's bullshit environmental taxes and ridiculous regulation, will become even cheaper than it ever has been before. So our focus and our culture is all about: make it cheap, make it on-time. After that, go away, you're not getting anything else, and then be viciously obsessively focused on how do we make it cheaper, cheaper, cheaper by constantly reinventing the system and fundamentally changing customer behavior. Charging for bags was one of the greatest initiatives we've ever come up with, not because—and everybody's obsessed with "oh, we just want the money." I would happily give away the money ultimately to shareholders, but give away the money if I could get rid of the last 20% of bags. I would have no check-in staff, I'd have no baggage handling staff, I'd have no lost baggage staff. The aircraft fly about 3% lighter which reduces my fuel consumption. My fuel bill's about 5 billion a year. So the hidden benefits of getting rid of check-in bloody bags is that we're consuming less fuel and with jet oil today at about $157 a barrel, anything I can do to get out those kind of costs, we will do. And that's why we are the most profitable airline in the world. We are certainly the biggest international airline in the world. And yet we plan to grow in size from 200 million passengers last year to 300 million passengers in the next eight years by taking delivery of 300 new aircraft from Boeing, the Max 10 aircraft, which are 20% bigger in terms of seats, but they burn 20% less fuel per seat. I am going to transform my economics for the next 10 years with this new technology. They'll be flown by the same two pilots, the same four or five cabin crew, which should make us hugely lower cost. It will widen the price advantage I have over every other airline in Europe and I'm going to blow them all up and own the industry until somebody in a back bedroom somewhere invents some way of beaming people across Europe and then I'm screwed. Excellent. You operate in a hyper-competitive industry. Hyper-competitive industry? Is it? In Europe? No, they're all useless. Government owned, heavily regulated, hugely government subsidized. All the legacies are running around at the moment trying to get bailouts. God bless the Republicans in the United States, they're now about to bailout two of the most bankrupt airlines, Spirit and JetBlue, who should have been allowed to go to the wall many years ago. So no, it's not a hyper-competitive industry. It's a very badly regulated industry. It is government owned which is why it is inefficient, hugely expensive and inefficient in Europe. So all I have to be is modestly competitive to be better than the rest of the competition in Europe and that's not setting the bar very high. Amazing. Most people though, most employees, they don't like an over focus on cost. How do you keep them motivated? Fear, terror, stalking the corridors late at night. We don't. I mean, you know, the great thing about Ryanair is we've been doing it this way for 30 years. So you know, we hire about 50 graduates every year. We hire people who want to work hard. We try to set an example. There is a very slim kind of management; there are only three layers of management between somebody on the front line and me. And therefore we keep it very flat. But the advantage we have is we're a single, one-goof kind of operation. It's only an airline; all we have to sell is a seat. So we don't need complexity. We avoid M&A. We don't get involved in buying hotels or buying airports or buying whatever else it is. We buy aircraft, hopefully at times of distress when Boeing or Airbus needs orders. And then all we do is sell seats all day long. So we don't need a lot of complicated management. And then but we are very good because I think we've a very flat management structure. We're very good at pouncing on new initiatives as they come along. And the great advantage of being the great strength of Ryanair is we're not very much beloved. We finished last in almost every passenger survey. There's an award in Ireland for the Irish Travel Agents Association, and there's only two airlines in Ireland. Aer Lingus have won those awards, which is the ex-state airline. Aer Lingus have won the Travel Agents Airline of the Year award for 30 years in a row. I have promised that I will quit if we ever win that award. The advantage of not being overly sensitized about my reputation or our image or being culturally popular is we're ruthlessly low cost and that gives us huge freedom to chase those kind of cost saving measures or to reinvent the way we drive the airlines. And then ultimately all the other morons will follow us along. I mean the American airlines have just discovered charging for bags, 15 or 20 years after we started doing it. Now, they're trying to do it as a way of gaining revenue whereas we did it not as a way of generating revenue but as a way of eliminating cost. It's fascinating because most of the CEOs we talk to, they talk about more customer centricity, keep the customer happy, put the customer in the center of everything. Yet you're doing the opposite but win. But that's because we have an enormous price advantage over everybody else. Customers ultimately in every survey say, "oh, I wish they were nicer, I want a refund of a non-refundable." What part of "no refund" don't you understand? I don't care if your granny died or your cat was ill and you couldn't travel on the day. It was a non-refundable fare. Now, I want you to fly because I can sell you about 6 euros of ancillary revenues on board my aircraft. But we appear occasionally—it happens even to us—we occasionally mismanage situations. We get huge amounts of adverse publicity and what I notice every time we get a lot of adverse publicity is bookings go up. Elon Musk, who I've never met, never had any interaction with... we had this issue where we were trying to put Wi-Fi on board our aircraft at the start of the year. I think free Wi-Fi on board aircraft is coming. The problem at the moment is the technology doesn't yet exist. We'd have to put a couple of dimples on the outside of the aircraft. It would be about a 2% fuel drag. My fuel bill is 5 billion a year. 2% fuel drag is something of the order of about 100 million and I'm not paying 100 million to put free Wi-Fi on board for my passengers. Starlink, which is a terrific technology, I've no issue with the Starlink technology. Starlink believe passengers will pay you 1 euro, 2 euro, and it'll pay for itself. They clearly have never met any Ryanair passengers. Because by the time we've gone up and down the cabin selling them teas, coffees, scratch cards and anything else we can kind of think to sell them, we don't have time to be running up and down taking 1 euro transactions yada yada. So I happened to say look, Starlink is great but you know the system doesn't work. We're not taking a fuel penalty of 100 million a year because that would be 50 cents on every ticket. And someone told Elon Musk that I'd slagged off Starlink so he popped up something on his internet feed going "who is this wanker in Ryanair, who's this Ryanair gobshite yada yada. He's clearly an idiot he knows nothing about flying." So I popped back, "who is this eejit in Twitter running that cesspit that's Twitter having a pop off me? I'm correct; it does have a fuel drag." He says it doesn't have a fuel drag and then he says I know nothing about avionics, which is probably true; I know very little about avionics, but he knows nothing about flying low cost airlines either. He then ran a poll on his Twitter X—I don't have a Twitter site by the way because I have better things to do with my life—but he ran a poll on his 1.5 million: "should I buy Ryanair?" We politely pointed out, "you're American therefore you can't buy Ryanair, we're European and you have to be more than 50% EU owned." Next thing it fucking exploded during the second week of January which is one of the quietest weeks for bookings in Europe and my bookings just took off. Next thing we're running around how do we manage, so we ran a big idiot seat sale. We got AI to come up with some ads of me smacking him over the head and him choking me yada yada. And we manufactured about $30 million of free publicity. I was holding a press conference in Dublin back in January to do my kind of usual "this summer we'll do 25 new routes" bla bla bla. They took the press conference live on Sky; CNBC went live to this eejit in Dublin desperately trying to make some news about how Elon Musk is wrong yada yada yada. We got about 30 million free publicity out of it and bookings for about six weeks went up by about three percentage points. Did you send him a thank-you letter? Pardon me? Did you send Elon Musk a thank-you letter? No, actually we sent him a free voucher for a free Ryanair flight round to the Twitter offices in Dublin and got a photograph of me handing in the free Ryanair voucher. He hasn't claimed it yet but I hope he will at some point in time in the not too distant future. So one of the other things we do is we will jump on any bit of cheap publicity we can because cheap publicity in our business, where you have such a price advantage, drives enormous sales and enormous bookings. And so as long as it doesn't revolve around safety. I remember Trump getting elected the first time said if he'd murdered somebody on 5th Avenue he'd still get elected. And we were thinking well maybe if we murdered somebody on 5th Avenue would it drive bookings up on Ryanair? We thought no, that was probably pushing the envelope a little bit too far. But other than that, if it doesn't involve safety, we're up for any bit of cheap publicity we can get. Okay, so to summarize: a cost focus, free marketing, and an incredible sense of humor definitely created a winning culture. Thank you very much, Michael. Please join me with a huge round of applause. Thank you. Thank you very much. Thank you for being here.

Build your AI Muscle 💪❗️| Keynote @qsummit Startup Conference

Mathe by Daniel Jung(943.0K)·32:02·150 views·2026-05-07
Daniel JungEducator and Keynote Speaker
TL;DRWe are in a 1,000-day window of fundamental economic and educational transformation where AI agents and "personal tutors" allow for hyper-lean, billion-dollar scale.
Success in this era requires a shift from passive social media consumption to a daily practice of "building...

TL;DR

We are in a 1,000-day window of fundamental economic and educational transformation where AI agents and "personal tutors" allow for hyper-lean, billion-dollar scale. Success in this era requires a shift from passive social media consumption to a daily practice of "building AI muscle" through hands-on experimentation.

The New Economic Reality: The Rise of the "N-of-1" Giant

  • Hyper-Lean Scaling: The era of the one- or two-person billion-dollar company has arrived.
      • Example: Matthew Gallagher’s company generates $1.8 billion in sales with only one employee, managing everything else through AI tools like Claude and Gemini.
  • The 1,000-Day Window: Former Stability AI CEO Emad Mostaque posits that we are in a 1,000-day cycle where economics will change fundamentally.
  • The Shift to Agentic AI: Estimates suggest agentic AI (autonomous agents) will own 60% of software economics by 2030, though it will likely happen much sooner.
  • Internal Disruption: Daniel Jung revealed he transitioned his own business from a team of 12 employees to a model run entirely by himself and AI, significantly reducing costs while increasing reach.

The Evolution of Learning: Solving the "2 Sigma Problem"

  • Personal AI Tutors: The "2 Sigma problem"—the idea that students with 1-on-1 tutors perform two standard deviations better—is effectively solved. Anyone can now build a "Jarvis-style" tutor in their pocket.
  • RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation): To avoid AI hallucinations in education, Jung emphasizes RAG pipelines.
      • By training AI on specific, trusted datasets (like his own 3,000+ math videos and scripts), he creates tutors that provide 100% accurate, curated answers with source citations.
  • Meta-Skill Mastery: Nobel Prize winner Demis Hassabis (Google DeepMind) emphasizes that "learning how to learn" is the only vital skill for the next decade of rapid change.
  • Evidence-Based Success: Harvard studies show that AI tutoring now outperforms traditional active learning methods in subjects like physics.

Building "AI Muscle": From Consumption to Action

  • The Daily AI Ritual: Jung urges a commitment of 30 to 90 minutes every day to learn AI tools.
      • This isn't "nerd stuff" anymore; it is the new literacy.
  • The "Do, Learn, Repeat" Framework: Move away from theoretical learning to "Action Mode."
      • Tool Spotlight: NotebookLM is cited as "the best tool on earth" for grounding AI in specific PDFs and podcasts to create summaries, quizzes, and personalized study guides.
      • Automation: Tools like n8n allow for drag-and-drop workflow automation, enabling users to build complex systems without deep coding knowledge.
  • Algorithm Curation: Jung recommends creating new, clean social media accounts (TikTok/Instagram) dedicated solely to training the algorithm on AI news and experts to eliminate distraction.

Global Scaling via Personal Branding

  • Global Reach without Language Barriers: Jung is currently scaling his brand toward "1 billion views" by using AI agents to dub his math content into English, Portuguese, and Spanish.
      • The process is entirely automated; he does not speak the languages, yet the AI maintains his voice and persona.
  • The Trust Factor: As deepfakes become indistinguishable from reality, the value of a trusted "Personal Brand" increases. AI should be used to empower the human element, not replace the need for trust.

Notable Quotes

"The greatest shortcoming of the human race is our inability to understand the exponential function... if you double your steps 30 times, you land nearly two times to the moon and back. This is the time we are in." — Daniel Jung
"Learning to learn is the key skill. The only thing we know for sure is it’s going to be a lot of change over the next 10 years." — Demis Hassabis (Founder, Google DeepMind)
"I’m running my company with no persons now. I had 12 employees; everything is running on AI. For me, it’s a cost-saver and a time-saver." — Daniel Jung
"Don’t quit university, but if you deal with [AI] in the best case, you earn a lot of money... it is your turn now. Wake up your inner child and build your AI muscle." — Daniel Jung

If you don't do it, I filmed this keynote and will launch it on my YouTube channel. The business went quite well. During the 2000s, what today is AI, back then was digitization. I thought about how to digitize my own company. I thought true innovation comes from combining things to create something new. So I looked at the USA, Stanford, and MIT. So I said okay, watch it, you could do it too. Who knows the guy on the left side? Dr. Eberhard Weiß, Telekolleg. He's the first Daniel Jung, since the 70s. Yeah, it was a Sunday morning and he was talking about fractions and differentiation, etc. Nobody was watching it back then, but I was watching it. In the middle is Dr. Gilbert Strang, a professor at MIT now known as a YouTuber. He has the best lectures. I mean, even today, if you want to learn linear algebra, he is a phenomenon. I said okay, TV was linear. Gilbert Strang was 1 hour and a half long. In 2011, I was doing on-site learning and there was the question: "What is a fraction? What is a linear function?" I could explain it in 5 minutes, so I decided to record it in 5 minutes. I didn't know what would happen the next 10 to 15 years. They said video will never be a thing. I said give it a shot. Today it's not only 16 to 9, it's 9 to 16 because everyone is scrolling like hell on TikTok and Instagram, and you have to step in. I produced a lot of content out of my 3,000 videos on YouTube and you know the feedback: everyone is learning with me. Meanwhile, there are other creators who are producing phenomenal content. You all know Lehrer Schmidt, SimpleClub, Mathematrick, and MathePeter. Everyone is producing content, content, content, and getting this feedback. YouTube was a door opener throughout the 2010s. By the middle of the 2010s, coming from mathematics, I was dealing with artificial intelligence. When I was watching the whole world, Google DeepMind got the famous game AlphaGo and they beat Lee Sedol, the best player in the world at the game of Go. If you don't know Go, it's chess on a much higher level. I said this is a breakthrough and now everything is changing. By 2016 and 17, you can go to my channel and watch my old content from back then. I was talking about machine learning processes and what AI can do for learning. That was back in 2018 and 19 when AI wasn't at this point yet. This was all about content, content, content. But what about AI? I mean, when it comes to learning, trust is everything. Körner is one of the leading experts worldwide. He did his PhD at KIT. His company is now acquired by Aleph Alpha. You probably know the company. We are releasing Mathe Vorkurs, Abi Kurs, and more. Why am I doing it? It makes a lot of fun. It's really interesting to see that if you are in the app and ask a question, you can be 100% sure that it's not a hallucination. This is because it's trained on the data coming from the videos and the scripts. It's called RAG pipelines. If you don't know RAG pipelines, RAG stands for Retrieval-Augmented Generation. It's trained on the data you put in. It's not like, "Oh, is ChatGPT or Claude doing the right thing if you ask them?" So, RAG pipelines are very important if you build something in the future. I hope you make a note of RAG pipelines. How does it work? Und auf den Daten der Videos, der Skripte, der Quizzes, kuratiert erstellte Inhalte abgesegnet von der akkreditierten Hochschule INU überprüft, könnt euch sicher sein, dass das was hier dann ausgespuckt wird auch richtig ist und ihr habt die entsprechenden Quellenverweise und ihr könnt euch noch mal eine Flashcard...
